Immigration activist Bob Libal commented in a telephone interview that "These tragedies are compounded when it's incredibly dangerous and incredibly expensive and we push migration into the hands of illicit actors."

In the aforementioned 2003 case in Victoria, Texas, prosecutors stated that the driver heard the immigrants begging and screaming for their lives but refused to release them. That driver was ultimately sentenced to nearly 34 years in prison. The Border Patrol has reported multiple truck seizures in the Laredo, Texas, area in the preceding month, including one on July 7 where agents found 72 individuals crammed into a truck with no escape route. These individuals were from Mexico, Ecuador, Guatemala, and El Salvador. Mexican authorities have also encountered similar situations, such as the discovery of 110 migrants suffocating in a truck in Veracruz last December, and four migrants suffocating in a truck carrying 55 people in Veracruz last October.

This report was compiled with contributions from Associated Press writers Mike Graczyk in Houston, Elliot Spagat in San Diego, Peter Orsi in Mexico City, and Frank Bajak in San Antonio.

Tyrone M. Williams had barely been on the road for an hour when he heard the banging and cries coming from the trailer of his 18-wheeler. It was almost midnight on May 13, 2003, and Williams, a dairy trucker, was carrying human cargo. Earlier that night, human traffickers in southeast Texas had loaded dozens of undocumented immigrants into the rear compartment. For a fee of $7,500, Williams had agreed to drive them 120 miles north, through a Border Patrol checkpoint. The plan went awry when two of the smugglers who were supposed to rendezvous with Williams farther up the highway were detained. Williams was ordered to take his passengers all the way to Houston, an additional 200 miles. Driving through the hot Texas night, Williams never turned on the refrigeration unit in his dairy truck. As the temperature inside the cramped trailer rose to an unbearable 173 degrees, the people inside grew desperate, kicking holes in the walls, clawing at the insulation and screaming for help. Hours later, when Williams finally opened the doors on a stretch of highway in Victoria, Tex., 19 people inside were dead of dehydration, overheating and suffocation. The incident has been called the nation’s deadliest and most thoroughly documented human trafficking case, and it received renewed attention on Sunday, after authorities discovered a strikingly similar scene in San Antonio. At least 39 people were found packed into a sweltering tractor-trailer in a Walmart parking lot, appearing to have been loaded like cargo into the tight container without working air conditioning. Eight men died inside, and a ninth died later at the hospital. Many others were critically overheated, with some believed to have suffered brain damage. Survivors told investigators that as many as 100 people were crowded in the truck’s trailer at one point, as The Washington Post reported. The driver has been arrested and is expected to be charged in federal court. Police found a truck July 22 near a Walmart in San Antonio that contained at least 39 people suspected of crossing the border illegally. Eight of the people died at the scene, and two died later in a hospital. (Monica Akhtar/The Washington Post) Authorities said it bore all the hallmarks of the immigrant smuggling operations that are common in that part of the country. Officials with the U.S. Department of Homeland Security have indicated smuggling by truck is on the rise, with several operations uncovered in Texas in recent months. Investigators have revealed little about the chain of events that led to the deaths in San Antonio, but the 19 immigrant deaths in Victoria in 2003 highlight the perils of traveling by truck to evade border agents, as well as the complex networks that smuggle people into the country. According to court documents from the case, a man and woman who led a smuggling operation in southern Texas had been recruiting American truck drivers to carry undocumented immigrants through checkpoints near the border, paying thousands of dollars for short drives. Court papers say they were especially interested in non-Hispanic drivers with out-of-state licenses because they were less likely to be scrutinized by authorities. Tyrone M. Williams fit the bill: He was a native of Jamaica who had immigrated legally to the United States, and he drove a truck licensed in New York. On top of that, he ran his own trucking business, and his vehicle was refrigerated. He had recently transported dairy products to from New York to Texas, so he had legitimate reasons to be in the state. How exactly the smugglers found Williams is not clear, but he started driving for them in the spring of 2003, according to court records. In May of that year, he agreed to pick up a group of undocumented immigrants from Harlingen, Tex., near the Mexico border, drive them through a checkpoint in Sarita, Tex., then drop them off with other members of the smuggling operation in Robstow, Tex. The trip was expected to take less than two hours, and Williams was to be paid $7,500. Williams and a female companion arrived in Harlingen late at night on May 13, 2003. They stayed in the cab of the truck while human traffickers loaded 74 people into the trailer and locked the doors. Instead of keeping the interior at a cool 55 degrees, as the smugglers had advised him, Williams turned off the refrigeration unit entirely, probably to appear more convincing to inspectors. When the truck reached the checkpoint in Sarita, he told an agent that his trailer was empty and that he was headed to Houston to pick up produce. The agent waved him through without trouble. The remaining 60 miles should have been easy. But shortly after pulling out of the checkpoint, Williams got a call from the head of the operation: the smugglers who were supposed to pick up the immigrants in Robstown had been detained. Instead of stopping in Robstown, Williams was told to drive all the way to Houston — some 200 miles away. Around that time, Williams and his companion, Fatima Holloway, started to hear the clamor from the compartment behind them. A fellow trucker flagged them down and said human arms were protruding from holes in the trailer walls. Holloway pleaded with Williams to let the passengers out, but Williams drove for at least another hour before stopping in a small town. He passed them several bottles of water through the holes but otherwise ignored their cries for help. At one point, worried about the damage to his rig, he called the smugglers to demand more money. Williams drove for another 90 minutes through southeast Texas before stopping again, this time at a truck stop in Victoria, Tex., 100 miles outside Houston. There, a witness spotted him passing water bottles through the holes in the trailer and alerted a clerk, who called police. Williams unhooked the trailer and sped off into the night. When authorities arrived at the truck stop, they found a scene of unspeakable agony and death. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit described it in grim detail in a 2010 ruling: There were several dead bodies on the ground by the trailer doors. Bodies, both dead and living, were stacked in a pile in the trailer. Some of the aliens were standing behind the pile. The aliens were stripped down to their underwear and were sweating. They had clawed at the foam on the inside of the trailer, and the trailer smelled of vomit, urine, feces, and blood. Seventeen people riding in Williams’s truck had died inside from dehydration, overheating and suffocation. Two died later at the hospital of the same causes. A 5-year-old boy was among the victims. Williams was arrested and indicted on dozens of counts, including conspiracy and harboring. Prosecutors sought the death penalty. In court, survivors testified that they took off sweat-soaked clothes, pounded at the walls and gasped for air through the small openings they created as the temperature inside the trailer reached 173 degrees, the Associated Press reported in 2005. Some of them frantically tried to get the attention of passing motorists, pushing shoes through the holes to show they were in distress. Holloway testified that she and Williams could hear the passengers screaming “el nino, el nino,” referring to the 5-year-old dying behind them, according to the Los Angeles Times. Williams argued he would have opened the doors if he had known the passengers were in serious danger, telling a judge, “I don’t kill people.” Prosecutors alleged he was aware of their suffering all along but did nothing. In 2011, after his case wound through the court system, Williams, then 40, was sentenced to nearly 34 years in prison without parole. Another 13 people were indicted in connection with the deaths. Most received sentences ranging from one year to 23 years in prison. In May 2013, on the 10th anniversary of the 19 immigrants’ deaths, an emergency room worker who treated the victims told ABC 13 she was still haunted by the scene. “If I knew then what I saw that night, I would have never gone into the medical field,” Mary Rose Garcia said. “There’s no way you can forget.” Thomas Homan, the acting director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, was among the agents who personally worked on the 2003 case. In a statement Sunday, he said the 19 immigrants died “as a result of the smugglers’ total indifference to the safety of those smuggled and the law.” “By any standard,” he said, “the horrific crime uncovered last night ranks as a stark reminder of why human smuggling networks must be pursued, caught and punished.” More from Morning Mix: Pennsylvania police, hunting for stolen laptops, say they stumbled on $40 million bitcoin scam Utah couple arrested after drugging heroin-addicted newborn to hide symptoms, police say Fukushima’s melted nuclear fuel likely sighted in ghostly underwater images

In a grim discovery that highlighted the perilous journey of migrants, San Antonio police officers investigated a scene on Sunday, July 23, 2017, where eight individuals were found deceased inside a tractor-trailer. This harrowing incident, which occurred outside a Walmart store amidst stifling summer heat, involved a vehicle packed with at least 30 other people. Federal authorities confirmed on Monday that the death toll had risen to 10 in this tragic case of a broiling tractor-trailer discovered packed with immigrants. A suspect in the smuggling operation, identified as James Mathew Bradley Jr., 60, of Clearwater, Florida, was awaiting a court appearance. While local authorities had indicated over the weekend that the driver was arrested, federal prosecutors did not immediately confirm Bradley's role as the rig's driver.

The discovery of eight bodies within the crowded 18-wheeler, parked in the oppressive summer heat, was followed by the deaths of two more victims at the hospital. Officials expressed grave concerns that the death toll might escalate, as nearly 20 individuals rescued from the truck were in critical condition, many suffering from severe dehydration and heatstroke. San Antonio Police Chief William McManus described the event as a "human-trafficking crime" and a "horrific tragedy." It remained unclear whether Bradley had legal representation. Authorities also did not confirm if the trailer was locked upon their arrival, but it was noted that the vehicle lacked functioning air conditioning. Fire Chief Charles Hood stated that the victims were "very hot to the touch" and appeared to have had "no signs of any type of water."

This incident marks another tragic end to a smuggling-by-truck operation. In a comparable case in 2003, 19 immigrants perished after being locked inside a stifling rig in Victoria, Texas. Based on initial survivor interviews from the San Antonio case, Thomas Homan, acting director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, indicated that over 100 people may have been transported in the truck at some point. Rescuers found 39 individuals inside the trailer, with the remainder believed to have escaped or found alternative transportation. Diplomats confirmed that some of the victims hailed from Mexico and Guatemala. Notably, four survivors were identified as minors, aged between 10 and 17 years old.

Details regarding the rig's origin and destination were not provided. However, Homan suggested it was improbable the truck was used for cross-border transport into the United States, explaining that migrants from Latin America typically cross on foot and are then met by drivers. Homan also asserted that despite the current arrest, authorities anticipate identifying and prosecuting additional individuals involved in the smuggling network. The tractor-trailer was registered to Pyle Transportation Inc. of Schaller, Iowa, and bore an Iowa license plate. A company representative did not immediately respond to requests for comment. San Antonio is situated approximately 150 miles (240 kilometers) from the Mexican border. On the day of the discovery, Saturday, July 22, 2017, San Antonio's temperature reached 101 degrees Fahrenheit (38 Celsius) and remained above 90 degrees Fahrenheit (32 C) until late evening.

The tragedy came to light when an individual from the truck approached a Walmart employee seeking water late Saturday night or early Sunday morning. The employee provided water and subsequently alerted the police. On Sunday evening, a vigil was held at a San Antonio church, drawing approximately 100 attendees to mourn the deceased. Immigrants' rights activists and church officials displayed signs with messages such as: "Who here is not an immigrant" and "No human is illegal." During the vigil, a moment of silence was observed, followed by speeches that attributed the deaths, in part, to federal and Texas authorities' stringent immigration policies, which they argued force migrants to undertake greater risks. Immigration activist Bob Libal commented in a telephone interview that "These tragedies are compounded when it's incredibly dangerous and incredibly expensive and we push migration into the hands of illicit actors."

In the 2003 case in Victoria, Texas, the driver was aware of the immigrants' pleas for help but refused to release them. That driver received a sentence of nearly 34 years in prison. Recent Border Patrol reports indicate at least four truck seizures in and around Laredo, Texas, during the month of July. On July 7, agents discovered 72 individuals confined in a truck with no apparent means of escape; these individuals were from Mexico, Ecuador, Guatemala, and El Salvador. Mexican authorities have also reported similar discoveries: In December of the previous year, 110 migrants were found trapped and suffocating in a truck in Veracruz. In October of the previous year, four migrants died from suffocation in a truck carrying 55 people, also in Veracruz state.

In a grim discovery, eight individuals were found deceased in the rear of a semi-trailer truck parked at a Walmart in southwest San Antonio. This incident, occurring on July 23, 2017, brought to light a suspected human smuggling operation. Officials are actively investigating the truck that contained individuals believed to be undocumented immigrants.

Despite the horrific loss of life in this Texas tractor-trailer tragedy, statistics reveal a significant decrease in apprehensions for illegal border crossings from Mexico in recent months when compared to previous years. Department of Homeland Security data indicates that in June, 21,659 individuals were apprehended or denied entry at U.S. ports of entry along the Mexican border. While this figure is slightly higher than May's numbers, it represents less than half of the total recorded in June 2016. This marks the fifth consecutive month where border apprehension numbers have been notably lower than those from the preceding year.

The discovery in San Antonio occurred early Sunday morning. Eight bodies were found inside the truck. Tragically, at least 30 other individuals discovered in the trailer were rushed to nearby hospitals, many in critical condition. Officials confirmed that one of these individuals later died on Sunday. Authorities reported that temperatures on Saturday had surpassed 100 degrees Fahrenheit, and the truck lacked any functioning air conditioning.

San Antonio Police Chief William McManus characterized the event as a "human trafficking crime." The driver of the truck was apprehended, and Homeland Security's Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) took the lead in the investigation. ICE Acting Director Thomas Homan stated, "By any standard, the horrific crime uncovered last night ranks as a stark reminder of why human smuggling networks must be pursued, caught and punished."

While President Trump's immigration policies have led to an increase in arrests of undocumented immigrants already within the country, border apprehensions began a sharp decline in February. Homeland Security Secretary John Kelly attributed this reduction to an overall decrease in the flow of illegal immigrants across the border. Kelly credited President Trump's executive actions, including those aimed at accelerating the construction of the U.S.-Mexico border wall and expanding the authority of federal immigration officials to detain undocumented immigrants. Although some of President Trump's directives have faced legal challenges, the prevailing atmosphere created by the administration's policies may be discouraging immigration.

"Since President Trump took office on Jan. 20, we have seen a dramatic drop in numbers," Kelly remarked in March. He further noted that this decrease was encouraging as it signified "many fewer people are putting themselves and their families at risk of exploitation, assault and injury by human traffickers and the physical dangers of the treacherous journey north."

Kelly also explained that enhanced border security measures have driven up the fees charged by human smugglers. Since November 2016, the cost for "coyotes" to transport individuals has more than doubled in certain areas, with prices reaching $8,000 compared to $3,500 in some mountainous regions. "The early results show that enforcement matters, deterrence matters, and that comprehensive immigration enforcement can make an impact," he asserted.

The majority of human smuggling cases operate outside public scrutiny. In fiscal year 2016, ICE's Homeland Security Investigations unit launched 2,110 investigations into human smuggling, resulting in 1,522 criminal convictions.

In the San Antonio incident, police were alerted to the dire situation when an individual from inside the truck requested water from a Walmart employee. The employee subsequently contacted the authorities. A similarly tragic event occurred in Texas in May 2003, which proved even more fatal, claiming the lives of 19 immigrants inside a sweltering tractor-trailer. Prosecutors in that case reported that the driver had heard the immigrants' desperate pleas but refused to release them. He was initially sentenced to multiple life terms, but an appeals court overturned the sentence. In 2011, he received a new sentence of 34 years in prison. Thomas Homan was personally involved in that case. "So long as I lead ICE, there will be an unwavering commitment to use law enforcement assets to put an end to these practices," he vowed.

San Antonio police officers investigate the scene Sunday, July 23, 2017, where eight people were found dead in a tractor-trailer loaded with at least 30 others outside a Walmart store in stifling summer... (Associated Press) SAN ANTONIO (AP) — The Latest on the deaths of 10 people whose bodies were found in a roasting tractor-trailer in a Walmart parking lot in San Antonio, Texas (all times local): 10:30 a.m. Federal authorities in Texas have charged the driver of a tractor-trailer with transporting immigrants in the U.S. illegally, an incident resulting in the death of 10 people. A complaint filed Monday accuses James Matthew Bradley of driving a trailer packed with immigrants for "commercial advantage or private financial gain." The charge carries the possibility of the death penalty. Bradley is expected to appear Monday morning in San Antonio. Authorities fear the death toll could rise because many of those rescued from the sweltering truck in San Antonio have been hospitalized with extreme dehydration and heatstroke. ___ 9:30 a.m. Federal authorities say a 10th person has died after being discovered in the back of a sweltering tractor-trailer in Texas. Prosecutors said in a statement Monday that the person died at a hospital. Nearly 20 others were hospitalized in dire condition after they were found in the truck outside a San Antonio Walmart early Sunday. ___ 9 a.m. Federal prosecutors say a man arrested following the deaths of nine people in a hot tractor-trailer outside a Texas Walmart will be charged Monday. Prosecutors say 60-year-old James Mathew Bradley Jr., of Clearwater, Florida, was taken into custody at the weekend after the bodies were discovered alongside nearly 20 more people in dire condition in the San Antonio parking lot. He will appear in federal court in San Antonio on Monday. The U.S. Attorney's Office haven't said Bradley was the alleged driver of the truck and prosecutors haven't outlined the charges he will face. Authorities fear the death toll from the grim weekend discovery could rise because many of those hospitalized were suffering from extreme dehydration and heatstroke. San Antonio Police Chief William McManus calls the deaths a "human-trafficking crime." ___ 12:07 a.m. A 60-year-old man who was arrested after authorities say at least nine people died in the back of a sweltering tractor-trailer found outside a Walmart in San Antonio is due in federal court. Federal prosecutors say James Mathew Bradley Jr. of Clearwater, Florida, will be charged on Monday. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement acting Director Thomas Homan says the truck driver is in custody, but the local U.S. Attorney's Office wouldn't say whether Bradley was the alleged driver who was arrested. Officials say nearly 20 other people rescued from the back of the rig early Sunday were hospitalized in dire condition, many with extreme dehydration and heatstroke. Foreign officials from Mexico and Guatemala confirmed people from those countries were found in the abandoned tractor-trailer.

**San Antonio Migrant Deaths: Officials Condemn "Horrific" Human Smuggling Tragedy**

A devastating incident in San Antonio, where nine migrants perished and thirty others were hospitalized due to extreme heatstroke and dehydration within a semi-trailer, has ignited urgent calls for action from numerous officials. Law enforcement has unequivocally labeled this event a "horrific" instance of human smuggling, intensifying the ongoing national discourse surrounding border security and immigration reform.

The reactions from local, state, and federal leaders underscore a profound sense of sorrow, coupled with a strong demand for legislative and enforcement measures. Mayor Ron Nirenberg of San Antonio poignantly addressed the "plight of immigrants looking for a better life and victims of human trafficking," while finding solace in the community's immediate compassionate response. San Antonio City Councilman Rey Saldaña voiced his sadness over the lost lives and the suffering from heat exhaustion and dehydration, describing it as a "tragic situation that often goes unnoticed."

Archbishop Gustavo Garcia-Siller of San Antonio conveyed deep sadness, despair, and anger, characterizing the deaths as "incomprehensible" and offering prayers for an end to this "monstrous form of modern slavery." U.S. Attorney Richard L. Durbin, Jr., for the Western District of Texas, highlighted the punishing South Texas heat and the profound suffering of those "helpless in the hands of their transporters." Former ICE Acting Director Thomas Homan emphasized that this crime serves as a "stark reminder of why human smuggling networks must be pursued, caught and punished" due to their "reckless disregard for those they smuggle."

Governor Greg Abbott of Texas deemed the loss of life "heartbreaking" and reaffirmed the state's unwavering commitment to eradicating human trafficking and vigorously prosecuting perpetrators. Lt. Governor Dan Patrick offered his thoughts and prayers, reiterating the critical need for a secure border and comprehensive legal immigration reform to ensure safe and dignified entry. U.S. Senator John Cornyn (R-Texas) asserted that robust border security is paramount to preventing such tragedies, stating that the "status quo is not compassionate."

U.S. Congressman Joaquin Castro (D-San Antonio) and U.S. Congressman Lloyd Doggett (D-Austin) both called for the smugglers to be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law, with Congressman Doggett describing the scene as "horrific" and urging prayers for survivors alongside immigration system reform. Texas State Senator José Menéndez (D-San Antonio) stressed that comprehensive immigration reform is essential to prevent such "senseless, disappointing, tragic and preventable" tragedies. Texas State Representatives Diego Bernal (D-San Antonio), Tomas Uresti (D-San Antonio), and Roland Gutierrez (D-San Antonio) shared sentiments of heartbreak, prayers for victims and survivors, and recognition of the immense risks taken by individuals seeking the "#AmericanDream." The organization RAICES mourned the loss of life, prayed for the recovery of survivors, and honored the lives of those who undertake such perilous journeys in search of refuge.

**San Antonio Human Trafficking Tragedy: A Deadly Journey's End**

In a devastating incident that underscored the perils of human trafficking, authorities in San Antonio discovered a tractor-trailer packed with individuals on Sunday, July 23, 2017. The discovery, made in the parking lot of a Walmart, revealed a scene described as "horrific," resulting in the deaths of ten people and leaving many others in critical condition. This event serves as a stark reminder of the dangerous journeys undertaken by migrants seeking a better life.

The grim discovery occurred around midnight at the Walmart located at IH 35 South and Palo Alto Road. Initially, eight individuals were found deceased within the sealed trailer. By Monday morning, the death toll had risen to ten, with several survivors transported to local hospitals in serious to critical condition, primarily suffering from the effects of extreme heat exposure.

### The Discovery and Initial Response

Law enforcement was alerted to the situation by a Walmart employee who was approached by someone from inside the truck requesting water. Upon arrival, San Antonio Police Chief William McManus and San Antonio Fire Chief Charles Hood encountered a scene of immense suffering. Responding paramedics and firefighters observed severe signs of heat stroke and dehydration. Chief Hood noted that patients exhibited heart rates exceeding 130 beats per minute and were dangerously hot to the touch.

### Escalating Death Toll and Victim Demographics

The initial discovery of eight deceased individuals was followed by further fatalities. A ninth person died Sunday afternoon, and the U.S. Attorney's Office confirmed the tenth victim on Monday morning. All confirmed victims were adult males, though officials noted that two of the youngest individuals discovered were 15 years old, highlighting the vulnerability of those involved.

### Survivors' Critical Condition

More than two dozen individuals were rescued from the trailer and rushed to area hospitals. University Hospital and SAMMC received 17 individuals with life-threatening injuries, including five men and one woman in critical condition suffering from severe heat exhaustion. An additional thirteen victims were transported to five different hospitals with non-life-threatening injuries, but still requiring urgent medical attention.

### The Driver and the Smuggling Operation

The driver of the tractor-trailer, identified as 60-year-old James M. Bradley Jr. of Florida, was arrested and subsequently booked into a federal jail in San Antonio. U.S. Attorney Richard Durbin characterized the event as "an alien smuggling venture gone horribly wrong." The tractor-trailer, bearing the logo of Pyle Transportation, had reportedly been parked at the back of the Walmart lot since Saturday evening. Pyle Transportation representatives stated that Bradley operated the vehicle as an owner-operator and was not authorized to be hauling in Texas at the time of the incident, expressing shock and dismay.

### Cause of Death and Environmental Factors

According to a San Antonio Police Department news release, the immigrants are believed to have died from heat exposure and asphyxiation. The Bexar County Medical Examiner's Office was tasked with officially determining the precise cause of death. While the trailer was reportedly refrigerated, its air conditioning system was not functioning. Experts from the National Weather Service estimate that temperatures inside a closed vehicle can rapidly soar to dangerous levels. With an outside temperature of 80 degrees Fahrenheit, the interior of a closed vehicle can reach 115 degrees within just 30 minutes. San Antonio experienced a high of 100 degrees Fahrenheit on Saturday, the day the trailer was parked.

### A Pattern of Trafficking Tragedies

This incident is not an isolated event and tragically echoes previous smuggling tragedies. Border Patrol agents in Laredo have reported a concerning uptick in tractor-trailer smuggling attempts, including several significant incidents in the weeks and months leading up to the San Antonio discovery:

*   **June 19:** 44 individuals from Mexico and Guatemala were found after an 18-wheeler was stopped near an international bridge.
*   **July 7:** 72 individuals from Mexico, Ecuador, Guatemala, and El Salvador were discovered inside a locked trailer.
*   **July 8:** 33 individuals from Mexico and Guatemala were found in a trailer stopped at a Border Patrol checkpoint.
*   **The week prior:** 16 individuals were found inside a locked trailer at a Border Patrol checkpoint.

Laredo Sector Assistant Chief Patrol Agent Gabriel Acosta condemned these acts, stating, "These criminal organizations view these individuals as mere commodities without regard for their safety."

A particularly significant smuggling tragedy occurred in Victoria, Texas, in 2003, where 19 people died after being abandoned in a trailer. Thomas Homan, then acting director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, emphasized the agency's commitment to dismantling smuggling networks. Homan, who was personally involved in the investigation of the 2003 Victoria case, described it as a stark reminder of the smugglers' "total indifference to the safety of those smuggled and to the law." The San Antonio incident tragically reinforces this grim reality.

San Antonio, TX – Federal prosecutors have charged James Matthew Bradley Jr., 60, in connection with the deaths of 10 individuals found in a sweltering tractor-trailer packed with immigrants. The driver faces potential capital punishment for his alleged role in this human smuggling operation.

The grim discovery was made on Sunday, July 23, 2017, when San Antonio police officers responded to a scene outside a Walmart store. Inside the tractor-trailer, authorities found eight deceased individuals, with two more succumbing to their injuries at the hospital. Nearly 20 other survivors were rescued in critical condition, suffering from severe dehydration and heatstroke, raising fears that the death toll could climb.

According to the federal complaint, prosecutors described the trailer as a dark, suffocating space, estimated by some to hold around 90 people. One passenger recounted that individuals took turns breathing through a small opening and pounded on the walls to alert the driver. Bradley, a resident of Clearwater, Florida, appeared in federal court on charges of illegally transporting immigrants for financial gain, resulting in death. He was ordered to be held pending a further hearing.

Bradley reportedly told investigators that he was transporting the trailer, which he claimed had been sold, from Iowa to Brownsville, Texas, for his employer. He stated he was unaware of the passengers until he parked the vehicle to urinate. Upon hearing banging and shaking, he opened the door and was allegedly "surprised when he was run over by 'Spanish' people and knocked to the ground," the complaint details. Bradley also allegedly admitted to knowing the trailer's refrigeration system was non-functional and that the four ventilation holes were likely clogged. Crucially, he did not call 911, even after realizing some individuals had already died.

The tractor-trailer, bearing an Iowa license plate, was registered to Pyle Transportation Inc. of Schaller, Iowa. Company President Brian Pyle stated that he had sold the truck in May to an individual in Mexico and that Bradley was an independent contractor tasked with delivering it to a pickup point in Brownsville. Pyle expressed his shock and sorrow, stating he was unaware of any issues with the truck until contacted by media following the incident.

A passenger informed investigators that they had crossed the Mexican border into the U.S. by raft and were then guided into the trailer for transport to San Antonio, a journey of approximately 150 miles from the border. This passenger also reported that no food or water was provided to those inside the truck, and that the immigrants soon began struggling to breathe and passing out. Diplomats from Mexico and Guatemala confirmed that some of the victims were from their respective countries.

Latin American migrants often utilize smuggling networks to navigate the U.S. border, secure safe houses, and arrange transportation to their final destinations.

Thomas Homan, acting director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, commented on the case, stating, "Even though they have the driver in custody, I can guarantee you there's going to be many more people we're looking for to prosecute."

The temperature in San Antonio on Saturday, July 22, 2017, reached 101 degrees Fahrenheit (38 Celsius) and remained above 90 degrees Fahrenheit (32 C) until after 10 p.m. The tragedy came to light when a person from the truck approached a Walmart employee seeking water late Saturday night or early Sunday morning. The employee provided water and then alerted the police, according to Police Chief McManus.

This incident is the latest in a series of fatal smuggling-by-truck operations. In one of the most severe cases on record in the U.S., 19 immigrants died in Victoria, Texas, in 2003 after being locked inside a stifling rig. Immigrant rights advocates, such as Bob Libal, have attributed such tragedies to the dangerous and expensive nature of migration, which can push individuals into the hands of illicit actors due to stringent federal and Texas immigration policies.

In the May 2003 case, the driver was sentenced to nearly 34 years in prison after prosecutors stated he ignored the pleas and screams of the immigrants. The Border Patrol has reported multiple truck seizures in and around Laredo, Texas, this month. For instance, on July 7, agents discovered 72 individuals crammed into a truck with no escape route. These individuals were from Mexico, Ecuador, Guatemala, and El Salvador. Mexican authorities have also reported similar discoveries.

This report was compiled with contributions from Associated Press writers Mike Graczyk in Houston, Elliot Spagat in San Diego, Peter Orsi in Mexico City, and Frank Bajak in San Antonio.
