query: What reasons did Donald Trump provide for not participating in the Republican presidential debate on Fox News?

documents.text: Citing unfair treatment by Fox News, Donald Trump will not participate in the Republican presidential debate on Thursday, his campaign manager told reporters on Tuesday. Corey Lewandowski said the campaign is moving ahead with plans to host a rally in Iowa for the non-profit group Wounded Warriors during the debate, which is scheduled to begin at 9 p.m. Eastern time. “He will not be participating in the Fox News debate on Thursday night,” Lewandowski said. His comments came just minutes after Trump told reporters during a press conference before a rally in Marshalltown, Iowa, that he “probably won't bother” participating in the debate. Trump had attacked Fox News host Megyn Kelly for treating him “unfairly” at the first debate of the election cycle, prompting the network to issue a response earlier Tuesday. “We learned from a secret back channel that the Ayatollah and Putin both intend to treat Donald Trump unfairly when they meet with him if he becomes president,” the cable network said in a public statement. Trump slammed the statement, saying “it was written by a child.” News anchor Megyn Kelly speaks on Oct. 13, 2015, in Washington. Photographer: Paul Morigi/Getty Images for Fortune/Time Inc. On her Fox News program Tuesday night, Kelly said she will still co-moderate the debate. “The truth is he doesn't get to control the media,” Kelly said. “I’ll be there. ... The debate will go on with or without Trump.” The network said in an e-mailed statement that “capitulating to politicians’ ultimatums about a debate moderator violates all journalistic standards, as do threats.” Fox said it had “warned” Lewandowski “not to level any more threats” against Kelly, as when he allegedly told the network that “Megyn had a ‘rough couple of days after that last debate’ and he ‘would hate to have her go through that again.’” “‘We can’t give in to terrorizations toward any of our employees,” the network said. Trump told reporters the campaign will “raise some money for the Wounded Warriors.” “We’ll raise money for the vets,” Trump said. “I was all set to do the debate. I came here to do the debate. When they sent out the wise-guy press releases a little while ago, done by some PR person, along with Roger Ailes, I said: ‘Bye, bye.’” Donald Trump Says He’ll Skip Fox Debate Trump's campaign also released a statement saying that “Mr. Trump knows a bad deal when he sees one.” “FOX News is making tens of millions of dollars on debates, and setting ratings records (the highest in history), where as in previous years they were low-rated afterthoughts,” the statement said. “Unlike the very stupid, highly incompetent people running our country into the ground, Mr. Trump knows when to walk away.” Senator Ted Cruz wasted no time in attacking Trump for skipping the debate, telling radio host Mark Levin that Trump was “afraid” to face questions. “This is a job interview,” Cruz told Levin. “You have to be able to answer the questions of the American people.” Cruz also challenged Trump to a one-on-one debate. Retired neurosurgeon Ben Carson said he was skeptical that Trump was for real. “I'll believe it when I see it,” Carson told Levin. New Jersey Governor Chris Christie told Kelly on Fox News that Trump's absence at the debate will “give us more time to talk.” “That's OK with me, but the American people should wonder: If you're not willing to show up when everything isn't going your way? That's not the way your job as governor goes,” Christie said. “I'll be there.” Jeb Bush, the former governor of Florida, answered “Exactly” when retweeting conservative host Erick Erickson. “So Donald Trump can't handle tough questions from Megyn Kelly, but he'll be able to handle Hillary Clinton. Hahahahahahahahahahahahahahahaha,” Erickson wrote. Polls have Trump leading in Iowa ahead of the Feb. 1 caucuses. Cruz, of Texas, is in second place. Thursday's debate is Republicans' final one ahead of the caucuses. Trump has flirted in the past with skipping debates and also advocated that networks donate a portion of the money they earn in advertising to Wounded Warriors, which helps military members who have been injured in combat. Trump's involvement in the debates has led to record ratings, including the Fox News debate moderated by Kelly that drew more than 24 million viewers. On stage at Tuesday's campaign rally, Trump only alluded to the news his campaign had just broken moments before. “I have no objection to Fox News,” Trump said. “I made a little bit of an announcement before, but it's not that big of a deal. ... After a year you're going to say, ‘Wow. What a difference.’ You're going to be so proud of your country again. And we're going to make America great again.” Trump isn't the first top-tier presidential candidate to skip a debate. Ronald Reagan did not attend a Republican debate ahead of the 1980 Iowa caucuses, which he lost to George H.W. Bush. Reagan went on to the win the nomination and the presidency. —With assistance from Sahil Kapur and Chelsea Mes.

Donald Trump's decision to skip the Fox News debate in the 2016 presidential race stemmed directly from his ongoing conflict with co-moderator Megyn Kelly. This wasn't an impulsive move but the result of escalating disagreements, notably their initial confrontation during a debate in August 2015.

Trump's campaign released a statement detailing his absence from the scheduled Thursday debate. This communication clarified Trump's reasoning, portraying the situation as an unacceptable arrangement. Leveraging his experience as a businessman and author of "The Art of the Deal," Trump highlighted his knack for identifying and refusing unfavorable terms. He pointed out that Fox News had profited significantly and achieved record viewership in prior debates, contrasting this with their usual audience size. The campaign stressed Trump's firm stance, asserting he would not be manipulated by Roger Ailes and Fox News, whom he accused of intentionally provoking him.

The campaign further explained Trump's view of his debate performance and overall strategy. Trump believed he had won all six previous debates, citing online polls from sources like the Drudge Report, Slate, and Time Magazine as validation. While he saw himself more as a job creator than a conventional debater, Trump enjoyed the debate format and its positive impact on his poll numbers and public image. As an alternative to the Fox News debate, Trump announced he would host a fundraising event in Iowa for Veterans and Wounded Warriors, framing it as a response to the perceived inaction of current political leaders.

This strategic choice, which garnered significant attention from political analysts, was presented as a demonstration of the "guts" and decisive leadership essential for his "Make America Great Again" agenda. Commentators from outlets like The New York Times and The Washington Post debated its potential impact on voter perception and the media landscape. Experts, including political strategist David Axelrod, offered their insights on CNN, analyzing the deliberate nature of Trump's decision.

Donald Trump's decision to skip the final presidential debate before Iowa's caucuses, choosing instead a public dispute with Fox News, has effectively solidified his campaign's dominance in the crucial leadoff state. This move, while unconventional for most candidates, highlights Trump's distinctive approach to presidential politics. His campaign stated, "This takes guts, and is the kind mentality our country needs in order to Make America Great Again," confirming his plan to host a separate event to raise funds for wounded veterans.

This strategic maneuver places the other seven Republican contenders in a difficult position. They must decide whether to confront the absentee front-runner, potentially amplifying his message and drawing more attention, or to ignore him, leaving voters to question their responses to the national front-runner's bold action just days before the February 1st caucus. New Jersey Governor Chris Christie, a rival for the GOP nomination, commented on Fox News, "I think, you know, the media's been handled by him. And I think, you know, folks have allowed him to do things that no other candidate's ever been allowed to do in American presidential history."

Trump's presidential bid has consistently been characterized by such attention-grabbing episodes. Past actions, including questioning Senator John McCain's war hero status, criticizing Fox News anchor Megyn Kelly, and proposing a ban on Muslims entering the United States, have each been met with predictions of his political demise. Nevertheless, Trump has consistently maintained his lead in preference polls, reflecting his adeptness at connecting with a Republican electorate skeptical of the party establishment and mainstream news organizations. Yvonne Galusha, a 57-year-old Trump supporter and University of Iowa employee, articulated her perspective at a rally in Iowa City, stating, "I think in some ways, we've got to start pushing back. And I think he's just demonstrating that, pushing against the liberal media."

The dispute with Fox News escalated after Trump requested Kelly's removal as a debate moderator. The network's pointed response, suggesting Iran and Russia would treat Trump unfairly if he became president, prompted Trump's retort: "Let them have their debate and let's see how they do with the ratings." Rivals attempted to frame his withdrawal as an indicator of unsuitability for the White House. Christie told Kelly, "If he doesn't want to be there, that's okay with me. But I'll tell you this: the American people should wonder if you're not willing to show up when everything isn't going your way and exactly the way you want to."

This line of argument has yet to significantly alter Trump's standing. His supporters interpret his actions as a manifestation of his core appeal: he operates according to his own rules, even when it means challenging party leadership and favored media outlets. Trump himself remarked, "With me, they're dealing with somebody that's a little bit different. They can't toy with me like they toy with everybody else." Republican officials, while striving to conceal their disappointment and apprehension regarding Trump's influence, acknowledged his prerogative to withdraw. Steve Duprey, who oversees the Republican National Committee's debate initiatives, commented, "Every candidate has the right to decide not to participate in a sanctioned debate. Time will tell whether this works to his benefit or hurts his campaign." Trump's campaign manager, Corey Lewandowski, asserted on MSNBC, "Mr. Trump doesn't play games. ... He walks away from bad deals. At the end of the day, Mr. Trump is going to have the last laugh." Fox News, in its official statement, conveyed uncertainty about how Iowan voters would perceive his actions. "We're not sure how Iowans are going to feel about him walking away from them at the last minute," the network stated. Marc McGee, a graphic artist from Cedar Rapids, believed Trump erred in his decision, saying, "You should show up and go through the motions, you know. If I was mad at work, I don't just stay home, you know." However, McGee also conceded that the move was likely a calculated attempt to garner attention, describing it as "very ingenious."

*Associated Press writer Jill Colvin contributed to this report from Iowa City, Iowa.*
*Steve Peoples is a national political reporter for The Associated Press, covering the Republican candidates for president. Follow him on Twitter at: http://twitter.com/sppeoples*

Donald Trump's recent social media pronouncements, specifically his contemplation of skipping the upcoming Fox News GOP debate due to perceived unfairness from co-moderator Megyn Kelly, have elicited a pointed and somewhat humorous response from the network. Trump took to Twitter and Instagram on Tuesday to solicit his followers' opinions on his potential participation, a move that prompted Fox News to offer a playful retort.

In a statement provided to Mediaite, Fox News humorously suggested that intelligence from a "clandestine source" indicated that both Ayatollah Khamenei and Vladimir Putin intend to treat Donald Trump unfairly should he ascend to the presidency, drawing a parallel to Trump's stated concerns about Megyn Kelly. Furthermore, a "shadowy informant" had apparently revealed Trump's own covert strategy: to gauge his attendance at future meetings by substituting his Cabinet with his Twitter followers. This lighthearted response followed an earlier, more direct statement from a Fox News spokesperson on Monday, who expressed surprise at Trump's apprehension regarding questioning from Kelly, emphasizing that as a presidential candidate, he would eventually need to accept that he cannot control which journalists cover him.

**Key Developments:**

*   **UPDATE — 4:57 p.m. ET:** In direct response to Mediaite's inquiry regarding potential alterations to the GOP debate lineup, Fox News CEO Roger Ailes issued a definitive statement via email, unequivocally affirming, "Megyn Kelly is an excellent journalist and the entire network stands behind her — she will absolutely be on the debate stage on Thursday night."
*   **UPDATE — 5:48 p.m. ET:** Donald Trump reacted to Fox News's statement via Twitter late Tuesday afternoon, characterizing it as a "pathetic attempt" to artificially inflate debate ratings, tweeting, "Pathetic attempt by @foxnews to try and build up ratings for the #GOPDebate. Without me they'd have no ratings!"

Donald Trump's campaign ignited a political firestorm Tuesday evening by announcing his decision to boycott Fox News's final debate before the Iowa caucuses on February 1. The billionaire candidate cited his belief that moderator Megyn Kelly would exhibit "really biased" treatment, expressing a lack of confidence in Fox News's ability to provide a fair platform. Instead of participating, Trump declared he would "host an event in Iowa to raise money for the Veterans and Wounded Warriors." This move prompts critical questions: Is this another calculated masterstroke from a candidate seemingly defying political gravity? Or is it an act of profound arrogance destined to be the undoing of his campaign? Alternatively, could this be a strategic negotiation tactic, culminating in a last-minute agreement to debate? This analysis explores these three distinct possibilities.

### Scenario 1: A Calculated Masterpiece

Donald Trump currently holds the frontrunner position, with his national and Iowa polling trends demonstrating consistent upward momentum. From his perspective, the potential gains from another debate appear minimal, while the risks are substantial. Trump likely perceives Megyn Kelly as having a personal vendetta, perhaps fueled by his own provocative behavior towards her, and fears she might engineer a moment designed to humiliate him and derail his campaign just days before the Iowa caucuses.

By abstaining, Trump is poised to dominate media coverage surrounding the event, generating a significant media spectacle that overshadows his rivals in the crucial days leading up to Iowa. Furthermore, his alternative event, a fundraiser for wounded veterans, offers a positive public image. The actual debate, diminished by his absence, may appear lackluster and inconsequential. This decision also sends a clear message to future debate hosts: Trump is prepared to withdraw if his demands are not met. The most advantageous outcome for Trump would be Fox News capitulating. This could involve Megyn Kelly stepping aside, perhaps with another moderator taking her place for the network's benefit. In such a scenario, Trump would emerge as a brilliant negotiator, having compelled a powerful media institution to bend to his will.

### Scenario 2: A Monumental Miscalculation

By conventional political standards, Trump's decision appears remarkably ill-advised. With the Iowa caucuses mere days away, many voters finalize their decisions late in the process. A significant portion of conservative voters rely on Fox News for their political information and would likely tune into a debate hosted by the network, a preeminent conservative media outlet. Trump is forfeiting a prime opportunity to sway these undecided voters, who could be influenced by a candidate who actively participates and performs well in the debate.

Moreover, refusing to debate can be perceived negatively by voters. The established norm for major presidential candidates is to participate in debates, a practice even sitting presidents generally adhere to. This refusal could project an image of weakness and apprehension. While Trump can legitimately boast about his poll numbers, he has yet to secure any actual electoral victories. The debate itself, despite Trump's absence, is unlikely to suffer a drastic drop in viewership. Given its proximity to the Iowa caucuses and the unsettled nature of the Republican race, voters will still be keen to observe the proceedings. Crucially, by not being present, Trump grants his opponents a free platform to criticize him repeatedly without the possibility of immediate rebuttal. The question arises: why not simply attend and articulate his platform? He has navigated previous debates effectively.

### Scenario 3: A Strategic Negotiation Tactic

As articulated by former Obama administration official Dan Pfeiffer, a potential outcome involves Trump holding out until the eleventh hour, securing assurances of fair treatment from Fox News executives like Roger Ailes, and then making a dramatic entrance.

During a rally in Clear Lake, Iowa, earlier this month, Trump critiqued the Obama administration's handling of the Iran nuclear deal negotiations, arguing that they failed to leverage the situation to secure the release of American prisoners held by Iran. He outlined his preferred approach: "You say, 'We want our prisoners back, gotta have 'em back before we start negotiations.' They’ll say no. And what we say is, 'Bye bye, we gotta go, enjoy yourself, we’re leaving, bye!' and wave goodbye, get up, leave." Trump suggested the administration should have walked away from the negotiation multiple times. He further proposed escalating sanctions, predicting that Iran would quickly agree to release the prisoners to resume negotiations.

While the subsequent release of American prisoners days later casts some doubt on Trump's assertion of the Obama administration's incompetence, this anecdote highlights Trump's propensity for using walk-away tactics as a negotiation strategy. Is this the case here? According to Gabriel Sherman, a highly regarded reporter covering Fox News for *New York* magazine, Trump's team has indicated that negotiations with Fox News are ongoing, and that Trump is open to discussions with Rupert Murdoch.

Sherman reported via Twitter:
*   "Trump source says Trump isn't taking Ailes calls. Hannity just tried calling trump as an emissary. Trump only wants to deal with Murdoch now."
*   "Per Trump source: Trump's position now is he is chairman of his company and he will only deal with Murdoch at a chairman-to-chairman level."

The specifics of a potential resolution remain unclear. Trump's primary concern is his perception of Megyn Kelly's bias. However, it would represent a significant concession for Fox News to remove Kelly as moderator or restrict her questioning, potentially leading to embarrassment for the network. Nevertheless, the debate represents a substantial financial opportunity for Fox News, and the network would undoubtedly prefer Trump's participation. Conversely, the risk for Trump in skipping the final debate before Iowa is considerable. Therefore, an accommodation may well be reached. The situation warrants continued observation.

**VIDEO:** Understanding the Iowa Caucus Process

*Subscribe to Vox on YouTube for more insightful explainer videos.*

Photo Donald J. Trump’s campaign manager used slightly less absolute words on Wednesday about Mr. Trump’s plans to skip Thursday night’s Republican presidential debate, saying he didn’t “think” there was any way the candidate would change his mind about attending the event on Fox News. Corey Lewandowski, the campaign manager, spoke on MSNBC’s “Morning Joe” program. One of the hosts, Mika Brzezinski, pressed him on whether Mr. Trump might change his mind and join the debate in Iowa, the final one before the state’s caucuses on Monday. “Any chance that he will do this debate in any way, shape or form?” she asked. “Any way to turn this around?” “I just don’t think so,” Mr. Lewandowski said. “What you have with Mr. Trump, that you have a clear leader. A person who he says at best is not going to be toyed with. A person who understands when a bad deal is in front of him and is ready to walk away from a bad deal. Something that this country should be able to do.” He added, referring to the debate’s possible ratings: “And I just don’t see in any way where Fox is going to have a successful debate now, when you got the clear G.O.P. front-runner who has decided to walk away from this debate because he can’t be treated fairly at the debate stage. Maybe Fox was going to have a 20- or 25-million person debate. But without Mr. Trump’s participation, I think they may have a one- or two-million person debate.” A day earlier, Mr. Lewandowski said definitively that Mr. Trump would not be there. Mr. Trump and the network engaged in a high-stakes game of chicken, largely over a feud about Megyn Kelly moderating the debate. On Monday afternoon, the network released a statement mocking Mr. Trump in stark terms. That’s what the candidate said he was reacting to on Tuesday night.

As many of our viewers know, FOX News is hosting a sanctioned debate in Des Moines, Iowa on Thursday night, three days before the first votes of the 2016 election are cast in the Iowa Caucus. Donald Trump is refusing to debate seven of his fellow presidential candidates on stage that night, which is near unprecedented. We’re not sure how Iowans are going to feel about him walking away from them at the last minute, but it should be clear to the American public by now that this is rooted in one thing – Megyn Kelly, whom he has viciously attacked since August and has now spent four days demanding be removed from the debate stage. Capitulating to politicians’ ultimatums about a debate moderator violates all journalistic standards, as do threats, including the one leveled by Trump’s campaign manager Corey Lewandowski toward Megyn Kelly. In a call on Saturday with a FOX News executive, Lewandowski stated that Megyn had a ‘rough couple of days after that last debate’ and he ‘would hate to have her go through that again.’ Lewandowski was warned not to level any more threats, but he continued to do so. We can’t give in to terrorizations toward any of our employees. Trump is still welcome at Thursday night’s debate and will be treated fairly, just as he has been during his 132 appearances on FOX News & FOX Business, but he can’t dictate the moderators or the questions.

query: What led to Liu Xiaobo's extended imprisonment and eventual diagnosis with liver cancer?

documents.text:
The passing of Liu Xiaobo, a Nobel Peace Prize laureate and a leading voice for democracy, freedom, and human rights in China, has sent ripples of grief across the globe. Liu, who spent over a decade imprisoned for his activism, died on Thursday at the age of 61, succumbing to liver cancer.

Authorities in Shenyang, Liaoning province, confirmed his death, citing multiple organ failure. Doctors present at the hospital shared that Liu passed away at 5:35 PM, surrounded by his wife, Liu Xia, and his brothers, Liu Xiaoguang and Liu Xiaoxuan. His final words to his wife were a poignant, "Live on well." The medical team described his cancer as a rare and aggressive form that progressed rapidly within a week.

News of Liu's death prompted an outpouring of sorrow and tributes from friends and international supporters, including the Norwegian Nobel Committee. Governments in the United States, Germany, and France issued statements urging Beijing to lift travel restrictions on his wife and family.

Shenyang authorities announced Liu's death shortly after 9 PM, with the state news agency Xinhua reporting it later that evening. Xinhua noted that prominent Chinese doctors and international experts from the United States and Germany had been involved in his treatment. In Hong Kong, over 100 individuals gathered outside Beijing's liaison office, transforming a protest for Liu's release into a solemn memorial vigil. Law Yuk-kai, director of the Hong Kong Human Rights Monitor, remarked, "Liu’s case shows that Beijing will hold its grip very tight despite international pressure. And we can foresee that Hong Kong’s road to democracy will get more difficult." Despite appeals from his family, friends, and the international community, Liu died before he could fulfill his wish to receive treatment in Germany with his wife. Liu's death marks him as the second Nobel laureate to die in custody, following the German pacifist Carl von Ossietzky, who died in a Nazi-controlled hospital in 1938.

Liu was arrested in December 2008 for his involvement in drafting "Charter 08," a manifesto calling for political reform in mainland China. He was subsequently sentenced to 11 years in prison in 2009 and awarded the Nobel Peace Prize a year later. The Shenyang Justice Bureau stated that Liu was diagnosed with late-stage liver cancer during a routine check-up in late May, though news of his illness did not become public until the end of June. Beijing consistently maintained that Liu was receiving the best possible care, dismissing international appeals for him to seek treatment abroad.

According to two friends in direct contact with Liu's family, the activist fell into a coma early on Thursday but briefly regained consciousness before lapsing back into a coma around 11 AM. Exiled Tiananmen student leader Wang Dan described Liu as "a great man who left us, a light extinguished." Wang declared, "This is the second June 4. This is a naked political murder," referencing the 1989 crackdown. He expressed hope that the world would "forever remember Xiaobo’s contribution to China’s progress" and pledged that he and his friends would "bear the heavy burden left by Liu Xiaobo to continue the lifelong struggle for China’s democratisation."

A joint statement released by ten of Liu's close friends on Thursday night hailed him as "one of the most important voices of the contemporary freedom and human rights movement in China" and a symbol of "the Chinese people’s relentless struggle for freedom." Bao Tong, a former top aide to the late Zhao Ziyang, stated on Twitter that Liu defined patriotism, adding, "The state may have convicted him of subversion, but it must be pointed out that he was a great patriot."

Berit Reiss-Andersen, head of the Norwegian Nobel Committee, held the Chinese government "deeply responsible" for the laureate's "premature death," expressing concern that Liu was not transferred for adequate medical treatment before becoming terminally ill. United Nations Secretary General Antonio Guterres conveyed his deep sadness. German Chancellor Angela Merkel mourned Liu as a "courageous fighter for human rights and freedom of expression." Berlin and Washington reiterated their calls for Chinese authorities to permit Liu's relatives to leave the country.

German Foreign Minister Sigmar Gabriel questioned whether China could "quickly, transparently and plausibly answer the question of whether [Liu Xiaobo’s] cancer could not have been identified much earlier." U.S. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson joined global mourners, calling for the Chinese government to release Liu Xia from house arrest and allow her to depart China. Wang Dan and fellow dissident Hu Jia echoed these calls, emphasizing the need to continue advocating for Liu Xia's freedom and the unfulfilled aspirations of "Charter 08" to "sustain the spirit of Liu Xiaobo."

BEIJING — In his final days, as China's most prominent political dissident, Liu Xiaobo, succumbed to liver cancer while under strict police surveillance, he dedicated himself to completing what was likely his last literary endeavor. This poignant work was not a political manifesto, but rather a deeply personal and often enigmatic tribute to his wife, Liu Xia. An artist and poet in her own right, she endured prolonged house arrest during his 11-year imprisonment.

Among the handwritten fragments penned in a Shenyang hospital, Liu Xiaobo offered a glimpse into his profound affection: "Love as intense as ice, love as remote as blackness." He also reflected on his own expressions, writing, "My praise is perhaps an unforgivable poison." These brief, sometimes fragmented, notes were intended for the preface of an as-yet-unpublished collection of his wife's photographs, tentatively titled "Accompanying Liu Xiaobo."

The existence of these notes and the photo collection was revealed by a Chinese editor, a close confidant of the couple who assisted in the book's compilation. The editor disclosed that Liu Xiaobo initiated contact late last month, and subsequently, individuals close to him shared images of his hospital writings. The editor, who requested anonymity due to concerns about potential repercussions, facilitated the sharing of this deeply personal testament.

While this final tribute offers a tender portrait, Liu Xiaobo will undoubtedly remain most recognized for his unwavering commitment to dissent. His legacy is cemented by his 2010 Nobel Peace Prize, awarded while he was incarcerated. His 2009 sentencing followed his involvement in a petition advocating for democratic reforms, an act that ultimately led to his arrest.

Key aspects of Liu Xiaobo's final work and life include:

*   A deeply personal tribute to his wife, Liu Xia.
*   Handwritten notes reflecting on love and his own expressions.
*   Intended preface for a collection of his wife's photographs.
*   His enduring legacy as a Nobel Peace Prize-winning dissident.

China’s most famous dissident, Liu Xiaobo, has died in custody at age 61. The cause was liver cancer. His death, confirmed Thursday evening local time on July 13 by Chinese justice authorities (link in Chinese) in the northeastern city of Shenyang, comes more than two weeks after prison authorities announced he was being moved to a hospital for treatment, news that was followed by pleas from around the world to allow him to travel abroad for care. Thursday’s brief statement didn’t mention the Nobel Prize awarded to Liu for a life dedicated to the memory of the protesters killed in Beijing’s Tiananmen Square in 1989. In April 1989, Liu, a literary critic and comparative literature professor, was in New York City as a visiting scholar at Columbia University when he heard of the protests in Tiananmen Square. He hurried back to Beijing and spent weeks among the student protesters. Two days after the Communist Party’s deadly crackdown on June 4, Liu was detained and jailed. When he was freed in 1991, he resumed his public demands for greater freedoms in China over the next two decades. In 2008, along with other dissidents, he drafted Charter 08, a document that called on the Chinese state to fundamentally change its character and abandon one-party rule. Soon after he was tried for subversion. According to freedom-of-speech advocacy group PEN America, he was convicted in 2009 for writing seven sentences, a total of 224 Chinese characters. He was given an 11-year sentence. On June 26, 2017, after serving more than seven years of his sentence, the prison holding Liu announced that he was being transferred to a nearby hospital to be treated for late-stage liver cancer—the same disease that killed his father. Here is a timeline of the life of a man who has been deeply admired, who is being deeply mourned, and whose last days have become for many a symbol for many of the darkness that clouds the story of China’s economic rise. 1955 Liu Xiaobo is born on Dec. 28 in northeastern China’s Jilin province, the third of five brothers. 1966 The Cultural Revolution begins. Millions of urban youth head to the countryside to be “re-educated” by peasants, following leader Mao Zedong’s exhortations to “go up into the mountains and down to the countryside (上山下乡运动)” (paywall). Liu’s grandmother, whom he is very close to, is designated a “landlord” and sent away. Meanwhile, his father, a Communist Party member, is busy, and his older brothers go away to become Red Guards. Liu remembers this time of turmoil as one of unexpected freedom according to his biography, Steel Gate to Freedom: “My parents were off ‘revolutionizing,’ schools stopped, and I was for a time able to be rid of the constraints of an ‘education.'” 1977 Liu is admitted to Jilin University and studies Chinese literature. During his university life, he organizes The Innocent Hearts (赤子心), a poetry group with his friends. 1982 Liu starts a two-year master’s program in Chinese literature at Beijing Normal University. After receiving his master’s, Liu stays at Beijing Normal University to teach and marries Tao Li, with whom he has a son, Liu Tao. 1987 Liu begins his doctoral studies and publishes literary criticism in dozens of magazines. In 1987 he publishes his first book, Criticism of the Choice: Dialogues with Li Zehou, which critiques a prominent intellectual. It becomes a bestseller. 1988 Liu works abroad at universities in Oslo and Hawaii and at Columbia University as a visiting scholar. 1989 While Liu is still in America, Chinese students break into massive pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square. He flies back to stand staunchly on the side of students. Liu, as well as others, including Zhou Duo and Hou Dejian (a Taiwan-born singer), play pivotal roles, including organizing a hunger strike. Thanks to Liu’s efforts to persuade students to leave the square, many of them escape death. However, because of his leadership role in the protest, Liu is detained, fired from his job as a teacher, and his books are banned. He later divorces his wife Tao in order to protect his family, according to the Guardian newspaper. His son, then about six years old, goes to live with his grandparents; Liu will see little of him for the rest of his life, according to his biography. 1992 Liu doesn’t stop writing. His book The Monologues of a Doomsday’s Survivor, about his memories and evaluation of the Tiananmen Square protest, is published in Taiwan. In the book, he points out what he sees as the misjudgments of the movement. He says that it delayed China’s democratization, and that students and intellectuals had become “conceited—just as if we had reverted to the time of the Cultural Revolution.” Will Burgess/Reuters Pro-democracy activist Liu Xiaobo makes a point during a conversation in March 1995. 1996 Liu is sentenced to three years behind bars for a letter criticizing party rule. While still in the labor camp, he marries his current wife, fellow poet Liu Xia. According to Ian Johnson, writing for the New York Review of Books: They had met in the 1980s but became close only after Tiananmen. In 1995, Liu Xiaobo was detained for writing an open letter calling for basic rights and then formally sentenced in 1996 to three years in prison. Liu Xia traveled regularly up to the labor camp and—in a phrase that is now legendary—told the guards that “I want to marry that enemy of the state!” Eventually she did, while he was still in prison; their wedding banquet was in the prison cafeteria. David Gray/Reuters Liu Xia, wife of Liu Xiaobo, was placed under house arrest in 2010. 1999 Liu Xiaobo is released in October. 2001 Liu Xiaobo helps establish the Independent Chinese PEN Center, a “non-political, non-profit organization of writers that fights for the protection of freedom of expression and publication,” and serves as its president from 2003 to 2007. During this time he writes the essay “The internet is God’s present to China,” about his belief that the internet is making China freer (republished later in the Times of London). 2008 Liu Xiaobo co-authors Charter 08 with other dissidents, a political manifesto. The document appeals for a fundamental political transformation including a new constitution, separation of powers, and guarantee of human rights. In December 2008, he is detained. 2009 He is put on trial. On Dec. 25, he is convicted of “inciting subversion of state power.” According to PEN America, his conviction is largely for writing seven sentences, including this one: “One-party monopolization of ruling privileges should be abolished.” 2010 In October, Liu wins the Nobel Peace Prize while in custody, “for his long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China.” His wife Liu Xia is placed under house arrest in Beijing the same day. At the December ceremony (video), the medal and diploma are placed on an empty chair since Liu is still in prison. Norwegian actress Liv Ullmann reads the statement Liu had written prior to his sentencing: “I have no enemies and no hatred.” Heiko Junge/Scanpix/Reuters The empty chair where 2010 Nobel Peace Prize winner, jailed Chinese dissident Liu Xiaobo, would have sat during the ceremony at Oslo City Hall on Dec. 10, 2010. 2011 Liu’s father dies of liver cancer; he is briefly paroled to attend the funeral, according to Steel Gate to Freedom. 2017 On June 26, the Liaoning Prison Administrative Bureau prison makes the announcement that Liu is being transferred from prison in Jinzhou to a nearby hospital in the final stages of liver cancer. His lawyer confirms the news, setting off an outpouring of grief and anger among people who know and respect him globally. As Liu’s condition deteriorates, concerns over his treatment grow. The couple seeks to go abroad for treatment, news reports say, but his doctors in China say he is too ill to travel. China invites German and US doctors to visit him. Liu Xia is with her husband in hospital in his final days.

Former leader of the secretive sect Colonia Dignidad, Paul Schaefer (C), sits in a wheelchair outside the Interpol police station after questioning in Santiago, March 14, 2005. REUTERS/Eliseo Fernandez/Files SANTIAGO (Reuters) - Germany and Chile have agreed to create a commission to document crimes committed at a secretive sect founded by an ex-Nazi in the foothills of the Andes, the Chilean foreign relations ministry said on Thursday. The agreement, signed late Wednesday, will create a documentation center for the community, as well as a memorial for victims that were tortured and killed within its confines. Colonia Dignidad was founded in 1961 by former World War II German army medic Paul Schaefer. The community, which embraced ultra-traditional values, operated as an isolated cult under Schaefer, who sexually abused and tortured dozens of youths. During the 1973 to 1990 dictatorship of Augusto Pinochet, the community also served as a detention and torture site for enemies of the regime. As Latin America has transitioned to democracy in recent decades, truth commissions and public displays documenting past abuses have become common in many countries, including Chile. In 2006, former members of the cult issued a public apology and asked for forgiveness for 40 years of human rights abuses in their community, saying they were brainwashed by Schaefer, who many viewed as a God. Colonia Dignidad continues to stir controversy in Chile, as residents have rebranded the community as a rustic, Bavaria-themed retreat for Santiago residents, which victims argue is disrespectful. Schaefer died in 2010, while serving a 20-year sentence for sex abuse.

Beijing dismisses ‘improper remarks’ over decision to not allow democracy advocate to be treated overseas for liver cancer China tells world to stay out of its 'domestic affairs' over Liu Xiaobo's death China has pushed back against a wave of international censure over the death of democracy advocate Liu Xiaobo, telling the world to stay out of its “domestic affairs” and labelling the 2010 decision to award the late activist a Nobel peace prize “a blasphemy”. Liu, 61, died of multiple organ failure on Thursday, the first Nobel peace prize winner to die in custody since the German pacifist Carl von Ossietzky, who died under surveillance in 1938 after years confined to Nazi concentration camps. Beijing had ignored international calls for Liu to be allowed to seek treatment abroad after he was diagnosed with late-stage liver cancer in May, apparently fearing he would use his final days of freedom to denounce its authoritarian rule. Geng Shuang, a spokesman for China’s foreign ministry, on Friday tried to downplay international condemnation of his government as the work of “a few foreign officials”. Berit Reiss-Andersen, the leader of the Norwegian Nobel committee, on Thursday accused Beijing of bearing “a heavy responsibility” for Liu’s death, while the British foreign secretary, Boris Johnson, said China’s treatment of him was “wrong”. “Foreign countries are in no position to make improper remarks,” Geng said, according to an English-language report published by a Communist party tabloid. Liu Xiaobo obituary Read more He told a daily press briefing that China had lodged representations with “certain countries” over their comments relating to Liu’s death. He also lashed out at the decision to award the dissident a Nobel prize in 2010, the year after he was sentenced. “Conferring the prize to such a person goes against the purposes of this award. It’s a blasphemy of the peace prize,” Geng said. He nevertheless claimed “all-out efforts” had been made to treat the dissident with “humanity and in accordance with the law”. Human rights activists and friends reject those claims as lies designed to mask Beijing’s responsibility for Liu’s demise, who was sentenced to 11 years in jail for his role in a pro-democracy protest. “This is ultimately a political murder,” said Hu Ping, who had known Liu since the spring of 1989, when pro-democracy protests shook Beijing. On Friday, there were signs that having condemned Liu to almost a quarter of his life behind bars, China’s leaders were also seeking to control his funeral. Activist Hu Jia said authorities were pressuring Liu’s family to quickly cremate his body. Relatives would only be allowed to hold “a simple farewell ceremony, under severe surveillance,” Hu said, adding that Liu’s friends had been unable to find out specific details. “Most likely, it was already held this morning or will happen at some point this afternoon,” he said. A brief report in China’s state-run media claimed Liu’s funeral “would respect the wishes of his family and local customs, with relevant authorities providing assistance if the family requests it”. Ye Du, another activist and friend of Liu, said he had been warned by Chinese security services that he was forbidden to travel. A “national ban” had been placed on activists hoping to attend Liu’s funeral, he added. Reiss-Andersen said that the Chinese consulate in Oslo had refused to receive her visa application for travel so she could attend the funeral. “I was told that my visa application was incorrectly filled in ... because I did not have an invitation from the person I was visiting,” she told Reuters. “When I told them I would be attending a funeral and that the person had passed away, I was told I should try a relative. I told them she was kept in isolation ... I was also told that I should have a hotel and plane ticket booked.” Internet censors meanwhile scrubbed Chinese social media of comments related to the Nobel laureate, blocking search terms including Liu Xiaobo, LXB and RIP. The phrase “I have no enemies” – Liu’s famous Tiananmen Square rallying call – was also banned. With Beijing desperate to tamp down discussion of Liu’s fate and ideas, China’s state-run media all but ignored his death. An editorial in one English-language tabloid sought to portray Liu not as a victim of the Communist party, but of western “forces” who had tried to use him to undermine party rule. “The west has bestowed upon Liu a halo, which will not linger,” the newspaper said, before attempting to rubbish his legacy. “Even if he could live longer, he would never have achieved his political goals.” Jerome Cohen, an expert in Chinese law and human rights at New York University, said Beijing’s treatment of Liu and his wife revealed “the Orwellian China that is developing under Xi Jinping”. He predicted that even after Liu’s death, Beijing’s attempts to cover up his message of democratic change would persist. “I’m sure that [his funeral] will be kept under strict wraps. They will have to probably make sure they deny him a prestigious place of burial. They will restrict the mourners to just a few people who are close to him. “There will not be publicity about the site of the burial. There will not be any sort of public mourning or funeral service. And then they will hope the whole thing will be buried with him.” However, Cohen said he feared Beijing’s persecution of the dissident’s wife, Liu Xia, would continue as it fought to prevent her becoming a symbol of political resistance and freedom of expression. “One-party dictatorships can’t allow free speech. That is why they tried to keep her quiet,” he said. “They may decide just to keep her under wraps indefinitely until she, too, succumbs to either mental illness or physical illness.” He said Beijing had prevented Liu from travelling and making a final denunciation of its authoritarian rule to avoid “an enormous public relations disaster”. Friends and activists have urged western governments to demand Liu Xia’s freedom from China. Additional reporting by Wang Zhen

BEIJING — The death on Thursday of China’s most prominent political prisoner, Liu Xiaobo, set off a frenzied effort by government censors to block discussion of his legacy online. Candle emoticons and the phrase “R.I.P.” were banned on Weibo, a popular Chinese microblogging site. On many sites, searches of Mr. Liu’s name turned up zero results. Still, Mr. Liu’s admirers found creative ways around the controls, using code words, videos and photographs to show solidarity and to criticize the government’s treatment of China’s only Nobel Peace laureate. Here’s a look at the reaction: ‘Heaven Is Watching’

The Norwegian Nobel Committee has expressed deep concern for Liu Xia, the widow of Liu Xiaobo, China's most prominent dissident, who died of liver cancer. The committee, along with the UN, has urged China to release Liu Xia, who has been under house arrest without charge since 2010. China has dismissed international criticism regarding its refusal to allow Liu Xiaobo, who was serving an 11-year sentence for "subversion," to receive medical treatment abroad.

The Nobel Committee, which awarded Liu Xiaobo the Peace Prize in 2010, stated that Beijing bears a "heavy responsibility" for his death. Berit Reiss-Andersen, the committee's leader, revealed that the Chinese consulate in Oslo rejected her visa application, preventing her from attending a potential funeral for Liu Xiaobo. She told the BBC that while she wished to pay her respects to the dissident's family during this "tragic situation," Beijing had missed an opportunity for a "fantastic gesture" by denying her travel.

Amidst this controversy, attention has shifted to Liu Xia, with growing worries about her mental well-being. The Norwegian Nobel Committee issued a statement expressing its "deeply worried about Liu Xia's situation" and called on Chinese authorities to "lift all restrictions they have put upon her." The statement emphasized, "If she wants to leave China, there is no justification for denying her the opportunity to do so."

Key concerns and calls for action regarding Liu Xia include:

*   **House Arrest and Isolation:** Liu Xia, a poet, is reportedly suffering from depression due to years of house arrest and surveillance. She was permitted to visit her husband in the hospital.
*   **"Contaminated by her husband's situation":** Ms. Reiss-Andersen described Liu Xia as having been "contaminated by her husband's situation," noting her isolation and lack of political activism compared to her husband.
*   **International Appeals:** Germany, the UK, France, the United States, and Taiwan have called for China to allow Liu Xia to leave the country if she wishes.
*   **UN Endorsement:** The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, Zeid Ra'ad Al Hussein, endorsed these calls, urging China to "guarantee Liu Xia's freedom of movement."
*   **Amnesty International Petition:** The human rights group Amnesty International has launched a petition for her release, stating, "it's time the Chinese authorities stop cruelly punishing" the artist.

The Chinese foreign ministry maintains that Liu Xia will be treated in accordance with the law.

**Who was Liu Xiaobo?**

Liu Xiaobo, a university professor and dedicated rights campaigner, was labeled a criminal by Chinese authorities and repeatedly imprisoned throughout his life. He is recognized for his role in potentially saving lives during the 1989 Tiananmen Square student protests, where he and other activists helped negotiate the safe exit of hundreds of demonstrators before the government's violent crackdown.

His 11-year prison sentence, handed down in 2009, stemmed from his co-authorship of the Charter 08 manifesto, which advocated for multi-party democracy. Liu Xiaobo was convicted of attempting to overthrow the state.

The Chinese foreign ministry has defended its handling of Liu Xiaobo's case, asserting it as an internal affair and criticizing foreign interference. Despite being awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010 for his "long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China," he was prevented from traveling to Norway to accept it.

In his final days, Liu Xiaobo was moved from prison to a hospital in Shenyang due to liver cancer, under heavy security. Western nations repeatedly requested permission for him to seek palliative care abroad, a request Beijing denied. While Chinese medical experts claimed he was too ill to travel, Western doctors who examined him held a different view. Liu Xiaobo died peacefully on Thursday afternoon, surrounded by his wife and relatives. His doctor, Teng Yue'e, reported his final words to his wife were: "Live on well."

International reporting on Liu Xiaobo's death has been heavily censored in mainland China, with local media offering minimal coverage, primarily in English. The Communist Party mouthpiece, Global Times, described him in an English editorial as "a victim led astray" by the West. Online discussions in China regarding his death have also faced censorship. Friends of Liu Xiaobo in China have reportedly been warned by authorities against organizing memorial events, with some activists, like Germany-based Tienchi Martin-Liao, telling the BBC that "many have been detained already." In contrast, Chinese activists outside mainland China have openly mourned him, with hundreds attending a vigil in Hong Kong.

The death of Nobel Peace Prize laureate Liu Xiaobo has prompted widespread global sorrow and strong condemnation, a stark contrast to the heavily censored and subdued response within China. Liu, a renowned writer and dedicated human rights advocate, passed away on Thursday evening at the age of 61. He was in custody at the time, having succumbed to liver cancer at a hospital in Shenyang. His funeral, held on Saturday morning in the same city, was attended by "family members and close friends," according to a spokesperson for the city government. Liu's remains were cremated, and his ashes were scattered at sea in a private ceremony, a measure reportedly implemented to prevent his grave from becoming a focal point for dissent.

### The Authorities' Efforts to Erase a Symbol

"The authorities cannot afford to see Liu's grave become a landmark and symbol where people gather to pay tribute," explained Hu Jia, a prominent Chinese human rights activist. "They aim to erase all traces of him." Hu Jia further commented, "But three-quarters of the Earth's surface is covered by water. His grave is now everywhere."

During a government-organized press conference, Liu Xiaobo's eldest brother, who reportedly never supported his activism, conveyed the family's "profuse gratitude" to the state. He thanked the government for its "humanity" in providing medical treatment and arranging "perfect" funeral services. He departed the conference without taking questions. The Chinese government, which had long suppressed Liu's writings and even his name, continues to censor news of his death. Social media posts have been deleted, and online searches related to him are blocked. CNN's broadcasts in China are also blacked out when Liu's images or story are featured.

Domestic media, under the control of the Communist Party, largely ignored the news. The front page of the *People's Daily*, for instance, focused on President Xi Jinping's meeting with his Canadian counterpart. A few English-language outlets published brief reports, which emphasized Liu's "criminal" background and the efforts of Chinese doctors.

### Liu Xiaobo's Activism and Conviction

Liu Xiaobo was serving an 11-year prison sentence for "inciting subversion of state power." His conviction stemmed from co-authoring a document advocating for human rights and political reform in China. In 2010, while imprisoned, he was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his "long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China," a decision that angered Beijing.

The state-run *Global Times*, in a nationalistic editorial, characterized Liu as "a victim led astray by the West." It suggested his attempts to confront mainstream Chinese society, influenced by Western ideas, led to his "tragic life." The newspaper also posted a now-deleted message on Sina Weibo, mocking international reactions to his death.

### China's Response to International Condemnation

In response to global condolences and condemnations, the Chinese government issued a statement calling foreign officials' comments "improper." Foreign ministry spokesman Geng Shuang asserted that the handling of Liu's case is an internal affair and urged countries to respect China's judicial sovereignty. China conveyed its "strong dissatisfaction and firm opposition" to governments and international entities that commented on his death.

Despite China's warnings, even U.S. President Donald Trump expressed sorrow and referred to Liu as a "political prisoner." He also highlighted Liu Xia, Liu Xiaobo's wife, a poet and artist. Liu Xia has been under de facto house arrest since her husband's Nobel win, with her communication severely restricted. Her brother's imprisonment on what supporters claim are trumped-up charges has added to her distress.

### The Campaign for Liu Xia's Freedom

An international campaign is now focused on securing Liu Xia's freedom and her right to live abroad, with many believing these were her husband's dying wishes. "He loved her so much that he felt he owed it to her," said Liao Yiwu, a writer and friend of the Lius. "He didn't know about her condition and what had happened at home until not that long ago. That's why he agreed to go abroad with her."

Liu Xiaobo's doctors confirmed that Liu Xia was present at his deathbed. His last words urged her to "live a good life." Supporters fear the Chinese government's apprehension about Liu Xia becoming an international symbol for human rights. "Few knew Liu Xiaobo's thoughts in the last eight years, but she did, and they talked during his last days," Hu Jia noted. "I think the authorities are afraid of seeing even the simplest last words from him get out and spread."

Geng, the foreign ministry spokesman, repeatedly declined to comment on whether Liu Xia would be allowed to leave China, stating her case would be handled "in accordance with law." A Shenyang city government spokesman declared Liu Xia "free" but in need of privacy. Government photos and videos released after the funeral showed a grieving Liu Xia and purported evidence of her consent to the sea burial. Liu Xiaobo's eldest brother cited her "extremely weak condition" as the reason for her absence from the funeral press conference.

Amidst the censorship, some internet users have shared lines from Liu Xia's poems dedicated to her late husband. These include verses from "Wind": "Walls make you suffocate / you are the wind, and the wind / never tells me / when it comes and when it goes."

query: What modifications did Stephen Paddock make to his weapons to increase their lethality during the Las Vegas shooting?

documents.text: CLOSE Newly released body camera footage from the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department offers a firsthand glimpse of the massacre as it happened. USA TODAY Mourners pay tribute at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip for the victims of a mass shooting in Las Vegas on Monday. (Photo: Eugene Garcia, EPA-EFE) LAS VEGAS — Investigators say that a retired accountant's shooting rampage was meticulously planned and included specially modified weapons and surveillance cameras aimed to spy on any police intruders who would try and stop his murderous deluge. At a late-night press conference Tuesday, authorities said Stephen Paddock made his attack even more deadly by adding more lethal components to his weapons. He had devices attached to 12 semiautomatic rifles that allowed them to mimic fully automatic gunfire. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms Special Agent in Charge Jill Schneider said Paddock had nearly 50 guns — a combination of rifles, shotguns and pistols — in three locations. The gun attachment that mimics automatic gunfire is a little-known device called a “bump stock” that is not widely sold. The stocks have been around for less than a decade, and Schneider said officials determined they were legal. Paddock, who killed 58 people and eventually himself, also injured 527 others in what is the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history. He also used surveillance cameras to monitor police approaches to his room at the Mandalay Bay high-rise — including a camera he positioned in the peephole of the door. “I anticipate he was looking for anybody coming to take him into custody,” Sheriff Joseph Lombardo said. During the Sunday night rampage, a hotel security guard who approached the room was shot through the door and wounded in the leg. “The fact that he had the type of weaponry and amount of weaponry in that room, it was preplanned extensively,” the sheriff said, “and I’m pretty sure he evaluated everything that he did and his actions, which is troublesome.” While Paddock's motive remains unclear, authorities were putting together a more complete picture of his work history and his past. The killer worked as a letter carrier for the U.S. Postal Service, an IRS agent and in an auditing department over a 10-year period. A spokeswoman for the Office of Personnel Management told The Associated Press on Tuesday that Stephen Paddock’s employment included about two years as a mail carrier from 1976 to 1978. After that, he worked as an agent for the Internal Revenue Service for six years until 1984. And then he worked a defense auditing job for about 18 months. He graduated from college in 1977 from Cal State Northridge and also worked for a defense contractor in the late 1980s. President Trump, as he left Washington for a trip to storm-ravaged Puerto Rico, briefly referenced Paddock, whose carnage Sunday night left 59 people dead and more than 500 injured. "He was a sick man, a demented man with a lot of problems, I guess, and we are looking into him very, very seriously," Trump said. "We are dealing with a very, very sick individual." The president also said the administration would be "talking about gun laws as time goes by," and he lauded the efforts of Las Vegas law enforcement. "How quickly the police department was able to get in (to Paddock's hotel room) was very much a miracle," he said. "They've done an amazing job." Read more: Why motive matters Gun dealer: 'My gut fell out' Country music star Jason Aldean was performing when Paddock opened fire from the window of his 32nd-floor hotel room overlooking the Route 91 Harvest music festival. Aldean, who was not injured, tweeted a statement Tuesday saying he has been overwhelmed with emotion since the attack. "Something has changed in this country and in this world lately that is scary to see," Aldean wrote. But he also added that it is "time to come together and stop the hate." Stories of heroism continued to emerge. One minute Marine veteran Taylor Winston was dancing the two-step at the concert, the next he was commandeering a pickup and shuttling the wounded to hospitals before ambulances arrived. Dawn-Marie Gray, a concertgoer who happened to have seven years experience as a paramedic, found herself providing CPR and tying tourniquets. When Nick Jones, 30, of Vancouver, Wash., heard about the shooting, he drove 20 hours to help. He offered to donate blood, but the need had been met. He offered to volunteer at hospitals, but they had plenty of help. Even the family assistance center was overwhelmed with volunteers. “I was worried that no one would do anything,” Jones said Tuesday. “I was happy to be proven wrong.” Assistant Sheriff Todd Fasulo pleaded for patience during the investigation, saying authorities were "hunting down" every clue to learn more about Paddock. Several minutes after the carnage began, officers who blasted into the room found Paddock's body. His brother Eric, who lives in Florida, says his multimillionaire brother was a big spender at casinos and often received free meals and rooms there. Eric Paddock also said he believed his brother owned a couple guns. Police found 23 guns, including semiautomatic rifles, in Paddock's room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino. They found 19 more at his home in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of here. And in yet another home owned by Paddock in Reno, authorities found five handguns, two shotguns, and a plethora of ammunition. CLOSE Las Vegas Metro Police have released footage from the body cameras worn by officers responding to the scene of the shooting Oct. 2, 2017. Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department A federal law enforcement official, who was briefed on the matter but not authorized to comment publicly, said police also found two tripods positioned at the hotel windows in what appeared to be a fully equipped sniper’s nest. While the investigation intensified, authorities touted the city’s resilience in the aftermath of the tragedy. Fully stocked blood banks were soon turning away would-be donors. A GoFundMe page collected $3.3 million from almost 50,000 donors in little more than 24 hours. Overall, donations for the victims have exceeded $3.7 million, Commissioner Stephen F. Sisolak said Tuesday afternoon. Pieces of normalcy slowly returned to the iconic Strip. Club promoters hawked 2-for-1 deals, tourists took selfies and police cracked down would-be criminals. “Do you know how disrespectful it is to come out here and deal drugs the day after 50 people died one block away?’’ a bike cop snapped at a man in handcuffs Monday night. “Can’t you take one day off?’’ Derek Koerner, who identified himself as a licensed club promoter, said he was encouraged by the sizable crowds — and particularly grateful for the SUV full of men interested in his 2-for-1 deals on strip clubs. A $40 cover for a two-drink minimum gets the buyer into the second strip club for free. “There’s more people out here than I thought there’d be,’’ said Koerner, 48. “People seem to be upbeat and have a good energy. People seem to be moving on with their lives.’’ But less than a mile away, the mood was noticeably different. Dozens of people, some arriving with candles and flowers, returned to the scene of the horrific crime, marked by yellow police tape. Herman Gold and his ex-wife, Sara, stood near the concert grounds and explained how they escaped the hail of bullets and why they came back. “I just want closure,’’ Herman Gold said. “Why? What’s the reason that on that particular night, one individual would sacrifice everyone else for his insanity?’’ Joe Larsen, standing at the same corner and wearing a VIP wristband from the Aldean concert, expressed gratitude. Larsen, 49, who flew in from New Jersey for the concert, said that he watched the Twin Towers fall in 2001, lost his wife to cancer in 2015 and, on Sunday night, feared he was about to leave his son and daughter without a parent. He said he saw a young woman die from a bullet to the head before he called his father during the bedlam. “I told him, ‘I don’t know if going to make it. Tell my kids to live a good life’ ” Larsen recalled. As he and others shared their stories and wiped away tears, police officers closely monitored pedestrians, especially the wayward ones. “Sir, get out of the street,’’ one officer barked. “It’s the last time I’m telling you.’’ Read more: Shooter may have used trigger crank Machine guns have long been a draw in Las Vegas Bacon reported from McLean, Va. Contributing: Kevin Johnson and Ashley May, USA TODAY; Brett Kelman, The Desert Sun; Megan Cassidy, The Arizona Republic; Mike Benner, KGW-TV, Portland, Ore. **New Evidence Suggests Las Vegas Shooter May Have Targeted Another Festival**

Investigators are examining a compelling new theory regarding the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history. New information suggests that Stephen Paddock, the perpetrator of the Las Vegas massacre, may have initially set his sights on a different music festival. A senior law enforcement official, privy to the ongoing inquiry, has revealed that Paddock had rented several condominiums with a direct view of the annual Life Is Beautiful Festival. This event occurred the weekend immediately preceding the tragic Route 91 Harvest Festival. The lineup for this year's Life Is Beautiful, as confirmed by the source, included notable performers like Lorde and Chance the Rapper.

To verify these details, The Daily Beast looked into the Ogden, a high-rise luxury condominium complex. This building offers an unobstructed perspective of the festival grounds. Melissa Warren, a public affairs representative for Fais Foley Warren, the property's owner, stated, "We are unable to confirm or deny any information regarding Mr. Paddock's activities. We recommend contacting Metro [police]. As you are aware, this is an active investigation."

The same law enforcement source further indicated that Paddock might have altered his intentions or experienced a change of mind. This potential shift in plans led him to check into the Mandalay Bay Hotel on September 28. It was from a suite within the Mandalay Bay that Paddock unleashed gunfire upon an estimated 20,000 concertgoers at the Route 91 Harvest Festival.

During the extensive investigation, law enforcement personnel, including Sheriff Joseph Lombardo of the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department, discovered a significant cache of weapons. Within Paddock's hotel suite, 23 firearms were found. Sheriff Lombardo reported to the press that this extensive arsenal, which contained multiple rifles capable of fully automatic fire, had been discreetly transported in 10 suitcases. Subsequent searches of Paddock's residence in Mesquite, Nevada, yielded additional discoveries. Authorities found at least 19 more firearms, thousands of rounds of ammunition, and materials indicative of bomb construction. Police also confirmed the presence of ammonium nitrate, a chemical commonly used in explosives, within his vehicle.

Investigators trying to determine what sparked the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history have found the massacre to be the work of a sophisticated planner with the means and desire to inflict unprecedented carnage. But on the second full day after Stephen Paddock smashed out the windows of a high-floor suite on the Las Vegas Strip and opened fire on a crowd of unsuspecting concertgoers, authorities still were trying to understand what drove him to such evil. Several new details emerged Tuesday about Paddock — a 64-year-old retired accountant — and how he worked methodically to thwart law enforcement as he killed scores of people and injured hundreds more. [ Two strangers bond over country music and beer. Then the gunshots started. ] As he fired round after round during an 11-minute stretch from a suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino, Paddock used video cameras to keep an eye out for police storming his hotel room, according to Clark County Sheriff Joe Lombardo. Paddock hid one camera in the peephole of his suite and two more in the hall, at least one of them disguised on a service cart, authorities said. At one point, he shot numerous rounds through the door, wounding a security guard. Paddock eventually put a gun in his own mouth and pulled the trigger as SWAT officers closed in. They found him with blood pooling behind his head and around the empty shell casings that littered the carpet, a handgun near his body. “It was preplanned, extensively, and I’m pretty sure that he evaluated everything that he did in his actions, which is troublesome,�? Lombardo said. The sheriff said investigators were “making progress�? on determining a motive, but complete answers remained elusive. There were precious few clues in Paddock’s background. An investigator works in the room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino on Tuesday, in Las Vegas. A gunman opened fire from the room, killing at least 58 people and injuring hundreds. (AP Photo/Marcio Jose Sanchez) Neighbors in several states where Paddock owned homes in retirement communities describe him as surly, unfriendly and standoffish. Paddock was the son of a bank robber who was once on the FBI’s most-wanted list and who authorities described at the time as a “psychopath,�? but Paddock’s brother said their father was not involved in their lives when they were children. Relatives say the roots of Paddock’s loner lifestyle might have been planted on July 28, 1960. On that day, when Paddock was 7, a neighbor from across the street took him swimming. The neighbor told a local newspaper at the time that she knew authorities were coming for his father, and she wanted to spare the young boy from the trauma of seeing his father taken away. From that point on, Paddock’s family was never the same. Until carrying out the massacre Sunday night, Paddock had no criminal history himself. Despite repeated claims by the Islamic State to the contrary, he also had no ties to international terror groups, authorities said. He had done some government work during his career, as a letter carrier for the Postal Service, an agent for the Internal Revenue Service and an auditor for the federal government’s Defense Contract Audit Agency in the late 1970s and 1980s. He was divorced twice and recently had been dating a woman from the Philippines who has Australian citizenship. He was known to gamble routinely and extensively. Some public officials seemed to suggest Paddock’s mind was troubled, though there were no immediate indications that he had been diagnosed with a mental illness or was anything other than fully aware of what he was doing. “A normal person would not cause this type of harm to innocent people,�? said Rep. Ruben Kihuen (D-Nev.). “Clearly, there was something wrong with this man.�? People close to the investigation also said that in the weeks before the attack, Paddock transferred a large amount of money — close to $100,000 — to someone in the Philippines, possibly his girlfriend. The significance of that development was not immediately clear, though investigators said they were interested in probing Paddock’s finances and his avid interest in high-stakes gambling. The girlfriend, Marilou Danley, returned to the United States from the Philippines Tuesday night and was met at Los Angeles International Airport by FBI agents, according to wire reports. Investigators considered her a “person of interest,�? Lombardo said. Sen. Dean Heller (R-Nev.) said that police are eager to talk to her, as Paddock “doesn’t meet any profiles�? and the “best lead is through this girlfriend.�? “They don’t know a lot about who the girlfriend is and why she left the country a week prior to the shooting,�? said Heller, who has been briefed by authorities. “She is someone they need to have this discussion with to better understand the shooting and what his thought process was.�? Douglas and Sylvia Vick from Texas pray at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip on Tuesday. (Mark Ralston/AFP/Getty Images) At his home in Orlando, Eric Paddock, Stephen Paddock’s brother, said he doubts Danley had any prior knowledge of the incident and speculated that Stephen might have been trying to quietly ensure her financial stability. Stephen Paddock loved and doted on his girlfriend, whom he had met when she was a hostess at a casino, Eric Paddock said. The couple often gambled side by side. “He manipulated her to be as far away from here and safe when he committed this,�? Eric Paddock said. “The people he loved he took care of, and as he was descending into hell he took care of her.�? The shooting — which left 59 people dead and injured more than 500 others — reignited the debate on Capitol Hill and across the country about whether lawmakers should impose new restrictions on firearms. Clark County Coroner John Fudenberg on Tuesday evening clarified that Paddock was among the 59 slain in the incident; previously, authorities had said he wasn’t. Undersheriff Kevin C. McMahill, speaking after Fudenberg, warned that the number of dead and injured could fluctuate as the investigation progresses. “The answer that the coroner provided you is the most recent, relevant number that we have,�? McMahill said. Caution tape lines the doors of the gunman’s suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay hotel. (Bild Exclusive/Polaris.) Several Democrats used the massacre to push for new restrictions, and House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.) asserted Tuesday that Republican leaders have no plans to advance a bill, which passed a House committee last month, that would make it easier to buy gun silencers. Leaving the White House to visit hurricane-ravaged Puerto Rico on Tuesday morning, President Trump said that “we’ll be talking about gun laws as time goes by.�? Paddock brought 23 guns to his Mandalay Bay suite, which overlooked the concert grounds on the southern end of the Strip. Included in that cache was an AR-15-type rifle with a high-capacity magazine and 12 “bump stocks,�? a modification that would allow a gun to fire as if it were an automatic weapon. He also brought another AR-15-type rifle with a magnification scope commonly used for hunting and a bipod stand to help steady it, according to law enforcement officials and experts who reviewed images of the weapons posted online. “For this individual to take it upon himself to create this chaos and harm is unspeakable,�? Lombardo said. [ The lives lost in the Las Vegas massacre ] The guns appeared to be but a piece of the arsenal Paddock had acquired during a period of years, at least some of it legally obtained from major retailers such as Cabela’s, national retailer of guns and outdoor gear, and Guns & Guitars, a store in Mesquite, Nev., according to people familiar with the case. ATF Special Agent in Charge Jill A. Snyder said Tuesday Paddock had purchased shotguns, handguns and rifles in Nevada, Utah, California and Texas. At Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev., investigators recovered 19 more guns and an explosive, and in his car, they found ammonium nitrate, a chemical that can be used in bomb making. At another property in Reno, Nev., investigators recovered five more handguns, two shotguns and a “plethora�? of ammunition, Lombardo said Tuesday. Paddock probably secreted the weapons to his Mandalay Bay suite using more than 10 suitcases he brought with him. Lombardo said hotel housekeeping had been there since Paddock checked in on Sept. 28 and spotted nothing nefarious. Lombardo said investigators had scoured Paddock’s suite, his car and the homes in Nevada, and they were examining surveillance and body camera video evidence and other electronic items they had seized in their searches. Separately, Lombardo said the department had opened an investigation into the unauthorized release of images that show the crime scene. Another sheriff’s official confirmed the authenticity of those images. Police also continued documenting the crime scene and performing the grim task of identifying those slain, some of whom died blocks away from the concert as they tried to flee or seek help. As of late Tuesday afternoon, authorities had tentatively identified all of the victims. [ How the shooting unfolded ] Since the shooting, horrific stories have emerged of lives cut short, some taking bullets as they sought to protect their loved ones. Bobby and Jenny Parks, a married couple from California who had been high school sweethearts, were swaying to a love song when a bullet struck her. He held her as she died. After Cameron Robinson, a 27-year-old from Utah, was shot in the neck, his boyfriend, Robert Eardley, carried him to a vehicle. Robinson died before they made it to a hospital. Two people who met at the festival — Kody Robertson and Michelle Vo — were watching the final act together when a bullet struck her chest. Robertson threw his body over hers to protect her. Later, they were separated; he tracked her down to a hospital and was told late Monday morning that she was dead. Paddock’s weaponry would have allowed him to fire both rapidly and precisely at the crowd that was several hundred yards away and below him. The shots — fired from two windows of Mandalay Bay’s golden facade, which Paddock had smashed out — sent thousands of people scrambling for safety under Vegas’s neon glow. Police on Tuesday showed body camera footage of officers scrambling to determine where the shots came from as they yelled for people to leave the area. “They’re shooting right at us, guys,�? an officer can be heard saying. “Stay down! Stay down!�? Hospitals across the region continued to treat patients from the scene, many of them seriously injured. Sunrise Hospital and Medical Center says as of Tuesday night, 59 patients remain in the hospital and 31 of them are still in critical condition. University Medical Center said it had 64 patients from the attack, 12 of them critical. Roads inside the Mandalay Bay Resort are closed for investigation on Tuesday in Las Vegas.
