Davis, William Wan and Sari Horwitz in Washington contributed to this report.

## Diazepam Prescription Issued to Las Vegas Shooter Stephen Paddock Months Before Attack

A prescription for the anti-anxiety medication diazepam was issued to Stephen Paddock, the 64-year-old Mesquite resident who carried out the devastating Las Vegas shooting, by a doctor in Henderson. This prescription was filled on the same day in Reno, according to records obtained by the Review-Journal from the state's Prescription Monitoring Program. The anti-anxiety drug, known for its potential to induce aggressive behavior, was prescribed to Paddock in June, just months prior to the massacre where he killed at least 58 people and injured hundreds more with high-powered rifles.

### Details of the Diazepam Prescription

Records from the Nevada Prescription Monitoring Program, acquired on Tuesday, reveal that Paddock was prescribed 50 10-milligram tablets of diazepam by Henderson physician Dr. Steven Winkler on June 21. A representative from Dr. Winkler’s office declined to comment, neither confirming nor denying Paddock's status as a patient. Paddock purchased the medication, widely recognized by its brand name Valium, without insurance at a Walgreens in Reno on the very day it was prescribed. The prescribed dosage was one pill per day.

### Understanding Diazepam and Benzodiazepines

Diazepam belongs to a class of drugs known as benzodiazepines, which are classified as sedative-hypnotics. Scientific research indicates that these types of medications can, in some instances, trigger aggressive behavior. Furthermore, according to information from drugabuse.com, chronic use or misuse of sedatives like diazepam can also lead to the development of psychotic experiences.

### Expert Insights on Potential Behavioral Effects

Dr. Mel Pohl, chief medical officer at the Las Vegas Recovery Center, provided insight into the potential effects of such medications. "If somebody has an underlying aggression problem and you sedate them with that drug, they can become aggressive," Dr. Pohl explained. "It can disinhibit an underlying emotional state. It is much like what happens when you give alcohol to some people; they become aggressive instead of going to sleep." Speaking from the Netherlands, Dr. Pohl also noted that alcohol can significantly amplify the effects of diazepam.

### Research Linking Benzodiazepines to Aggression

A significant 2015 study published in the journal *World Psychiatry* examined 960 Finnish adults and teenagers who had been convicted of homicide. This research found that their likelihood of committing a killing was 45 percent higher during periods when they were taking benzodiazepines. Earlier, the *Australian and New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry* published a study titled, “Benzodiazepine Use and Aggressive Behavior.” The authors of this study concluded, “It appears that benzodiazepine use is moderately associated with subsequent aggressive behavior.”

### Expert Opinion on Premeditation vs. Impulsivity

Dr. Michael First, a clinical psychiatry professor at Columbia University and a recognized expert on benzodiazepines, commented on the Finnish study, stating that its findings were compelling. However, he also shared with the Review-Journal on Tuesday his belief that these drugs are more likely to fuel impulsive aggression rather than premeditated actions. Dr. First pointed to the Las Vegas attack as an example that appeared to be meticulously planned, referencing reports of Paddock bringing an extensive arsenal of weapons into the Mandalay Bay hotel and setting up surveillance cameras in and around his room prior to the shooting.

### The Significance of Prescription History

Dr. First emphasized the importance of understanding the underlying reasons behind Paddock's prescription. "That may have more to do with why he did what he did," he suggested. The Nevada state monitoring report also indicated that Dr. Winkler had prescribed 50 10-milligram tablets of diazepam to Paddock in 2016. That prescription was also filled on the same day it was written, this time at Evergreen Drugs in Henderson, with a prescribed dosage of two tablets per day.

### Broader Discussion on Psychiatric Medications and Violence

The potential link between psychiatric medications and mass shootings in the United States has been a subject of ongoing discussion within scientific and public spheres. However, researchers have yet to establish a definitive causal connection despite numerous studies. Critics of such discussions argue that unsubstantiated claims circulating in the media and on social media, which link psychiatric drugs to violence, can unfairly stigmatize individuals who benefit from these medications. This stigma, they contend, could potentially lead them to discontinue life-saving treatments. Actress Kirstie Alley recently sparked controversy with a tweet suggesting that guns and psychiatric drugs are common factors in recent U.S. mass shootings, although she provided no specific evidence regarding Paddock's drug use.

**Off-Duty Firefighters Transform into First Responders During Las Vegas Shooting**

In the face of unimaginable terror, off-duty firefighters demonstrated extraordinary courage and quick thinking during the Las Vegas country music festival shooting. Benjamin Kole, witnessing concertgoers fleeing in panic, issued a decisive command: "Not one (vehicle) leaves without a patient, a victim." This immediate directive underscored the urgency to provide aid amidst the chaos. Jesse Gomez, despite his wife's pleas to leave the dangerous scene, prioritized helping the injured, instructing her to take a wounded concertgoer to the hospital while he remained to assist. Anthony Robone, after ensuring his wounded brother was safely evacuated, bravely returned to the site of the massacre.

These three men, ordinarily engaged in their civilian lives at the festival, found themselves thrust into a critical role. Without their standard issue equipment, such as radios, they relied on their extensive training and innate desire to help. As the gunfire erupted for a harrowing eleven minutes, these off-duty first responders, alongside untrained civilians, sprang into action. They provided immediate medical care, triaged the wounded, established makeshift triage areas, and coordinated the transport of numerous injured individuals to nearby medical facilities.

Kole, Gomez, and Robone were among dozens of off-duty firefighters present at the concert. Tragically, twelve other firefighters sustained gunshot wounds, with two receiving CPR at the time of their injury, as reported by the International Association of Fire Fighters union. Speaking at the Las Vegas Fire Fighters Union Hall less than 48 hours after the devastating event, the firefighters shared the profound emotional toll and difficult decisions they faced. Kole recounted the agonizing necessity of apologizing to critically wounded individuals, stating, "there was nothing we could do" and they had to move on to assist others.

Many initially mistook the initial sounds for celebratory fireworks during Jason Aldean's performance. However, Kole, accompanied by a friend who is a corrections officer, recognized the distinct pattern of gunfire during a second volley. His friend's quick action in knocking him to the ground saved him from immediate harm. Kole then realized his daughter, Rachel Kole, who worked for an ambulance company, was also present at the concert. Despite the ongoing gunfire, he ran to her location. Upon reuniting, he declared, "it was time to get to work," and together they began tending to the injured. Kole also noted the remarkable composure and assistance provided by regular citizens who remained calm and offered help.

Jesse Gomez attended the concert with his family. When the shooting began, he realized the sounds were not fireworks as the performers fled the stage. Amidst the unfolding panic, he saw a woman bleeding profusely from her head. He directed his family to safety while he and others aided the injured woman. He then contacted his wife, informing her of his decision to stay and help. When they met in the parking lot, his wife implored him not to return, but he felt compelled to do so. He described the terrifying sensation of the bullet impacts seemingly drawing nearer, as if a gun battle was unfolding mere feet away. He and another man then "went back to work," a testament to their dedication. Gomez expressed his astonishment at the number of people who chose to stay and offer assistance. He observed civilians utilizing tarps, trash cans, and fences to transport the wounded. He also described the heartbreaking task of informing grieving individuals that they had to move their deceased loved ones, as they were still in the line of fire.

Anthony Robone initially reassured his girlfriend that everything was fine. However, upon realizing the sounds were likely gunshots, he turned to discover his brother had been shot and was spitting blood. While a friend escorted his girlfriend to safety, Robone and another companion carried his brother towards what appeared to be medical tents. He described the relentless nature of the gunfire, stating, "The rounds seemed like they’d never stop." After applying makeshift bandages to his brother's wounds, Robone's professional training took over. He witnessed numerous fatalities, lamenting, "Unfortunately we couldn’t help at that time." Yet, he also observed an inspiring display of community resilience, with civilians applying pressure to wounds and fashioning tourniquets from their own clothing. He highlighted the profound selflessness of those with survivable injuries, recounting how some, despite being shot themselves, urged responders to prioritize those with more severe wounds, such as head or neck injuries.

The deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history unfolded in Las Vegas on Sunday, October 1, 2017, when a gunman opened fire on an outdoor music concert. The attack, which claimed the lives of 59 people and injured over 500, has prompted a shift in the investigation towards the gunman's girlfriend, Marilou Danley. Danley, who had been abroad for weeks, returned to the United States from the Philippines on Tuesday night, arriving in Los Angeles where FBI agents were present. Authorities, including Sheriff Joseph Lombardo, are hopeful that Danley can provide crucial insights into the motives behind Stephen Paddock's actions.

Meanwhile, interviews with Danley's family in Australia have shed some light on her potential involvement, or lack thereof. Her sisters, speaking anonymously to Australia's Channel 7 TV network, expressed their belief that Paddock may have sent Danley away to prevent her from interfering with his planned rampage. One sister tearfully stated that Danley, whom she described as a "good person," would have intervened had she been present. They also conveyed surprise at Danley's departure to the Philippines two weeks prior to the shooting.

Further adding to the shock and disbelief surrounding the event, a nephew of Stephen Paddock's girlfriend, Jordan Knights, revealed his astonishment at the gunman's actions. Knights, who spent time with Paddock and Danley in Las Vegas just months before the shooting, told Australia's Channel 9 that Paddock "didn't seem like he was the type of guy to do that." He described Paddock as someone who "just looked after my aunty and that was it," and admitted he had no prior knowledge of Paddock's interest in firearms. The 23-year-old nephew only gave Paddock another thought after he was identified as the perpetrator of the horrific attack.

As the investigation continues, President Donald Trump is scheduled to visit Las Vegas on Wednesday to meet with public officials, first responders, and some of the individuals injured in the attack. The return of Marilou Danley to the United States marks a significant development, with law enforcement officials, speaking on condition of anonymity to the Associated Press, emphasizing the importance of her testimony in understanding the circumstances leading up to this tragic event.

A federal law enforcement official earlier said two rifles were outfitted with scopes and set up on tripods in front of two big windows. Another official said that among the weapons were AR-15-style assault rifles. Both officials spoke on the condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to divulge details of the investigation. Sheriff Lombardo said that Mr. Paddock brought at least 10 suitcases into his hotel room over a period of time. The sheriff said that Mr. Paddock fired through his hotel room door at security guards, striking one in the leg. The guard is still alive, he said. SWAT officers went in after the guard was shot. In addition to the weapons at the hotel, the sheriff said the police retrieved 19 firearms, as well as explosives, several thousand rounds of ammunition and “electronic devices” from Mr. Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev. Paddock raised no suspicions at a store where he bought firearms. The investigation into Mr. Paddock’s gun arsenal led investigators to New Frontier Armory, a large North Las Vegas dealer, distributor and manufacturer of firearms. David M. Famiglietti, the company’s president, issued a statement on Tuesday confirming that Mr. Paddock purchased “several” rifles and shotguns from the business last spring — all at the same time. It’s not uncommon for customers to do this to save money on background check fees, Mr. Famiglietti said. “The firearms he purchased did not leave our store capable of what we’ve seen and heard in the video without modification,” he said. “They were not fully automatic firearms, nor were they modified in any way — legally or illegally — when they were purchased from us.” Mr. Famiglietti said that nothing about Mr. Paddock raised red flags with the store’s sales clerk. On the contrary, according to Mr. Famiglietti, Mr. Paddock seemed to be interested in competitive shooting, asking the clerk a lot of questions about three-gun shooting matches — an increasingly popular sport in which players use a rifle, a shotgun and a pistol. “He just seemed like a normal guy,” said Mr. Famiglietti, whose store has been the target of hate mail, threatening phone calls and fake reviews since the shooting. “We obviously did not sell him these firearms with the intent that he would use them to hurt anyone in any way.” On Monday, a Mesquite, Nev., gun dealer, Guns & Guitars, acknowledged having sold two rifles and a handgun to Mr. Paddock. Like the purchases at Frontier, those were cleared by state and federal background checks. The gunman was ‘not an avid gun guy at all,’ said one of his brothers. Mr. Paddock, 64, was described as a high-flying gambler who lived in a quiet retirement community and played golf. Officials said he had no significant criminal history and drew little attention to himself. Investigators are trying to piece together his financial history to search for clues that could help determine what set him off. Image Stephen Paddock in a photo provided by his brother Eric Paddock. Credit via Associated Press Mr. Paddock often bet heavily at the major casino hotels, and his girlfriend, Ms. Danley, 62, who was out of the country at the time of the shooting, had worked for some of those hotels. They lived in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of Las Vegas, and he also owned a home in Reno. Ms. Danley boarded a flight from Manila to Los Angeles on Tuesday, according to Antonette Mangrobang, a spokeswoman for the Philippine Immigration Bureau. The authorities, who met Ms. Danley at the Los Angeles airport, have called her a “person of interest” in the shooting investigation, which does not necessarily mean that she is suspected of committing a crime. ‘I’ve never been that scared in my life,’ said one witness. The police estimated that when the shooting began, there were 22,000 people at the Harvest Festival, listening to Jason Aldean, the final act of the three-day event. Video of the shooting captured nine seconds of continuous, rapid fire, followed by 37 seconds of silence from the weapon and panicked screaming from the crowd. Gunfire then erupted again and again in extended bursts. Some concertgoers thought the noise was fireworks, but as it became clear what was happening, people fled, many of them unsure where the shots were coming from, or where they should go. “Everyone was running, you could see people getting shot,” said Gail Davis, one of the witnesses. “I’ve never been that scared in my life,” she added. “To have this happen, I can’t wrap my mind around it.” Video from the shooting showed Mr. Aldean running off the stage as the gunfire erupted.

Following the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history, gun control advocates are intensifying calls to ban "bump stocks." These largely unregulated novelty devices may have been used by Las Vegas shooter Stephen Paddock to convert semi-automatic rifles into weapons mimicking fully automatic fire. The Associated Press, citing law enforcement sources, reported that at least two bump stocks were recovered from Paddock's hotel room, though their use in the attack remains unconfirmed.

Bump stocks attach to semi-automatic firearms and leverage the weapon's recoil to enable a shooter to fire rounds at a significantly accelerated rate compared to manually pulling the trigger. As Bump Fire Systems advertised on its website, users could achieve "simulated full-auto firing" legally for $99.99. However, gun experts like those cited by the National Shooting Sports Foundation have characterized bump stocks as "toys" or items for "gun geeks," rather than serious shooting tools where accuracy is paramount. Prior to the Las Vegas incident, these devices were prominently featured in online videos by gun enthusiasts, including those shared by channels like Full30.

Key figures in the gun control movement are urging immediate action:

*   **Senator Dianne Feinstein**, who previously authored the 1994 assault weapons ban and has sought its reinstatement, stated that she has long advocated for prohibiting bump stocks. "This is the least we should do in the wake of the deadliest mass shooting in US history. It should be our highest priority," Feinstein declared.
*   **Shannon Watts**, founder of the gun control organization Moms Demand Action, echoed this sentiment on Twitter, asserting that "Hardware that turns legal guns into automatic weapons should be prohibited," as it circumvents federal regulations on automatic weapons.
*   **David Chipman**, a former ATF agent and policy advisor for Americans for Responsible Solutions, a gun control advocacy group, noted that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) has consistently determined that bump stocks and similar devices are not strictly regulated under the National Firearms Act or the Gun Control Act. Chipman explained that bump stocks facilitate rapid fire by "throwing the trigger against your finger as opposed to your finger pulling on the trigger," describing the device as a "masterful creation of a technical workaround creating a loophole to circumvent the intention of the law."

One individual, a refinery operator from Wilmington, Delaware, who requested anonymity, shared his experience. He purchased a Bump Fire Systems device approximately two years prior for around $100. He sought it as the "closest thing that I could have that would simulate a machine gun," particularly in Delaware, where machine gun ownership is prohibited. He noted that purchasing the bump stock did not require a background check, stating, "It’s just like ammo: you order them online, they ship them to your house." While he found the device enjoyable as a "range toy," he acknowledged its difficulty to master and its inaccuracy, emphasizing that "This thing requires practice." He also suggested that many gun stores refrain from selling bump stocks, potentially to avoid attracting "irresponsible gun owners" who might misuse them after viewing online videos. Similarly, many shooting ranges, such as those affiliated with the National Shooting Sports Foundation, prohibit their use.

Another individual, who identified himself as Rich, found the novelty of the bump stock eventually wore off. He concluded it was "definitely not reliable as a self-defense method or anything else." He anticipated that the devices would face intense scrutiny and calls for bans, which he personally finds regrettable. "I don’t want to see anything banned because of the actions of one person," he stated, adding, "That just doesn’t jive with my principles of freedom."

query: What is the primary goal of the Internet Archive's initiative to crawl and archive web pages?

documents.text: These crawls are part of an effort to archive pages as they are created and archive the pages that they refer to. That way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved.Then the Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.The goal is to fix all broken links on the web . Crawls of supported "No More 404" sites.

HBO's *Game of Thrones* season seven premiere, episode one, was a pivotal installment, packed with significant plot developments. However, the episode's narrative momentum, including Daenerys Targaryen's strategic arrival in Westeros and the looming threat of the White Walkers, was largely overshadowed by a pre-announced celebrity cameo. The most widely discussed moment was not the intricate political maneuvering or the impending war, but the appearance of pop superstar Ed Sheeran. This focus on a musician, rather than the show's complex storylines, highlights a recurring pattern within *Game of Thrones*: a perceived tendency to feature artists whose appeal stems more from mass-market popularity than dramatic depth.

While the episode offered compelling moments, such as Arya Stark's infiltration of the Riverlands, these were largely predictable outcomes of established plotlines. The true talking point, known since March, was Sheeran's brief role. His scene, where he encountered Arya while she was alone, served as a brief respite from the episode's darker themes. He shared wine and spoke of the futility of war and the importance of kindness, offering Arya a moment of reflection after her brutal experiences.

Sheeran's cameo, while not particularly memorable in itself, reinforces a trend observed throughout *Game of Thrones*' run. The series has a history of enlisting musicians whose appeal lies more in their mainstream popularity than their dramatic talent. This pattern suggests a deliberate choice by the show's creators, including executive producers David Benioff and D.B. Weiss, to integrate these artists into the Westerosi landscape.

Key musical cameos in *Game of Thrones* include:

*   **Season Three:** Will Champion of Coldplay.
*   **Season Four:** Gary Lightbody of Snow Patrol and the band Sigur Rós.
*   **Later Seasons:** Robson and Jerome were notably cast in the role of Bronn.

Sheeran's appearance, while potentially a "dud" in terms of narrative impact, undeniably placed him within this established lineage of musical cameos. Critics, such as The Ringer's Joanna Robinson, have noted this trend, underscoring the ongoing debate about the show's casting choices and their effect on the immersive quality of the narrative. The author concludes on a somewhat positive note, suggesting that at least the cameo was not as potentially jarring as an appearance by Justin Bieber might have been. This perspective highlights the delicate balance the show attempts to strike between its epic fantasy world and the inclusion of contemporary cultural figures.

Season 7, Episode 1, ‘Dragonstone’ If you have designs on a dynasty, the first thing you have to do is get yourself a sweet map. That was one lesson from the Season 7 premiere of “Game of Thrones,” as the story’s two most powerful queens plotted on opposite sides of Blackwater Bay. But there were others: Shared leadership is best shared out of public view. Be decent to people, lest you later find their skeletons in your closet. The pursuit of knowledge can be a filthy business. And if your cantankerous uncle unexpectedly invites you to a festive party, with lots of toasting and wine, maybe let him take the first sip. [Want exclusive “Game of Thrones” explainers as well as the internet’s best articles on this week’s episode? Sign up for our Season 7 newsletter here.] Sunday’s episode, largely picking up where the Season 6 finale left off, sketched out the contours of the clashes to come, suggesting some possible paths of convergence and identifying free agents who will fill in the gaps.

Game of Thrones, a series renowned for its intricate plotlines and dramatic intensity, has rarely been associated with outright comedy. However, the premiere episode of Season 7, titled "Dragonstone," defied this convention, delivering an unexpected abundance of humorous moments that surpassed those seen in many previous seasons. This comedic shift can likely be attributed to the writing team of David Benioff and Dan Weiss, who also penned the memorable and witty "Kissed By Fire" episode from Season 3. Perhaps, with the absence of immediate character deaths or severe torture in this particular installment, the showrunners felt at liberty to inject a dose of levity before the inevitable descent into peril.

The episode commenced with a darkly humorous sequence. Arya Stark, masterfully impersonating the deceased Walder Frey, orchestrated the poisoning of his remaining kin. This act of vengeance was a direct response to their complicity in the Red Wedding, a brutal event that claimed the lives of Arya's mother, brother, and numerous allies. While the internet buzzed about Ed Sheeran's cameo, the amusement derived from it was more a reaction to his presence rather than genuine comedic appreciation. The majority of the episode's genuine chuckles, however, stemmed from the interactions between Cersei, Jaime, and the newly introduced Euron Greyjoy. Euron, sporting a look that suggested a recent consultation with Captain Hook from "Once Upon a Time" for style advice, made a grand entrance. His primary objective appeared to be impressing Cersei, which he attempted by delivering pointed barbs at Jaime. "Ever since I was a little boy, I wanted to grow up and marry the most beautiful woman in the world, so here I am with a thousand ships and two good hands," he declared with a smirk, ostentatiously displaying his intact fingers to a visibly irked Jaime. This jab was followed by another when Cersei questioned Euron's trustworthiness, citing his fratricide. Euron's gleeful response, "You should try it, it feels wonderful," elicited a priceless reaction from Jaime.

Beyond the dialogue, the episode also found humor in the extended montage depicting Samwell Tarly's arduous and rather unpleasant duties at the Citadel, specifically his task of shoveling excrement. While the visual and olfactory aspects were undeniably gross, the escalating rhythm of the cuts and the almost musical quality of the slopping liquids contributed to a surprisingly spectacular, albeit revolting, comedic effect. The show's strength in character development was further highlighted by moments that elicited laughter without a single word being spoken. The interaction between Tormund and Brienne, where Tormund's persistent, almost predatory advances were met with Brienne's weary resignation, provided a subtle yet effective comedic beat.

However, the standout comedic performance of the night belonged to Sansa Stark. Her sharp and dismissive responses to Petyr Baelish showcased a newfound assertiveness. When Littlefinger, in his characteristic intrusive manner, inquired, "What do you want that you do not have?" Sansa retorted with the cool, withering disdain of her mother, "At the moment, peace and quiet." Her ability to effortlessly shut down his subsequent attempts to re-engage in conversation, by stating, "No need to seize the last word, Lord Baelish, I'll assume it was something clever," demonstrated a remarkable command of wit. This display of sass proved to be a welcome addition to the series, suggesting that "Game of Thrones" could indeed benefit from embracing more such moments of sharp, character-driven humor.

The highly anticipated East Coast premiere of *Game of Thrones* season seven was marred by significant technical difficulties affecting HBO's digital infrastructure. Viewers eager to watch the popular series faced considerable challenges accessing the content. HBO.com, the network's primary website, experienced a complete outage. This prevented many fans from streaming the show as the White Walkers, a central threat in the series, loomed large on screen.

Those attempting to navigate to HBO.com were met with frustrating error messages, indicating a failure to load the page. Fortunately, the website was eventually restored to full functionality. This restoration occurred during the latter half of the episode's premiere broadcast.

The technical issues were not confined to the website alone. HBO's over-the-top streaming service, HBO Now, also encountered problems. Some viewers reported that this service was unavailable during the initial portion of the episode. Consequently, for a segment of the audience, the highly anticipated new installment, which was made available simultaneously across all time zones, proved inaccessible. User reports described a persistent loading bar or agonizingly slow buffering speeds, severely hindering their ability to watch the premiere.

Understandably, frustrated fans turned to social media platforms, particularly Twitter, to voice their discontent. Many shared images of their television screens, lamenting their inability to stream the highly anticipated episode. However, other users on Twitter indicated they were successfully viewing the episode via HBO Now. This suggested that the problem, while significant for some, was not universally experienced.

A spokesperson for HBO provided a statement to *The Hollywood Reporter*, offering clarification. She explained that HBO Go was experiencing outages specifically in Latin America on Sunday. She confirmed that the technical team in that region was actively engaged in resolving these issues. Crucially, she added that HBO had not observed any widespread outages affecting either HBO Go or HBO Now within the United States.

The premiere of *Game of Thrones* season seven had been particularly anticipated. This heightened expectation stemmed from a delayed production schedule, which had pushed back the show's return to television. HBO Now, launched in 2015 by HBO, offers a standalone streaming option. It is available for $15 per month and requires no cable subscription. HBO Go, conversely, is provided as a benefit to existing cable subscribers.

This incident is not the first time HBO's streaming services have faltered during a major event. Several past incidents highlight recurring challenges with their digital delivery:

*   **2016:** HBO Now encountered similar outages during the broadcast of the pivotal "Battle of the Bastards" episode from *Game of Thrones*' sixth season.
*   **2014:** HBO Go experienced a critical crash during the finale of the first season of the critically acclaimed series *True Detective*.
*   **2014:** HBO Go also experienced widespread problems during the premiere of *Game of Thrones* season four, impacting many viewers.

Notably, HBO Go did not appear to suffer the same widespread outages as HBO Now on Sunday evening.

The seventh season of *Game of Thrones* features the return of beloved characters. These include Jon Snow, portrayed by Kit Harington, and Daenerys Targaryen, played by Emilia Clarke. This season is set to run for a condensed seven episodes. It will precede the series' conclusion with its final season.

*Update: July 16, 6:54 p.m. This report has been updated to reflect that HBO.com resumed service and to incorporate an official response from HBO.*

The season seven premiere of HBO's *Game of Thrones* featured a scene that drew immediate comparisons to the *Harry Potter* series, sparking considerable fan discussion. During the episode, Samwell Tarly, portrayed by John Bradley, requested access to the "restricted area of the library" at the Citadel to further his studies. Archmaester Ebrose, played by Jim Broadbent in his *Game of Thrones* debut, denied Sam's request.

This moment strongly resonated with *Harry Potter* fans, who recognized the parallel to Hogwarts' own "Restricted Section." This area in the *Harry Potter* books and films contained forbidden texts on dark magic. It was famously where Harry Potter researched Horcruxes, the artifacts used by Lord Voldemort to conceal pieces of his soul.

Adding to the connection, fans recalled that in *Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince*, a young Tom Riddle (Voldemort) also sought entry to "the Restricted Section of the library" from Professor Slughorn. The striking link lies in the fact that both Archmaester Ebrose and Professor Slughorn are played by the same acclaimed actor, Jim Broadbent.

This perceived homage to the *Harry Potter* universe generated significant buzz online, with social media platforms abuzz with fan reactions.

*   Many fans expressed their delight, with one widely shared tweet exclaiming, "Sam literally just became Harry Potter... talking to Slughorn and trying to get into the restricted section of the library. #gameofthrones"
*   Another user humorously observed the actor's recurring role, noting, "Poor Jim Broadbent, everywhere he is, people are asking him about the restricted section in the library #GameOfThrones meets #HarryPotter."
*   The visual similarity was also a popular talking point: "Sam going into the restricted area at the Citadel is straight out of Harry Potter! #GameOfThrones."

The *Game of Thrones* season seven premiere, titled 'Dragonstone,' also included a cameo by Ed Sheeran, which held particular interest for followers of George R.R. Martin's original book series. In other premiere-related news, one fan undertook an analysis of character screen time to identify the show's most pivotal character. The episode was scheduled for a repeat broadcast on Sky Atlantic and NOW TV on July 17th at 9 pm.

It's been a long year-and-three-weeks since fans of HBO's mega-hit Game of Thrones said our temporary and permanent goodbyes to our friends and frenemies in King's Landing, the North, Dorne, and sundry other spots in George R.R. Martin's fictional continents of Westeros and Essos. By the end of Season Six, the show had completely outpaced all of Martin's written material; the existing sample chapters from the sixth A Song of Ice and Fire book either take place well before the opening of Season 7, or involve characters who were cut from the show (Arianne Martell, we never knew ye.) Voyager has passed the termination shock. With that, all elements of Season Seven, Episode 1, “Dragonstone” are a surprise, even for book readers. Here are eight favorites: “Tell them the North remembers. Tell them winter came for House Frey.” There’s no suspense on the demise of House Frey. Arya poisons all the men at once in the cold open, using a dose of the strangler and “the finest Arbor gold-proper wine for proper heroes.” The Frey children should have been suspicious when the irascible Lord Walder praised them. Welcome to the Map, Oldtown For the first time, Oldtown appears on the map, and it’s adorable, with its flaming lighthouse tower. It’s always a fun moment when they add one more thing to the map. Also, the camera fails to cross the narrow sea. It’s a good sign that we are done with Essos. “I don’t need your permission to defend the North.” That’s what fierce little lady Lady Lyanna Mormont sticks it to the lords once again when they object to Jon’s orders that the Northern women ages 10 to 60 be trained to fight the Wight Army alongside the men. She’s formidable, and a delight. Euron’s Black Ships Euron built that armada pretty darn quick, but it’s stunning, all black and silver. Euron, in a jab at Jaime, offers Cersei “a thousand ships, and two good hands.” His boats are incredible to look at, although it’s curious that he could have built a thousand majestic ships in the time elapsed since Yara and Theon stole his last fleet towards the end of Season 6. More ‘Shipping With Brienne and Tormund Tormund and Brienne have rarely interacted. It's cool that he’s into her martial prowess. Mostly, Tormund gazes lasciviously at her, while she rolls her eyes skeptically in return. Tormund directly signaled his attraction last season by gnawing lustily on what looked like the roasted rib bone of some large, dead animal. And a thousand memes were borne. It was satisfying to have more. Ed Sheeran’s Cameo Ed Sheeran shows up as a singing soldier in the Lannister army, and he and his friends share a rabbit and some blackberry wine with Arya. Thank the gods that she says “I’m going to kill the Queen” and spares them. Arya has become such a little sociopath that we might have worried for a minute. Dragonstone is a Mountain of Dragonglass Sam gets his moment of revelation after suffering through the chamberpot montage, which is strikingly shot but too gross to qualify as a cool moment on its own, as is Jorah’s scaly arm. Poor Jorah. It doesn’t look like greyscale victims have library privilege at Oldtown. He must be bored waiting for the Dragon Queen. Which leads us to… Daenarys’s Landing: “Shall We Begin?” There are many gorgeous shots in this sequence, wordless up to the last moment. It seems odd that Stannis didn’t leave any guard on Dragonstone, but that’s not worth quibbling about. We see dragons fly around Dragonstone, and Daenerys leaves a handprint in the sand on the island she was born on. She’s never been home before. Every moment feels full of connection, for her, to the family she never knew, aside from abusive Viserys. Another plus: the ever-annoying Sand Snakes make zero appearances in this episode. Their absence is welcome. Here’s something we haven’t seen yet -- another Stark sibling reunion. Remember, the only one we’ve seen so far was between Jon and Sansa in Season 6 -- and they barely exchanged a line of dialogue back in Season 1. GoT wouldn’t give us a Bran/Jon reunion so soon. That would be too much for one season premiere.

The look that launched one thousand ships. HBO The INSIDER Summary: "Game of Thrones" put Brienne and Tormund together again on Sunday's season seven premiere. Brienne is not interested. Fans of the series are. "Game of Thrones" gave fans exactly what they wanted in regard to one of the best non-couples during the season seven premiere of the HBO show Sunday. While at Winterfell, Brienne is training Podrick, her squire, in the art of sword-fighting. She continuously bests him, but she becomes distracted by Tormund Giantsbane's stares and Podrick lands a hit on her shoulder. This brings Brienne back to her senses, and she knocks Podrick to the ground. "You're a lucky man," Tormund says, giving Brienne a look of admiration. She looks annoyed and disgusted, then finishes the training session for the afternoon. Brienne, played by Gwendoline Christie, is not having it. HBO The non-existent relationship between Brienne and Tormund became one of the show's best couples — even though they aren't actually a thing — the first moment Tormund looked at Brienne. The two of them met back on season six, episode four. Tormund gives her an eye back at Castle Black and then again over a slab of meat during a meal. These looks launched a thousand ships — a term meaning fans hope that the relationship would come to fruition. This recent interaction led to the same reaction. Brienne doesn't seem at all interested in Tormund, but fans are waiting and wishing for these two to get together.

query: What was the cost of the cladding installation at Grenfell Tower, and which company carried out the work?

documents.text: The company paid £3million to install the cladding, Harley Curtain Wall Ltd, has since gone bust after one of their customers refused to pay them almost £500,000 for disputed work. The director of the company, Ray Bailey, who has since started a new firm Harley Facades Limited, said that they had used a common Aluminium Composite Material (ACM) panels which are a commonly used product in the refurbishment industry. The London Fire Brigade warned ministers that building regulations were not taking into account the vertical fire issues that cladding could cause in 1999, warning that because of cladding regulations were "inadequate". Glyn Evans from the Fire Brigades Union told a Select Committee on Environment, Transport and Regional Affairs that after the Great Fire of London only horizontal fires were considered and "we do not really recognise the problem of vertical envelopment. If you get multistorey buildings you will get fire spread up the outside if the cladding will permit it." The Health and Safety Executive, the police and the fire service are now expected to launch a large-scale investigation into the safety of the building and whether regulations were complied with.

**Grenfell Tower Fire: A Nation's Grief and the Intensifying Search for Answers**

The Grenfell Tower fire, a catastrophic inferno that consumed a west London residential block, has left the nation in profound mourning. As the devastating scale of the tragedy unfolds, the death toll has tragically climbed, with authorities issuing stark warnings that this figure is likely to rise further. The sheer ferocity of the blaze has left rescue teams with little hope of finding additional survivors within the smoldering remains of the 24-storey building in North Kensington. Specialist search dogs are now meticulously working through the debris, a painstaking effort to uncover evidence and aid in the identification of those still missing. Her Majesty The Queen has expressed her deep sorrow, stating her "thoughts and prayers" are with all those affected by this terrible event. Currently, over 30 individuals remain hospitalized, with 17 in critical condition, a grim testament to the severity of injuries sustained. Prime Minister Theresa May has unequivocally pledged a thorough and comprehensive investigation into the circumstances surrounding the fire, while Labour Leader Jeremy Corbyn has stressed the paramount importance of "the truth to come out."

### The Night of the Inferno

The fire ignited in the early hours of Wednesday morning, trapping hundreds of residents, many of whom were asleep. Awakened by neighbours or urgent shouts, a significant number managed to escape the rapidly escalating danger. Firefighters displayed extraordinary bravery, rescuing 65 individuals, though tragically, some were trapped by the swift and relentless advance of smoke and flames. London Fire Commissioner Dany Cotton confirmed that while some individuals have been identified, the full casualty count is still being painstakingly determined. She cautioned that a complete search operation could take several weeks due to the immense size and complexity of the building. Met Police Commander Stuart Cundy noted an extraordinary detail: one individual had been reported to the casualty bureau an unprecedented 46 times, underscoring the widespread concern and the potential for overlooked warnings. Commander Cotton described the ongoing search as "difficult and painstaking," a sentiment echoed by all involved.

### Scrutiny on Refurbishment and Building Regulations

Urgent questions are being raised about the fire's rapid and devastating spread. Reports from BBC Newsnight's Chris Cook have suggested that the cladding installed during a 2015 refurbishment, which featured a polyethylene core, may have significantly contributed to the inferno's ferocity. This type of cladding has been implicated in previous fires internationally, raising serious concerns about its use. In response to these alarming findings, the government has announced comprehensive checks on tower blocks with comparable upgrades across the country. Rydon, the construction firm responsible for the refurbishment, initially stated that the work met "all fire regulations." However, Sian Berry, housing committee chairwoman of the London Assembly, has suggested that fire risk assessments in tower blocks have become "less rigorous" over time. The absence of functioning fire alarms and sprinklers in the building has also drawn intense scrutiny. Russ Timpson of the Tall Buildings Fire Safety Network noted that while tall buildings can be constructed without sprinklers, regulations may need to be updated, and he expressed that overseas colleagues are "staggered" by the UK's practice of building tall residential structures with only a single staircase. Roy Wilsher, chair of the National Fire Chiefs Council, posited that sprinklers might not have been effective if the fire spread externally, emphasizing that current building regulations are primarily designed to contain fires within individual flats. "Clearly something's gone wrong in this case," he stated, a sentiment that resonates with the public's growing unease.

### Community Response and the Search for the Missing

Dozens of displaced residents found immediate refuge in makeshift shelters, and an overwhelming outpouring of support saw community centres inundated with donations of essential items. Notable figures, including singer Adele and Rita Ora, visited the scene to offer comfort and practical assistance to those affected. Messages of love, hope, and palpable anger have been inscribed on a wall of condolence, with some expressing that their prior safety concerns had been tragically disregarded. Kensington and Chelsea council has placed 44 households into emergency accommodation, a crucial step in providing immediate support. Appeals for information on social media continue to circulate, seeking details on those still unaccounted for. Among the missing are 12-year-old Jessica Urbano Ramirez and 66-year-old Tony Disson. Labour MP David Lammy is actively seeking information about his friend, Khadija Saye, and her mother, Mary Mendy, highlighting the personal impact of this tragedy. An emergency hotline, 0800 0961 233, has been established to assist those seeking information or to report missing persons.

### Harrowing Survivor Accounts

Heart-wrenching accounts from survivors are emerging, painting a vivid and terrifying picture of the night's events. One local resident described witnessing desperate screams of children, noting a family with five young children under the age of 10 is still missing. Michael Paramasivan, who resided on the seventh floor, defied official advice to remain in his flat, instead fleeing with his family through smoke-filled corridors. He recounted, "If we had stayed in that flat, we would've perished." Witnesses have reported seeing a baby thrown from a window in a desperate bid for survival and individuals attempting to descend the building using makeshift ropes. Jody Martin recounted his pleas for evacuation being met with the devastating response, "We can't leave our apartments, the smoke is too bad on the corridors." These personal testimonies underscore the sheer terror and the desperate choices faced by residents during the Grenfell Tower fire.

06:41 Missing following the fire at Grenfell Tower in west London: Clockwise from top left: Khadija Saye, Mariem Elgwahry, Ali Yawar Jafari, and Jessica Urbano Ramiez. Composite: Social media/Handout/PA Here’s what we know about those people still missing. Khadija Saye Saye, 24, is a photographer who recently exhibited her work at the Venice Biennale, and is believed to have been living in a flat with her mother, Mary Mendy. David Lammy, MP for Tottenham, a friend, has appealed for information, tweeting to say the young photographer was a “dear friend, a beautiful soul and emerging artist”. Saye was last heard from at 3am when she messaged on Facebook that she was unable to get out of the flat as the smoke was so thick. Mary Mendy, 53, has been registered as living in the tower block for at least 20 years. She is believed to have been living on the 17th floor of the building. Jessica Urbano Jessica, 12, lived on the 20th floor and became separated from her family. Ana Ospina, a makeup artist, said she had visited a number of hospitals trying to find out about her niece. She was also reported to have been put into an ambulance. Another aunt, Sandra, told the Mirror “somebody gave her a phone and she rang her mum at 1.29am and at 1.39am”. She was believed to be a with a group of people on the fire stairs. Farah Hamdan, her husband Omar Belkadi and six-month-old daughter Leena Belkadi The family, including two other children who have since been found, were on the 20th floor. Farah called her sister at around 1am. She was told by the family to leave, but said she had been told to “stay put”. Her cousin, Adel Chaoui, said the two children found were in hospital, but one was in a coma. “No one knows what will happen. The other one is traumatised and sedated but otherwise OK.” Mariem Elgwahry Friends appealed for information about the 27-year-old, who is believed to have been living on the 19th floor. One woman posted she had last been heard from at 2.30am and was with her mother when she called. According to records, a man called Ahmed Elgwahry was also living in the property. Abdulaziz Wahabi, wife Faouzia, and children Nurhouda, Yassin and Medhi All lived on the 21st floor of the tower, according to Wahabi’s sister, Hana, who spoke to him on the night. “He said he had been told to stay inside, stay in one room together and put towels under the door. I told him to leave.” But he said there was too much smoke. “The last time I saw him they were waving out the window. The last time I spoke to his wife, he was on the phone to the fire brigade.” Ali Yawar Jafari His son, Hamid Ali Jafari, appealed on Twitter for information. He said the 82-year-old was living on the 10th floor of the building. He said his father had lost contact with his mother and sister who lived with him. “He was with my mother and sister in the lift and she said the lift stopped on the 10th floor and he said there was too much smoke and he couldn’t breathe and he got out of the lift and then the door shut and it didn’t stop again till the ground floor,” he told the Telegraph. Mohamednur Tucca He is missing along with his wife Amalahmedin and his three-year-old daughter after visiting relatives. His manager, Mike Morgan, chief executive of the Soho-based PR agency the Red Consultancy, told Sky News: “Mo is part of the family. He will have been with us for 10 years next month and is due to be awarded his decade milestone. We all just want to hear he is OK.” Gloria Trevisan and Marco Gottardi The young Italian couple recently moved into the 23rd floor of the block. “I don’t have news from them since last night and their mobile phones are off,” Gottardi’s cousin wrote on Facebook. “I’m praying to God they make it out safely.” Trevisan, 26, called her mother in Italy at roughly 3.30am to say their building was ablaze, according to the Italian newspaper Corriere del Veneto. They have not been heard from since. Raymond “Moses” Bernard A family friend told the Press Association that Raymond Bernard, 65, known to friends as Moses, was missing. He was registered at flat 201, along with Karen Bernard, 53. They had lived there since at least 2003. Rania Ibrham Believed to be the mother of two small children, aged three and five, the 30-year-old uploaded a Facebook Live video as she appeared to be stuck at the top of the tower block, and has not been heard from by friends since. In the footage, she can be seen seeking help in the smoke-filled corridor before going back into her home and looking down on the street below from her balcony. Her husband was reportedly not at home because he is on holiday. Hesham Rahman Noha Baghdady shared a picture of her brother Hesham Rahman, 57, on social media, saying he lived on the 20th floor. His nephews were searching hospitals. Sky News reported that his last contact with family was at around 3am when he told them he could smell smoke. He reportedly has diabetes, making it difficult for him to walk down stairs. Dennis Murphy Stevan Racz shared a post seeking information about his uncle Dennis Murphy, who he said had called while trapped on the 14th floor of the building. “We haven’t heard anything since.” Tony Disson His son Lee Disson posted on Facebook to say: “If anyone has seen my dad, Tony Disson, could they let us know.” He is believed to have been living in flat 194. Another relative tweeted: “My cousin is desperate to trace his dad. Tony Disson. He lived on the 10th floor. We are hearing terrible news from neighbours.” The 66-year-old lived with his wife Cordelia. Mohamed “Saber” Neda A friend of Neda, Ariana Neumann Rodger, posted on Facebook that “his family are severely injured and in hospital and desperate to find out where he is”. Zia Popal, who described him as his uncle, posted: “He was on the top floor of the building trying to help [people] and went missing since.” He added that none of the hospitals had him registered. Zainab Dean Francis Dean, 47, said his sister Zainab had called him to say firefighters had instructed her to remain in her 14th-floor flat, with her two-year-old son Jeremiah. That was in the early hours of Wednesday. He said he feared the worst. Sheila Smith Adam Smith appealed for information about his 84-year-old mother, Sheila. She is believed to have been living at flat 132 in the building. Nadia Choucair Council records showed that Nadia and Malak Choucair were living in flat 193. Nadia, 33, worked as a nursery officer at the Avondale Park primary school. She is understood to have lived on one of the top floors with her husband, three daughters and mother-in-law. A resident, called Hulya, who lives close by told the Telegraph: “Nadia’s daughter rang a friend saying: ‘I don’t think I’m going to make it.’ It’s heartbreaking.” Nura Jamel Friends said she had contacted them from the building as the fire took hold. According to records, Jamel was living at flat 192. Hibo Yussuf, 35, a friend of the missing woman, told the Telegraph: “We’ve heard that Nura’s daughter is in hospital but hasn’t been able to get hold of her mum. Nura called one of her friends to say: ‘Forgive me, the fire is here, I’m dying.’”

In the wake of the Grenfell Tower fire, a powerful testament to London's resilience and community spirit emerged as hundreds of residents formed a human chain to manage an unprecedented surge of donations. This spontaneous initiative, documented by various news outlets and observed by participants, highlighted the profound generosity and solidarity that can arise even in the most tragic circumstances.

The human chain, a diverse assembly of individuals, worked with remarkable efficiency to sort and distribute essential items. These donations, which poured in from across the city and beyond, included:

*   **Personal Care Items:** Toiletries, hygiene products, and sanitary supplies.
*   **Clothing:** A wide range of apparel for all ages.
*   **Food and Water:** Non-perishable food items and bottled water to meet immediate needs.

The sheer volume of public support was so immense that, as reported by sources like the BBC, some collection points were temporarily overwhelmed and had to pause accepting further contributions. This situation underscored the critical need for organized distribution, which the human chain effectively addressed.

Key elements of this community-driven response included:

*   **Spontaneous Organization:** Volunteers, without formal direction, quickly self-organized into a functional human chain.
*   **Efficient Logistics:** The chain enabled the rapid and systematic movement of goods from drop-off points to sorting areas.
*   **Overwhelming Generosity:** The scale of donations reflected a deep well of empathy and a desire to help those affected by the fire.

LBC presenter Matthew Stadlen, who was present and participated in the human chain, captured the sentiment, describing the scene as "London at its very best in truly tragic circumstances." He further elaborated on the broad participation, noting that "people from every walk of life have been rallying around. There has been an enormous outpouring of human kindness of empathy as well." The Grenfell Tower, a 24-story residential building comprising approximately 120 flats, was home to an estimated 400 residents. The collective effort to manage the donations, as observed by numerous witnesses and reported by organizations like the Red Cross, served as a powerful symbol of the deep-seated community bonds that were forged and strengthened during this profoundly difficult period.

## Grenfell Tower Fire: Questions Mount Over Safety and Speed of Blaze

A devastating fire ripped through Grenfell Tower in west London on Wednesday, June 14, 2017, claiming at least 12 lives and leaving residents and community leaders demanding answers. The ferocity and speed with which the flames engulfed the 24-story public housing complex have raised urgent questions about fire safety protocols and the materials used in recent renovations.

### The Unprecedented Speed of the Fire

Eyewitness accounts and extensive video footage depict flames climbing the exterior of Grenfell Tower with alarming rapidity. "I've never seen a fire like that in my life," stated Joe Ruane, former deputy chief fire officer for U.S. Air Force bases in Britain, emphasizing the unusual nature of the blaze in a residential building. Fire and police officials have yet to pinpoint the exact cause, but investigators are scrutinizing the materials used in the building's recent £10 million ($12.8 million) refurbishment, which included the installation of insulated exterior cladding and double-glazed windows. Ruane suggested that the rapid spread indicates multiple fire protection safeguards failed, rather than a single issue.

### The "Stay Put" Policy Under Scrutiny

A key point of contention is the building's fire safety policy, which, according to a 2014 newsletter from the Kensington and Chelsea Tenant Management Organization (KCTMO), advised residents to "stay put" in their apartments during a fire unless the blaze was in their immediate vicinity or they were instructed to evacuate. This policy was reportedly based on the building's design adhering to "rigorous fire-safety standards," including new apartment doors designed to withstand fire for 30 minutes. While effective in smaller incidents, this strategy proved tragically inadequate as the Grenfell Tower fire rapidly breached apartment doors and filled escape routes with smoke and flames. The London Fire Brigade, arriving within six minutes of the initial reports, found themselves unable to reach residents on higher floors.

### A History of Ignored Warnings

Survivors and community groups have pointed to a pattern of ignored warnings regarding fire safety at Grenfell Tower. The Grenfell Action Group, a local community organization, had been raising concerns about fire hazards since 2013 through blog posts detailing issues with fire-fighting equipment maintenance and blocked emergency access. "All our warnings fell on deaf ears, and we predicted that a catastrophe like this was inevitable and just a matter of time," the group stated on Wednesday.

Furthermore, the KCTMO, which manages Grenfell Tower and other properties for the Kensington and Chelsea Council, has a documented history of fire safety citations. An October 2015 arson fire at Adair Tower, another KCTMO-managed building, resulted in the London Fire Brigade issuing an enforcement notice for improvements to fire safety in staircases and the installation of self-closing devices on apartment doors. Similar notices were issued for Hazelwood Tower.

### Parallels to Past Tragedies

The Grenfell Tower disaster bears a disturbing resemblance to the 2009 Lakanal House fire, where a fast-moving blaze in a 14-story public housing project killed six people. A coroner's inquest into the Lakanal House fire identified a series of failures and made recommendations to prevent future incidents. Jim Fitzpatrick, a former firefighter and Member of Parliament, noted that investigators will need to examine whether these recommendations were implemented at Grenfell Tower. "These will be matters for the scientists and the engineers... to find out exactly how the fire started, why it spread so quickly and what could have been done to prevent it," Fitzpatrick told Sky News.

In response to the tragedy, the Kensington and Chelsea Council has pledged a full investigation and public accounting. The UK government has also initiated checks on tower blocks undergoing similar renovations to those at Grenfell Tower. The KCTMO stated it is cooperating with investigators and taking all tenant concerns seriously, which will be part of the forthcoming investigations.

I went to the kitchen, grabbed a tea towel, made it wet and I covered my face. “I’ve been living there for 34 years, I know where the fire exit is. So I went out quickly into the hallway, it was pitch black with the smoke. “I was lucky because when I opened the fire escape there were two men going down. They put me on their shoulders so I didn’t have to walk. It was amazing.” Ms Ghavimi said she still does not know the identities of the two men who rescued her. After escaping the building the grandmother was reunited with her son Dariush, who had been searching for her among the crowds. She said: “I was sitting on the pavement looking at the building. I saw my flat burning. “I turned around and it was Dariush. He was holding me for so long. I’m just lucky.” The tower, which is managed by Kensington and Chelsea Tenant Management Organisation on behalf of the council, has no external fire escape or sprinkler system, and only one internal staircase. With the lifts out of action during a fire, this was the only escape route. Many residents, including 35-year-old father Mohammed Rasoul, said they did not hear any internal alarms when the blaze broke out. He said: “I was half-awake, I could smell smoke and hear the fire sirens.
