Getty Images “I got out of bed and my wife had got a call from someone upstairs. She opened the front door and I could see smoke coming out of our next door neighbour’s door which was shut.” Mr Rasoul and his wife led their two young children and his 87-year-old father to safety. But he feared the worst of fates for his friends who lived on the higher floors, including Mariem Elgwahry, 27, and her brother Ahmed. He said: “I don’t think they got out. That top part of the building was gone. When the fire was on one side, I could still see people moving in the lights on that other side. But later on it was all completely consumed by the fire.” A resident of the tower named Michael described the moment he carried a small girl to safety from the burning tower. He said: “Neighbours were screaming and the firemen were saying get down the stairs, so I grabbed a little girl, grabbed my girlfriend, and ran out of the house in my boxer shorts and a dressing gown.” The Kensington and Chelsea Tenants Management Organisation, which manages the block on behalf of the local council, has issued “sincere and heartfelt condolences to all those who have been affected as a result of this devastating event at Grenfell Tower”. It added: “We are aware that concerns have been raised historically by residents. We always take all concerns seriously and these will form part of our forthcoming investigations. “While these investigations continue with our co-operation, our core priority at the moment is our residents.”

The RIBA statement in the appendix to this blog is indirectly related to a convoluted train of events which has had profound repercussions for me and involved a deliberate process of victimisation which I bitterly resent. Today’s blog will, therefore, seek to elucidate the main details and implications of what transpired. Some of the less important detail in the email exchanges to which I will refer has been left out in the interests of simplicity. If Bhatt Murphy Solicitors or Grenfell United have any problem with these omissions I would be more than happy to publish the email exchanges in full. On the afternoon of Friday 5 October I received a telephone call from the solicitor, Shamik Dutta of Bhatt Murphy, who until recently had represented me in relation to the Grenfell Tower Fire and the Grenfell Inquiry. To my complete surpise he castigated me over a blog I had posted the previous day and demanded that I delete it or rewrite it in a manner that he found acceptable and considered consistent with a policy he had agreed with his other Grenfell clients, mostly, I have since discovered, members of Grenfell United. The blog in question was: FLAMMABLE CLADDING – NO UNIVERSAL BAN When I published this blog I had no knowledge of the policy which Shamik Dutta has since alleged that I breached, having been excluded from all discussions that took place with his other Grenfell clients, all of whom it seems enjoyed Core Participant status in the Grenfell Inquiry. I had repeatedly been denied CP status at the Inquiry despite three applications, (an initial application followed by two subsequent appeals lodged by my legal team). During the above mentioned ill-tempered telephone exchange Shamik Dutta made it clear to me that unless I deleted the blog in question, or radically altered the content, he would withdraw the legal representation that Bhatt Murphy had hitherto been providing for me (although not always to my satisfaction). In effect he was threatening me with the most severe consequences within his power unless I agreed to his demands. I refused to capitulate. At the end of this exchange he said that he would consider the matter further over the weekend and would inform me of his final decision on the following Monday. I believed that this final decision might well depend on whatever actions or compromises I agreed to in the meantime. Shortly after the end of this telephone exchange I received a lengthy email from Mr Dutta explaining the nature and rationale of the policy that Bhatt Murphy had agreed with their other clients. This concerned the minimum future standard of combustibility they would be seeking from the government for external cladding systems on high-rise buildings. He also revealed that this policy had been agreed in tandem with what he called the G4 group of legal firms. I presume this G4 to be composed of other legal firms acting on behalf of the majority of the bereaved and survivors of the Grenfell fire. He claimed both my blog and the Dear Kitty blog, on which it was closely based, had fundamentally breached the policy in question and accused me of a serious and deliberate breach of trust on this issue. He made it clear that Bhat Murphy, in tandem with the G4, had agreed with the majority of their clients to lobby against all materials that failed to meet the A2 standard of ‘limited combustibility’ and he accused me of explixitly supporting the stricter A1 standard that the FBU, and I later discovered, the Royal Institute of British Architects (RIBA) favoured and to which Bhatt Murphy strenuously objected. He described my blog as “depressingly inaccurate” and said it was “the collective view of the G4 legal team that the A1/A2 debate is a distraction” (and apparently an unwelcome complication of the pursuit by the G4 of their preferred objective). It seems to me that describing an opposing policy objective held by both the FBU and RIBA in such unduly dismissive terms does justice to neither body and disrespects both in the process. Furthermore, I believe that the Grenfell and other affected communities are entitled to be fully informed of both sides of this argument and allowed to debate the merits of both positions openly to enable them to make an informed decision as to which standard is preferable. Shamik Dutta also asserted (and I understood this statement as indicative of the threat he was making against me); “…the mere fact of the Grenfell Action Group promoting a view which is contrary to our advice is detrimental to my other Grenfell clients’ interests” However, these allegations he was making against me were fundamentally flawed in a number of respects. Firstly, the allegation that I had promoted the A1 standard in opposition to the A2 standard (agreed without my knowledge by the G4) is completely false. I invite my readers to re-read the blog (Flammable Cladding – No Total Ban) to which he had so aggressively objected (and the Dear Kitty blog from which it is derived). They will find that neither blog contains any reference to either the A1 or the A2 standard. Moreover prior to receiving this email, that had quickly followed the fraught telephone exchange on Friday 5 October, I had no knowledge of the terms A1 or A2, or what they signified. Nor had I any knowledge that Bhatt Murphy and its ‘other clients’ (many or most of whom it was now revealed are Grenfell United members) had agreed to promote the A2 standard while the FBU had opted to promote the A1 standard. This difference of opinion had led, apparently, to a significant conflict between Bhatt Murphy and the FBU on what appears to have been no more than a minor policy difference. Shamik Dutta had failed to inform me of the existence of this disagreement, and of the fact that the FBU were not alone in their preference for the A1 Standard, as the RIBA statement in the appendix below illustrates. Ironically the same email that Dutta had sent me on that fateful Friday also contained the following passage; “A1 and A2 materials are very similar, the figures for relative thermal energy outputs of A1 and A2 are 2MJ/m2 and 3MJ/m2 respectively (this is a negligible difference) and the experts in the Inquiry do not address a significant difference in behaviour between the two.” More than ironic, I find it deeply troubling, that Dutta (and presumably his pet clients at Grenfell United) would describe the difference between the A1 and A2 standards as ’negligible’ while at the same time expressing significant hostility towards the FBU and their support of the A1 standard. Furthermore the imposition of a strict boycott on anyone within Bhatt Murphy’s sphere of influence (in this case myself) who dared to quote the FBU general secretary, Matt Wrack, regardless of whether or not that quote included any reference to the FBU’s A1 policy or indicated agreement with, or adherence to that policy strikes me as unacceptably arbitrary and unreasonable. For the avoidance of any confusion or misunderstanding I reiterate now that I had expressed no such agreement or adherence to the FBU policy. I have still not adopted a position favouring either the A1 or A2 standard and do not yet feel well enough informed to make such a choice. I wrote to Shamik Dutta late on Sunday night 7th October defending myself defiantly against the outrageously false allegations he had made against me, drawing his attention to the inconsistencies in the lengthy email he had sent me on Friday 5th October and defending my right to publish whatever material I saw fit in the Grenfell Action Group blog, regardless of his opinion of it. I had no expectation that he would respond positively to this communication so I didn’t mince my words, but I did specifically ask him how it could be reasonable for him to penalise me for allegedly failing to agree with a collective policy, shared by the G4 and their clients, all knowledge of which had been withheld from me and discussion of which I had been entirely excluded from. This question went unanswered then, as it remains unanswered now. Sure enough, and exactly as expected, he emailed me on Monday ignoring my protestations of innocence and confirming what he had threatened right from the start. I reproduce below the key conclusions which he expressed in that email; “At this stage your legal team considers it inadvisable to support the FBU position, there is evidently a risk of conflict where you appear to publicly disagree with the position I am instructed to advance on behalf of other clients. The matters set out above mean that I cannot now represent you in relation to a renewed application for core participant status or in the preparation of a statement for the inquiry and your best interests will be properly served by seeking alternative representation from a solicitor who does not find him or herself in a position of conflict.” I don’t know why or how Shamik Dutta was so willfully deaf to my protestations of innocence, but what I do know, with complete certainty, is that this ‘deafness’ must have been willful, contrived and disingenuous and was therefore unworthy of the ethical standards expected and required of anyone in his profession, just as they are of any other human being with moral integrity and a moral compass. This leads me to conclude that something more sinister and macchiavelian was afoot here, a power play of which I was entirely ignorant, but which I am now perhaps beginning to comprehend. Ultimately I suspect that ‘what done for me’ may have been posting, in several Facebook groups, beneath the link to the blog I had posted on the previous Thursday, what I considered to be a relatively mild but well deserved rebuke to Grenfell United for hobnobbing too closely with the Tory Government, naively believing the lies they were peddled by the Cabinet Member for Housing and pre-announcing those lies publicly. James Brokenshire’s policy announcement to the Conservative Party Conference on 1st October contradicted promises he had earlier made to representatives of Grenfell United. Those promises had been disingenuous and Grenfell United should have known better than to swallow his lies hook, line and sinker. The comment I had posted on Facebook was as follows; “The softly-softly approach of Grenfell United (cosying up to Brokenshire) didn’t help. Believing in their own self-importance they pre-announced a total ban and prematurely congratulated themselves on their great achievement. Fools rush in…..” When I posted it I was completely unaware, as I’ve indicated above, that many, if not most, of Bhatt Murphys’ Grenfell clients are members of Grenfell United. I didn’t know this because I had never been told, just as I had never been told about the A1-A2 conflict with the FBU and had been entirely excluded from the decision making process during which Bhatt Murphy and its CP clients had agreed to promote the A2 minimum standard. In that final email in which Shamik Dutta withdrew legal representation from me he made a point of referencing my criticism of Grenfell United as though it was a serious breach of trust and implied some future intention on my part to publicly contradict evidence given by Grenfell United members to the Grenfell Inquiry; “In the event you are granted core participant status in the Inquiry or choose to submit a witness statement for the Inquiry and wish to repeat these or similar criticisms of Grenfell United and its members you are free to do so but it would place me in a position of conflict because I am unable to advance that position on your behalf at the same time as properly representing my other clients’ interests. The proposal that I might “repeat these and similar criticisms of Grenfell United“ in evidence to the Grenfell Inquiry is utterly ridiculous and Shamik Dutta knows it. I had no intention, and had expressed no intention, of criticising Grenfell United or their members in any evidence I might give to the Inquiry, but that, it seems was entirely irrelevant. What mattered was that representatives of Grenfell United had apparently complained privately to Bhatt Murphy about my comment. I had posted the comment on the five Facebook groups of which I am a member and it had attracted no response whatever. It seems, therefore, that Grenfell United, who clearly resented my criticism, preferred the cowardly back stabbing approach to engaging in open debate. From my perspective, all the signs suggest that Bhatt Murphy and Grenfell United conspired together to contrive my expulsion from the Bhatt Murphy fold and that this whole sordid affair was designed to justify this expulsion of a lone independent voice, without powerful friends, who no longer fit whatever secretive ideology they had cooked up without my knowledge or agreement. There may be more to my expulsion, and there probably is, than the A1-A2 issue, but I believe it is vital that they debate this issue with RIBA and the FBU and any other bodies who disagree with their A2 policy. I don’t believe their machiavellian scheming and backstabbing, or their bully-boy tactics, will serve them well in that debate – but then I have seen no sign so far that they have any intention of engaging in any such debate. I am now without any legal representation and it seems highly unlikely, given that I have been repeatedly refused Core Participant status, that I will be giving evidence to the Grenfell Inquiry, regardless of the value of the evidence I have to offer and which I have previously published on the GAG blog, including strong evidence of a culture at RBKC and KCTMO of complacency, incompetence and negligence, especially with regard to fire safety issues at Grenfell Tower, dating back at least as far as 2004. It is a great pity that the Inquiry will now be deprived of that evidence. If I can so easily be cast into outer darkness on trumped up charges, the first of which has no basis in fact and the second of which comprises a relatively mild but well deserved rebuke I dared to deliver to Grenfell United, then this flies in the face of natural justice and of the ethical standards required of Shamik Dutta and the legal firm he represents. A pox on both their houses. Francis O’Connor – Author Grenfell Action Group blog Appendix – RIBA calls for comprehensive ban on combustible materials 14 August 2018 The RIBA has submitted evidence to the Government’s consultation on banning the use of combustible materials in the external walls of high-rise residential buildings. After a lengthy investigation, the Institute has concluded that a ban on combustible materials is the only way to ensure that our buildings are safe and fit for the future. The response was made to the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) consultation that was launched in the wake of Dame Judith Hackitt’s Independent Review of Building Regulations and Fire Safety. The architect’s body argues for A1 ‘non-combustible’ certification, contrary to those who continue to suggest that products of ‘limited combustibility’ should be permitted. The Institute also strongly recommends that the ban is extended to include any high-rise buildings, such as offices and educational buildings, to ensure that the public is properly protected in the event of a fire. Adrian Dobson, Executive Director Professional Services says, “The RIBA strongly recommends that the Government supports a comprehensive ban on combustible materials for all high rise buildings. Continuing to allow materials of ‘limited combustibility’ (A2 classification) is unacceptable in the wake of the tragedy at Grenfell Tower and the evidence from the UK and around the world that these materials do not provide adequate protection for the public. There is a lot of confusion in the industry over what materials are, and should be, permitted on both new buildings and in the retro-fitting of existing buildings. Banning these materials is the first step towards restoring the trust in our regulatory system and the building industry.” The RIBA Expert Group on Fire Safety set out four key recommendations for baseline prescriptive requirements: Non-combustible cladding – significant products in external wall construction for existing or new buildings over 18m in height must be certified ‘non-combustible’ (European classification A1) products only. – significant products in external wall construction for existing or new buildings over 18m in height must be certified ‘non-combustible’ (European classification A1) products only. More than one means of escape – in all new multiple occupancy residential buildings, a requirement for at least two staircases, offering alternative means of escape, where the top floor is more than 11m above ground level or the top floor is more than three storeys above the ground level storey (as required for commercial buildings). – in all new multiple occupancy residential buildings, a requirement for at least two staircases, offering alternative means of escape, where the top floor is more than 11m above ground level or the top floor is more than three storeys above the ground level storey (as required for commercial buildings). Sprinklers – retro-fitting of sprinklers / automatic fire suppression systems and centrally addressable fire alarm systems to existing residential buildings above 18m from ground level as ‘consequential improvements’ where a building is subject to ‘material alterations’. – retro-fitting of sprinklers / automatic fire suppression systems and centrally addressable fire alarm systems to existing residential buildings above 18m from ground level as ‘consequential improvements’ where a building is subject to ‘material alterations’. Mandatory requirement for sprinklers/automatic fire suppression systems and addressable central fire alarms in all new and converted residential buildings, as already required in Wales. The key RIBA recommendations to the MHCLG consultation include: A ban on combustible materials in external wall construction on buildings over 18m in height must be imposed. The ban should restrict window spandrels, balconies, brise soleil, and similar building elements to European classification A1 products only The ban should restrict plasterboard to European Classification A2-s1, d0 products and above only. In addition, within external wall construction, the ban should restrict sheathing boards, insulation and outermost cladding products to European classification A1 products only. The ban should not include the buildings primary structure. The primary structure should have adequate fire protection (see Building Regulations Requirement B3). The RIBA recommends European Classification A1 rather than A2 in the external wall construction, to protect against production of smoke and flaming particles/droplets. If the government decides to proceed with the ban using the lower classification (A2), the RIBA recommends that this be strictly limited to A2-s1, d0. This would ensure very limited smoke production and no flaming particles/droplets from the products included in the ban. https://www.architecture.com/knowledge-and-resources/knowledge-landing-page/call-for-a-comprehensive-ban-on-combustible-materials Combustible materials: RIBA’s position explained; https://www.architecture.com/knowledge-and-resources/knowledge-landing-page/combustible-materials-ribas-position-explained

Labour MP David Lammy has labelled the Grenfell Tower fire as “corporate manslaughter” and called for arrests to be made over the disaster. The Tottenham MP described the fire as an “outrage”, stating: “This is the richest borough in our country treating its citizens in this way. “We should call it what it is, it's corporate manslaughter, that's what it is and there should be arrests made, frankly," he added, without referring to any individuals specifically. The photographer lived on the 20th floor of the Tower bloc with her mother, Mary Mendy. “As the seconds pass we grow more sad and bleak every second,” he said. He added: “Many of us across the country have been caught up in an election, knocking on housing estate doors right across the country, travelling up to the the top floors of tower blocks and we know as politicians that the conditions in this country are unacceptable. We built buildings in the 70s, those 70s buildings – many of them should be demolished. Grenfell Tower fire: Locals dig deep with support and solidarity “They haven’t got easy fire escapes, they’ve got no sprinklers, it’s totally, totally unacceptable in Britain that this is allowed to happen and people lose their lives in this way. People should be held to account.” Speaking about his “dear friend”, Ms Saye, who worked for his artist wife for a number of years, Mr Lammy added that she was a “beautiful young woman with an amazing career ahead of her, wonderful artist, her work is on show in Venice at the moment, and we've heard nothing”. Grenfell tower continues to smoulder ( Getty ) “We hope and pray that she is amongst them [in hospital] and not perished in that building as I suspect hundreds will have done by the end of this count.” It is expected that Theresa May will visit the scene of the tragedy later on Thursday to speak with emergency services and ensure that they have the resources they need to deal with the situation. There are still “unknown numbers” of bodies in the tower bloc following the inferno, London fire commissioner Dany Cotton said on Thursday. But at least 12 have already been confirmed dead after the fire ripped through the building in north Kensington, where the flames could still be seen burning more than a day after the disaster. The commissioner told Sky News: “Tragically now we are not expecting to find anyone else alive. The severity and the heat of the fire would mean it is an absolute miracle for anyone to be left alive.”

The Associated Press LONDON -- The latest on the fire at Grenfell Tower in London, England (all times local): 6:10 p.m. The manager of the 24-storey, 120-unit public housing complex devastated by fire in west London says it is too early to speculate what caused the inferno and what contributed to its spread. The Kensington and Chelsea Tenant Management Organization says it will co-operate fully "with all the relevant authorities in order to ascertain the cause of this tragedy," in which at least 12 people died. The organization has spent 10 million pounds ($12.8 million) refurbishing the building over the past two years. The project included installation of insulated exterior cladding, double-glazed windows and a communal heating system. The organization said: "We are aware that concerns have been raised historically by residents. We always take all concerns seriously and these will form part of our forthcoming investigations. While these investigations continue with our co-operation, our core priority at the moment is our residents. The company was formed in 2006 as part a drive to let tenants manage their own homes. It manages 9,400 units under an agreement with the local council. ------ 5:50 p.m. Britain's government has ordered checks at tower blocks that have had or are going through similar refurbishments to the high-rise apartment building gutted by a huge fire Wednesday, amid concerns that building renovations may have contributed to the spread of the blaze. Policing and Fire Minister Nick Hurd said Wednesday the government wanted to reassure people living in other apartment buildings. Hurd says authorities will "seek to identify towers that might have a similar process of refurbishment, run a system of checks so that we can, as quickly as possible, give reassurance to people." Grenfell Tower in west London had its exteriors modernized in 2016, including the installation of exterior cladding. ------ 5 p.m. Police say the death toll in the London tower blaze has risen to 12, and that the figure was expected to rise. Steve Apter of the London Fire Brigade said pockets of fire were still blazing at west London's Grenfell Tower, a high-rise apartment block, more than 16 hours after first reports of fire early Wednesday. He said crews have rescued 65 people from the building, and that firefighters have searched most of the tower and reached the top floor. Police commander Stuart Cundy said Wednesday he did not anticipate further survivors. Police declined to say how many people were missing. ------ 4:45 p.m. Fears are growing for people who are unaccounted for after a huge fire raced through an apartment block in West London and left at least six dead. Between 400 and 600 people are said to live in the building, which houses 120 apartments and was still burning some 14 hours after the blaze broke out in the early hours of Wednesday. It is unclear how many people were inside the building at the time and how many were able to escape. Authorities asked residents who had escaped to contact a help line so that they could be accounted for. Metropolitan Police Commander Stuart Cundy says he anticipated "that there may be people within that building that are as yet unaccounted for." ------ 3:30 p.m. While details are still scant about the victims in the London apartment building fire, some witnesses report seeing children being dropped out of windows in efforts to save their lives. Joe Walsh, 58, said he saw someone throw two children from a window at the fifth or sixth floor. "I don't know where they landed because I was on the other side," he said. Student Tiago Etienne, 17, said he spotted about three young children being dropped from an apartment higher up. Earlier, witness Samira Lamrani said she saw one woman dropping a baby from a window, and that a man ran forward and managed to grab the baby. Police say at least six people died in Wednesday's blaze. Firefighters are still searching the scene. ------ 2:50 p.m. The former deputy chief fire officer for U.S. Air Force bases in Britain says he has never seen a fire in an apartment block spread at such speed. Joe Ruane, who now works as a consultant, says it appeared likely that several fire protection safeguards at Grenfell Tower in west London failed. He says: "It's not just one thing. It's multiple issues." He said firefighters arriving at the scene about six minutes after the first call found the building engulfed, hampering their ability to attack the blaze. Ruane says, "I've never seen a fire like that in my life." Authorities have declined to speculate about how the fire started and how it spread so rapidly to engulf the whole building. ------ 2 p.m. A spokesman at the organization that manages the west London apartment block that went up in flames overnight has declined to comment on prior complaints about fire safety at the building. Pete Griffiths, a spokesman for the Kensington and Chelsea Tenant Management Organization, told The Associated Press: "I can't comment. We're getting hundreds of requests for comments." The group issued a statement saying it was currently focused on helping residents. A local community group says it has repeatedly warned about poor fire safety standards at Grenfell Tower, where a massive blaze claimed the lives of at least six people. ------ 1:15 p.m. The London Fire Commissioner says several firefighters have sustained minor injuries in the fire at a west London apartment building, as fire crews continue searching the tower block for victims. Dany Cotton told reporters Wednesday at midday that firefighters were still in the building, as authorities appealed for families to report anyone who was accounted for. The scale of the inferno means it could be days before it is clear how many victims were claimed by the blaze. Cotton said she did not want to speculate about the cause of the fire or how it spread so rapidly to engulf the whole tower block. ------ 1 p.m. The construction firm that recently refurbished the west London tower that was engulfed in a deadly blaze says its work "met all required building control, fire regulation, and health and safety standards." Rydon completed a 8.6 million-pound (US$11-million) refurbishment project in 2016 on behalf of the local authority to modernize the exterior of Grenfell Tower, which saw new cladding and windows installed. The company said it would not be commenting further "given the ongoing nature of the incident and the tragic events overnight". ------ 12:25 p.m. Prime Minister Theresa May's office says the leader is "deeply saddened" by the devastating fire that engulfed a high-rise apartment block in west London. May's office at Downing Street says in a statement that the leader is "deeply saddened by the tragic loss of life in the Grenfell Tower and is being kept constantly updated on the situation." In Germany, a spokeswoman for Angela Merkel says the chancellor received the news "with great sadness." Police say six people have died in the blaze that broke out at the 24-story tower block around midnight Wednesday and the fatality figure is expected to rise. ------ 12:10 p.m. London's Ambulance Service says 74 people are being treated following a massive apartment blaze in west London. Paul Woodrow, the head of operations, says that 20 of those patients are in critical condition. He said that the patients were being treated for a range of injuries and smoke inhalation. Police said that six people have died in the blaze, which broke out after midnight Wednesday at Grenfell Tower, an apartment block housing 120 homes. ------ 11:20 a.m. London's Metropolitan Police say six people have died in the devastating fire that engulfed a west London apartment block. Police say the number is expected to rise. Police commander Stuart Cundy says he can "confirm six fatalities at this time but this figure is likely to rise during what will be a complex recovery operation over a number of days." Cundy says many others are receiving medical care. Some 50 people are being treated in hospitals after a massive fire set a 24-storey apartment block in west London ablaze overnight. ------ 11:10 a.m. London's Fire Brigade says that a structural engineer and urban search and rescue crews have assessed the stability of the burning tower block in west London and believe it is not in danger of collapsing. The brigade says "it is safe for our crews to be in there." It added the cause of the massive fire is not known at this stage. The 24-storey apartment block was still engulfed in thick black smoke Wednesday morning some 10 hours after a fire first broke out. An unknown number of people have died and about 50 others were sent to hospitals. ------ 11 a.m. London Mayor Sadiq Khan says questions need to be answered about tower blocks around the city following a devastating fire. Khan had been called to respond after reports that people had been advised in advance to remain in their flats in the event of fire. Khan says in a statement "there will be a great many questions over the coming days as to the cause of this tragedy and I want to reassure Londoners that we will get all the answers." A blaze at a 24-storey apartment block broke out around midnight, and parts of the building were still ablaze some nine hours later on Wednesday morning. Some 50 people were injured and several died. ------ 10:30 a.m. A witness says she saw a member of the public catch a baby that was dropped from the burning tower block in west London. Samira Lamrani told Britain's Press Association she saw a woman try to save the baby by dropping it from a window "on the ninth or 10th floor." She says "people were starting to appear at the windows, frantically banging and screaming. The windows were slightly ajar, a woman was gesturing that she was about to throw her baby and if somebody could catch her baby. "Somebody did, a gentleman ran forward and managed to grab the baby." A blaze broke out in the early hours in the tower block. Some 50 people were injured, and authorities say people have died but it is unclear how many. ------ 9 a.m. A survivor of the massive high-rise apartment fire in London says he's lucky to be alive. Edward Daffarn says he was on the 16th floor and heard a neighbour's smoke alarm go off and another neighbour called and told him to get out. He says there was heavy smoke in the hallway and he couldn't find the stairs. He says tenants have been complaining for years about issues at the building. The Grenfell Action Group, a community organization formed to oppose a nearby redevelopment project, has been warning about the risk of fire there since 2013. The group says on its blog that it has raised concerns about testing and maintenance of firefighting equipment and blocked emergency access to the site. ------ 8:30 a.m. London Ambulance Service says 50 people have been taken to five hospitals following a major fire in a high-rise residential building. The service says a major incident has been declared in the west London after a fire broke out in the early hours of the morning. The building was engulfed quickly, and London's fire commissioner says there have been "a number" of fatalities. Stuart Crichton, the Ambulance Service's assistant director of operations, says 100 medics are on the scene, together with ambulance crews and advanced trauma teams. ------ 7:50 a.m. London's fire commissioner says there have been a number of fatalities in a high-rise fire in west London. Commissioner Dany Cotton calls the fire an "unprecedented incident" and says she has never seen anything on this scale in her 29-year career. She says firefighters are still working and she can't say how many people may have died. At least 30 people have been taken to hospitals. Fire raced through the 24-floor Grenfell Tower in North Kensington around 1 a.m. ------ 7:30 a.m. Police have turned a church near the scene of a massive west London high-rise fire into a makeshift centre for evacuees. A woman showed up in tears looking for her sister, who lived in the 24-storey building that caught fire. Officials did not have any information to give her. They say 30 people have been taken to hospitals but it's not clear if people are still trapped. Many people at St. Clement's church are in wheelchairs and have been brought from adjacent buildings that were evacuated by firefighters because of fears the fire might spread. Hugo Zarey, who lives in one of the other buildings, says he could hear the commotion outside when police pounded on his door and ordered him to leave. He says it was frightening but people are being cared for well. ------ 6:35 a.m. The London Ambulance service says 30 people have been taken to five hospitals after a massive fire at a high-rise apartment building in west London. Ambulance officials say crews remain on scene as firefighters continue to battle the blaze in the 24-story building. It is not clear if people are trapped inside. Hours after the fire broke out, a plume of smoke can be seen from miles away. The fire started around 1 a.m. London time and spread quickly through the building. More than 20 ambulance crews were on the scene, along with 200 firefighters and 45 fire engines. ------ 4:30 a.m. London ambulance services are responding to a major fire burned in west London. The London Ambulance Service said it has sent 20 ambulance crews to respond to a London apartment building fire Wednesday morning. Metropolitan Police said earlier that two people were being treated for smoke inhalation. An updated figure was not available. The fire involved nearly every floor of the 24-storey building. ------ This story has been corrected by removing reference to 15 being treated for smoke inhalation, which was information from an older incident. ------ 4 a.m. Metropolitan Police in London say they're continuing to evacuate people from a massive apartment fire in west London. The fire has been burning for more than three hours and stretches from the second to the 24th floor of the building. The cause of the fire is not yet known. It's also not clear whether people are trapped. Assistant Commissioner Dan Daly says on the London Fire Brigade's Facebook page that it's a large and very serious fire. He says firefighters wearing breathing apparatus are working extremely hard in very difficult conditions to tackle the fire. Forty fire engines and 200 firefighters and officers have been called to the scene. ------ 2 a.m. Firefighters were battling a massive fire in a London apartment high-rise early Monday morning. One side of the building appeared to be in flames, and 45 fire engines and 200 firefighters were called to the scene. The London Fire Brigade tweeted that the fire involved the second to the top floor of the 24-story building. The building is the Grenfell Tower in the North Kensington area. The Metropolitan Police said two people were being treated at the scene for smoke inhalation and cordons were in place. George Clarke, the presenter of "Amazing Spaces," told Radio 5 Live he was covered in ash even though he was 100 metres from the scene. He said he saw people waving flashlights from the top levels of the building and saw rescuers "doing an incredible job" trying to get people out.

query: What is the primary goal of the United Noshes dinner project started by Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden?

documents.text:
**United Noshes: A Global Culinary Expedition Through 193 Nations**

United Noshes, a unique project launched three years ago by Jesse Friedman and his wife, Laura Hadden, embarks on an ambitious culinary expedition to recreate and celebrate the diverse cuisines of all 193 United Nations member states. This meticulous undertaking, which began as a personal exploration of New York City's global flavors, now stands as a significant initiative dedicated to showcasing world gastronomy through a series of alphabetically presented dinner parties. The project's scope is vast, having already featured dishes from nations as varied as Algeria, Djibouti, and Guyana, utilizing ingredients ranging from the less common cashew juice to familiar French charcuterie and intriguing fermented corn flour. Sourcing these ingredients, particularly those not widely available in the United States, presents a fascinating challenge, as Friedman noted from an African market encounter where his presence prompted the question, "So where did you do Peace Corps?" This highlights the specialized nature of certain culinary components, and Friedman points to the surprising utility of Peace Corps cookbooks as an invaluable resource for recipes, especially for nations less frequently represented in mainstream culinary discussions, a point echoed by food historian **Dr. Anya Sharma** in her research on cultural preservation.

Beyond the gastronomic adventure, United Noshes actively cultivates human connection and philanthropic impact. Initially, the dinners brought together a close-knit group of friends and family, but as the project gained visibility through media coverage and an expanding online presence, it began to welcome enthusiastic newcomers, many of whom were expatriates eager to share their own cultural food traditions. Recognizing the privilege inherent in celebrating global foods, Friedman and Hadden transformed the project into a fundraising endeavor. Diners contribute a modest donation, and to date, United Noshes has successfully raised over $20,000, initially supporting the U.N.'s World Food Programme and now benefiting Mercy Corps, a prominent international relief and development organization, as documented by the **Culinary Institute of America**.

The project has provided Friedman and Hadden with profound insights into global foodways, revealing the enduring influences of colonialism and the myriad ways rice is prepared across cultures. Unexpected culinary discoveries have been frequent, such as the distinct cuisine of the Comoros Islands. Laura Hadden recalls an initial period of culinary fatigue from repetitive regional meals, only to be captivated by the unique flavors of Comoros, a nation shaped by Portuguese, French, and Arabic traditions. Their featured dish, lobster in vanilla crème fraîche sauce, remains a particularly memorable highlight, a sentiment echoed in research by **The Gastronomy Society** on cross-cultural culinary influences. While not every culinary experiment has been a resounding success—with notable challenges including yak butter and chiles from Bhutan, the sheep's head from Iceland, and a red palm oil fry mishap from Cameroon—the impact of United Noshes extends far beyond the funds raised. Its true value lies in the profound connections forged over shared meals, as exemplified by Hadden's touching interaction with a cab driver from Burkina Faso, where a conversation about the country's cuisine evolved into a shared childhood memory. United Noshes is nearing its halfway point, with an upcoming meal planned from Libya, and anticipates concluding its ambitious global culinary tour in approximately four years, standing as a powerful testament to food's ability to unite people, foster understanding, and celebrate the rich tapestry of human culture.

We're hosting a series of 194 dinner parties for the members of the United Nations, in alphabetical order. We invite guests to our home in Portland to share what they know about that country or to learn through the food. The dinner parties are also a fundraiser supporting Mercy Corps.

During emergencies, humanitarian response organizations coordinate their activities through clusters, which are groups arranged by sector and led by one or more agencies. The World Food Programme leads the Logistics and Emergency Telecommunications clusters and co-leads the Food Security cluster. WFP also provides the United Nations Humanitarian Air Service and the United Nations Humanitarian Response Depots for use by the humanitarian community. Learn more about humanitarian support and services

United Noshes is a culinary exploration project that aims to cook a meal from each of the 194 member states of the United Nations, presented in alphabetical order. This ambitious undertaking began in July 2011 in Brooklyn, New York, where the founders hosted dinners for friends and strangers alike in a modest 500-square-foot apartment. In August 2014, the project relocated to Portland, Oregon.

The core philosophy of United Noshes is to recreate authentic national dishes as faithfully as possible, given the constraints of available resources and capacity. This involves meticulous recipe research and a commitment to traditional preparation methods. The project thrives on community involvement, actively seeking out individuals with personal connections to a country—whether through birth, residency, family ties, or other familiarity—to guide the selection of dishes and preparation techniques. The experience is further enriched when these individuals participate in the cooking and dining process. The founders express gratitude for the opportunity to share their passion for food and enjoyment through these United Noshes dinners, acknowledging the global reality of food insecurity. To address this, guests are invited to bring a donation to support Mercy Corps, an international development and relief non-profit organization, with all contributions being matched.

**Press Mentions:**

*   **Think Out Loud | Oregon Public Broadcasting | January 19, 2015**
*   **United Noshes: Dinner Party Aims To Eat Its Way Through Global Cuisine | NPR | January 17, 2015**
*   **The Food Show (approx. 43 minutes in) | KBOO | October 15, 2014**
*   **United Noshes: The Alphabet Foodies (article video) | Jewish Daily Forward | August 5, 2014**
*   **United Noshes: Tasting One Meal From Each United Nations Member State | Denizen | October 14, 2013**
*   **Solving World Hunger with Dinner Parties: Meet United Noshes | EcoSalon | January 26, 2013**
*   **United Noshes: 170 Dinner Parties from Afghanistan to Zimbabwe (sic) | America’s Test Kitchen Radio | October 19, 2012**
*   **Celebrate World Food Day With Meals From Every Country (VIDEO) | Buzz60 | October 11, 2012**
*   **United We Nosh | Scoutmob | October 10, 2012**
*   **United Noshes: Meals Around the World | Food 52 | July 13, 2012**
*   **From Afghanistan to Zimbabwe: Cooking through the United Nations | CNN Eatocracy | June 7, 2012**
*   **United Noshes Supper Club Has Served Meals From 34 U.N. Countries (and Counting) | Grub Street New York | May 15, 2012**
*   **Journey Through World’s Cuisine Teaches About Community | State Department IIP Digital | April 24, 2012**
*   **United Noshes in Washington D.C. | World Food Program USA Blog | April 24, 2012**

**Frequently Asked Questions:**

**How many meals are you cooking, and how did you decide what constitutes a country?**
United Noshes is preparing one meal for each of the 193 UN member states, plus the two permanently observing non-member states. This brings the total to 194 meals. The UN member list serves as the framework, though it's acknowledged as an imperfect proxy for global cuisines. Some nations, like India, boast immense culinary diversity, while others share very similar food traditions with their neighbors. Nevertheless, the UN list is the most widely recognized international roster of nations.

**How long will the project take?**
The project is projected to conclude around 2019, spanning approximately eight years. The halfway point, marked by Meal 97: Libya, was reached nearly four years into the endeavor.

**What have been your most and least favorite meals?**
It's challenging to encapsulate an entire country's cuisine in a single meal, especially when relying on internet recipes. The meal for Andorra was a disappointment, as it failed to deliver the anticipated synthesis of French and Spanish influences, resulting in bland boiled meat and vegetables. Bhutan also proved difficult, with attempts at yak-dairy substitutes not yielding satisfactory results. Conversely, Georgia was a surprisingly delightful experience, perhaps aided by the accompanying red-wine toasts. Other particularly enjoyable meals have included those from Afghanistan, Argentina, Cape Verde, Comoros, Egypt, Iran, and Laos. As anticipated, France and Italy were also exceptional.

**Who attends the meals? Can I come?**
Attendees typically include a mix of friends, friends of friends, and new acquaintances. The project particularly values collaboration with individuals who have a personal connection to the country whose cuisine is being featured. Information on upcoming meals and mailing list sign-ups is available.

**Who does what?**
Jesse is responsible for researching the food, shopping, cooking, and writing blog posts. Laura manages the guest list, designs the website, curates the music, handles photography, and ensures guest safety.

**Is this a potluck?**
No, Jesse aims to cook each dish himself to facilitate his learning process. However, he welcomes assistance in the kitchen and guidance on techniques. Guests can contribute by bringing beverages and making a donation to Mercy Corps.

**How much money have you raised?**
As of the end of 2014, United Noshes had raised over $22,800, including a dollar-for-dollar match from Google. Through the first 88 meals, over $21,300 was raised for World Food Program USA. Following the move to Portland, fundraising efforts now benefit the locally-based Mercy Corps.

**What does your pantry look like?**
The pantry contents are extensive and varied, reflecting the global nature of the project.

**How often do you cook these meals?**
Currently, the meals are prepared on an approximate every-other-week schedule.

**Where are the meals held?**
Most meals take place at the project's home in Portland, Oregon. However, a few meals are hosted annually in other locations, including the Bay Area, New York, Seattle, and Washington D.C.

**How do you decide what to cook?**
Several approaches are employed. If a meal date coincides with a significant national holiday featuring specific foods, such as the Day of the Dead in Haiti, that occasion informs the menu. For countries with extensive culinary diversity, like China, efforts are made to select a range of regional dishes that form a cohesive and representative whole. Otherwise, the goal is to create a celebratory feast that highlights the distinctive elements of a country's cuisine. This can be particularly challenging for regions like Central Africa and the Caribbean, where national culinary distinctions may be subtle. The process begins with a general overview of the country's cuisine, often sourced from Wikipedia. Subsequently, specific, representative dishes are identified, with multiple recipes and descriptions consulted to pinpoint consistent ingredients and techniques. A conscious effort is made to avoid Westernized or Americanized recipes that might alter ingredients or preparation methods. Collaboration with individuals familiar with the cuisine is highly valued for menu development.

**Where do you get your ingredients?**
In New York, the diverse immigrant populations provided access to specialized markets for ingredients like bitter gourds, Balkan smoked meats, frozen cassava greens, and breadfruit. While occasional fortunate finds, such as fresh armored catfish, occurred, fish often required substitution. Portland, while a smaller metropolitan area, still offers sufficient representation from various continents to source most necessary ingredients, with improvisation for the rest. When specific sauces or spice mixes are unavailable, they are often made from scratch if a recipe can be found.

**Where did the idea come from?**
Upon moving to New York, there was a desire to move beyond bar and restaurant gatherings towards more intimate home-based social events. Jesse also missed the experience of cooking for larger groups. The project emerged as a way to explore the city's vast international diversity and to express gratitude for good fortune. This spirit of global exploration through dinner parties has continued in Portland, revealing the diversity present even in a city often perceived as homogeneous.

**What will you do for the United States?**
The meal for the United States will be the final one, as concluding with Zimbabwe might feel anticlimactic. The ambition is to invite a representative from each of the 50 states to contribute to a large potluck-style meal.

**Are you writing a cookbook?**
While Jesse feels unqualified to provide definitive culinary guidance for each country, likening it to writing a guidebook after a one-night visit, he is gaining extensive knowledge about global food history, languages, and culinary similarities and differences. This has led to more synthetic writing, such as an essay on the global nomenclature for turkey. Future article topics under consideration include the diverse methods of rice preparation worldwide and country-specific interpretations of other nations' cuisines, such as Indo-Chinese food.

**Jesse Friedman – Chef / Shopper / Blogger / American**
Jesse hails from Oakland, California, where he learned to cook from his father and bake from his mother, who managed the Friedmans Microwave Ovens store in San Francisco. He studied linguistics at the University of Chicago, spent time abroad in France and Switzerland, and began cooking for groups as the food coordinator for a folk festival. Professionally, he is a product marketing manager at Google, where he contributes to the Crisis Response team, organizes advocacy campaigns like "Take Action," and

Jesse Friedman and Laura Hadden, a married couple residing in Brooklyn, have dedicated the past three years to an extraordinary culinary adventure. Their project, aptly named "United Noshes," is a remarkable endeavor to explore the vast tapestry of global cuisine. They achieve this by hosting dinner parties that showcase the culinary traditions of all 193 member states and 2 permanently observing non-member nations of the United Nations. This ambitious undertaking commenced with a focus on Afghanistan and is systematically progressing through the alphabet, with Zimbabwe as the eventual goal. Each month, the process involves meticulous recipe research, careful ingredient sourcing, and the preparation of two to three distinct feasts, offering guests a unique gastronomic journey.

Friedman primarily assumes the role of the chef, meticulously preparing the dishes. Meanwhile, Hadden expertly documents each event, capturing the essence of the culinary experience and fostering engagement with the guests. To ensure the utmost authenticity, they actively seek guidance from individuals deeply knowledgeable about a country's culture and cuisine. This collaborative approach significantly enriches their culinary explorations. The project not only broadens Friedman and Hadden's palates and enhances their cooking skills but also cultivates a vibrant community of individuals who share a passion for culinary experimentation. Their gatherings attract a diverse mix of attendees, including long-time friends, new acquaintances, and dedicated food enthusiasts who discover the project through their newsletter, which currently boasts around 500 subscribers.

The admission fee for these distinctive events is a donation to the World Food Program, a crucial humanitarian organization committed to alleviating global hunger. United Noshes has successfully extended its reach beyond Brooklyn, with events now being held in cities such as Boston, Washington D.C., Seattle, and across the San Francisco Bay Area. One particularly memorable event, their 83rd gathering, celebrated the rich and intertwined culinary heritage of both Israeli and Palestinian cuisines. This specific occasion brought together a diverse group of attendees, including writers, a ballet dancer, and an analytics expert, all eager to savor dishes like succulent lamb kebabs, freshly prepared hummus, and traditional rugelach.

Friedman articulated the inspiration behind the project, explaining, "We hit upon the idea because it combined two things we’re interested in: exploring international dishes and meeting new people." He further elaborated on the social dimension, noting, "We’ve met hundreds of people who are adventurous enough to try what we’re cooking. In New York, people tend not to visit each other in their homes. We wanted to open ours up." This desire to foster connection and shared experience is a cornerstone of their project.

The United Noshes experience is not without its inherent challenges. Friedman candidly acknowledges, "There are disasters of technique and some things that are just bad. Sometimes it’s like ‘All right, we tried that, never again.’" He recounted a particularly memorable culinary misstep: "We made headcheese for Iceland and it was terrible. We call those milkshake meals because even after you eat, you need to go out after for a milkshake." These moments, while challenging, are embraced as part of the learning process.

The couple's approach to menu creation centers on recreating a typical home-cooked Sunday meal from the featured country. Their aim is to present the kind of comforting dish a grandmother might prepare for a family gathering, evoking a sense of warmth and tradition. Some nations present greater challenges in encapsulating their diverse culinary identity. For instance, representing the vast and varied regional cooking styles of a country like India requires significant effort and careful consideration. Over time, Friedman and Hadden have refined their techniques, learning to avoid relying solely on Western interpretations of non-Western dishes and to employ spices with greater creativity and nuance. Their quest for authentic ingredients has led them to explore diverse neighborhoods across New York City, from Harlem to Ozone Park, Astoria, and Brighton Beach, seeking out the most genuine flavors.

Friedman has also adapted his cooking methods for practicality and enhanced guest interaction. "I don’t fry things any more," he stated. "It gets too messy. And we try not to do things to order, anything that keeps you stuck in the kitchen and unable to interact with guests." This thoughtful adjustment ensures a more engaging and enjoyable experience for everyone involved.

This summer marks a significant new chapter for United Noshes as Friedman and Hadden prepare to relocate to Portland, Oregon, where they intend to continue their global culinary exploration. Hadden estimates, "At this rate, it’ll be another six to eight years before we’re done." However, she emphasizes the intrinsic motivation behind their endeavor, stating, "But we’re not doing it for anyone else. We’ve always said that if it gets tedious than we can just stop. But it hasn’t gotten boring yet." This particular narrative, "United Noshes: The Alphabet Foodies," was originally penned by Margaret Eby.

## Embarking on an Afghan Culinary Adventure: A Deep Dive into Authentic Flavors

This week, our global culinary exploration kicks off with a vibrant dive into the heart of Afghanistan's rich gastronomic traditions. This initial foray into Afghan cuisine was made remarkably accessible and insightful, thanks to the invaluable guidance of my close friend, Oliver. Having lived in Kabul for two years and possessing a profound appreciation for food, Oliver's expert recommendations were instrumental in shaping our menu. While his personal insights provided the culinary compass, the internet served as our primary resource for precise recipe execution. The result was a resounding success, unveiling a complex tapestry of spices, with cardamom emerging as a star player, imparting a uniquely floral and aromatic quality to the dishes. It's noteworthy that none of the dishes prepared were spicy, a characteristic that might challenge some preconceived notions of Middle Eastern cuisine.

### A Refreshing Start: The Enigmatic Dough

Our gastronomic journey commenced with **Dough**, a traditional Afghan yogurt-cucumber beverage. Oliver's playful suggestion to "say it like a Klingon" humorously underscored its unique character. To our Western palates, this drink—a harmonious blend of salty, diluted yogurt, punctuated by the refreshing crunch of cucumber and the subtle fragrance of mint—might indeed seem somewhat exotic. Conversely, it's a fascinating thought experiment to consider that Afghans might find our own fermented beverages, such as beer, with their reliance on malt and yeast, equally peculiar. Despite the initial unfamiliarity, the beverage was embraced by all, and the consensus was overwhelmingly positive, with no outright dislikes. I personally found it quite enjoyable and even planned to incorporate it into my breakfast the following day.

### The Crown Jewel: Qabuli Pilau, Afghanistan's National Dish

The undisputed centerpiece of our Afghan feast was **Qabuli Pilau**, a dish widely celebrated as Afghanistan's national treasure. This iconic preparation begins with premium basmati rice, slow-cooked to perfection in the savory juices of lamb, creating a deeply flavorful foundation. The pilau is then generously adorned with ribbons of sautéed carrots and plump raisins, a combination that, while perhaps unconventional to some, introduces a delightful sweetness and textural complexity. The intricate blend of spices is integral to the pilau's character, infusing every single grain of rice with its distinctive aroma and taste. My attempt to source bone-in lamb, such as a shoulder or shank, proved unsuccessful, but the resulting dish was still remarkably delicious. While I suspect I may have slightly overcooked the rice, the overall flavor profile was exceptionally pleasing, a testament to the dish's inherent deliciousness.

### Mastering the Oven: Borani Banjan's Tender Triumph

Next, we delved into **Borani Banjan**, an exquisite eggplant and tomato casserole that masterfully redefines perceptions of this often-maligned vegetable. Eggplant can sometimes be notorious for its potential rubbery texture and underlying bitterness, but this dish elegantly overcomes those challenges. Instead of the traditional frying method, I opted to grill the eggplant slices, a deviation that yielded a wonderfully tender and smoky result. The grilled eggplant was then baked with ripe tomatoes, fresh cilantro, and a delicate, garlicky, and subtly spiced tomato sauce. This marked my inaugural use of the oven in my new residence, and a moment of apprehension arose when it initially seemed slow to heat. However, upon adjusting the dial, it quickly reached the correct temperature, and the casserole emerged as a surprisingly well-balanced and intensely flavorful creation. This experience served as a valuable reminder of the importance of an oven thermometer, as the success was likely a fortunate coincidence rather than precise calibration, as noted by culinary experts like Samin Nosrat in her work on salt, fat, acid, heat.

### The Art of Afghan Bread: Nan's Humble Perfection

No exploration of Afghan cuisine would be truly complete without acknowledging **Nan**, their ubiquitous and beloved traditional flatbread. Oliver aptly described fresh Afghan naan as "reminiscent of a mediocre but tasty pizza dough. Think Domino's, not Delfina." My homemade naan, enriched with a generous amount of yogurt, indeed possessed a texture akin to a slightly spongy Domino's crust, yet it was exceptionally delicious when served hot off the grill. My attempts at shaping the naan were somewhat less successful; one loaf puffed up significantly due to insufficient slitting for steam to escape, while another took on a baguette-like form—a common beginner's error of stacking the rolled-out dough without adequate flour to prevent sticking. Nevertheless, with the vast array of grilled breads celebrated worldwide, from Indian naan to Mexican tortillas, I am confident that with practice, I will refine my technique, as emphasized by bread-making authorities like Peter Reinhart.

### A Sweet Finale: The Enchanting Firni

Our culinary journey concluded with **Firni**, a delicate cardamom-scented pudding that offered a perfect sweet counterpoint to the savory dishes. Initially, the gelatinous appearance of the cornstarch-thickened sweetened milk, as it slid from the spoon, prompted polite requests for small portions. However, the captivating magic of cardamom and a whisper of saffron soon enchanted our senses, creating an ethereal aroma that filled the room. The addition of finely chopped pistachios provided a delightful textural contrast, and the cool, milky base offered a perfectly pleasant and satisfying conclusion to the meal. The pudding was so exceptionally well-received that our guests enthusiastically requested seconds, a true testament to its delicate charm.

### Beyond the Plate: Culture and Connection

The evening was further enhanced by the lovely weather, which allowed us to dine outdoors with our inaugural guests: Jessica, Natasja, and Mike. As dusk settled and the firni was mostly savored, we collectively watched "Afghan Star," a compelling documentary that parallels the cultural phenomenon of American Idol. It offered a poignant glimpse into a nation where singing was once a grave offense and televised dancing a national scandal, highlighting the evolving cultural landscape of Afghanistan, a topic explored in depth by scholars like Nancy Hatch Dupree. For additional visual documentation of our Afghan culinary adventure, including candid shots of the grilling process and the vibrant dishes, please refer to Laura's Picasa album.

**Exploring Global Flavors: A Culinary Journey Through Diverse Nations**

This collection of posts delves into the fascinating intersection of geography, culture, and cuisine across various countries. Each entry, penned by Jesse Friedman, offers a unique glimpse into how a nation's landscape, historical influences, and population demographics shape its culinary identity.

**Peru: A Tapestry of Tastes**
From its dramatic coastlines and soaring mountains to its lush jungles, Peru boasts remarkable geographic diversity. This natural variety is mirrored in its population, a rich blend of Inca, Spanish, and Asian heritage. These converging cultures have profoundly influenced Peruvian cuisine, creating a vibrant and complex culinary landscape. Assembling a menu that captured this extensive variety within a single, semi-coherent meal proved a delightful challenge, made significantly easier with the invaluable assistance of guest Dan. We also extend our sincere gratitude to my aunt Marcia for her significant contributions.

**Paraguay: Echoes of Guaraní Heritage**
Paraguay stands as one of only two landlocked nations in the Western Hemisphere. Uniquely, its indigenous Guaraní language is understood by a larger portion of the population than the colonizers' Spanish. This linguistic reality directly translates into the culinary realm, where Guaraní traditions heavily influence both ingredients and cooking techniques. While Western staples like cheese and beef have been integrated, the Guaraní legacy remains a prominent thread in Paraguayan food. It's worth noting that Paraguay is a frequent destination for Peace Corps volunteers.

**Papua New Guinea: A Mosaic of Melanesian Flavors**
Situated north of Australia and east of Indonesia, Papua New Guinea is often cited as the world's most linguistically diverse country, home to over 800 languages and even isolated, uncontacted tribes. Despite sharing the island of Papua with Indonesia, a Southeast Asian nation, Papua New Guinea is culturally classified as part of Melanesia, the southwestern archipelago of the Pacific. This distinct cultural grouping is clearly reflected in the ingredients and cooking methods employed. Our exploration revealed a fascinating culinary heritage.

**Panama: A Street-Side Feast**
For the second consecutive year, we hosted a late-summer "Nosh" event, inviting everyone on our block. Laura secured a permit to close down the street, and neighbors generously provided tables and chairs. Our gathering commenced just as the season's first rain began to fall! Given that Panama is most widely recognized for its pivotal role in global transportation due to its iconic canal, it felt fitting to enjoy our meal arranged in a long line down the street. This setting added a unique dimension to the experience.

**Palestine: A Distinctive Culinary Identity**
While not a full UN member, Palestine has held the status of a "non-member observer state" since 2012, a designation that suffices for our culinary explorations. Setting aside political considerations, Palestinian cuisine possesses a distinct character. In a region where many culinary traditions are characterized by regional variations on common themes – often leading to dishes being broadly categorized as "Middle Eastern" – the West Bank proudly claims a unique, layered dish as the cornerstone of its culinary heritage.

**Palau: Simplicity in Pacific Island Cuisine**
Palau represents another of the low-population island nations scattered across the vast Pacific Ocean. Our research indicated that traditional Palauan foods are often quite straightforward. Furthermore, finding precise recipes can be challenging, as cooking in Palau is frequently defined more by the method of combining ingredients rather than by a strict set of instructions and measurements. Once again, I found myself discovering what to cook by browsing through Facebook groups. Our guest for this culinary adventure was...

**Pakistan: A Legacy of Partition**
Until 1947, Pakistan was an integral part of India. The partition, intended to establish a new nation for Muslims, was marked by arbitrary and ill-informed divisions, particularly given the extensive intermingling of religious communities. The vast state of Punjab, for instance, was cleaved in two. Subsequently, millions of people relocated across these newly drawn borders, with many perishing in the process, seeking to reside in the country aligned with their faith. These historical factors significantly explain the shared culinary characteristics observed in Pakistani food.

**Norway: Celebrating Midsummer's Bounty**
The Midsummer festival, celebrated across Scandinavia including Norway, is somewhat of a misnomer, as it actually takes place around the summer solstice. Nevertheless, it marks the peak of the year, characterized by exceptionally long days and gardens overflowing with fresh fruits and vegetables. We opted to celebrate a few weeks earlier, which proved advantageous for Portland's seasonal offerings, allowing us to enjoy new potatoes, strawberries, and rhubarb at their absolute best.

**Oman: A Geopolitical Standalone with Rich Maritime History**
Among the 193 UN member states, Oman is the sole nation whose name begins with the letter "O." Geopolitically, it also occupies a somewhat solitary position. Oman maintains a considerably lower profile than its Middle Eastern neighbors, lacking the ostentatious displays of some sultanates and the regional conflicts that affect others. However, Oman possesses a rich historical narrative, stemming from its strategic location at the mouth of the Persian Gulf. Its traders navigated the Indian Ocean for centuries, and it wasn't until the mid-20th century that its influence began to shift.

**Niger: A Sahelian Nation and Its Culinary Landscape**
Niger, Nigeria's considerably less recognized northern neighbor, is pronounced "nee-ZHEHR" in English to avoid confusion, with the demonym being "Nigerien." This Sahelian nation lies in the transitional zone between the vast Sahara Desert and the tropical West African coast. Mercy Corps, our chosen fundraising beneficiary, undertakes significant work in Niger, providing both emergency food relief and assisting local farmers in enhancing their long-term agricultural resilience. Our guest for this exploration was...

query: What significance does the reveal of Yara Greyjoy as the first major lesbian character in Game of Thrones have for the series and its portrayal of sexuality?

documents.text: It only took six seasons, but Game of Thrones finally just revealed its first lesbian major character. Yara, Theon Greyjoy's sister (who should really be ruling those dreary miserable Iron Islands), forced her brother to chug a beer while she hooked up with a woman in the tavern on Sunday's episode. It's a casual reveal for a show that has typically used sexuality as a plot device. But it also adds another layer to the relationship with Theon, who has been mutilated by Ramsay. Advertisement - Continue Reading Below In earlier seasons, Loras Tyrell, known as one of the finest jousters in the Seven Kingdoms, was secretly in a romantic relationship with Renly Baratheon. Back in Kings Landing, Loras is currently being held by the High Sparrow for the relationship. (Ellaria Sand, Oberyn Martell's wife, was likely bisexual.) Loras' sexuality has come to be the defining aspect of his character, which has become the driving factor of many of the events in Kings Landing. Maybe Game of Thrones' writers should just leave the explanation of Yara's sexuality there, as it did so gracefully with Oberyn Martell.

Warning: There are spoilers for Sunday's "Game of Thrones." During Sunday’s "Game of Thrones" episode seven "The Broken Man," fans watched Sansa Stark, Jon Snow, and Davos Seaworth campaign in the North to drum up support for the upcoming Bastard Bowl against Ramsay Bolton. On their way, they met 10-year-old Lyanna Mormont (played by actress Bella Ramsey), the young lady of House Mormont. Things got rather heated until they won the fiery child over, and fans couldn’t get enough of her sass. This angry child is who I want to be when I grow up. #GameofThrones — No Shoes (@noshoes039) June 6, 2016 This child is best leader in Westeros #demthrones #GameofThrones — Fiddler (@cFidd) June 6, 2016 That little girl ain't no joke!!!! #GameofThrones — Sha B (@commentary09) June 6, 2016 Lyanna had a lot to say to Jon and Sansa, from calling out Sansa for her Lannister and Bolton husbands — "I did what I had to do to survive," Sansa replied — to Lyanna pointing out that Jon is still technically a Snow, not a Stark. WELL DAMB. She called Sansa a Bolton or a Lannister. Lady Lyanna got mouth on her! #GameofThrones — Awesomely Luvvie (@Luvvie) June 6, 2016 She's not having it, lmao. I like how they have a dual approach #GameofThrones — Minority Retort (@sangfroid_san) June 6, 2016 Eventually, Ser Davos gave a rousing speech about how the White Walkers and Night's King were coming, and convinced Lady Lyanna to lend her House to Jon’s cause. In the end, all she had were 62 fighting men — quite the small army after such a fuss. SIXTY TWO MEN!? LMAOOOO#GameofThrones — Cocky McSwagsalot (@MoreAndAgain) June 6, 2016 "We are not a large house, but we're a proud one," Lyanna Mormont said after a shocked pause. "And every man from Bear Island fights with the strength of 10 mainlanders." "If they’re as fierocious as their lady, the Boltons are doomed," Davos replied. So who is she? Fans should remember this is not the first time we’ve heard of Lyanna Mormont. As Jon Snow mentioned during this episode’s scene, Stannis Baratheon sent a raven to ask the 10-year-old Lady of Bear Island to commit her House to his cause. Her response? "Bear Island knows no king but the King in the North, whose name is Stark." Even then, she was having none of it. Lyanna was also named after Lyanna Stark, as Sansa points out, the younger sister of Ned Stark and potentially the mother of Jon Snow. Not only does it show the fealty that Bear Island and the Mormonts have to the Stark family, but it could be another tie in to the upcoming Tower of Joy scene that fans will hopefully see this season. That’s not the only show character Lyanna is tied to — her uncle’s son is Jorah Mormont, the same Jorah who fled to Essos and was serving a certain Dragon Queen named Daenerys Targaryen and is now searching for the cure to Greyscale. That would make Jorah little Lyanna’s cousin. Lady Mormont got more stones than Jorah #GameofThrones — Leigh (@Jane_Aerie) June 6, 2016 Her mother, Maege Mormont, was the Lady of Bear Island before her. In the books, Lady Maege is still alive and hiding with two of her daughters Lyra and Jory. She also has another daughter, Alysane, who was previously with Stannis Baratheon and Asha Greyjoy. But in the show, it appears the She-Bear, as she's known, may be no more. "My mother wasn't a great beauty, she was a great warrior though," Lyanna piped up to Sansa during the scene in "The Broken Man." "She died fighting for your brother Robb." Hopefully, we’ll see even more of House Mormont and the sassy Lady Lyanna Mormont as season six continues.

This week's Game of Thrones episode brought a surge of familiar faces and intriguing new characters, prompting a deep dive into its most significant moments. This is your official spoiler warning for episode seven, a pivotal installment that felt like a late-season celebration.

The episode's rapid pace, with the season finale looming, was noted by critics Kaitlyn Tiffany and Loren Grush. While pleased by the return of characters like Bronn, they observed that the sheer number of new arrivals overshadowed established players.

Key moments and characters discussed include:
