query: What were some of the key events and relationships that marked Fidel Castro's rule in Cuba?

documents.text: Mourning, Celebration Follow Death Of Fidel Castro Enlarge this image toggle caption AFP/AFP/Getty Images AFP/AFP/Getty Images Fidel Castro, the controversial ruler who took power during the Cuban revolution in 1959 and led his country for nearly half a century, died in Havana, Cuba, at age 90. Castro lived through 10 U.S. presidents who were determined to overthrow him, as NPR's Tom Gjelten reported, in addition to surviving the collapse of the communist alliance that bolstered his success. The former president has suffered ill health since undergoing emergency intestinal surgery in July 2006, when he ceded power to his younger brother, Raúl, who formally assumed the presidency in 2008. In a short, televised message, an emotional President Raul Castro announced that his brother had passed at 10:29 p.m. late Friday night, as NPR's Carrie Kahn translates: "With profound pain I share with our people and the friends of America and the world that today, on the 25th of November at 10:29 p.m., the head commander of the Cuban Revolution, the comrade Fidel Castro Ruz died. In following the wishes expressed by Fidel, his remains will be cremated. "In the early hours of tomorrow, Saturday the 26th, the funeral organizing commission will provide the people detailed information about the organization of a posthumous tribute to the founder of the Cuban Revolution. Towards victory forever." Castro's rule was marked by his relationship with two global powers: the Soviet Union as his ally, the U.S. as his adversary, Tom tells our newscast. "The Eisenhower Administration initially welcomed Castro to power; but as a nationalist he was determined to assert Cuba's independence from the United States, and by early 1960, Castro was reaching out to the Soviet Union for support. Before long, the U.S. government was helping Cuban exiles who wanted to overthrow Castro or even have him assassinated. "The Soviet Union meanwhile gave Castro billions of dollars per year — enough to support health and education programs that won Cuba praise around the world. Castro lost that help when the Soviet Union collapsed; Cuba soon became impoverished. "Relations with the U.S. never really improved during the time he was in charge, and Fidel Castro will be remembered as much for what he survived as for what he achieved." The death of the polarizing former president has set off both mourning and celebration worldwide. Silence has swept much of Cuba. The Cuban government announced Saturday morning that Castro's ashes will be interred on December 4, following nine days of national mourning, which officially began at 6 a.m. on Saturday. As CNN notes, citing the state-run outlet, Granma, "During this time, all activities and public performances will stop, and the flag will be flown at half-staff in public and military establishments. Radio and television will broadcast patriotic and historical programming." Foreign allies are paying tributes and expressing sorrow on Twitter, including Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Mexican President Enrique Pena Nieto, who said Castro "was a friend" of Mexico. El Salvador's President Salvador Sanchez Ceren called him an "eternal companion." Venezuela's socialist President Nicolas Maduro said "revolutionaries of the world must follow his legacy." President Obama released a statement acknowledging the United States' fraught history with Cuba under Castro's regime and the two countries' renewed diplomatic relationship. He also said: We know that this moment fills Cubans - in Cuba and in the United States - with powerful emotions, recalling the countless ways in which Fidel Castro altered the course of individual lives, families, and of the Cuban nation. History will record and judge the enormous impact of this singular figure on the people and world around him. Obama's statement concluded: "Today, we offer condolences to Fidel Castro's family, and our thoughts and prayers are with the Cuban people. In the days ahead, they will recall the past and also look to the future. As they do, the Cuban people must know that they have a friend and partner in the United States of America." On Saturday morning, President-elect Donald Trump tweeted: "Fidel Castro is dead!" Trump later released a longer statement saying his "administration will do all it can to ensure the Cuban people can finally begin their journey toward prosperity and liberty." Meanwhile, just some 200 miles from Havana, the Cuban exile community in Miami, Florida's Little Havana, are spilling into the streets in jubilation, waving Cuban flags, cheering and dancing. Cuban-Americans in Congress also reacted to the news of Castro's death. Democratic Sen. Bob Menendez of New Jersey released a statement that read, in part: Since he took power over half a century ago, Fidel Castro proved to be a brutal dictator who must always be remembered by his gross abuses of human rights, systemic exploitation of Cubans, unrelenting repression, and stifling censorship upon his own people. Fidel's oppressive legacy will haunt the Cuban regime and our hemisphere forever. In a statement, Republican Sen. Ted Cruz of Texas said: Fidel Castro's death cannot bring back his thousands of victims, nor can it bring comfort to their families. Today we remember them and honor the brave souls who fought the lonely fight against the brutal Communist dictatorship he imposed on Cuba. Republican Sen. Marco Rubio of Florida echoed these sentiments in his statement, adding: The dictator has died, but the dictatorship has not. And one thing is clear, history will not absolve Fidel Castro; it will remember him as an evil, murderous dictator who inflicted misery and suffering on his own people. And Republican congresswoman Ileana Ros-Lehtinen of Florida's 27th district celebrated Castro's death on Twitter. The U.S. is home to roughly 2 million Cuban-Americans because they, or the generations before them, fled the island during Castro's rule. The majority live in Florida, NPR's Greg Allen reports to our newscast, where the U.S. government once launched failed attempts at overthrowing the young Castro regime: "As Castro tightened his grip on Cuba, large numbers of people began fleeing the island. There were firing squads and property seizures. ... The first wave of exiles were mostly from Cuba's elite, businessmen and professionals. They came to Miami where many began working on an urgent goal—to oust Fidel Castro and take back their homeland. In Miami, a group of Cubans formed a paramilitary group, Brigade 2506. They worked with the CIA on a plan to retake Cuba after landing on a beach in the Bay of Pigs. The invasion was a disaster. More than 120 members of the invading force were killed. 1,200 were captured by Castro's military. Most of those captured were released a year later." This post has been updated to include reactions to Castro's death.

In this photo provided by the Bolivian Information Agency (ABI), Cuba's Fidel Castro, right, Bolivia's President Evo Morales, center, and Venezuela's President Nicolas Maduro sit inside a van after Maduro and Castro picked up Morales at his hotel in Havana, Cuba, Thursday, Aug. 13 2015. Morales and Maduro were in Cuba to celebrate Castro's 89th birthday. Ivan Maldonado AP

The death of Fidel Castro, Cuba's transformative revolutionary leader, has ignited a spectrum of reactions in Havana, highlighting the deep-seated divisions and lingering questions that defined his extensive rule. While some citizens, such as Concepcion Garcia, 55, conveyed a profound sense of sorrow, mourning the conclusion of an epoch and the perceived demise of a cherished experience, others, particularly younger Cubans, exhibited a notable detachment. This disparity in sentiment underscores a significant generational divide and the varied interpretations of Castro's enduring impact.

For a considerable number of older Cubans, Castro represented an unyielding constant, the singular leader they had always known. Graciela Martinez, 51, a cafe worker, expressed her grief, reflecting on her father's revolutionary zeal and her family's displacement to the United States, a poignant illustration of the deeply personal connections and conflicts associated with Castro's name. "For those who loved him, he was the greatest," she stated, "For those who hated him, there was no one worse."

Cuba, a nation of 11 million navigating gradual free-market reforms, now stands at an international juncture. Castro's passing coincides with Venezuela's reduced financial aid, stemming from its own internal crises, and the potential reversal of the diplomatic thaw initiated by President Obama under President-elect Donald J. Trump. While Obama extended condolences and a "hand of friendship to the Cuban people," Trump unequivocally characterized Castro as a "brutal dictator."

The future direction of Cuba remains a subject of considerable conjecture. Elaine Díaz, an independent blogger, posits that Castro's absence may encourage a broader range of viewpoints within the Cuban leadership, potentially revealing internal factions beyond the government's carefully curated image. She observed, "This is going to bring that out in public."

However, the most striking divisions are manifesting across generations. Older Cubans often remain profoundly linked to Castro's legacy, finding it challenging to conceive of Cuba independent of his influence. In contrast, younger generations, who grew up with Castro largely removed from the national spotlight following his 2006 transfer of power to his brother Raúl Castro, display less personal identification with him. This cohort is frequently characterized by a perception of politics as largely ineffectual, a strong desire for technological advancement, and a greater inclination to leave Cuba than to champion fading Communist ideals. Elaine Díaz, 31, noted, "In my parents' generation, there is also still a lot of loyalty. In my generation, you’ll see more differences." She further elaborated, "In a large portion of the young people, what you will see is apathy." This apathy was palpable on Friday night, with many young individuals showing no overt emotional response to the news of Castro's death. Their Saturday routines continued largely unchanged, with many expressing skepticism regarding any imminent transformations. Abraham Jimenez Enoa, 27, a co-creator of the independent blog "el estornudo" ("the sneeze"), commented, "The country will continue on the path that it’s on. I don’t really see changes in the near future."

The official response, in stark contrast, was one of immediate solemnity. Raúl Castro announced his brother's death, framing it as a national tragedy. By sunrise, Havana's flags were lowered to half-staff, and small, subdued gatherings replaced the typically lively street interactions. Many in Havana articulated a sense of profound uncertainty regarding how to process their emotions. Francisco Rodríguez Cruz, a prominent blogger and gay activist who supports the revolution, likened the feeling to losing a parental figure, stating, "With this death, you feel that your own life is spread before you." Others, like Concepcion Garcia, attributed tangible benefits to Castro and the revolution. She pointed to her eyes, crediting Cuba's socialized medical system for her cataract surgery, which was provided at no personal expense. "He put Cuba on the map," Garcia asserted, "and the world has recognized that." Her neighbor, Josue Carmon Arramo, 57, shared this perspective, believing Castro's work would persist through his followers' dedication to nationalism and solidarity. The state apparatus appeared prepared for the announcement, with reports of increased military presence preceding the official news. Cuban national television preempted its regular programming with historical footage of Castro and scenes from the Elián González repatriation. Near the American Embassy, plainclothes officers maintained a discreet but watchful presence. The Malecon, Havana's iconic seaside promenade, usually bustling with activity, was unusually subdued. While details regarding Castro's cremation and official mourning periods were disseminated, his legacy remained a more sensitive subject. In Miami, however, celebrations erupted at Versailles restaurant in Little Havana, with patrons posting videos of champagne toasts on social media. In Havana, the prevailing sentiment, as articulated by Miguel Fernandez, 56, was one of tempered expectations and a focus on economic survival. "It closes one chapter and starts another," he remarked, "But it won’t bring about anything substantial. He’s been out of the picture for a while."

Fidel Castro will be interred in a cemetery on December 4 in the eastern city of Santiago, following several days of mourning and a tour of his ashes throughout Cuba, the country's government has announced. Castro grew up near Santiago and attended school there as a youth. He also proclaimed the victory of the Cuban Revolution from a balcony at Santiago's city hall. Beginning Monday at 9 a.m., there will be a memorial for the public to pay their respects at Memorial a José Martí in Havana. The memorial will run until Tuesday at noon. On those days, Cubans throughout the country will have the chance to pay homage and pledge their allegiance by signing a "solemn oath of complying with the concept of the revolution." There will also be a mass gathering in Havana's Plaza de la Revolucion, where Castro often addressed massive crowds for hours. Then on Wednesday, Castro's ashes will make a cross-country tour from Havana to Santiago, that will end on Saturday, December 3. At 7 p.m. that day, there will be another mass gathering in the Plaza Antonio Maceo. The following day, on Sunday, December 4, Castro's remains will be interred at Cementerio Santa Ifigenia. ABC News' Kirit Radia contributed to this report.

The CIA and its allies embarked on an extraordinary, decades-long campaign to assassinate Fidel Castro, employing a bewildering array of tactics ranging from poison pills to exploding molluscs. These elaborate schemes, spanning nearly fifty years, aimed to eliminate the Cuban leader. While none of these plots ultimately succeeded, one of Castro's security personnel meticulously calculated that a staggering 634 attempts, encompassing both the ludicrous and the deadly serious, were made on the life of "El Comandante."

One particularly inventive, albeit ultimately abandoned, plan capitalized on Castro's known passion for scuba diving. Declassified documents confirm the CIA's investment in a substantial quantity of Caribbean molluscs. The objective was to locate a specimen large enough to conceal a lethal charge of explosives. The intention was to adorn these molluscs with vibrant colors, thereby attracting the attention of an underwater Castro. This audacious concept was eventually shelved, as was another equally bizarre proposition: a custom-designed diving suit deliberately infected with a fungus engineered to induce a debilitating skin disease.

Fabian Escalante, who served as the head of the Cuban secret service during the peak of these assassination efforts, detailed these plots in his book, "Executive Action: 634 Ways to Kill Castro." This work later inspired a 2007 Channel 4 documentary titled "638 Ways to Kill Castro." The relentless pursuit of Castro by the CIA and their increasingly desperate exile allies drew parallels to the modern-day hunt for Osama bin Laden. As Wayne Smith, former head of the US interests section in Havana, aptly observed, "Cuba seems to have the same effect on American administrations that a full moon has on a werewolf. We may not sprout hair and howl but we behave in the same way."

The assassination attempts commenced during the presidency of Dwight Eisenhower and continued under John F. Kennedy and Lyndon B. Johnson. However, Richard Nixon's tenure saw the highest number of such endeavors, with precisely 184 attempts recorded. Many of these were conceived and executed by Cuban exiles, often with covert CIA support, without the explicit knowledge of the administrations. The plotting began almost immediately following the 1959 revolution. In 1961, during the ill-fated Bay of Pigs invasion, a plan was in place to assassinate Fidel and Raúl Castro, along with Che Guevara. At times, it appeared as though US security services were more preoccupied with eliminating the Cuban head of state than safeguarding their own. On the very day President Kennedy was assassinated in 1963, an agent, equipped with a pen-syringe obtained in Paris, was dispatched on a mission to assassinate Castro.

The repertoire of assassination plots reads like a collection of James Bond-esque fantasies. Among the most famous was the "exploding cigar," purportedly intended for Castro during a visit to the UN in New York. Another idea involved contaminating a cigar with botulinum toxin, though this never materialized, and Castro ultimately quit smoking in 1985. A former lover of Castro's was recruited as a potential assassin and provided with poison pills by the CIA, which she concealed within her cold-cream jar. The pills, however, melted, diminishing her chances of administering them while Castro slept. According to her account, Castro, aware of her intentions, gallantly offered her his own pistol to complete the task. "I can't do it, Fidel," she reportedly replied.

The CIA's operational endeavors extended to experiments with bacterial poisons intended for his tea or coffee. Other schemes included a toxic fountain pen, a botulin-poisoned chocolate milkshake slated for the former Havana Hilton, and a non-lethal plot to discredit Castro by spraying an LSD aerosol near him during a broadcast, with the expectation that he would experience a public breakdown. When Castro traveled abroad, the CIA collaborated with Cuban exiles on more serious assassination attempts. As recently as 2000, during Castro's planned visit to Panama, a plot was uncovered to place 200lb (90kg) of high explosives beneath the podium where he was scheduled to speak. Castro's personal security team, conducting their own pre-arrival checks, successfully foiled this dangerous scheme. Four individuals, including Luis Posada, a veteran Cuban exile and former CIA operative, were apprehended and subsequently jailed, though they were later pardoned and released. Posada eventually retired to Florida, a hub for the Cuban exile community. He later faced accusations, which he denied, of involvement in the 1976 bombing of a Cuban airliner that claimed 78 lives.

Beyond utilizing their own operatives and anti-Castro Cubans, the CIA also explored outsourcing the assassination. American underworld figures from the mafia, still resentful of their expulsion from Cuba by Castro, were approached to ascertain their willingness to carry out a hit. One aspiring sniper was apprehended by security personnel at the University of Havana. A grenade attack at a baseball game represented another failed ploy. Officially, the US eventually ceased its direct assassination attempts, opting instead for a trade embargo on the island as a means to destabilize Castro's regime. Nevertheless, the Cuban security service remained perpetually vigilant regarding gifts from foreign "well-wishers." The use of body doubles and Castro's constant movement across the country were employed to thwart potential assassins. When questioned about wearing a bullet-proof vest, Castro famously quipped, "I have a moral vest." His remarkable ability to survive numerous attempts on his life became a source of considerable humor. One anecdote recounts him being presented with a Galápagos turtle as a gift. Castro politely declined, having learned that the turtle's lifespan was approximately 100 years. "That's the problem with pets," he remarked. "You get attached to them and then they die on you."

(CNN) President Barack Obama issued a statement from the White House on Saturday morning following the death of Fidel Castro, extending condolences to his family and offering a hand of friendship to the Cuban people. This statement marked a significant moment in the ongoing efforts to redefine U.S.-Cuba relations.

Obama emphasized the administration's commitment to moving beyond historical divisions. "During my presidency, we have worked hard to put the past behind us, pursuing a future in which the relationship between our two countries is defined not by our differences but by the many things that we share as neighbors and friends -- bonds of family, culture, commerce, and common humanity," he stated. This reflects a key tenet of his foreign policy, as detailed in policy papers from the U.S. Department of State.

The President acknowledged the profound impact of Castro's passing. "We know that this moment fills Cubans -- in Cuba and in the United States- - with powerful emotions, recalling the countless ways in which Fidel Castro altered the course of individual lives, families, and of the Cuban nation," the statement read. It further noted, "History will record and judge the enormous impact of this singular figure on the people and world around him." This sentiment was echoed by scholars at the Brookings Institution, who have extensively analyzed Castro's legacy.

The statement concluded with a forward-looking message:
*   "Today, we offer condolences to Fidel Castro's family."
*   "Our thoughts and prayers are with the Cuban people."
*   "In the days ahead, they will recall the past and also look to the future."
*   "As they do, the Cuban people must know that they have a friend and partner in the United States of America."

This approach aligns with the Obama administration's broader diplomatic strategy, as outlined in speeches by then-Secretary of State John Kerry. Shortly after Obama released his statement, Republican Sen. Marco Rubio responded via Twitter, criticizing the President's comments.

People celebrate the death of Cuban leader Fidel Castro in Miami, Saturday, Nov. 26, 2016. Castro, who led a rebel army to improbable victory, embraced Soviet-style communism and defied the power of 10... (Associated Press) People celebrate the death of Cuban leader Fidel Castro in Miami, Saturday, Nov. 26, 2016. Castro, who led a rebel army to improbable victory, embraced Soviet-style communism and defied the power of 10 U.S. presidents during his half century rule of Cuba, died at age 90. (Michael Laughlin/South Florida... (Associated Press) The death of Cuban leader Fidel Castro prompted celebrations among the country's exiles in Miami, expressions of sorrow from some world leaders, and measured responses from governments that never stopped seeing the devoted socialist as a threat. Within half an hour of the announcement Saturday of the 90-year-old revolutionary leader's death, cheers were heard in Miami's Little Havana. Thousands of people banged pots, waved Cuban flags and whooped in jubilation. "Cuba si! Castro no!" they chanted, while others screamed "Cuba libre!" U.S. President-elect Donald Trump took to Twitter hours later to share a thought that proved pithy even for the medium: "Fidel Castro is dead!" However, Castro was honored and mourned by some present and former national leaders. "Free and independent Cuba, which he (Fidel Castro) and his allies built, became an influential member of the international community and became an inspiring example for many countries and nations," Russian President Vladimir Putin said in a telegram to President Raul Castro. Salvador Sanchez Ceren, the president of El Salvador, said he felt "deep sorrow ... of my friend and eternal companion, Commander Fidel Castro Ruz." Mexican president Enrique Pena Nieto tweeted that "Fidel Castro was a friend of Mexico, promoting bilateral relations based on respect, dialogue and solidarity." "India mourns the loss of a great friend," Indian Prime Minister Nerendra Modi said on Twitter. The country's president, Pranab Mukherjee tweeted: "Heartfelt condolences on sad demise of Cuba's revolutionary leader, former president & friend of India, Fidel Castro." Chinese President Xi Jinping said Castro "made immortal historical contributions to the development of socialism around the world." "With his death, the Chinese people have lost a close comrade and a sincere friend. His glorious image and great achievements will be recorded in history forever," Xi said in a telegram to Raul Castro, state broadcaster CCTV said. Greek Prime Minister Alexis Tsipras tweeted: "Goodbye, commandante. Until the peoples' eternal victory." In a telegram to Raul Castro, Pope Francis offered "my sense of grief to your excellency and family." In a sign of his personal esteem, Francis signed the telegram, breaking from the Vatican's usual practice of have the secretary of state send such messages. Francis met Castro during the papal visit to Cuba in September 2015. Former U.S. president Jimmy Carter said he and his wife Rosalynn "remember fondly our visits with him in Cuba and his love of his country." The couple visited Cuba in 2002, long after Carter left office. Peter Hain, a former member of the British Cabinet and anti-apartheid campaigner, tempered praise for Castro with criticism of some aspects of his long rule. "Although responsible for indefensible human rights and free-speech abuses, Castro created a society of unparalleled access to free health, education and equal opportunity despite an economically throttling USA siege," Hain said. "His troops inflicted the first defeat on South Africa's troops in Angola in 1988, a vital turning point in the struggle against apartheid." A statement from the Spanish government hailed Castro as "a figure of enormous historical importance." "As a son of Spaniards, former president Castro always maintained close relations with Spain and showed great affection for his family and cultural ties. For this reason Spain especially shares the grief of Cuba's government and authorities," the government statement said. There were shouts and insults in Madrid as a small crowd composed of both pro- and anti-Castro supporters met in front of the Cuban embassy. "Fidel Castro in the 20th century did everything possible to destroy the colonial system, to establish cooperative relations," former Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev was quoted as telling the Interfax news agency. "Fidel survived and strengthened the country during the most severe U.S. blockade, while there was enormous pressure on him, and still led his country out of the blockade on the road of independent development." Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro recalled Castro's departure from Mexico on the yacht Granma with his brother Raul and several dozen supporters to start their revolution. "Sixty years after the Granma sailed from Mexico, Fidel sails toward the immortality of all those who fight their whole lives," Maduro tweeted. "Onward to victory, always!" Bolivian President Evo Morales said on Twitter that Castro was "the leader who taught us to fight for the sovereignty of the state and the dignity of the peoples of the world." While most of the official remembrances were complimentary, a few emphasized less-flattering views of the late leader. Ed Royce, chairman of the U.S. House of Representatives Foreign Affairs Committee, said "no one should rule anywhere near as long as Fidel Castro did." "His legacy is one of repression at home, and support for terrorism abroad. Sadly, Raul Castro is no better for Cubans who yearn for freedom," Royce said. "I hope his death can start a freedom revolution in Cuba," Denmark's Foreign Minister Kristian Jensen said. "Any demise is sad. In this case I believe that it can bring something good." Lebanon's militant Hezbollah group described Castro as a great leader. Ammar al-Moussawi, who is in charge of the group's international relations, lauded Castro as "a historic symbol whose life was a lighthouse to all revolutionaries around the world." Guyanese Prime Minister Moses Nagamootoo said Castro shared his island's resources with any nation that dared ask for help. The Castro government sent thousands of doctors and nurses to work in remote Caribbean areas where local and other foreign medical personnel had refused to go, Nagamootoo said. "His and Cuba's contribution to humanity and the Caribbean is unmatched by any other nation in terms of brotherly and sisterly relations. He was an international gift to humanity," he said.

In March, Obama became the first U.S. leader in nearly 90 years to visit the island nation, and in October he lifted several trade restrictions, a move that included an end to limits on the Cuban cigars Americans can bring home. Administration officials said at the time that the Obama was using the new rules to expand and solidify its opening to Cuba before Obama leaves the White House in January.

MEXICO CITY — For half a century, Fidel Castro's revolution transformed Cuba into a Communist state. During this time, his brother, Raúl, worked in his shadow, a loyal and junior partner. However, by the time Fidel passed, Raúl had significantly changed the island nation. He assumed presidential powers in 2006 and officially took the title in 2008. Raúl Castro then guided Cuba toward a future that was both notably different and surprisingly familiar.

Raúl systematically dismantled some core principles Fidel had considered sacred, subtly adjusting the Communist structure his brother had carefully built. In a major diplomatic move that surprised many, he began and finalized negotiations to end the fifty-year diplomatic standoff with the United States. This was a position Fidel had strictly maintained. This period is now clearly Raúl's Cuba, marked by several key developments.

Younger generations, like millennials, are now able to connect with relatives overseas using services such as Skype. Private farm owners, who are entrepreneurs, are actively finding and buying produce, including zucchini, to supply their restaurants. There has also been a significant increase in American tourists, with visitors now filling the historic streets of Old Havana.

query: What are the five flavors of the new line of finishing butters launched by Paula Deen?

documents.text: In the midst of a weak attempt at rebranding herself as "healthy," Food Network personality Paula Deen is launching her own line of butter at Walmart. After revealing her diabetes diagnosis a year and a half ago, Deen had been trying to move away from her image as a lying liar, pharma shill, shameless recipe thief, and private jet aficionado. In that light, butter may seem like an odd choice for her, but never fear, Deen's new butters are actually, really, truly being marketed as healthy. They're "finishing" butters that "let cooks bring a wonderful fresh butter taste to various dishes while just adding butter to the end of the cooking process," and they come in five flavors: A Look at Paula Deen's Butters [Photos: Neil Chopra/Eater] · Southern Grilling Butter ("Garlic goes with everything.") · The Lemon Dill Butter ("Perfect for a tangy and creamy grilled chicken y'all.") · The European Style Butter ("Y'all know that I love my travels to Europe and I call my European Style Butter 'Bread's Best Friend.'") · Sweet Citrus Zest Butter ("My Sweet Citrus Zest butter is hard to practice in moderation...it feels so fresh and clean on your palette [sic]." · Garden Herb Butter ("The herbs just explode in my mouth.") She also is launching a line of tortilla chips and sugar free chocolates, available at Walmarts in the Southeast. Video: Paula Deen on Today Yesterday, Deen made a couple appearances on television in which she used butter at the end of a recipe, as opposed to her old schtick of all-butter-all-the-time. It's healthier that way, or something? This is a woman who has a recipe for deep fried butter balls that takes two and a half hours to prepare. What happened to Deen not wanting to "associate her name with butter anymore?" About a year ago, on HGTV's Celebrities at Home, Deen said, "When you hear the name Paula Deen...I want you to think of the word 'hope.'" Hope you like compound butters. Anyway, she never mentioned her Walmart line in her appearances. Instead she appears to be laying last minute ground work to make it seem like finishing butter is just a thing that she does. Instead of promoting a product on television (which producers would likely not allow), she's selling the concept. Stopping by Today, she tells Matt Lauer, "I know you're going to find this heard to believe, but I've been trying to cook with less butter...I'm trying not to use butter during the cooking process. I wait til the end, and then I put the finishing butter on it." At no point does she mention she has a line of finishing butters at Walmart. "By the way, that still counts," Lauer astutely points out. Deen retorts, "But it only counts once instead of like six times." Video: Paula Deen on The Chew Next it was on to The Chew, where Deen made ribs with Mario Batali. They were finished with butter, obviously: "Y'all this is what I've been trying to do lately. I've been trying not to cook with butter like through the whole thing, but I just take the finishing butter and I finish up." The succotash was also finished with butter, which Batali points out got its fat from non-butter sources: "Instead of using that pesky butter, we use three pounds of bacon fat and then we finish it with a little bit of this finishing butter." Again, on neither The Chew nor Today did Deen mention she was selling finishing butters. Both The Chew and Today were a large part of her diabetes announcement tour. Deen announced her diabetes diagnosis and pharma sponsorship on Today on January 17, 2012. The next day, doing damage control, Deen went on The Chew and said she would donate an undisclosed percentage of her pharma profits. Video: Paula Deen on Paula Deen Foods Deen also released a truly bizarre video via Twitter in which she talks about why she is launching her food line. It has to do with death, and memories: "So with the food company, it's given me an opportunity to encourage families to come back to the kitchen together and build those memories, because you never know when you're going to have to count on those to get you through the day-to-day struggles." Buy Paula Deen's butter, because some day your family will die? Here's the full transcript and the video: Because of different things that have happened to me in my life, y'all, it really brought me to this stage in my life with a few passions. You know, I lost my folks early on when my brother and I were both very young, so I really, really, really count on my memories to fill in the gaps for me and to kind of complete my family. So with the food company, it's given me an opportunity to encourage families to come back to the kitchen together and build those memories, because you never know when you're going to have to count on those to get you through the day-to-day struggles. It's my hope to bring affordable, every day luxuries to these young mothers and fathers out there raising families, and to older couples that don't do a whole bunch of cooking any more. And you know one of the best parts of this whole Paula Deen Foods is a percentage of the profits are going to my new foundation called the Bag Lady Foundation. So it makes me very, very happy to know that I'm going to have a small part in paying it forward to others in their struggles. And I thank y'all so much for considering my products. I will try my best to never, ever let you down. Butter profits are going to charity, y'all. Here's the press release: PAULA DEEN FOODS MAKES A SPECIAL OCCASION FOR EVERYDAY MEALS America's Favorite Chef Brings the Magic of Her Traditional Recipes & Lighter Fare to Leading Retailers Across America Savannah, GA (June 5, 2013) – America's favorite cook, restaurateur, author and entrepreneur Paula Deen has launched her own line of food products at retail; Paula Deen Foods (www.pauladeenfoods.com), based on her traditional Southern recipes and lighter fare as well. Her initial product line will include all-natural tortilla chips made with fresh vegetables and a line of compound "finishing" butters that let cooks bring a wonderful fresh butter taste to various dishes while just adding butter to the end of the cooking process. Leading retailers Walgreens and Walmart will be featuring the all-natural chips and finishing butters, respectively. As well, Deen brings a very special benefit to this new venture, as a portion of the proceeds from the sales of her products will be donated to the organization that she has helped found, The Bag Lady Foundation (www.thebagladyfoundation.org}, which has a mission of serving women and families in need. "It is such an honor to be able to not only let the everyday cook bring special fresh meals, quality ingredients and great-tasting snacks to their tables to share with their friends and families, but it is an overwhelming joy to able to give back to communities across the country through The Bag Lady Foundation," says Paula Deen Foods co-founder Paula Deen. "It is my dream that in the years to come, I'll be inspiring the next great cooks to enjoy the best ingredients and products available today with their families, friends and those they love." It was just a little over two decades ago that Deen herself was challenged with supporting her family, as a single mom with two young boys. She was fighting agoraphobia and the only way she could make money was to cook in her own home and send her young boys door-to-door selling bag lunches and her first business "The Bag Lady" was born. Just a few decades later, Deen has launched this new venture in partnership with Buffalo, New York-based Nanco Group, whose principal Steven Nanula was involved with the successful Northeastern retailers Tops Neighborhood Market & Wilson Farms. "We're honored to bring Paula's great products and recipes to American families to provide fresh meal solutions with the best ingredients that make every day eating a special occasion," says Paula Deen Foods CEO, Steven Nanula. In 2013, in addition to her all-natural chips and finishing butters, Deen will be bringing a line of sugar-free and premium chocolates to the marketplace with these confections having been developed from her special private blend of chocolate. Late last year Deen launched her chocolates on QVC and the sales of her sugar-free line were tremendously successful. · Paula Deen Foods [Official Site] · Paula Deen and Sons Grill Up Quick and Easy Steak [Today] · Paula Deen's Camp Menu [ABC] · All Paula Deen Coverage on Eater [-E-]

Paula Deen, the renowned Southern chef, is venturing into the fashion industry with a new clothing line specifically designed to celebrate and flatter women of all body types. This ambitious expansion, a long-held dream for Deen, sees her partnering with the home shopping network Evine to offer stylish and comfortable apparel. As Deen shared with *People* magazine, "I've been trying to do this for years," noting that fan inquiries about her personal style and clothing choices are a constant across her platforms.

Deen's design philosophy is deeply rooted in her observations of diverse body shapes, famously stating, "You see all these things made for bananas. Well, I'm an apple. Most women are an apple or a pear. There are not many bananas." This personal insight directly informs the collection's focus on creating garments that enhance various figures. Evine's official website details that the collection includes contemporary pieces such as tops and tunics with designs like kimono sleeves and generous coverage, adaptable and breathable maxi dresses, and pants tailored to complement a range of body shapes, incorporating modern elements like ombre knits.

The collection aims to empower women, particularly those over 30, who may not feel they have "perfect bodies." Deen's objective is to design clothing that "accentuate your good points and try to camouflage your bad ones," applying her signature attention to detail to fabric selection and practical garment features. A key requirement, as highlighted by fashion consultants at The Style Network who advised on current trends, was for tops that extend below the hips to prevent wardrobe mishaps. Deen candidly expressed her design needs, stating, "I don’t know how to say this to you in a nice way, but it has to cover my front privates." She also playfully addressed common concerns about arm definition, coining the term "catfish belly" arms.

Consumers can explore Paula Deen's new fashion collection online, with prices ranging from approximately $35 for tops to $60 for longer dresses. This marks Deen's second collaboration with Evine, following a successful prior launch of her food products. Evine Live, Inc. announced that Deen is scheduled for appearances on Evine throughout the current month to promote her fashion line. Furthermore, experts from the Fashion Institute of Technology (FIT) have recognized the collection's strong emphasis on inclusivity.

EVINE Live is set to unveil "Paula Deen's Home," a new home décor collection premiering March 13 and 14, marking an exciting expansion of their successful partnership with the celebrated chef. This venture moves beyond Deen's established culinary success with EVINE Live, which began in 2015 with a popular line of kitchen and pantry items, to showcase her personal style and passion for interior design through a sophisticated range of home furnishings.

Penny Burnett, Chief Merchandising Officer at EVINE Live, emphasized Deen's diverse talents, noting, "While Paula Deen is celebrated as the 'Queen of Southern Cuisine,' her talents extend to painting, fashion, and a keen eye for home décor." Burnett further elaborated on the collaborative process, stating, "Our team collaborated closely with Paula, drawing inspiration from her art, personal style, and her Savannah, Georgia residence to create this collection. 'Paula Deen's Home' truly reflects her unique design philosophy, and we are thrilled to introduce it to her vast fan base." Paula Deen herself expressed her enthusiasm, sharing, "My experience with EVINE Live and connecting directly with my fans has been incredibly rewarding. I approach style much like I approach cooking – it's about combining the right elements. A touch of color, a hint of texture, and a balance of comfort and elegance. I'm eager to share my passions for art, style, and design with EVINE Live's customers."

The "Paula Deen's Home" collection will debut with an array of elegant bedding, featuring vibrant designs inspired by Deen's artwork and personal home aesthetic. During the premiere event, viewers can expect to see patterned and embroidered coverlet sets, comforter sets, embroidered bed sheets, and cotton blankets, all meticulously designed to infuse any bedroom with charm and comfort. This expansion into home décor highlights a significant evolution in Deen's brand presence with EVINE Live, demonstrating a broader creative vision beyond her renowned culinary expertise.

Viewers can access EVINE Live programming through various platforms, including cable and satellite television, dedicated mobile applications, and live streaming via www.evine.com. The network is readily available on DIRECTV Channels 73 and 316, DISH Network channels 134 and 228, and is carried by major cable providers nationwide. For those seeking to locate EVINE Live in their specific area, a helpful resource can be found at bit.ly/1CNa450. Additional details regarding Paula Deen's EVINE Live collections are accessible at www.evine.com/PaulaDeen, with general information about EVINE Live available at www.evine.com.

**About EVINE Live Inc.**
EVINE Live Inc. (NASDAQ:EVLV) operates as a digital commerce company, delivering a diverse selection of proprietary and name-brand products through an engaging television, online, and mobile shopping experience. Reaching an estimated 88 million cable and satellite homes, EVINE Live provides 24-hour programming designed to foster community engagement with its customer base via a comprehensive digital shopping platform.

**About Paula Deen**
Paula Deen is a distinguished New York Times bestselling author, having sold over 11 million cookbooks. Her "Paula Deen Live!" tour, initiated in 2014, featured interactive cooking demonstrations and fan engagement. In the same year, she launched the "Paula Deen Network," an interactive digital platform offering exclusive content. Deen's culinary ventures also include The Lady & Sons restaurant in Savannah, Georgia, co-founded with her sons Jamie and Bobby Deen, and Paula Deen's Family Kitchen in Pigeon Forge, TN, which opened in April 2015. Her media footprint extends to the podcast "Get Cooking with Paula Deen" and the radio show "What's Cooking with Paula Deen." Her 2015 cookbook, "Paula Deen Cuts The Fat: 250 Favorite Recipes All Lightened Up," was released in September. In the fall of 2015, Deen participated in the twenty-first season of ABC's "Dancing with the Stars." She commands a substantial social media presence, with millions of likes and followers across Facebook, Twitter, and Pinterest. Paula Deen Ventures is headquartered in Savannah, Georgia, with additional offices in New York.

Paula Deen, a culinary icon renowned for her Southern comfort food and a former contestant on "Dancing With the Stars," has consistently pursued ambitious career paths. Her latest venture marks a delightful departure from the kitchen, stepping directly into the world of fashion with the launch of her brand-new clothing line, "Paula Deen's Closet." This highly anticipated collection recently debuted in collaboration with Evine, signaling a significant expansion of her established brand.

### A Long-Held Fashion Ambition

While this transition might seem unexpected, Deen asserts it has been a long-standing aspiration. "I've been trying to do this for years," Deen confided to PeopleStyle. "It was a little hard for me to break out of the kitchen because that’s how people think of me. But if you notice, I never cooked naked. I was always wearing clothes!" She further elaborated on the inspiration, noting, "One of my most asked questions out there — whether it’s in person, through the mail, or on Facebook — is ‘Paula, where did you get that top? Where did you get that outfit?’" These persistent inquiries underscored a clear demand and presented an avenue for Deen to engage with her audience on a novel platform.

### Designing for Real Bodies

Deen's vision for "Paula Deen's Closet" is deeply informed by her understanding of diverse female physiques. "I wanted to do something like this because I understand the body that’s not perfect," she stated. "You see all these things made for bananas. Well, I’m an apple. Most women are an apple or a pear. There are not many bananas." This core philosophy directly shaped the collection's design, featuring:

*   **Flattering Fits:** Tops, dresses, and pants are meticulously crafted to enhance various body shapes.
*   **Diverse Styles:** The line includes ombré knits, stretch maxis, and kimono sleeve tunics, all designed to celebrate individuality.

"When you get up over 30, most of us don’t have those perfect bodies anymore," Deen candidly remarked. "You have to accentuate your good points and try to camouflage your bad ones. I want to cover all body types. I am not trying to please just one person. I’m trying to please a multitude of people with this line. And I think people trust me. They know that I will not lie to them … To the best of my knowledge, I will tell the truth." This unwavering commitment to authenticity and inclusivity forms the bedrock of her brand, a sentiment echoed by fashion experts who emphasize the growing market for size-inclusive and body-positive apparel.

### Practical Design Elements

Deen meticulously prioritized several key elements during the development of "Paula Deen's Closet":

*   **Strategic Length:** Tops are designed to extend comfortably below the hips. "I don’t know how to say this to you in a nice way, but it has to cover my front privates," she explained. "I want my shirttail to cover my front privates and hopefully my back, too."
*   **Addressing Common Concerns:** The line tackles issues like what she humorously terms "catfish belly" arms. "Have you ever seen a catfish? You know how they have a smooth floppy belly on them? Well, that’s what we refer to as ‘catfish belly’ arms," she elaborated, showcasing her practical design approach. This focus on relatable design challenges has been praised by fashion commentators for its down-to-earth appeal.
*   **Modest Cleavage Coverage:** "When I’m doing V-necks, I want that placement perfect so you can lean over and you don’t have wardrobe malfunctions," Deen stated. "I don’t like when you show everything." This focus ensures confidence and comfort, a principle that aligns with the growing movement towards modest fashion.

### Value and Comfort at the Forefront

Affordability is another significant consideration, with every item priced under $60. This dedication to value mirrors her entrepreneurial origins. "Just like when I opened my very first restaurant, I told my sons, ‘We want to serve the very best meal that we’re capable of preparing at a fair price’," she recalled. "So everything I do must have value. People must feel like they are being treated fairly. I’m not interested in trying to gouge anybody." This commitment ensures accessibility for a broad customer base, a strategy that has proven successful for brands like Old Navy and Target, as noted by retail analysts.

Comfort is paramount, reflecting Deen's personal values. "I like to think that I’m comfortable to be around, and that definitely transfers into the clothing line," she said. "And at the stage I’m in, comfort is important. That’s definitely the way I feel about my shoes. I think so many young girls kill their feet with their shoes." This emphasis on comfort resonates with a broader trend in fashion, where practicality and well-being are increasingly prioritized, as highlighted in recent reports by the Fashion Institute of Technology.

### Navigating Modern Trends

Deen also expressed a degree of bewilderment regarding certain contemporary fashion trends, particularly those embraced by younger demographics. "I’m quite puzzled when I see a lot of things," she admitted. "There are a lot of funky styles out. I had to get used to the jeans with rips in them. But now I’m cool with it. I get it now. But that took me a while to understand why you’d want to wear things with holes on them."

However, she remains perplexed by the increasingly revealing styles prevalent on red carpets today. "Definitely [young people are] showing a lot more than when I was their age," she observed. "Do you think the Kardashians are somewhat responsible for that? I don’t know. I mean I don’t know them. But the role models are there that these girls have to follow, they want to be trendy too." This observation touches upon the influence of celebrity culture and social media on fashion choices, a topic frequently discussed by cultural critics and fashion journalists.

Paula Deen recently returned to the Evine studios to promote her new clothing line, which is now available for viewing and purchase on evine.com. This launch signifies an exciting and significant new chapter for the beloved Southern personality, marking her successful transition into the fashion industry.

Paula Deen is orchestrating a significant comeback in the culinary landscape, strategically re-establishing her position as a preeminent figure in Southern cuisine. This multifaceted resurgence is characterized by a robust expansion into digital platforms, a foray into mobile gaming, and a notable return to television. Following the successful launch of her subscription-based digital hub, the Paula Deen Network, last fall, Deen has now introduced a mobile game, "Paula Deen’s Recipe Quest." This digital initiative is complemented by her reentry into television via Evine Live, a prominent broadcast shopping network. This marks her return to the medium after her departure from the Food Network in 2013. On Evine Live, Deen is actively promoting her exclusive line of branded food products, positioning herself as a direct competitor to established shopping giants such as QVC and the Home Shopping Network (HSN).

A striking aspect of Deen's current public image is her noticeably slimmer and healthier appearance. She has successfully shed 35 pounds, a personal transformation that resonates with her ongoing journey managing Type 2 diabetes. Despite these profound personal shifts, her commitment to the comforting and familiar flavors of Southern cooking remains the bedrock of her brand identity. Mark Bozek, CEO of Evine, articulated his considerable enthusiasm for Deen's return, stating to FoxNews.com, "Deen’s team approached us with a fresh concept, and we were on board within five minutes. She possesses a large and exceptionally loyal fan base that is clearly eager for her return." This sentiment is further reflected in the nature of her new culinary offerings. These include two distinct versions of her renowned Ooey Gooey Cake – in classic butter and decadent chocolate flavors – alongside two varieties of smoked sausages, premium holiday hams, savory glazes, and a curated line of chocolate candies. Notably, this confectionary range features a sugar-free option, catering to a broader audience.

### Innovative Culinary Creations and Product Details

Among the more distinctive items in Deen’s latest food collection are chocolates meticulously crafted in the shape of butter, an ingredient intrinsically linked to her culinary persona. These unique confections are available in milk, dark, and white chocolate varieties. Initially, there was some ambiguity regarding their classification – whether they were chocolate-flavored butters or a novel Deen recipe. However, the Evine team has clarified that these are not categorized as baker's chocolate. While they can be incorporated into recipes as decorative shavings or melted for drizzling, their primary appeal lies in their novelty and their humorous resemblance to shaped Hershey bars.

In terms of taste, the chocolates are decidedly sweet. The dark chocolate, while possessing a smooth texture, lacked the profound cocoa intensity often sought in richer varieties. The milk and white chocolate options, though creamy, presented a relatively straightforward flavor profile. A significant practical challenge associated with these products is their physical form. Measuring approximately an inch in diameter, their shape makes them difficult to cut or bite into, rendering direct consumption somewhat impractical. Melting them proved to be a necessary step to enjoy smaller portions, suggesting that as a standalone snack, they might demand more effort than anticipated.

### Strategic Product Placement and Brand Alignment

Despite the practical considerations, the novelty factor of these butter-shaped chocolates is undeniable. This is further amplified by their cheerful packaging, which features a welcoming "Hey Y'all" greeting and a smiling image of Deen. This strategic inclusion of conversation-starting products aligns seamlessly with Evine's business model, which also showcases unique items such as seafood sourced directly from the fishermen featured on the popular show "Deadliest Catch." Bozek further elaborated on Deen's widespread appeal, noting, "Part of what she does is simply have fun—there's no doubt about it. We are in the business of entertaining customers. Having a personality that brings smiles to people's faces perfectly matches our mission." This approach underscores Deen's enduring ability to connect with consumers through personality and relatable, yet distinctive, culinary offerings.

Preparation Cream the butter, cream cheese, salt and pepper together with an electric mixer until smooth. Using a very small ice cream scoop, or melon baller, form 1-inch balls of butter mixture and arrange them on a parchment or waxed paper lined sheet pan. Freeze until solid. Coat the frozen balls in flour, egg and then bread crumbs and freeze again until solid. When ready to fry, preheat oil in a deep-fryer to 350°. Fry balls for 10 to 15 seconds until just light golden. Drain on paper towels before serving. Yields about 30 balls.

Paula Deen, the beloved Southern culinary icon, is expanding her brand into the world of fashion with the launch of "Paula Deen's Closet," a new clothing line available through the digital commerce company Evine Live. This venture marks a significant evolution for Deen, solidifying her presence as a comprehensive lifestyle personality on the Evine platform.

"If I have something that all my friends out there want, I want to be able to bring it to them for a reasonable price," Deen explained. The creative process behind Paula Deen's Closet involves a unique collaboration. Evine's dedicated design team makes periodic visits to Deen's Savannah, Georgia, home. There, they draw inspiration directly from her personal wardrobe, exploring her extensive closet to inform the development of the new line. Deen's personal collection is substantial, housed in a closet measuring approximately "18 feet by 20 feet." This impressive space is optimized for storage, with much of the room's volume utilized by its height. To maximize this vertical space, Deen employs a "moving dry-cleaning rack" system, a practical solution for organizing her vast array of garments.

The inaugural presentation of Paula Deen's Closet on Evine was scheduled for Monday afternoon, with a follow-up showing planned for Tuesday night, spanning two hours. Deen has indicated that the intention is to introduce new items to the collection on a monthly basis, ensuring a dynamic and evolving offering for consumers. Looking ahead, with her culinary, home goods, and fashion lines established, Deen has expressed interest in exploring further product categories. She has voiced a desire to pursue jewelry as the focus of her next potential product line.

This expansion into fashion aligns with Evine Live's strategic initiative over the past two years to bolster its offerings with exclusive celebrity-driven product lines. In the realm of fashion, Evine has recently welcomed other notable personalities, including Vanessa Williams, Nancy O’Dell, and Karen Fairchild, further diversifying its celebrity partnerships. The inclusion of Paula Deen's Closet represents a significant addition to this strategy, leveraging her established brand recognition and loyal following to drive sales and enhance the platform's appeal. The emphasis on comfortable, stylish, and washable clothing, catering to a broader range of sizes, positions Paula Deen's Closet as a potentially impactful player in the accessible fashion market.

This collection showcases a curated selection of kitchen and pantry essentials, stylish apparel, and charming home décor from the celebrated "Queen of Southern Cuisine." **About the Guest:**

Paula Deen, a true culinary icon and the undisputed queen of southern cooking, is a testament to self-made success. Her culinary journey began with secrets learned from her grandmother. Deen's entrepreneurial spirit first took flight with her business, The Bag Lady, which paved the way for her to open her beloved restaurant, The Lady and Sons, alongside her sons Bobby and Jamie just five years later. query: What joking remark did Rep. Debbie Wasserman Schultz make about Mitt Romney during 'Fox News Sunday'?

documents.text: Rep. Debbie Wasserman Schultz didn’t miss an opportunity to make a dog joke at Mitt Romney’s expense Sunday morning. “I think the headline of the day is that you just drove 2000 miles from Florida all the way to New Hampshire with four dogs, the cat, the kids and the husband. How are you after all that?,” said "Fox News Sunday" host John Roberts, filling in for Chris Wallace. “And lived to tell about it. And all of the dogs were actually in the car! So I proved it can be done,” the Democratic National Committee chairwoman said, laughing. The presumptive Republican presidential nominee, of course, has been hounded by Democrats and dog lovers for driving down the highway with the family dog, Seamus, in a carrier strapped to the roof of the car in the 1980s. Bill Kristol, editor of The Weekly Standard, issued a pointed warning to the Romney campaign on Sunday, asserting that the presumptive Republican presidential nominee faces an uphill battle against President Barack Obama unless he can convince voters he possesses a concrete strategy for economic revitalization.

Kristol articulated his concerns during an appearance on "Fox News Sunday," stating, "President Obama had three disappointing months, but he's holding his own. And if I were in the Romney campaign, that would worry me." He underscored his point by referencing a recent Fox News poll, which revealed that a mere 41 percent of voters believe the incumbent president has a clear plan for economic improvement. While Kristol acknowledged these figures are not ideal for an incumbent facing a sluggish economy, he highlighted that the numbers for Romney were considerably more concerning.

The poll indicated that only 27 percent of voters believe Governor Romney has a clear plan to improve the economy, with 55 percent disagreeing. Kristol emphasized the critical nature of this disparity: "I don't think you can beat an incumbent president even if the economy’s slow if 27 percent of the voters think you as the challenger don't have a clear plan for improving the economy."

Key takeaways from Kristol's assessment include:

*   **Incumbent's Position:** President Obama, despite a slow economy, garners 41% support for his economic plan, a figure Kristol finds less than ideal but not insurmountable.
*   **Challenger's Hurdle:** Governor Romney's economic plan approval stands at a significantly lower 27%, a deficit Kristol deems too substantial to overcome an incumbent.
*   **Voter Perception:** The core issue, according to Kristol, is voter confidence in Romney's ability to present a clear and viable economic recovery strategy.

Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley launched a strong critique of Mitt Romney's financial dealings on Sunday, asserting that a presidential candidate with offshore accounts, particularly those in Swiss banks, signals a deficiency in dedication to American employment and infrastructure. Speaking on ABC's "This Week," O'Malley, who also leads the Democratic Governors Association, remarked, "President Obama isn't running against the Almighty; he's running against the alternative."

He identified this alternative as Mitt Romney, referencing Romney's tenure as governor, which O'Malley characterized as having the "47th worst job creation rate." Additionally, O'Malley accused Romney of possessing a "penchant—a talent—for offshoring American jobs, sending them overseas and also maintaining offshore bank accounts in Bermuda, Swiss bank accounts."

O'Malley underscored his belief that these financial strategies do not foster national advancement. "I’ve never known of a Swiss bank account to build an American bridge, a Swiss bank account to create American jobs, or Swiss bank accounts to rebuild the levies to protect the people of New Orleans," he declared. "That's not an economic strategy for moving our country forward."

The governor drew a contrast with President Barack Obama's priorities. O'Malley contended that while President Obama emphasizes investments in education, infrastructure, and innovation, Romney's actions suggest his main concern is protecting the assets of wealthy Americans. "He wants to make it even easier for very wealthy people to avoid paying taxes, to offshore American jobs, and to maintain their Swiss bank accounts while our roads and bridges and levies crumble," O'Malley concluded.

Obama campaign senior adviser Robert Gibbs expressed concerns on Sunday regarding the public's understanding of Mitt Romney's financial dealings, stating that Americans lack sufficient information to ascertain if any of Romney's activities are illegal. "We don't know. Nobody knows," Gibbs, a former White House press secretary, remarked on CNN's "State of the Union" when questioned about whether the presumptive Republican presidential nominee might be circumventing tax law.

Gibbs elaborated on CNN, urging Romney to provide greater transparency. "The one thing he can do, Candy, to clear up whether or not he's done anything illegally, whether he's shielding his income from taxes in Bermuda or Switzerland, is to do what every other presidential candidate has done and that's to release a series of years of their own tax returns," Gibbs told host Candy Crowley. He emphasized, "The best way to see if Mitt Romney is complying with American tax law is to have him release more of his tax returns."

While Romney has released his 2010 tax return and an estimate for his 2011 taxes, he has resisted releasing returns from other years. Gibbs drew a parallel to personal financial choices, stating, "I pick a bank because there's an ATM near my home." He then highlighted specific financial details, noting, "Romney had a bank account in Switzerland."

Further questioning the transparency of Romney's finances, Gibbs stated, "Nobody knows why he has a corporation in Bermuda." He also raised concerns about disclosure, asking, "Why he failed to disclose that on seven different financial disclosures. Why he transferred it to somebody else's purview the day before he became governor of Massachusetts." Gibbs concluded with a pointed critique of Romney's campaign message, remarking, "This is a guy whose slogan is 'Believe in America' and it should be 'business in Bermuda.'"

Read more about: Robert Gibbs, Mitt Romney, Tax Returns

query: What happened to Vera Smith's body after Hurricane Katrina, and who took action to ensure she was buried?

documents.text: For five days after Hurricane Katrina struck, the body of Vera Smith lay where it fell, alone on the roadside, partly covered by a tarpaulin. Eventually – with the authorities apparently having neither the resources or inclination to remove the corpse – a local man buried her under a makeshift grave. He said prayers, fashioned a cross and placed a plastic sheet over the soil on to which someone painted the words: “Here Lies Vera. God Help Us”. Ten years after the storm that killed 1,833 people and whose destruction topped $100bn, the death of 66-year Vera Smith and the utter break-down of authority symbolised by her abandonment, continue to haunt the city. There are some in New Orleans – many of whose residents have an intense relationship with the dead – who literally believe they have felt her spirit. Vera Smith, in an undated photograph provided by her family Visiting the junction of Magazine Street and Jackson Avenue in the Garden District today, it is hard to imagine the scenes that played out ten years ago in the chaotic aftermath of the storm, when Ms Smith had gone to a local shop to try and find supplies for her and her partner, Max Keene. Across the US and around the world, people watched stunned and outraged as the media carried images of the seemingly countless corpses left stranded by the storm. They lay rotting on the roadside, floating in floodwater, hanging from wires. The US government appeared both hapless and heartless, failing to come to the aid of some of its most most vulnerable people, a large percentage of whom were black. Television viewers could not believe that what they were watching was happening in their country, rather than somewhere in the distant developing world. President Barack Obama, who will this week visit the city to mark the anniversary, said there had been a “shameful breakdown in government.” More than 1,800 were killed by Katrina and the flooding it triggered Ms Smith did not die in the actual storm but was killed in the lawless aftermath, apparently the victim of a hit and run accident, the perpetrator of which was never found. Her frail partner – who passed away the following year - did the little he could to provide some dignity to her body. After five days of intense heat, someone decided more serious was required. That person was John Lee, an engineer and businessman who has a home in the Garden District. Over a bottle of root beer in Stein’s Deli, located on Magazine Street, Mr Lee said he had gone to the local police station and asked them to remove her body. Mr Lee said they not only declined to do so, but also refused him permission to move it. Eventually, they said he could bury it but could not move it. As a result, he dug the grave on the actual pavement. Mr Lee has written a book of his experiences, Our Sleepless Nights: Surviving Katrina and Burying Miss Vera. In it he details the break-down of authority and the looming fear that permeated – some of it exacerbated by inaccurate reports about looting – in the ten days after Katrina. But his book is also testimony to the acts of quiet heroism that took place, the coming together of people, often strangers, to try and help one another. John Lee led efforts to build the makeshift grave for Vera Smith “I think of her often,” Mr Lee said of Ms Smith, whom he used to see around the neighbourhood. “I do have faith. I do wish she is at rest.” He said he remembered the prayers that he and two black women – people he had met that day – spoke as they buried her. “We were praying for her soul.” Ms Smith, whose full name was Elvira Briones Smith, was born in Mexico and grew in Texas before she moved to New Orleans. She was married four time has two adult daughters. For 20 years or more she lived with CN Keene, better known as Max. She liked clothes and shoes, shopping and drinking, and was a regular at the local Catholic church, St Mary’s Assumption. They had met when she was working as a waitress in a bar and he was working off-shore for an oil company. “She was married, her old man left her. I had a different girlfriend then, she left me. It was the right time. We just got together. Every now and then it happens that way,” Mr Keene told The Independent ten years ago. “We used to lie in bed. I’d drink bourbon, she’d read books.” Large parts of New Orleans were inundated by flooding Ms Smith’s family learned of her death from the front page of a Texas newspaper. Three months after Katrina, Ms Smith’s body was eventually collected, taken to a mortuary and her ashes returned to her family in Santa Rosa. In November 2005, they were interred in a family plot. Ms Smith’s daughter, Cindy Briones, said her mother had returned to visit the family every year for Thanksgiving Day. She said she was also delighted to be among her relatives and spend weeks in advance of her visit buying special gifts for each of them. Speaking from Santa Rosa, she said she was grateful to Mr Lee and the others who had built the makeshift grave, but said it had been important for the family to have her returned to Texas. She said she and her sister remained traumatised by what happened to their mother and the their emotions had been stretched every year on the anniversary of her death. “I still have flash-backs. I have seen her body on the internet, pictures of before it was buried,” she said. This image - believed to be of Vera Smith - was left at the grave In some limited respects, Ms Smith’s family was fortunate in that they were able to locate and recover her body. Even today, some of those killed by the storm remain unidentified and unclaimed. Asked what officials had used to identify the remains, the office of Jeffrey Rouse, the coroner for New Orleans, said their work made use of dental records, DNA testing, plane manifests, unique scars and tattoos, follow-ups on missing persons cases, jewellery, unusual hair, finger-prints, medical implants and government identification. In the City Park neighbourhood, a memorial to Katrina, designed in the shape that the Category Five storm assumed as it made landfall, was erected in the memory of those who died. Even today, the remains of 30 people who died during Katrina have not been identified Despite the effort of the coroner’s office, the remains of 30 people were not identified while a total of 43 were never claimed. “The memorial received the remains of the unidentified or unclaimed,” read the words on a black granite stone. Jayson Orlando, who works at the nearby Sacred Grinds coffee shop, said New Orleans was less care-free since Katrina hit. “Even cities feel trauma,” he said. “It’s not just people.” Since 2005, several memorials have also been erected in the memory of Ms Smith. The one that currently stands attached to a wall was built by an artist, Simon Hardeveld, who is originally French, and metal worker Scott Evert. Mr Hardeveld, whose memorial features a working fountain and bright colours, said he had been asked to make it two years ago by the owner of an adjoining burger restaurant, built on the site where Ms Smith’s grave had stood. He had contacted him amid reports that the restaurant was haunted. The reports were taken so seriously that a local television channel dispatched a film crew. “This is New Orleans. People believe these things,” he said with a shrug. Blaine Presenbach built a restaurant on the plot where the makeshift grave had stood Blaine Presenbach, 36, the owner of the Charcoal restaurant, said some of his workers had joked after the “ghost of Vera” when a piece of equipment broke down. But he said nobody had really believed it. Rather, he had commissioned the memorial out of respect for Ms Smith and what had befallen her at that location. “We don’t want to forget it,” he said. Mr Evert, the man who did the metal work for the memorial, heard the stories too and was unsure what to think. And then he set about the task of shaping the memorial. “It was weird. I don’t really believe this sort of thing,” he said. “But the job became really tedious and difficult.” He said that each point in the job, something went wrong. Each star – something that would normally attach simply – fell off. “It seemed to me she did not like what I was doing,” he said. Simon Hardeveld and Scott Evert at the memorial they built Mr Evert said he too felt the trauma of having lived through the storm, having used a boat to help rescue stranded people and remembering the terrible scenes. Even now it upset him to recall what happened to his city, to people such as Ms Smith. He likened the memorial built by himself to small shrines he had seen when he visited Thailand, a place where a spirit might sit. “The point we wanted to make was that we wanted to remember Vera,” he added. “It was just to say ‘this was someone who lived here and something terrible happened to her and people think she was worth remembering’.”

Hurricane Katrina's legacy is often remembered through a lens of national unity and resilience. This narrative, championed by leaders like New Orleans Mayor Mitch Landrieu on its fifth anniversary in 2010, spoke of the storm as a force that washed away divisions. It fostered a shared understanding, echoing President Obama's calls for rebuilding and harmony, and highlighting acts of heroism and neighborly support. However, as significant anniversaries of this devastating event approach, a more complex and often overlooked narrative emerges. Katrina, which tragically claimed over 1,800 lives and displaced tens of thousands, not only exposed the raw fury of nature but also laid bare profound governmental failures. The inadequate response and scandalous mismanagement left many residents abandoned and without essential aid. This transformed the disaster into a stark symbol of governmental dysfunction and political collapse.

### Divergent Perceptions of Katrina

The catastrophe revealed the existence of multiple publics, each with distinct perspectives shaped by geography, religion, economics, ethnicity, and race, rather than a singular "American" experience. Crucially, the disaster continues to resonate differently within Black and White American consciousness. While White Americans often viewed Katrina as an unfortunate incident of bad luck and incompetence affecting everyone, Black Americans perceived it as a stark confirmation of systemic indifference to Black lives. This racialized interpretation was powerfully voiced by figures such as Rev. Jesse Jackson and Rev. Al Sharpton, and memorably captured by rapper Kanye West's poignant statement, "George Bush doesn't care about black people." Sociologist Ron Eyerman argues that Katrina left an "indelible mark on American collective memory, not merely as a severe storm but as a social disaster, a catastrophe which revealed the nation at its worst." For Black Americans, this was particularly true, as they witnessed harrowing images of survivors pleading for help and bodies floating through flooded streets.

### The Shift Towards Racial Pessimism

The events of Katrina marked a profound shift toward racial pessimism within Black America, a sentiment that persists even with a Black president. This collective memory directly informs contemporary movements like "Black Lives Matter," highlighting the ongoing struggle against police violence and systemic injustice.

### Key Factors Contributing to Divergent Perceptions

Several key factors contributed to this divergence in perception:

#### Racialized Media Coverage

News coverage and commentary played a significant role in shaping public understanding. Studies, such as those analyzed by the Pew Research Center, indicated a correlation between mentions of race and the use of terms like "refugees" for survivors. Exaggerated reports of violence and inaccurate accounts of crime were prevalent. For instance, an Associated Press photo depicted a Black man carrying soda as "looting," while a similar image of a White couple was captioned as "finding bread and soda." Conservative commentators like Bill O'Reilly further fueled this divide, characterizing the poor in New Orleans as "drug addicted" and "thugs."

#### Divergent Blame and Perceptions

Research, including analyses from institutions like the University of Michigan's Survey Research Center, indicated that White respondents were more likely to blame residents and the mayor for being trapped. Black respondents were more likely to view those who took supplies as desperate survivors. Right-wing media outlets often expressed outright disdain for those left behind, dismissing the idea of legitimate grievances.

#### Governmental Response and Inequality

Polls taken shortly after the hurricane, such as those conducted by Gallup, revealed a strong belief among Black Americans that New Orleans would have been better prepared and the government response faster had the victims been wealthier and whiter. A Newsweek poll corroborated this, suggesting the slow response was linked to the racial makeup of the affected population. Then-Mayor Ray Nagin articulated this sentiment, stating that a similar event in Orange County, California, or South Beach, Miami, would have elicited a different response. Research, such as the study "Feeling the Pain of My People: Hurricane Katrina, Racial Inequality, and the Psyche of Black America" by Dr. Angela Davis, confirms this heightened, racialized response, finding that Black Americans across the country identified with the primarily African American victims and experienced deep anger and depression.

### The Impact on Black Racial Optimism
