Ten years ago, on March 21st, a single Tweet launched a global phenomenon that continues to shape how individuals connect around shared interests and passions. As we commemorate this significant anniversary, our focus is squarely on celebrating you, our vibrant community, whose engagement has been instrumental in Twitter's evolution.

This day of profound appreciation and gratitude will commence as March 21st dawns across the globe, with international offices initiating celebrations that will gracefully follow the sun's arc, culminating at our headquarters in San Francisco. We eagerly anticipate sharing this momentous occasion with each and every one of you, recognizing your collective impact in making history, driving change, fostering support, and sharing laughter daily. Your ongoing engagement is actively defining Twitter's future trajectory, a testament to the community we've built together.

With sincere appreciation,

The Twitter Team

#LoveTwitter

## Jack Dorsey's Decade on Twitter: A Digital Footprint Examined

Ten years ago, Jack Dorsey, co-founder and CEO of Twitter, sent the platform's inaugural message: "just setting up my twttr." This simple declaration marked the genesis of a platform that has since profoundly reshaped the digital landscape. Today, Twitter navigates a complex environment, characterized by executive shifts, slowing growth, and considerable speculation about its future. Amidst this, Dorsey remains an active presence on the platform, his timeline offering a unique, if sometimes perplexing, window into his world. This document delves into a decade of Dorsey's public pronouncements, seeking to understand the man behind the microblogging revolution.

### A Decade of Tweets: From Bubble Tea to Scientology

Dorsey's Twitter feed is a diverse mosaic of interests and observations. Over the past decade, he has shared insights ranging from the simple enjoyment of bubble tea and often misattributed quotes to his admiration for figures like Alyssa Milano and Snoop Dogg. His tweets have also touched upon personal matters like dental hygiene, the intricacies of Scientology, and even the distinctive cadence of "souljaboytellem." To offer a public service and perhaps indulge a touch of masochism, I spent approximately 11 hours meticulously reviewing a decade of Dorsey's tweets. (For transparency, I did skip the numerous tweets about Stephen Curry, as the admiration for his athletic prowess became repetitive.) The rationale for this deep dive is simple: while Twitter has become an immense and often inscrutable corporate entity, Jack Dorsey, as an individual, has made his digital life publicly accessible for examination over the last ten years.

Following this extensive review, I present what I believe to be his most noteworthy and revealing tweets. Happy 10th birthday, Twitter! Your impact, much like the advent of electricity, has been profound.

### Early Affinities and Enigmatic Connections

Looking back, some early tweets warrant a second thought. The frequent and effusive praise for Alyssa Milano stands out as a peculiar characteristic of Dorsey's early online persona. Beyond their apparent personal connection, Milano has demonstrated her engagement with Dorsey's ventures, having written a comic book inspired by him and serving as an advisor for Square, his other prominent company. The exact nature of this relationship remains somewhat enigmatic, but it certainly adds an intriguing layer to his digital footprint. This particular tweet, a curious juxtaposition of individuals and concepts, represents a truly singular confluence.

### The "Product" of Jack Dorsey

As one former employee astutely observed, "The greatest product Jack Dorsey ever made was Jack Dorsey." Judging solely by his public tweets, this "product" is characterized by a penchant for clear-rimmed sunglasses, a notable affinity for Alyssa Milano, a fleeting engagement with social activism, a considerable amount of self-congratulatory boasting, and a pervasive sense of airy solipsism often presented as profound wisdom. Indeed, the most persistent question that arose after poring over thousands of his tweets was how he managed to achieve such remarkable success. With an estimated net worth in the tens of billions of dollars, there is surprisingly little within his digital pronouncements to suggest an inherently unique or groundbreaking intellect at play.

### Evolution of an Online Persona

Scrutinizing anyone's decade-long online presence is an inherently awkward endeavor, invariably serving as a stark reminder of our collective past embarrassments. However, Dorsey's deliberate decision to maintain his extensive tweet history, particularly given Twitter's inherent strength in capturing fleeting, in-the-moment interactions, is a curious choice. To his credit, Dorsey has demonstrably evolved in his online presentation. His tweets post-2010 are, on average, marginally less cringe-inducing, though arguably no less mundane, than their predecessors. They have also become more curated, shifting from the informal, status-update style of early Facebook to a more polished persona befitting an individual with a significant public profile to maintain. The Jack Dorsey of 2006 and the Jack Dorsey of 2016 both embody Silicon Valley's core ethos: the art of imbuing seemingly insubstantial concepts with an appearance of significant value and future promise. The latter, however, executes this with a far greater degree of sophistication.

### The Future of Twitter and its Founder

The broader implications of this for Twitter's future remain a separate and complex discussion. After wading through thousands of tweets, my conclusion is still a resounding "I don't know," but please, whatever happens, don't take @dog_rates away from us. Regardless of the platform's ultimate fate, we will always have this digital archive as a testament to its origins and its enigmatic founder. The insights gleaned from this decade-long digital diary offer a unique perspective on the evolution of a tech titan and the platform he helped create.

It’s hard to believe, but the microblogging service Twitter turns 10 years old today. On March 21, 2006, the service officially went live with Jack Dorsey sending the first tweet ever, which read: "just setting up my twttr." "On March 21, ten years ago, it began with a single Tweet. Since then, every moment of everyday, people connect about the things they care about most — all over the world," the company wrote in a blog post commemorating its tenth anniversary. "As we mark this milestone, it’s you we want to celebrate. As March 21 begins around the world, each of our global offices will kick off the day by showing our appreciation and gratitude — starting in Sydney and following the sun to headquarters in San Francisco. We are excited to celebrate with all of you. Throughout the years, you’ve made Twitter what it is today and you’re shaping what it will be in the future. Thank you for making history, driving change, lifting each other up and laughing together every day." The company also released a two-and-a-half minute commemorative video that shows Twitter’s role as a purveyor of information and activist platform for world events such as the Arab Spring, the 2011 Japanese tsunami, marriage equality, and the Black Lives Matter movement. The video also shows off the more humorous side of Twitter with funny tweets from celebs and viral videos and GIFs. Perhaps most interestingly, however—and a sign of what the company plans to do in the future—is the video ends with a thank-you tweet that goes over Twitter’s 140-character limit. The writer of the tweet then deletes a few characters and replaces them with emojis to fit the tweet in. This could be a sign that Twitter has decided to stick with its defining 140-character limit instead of raising the length of its tweets.

Twitter, a platform that once limited communication to 140 characters, now connects over 300 million active users globally. While its user base is smaller than Facebook's 1.5 billion, Twitter's influence is disproportionately vast, particularly among politicians, journalists, and celebrities. This document explores how this "little blue bird" has profoundly reshaped various sectors, drawing insights from a panel of experts.

In the realm of **Politics**, Sharon Coen, a senior lecturer in media psychology at the University of Salford, emphasizes Twitter's pivotal role in shaping political discourse. It serves as a powerful tool for raising awareness of critical issues, disseminating campaign messages, and coordinating collective action, as demonstrated by movements like #blacklivesmatter and #JezWeCan. However, Coen also cautions that Twitter can distort public opinion by amplifying certain viewpoints and creating "Twitter storms," often due to users primarily engaging with like-minded individuals, thereby limiting exposure to diverse perspectives. Furthermore, by granting politicians personalized profiles akin to those of celebrities, Twitter has contributed to their transformation from public servants into public figures.

For **Science**, Philip James, a senior lecturer in geographic information systems at Newcastle University, highlights how Twitter data offers novel avenues for research. Data collection can be passive, by capturing already published tweets, or active, by engaging users to contribute information. This rich data source not only provides valuable insights but also fosters public engagement with scientific endeavors. For instance, a project modeling urban floods utilized public tweets to refine simulation accuracy, enabling near real-time model updates. Another initiative mapped user journeys in northeast England by analyzing geolocated tweets, facilitating the modeling of traffic flow impacts from infrastructure changes like road closures.

In **Business**, Aleksej Heinze, a senior lecturer in digital business at the University of Salford, points to Twitter's facilitation of direct, two-way communication between businesses and their customers. This allows customers to easily voice grievances publicly, prompting swift responses from companies that can potentially reduce customer support costs. Xbox, for example, achieved a Guinness World Record in 2010 for being the "most responsive brand on Twitter," managing over 5,000 inquiries daily, a feat that builds customer trust. However, Heinze notes that high expectations can also lead to disappointment, as seen in a customer's query to @XboxSupport regarding service outages. Twitter also functions as a valuable market research tool, offering insights into customer sentiment and product preferences. Loyal customers can evolve into brand advocates, defending and promoting the brand online. In one notable instance, a mobile network operator successfully leveraged customer engagement on Twitter during an outage to transform negative sentiment into brand advocacy.

**Journalism** has been significantly disrupted by Twitter, according to Richard Jones, a lecturer in journalism and media at the University of Huddersfield. Information flow is no longer solely controlled by major news agencies like the Associated Press or Reuters; it is now shared instantly via tweets. The initial report of the raid that killed Osama bin Laden, for instance, originated from a local IT consultant, not an official source. However, journalists face new challenges in verifying information, as the platform facilitates the spread of exaggerated reports and hoaxes, making it harder to discern truth from fiction. The case of Sunil Tripathi, wrongly identified as a suspect in the 2013 Boston Bombing, exemplifies the difficulties in verifying social media content.

Regarding **Relationships**, Deborah Chambers, a professor of media and cultural studies at Newcastle University, observes that Twitter has redefined personal relationships as public performances. Unlike platforms like Facebook, Twitter's inherently public nature and the ability to follow users without mutual consent create opportunities for a new form of cyberstalking. Users frequently share intimate details, fostering a culture of public disclosure. While individuals employ strategies like coded language to manage privacy, Twitter has undeniably cultivated a culture of sharing personal feelings openly.

Finally, **Celebrity Culture** has been revolutionized by Twitter, asserts Kirsty Fairclough-Isaacs, a senior lecturer in media and performance at the University of Salford. Fans can now follow celebrities' daily lives and communicate with them directly, bypassing traditional management structures. This has shifted celebrity image from fixed characteristics to an evolving, participatory performance. Celebrities are compelled to maintain an image of intimacy and authenticity, whether through genuine self-expression or carefully crafted public personas. This blurred line between reality and image has fueled a new landscape of celebrity endorsements.

Twitter's 10th anniversary marks a significant milestone for its most prolific user, @VENETHIS, who is poised to send his 37,771,000th tweet. This remarkable volume solidifies his position as the platform's undisputed champion of tweet frequency, according to Twitter Counter. While many other high-volume accounts are automated bots or corporate entities, @VENETHIS appears to be an individual in Japan. His current Twitter activity primarily revolves around finding partners and opponents for online gaming, engaging with his 43,000 followers on games like Splatoon, often seeking tag-team matches or celebrating new in-game weaponry.

His lead in tweet count is substantial, surpassing the second-place account by nearly 31 million tweets. Since joining Twitter in August 2009, he has averaged over 15,000 tweets daily.

**Top Tweeters (as of March 20, 2016):**
*   @VENETHIS: 37.8 million
*   @___sa__sa___: 7.1 million
*   @___Scc____: 7.1 million
*   @notiven: 5.9 million
*   @acmc_clock_euro: 5.6 million
*   @Favstar_Bot: 4.9 million
*   @AmexOffers: 4.7 million
*   @octifx: 4.5 million
*   @urbandictionary: 4.3 million
*   @InternetRadio: 3.8 million

The identity and motivations behind @VENETHIS remain largely enigmatic. Attempts to contact him directly were unsuccessful; his website is under renovation, and its registration details proved to be a dead end. While he was observed discussing the inquiry on Twitter, the conversation quickly shifted to online gaming.

Several English-language media outlets have acknowledged @VENETHIS's record. However, earlier reports, including mentions in the webcomic XKCD, an app designed to block prolific tweeters, Quora, Yahoo Answers, and a data analytics book, previously identified @YOUGAKUDAN_00 as the top tweeter. Articles concerning @YOUGAKUDAN_00 described its holder, estimated to be around 25 years old, as a fan of anime, manga, gaming, and programming. In 2013, the sheer volume of his tweets was likened to filling "over 2,000 copies of Haruki Murakami’s magnum opus The Wind-Up Bird Chronicle," according to Fast Company. A tongue-in-cheek valuation by The Wall Street Journal in the same year placed their worth at $1.2 million.

The apparent discrepancy is resolved by understanding that both accounts belong to the same individual. @YOUGAKUDAN_00, now suspended and having posted its last tweet approximately two years prior, has its old tweets redirected to @VENETHIS. Essentially, @VENETHIS inherits the tweet history of @YOUGAKUDAN_00, making it the singular entity credited with the record.

Twitter Counter data reveals that the bulk of this record-setting activity occurred within a concentrated 40-day period ending March 1, 2012, accounting for 96% of his total tweets. The remaining 1.5 million tweets are insufficient to even top the Osaka-area leaderboard. His objective appeared to be sheer volume, with many tweets directed at @null, a largely inactive account with over 50,000 followers and only four posts, yet receiving numerous inscrutable replies, mirroring those sent by @VENETHIS. This juxtaposition of the most prolific and least prolific tweeters is a curious aspect of his activity.

The nature of these tweets remains a subject of speculation. Some were written in binary, decimal, or hexadecimal formats. Attempts to decode them yielded no clear meaning, with one analysis suggesting they were either "pseudorandom garbage following a very flat distribution, or encrypted with a strong cipher."

The immense volume of tweets did not necessarily alienate his followers, who numbered over 3,000 during his peak activity. Replies to other users were only visible to those who also followed the recipient account. More recognizable human interactions did occur. News of his No. 1 Twitter ranking in March 2012, shortly after setting the record, spread internationally. He responded to skeptics, confirming his ability to balance a social life with his tweeting schedule and acknowledging those who marveled at or mocked his achievement. He seemed to relish the attention while maintaining an air of playful mystery.

During an attempt to gather more information, direct messages were sent to nine of the 32 users @VENETHIS followed at the time whose direct messages were open. One contact, a 17-year-old high school student, provided a link to a 2012 Japanese article. This article identified @VENETHIS as Venetisu and indicated he was using a mobile Twitter client called Keitai Web. This tool reportedly exploited a loophole to bypass Twitter's then-1,000-tweets-per-day limit, enabling automatic and frequent posting. (Twitter has since increased this daily limit to 2,400 tweets.)

Further investigation, aided by Lani Alden, a scholar of Japanese culture, revealed that Keitai Web was not the sole method employed. When @VENETHIS encountered tweet volume limitations, he reportedly rented a server and developed his own tool to circumvent these restrictions. Alden stated that this was primarily done "to become the largest automated Twitter account on Twitter. It really wasn’t for any reason other than that." Twitter suspended the account at least seven times. According to the 17-year-old contact, after the final suspension, @VENETHIS promised to cease using automated posting tools. Twitter spokespeople declined to comment on the account's suspension and transfer.

Barring any changes to Twitter's tweet volume caps or the discovery of similar loopholes, @VENETHIS's record is likely to stand. Even with his current significantly reduced tweeting frequency (around 38 tweets per day this month), it would take the second-place tweeter nearly 35 years of maximum posting to catch up. The record-setting tweets appear to have served no purpose beyond enhancing the account holder's ego and notoriety, perhaps symbolizing some of the less constructive aspects of Twitter's first decade.

Conversely, unraveling the story of Twitter's most prolific user showcased the platform's positive potential: its capacity to connect individuals across diverse geographical, linguistic, and cultural divides. The author's late-night inquiries, conducted in an unfamiliar language, were met with helpful responses from strangers worldwide, underscoring the service's connective power.

Twitter turns ten today. As it enters its awkward pre-teen years, the company that invented fame in 140 characters has had a tough run recently. It lost its longtime CEO Dick Costolo, who was replaced by co-founder and formerly ousted CEO turned new CEO-slash-savior Jack Dorsey. Yes, people use Twitter (more than 300 million of them), and yes, Twitter brings in money (more than $500 million each quarter), but its stock plummeted to an all-time low this year as Wall Street worried over its slowing user growth. People rightfully complain that the platform can be a hotbed for hostility. It also offers a loudspeaker for the disenfranchised, but swarms of voices also silence through harassment. It’s a company, it’s a platform, and it’s the world’s biggest cocktail party. That means sometimes it’s a mess. But for all its problems, Twitter is everywhere. It’s the place where the world talks to itself, often sharing and even making news in the process. Twitter has become a powerful force, but it wasn’t always that way. It all begins with a tweet from Jack Dorsey himself. just setting up my twttr — Jack (@jack) March 21, 2006 The name Twitter came from @Noah Glass & the Oxford English: “a short inconsequential burst of information, chirps from birds.” #twttr — Jack (@jack) March 13, 2011 The San Francisco crew that birthed Twitter as a spinoff of a podcasting company starts chiming in. oh this is going to be addictive — Dom Sagolla (@dom) March 21, 2006 wishing I had another sammich — Biz Stone (@biz) March 21, 2006 The @reply is born. @ buzz – you broke your thumb and youre still twittering? that's some serious devotion — Robert Andersen (@rsa) November 3, 2006 As is the #hashtag. how do you feel about using # (pound) for groups. As in #barcamp [msg]? — Chris Messina ✌︎ (@chrismessina) August 23, 2007 In late 2008, co-founder Ev Williams takes over as CEO. couple near me at whole foods are discussing Twitter. she thinks it's boring. bitch. — Ev Williams (@ev) March 28, 2008 By 2009, news starts breaking on Twitter, most famously when Captain Chesley “Sully” Sullenberger made a successful emergency landing in the Hudson River. http://twitpic.com/135xa – There's a plane in the Hudson. I'm on the ferry going to pick up the people. Crazy. — Janis Krums (@jkrums) January 15, 2009 That same year, the first major Twitter celebrity passes 1 million followers. Victory is ours!!!!!!!! — ashton kutcher (@aplusk) April 17, 2009 The next day, Oprah joins. HI TWITTERS . THANK YOU FOR A WARM WELCOME. FEELING REALLY 21st CENTURY . — Oprah Winfrey (@Oprah) April 17, 2009 Then the first tweet posted from space arrives. From orbit: Launch was awesome!! I am feeling great, working hard, & enjoying the magnificent views, the adventure of a lifetime has begun! — Mike Massimino (@Astro_Mike) May 12, 2009 By 2010, the Library of Congress had determined Twitter is important enough to be preserved for posterity. Library to acquire ENTIRE Twitter archive — ALL public tweets, ever, since March 2006! Details to follow. — Library of Congress (@librarycongress) April 14, 2010 The next year, Twitter plays a central role in the Arab Spring #revolution. It’s also one of the first places where signs appeared that something was about to go down in Abbottabad, Pakistan. (Spoiler: Osama Bin Laden was killed.) Helicopter hovering above Abbottabad at 1AM (is a rare event). — Sohaib Athar (@ReallyVirtual) May 1, 2011 And it becomes an organizing tool for Occupy Wall Street. Can we get 20,000 people to barricade Wall Street until their demand for real democracy is met? http://bit.ly/re9ENL #occupywallstreet — Adbusters (@Adbusters) July 14, 2011 Hey, celebrities use Twitter to rant at airlines too! Flight attendant on American reamed me out 4 playing WORDS W FRIENDS while we sat at the gate, not moving.#nowonderamericaairisbankrupt — ABFoundation (@ABFalecbaldwin) December 6, 2011 By 2012, Twitter was getting weird. Everything happens so much — Horse ebooks (@Horse_ebooks) June 28, 2012 Welcome to social media, Pope Benedict. Dear friends, I am pleased to get in touch with you through Twitter. Thank you for your generous response. I bless all of you from my heart. — Pope Benedict (@PopeBenedictXIV) December 12, 2012 In 2013, Twitter launches Vine, the eminently shareable six-second video clips that have spawned the careers of a new kind of celebrity. The ad industry’s collective head explodes over the seeming potential of real-time advertising tied to major events, such as the power going out at Super Bowl XLVII. Weird Twitter stays weird. if your grave doesnt say "rest in peace" on it you are automatically drafted into the skeleton war — wint (@dril) July 28, 2013 Also in 2013, Twitter becomes a publicly traded company. We’ve confidentially submitted an S-1 to the SEC for a planned IPO. This Tweet does not constitute an offer of any securities for sale. — Twitter (@twitter) September 12, 2013 Ellen tweets the most retweeted tweet of all time at the 2014 Oscars. If only Bradley's arm was longer. Best photo ever. #oscars pic.twitter.com/C9U5NOtGap — Ellen DeGeneres (@TheEllenShow) March 3, 2014 Sports take over Twitter as the 2014 World Cup in Brazil becomes the site’s biggest sports event to date. Tweetstorms begin to form. 1/Few intellectual concepts in our time have been mangled by observers more than Clay Christensen's disruption idea. Some thoughts: — Marc Andreessen (@pmarca) December 2, 2014 Twitter acquires and launches live video service Periscope. And the winner is… @periscopeco — dick costolo (@dickc) May 3, 2015 Caitlyn Jenner becomes the fastest Twitter account to reach 1 million users. I'm so happy after such a long struggle to be living my true self. Welcome to the world Caitlyn. Can't wait for you to get to know her/me. — Caitlyn Jenner (@Caitlyn_Jenner) June 1, 2015 @jack is back. And POTUS finally tweets. Hello, Twitter! It's Barack. Really! Six years in, they're finally giving me my own account. — President Obama (@POTUS) May 18, 2015 It turns out people really like “faves” and really don’t like “likes.” [Twitter headquarters] "We're losing the social media war! What do we do! I need ideas!" "What if we change stars to hearts?" "BRILLIANT!" — Mike Drucker (@MikeDrucker) November 3, 2015 And, don’t forget Election 2016 Twitter… Sorry Mom — Jeb Bush (@JebBush) September 17, 2015 Ted Cruz didn't win Iowa, he stole it. That is why all of the polls were so wrong and why he got far more votes than anticipated. Bad! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) February 3, 2016 So, thanks Twitter. But, really, you have distracted from my creative process.

Twitter, a platform that began with Jack Dorsey's simple "just setting up my twttr" a decade ago, has transformed into a global communication powerhouse, with billions of posts having since gone viral. This exploration highlights the top 10 most retweeted tweets, revealing the diverse content that captures the public's imagination, from political statements and celebrity moments to personal anecdotes and iconic selfies, all showcasing the power of a single message to connect and engage millions.

Here are the most retweeted tweets of all time, as tracked by Favstar:

1.  **Ellen DeGeneres: Hollywood's Most Famous Selfie** Ellen DeGeneres's 2014 Oscar selfie, featuring numerous celebrities, became the most retweeted post in history, though reports later suggested it was partly a product placement for Samsung, adding a commercial layer to the viral phenomenon and sparking discussions about authenticity in online content.
2.  **Carter Wilkerson: The Quest for Free Nuggets** Carter Wilkerson's viral request to Wendy's for a year's supply of chicken nuggets in exchange for retweets demonstrated Twitter's ability to mobilize collective action for a lighthearted cause, resulting in unprecedented engagement.
3.  **Louis Tomlinson: The Enduring Bromance** A 2011 tweet from Louis Tomlinson celebrating his friendship with bandmate Harry Styles accumulated over two million retweets, becoming a fan favorite as One Direction's fame grew and capturing the essence of their bond.
4.  **Denny Januar Ali: Global Reach and a Message of Love** Indonesian political analyst Denny Januar Ali achieved 2.5 million retweets with messages supporting Joko “Jokowi” Widodo and advocating against discrimination, accompanied by an image of children holding "love" signs, highlighting that approximately 80% of social media users are outside the U.S. and underscoring the global nature of viral content.
5.  **Camila Cabello: A Million-Retweet Mystery** Camila Cabello's seemingly simple message initially received over a million retweets, but current metrics show a much lower number, with the reason for this discrepancy remaining unclear, highlighting the fluid nature of social media data and the challenges in tracking viral trends accurately.
6.  **Barack Obama: A Re-election Triumph** Obama's re-election night tweet in 2012, featuring a photo of him embracing Michelle Obama, became the most retweeted tweet at the time, showcasing the emotional impact of a single moment amplified by the platform and demonstrating the power of visual storytelling.
7.  **Barack Obama: A Farewell to Supporters** As his presidency concluded, Barack Obama thanked his supporters, expressing gratitude for their dedication over eight years and encouraging continued civic engagement in a poignant series of tweets that fostered a sense of community.
8.  **Adam Saleh: The YouTuber and the Controversy** YouTuber Adam Saleh claimed he was removed from a Delta flight for speaking Arabic, an incident that quickly went viral; however, later revelations about Saleh's history of prank videos led to speculation about whether the event might have been staged for content, highlighting the complexities of viral narratives.
9.  **Harry Styles: A Message of Solidarity** During a turbulent time for One Direction following Zayn Malik's departure, Harry Styles tweeted a message of encouragement to fans that deeply resonated with the band's large following, providing comfort during a period of uncertainty.
10. **Andrew Malcolm: The Ambiguous Philanthropy** Andrew Malcolm's tweet suggested that dog food company Pedigree would donate a meal to a dog for every retweet, but Pedigree had already announced reaching their goal of donating 210,000 bowls before Malcolm's tweet, raising questions about the tweet's actual impact, yet the message continues to be shared, demonstrating viral content's power even with a questionable premise and remarkable public engagement.

query: What were some key themes emphasized by President Trump in his CPAC speech?

documents.text: President Trump emphasized the importance of "conservative values" and prioritizing the well-being of Americans over global interests during his speech at the 2017 Conservative Political Action Conference, Feb. 24. (Sarah Parnass/The Washington Post) President Trump on Friday addressed the Conservative Political Action Conference, the event where he launched his political star in 2011. From the outset, he had one thing on his mind: the media. Trump began his speech with a lengthy tirade against the press. We have annotated the president's remarks, using Genius. To see an annotation, click on the yellow, highlighted text. To add your own, sign up for a Genius account. TRUMP: Thank you, everybody. So great to be with you. Thank you. (APPLAUSE) Great to be back at CPAC. It's a place I have really... AUDIENCE MEMBER: We love you! TRUMP: I love this place. (APPLAUSE) Love you people. So thank you -- thank you very much. First of all, I want to thank Matt Schlapp and his very, very incredible wife, and boss, Mercedes, who have been fantastic friends and supporters and so great. When I watch them on television defending me, nobody has a chance. So I want to thank Matt and Mercedes. (APPLAUSE) And when Matt called and asked I said, "Absolutely I'll be there with you." I mean, the real reason I said it, I didn't want him to go against me cause that... (LAUGHTER) ... that one you can't beat. So I said absolutely. And it really is an honor to be here. I wouldn't miss a chance to talk to my friends. These are my friends. (APPLAUSE) And we'll see you again next year and the year after that and I'll be doing this... (APPLAUSE) ... I'll be doing this with CPAC whenever I can and I'll make sure that we're here a lot. AUDIENCE MEMBER: You can do it! TRUMP: You know if you remember my first major speech -- sit down everybody. C'mon. (LAUGHTER) You know the dishonest media they'll say, "He didn't get a standing ovation." You know why? (LAUGHTER) No -- you know why? Because everybody stood and nobody sat. So they will say, "He never got a standing ovation," right? (Inaudible). They are the worst. (APPLAUSE) AUDIENCE: USA! USA! USA! TRUMP: So -- sit down. (LAUGHTER) "Donald Trump did not get a standing ovation." (LAUGHTER) They leave out the part, "They never sat down." They leave that out. So I just want to thank -- but you know my first major speak was at CPAC and -- probably five or six years ago. First major political speech. And you were there. And it was -- I loved it. I loved the people. I loved the commotion. And then they did these polls were I went through the roof and I wasn't even running, right? (LAUGHTER) But it gave me an idea. And I got a little bit concerned when I saw what was happening in the country. And I said, "Let's go to it." So, it was very exciting. I walked the stage on CPAC. I'll never forget it, really. I had very little notes and even less preparation. So when you have practically no notes and no preparation and then you leave and everybody was thrilled, I said, "I think I like this business." (LAUGHTER) I would've come last year but I was worried that I would be, at that time, too controversial. We wanted border security. We wanted very, very strong military. We wanted all of the things that we're going to get... (APPLAUSE) ... and people consider that controversial but you didn't consider it controversial. (APPLAUSE) TRUMP: So, I've been with CPAC for a long time. All of these years we've been together. And now you finally have a president, finally. Took you a long time. (APPLAUSE) Took you a long time. (APPLAUSE) And it's patriots like you that made it happen, believe me. Believe me. You did it because you love your country, because you want a better future for your children and because you want to make America great again. (APPLAUSE) The media didn't think we would win. The... AUDIENCE MEMBER: (OFF-MIKE) (LAUGHER) TRUMP: ... pundits -- you're right. They had an idea. The pundits didn't think we'd win. The consultants that suck up all that money -- oh, they suck it up. They're so good. (LAUGHTER) They're not good at politics, but they're really good at sucking up people's money. Especially my opponents, because I kept them down to a minimum. But the consultants didn't think we would win. But they all underestimated the power of the people: You. And the people proved them... (APPLAUSE) ... totally wrong. Never -- and -- and this is so true. And this is what's been happening. Never underestimate the people. Never. (APPLAUSE) I don't think it'll ever happen again. And I want you all to know that we are fighting the fake news. It's fake, phony, fake. (APPLAUSE) A few days ago I called the fake news the enemy of the people. And they are. They are the enemy of the people. (APPLAUSE) Because they have no sources, they just make 'em up when there are none. I saw one story recently where they said, "Nine people have confirmed." There're no nine people. I don't believe there was one or two people. Nine people. And I said, "Give me a break." Because I know the people, I know who they talk to. There were no nine people. But they say "nine people." And somebody reads it and they think, "Oh, nine people. They have nine sources." They make up sources. They're very dishonest people. In fact, in covering my comments, the dishonest media did not explain that I called the fake news the enemy of the people. The fake news. They dropped off the word "fake." And all of a sudden the story became the media is the enemy. They take the word "fake" out. And now I'm saying, "Oh no, this is no good." But that's the way they are. So I'm not against the media, I'm not against the press. I don't mind bad stories if I deserve them. And I tell ya, I love good stories, but we don't go... (LAUGHTER) I don't get too many of them. But I am only against the fake news, media or press. Fake, fake. They have to leave that word. I'm against the people that make up stories and make up sources. They shouldn't be allowed to use sources unless they use somebody's name. Let their name be put out there. Let their name be put out. (APPLAUSE) "A source says that Donald Trump is a horrible, horrible human being." Let 'em say it to my face. (APPLAUSE) Let there be no more sources. And remember this -- and in not -- in all cases. I mean, I had a story written yesterday about me in Reuters by a very honorable man. It was a very fair story. There are some great reporters around. They're talented, they're honest as the day is long. They're great. But there are some terrible dishonest people and they do a tremendous disservice to our country and to our people. A tremendous disservice. They are very dishonest people. And they shouldn't use sources. They should put the name of the person. You will see stories dry up like you've never seen before. So you have no idea how bad it is, because if you are not part of the story -- and I put myself in your position sometimes. Because many of you, you're not part of the story. And if you're not part of the story, you know, then you, sort of, know -- if you are part of the story, you know what they're saying is true or not. TRUMP: So when they make it up -- and they make up something else, and you saw that before the election: polls, polls. The polls. They come out with these polls and everybody was so surprised. Actually, a couple polls got it right. I must say Los Angeles Times did a great job, shocking because, you know, they did a great job. (APPLAUSE) And we had a couple of others that were right, but generally speaking, I mean, can tell you the network, somebody said a poll came out. And I say, what network is it? And they'll say, a certain -- let's not even mention names, right? Shall we? (APPLAUSE) Well, you have a lot of them. Look, the Clinton News Network is one. (APPLAUSE) Totally. Take a look. Honestly. Take a look, honestly. Take a look at polls over the last two years. Now you'd think they would fire the pollster, right? After years and years of getting battered, but I -- who knows, maybe they are just bad at polling or maybe they're not legit, but it's one or the other, look at how inaccurate -- look at CBS, look at ABC, also, look at NBC, take a look at some of these polls. They're so bad, so inaccurate and what that does is it creates a false narrative.
