David Bowie, a true icon of music, also carved out a significant acting career, imbuing his roles with a unique glamour and captivating physical presence. While he may not have garnered Oscar wins or dominated box office charts, Bowie's performances consistently elevated unconventional films and demonstrated remarkable versatility, extending far beyond his legendary musical personas like Ziggy Stardust. His acting endeavors, nearly as extensive as his musical output, saw him embody a diverse range of characters, from Pontius Pilate to Andy Warhol.

Bowie's cinematic journey commenced with a minor appearance in the 1967 short film "The Image." Early in his career, smaller roles and a reputation for unconventional behavior contributed to what Slate's Jessica Winter aptly described in 2010 as a "Method performance art" persona during mid-70s publicity tours. This led Dick Cavett to famously remark to a "sniffly, skeletal guest" in 1974, "To me… you seem like a working actor."

His breakthrough film role arrived in 1976 with "The Man Who Fell to Earth," where he portrayed an alien. The New York Times lauded his performance, noting, "Mr. Bowie gives an extraordinary performance. The details, the chemistry of this tall pale figure with black-rimmed eyes are clearly not human. Yet he acquires a moving, tragic force as the stranger caught and destroyed in a strange land." This pivotal role launched a series of films that, alongside his music, sustained his public profile.

Many of these films, despite occasional critical dismissal or commercial underperformance, have since achieved cult status, often owing to Bowie's distinctive screen presence or his musical contributions. Notable examples include:

*   **"The Hunger" (1983):** A stylish vampire film where Bowie played a vampire.
*   **"Labyrinth" (1986):** A beloved fantasy musical where he starred as Jareth, the Goblin King.
*   **"Absolute Beginners" (1986):** A musical drama set in 1950s London.

Bowie himself expressed confidence in his acting capabilities, stating in 1988, "I feel very confident about my acting abilities. Not that I think I’ll ever be a great actor, but I think I could be a substantially acceptable actor and offer something that no one else is offering." He was discerning about his projects and collaborators, choosing to work with acclaimed directors such as Tony Scott, Christopher Nolan, David Lynch, and Jim Henson.

Jim Henson's "Labyrinth" (1986) significantly shaped Bowie's image for a generation. Released after his hit album "Let's Dance," the fantasy film featured Bowie as Jareth, the Goblin King. The New York Times praised his casting, observing, "David Bowie is perfectly cast as the teasing, tempting seducer whom Sarah must both want and reject in order to learn the labyrinth’s lessons, and his songs add a driving, sensual appeal."

Bowie also collaborated with Martin Scorsese, portraying Pontius Pilate in "The Last Temptation of Christ" (1988). His depiction of the Roman official was chillingly indifferent, with Scorsese stating at the time, "David Bowie was my first and only choice for Pontius Pilate. I never considered anyone else."

In 1996, Bowie took on the role of Andy Warhol in "Basquiat," a figure he knew and had written a song about. Bowie described Warhol as a "lethal kind of Svengali figure," remarking in 1987, "Everything happened without him seeming to be taking any part of it. He was an extraordinary, hypnotic kind of guy." His performance as Nikola Tesla in "The Prestige" (2006) drew parallels to his earlier portrayal of Thomas Newton in "The Man Who Fell to Earth." As Jessica Winter of Slate noted in 2010, "Thirty years apart, these roles also prove that — in the hands of the right director — David Bowie, Thespian, is a snazzy magic trick."

query: What significant developments are expected to emerge from the Paris climate talks next month?

documents.text: The Paris climate talks are expected to conclude next month with a celebrated new international accord ratcheting down greenhouse gas pollution from the world’s richest and poorest countries. It could be a Kumbaya moment for President Barack Obama and his contemporaries as they tout their work trying to save the planet. But someone else also deserves some of the credit for what’s coming from the French capital: George W. Bush. Story Continued Below You read that right. The very same Republican president who doubted global warming science and fought new Environmental Protection Agency climate policies all the way to the Supreme Court merits a spot in the history books for kick-starting the very same negotiations that are about to bear fruit in Paris. That may come as a shock considering modern-day Republicans are bent on derailing the Paris negotiations and overturning pretty much all of Obama’s green agenda. But Bush did indeed have a big imprint near the end of his presidency — at a tropical resort on the Indonesian island of Bali. The president himself wasn’t there for the late 2007 negotiations. In fact, for two long weeks as the talks lolled along, the U.S. administration actually served as a convenient punching bag for pent-up global diplomatic futility. Al Gore, fresh off accepting his Nobel Peace Prize in Norway, had flown halfway around the world to deliver a speech digging at Bush and urging the negotiators to “save an open, large, blank space” in their documents for the next U.S. president to sign. On the conference floor, typical decorum disappeared when Bush officials were loudly booed in the session’s closing hours. Yet what emerged from all that chaos stands today as perhaps the most important breakthrough in more than 20 years of international global warming talks. For the first time ever, countries of all shapes, sizes and economic means pledged to pony up commitments to address global warming. The agreement came with a very wonky sounding name — the Bali Action Plan — and it provided only a very rough outline of where future negotiations would need to go. But what the Bush administration helped create in Bali stands to this day because it eliminated perhaps the biggest political albatross blocking major action in the United States and around the world on international climate policy: Finally, fast-growing developing countries like China, Brazil, India and South Africa were on record saying they would submit cleanup plans of their own. That small flame lit in Bali has several times been nearly extinguished. Outsized expectations surrounding Obama — fueled by his own Nobel award less than a year into his first term — nearly capsized the entire U.N. process when the president and more than 100 other world leaders traveled to Copenhagen in 2009 aiming to wrap up a major new agreement. They swung and missed in Denmark, and it took two more years before negotiators could get the original Bali game plan back on track during another climate conference in Durban, South Africa. There are still devilish details to be worked out in Paris on how to induce developing countries to make emission reduction pledges through tens of billions of dollars in technical help that kick-starts their use of cleaner sources of energy and their adaptation to an already changing climate. The foreign aid is also designed to get countries to make even stronger commitments that can be both measured and verified. But at its fundamental core, the concept enshrined in Bali that all countries will engage collectively in the climate crusade is the strongest sign yet that the upcoming talks in Paris will be a success. “I very much look back at Bali as a turning point,” said Yvo de Boer, the former top U.N. climate official who helped lead the Indonesian conference. “In fact, it’s often surprised me how often people have forgotten that turning point and its significance.” For outsiders, deciphering the meaning of a U.N. climate conference is no easy feat. Even experienced eyes often can be overwhelmed by the legal jargon, technocratic acronyms and political rhetoric that don’t easily translate into everyday English. These summits have taken place annually since 1995 and often the host country is in desperate need of some kind of accomplishment on paper to show the whole affair was worth it. Often what they end up touting sounds eerily similar to what was widely trumpeted as a victory in an earlier year. But the truth is that each session builds on its predecessors — and there’s also all manner of extra grunt work happening at side meetings around the world and in more formal gatherings every couple of months at U.N. climate headquarters in the old West German capital of Bonn. On occasion, a U.N. conference can become something bigger than itself. The treaty that started it all was negotiated at the Rio Earth Summit in 1992; George H.W. Bush, eager to campaign as the “environmentalist president,” traveled to Brazil to sign it. In a sign of just how different times were, the Senate ratified the treaty in October of that year without any objections. Bush was nonetheless booted from office a month later. Eager to turn international climate policy from a voluntary mission to a mandatory one, Bush’s successor, President Bill Clinton, signed off in 1995 on the “Berlin Mandate,” which declared that only developed countries like the United States, the United Kingdom, Japan and Canada were legally bound to reduce their emissions, while developing nations, including China and India, got a pass. Two years later in Japan’s ancient capital, Gore helped negotiate the Kyoto Protocol that put the Berlin language into a treaty. The U.S. did score some big wins aimed at making the agreement more flexible, including broad coverage of the six most potent greenhouse gases, rather than just carbon dioxide, and authorization of a cap-and-trade system so industry could make the most economically palatable emission reductions. But Kyoto was still a political disaster. The Senate had already voted unanimously 95-0 on a resolution from Sens. Robert Byrd (D-W.Va.) and Chuck Hagel (R-Neb.) vowing never to ratify a treaty that did economic harm to the U.S. and disapproving of any international climate agreement that exempted developing countries. Reflecting on Kyoto, several former Clinton negotiators admitted they erred in their push to get developing countries to do more than they were willing to do. After all, China, India and other similar nations blamed the West for causing climate change in the first place through decades of economic growth via unregulated fossil fuel consumption. “You could cut the hostility in the room with a knife,” said Stuart Eizenstat, who led the U.S. delegation during the Kyoto talks. “We completely struck out with developing countries.” "The Kyoto Protocol was a little too fast and too complicated,” added Daniel Bodansky, a former Clinton-era State Department climate coordinator. “It proved to be a false start.” While Clinton signed Kyoto, he never submitted it to the Senate for ratification. Still, George W. Bush took the brunt of the political blowback when he pulled the U.S. completely out of all talks related to the 1997 agreement negotiated by his predecessor. International ire only grew with media reports that the Republican White House populated with former oil industry officials had been censoring government science on climate change, as well as Bush’s backtrack from his own 2000 presidential campaign pledge to enact the first-ever cap on U.S. greenhouse gas emissions from power plants. Bush didn’t relent on climate issues in a substantive way until 2007. By then, Democrats were in control of both chambers of Congress for the first time under the GOP administration, and cap-and-trade advocate John McCain was seen as one of the leading contenders for the Republican presidential nomination. International pressure on the U.S. from its allies was growing to map out a different path. “When Bush came in and said we’re not going to do Kyoto, the obvious question was: What’s your alternative?” said Harlan Watson, who served as a lead Bush administration climate change negotiator. In Bali, consecutive all-night talks resulted in an agreement in which rich nations agreed to set up new mechanisms to help poor nations adapt to climate change and build new clean energy infrastructure. It also “really launched the seriousness of this idea of breaking down the firewall” between developed and developing countries, Watson said. Close observers inside the U.S. were even caught a bit off guard by the change. Doubts were widespread about the seriousness behind the Bush team’s motivations. “The environmentalists and a lot of Democrats just crapped on us,” George David Banks, a former senior Bush White House environmental aide, recalled. “They said we weren’t being ambitious enough. That we’d undermined the U.N. framework. It was a step back from Kyoto. But it was pretty obvious there was just no way. If you were concerned about the climate issue, just because of the math and the increase in emission levels [counting the world’s major economies], you had to do something different.” The negotiations since Bali have been anything but smooth. In Copenhagen, Obama and other world leaders saved face amid outsized expectations they could have left that conference with the makings of a legally binding treaty. Instead, they got a more nuanced accord in which rich and poor nations alike promised to keep making pledges on their environmental plans. They punted until later the actual legal framework that would tie the whole thing together — a promise that is finally coming to pass in Paris. Considering what’s happened since Bali, Banks credited the Obama administration for its adherence to those original 2007 goals and agreed that the Democratic administration’s likely success wouldn’t have come without Bush’s work. “We were able to break ground for them that they were able to exploit later,” he said. Even some greens credit Bush’s team with getting the ball rolling and teeing up Paris. “They do deserve credit, coming from a terrible place, undermining the system, to getting things done,” said Kalee Kreider, an environmental group veteran who had been a traveling Gore aide at the Bali talks. “Usually some of these [U.N. conferences] are more about mechanics. And some of them are political tipping points. That was definitely a tipping point moment.” That Bush could have played such an important role in setting the stage for Paris is a fact lost on the current crop of Republicans. Many leading GOP lawmakers reject mainstream climate science and attack Obama’s agenda at every turn. Earlier this month, they even approved a pair of resolutions aimed at overturning the president’s signature emission rules for power plants. Republicans have also vowed to use every tool at their disposal to undermine the Paris climate talks that their most recent president helped to launch. “There is a lot of Republican criticism of Paris,” said Nigel Purvis, a Clinton-era climate diplomat, “which is ironic because the Paris architecture is the architecture that conservatives said we needed.”

During the 2015 U.N. Climate Change Conference (COP21) in Paris, a powerful visual statement is being made on the facade of France’s National Assembly building. Over 500 projected images of individuals serve as a poignant reminder to world leaders that "the world is watching," as articulated by the artists behind this initiative.

This impactful project, titled "The Standing March," is a collaborative endeavor between renowned French artist JR and acclaimed American filmmaker Darren Aronofsky. The artists describe this "representation of humanity" as a direct message to the approximately 25,000 global leaders convened in Paris. Their objective is to negotiate a comprehensive plan aimed at limiting the rise of global temperatures to a critical 2°C (3.6°F).

Darren Aronofsky, known for his thought-provoking films such as *Requiem for a Dream* and *Black Swan*, emphasized the urgency in a statement released on the project's website. "We expect decisions, we expect commitments, and we expect time frames," he declared. Aronofsky further elaborated, stating, "It’s about people, from Tuvalu to New Orleans, whose lives are threatened by climate change. The world depends on a solution."

The conference, alongside this compelling exhibition which is slated to tour Paris for seven days following its initial display on the National Assembly building, unfolds less than three weeks after the devastating terrorist attacks that claimed 130 lives. In light of security concerns that have led to the prohibition of public marches in Paris, JR explained the artistic approach. "For security reasons, marches are forbidden in Paris. But our art piece is a silent march," he stated. "And we are marching, backed by the Assemblée Nationale, the heart of the French democracy. We must think about our future, the future of our environment and this is our answer to those who want to control our present." This initiative, supported by organizations such as the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) and the French government, underscores the global significance of COP21. Experts from institutions like the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) have consistently highlighted the dire consequences of inaction.

The 2015 Paris climate talks, officially known as COP21, convened delegates from 195 nations, with an estimated 50,000 individuals attending. This significant gathering included negotiators, diplomats, business representatives, activists, students, and journalists, all converging to influence, benefit from, or document the crucial two-week conference. Participants traveled from across the globe, with an average round-trip travel distance of approximately 9,000 miles. While various modes of transport were utilized, air travel dominated. Assuming full capacity for a Boeing 747, an estimated 27 million gallons of jet fuel were consumed, leading to approximately 575 million pounds of CO2 emissions from air travel alone. This figure, though substantial, represents a mere fraction of global annual CO2 output, equivalent to roughly 22 seconds of worldwide emissions. When factoring in emissions from accommodation, local transport, and daily consumption, the total environmental impact of the conference is estimated to be only slightly higher.

The primary objective of COP21 was to limit the rise in average global temperatures to 2°C above pre-industrial levels, a target that current national commitments appear insufficient to meet. However, progress is being made. Aimee Barnes, a delegate for the California Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), highlights the incremental nature of climate action, referencing the groundwork laid at earlier meetings like the 2010 Cancún climate talks and the 2009 Copenhagen summit. She points to the unprecedented achievement of securing 156 emissions commitments from 130 countries just two years prior to Paris as evidence of sustained dedication. Beyond national commitments, the role of sub-national entities, such as cities and states, is increasingly recognized for their economic power and ability to implement swift emissions reductions, with California serving as a prime example.

While optimism is vital, robust enforcement mechanisms and shared incentives are crucial for effective climate action, a lesson learned from the stalled negotiations following the Kyoto Protocol. Many experts, including economists and negotiators, advocate for a global solution like a price on carbon. Steve Stoft, a Berkeley economist associated with climateParis.org and carbon-Price.com, proposes a framework where all countries agree to price carbon emissions at $20 or $30 per ton, allowing nations to implement their own strategies to promote economic development while meeting this requirement. This concept has garnered support from economists, including Nobel laureates, and negotiators like Aimee Barnes. Despite these proposals, implementing a global carbon price at the Paris talks was considered unlikely, suggesting that the anticipated outcome would be gradual progress, the extent of which remains uncertain but may prove sufficient to offset the emissions generated by the conference itself.

President Obama's decision to join the Paris climate agreement without seeking Senate approval has sparked a significant legal and political debate. This centers on the President's authority to commit the United States to international accords. While a Republican-controlled Senate would likely reject a formal treaty, the administration argues the Paris accord is not a treaty. Therefore, it doesn't legally obligate the U.S. to specific actions. They assert that existing laws, like the Clean Air Act and the United Nations Framework on Climate Change (signed by President George H.W. Bush), provide the necessary authority. However, this approach carries a risk. Future presidents could abandon climate initiatives. A ratified treaty, conversely, would bind all administrations. The Obama administration intends to continue its current policies. These aim to reduce carbon emissions by 26 to 28 percent below 2005 levels by 2025.

This method of entering international agreements without Senate consent has historical precedent. Nigel Purvis, founding president of Climate Advisers and a former State Department treaty lawyer, explained this. He noted that agreements not requiring changes to U.S. law can be executed as "executive agreements."

However, this approach faces opposition. Senators like Jim Inhofe (R-Okla.), Roy Blunt (R-Mo.), and Joe Manchin (D-WestVa.) oppose it. They introduced a "sense of the Senate resolution." This resolution demands Senate advice and consent for any agreement from Paris. Senator Inhofe, chairman of the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee, stated that any substantive outcome would require Senate involvement. This opposition, including Blunt and Manchin, who support the coal industry, highlights Republican concerns about perceived executive overreach.

The core of the dispute is whether the Paris agreement is "legally binding." David Sandalow, a fellow at Columbia University’s Center for Global Energy Policy, clarified this. He explained that while some aspects of the agreement are legally binding, emission reduction commitments are not. Secretary of State John F. Kerry emphasized in the Financial Times that Paris would not include "legally binding reduction targets like Kyoto." This position drew criticism from European governments. They advocated for a binding international instrument. Administration officials maintain that Paris will rely on:

*   "Bottom-up country commitments."
*   Binding nations to a process of submitting climate action plans. This is instead of imposing top-down targets.

This model is similar to an earlier mercury emissions accord.

Professor of international law Michael Glennon of the Fletcher School pointed out that a state's intent determines its participation in a binding agreement. He cited the Iran nuclear accord. This was an instance where the administration bypassed Senate ratification. In response, a group of Republican senators, led by Mike Lee of Utah, proposed a "sense of the Congress resolution." This resolution urged the administration to submit all climate agreements to the Senate. It did so irrespective of their binding nature. It also threatened to withhold funding for the Green Climate Fund. Ultimately, compliance depends on national will and international pressure. Experts like Paul Bledsoe question the practical implications of "legally binding" without financial penalties. They suggest the U.S. will not agree to anything requiring Senate confirmation.

**Paris Climate Summit: Unpacking the Diplomacy Amidst a Global Crisis**

As over 140 world leaders gathered in Paris for critical treaty talks, the planet's climate crisis took center stage. While such a significant global issue might typically dominate headlines, the sheer volume of other world events, including the cancellation of a major climate march due to security concerns, meant the Paris climate talks, officially the 21st Conference of the Parties to the Kyoto Protocol (#COP21), might not get the attention it truly deserves. This is despite the fact that climate change presents a danger as profound as any nuclear standoff. As Climate Home observed, "a treaty of this magnitude has never been achieved before, and the one being constructed will shape how the entire global economy functions."

The intricate nature of climate negotiations, often obscured by diplomatic jargon and complex protocols, can mask the urgency of the situation. Even experienced diplomats find the language challenging. Oleg Shamanov, Russia's envoy to the Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform for Enhanced Action (ADP2), famously tweeted, "The most experienced lawyer on Earth will not be able to comprehend this text."

This year is shaping up to be a potentially record-breaking warm year since agriculture began 10,000 years ago. Atmospheric carbon dioxide levels are at historic highs, and emissions continue to rise. Yet, the crucial discussions and diplomatic efforts are frequently buried under layers of legalistic language and acronyms. For example, Manuel Pulgar Vidal, Peru's environment minister and a key negotiator, shared a link to a document titled: “Scenario note on the twelfth part of the second session of the Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform for Enhanced Action.” This title, while official, offers little public clarity.

To clarify these vital discussions, here's a look at why leaders are in Paris, the main challenges, and what the outcomes could signify:

*   **The Official Framework: ADP2 and the Durban Platform**
    *   The "Ad Hoc Working Group on the Durban Platform for Enhanced Action," or ADP2, is the formal name for these international climate discussions.
    *   For roughly four years, representatives from almost every nation have met regularly, mainly in Bonn, Germany.
    *   During these initial sessions, ADP2 developed the structure for a draft agreement, a substantial 54-page document.
    *   The name "Durban Platform" comes from a 2011 agreement in Durban, South Africa, where world leaders committed to finalizing a global climate treaty by 2015.

*   **The Paris Summit: #COP21**
    *   This significant meeting is officially known as the 21st Conference of the Parties to the Kyoto Protocol, or #COP21.
    *   The current draft text of the Durban Platform includes about 1,300 sets of square brackets. Each set represents alternative wording options that delegates must resolve.

**Key Points of Contention in the Negotiations:**

*   **Emission Reduction Goals:**
    *   The foundational climate treaty, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), signed in 1992 by 165 countries, established the principle of "common but differentiated responsibility." This means all nations should cut emissions, but developed countries with historically high emissions, like the United States, Japan, and Germany, are expected to make larger reductions.
    *   Before Paris, countries submitted voluntary pledges, called Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDCs). These were designed to let nations set their own emission reduction plans, with the expectation of international pressure to increase ambition.
    *   While INDCs show some progress, they are not enough on their own. A main focus in Paris is creating a system to continuously speed up emission reductions.

*   **Climate Finance:**
    *   A vital part of promoting climate-friendly development involves financial aid from wealthier nations to developing countries.
    *   Developed countries have promised $100 billion annually by 2020, but the specific sources for this funding are still unclear.
    *   Many developing nations have included their finance needs in their INDCs, emphasizing that access to international support would greatly speed up their shift to renewable energy.

*   **Loss and Damage:**
    *   Even with strong emission cuts, the planet will continue to warm due to the thermal inertia of oceans and the natural lag in the climate system.
    *   This means climate-related disasters are expected to worsen in the coming decades, disproportionately affecting poor and vulnerable countries.
    *   These nations are calling for a dedicated system to seek help and support for adapting to future extreme weather events.

*   **Ratchet Mechanism:**
    *   Recognizing that the Paris talks alone won't solve climate change, a key negotiation point is how often countries will be expected to submit more ambitious targets.
    *   Developed nations generally prefer reviewing and updating targets once every ten years. Developing countries, however, are pushing for new targets to be agreed upon every five years.

The UN climate talks operate on a consensus basis. To avoid the problems of past summits like Kyoto and Copenhagen, the current approach favors voluntary commitments. However, the European Union, with France playing a leading role as host, wants a legally binding agreement. This would require approval from the U.S. Congress, a prospect viewed with doubt given the current political climate. Therefore, the United States is strongly opposing strict legal language in the Paris accord.

**Key Groups to Observe:**

*   **G77 China:** This bloc, now with 134 members, represents the interests of developing countries. China's role is changing, and it often acts as a link between developed nations and those most susceptible to climate change.
*   **Vulnerable Nations:** Countries like Bangladesh, Burkina Faso, and the Maldives are on the front lines of climate impacts and are pushing for strong action.
*   **Advocacy Groups:** Organizations such as the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS), the Vulnerable Countries Forum (V20), and the Least Developed Countries (LDC) are advocating for the strongest possible climate deal.
*   **Fossil Fuel Producers:** Nations like Russia, Venezuela, and Saudi Arabia are expected to argue for weaker climate commitments.

For those who wish to follow the negotiations closely, a curated Twitter list of leading climate journalists, activists, and diplomats from the talks is available.

The 21st Conference of the Parties (COP21) to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCC), held in Le Bourget, north of Paris, marked a pivotal moment in global efforts to combat climate change. This landmark event, formally known as COP21, convened from November 30th to December 11th, 2015, bringing together representatives from 196 nations with a singular, ambitious objective: to forge a legally binding international agreement to curb global warming. The stakes were undeniably high, with the future of the planet hanging in the balance.

At the heart of the negotiations was the critical goal of limiting the rise in global temperatures to no more than 2 degrees Celsius (3.6 degrees Fahrenheit) above pre-industrial levels. This threshold, widely recognized by climate scientists, represents a crucial tipping point. Exceeding it, the scientific consensus warns, would unleash a cascade of increasingly severe and devastating consequences, including more frequent and intense droughts, destructive storms, and catastrophic floods. The urgency of this objective was underscored by the presence of numerous world leaders, including President Obama of the United States and China's President Xi Jinping. These two nations, being the largest emitters of greenhouse gases, held significant influence and responsibility in the proceedings. Beyond the heads of state, the summit was a convergence of thousands of delegates, policymakers, industry leaders, scientific experts, passionate environmental activists, and members of the global media, all focused on the shared challenge of climate change. It is worth noting that public climate change demonstrations, initially planned in Paris and across France, were unfortunately canceled in the immediate aftermath of the terrorist attacks that had recently struck the French capital.

The discussions at COP21 were multifaceted, delving into the specific commitments each nation intended to make regarding the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, such as carbon and methane, by the year 2020. A key aspect of these discussions involved reviewing the "Intended Nationally Determined Contributions" (INDCs) submitted by participating countries. These INDCs served as public pledges outlining each nation's proposed strategies and targets for emission reductions. For instance, the United States and China, major emitters stemming from their reliance on coal, oil, and gas, had publicly committed to transitioning their industrial sectors towards greener, low-carbon energy sources. The shadow of past failures loomed, particularly the 2009 climate talks in Copenhagen, which had faltered due to significant disagreements between developed and developing nations concerning the equitable distribution of the burden of emission cuts.

The implications of climate change extend far beyond abstract scientific projections, directly impacting the lives and well-being of individuals worldwide. The human cost of unchecked climate change is staggering. Over the preceding two decades, the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction calculated that weather-related disasters had resulted in an estimated 606,000 deaths, averaging approximately 30,000 fatalities annually, and an alarming 4.1 billion injuries. Furthermore, the UN's estimates indicate that a substantial 90% of major global disasters are attributable to extreme weather events, including floods, storms, heatwaves, and droughts. These events, numbering around 6,457 over the period, incurred an immense annual economic cost, estimated to be between $250 billion and $300 billion.

The question of whether a meaningful agreement would emerge from COP21 was met with cautious optimism. Experts had expressed a degree of hope, suggesting that extensive preparatory work had laid a solid foundation for the possibility of a binding action plan. This optimism was bolstered by the fact that a significant majority of the 196 participating countries, specifically 178 nations, had already submitted their INDCs. These submissions represented a crucial step forward, demonstrating a collective willingness to engage in concrete actions to address the climate crisis. The success of COP21 hinged on translating these individual pledges into a unified, enforceable global strategy, a challenge that would define the trajectory of climate action for years to come.

International climate negotiations are currently experiencing a significant breakthrough, offering renewed hope for global action against climate change. This pivotal moment in climate diplomacy, building upon lessons learned from past efforts such as the Kyoto Protocol and the Copenhagen Accord, centers on the ambitious Paris Agreement. A monumental development has been the collaborative agreement between the United States and China, the world's two largest emitters of greenhouse gases, which has galvanized over 170 nations, collectively responsible for more than 90% of global carbon emissions, to submit their individual emissions reduction plans. These national plans are poised to form the bedrock of a new global accord, signaling a strategic evolution in the approach to climate negotiations.

Unlike the exceptionally high expectations that characterized the Kyoto Protocol and the Copenhagen Accord, the current Paris talks are pursuing more pragmatic and foundational objectives, with the anticipated Paris Agreement envisioned as a crucial first step rather than a definitive resolution to the climate crisis. The overarching goal of global climate policy remains to limit the increase in global average temperature to 3.6°F (2°C) above pre-industrial levels, a target that becomes increasingly challenging as the Earth has already warmed by approximately 1.7°F (0.9°C) since the Industrial Revolution. Independent analyses, including those from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), indicate that even if current national plans are fully implemented, the world could still face a global temperature rise of up to 6°F (3.3°C). While this level of warming presents substantial risks, such as widespread food shortages and significant biodiversity loss, it represents a considerably less severe outcome than the projected increase of over 8°F (4.4°C) that would likely occur without these mitigation policies. UN Assistant Secretary-General for Climate Change, Janos Pasztor, has optimistically stated that these submitted plans "will bend the temperature curve" and signify a "turning point," serving as a "floor, not the ceiling" for future climate action.

Key elements underpinning the Paris Agreement negotiations include a vital "ratchet" mechanism, whereby countries are expected to convene every five to 10 years to strengthen their emissions reduction commitments, although the implementation of more stringent policies may encounter considerable political hurdles for many national governments. Furthermore, the critical issue of funding remains a focal point, with developed nations having pledged $100 billion annually to developing countries by 2020 to support clean energy initiatives and climate adaptation efforts; however, a 2015 OECD report indicated that approximately $62 billion was transferred in 2014, a figure that developing nations argue is insufficient given the trillions of dollars in estimated costs. The establishment of a robust verification process that respects national sovereignty is also paramount, particularly in light of recent disclosures, such as China's increased coal usage, which have prompted questions regarding the credibility of its submitted plan. Recognizing the potential for U.S. Senate opposition to a traditional treaty, negotiators are strategically pursuing a system of voluntary national plans bolstered by international peer pressure, a sophisticated approach often termed "legal jujitsu," designed to circumvent formal ratification processes. The overarching objective is for major economies to cultivate an environment where nations feel compelled to honor their commitments to avoid international scrutiny and reputational damage. The United Nations' requirement for unanimous consent presents a significant challenge, as a single dissenting country, a "wild-card" nation, could potentially derail the entire agreement, a scenario reminiscent of the difficulties encountered in Copenhagen.

query: What are the main features of the iPhone X that were highlighted during the Apple event?

documents.text:
## Apple Event Unveils New Era of iPhone and Devices

Apple's highly anticipated product launch event, held at the newly opened Steve Jobs Theater, marked a significant moment for the company, introducing a new generation of iPhones and updates to its popular Apple Watch and Apple TV lines. The event, which began at 1 p.m. PT, showcased advancements in design, technology, and user experience, with a particular focus on the revolutionary iPhone X.

### The Dawn of iPhone X

The star of the show was undoubtedly the iPhone X, a device Apple CEO Tim Cook described as "a new generation of the iPhone and a huge step forward," calling it "the biggest leap forward since the original iPhone." Introduced ten years after the first iPhone, the iPhone X boasts a stunning edge-to-edge Super Retina display with OLED technology and the highest pixel density yet for iPhones, according to Phil Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of worldwide marketing. The device features a durable stainless steel frame and glass construction, engineered for water and dust resistance.

**Key iPhone X Features:**

*   **Face ID:** Replacing the traditional home button, the iPhone X utilizes Face ID for unlocking the device. Schiller stated the chance of a random person unlocking the iPhone X with Face ID is 1 in 1 million, a significant improvement over Touch ID's 1 in 50,000. Face ID adapts to the user's face over time and will work with Apple Pay and third-party apps. An optional passcode feature is available to counter potential "evil twin" scenarios.
*   **No Home Button:** To wake the phone, users simply tap the screen. Swiping up from the bottom navigates to the home screen, a move Schiller deemed "simple" and "intuitive."
*   **TrueDepth Camera System:** The iPhone X's TrueDepth camera enables users to take selfies with Portrait Mode and includes a Portrait Lighting feature. Apple collaborated with Snapchat to develop advanced face filters that accurately track user expressions.
*   **Animoji:** Animated emojis that mimic the user's facial expressions are a new addition to the iPhone X.
*   **Camera Enhancements:** While sharing camera updates with the iPhone 8, the iPhone X features dual optical stabilization.

The iPhone X is priced at $999, with preorders beginning October 27 and shipping commencing November 3.

### iPhone 8 and 8 Plus: A Powerful Evolution

Alongside the iPhone X, Apple also introduced the iPhone 8 and iPhone 8 Plus. Cook described these models as a "huge step forward" for the iPhone, featuring an all-new design with glass on the front and back, available in silver, space gray, and a new gold finish. Schiller highlighted the phone's glass as the "most durable ever" in a smartphone.

**Key iPhone 8 and 8 Plus Features:**

*   **A11 Bionic Chip:** Both models are powered by the new A11 Bionic chip, which Schiller called "the most powerful and smartest chip ever in a smartphone."
*   **Wireless Charging:** The iPhone 8 and 8 Plus offer the "freedom of wireless charging," a feature Schiller noted fulfilled Apple's vision of a wireless future.
*   **Camera Upgrades:** The cameras include a 12-megapixel sensor capable of capturing over 80 percent more light for improved low-light performance. They also feature Portrait Lighting and offer the highest-quality video capture in a smartphone.
*   **Augmented Reality:** Schiller announced the iPhone 8 as the first smartphone designed for augmented reality.

The iPhone 8 starts at $699, and the iPhone 8 Plus at $799. Both are available with 64GB or 256GB of memory. Preorders began September 18, with availability starting September 22.

### Apple Watch Series 3: Connected and Capable

Jeff Williams, Apple's Chief Operating Officer, unveiled updates to the Apple Watch, which he stated is the number one watch brand globally. The Apple Watch Series 3 introduces a cellular signal built-in, allowing users greater freedom.

**Key Apple Watch Series 3 Features:**

*   **Cellular Connectivity:** With cellular built-in, users can make calls, send texts, and stream music directly from their wrist without their iPhone. Williams demonstrated this capability by taking a call while paddleboarding.
*   **Music Streaming:** Apple Watch users can stream 40 million songs.
*   **New Band:** The sport loop band is a new addition.
*   **Exercise Machine Connectivity:** The watch can connect with exercise machines.
*   **Watch OS 4 Updates:** Enhancements to workout features, including smart activity coaching and high-intensity training, were highlighted. The heart rate sensor, described as the most used in the world, also received improvements.

The Apple Watch Series 3 with cellular costs $399, and the model without cellular is $299. Preorders began September 15, with availability starting September 22 in nine countries, supported by 14 carriers.

### Apple TV 4K: A Cinematic Experience

Eddy Cue, Apple's senior vice president of internet software and services, introduced the new Apple TV 4K, which is significantly faster than its predecessor, with CPU performance more than twice as fast and graphics four times as fast.

**Key Apple TV 4K Features:**

*   **4K HDR Support:** Cook emphasized the "most stunning visuals ever," bringing cinematic quality to viewing experiences.
*   **iTunes Store Pricing:** The cost of 4K movies in the iTunes store will be the same as for HD movies.

The Apple TV 4K is priced at $179, with preorders on September 15 and shipping a week later.

### Today at Apple: In-Store Learning

Angela Ahrendts, Apple's senior vice president of retail, discussed "Today at Apple," a new program designed to offer in-store learning sessions on topics like coding, photography, and illustration. Updates to Apple's retail stores in Paris, Milan, and the iconic 5th Avenue store in New York, which will see the return of its glass cube, were also mentioned.

The event commenced with a poignant tribute to Steve Jobs, featuring a recording of his voice, before Tim Cook took the stage to officially open the Steve Jobs Theater.

Phil Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of worldwide marketing, demonstrated the iPhone X on Tuesday, touting what CEO Tim Cook called "the biggest leap forward since the original iPhone." It will be $999 and will ship on Nov. 3. As anticipated, the phone features a souped-up display and design and prominently features facial recognition technology. It has what Schiller called a "super retina" display with 2.7 million pixels, the highest resolution in pixel density ever in an iPhone. It has a 1 million-to-one contrast ratio on its 5.8-inch OLED display. The handset, pronounced "iPhone ten," has a glass back with pearly finish and comes in space gray and silver. It has 2 hours more battery life and supports wireless charging. With no home button, the phone wakes up with a tap, and Siri can be activated with a larger side button. To unlock the phone, users can activate Face ID, which identifies users with a one-in-a-million chance of mistakes. (But it's not immune to glitches, it seems). Face ID doesn't work if you're looking away, but does work with new hairstyles and facial changes, Apple said. The feature works with Apple Pay, and the sensors can animate emojis. The phone also has 3-D touch. The full coverage of the iPhone X is here.

Apple is embracing a wireless future, finally enabling users to charge their iPhones without a cable. The newly unveiled iPhone 8, iPhone 8 Plus, and iPhone X all feature support for Qi wireless charging, aligning with the standard used by Samsung devices. This move towards interoperability is a significant step for the company.

While Apple highlighted this advancement at its recent event, it did not release any dedicated wireless charging hardware for these new iPhones this year. Instead, a preview was offered of their upcoming charging solution:

*   **AirPower:** This innovative charging mat is designed to power multiple Apple devices simultaneously, including iPhones, the Apple Watch, and AirPods.
*   **Universal Compatibility:** Apple is collaborating with the Qi standard to integrate this functionality, ensuring broader compatibility.
*   **Fast Charging:** The AirPower mat will also incorporate fast charging capabilities.

The AirPower charger is slated for release next year. For the interim, Apple confirms that all Qi-certified products are compatible with the new iPhones, including those manufactured by third-party brands like Belkin and Mophie. A comprehensive list of compliant devices can be found on the Qi website.

The new iPhone X packs more new stuff into any device since the original iPhone. It's the most complete redesign of the product ever and even offers a glimpse at what the iPhone might become when the world no longer wants smartphones. Of course, you probably won't buy one. Even if you can afford the super high price, getting your hands on an iPhone X in the next few months will be like hunting for the holy grail. Except in this case, the fancy one is the right answer. Today, at an event in Cupertino that doubled as a grand opening of its new spaceship of a campus, Apple launched three new iPhones. Yes, three. The iPhone 8 and 8 Plus upgrade the existing models without completely changing them. (More on those in a second.) The third model, the iPhone X, is another thing entirely. Meet the X First of all, the X looks like no other phone. It doesn't even look like an iPhone. On the front, it's screen head to foot, save for a small trapezoidal notch taken out of the top where Apple put selfie cameras and sensors. Otherwise, the bezel around the edge of the phone has been whittled to near-nonexistence and the home button disappeared—all screen and nothing else. The case is made of glass and stainless steel, like the much-loved iPhone 4. The notched screen might take some getting used to, but the phone's a stunner. It goes on sale starting at $999 on October 27, and it ships November 3. Gadget Lab ———- Sign up for WIRED's Gadget Lab newsletter and get everything you ever wanted to know about the new iPhone in your inbox. The screen itself, called a Super Retina Display, is a 1125x2436 OLED display reportedly made by Samsung. It's the first time Apple's used OLED tech in an iPhone, and it offers some big advantages. On a normal LCD screen, even black pixels are lit up a bit, which means you're never seeing true black—just really dark gray. OLED, on the other hand, can light some pixels but not others, which means black pixels just stay off. Your dark colors will seem much darker, other colors even richer, and even text becomes more pleasant to read. Apple's also incorporated its TrueTone technology for white-balancing the screen in different conditions, which should make everything look even better. Apple Since there's no room for the home button on the front, a bunch of the feature's functions have been moved to the power button, which Apple's apparently calling the "side button" now. Long-press it for Siri, double click for Apple Pay. There's still a little tactility in this phone, even as it becomes little more than a pane of glass. Even with the huge screen, the iPhone X more closely resembles the iPhone 7. The iPhone's screen-to-size ratio was always one of the smallest in the industry, thanks to the home button and Apple's undying love for design symmetry. Getting rid of the home button allowed Jony Ive's design team to get rid of so much more—just imagine your iPhone 7, but everywhere there's metal, imagine screen. That's the iPhone X. Like the other two phones, the iPhone X runs Apple's latest processor, the A11 Bionic, along with 3 gigs of RAM. It's been years since the iPhone wanted for processing power, but the iPhone X might need all it can get. It has to power all that screen, for one thing, but Apple also seems to have created the iPhone X as a foray deeper into both artificial intelligence and augmented reality. Both require serious horsepower. The iPhone X's stated battery life has improved—Apple says it lasts two hours longer than the iPhone 7—and so has its charger. After years of observing the Android ecosystem's messy and multi-standard embrace of wireless charging, Apple's finally in the game. You won't have to plug your X into the wall to charge it; you'll just lay it down on the inductive pad. Apple is adopting the Qi standard, so it will work with current Qi pads by companies like Mophie and Belkin. Apple is also releasing its own charging accessory called AirPower that has room for your iPhone and your Apple Watch. The camera, of course, has always been one of the iPhone's standout features. rolling out a series of upgrades to the experience: You'll be able to capture slow-motion film in higher definition or shoot film-style 4K. With the new Portrait Lighting mode, you'll be able to tweak the flash on the back of the iPhone to fill a little more naturally—or make it look like your subject's lit by a spotlight on stage. Up to you. The back-facing camera array has been rotated 90 degrees so the bump runs vertically. Inside are dual 12-megapixel cameras, just like in iPhone 8 Plus, with f/1.8 and f/2.4 apertures. Dual optical image stabilization mitigates shakes in both cameras. The Samsung Galaxy Note 8 has this too, and it looks amazing. The iPhone X's camera array also has a quad-LED True Tone Flash. Turn and Face the Strange In terms of how you actually use your iPhone, the new Face ID may be the most important new feature of the iPhone X. When you first start using an iPhone X, you'll go through a short setup, the face version of registering your thumbprint—you'll peer into the front-facing camera, framing your face just right, then turn your head left and right so the iPhone's sensors can get a good look. Once you set up Face ID, the feature essentially replaces your fingerprint. You'll unlock your phone by holding it up to your face, pay for things by holding it up to your face, and log in to apps … by holding it up to your face. It requires your attention to unlock the phone, and Apple says it has done extensive work to make sure masks and photos can't fool it. Apple appears to see the feature as a way to help you use the phone while you're using it. The iPhone X can double-check whether you're using the phone before shutting off the screen, and if it sees you're looking it won't play loud alert sounds. There are a lot of things Apple could do when it knows you're looking at the phone, many of them cool and some of them very creepy. (Be sure to read Andy Greenberg's initial dive into the security of Face ID.) All of these facial-recognition features are made possible by an array of cameras and sensors packed into that notch at the top of the screen. The TrueDepth array, as Apple calls it, projects infrared dots onto your face to map it, captures that image, then uses a dedicated processor to interpret the face data. The promise is that it can always be sure it's really you looking at the phone, even if you're wearing glasses or you change your hair or grow a beard. The same tech that powers Face ID also comes to life inside the new Animoji, which are iMessage emoji that map to your face, so you can put your voice into the mouth of a unicorn or a dog. Not creepy! Apple touted Face ID as both more secure and more convenient than a fingerprint, and the onstage demos certainly made it look that way. It's very fast; about as snappy as Touch ID. But Samsung and others have already tried to make the feature work, and it's been neither secure nor convenient. Phil Schiller, senior vice president of worldwide marketing at Apple Inc., speaks about the new iPhone 8 and 8 Plus on September 12, 2017. David Paul Morris/Bloomberg/Getty Images Lucky Number 8 If you can't get your hands on an iPhone X in the near future, Apple still has two new models for you. The iPhone 8 and 8 Plus both look like the iPhone 7—with home buttons!—but offer a few big upgrades to match the iPhone X. Both new models support wireless charging, run the latest A11 Bionic processor, and have 2 gigs of RAM. They also have glass backs, which gives them a glossy new look. They don't have OLED screens, but they're getting the same TrueTone tech as the X, and they can shoot video in 4K. In most years, the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus would be the "S" phones, a mid-generation upgrade. But this year, partly as a show of strength and partly to keep up with the Note and Galaxy numberings, they're the iPhones 8. You can buy them in black, silver, and a newly retooled gold finish for $699 and up for the regular one, $799 for the big one. You can preorder the 8 and 8 Plus on September 15, and the phones arrive in stores on September 22. If you've been obsessively following rumors and firmware leaks, little about the iPhone announcements came as a surprise. After Tim Cook promised to "double down on secrecy," the secrets spilled faster than ever. Bloomberg's Mark Gurman reported many of the device's details, as did developer Steve Troughton Smith. But the biggest leak came last weekend, just a few days before the announcement, when the final build of iOS 11 leaked to 9to5Mac. The firmware was absolutely loaded with details, from Animoji images to Face ID setup videos to the names of the new devices. This was Apple's biggest announcement in years, and possibly its least surprising. Still, the iPhone X should excite you. Not just because it's cool-looking and futuristic, one giant step closer to the "just a screen in your hand" device so many science fiction lovers imagined. This iPhone moves a number of important ideas forward. Apple has the unique power to make, say, wireless charging work—and between the three new models, Apple instantly makes relevant whatever standard it chooses. Most of all, the iPhone X makes a powerful statement that augmented reality is coming. It might be here already. When Apple launched the iPhone 10 years ago, the device was three things: a phone, an internet communicator, and an iPod. It's so many more now, to so many more people, but the iPhone X may be the first to be redesigned around a specific new purpose. The more dynamic screen, the vertically stacked cameras, the tiny bezels, the faster processor—all those things work together to make the iPhone X primarily about what's on the other side. You're not supposed to look at this device, but through it. iPhone, You Phone

Apple has officially launched the iPhone X, a premium device anticipated to be in high demand with limited initial supply. Pronounced "10," this highly awaited release signifies a major evolution for Apple's flagship smartphone. Alongside the iPhone X, Apple also introduced the iPhone 8 and iPhone 8 Plus, which represent more incremental updates.

The iPhone X features a striking new design with a nearly borderless, 5.8-inch "Super Retina" OLED display. While this is a first for iPhones, OLED technology has been a hallmark of Samsung devices for years, with models like the Galaxy S8 and Note 8 already showcasing similar edge-to-edge designs.

A pivotal innovation in the iPhone X is Face ID, a new facial recognition system that replaces the Touch ID fingerprint sensor. This advanced technology projects 30,000 invisible infrared dots to map a user's face, enabling secure unlocking and Apple Pay authentication. The elimination of the home button was crucial to accommodate the expansive, all-screen display.

Further enhancing the iPhone X are its capabilities:

*   **Wireless Charging:** Enabled by a glass back.
*   **A11 Bionic Processor:** A powerful new chip.
*   **Upgraded Camera:** A 12-megapixel sensor.
*   **Animoji:** Animated emojis controlled by facial expressions, powered by Face ID.

Pricing for the iPhone X begins at $999 for the 64GB model, with a 256GB version priced at $1,149. This marks a substantial price increase from the iPhone 7's base model launch price of $549. Preorders for the iPhone X open on October 27, with devices shipping on November 3.

The launch event was held at the new Steve Jobs Theater on Apple's Cupertino campus, a symbolic location honoring Apple's co-founder. The iPhone X also commemorates the 10th anniversary of the original iPhone, which Steve Jobs famously described in 2007 as "a revolutionary and magical product that is literally five years ahead of any other mobile phone."

The iPhone has been a cornerstone of Apple's success, driving over two-thirds of its sales. However, the company has faced challenges with declining iPhone sales, mirroring a broader smartphone market slowdown. The industry experienced its slowest growth rate last year, with total shipments rising only 2.5 percent to 1.47 billion, according to IDC. Projections indicate a modest 1.7 percent increase for the current year, with continued but less explosive growth anticipated through 2021.

Despite these market pressures, Apple is tasked with delivering groundbreaking devices. The iPhone remains its most critical product, fueling sales of other Apple ecosystem products like Macs, Apple Watches, and the upcoming HomePod speakers. Apple CEO Tim Cook highlighted the iPhone's profound global impact, stating, "No other device in our lifetimes has had the impact on the world that the iPhone has." He positioned the iPhone X as a device that will "set the path for technology for the next decade."

The iPhone 8 and 8 Plus, while visually similar to their predecessors, incorporate several enhancements:

*   **Improved Camera:** A 12-megapixel sensor with enhanced video capabilities.
*   **A11 Bionic Processor:** The same powerful new chip found in the iPhone X.
*   **Augmented Reality Features:** New AR capabilities.
*   **Wireless Charging:** Enabled by new glass backs.

The displays remain 4.7-inch for the iPhone 8 and 5.5-inch for the iPhone 8 Plus.

However, the less dramatic upgrades on the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus could present a challenge for Apple, as these are likely to be the more popular choices for upgraders. Analyst Jan Dawson of Jackdaw Research noted, "What Apple doesn't want is for people to want the best but not be able to afford it, and therefore hold onto their existing phones rather than buying what they consider second best."

The iPhone 8 will be available in 64GB and 256GB configurations, starting at $699. The iPhone 8 Plus will offer the same storage options, beginning at $799. Preorders for both models commence on Friday, with availability on September 22.

Apple welcomed the press into its brand-new Steve Jobs Theater today to show off its latest slate of products . Many waited with bated breath for the new gizmos the company would announce. All morning, before the keynote began, the company’s stock remained pretty stagnant. Then, once it started, the price began to rise. As Tim Cook announced a new Apple Watch and Apple Store design, it rose steadily. Then, at around 1:55 p.m. EST, Apple stock peaked at $163.54. Coincidentally, this is also right when Apple announced its much anticipated new iPhone products: iPhone 8 and iPhone X. Since then, the price has fallen, eventually falling below its opening price of $161.50. The price is very likely to bounce back, of course, and shares are still up from last week when they bottomed out at $159.63. But if we take this immediate Wall Street reaction as any indication, not everyone is excited for the new iPhone models.

Apple's iOS 11, unveiled at its WWDC conference, brings a host of new features and enhancements to iPhones and iPads. The public release of iOS 11 is scheduled for September 19, following a beta period. While not a radical visual overhaul, iOS 11 introduces several significant improvements.

A standout addition is the new Files app. This feature centralizes all your documents, whether stored locally on your iPhone or in cloud services like iCloud and Dropbox. It functions similarly to the Finder on macOS, offering a streamlined way to manage and access your digital content. Developers can integrate their apps into the Files app, allowing individual applications to appear as folders and simplifying document transfer between programs.

The Control Center has also been redesigned with customizable, bubble-like icons. Users can now select which tools appear in the Control Center, much like customizing widgets. The expanded display area for the Control Center allows for more tools, and many functions will be accessible via 3D Touch.

Other notable iOS 11 features include Apple Pay integration within iMessage, an updated Siri with a new voice and the ability to type requests, and a revamped App Store that separates Games and Apps. For drivers, a "Do Not Disturb While Driving" mode will automatically silence notifications when the device detects you are in a moving vehicle.

iPads receive substantial upgrades in iOS 11, addressing a recent trend of fewer software enhancements compared to iPhones. The iPad's Dock is now persistent, always accessible even within apps. A swipe up from the bottom of the screen reveals the Dock, enabling easy access to other applications and the use of Split View for multitasking. The updated App Switcher, alongside the new Control Center, allows simultaneous access to both. The App Switcher will also remember preferred Split View app combinations, allowing users to resume multitasking sessions effortlessly.

The practical Drag and Drop feature allows users to highlight information and transfer it to another app, akin to copy-pasting. This is particularly useful in Split View but can also be used by dragging content to the Home screen and then opening another application.

Apple Pencil users will find expanded capabilities in iOS 11. Instant Markup enables screenshot annotation with the Apple Pencil. A "Mark Through PDF" option converts any screen into an annotatable PDF. Inline drawing allows users to handwrite directly into notes or emails, with typed text automatically formatting around the handwritten content for seamless integration of text and scribbles.

These advancements, particularly for iPad Pro users, position the iPad as a potential laptop replacement and a strong contender in the convertible and detachable device markets.

query: What did Rep. Paul Ryan describe the IRS targeting of conservative groups as, and what did he vow regarding the investigation?

documents.text: Rep. Paul Ryan said Sunday the IRS targeting of conservative groups “is arrogance of power, abuse of power, to the nth degree” and vowed the investigation into the misconduct will continue. “Look, people have no trust that their government is being impartial,” the Wisconsin Republican said on “Fox News Sunday.” “This is arrogance of power, abuse of power, to the nth degree, and we're going to get to the bottom of this.” Ryan said there is “credible evidence that donors were targeted, that the IRS leaked private information to the public, which served political purposes.” “So there's so much more that we have just uncovered that we do not know the root causes of. And so to suggest that this is some bureaucratic snafu, that's been disproven, Chris,” Ryan told Fox host Chris Wallace. “The other point I'd say, as bad as this is, the person in charge of this bureaucratic snafu has now been put in charge of Obamacare.” Putting Sarah Hall Ingram, who formerly oversaw the tax exempt division before heading to her current post helping the agency implement Obamacare, in charge of implementing the health care law is “rotten to the core,” Ryan said. “This is arrogance,” he said. “This is big government cronyism. And this is not what hard-working taxpayers deserve. People deserve a government they can trust, that's honest, that's impartial, equality before the law, and that is not what we're getting here. And so to try to suggest that this is just bureaucratic snafus, we already know that's not true.” Wallace also asked Ryan about how he views the Benghazi talking points in light of the fact during that time he was Mitt Romney’s running mate and whether he thought the White House purposely misled the American people in order to defeat the GOP ticket. “I don’t know the answer to that question, so I’m not even going to speculate, but that’s what good oversight does,” Ryan said, noting that he won’t make a conclusion before the investigation is completed. What the American people do know right now, he added, is that the White House was “misleading.” Ryan also blasted Obama’s second term, saying “now we're seeing big government in practice. Now we're seeing the arrogance, the cronyism in practice in this second term. And that is even uglier than big government in theory.” Read more about: Paul Ryan, IRS, Obamacare

The seed for this crawl was a list of every host in the Wayback Machine This crawl was run at a level 1 (URLs including their embeds, plus the URLs of all outbound links including their embeds) The WARC files associated with this crawl are not currently available to the general public.

Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell has asserted that a pervasive "culture of intimidation" exists within the current administration, citing the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) as the most recent manifestation of this issue. Speaking on NBC's "Meet the Press" with host David Gregory, the Kentucky Republican argued that the IRS's targeting of conservative groups exemplifies a governmental mindset where "the government knows best." He elaborated, stating, "The nanny state is here to tell us all what to do. And if we start criticizing, you get targeted."

McConnell suggested that such an environment sends a clear signal to IRS agents. "It's no wonder that the agents in the IRS sorta get the message," he remarked, adding that "The president demonizes his opponents." However, McConnell emphasized that further information is crucial, as the investigation into the matter "has just begun."

"I don't think we know what the facts are," McConnell stated. "The investigation has just begun, so I'm not going to reach a conclusion about what we may find. But what we do know happened is they were targeting tea party groups. We know that." This sentiment was echoed by various conservative organizations, including the Tea Party Patriots, who have been vocal in their concerns about potential overreach by federal agencies. Experts from the Heritage Foundation have also weighed in, highlighting the importance of transparency and accountability in government operations.

Veteran journalist Bob Woodward recently appeared on NBC's "Meet the Press" to dissect the current spate of Washington scandals. While he clearly differentiated these contemporary issues from the monumental Watergate scandal, Woodward identified a striking parallel to the period following the Benghazi attack. He suggested that certain individuals within the administration were exhibiting "Nixonian" tendencies, a behavior he deemed a significant concern.

Woodward articulated his perspective, stating, "This is not Watergate, but there are some people in the administration who have acted as if they want to be Nixonian. And that’s a very big problem." When host Chuck Todd sought further clarification, Woodward directed his attention to the developing narrative surrounding the Benghazi incident. He specifically referenced the initial draft of talking points prepared by the CIA, noting that this early version "very explicitly said we know that activists who have ties to al Qaeda were involved in the attack."

Expressing his apprehension regarding subsequent alterations to these points, Woodward observed, "And then you see what comes out a couple of days later and there is no reference to this." He emphasized the fundamental importance of truthfulness in governmental operations, concluding, "This is a business where you have to tell the truth and that did not happen here." Given Woodward's extensive experience as a journalist who covered the Watergate scandal, his remarks bring to light critical inquiries into the management of information in the wake of the Benghazi attacks.

Just back from a trip to Iraq, House Oversight and Government Reform Committee Chairman Darrell Issa said on Sunday the government in Baghdad is “still quite delusional” about the urgency of the war against the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant. “They're still talking about long-term training before they're ready to fight,” the California Republican said an ABC’s “This Week.” Issa said he was ready to vote to authorize the U.S. military campaign against ISIL — something President Barack Obama has requested. “The fact is we're already there,” Issa said. “We've had to be there.” Issa also said Iraqis — not Americans — should be the ones engaging in ground combat against ISIL. The Kurds were up to the challenge, he said. “I have no doubt whatsoever that the Kurds will fight,” he said. “All they need is our air support and our technical know-how, and they will do it.”

Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell charged on Sunday the Obama administration “made up a tale” about the attacks on the U.S. consulate in Benghazi, Libya, in fall 2012 because it “was inconvenient within six weeks of the election for the administration to, in effect, announce that it was a terrorist attack.” NBC “Meet the Press” host David Gregory also asked McConnell about the talk of impeachment from some Republicans over Benghazi and their accusation it was a “massive cover-up.” “I don't think I've said any of those things,” the Republican senator from Kentucky said. “I think you're talking about others may have said various things about this. Let me tell you what i think about it. It's clear that there was inadequate security there, and it's very clear that it was inconvenient within six weeks of the election for the administration to, in effect, announce that it was a terrorist attack. I think that's worth examining. It is going to be examined. And it's important, you know, this is the first time we've had an ambassador killed in the line of duty since the late 70s.” “You are the one of the leading Republicans in America,” Gregory replied. “Would you call on Republicans who talk about impeaching the president or who talk about this as an Nixonian style cover-up with regard to Benghazi, would you like them to stop it?” “What I think we ought to do is complete the investigation and find out what exactly happened,” McConnell said. “And I think we have a sense of what happened. We know there was inadequate security, we know an American ambassador and three other brave Americans got killed, and we know the administration kind of made up a tale here in order to make it seem like it wasn't a terrorist attack. I think that's worthy of investigation and the investigations ought to go forward.” Clearly, McConnell said, the talking points were “not accurate.” “Getting to the bottom of that is an important investigation,” he said. Read more about: Mitch McConnell, Libya, Benghazi

**Associated Press CEO Denounces DOJ Phone Record Seizure as "Unconstitutional Act"**

Gary Pruitt, the President and CEO of the Associated Press (AP), has strongly condemned the Department of Justice's (DOJ) seizure of the news agency's phone records, labeling it an "unconstitutional act." Pruitt articulated his concerns on Sunday during an appearance on CBS's "Face the Nation," asserting that while the DOJ has the right to conduct investigations, their methods in this instance were deeply flawed.

Pruitt characterized the DOJ's actions as:

*   **Sweeping:** Encompassing a vast and indiscriminate collection of data.
*   **Secretive:** Conducted without prior notification to the Associated Press.
*   **Abusive and Harassing:** Creating an environment of intimidation and undue pressure.
*   **Overbroad:** Exceeding the necessary scope of the investigation.

The DOJ's decision to obtain phone records, which reportedly involved "thousands" of AP calls, stemmed from an investigation into a security leak concerning a 2012 terrorist attack that was successfully thwarted by the CIA. The Associated Press had reported on this foiled attack, citing administration officials. Pruitt emphasized that the Obama administration was aware of the AP's accurate reporting on the incident. He recalled that the White House had requested the AP delay publication for five days due to national security concerns, a request to which the AP complied.

"I really don’t know what their motive is," Pruitt stated. "I know what the message being sent is — it’s that, ‘If you talk to the press, we’re going to go after you.’"

Pruitt further highlighted that the DOJ failed to inform the AP about its investigation. He contended that the agency violated its own internal guidelines by not narrowing its records request to the greatest extent possible. "Under their own rules, they are required to narrow this request as narrowly as possible so as to not tread upon the First Amendment," Pruitt explained. "And yet they had a broad, sweeping collection. And they did it secretly."

The AP CEO expressed concern that this action has already had a chilling effect, making sources hesitant to speak with journalists. "I think it will hurt journalists," Pruitt concluded, underscoring the potential damage to the free press and its ability to gather information. This incident raises significant questions about the balance between national security investigations and the First Amendment rights of the press, as previously discussed by organizations like the Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press.

Former Defense Secretary Donald Rumsfeld has called for "zero tolerance" in military sexual assault cases, suggesting that current handling methods may need reevaluation. Speaking on NBC's "Meet the Press" with host David Gregory, Rumsfeld addressed the possibility of altering the reporting structure for these crimes and the potential use of an outside special prosecutor.

While Rumsfeld expressed uncertainty about whether a special prosecutor is the definitive solution, he acknowledged a compelling argument for a different approach to managing these serious offenses. He speculated that a significant number of sexual assaults within the military might go unreported. "It's a terrible thing, there has to be zero tolerance," Rumsfeld stated, emphasizing the gravity of the issue. He further commented, "And it appears that something different's going to have to be done, and I wish I knew what the answer was, I don't." Rumsfeld concluded by stressing the importance of accountability, asserting that those in leadership positions "better really land all over people that are engaged in any kind of abuse of that nature."

query: What controversial comparisons were made about Stephen Bannon in a French cable news report?

documents.text: PARIS—Never mind that the French cable television report on Stephen Bannon made him look like one of the winos living on grates in Paris, or that it compared Bannon, Donald Trump’s grizzled éminence grise and newly-named White House chief strategist, to Adolf Hitler’s propaganda chief Josef Goebbels. And never mind the program’s citations of rampant sexism on Bannon’s pseudo-news service, Breitbart. All he had to do in that clip was say he’d like to expand Breitbart’s operations to France and allude to Marion Maréchal-Le Pen as “the new rising star” on the French version of the alt-right and she started gushing on Twitter in English: Interestingly, the citations from Bannon on that broadcast by the mainstream LCI network did not mention Marine Le Pen, the 48-year-old woman who has turned her father’s fringe right-wing party, the National Front, into the most dynamic and aggressive political force in the country. The reference cited was to “the Le Pen women” generally, as if there were so many, and then to the comely Marion, a member of the French parliament who is only 26 years old and has the xanthachroidal allure of a younger Ann Coulter or Laura Ingraham. Must be something in the air, l’esprit du temps, as they say. Let’s take a breath and look at what’s really going on here. Bannon’s support for European far-right parties runs far deeper than his interest in Marion Maréchal-Le Pen or the National Front. He brags about his international Breitbart operation as “the platform” for the American alt-right, and has for years been thinking globally, with an affinity for the United Kingdom Independence Party (UKIP), Alternative for Germany (AfD), and the Party for Freedom Party (PVV) in the Netherlands, all of which have earned glowing coverage on the pages of Breitbart. But the election of Bannon’s man Donald Trump as president of the United States has made the globalization of Breitbart and its message infinitely more plausible than it ever was before, and politicians once considered Europe’s deplorables are now rushing to bask in the gilded glow of Trump and Bannon.
