Queen Elizabeth is the longest-reigning monarch in British history. Look back at moments from her life so far. Queen Elizabeth is the longest-reigning monarch in British history. Look back at moments from her life so far. I was covering the Golden Jubilee in 2002 and we'd asked the Palace if on the opening day, she would adjust her schedule by a few minutes to help the broadcasters. The Palace agreed. So when I met Her Majesty, I thanked her for changing her schedule. Unfortunately, I pronounced it "SKedule" not "SHedule. I'd used the American pronunciation in front of the Queen AND in her Castle. Brilliant move! She gently corrected me and then laughed. The King was right. I still remember our meeting, which is why, even during my years living in America, I never use the American pronunciation. And if any one questions me about it, I tell them to ring Buckingham Palace for an explanation! Stage presence When you meet the Queen, she tries to put you at ease. But it's still nerve wracking. After I'd shaken her hand, I thought about all those world leaders, pop stars, sports legends and others who've shaken THAT hand. Despite being 5'4" tall, the Queen has enormous presence when she enters the room. It's a most unusual feeling. My first encounter with Her Majesty came during her Silver Jubilee in 1977. She visited my hometown of Hull in the North of England. I wonder if she'd forgiven the city for rising up against one of her predecessors, King Charles I. Hide Caption 10 of 10 The protests had ignited the English Civil War that led to the King losing his head -- and all future monarchs ruling not through the Divine Right, but with the blessing of their people. That first sight of the Queen was one I'll always remember. We didn't see her often in those days before 24 hour news. Sure, she was on our stamps, our coins and her picture hung in the school assembly hall. But to see her in the flesh was quite something. I was with hundreds of choirboys that day singing to her. I really hope she doesn't remember that! Patience - more than a virtue Over the years, I've seen the Queen in action on many Royal visits. God only knows how she does it. She travels all over the world, meets countless people, tries to ensure a routine day for her is a special day for those she meets. One event after another, listening to and giving speeches, shaking endless outstretched hands and all with a smile on her face. It must be tough because, in truth, some of the speeches can be dull and some of the events are pretty boring. But she can't exactly yawn when she's bored or, indeed, tired. And if she is bored or tired, you'd never know it. Little is known about her JUST WATCHED The contentious Brexit issue has even reached the Queen Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH The contentious Brexit issue has even reached the Queen 01:37 Despite being one of the most famous people in the world, very few of her subjects really know much about her. She's never given an interview, never written an autobiography. We know she likes horses, corgis and the outdoors. We know also that she's a deeply religious person and has a sense of humor. Apparently she's an excellent mimic. But we know nothing about her politics or her views on anything remotely controversial. Sense of duty Some commentators have criticized her for being a bit remote as a mother. Yet when she put her grandmotherly responsibilities to Princes William and Harry first in the days after Princess Diana died, she was criticized for that too. I remember that time very well and the angry mood of the British people -- it was unprecedented. That week saw the biggest wobble of her reign. What a remarkable turnaround that just five years later, millions of people around the Commonwealth came onto the streets to celebrate her Golden Jubilee. They met at school and even shared a house with others while students. Hide Caption 34 of 34 So why is the Queen so popular? JUST WATCHED The world's longest serving monarchs Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH The world's longest serving monarchs 01:23 Well for a start she's not a politician. She also stands for something and does not do knee-jerk reactions to some poll or focus group. Again, not a politician. She's seen as genuine, somebody who cares about her country, her Commonwealth and her people. In times of sadness and despair, people look to the Queen and her family for words of support. I remember her doing just that after the London bombings of 2005. She and her family are also there in times of joy to celebrate with us. Remember that dramatic arrival with James Bond at the opening of the London Olympics? The Queen also provides a sense of continuity in a changing world. Sure, there are people who would like to see an end of monarchy but they are few in number compared to those who, day in day out, welcome the Queen to their towns, cities and homes around the world. Her realms and territories extend across the globe. She's monarch in places ranging from New Zealand in the south to Scotland in the north, taking in Australia, Canada and a host of Caribbean countries. She's also Head of the Commonwealth -- a 53-nation club whose membership numbers almost a third of the world's population. On those occasions when millions turn up outside Buckingham Palace and sing God Save the Queen, they truly mean it. Queen Elizabeth II Through The Years Queen Elizabeth II In 1976 The Queen Elizabeth II, dressed in royal regalia, posed for this special Jubilee picture in the Throne Room of Buckingham Palace, after she had delivered the traditional Queen's speech at the 1976 official State Opening of Parliament. She wears the Imperial State Crown and the Robe of State, also known as the Parliamentary Robe. Around her neck is the Jubilee Necklace of diamonds and pearls and the chain is the Collar of the Order of the Garter, Britain's premier order of Knighthood. Her gown is of white silk with bands of gold and silver embroidery. (Photo credit /AFP/Getty Images)

Stepping into the shoes of Queen Elizabeth II, one of the world's most recognizable figures, presents a unique challenge and fascination for actors, especially as she approaches significant milestones. What is it truly like to embody the Queen, and what compels so many performers to take on this iconic role?

For many actresses, the prospect of portraying the Queen might initially seem daunting. However, two performers who have embraced this challenge on stage reveal a surprisingly crucial element for inhabiting the character: the wig. **Emma Handy**, who embodied the 1980s iteration of the Queen, nicknamed "Liz," in Moira Buffini's play *Handbagged*, found the costume to be profoundly transformative. The play ingeniously imagines older and younger versions of the Queen and Margaret Thatcher engaging in conversation. Handy remarked, "The wig and make-up and clothes do so much. It's extraordinary." She also identified a personal resonance: "She seems to have two faces – the public and the private face. There is very little footage of her private life, but she is known to be enormously mischievous – and these are things I have in common with her." Handy stressed her commitment to capturing the Queen's "essence" rather than merely performing an impersonation, noting that sitting "incredibly straight-backed" proved instrumental. She observed, "She's probably the most photographed person in the world, so everyone has an idea of her. Every year you see the Christmas broadcast, but that's not who she is. There is a massive side of her people don't know." **Marion Bailey**, who portrayed the elder Queen, referred to as "Q," in the West End production of *Handbagged*, described the role as "irresistible." For the London run, Bailey immersed herself in the Queen's Christmas messages and other available online footage. The audience's reception was deeply gratifying. "Some nights I would walk out and there would be gasps," she said. "It was to do with the look of it and the history, and I tried to get as many of the mannerisms as I could. On the West End transfer, I heard an American tourist whisper: 'Is that the Queen?'"

The Queen's pervasive presence in popular culture is undeniable, evidenced by numerous portrayals across diverse media. Peter Morgan's 2006 film *The Queen*, starring Helen Mirren, focused on the public's reaction to Diana, Princess of Wales's death. Morgan also penned *The Audience*, which saw Mirren and Kristin Scott Thomas take on the role on stage. Channel 4's *The Queen* presented five actresses, including Emilia Fox and Samantha Bond, depicting the monarch at various life stages, while the forthcoming Netflix series *The Crown*, written by Morgan and starring Claire Foy, will chronicle six decades of her life. Songs like The Beatles' "Her Majesty" and the Sex Pistols' "God Save the Queen" have referenced the monarch, and Alan Bennett explored her character in his book *The Uncommon Reader* and play *A Question of Attribution*. Queen lookalike Jeannette Charles cultivated a career impersonating the monarch, appearing in films like *The Naked Gun* and *Austin Powers in Goldmember*. Professor Philip Murphy, director of the Institute of Commonwealth Studies at the University of London, suggests the 1969 documentary *Royal Family* offered the public their most intimate glimpse of the "real" Queen, but notes, "The Royal Family effectively suppressed it. They decided it reduced the mystique around them. In a way, that creates a carte blanche for writers and satirists to make up personalities." He further elaborates on the inherent challenge: "That's the problem the Royal Family has – they want to appear human but they also want to appear somehow removed and keep this dignified distance." Professor Murphy cites *Spitting Image* as an example of satire that targeted authority figures, with lead writer John O'Farrell explaining their approach: "If you cast your mind back to the 1980s, there was an enormous reverential style. It was seen as poor form to laugh at the Queen's expense... But what we did was show her to be an ordinary person in an ordinary family, which is where it struck a comic nerve." Artist Alison Jackson has also courted controversy with her work, employing lookalikes to create seemingly candid photographs of celebrities in private moments, stating, "I thought it would be really good to get her doing normal things. She is a person, after all." Jackson finds it intriguing that one of her photographs, showing "the Queen" on the toilet, is her least published, suggesting that "people think breaking that taboo is tasteless." She clarifies that her intention is to be "thought-provoking, rather than controversial." While these fictional representations may be thought-provoking, they arguably do not bring us closer to understanding the Queen's true self. The sole instance of her playing herself was in a James Bond sketch for the London Olympics opening ceremony in 2012, alongside Daniel Craig. Nevertheless, this has not deterred continued efforts to capture the essence of this elusive, yet ever-present, figure.

Prince William has shared deeply personal reflections on the profound and unwavering support he received from his grandmother, Queen Elizabeth II. This support proved especially crucial during the challenging years that followed the tragic death of his mother, Princess Diana. Speaking in a candid interview with Sky News, as the nation prepared to celebrate Her Majesty's 90th birthday, the Duke of Cambridge described the Queen as a "strong female influence." He highlighted her vital guidance, particularly during his formative teenage years.

"Having lost my mother at a very young age, it has been particularly important for me that I had somebody like the Queen to look up to and who has been there and has understood some of the more complex issues when you lose a loved one," Prince William revealed. He was only 15 years old when his mother tragically died in a car accident in Paris in 1997. In the immediate aftermath of Princess Diana's death, the Queen and the Duke of Edinburgh faced considerable public criticism. This stemmed from their decision to remain at Balmoral with William and his younger brother, Prince Harry, instead of promptly returning to Buckingham Palace. This period, and his relationship with his grandmother during it, is an aspect of his life that Prince William has rarely discussed publicly.

When asked about particularly cherished childhood memories of the Queen, Prince William recounted a memorable incident at Balmoral. This event vividly illustrated the Queen's firm, yet loving, approach to discipline. He vividly recalled a chaotic moment involving a quad bike and a go-cart. "We were on a quad bike in Balmoral and we were chasing Zara around who was on a go-cart. Peter and I managed to herd Zara into a lamppost and the lamppost came down and nearly squashed her," he explained. The young prince’s memory of the event remains sharp. "I remember my grandmother being the first person out and running across the lawn in her kilt. She came charging over and gave us the most almighty b*****king." This stern reprimand, he admitted, left a lasting impression. It taught him the importance of being more mindful of his actions and their potential consequences, especially concerning his cousins.

Despite facing recent media scrutiny regarding his efforts to balance demanding royal duties, family life, and his vital role as an air ambulance pilot, Prince William emphasized that he has his grandmother's full endorsement. "Surprisingly, she is very supportive of what I'm doing at the moment - she understands that I'm a family man and I want to be around for my children as much as I can," he stated. He acknowledged the Queen's enduring leadership and active engagement in public life. He noted her "incredibly packed" schedule. He also recognized the significant contributions of his father, Prince Charles, and the broader royal family. This includes his brother Harry and his wife Catherine, in fulfilling their charitable and public commitments. Prince William expressed his understanding that there is a "time and a place for taking on more and more responsibilities" as the family evolves.

The Duke of Cambridge indicated his anticipation of gradually increasing his royal duties in the future. However, for the present, his primary focus remains on his young children. He also prioritizes his "incredibly worthwhile and meaningful job" serving the community through his work with the air ambulance service. He believes these commitments, combined with fulfilling his royal obligations as and when required, represent a "good balance."

The Queen and the Duke of Edinburgh were scheduled to undertake public engagements in Windsor on the day of the interview. This was in anticipation of Her Majesty's actual birthday the following day. Her official birthday celebrations are traditionally held in June.

A special program, "The Queen At 90," was scheduled to air on Sky News at 9 pm that evening. Extensive live coverage was planned throughout the following day.
