President Donald J. Trump's remarks following the events in Charlottesville have triggered a significant reaction within corporate America. This has resulted in a notable wave of departures from his business advisory councils. While the stock market experienced modest gains today, with the S&P 500 rising 0.2% to 2,468.63, the Dow Jones Industrial Average advancing 30.80 points (0.1%) to 22,029.79, and the Nasdaq Composite gaining 0.2% to 6,344.86, the primary focus has shifted to the high-profile exits from the President's manufacturing council.

This exodus saw chief executive officers from prominent companies, including 3M and Campbell Soup, announce their departures today. The situation further intensified when the Strategic and Policy Forum, a group comprising leaders from General Motors, BlackRock, and IBM, also decided to disband. The initial spark for these departures was Merck CEO Kenneth Frazier's announcement on Monday. He stated he was leaving the manufacturing council. This decision followed President Trump's initial response to the Charlottesville events. At that time, he attributed blame to "many sides." His subsequent comments, which appeared to defend white nationalist marchers by stating some were "very fine people," seem to have been the critical turning point for many in the corporate world.

Several key factors are driving these high-profile decisions.

One significant factor is the **risk of presidential displeasure**. There is a clear danger associated with displeasing President Trump. This is evident from his public criticisms, often delivered via Twitter. He notably responded to Merck's Frazier by suggesting the CEO now has more time to focus on lowering drug prices. He also labeled departing CEOs as "grandstanders."

Another crucial element is the **reputational impact**. Academic research consistently underscores the vital importance of corporate reputation for stock performance. A 2000 paper titled "Opportunity Platforms and Safety Nets: Corporate Citizenship and Reputational Risk," authored by Fombrun, Gardberg, and Barnett, argues that strong "reputational capital" provides distinct advantages. These include better contract negotiations, the ability to command premium pricing, and a reduced cost of capital.

Furthermore, the threat of **customer backlash** plays a significant role. A February 2007 Harvard Business Review article, "Reputation and Its Risks," by Eccles, Newquist, and Schatz, highlights a clear pattern. Companies are rewarded for positive reputations and penalized for negative ones. Under Armour's Kevin Plank experienced this directly in February. His praise for the President led to significant backlash. This backlash even came from sponsored athletes. This experience underscores a critical business reality: alienating customers is profoundly detrimental to operations.

CLOSE President Donald Trump disbands economic councils. Elizabeth Keatinge (@elizkeatinge) has more. Buzz60 President Trump at Trump Tower on Tuesday. (Photo: JIM WATSON, AFP/Getty Images) The fallout from Donald Trump's response to a deadly neo-Nazi rally in Charlottesville escalated Wednesday as two CEO-dominated groups organized to help the president chart a course toward U.S. economic and job growth were disbanded. Early Wednesday afternoon Trump tweeted "Rather than putting pressure on the businesspeople of the Manufacturing Council & Strategy & Policy Forum, I am ending both. Thank you all!" But a dissolution led by the members of at least one of the panels appears to have already been in the works and was set to be announced. Trump used Twitter to beat the members to the punch. Two administration officials said council members had spoken with White House officials in recent days, though they didn't know if Trump himself got involved. The CEOs held a conference call to discuss their role with the White House. The disbanding of the groups followed a cascade of departures from the manufacturing council kicked off Monday by Merck CEO Kenneth Frazier who said he quit the manufacturing council as "a matter of personal conscience." Frazier's resignation prompted an angry rebuke from Trump on Twitter. "Now that Ken Frazier of Merck Pharma has resigned from President's Manufacturing Council, he will have more time to LOWER RIPOFF DRUG PRICES!," Trump tweeted. Despite the attack against Frazier and the drugmaker, his exit was followed soon by CEOs of Intel and Under Armour and representatives from labor union AFL-CIO. Campbell Soup CEO Denise Morrison and 3M CEO Inge Thulin Wednesday joined the growing list of American chief executives who resigned from the council, an advisory group the White House formed this year. Rather than putting pressure on the businesspeople of the Manufacturing Council & Strategy & Policy Forum, I am ending both. Thank you all! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) August 16, 2017 Pressure from the business community had been intensifying following Trump's widely criticized response to violence that erupted after the rally in Charlottesville, Va., over the weekend After internal and external pressure, Trump relented and mentioned the hate groups, including neo-Nazis and KKK. But in a defiant return to form, Trump stood before reporters in the lobby of Trump Tower Tuesday afternoon and said counter-protesters were also to blame for the tragedy that left three dead and dozens hurt. CEOs who initially chose to stick with the councils had cited the need to stay engaged with the White House to make meaningful changes. But the steady drumbeat for them to disassociate from Trump was unrelenting and inspired the Twitter hashtag #Quitthecouncil. As manufacturing sector chief executives began to jump ship, none of the CEOs on Trump's economic council — the Strategic and Policy Forum — had quit as of Wednesday morning. But they also began to receive inquiries from activists and reporters about their intentions. And as resignations began to mount and the story refused to wane, the CEOs on the policy forum began contemplating its future. "We believe the debate over forum participation has become a distraction from our well-intentioned and sincere desire to aid vital policy discussions on how to improve the lives of everyday Americans," read the forum's statement that was issued through private equity firm Blackstone, whose CEO Stephen Schwarzman chairs the group. "As such, the President and we are disbanding the forum." The chief executives' resignations may have business motives as well, said Bill Klepper, professor of management at Columbia Business School. "I think they’re finding the cost of alignment with Trump is too high," he said. "They have a social contract to stakeholders (that says) here’s what we stand for. These are our core values. Here’s how we’re going to contribute and win as a business in society. And we’re going to do it through ethical principles." "And lots of things Trump has been doing or not doing come close to violating the social contract," Klepper said. There have been questions about the councils' effectiveness from the start, and some critics dismissed them merely as photo opportunities for a president whose central campaign was about restoring jobs. In quitting his spot, AFL-CIO President Richard Trumka said the manufacturing council has "yet to hold any real meeting" since it was formed. "There are real questions into the effectiveness of this council to deliver real policy that lifts working families,” he said. Contributing: David Jackson. Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2waPdMa

query: What hairstyle technique was highlighted by hairstylist Amit Abraham in Bibhu Mohapatra's spring 2013 ready-to-wear show?

documents.text: At Bibhu Mohapatra's spring 2013 ready-to-wear show, hairstylist Amit Abraham worked with this season's beloved braids to create a look that was on-trend while entirely pushing forward the artform. Inspired by the luna moth, he coiled fishtails into a sleek bun supposed to mimic the insect's cocoon. "The unexpected shape of the rectangular bun commands attention,” he says. Here he gives us the step-by-step lowdown on how to easily master this elegant runway look on your own.Begin by creating a dramatic center part from your forehead to the nape of your neck.Spritz hair with shine spray. Abraham used L'Oréal Professionnel Mythic Oil Reinforcing Milk on the roots to create a super-flat graphic part. Then separate your hair into two ponytails.Working with each ponytail, create a tight fishtail braid using L'Oréal Professionnel Architexture Matte Defining Paste , which keeps braids smooth as you go.To create a fishtail braid, simply divide the ponytail into a left and right section. Then, beginning at the top, take a small section from the outside of the left and pass it over to the right. Repeat on the right side by passing a section from the outside right to the left. Continue alternating left to right sections until you have braided the entire length of the ponytail.Interlace the two ponytails into a horizontal rectangular shape, making sure to stay two inches above the nape of neck and to keep the shape no wider than your hairline. Place bobby pins strategically around the bun to hold it in place.To finish, spray Aussie Instant Freeze Hair Spray and Garnier Fructis Style Brilliantine Shine Glossing Spray throughout hair for strong hold and ample shine. Photo: Diane Bondareff/Invision for L'Oréal Professionnel/AP Images

WASHINGTON (AP) — The latest release of Hillary Clinton's private emails show her, as secretary of state, dealing with the complicated politics of the Arab Spring, fending off questions about her role in the deadly 2012 Benghazi attacks and attempting to navigate an intensifying conflict between Israel and the Palestinians. Democratic presidential candidate Hillary Clinton smiles as she walks off-stage after speaking at the Atlantic Council Women’s Leadership in Latin America Initiative in Washington, Monday, Nov. 30, 2015.... (Associated Press) But they also give a glimpse into the private side of one of the world's most public people. Clinton's notes show her searching for videos on how to do a "fishtail bun" hairstyle and struggling to locate Showtime on her television. (She wanted to watch the CIA-centered drama "Homeland.") She schedules — and reschedules — flights, meals and hairstyling appointments. And as she flies around the globe — logging 956,733 miles over her tenure — she tries to keep track of the time zone. The roughly 7,800 pages of emails released Monday were part of a court-ordered disclosure of correspondence sent from the private server Clinton used while she was secretary of state. Clinton, now the front-runner for the 2016 Democratic presidential nomination, has faced questions about whether her unusual email setup was sufficient to ensure the security of government information and retention of records. Included in the most recent batch was an email that Intelligence Community Inspector General I. Charles McCullough III and State Department Inspector General Steve Linick deemed classified in July. At least two Senate committees are still investigating Clinton's email arrangement and seeking the release of correspondence from her top aides. The FBI is also investigating the security of Clinton's private email setup. Two-thirds of Clinton's 30,000 work-related emails are now at least partially in the public eye — minus numerous redactions by the State Department. Here's a closer look at some of the messages that churned through Clinton's very busy in-box in the batch released Monday: __ HEALTH POLITICS Get-well-soon notes poured in from across the globe after a stomach virus that Clinton picked up in 2012 became a serious condition that kept her out of the office — and off the congressional witness stand. After a dehydrated Clinton fainted and sustained a concussion — a condition she frequently called her "cracked head" in messages — she was forced to postpone her testimony before the House Foreign Affairs Committee about the deadly Benghazi attacks. Hollywood mogul Harvey Weinstein emailed her aide, Capricia Marshall, offering to send Clinton copies of any movies she wanted to watch while recuperating. Dorothea Hurley, the wife of rock star Jon Bon Jovi, checked in with Clinton's daughter, Chelsea, to say that they were "sending you all a big hug." Chelsea forwarded the message to her mother. Her health quickly became a political issue, with Republicans questioning her fitness should she ever run for president. Her staff moved quickly to tamp down the speculation. Top aide Philippe Reines told Clinton he'd reached out to former Tennessee Sen. Bill Frist, a heart transplant surgeon, and the NFL commissioner to enlist their support pushing back on right-wing commentators. "Just not letting these comments stand, no matter who says them," Reines said. Frist was happy to help, according to the documents. "I love her and respect her and I can help. Not sure how exactly but I know I can help. I will Keep all Confidential," he wrote. ___ 2012 ELECTIONS Although Clinton wasn't on the ticket in 2012, she and her supporters followed the race closely — and had plenty to say. In a January 2012 note, during the heat of the Florida GOP primary, Clinton refers to Mitt Romney as "Mittens" and Newt Gingrich as "Grinch." "If Mittens can't beat Grinch in Florida, there will be pressure on state Republican parties to reopen or liberalize ballot access," she writes to confidant Sid Blumenthal. ___ BENGHAZI At least a few of the messages have come back to haunt Clinton during her presidential bid. Shortly before 9 p.m. on Sept. 11, 2012, Clinton sent an email asking her daughter to call her at her office about the attack on the U.S. consulate in Benghazi, Libya. The email was addressed to an account under the name "Diane Reynolds," an alias Chelsea Clinton used for personal messages. "Two of our officers were killed in Benghazi by an al-Qaida-like group: The Ambassador, whom I handpicked, and a young communications officer on temporary duty w(ith) a wife and two young children," Hillary Clinton later wrote to her daughter. "Very hard day and I fear more of the same tomorrow." In October, that email was trumpeted by Republicans on the House Benghazi committee as evidence that Clinton knew very quickly the attack on the consulate was the work of Islamic terrorists, not a spontaneous street protest triggered by the release of a video considered an insult to the Prophet Mohammed. In a later television interview, then-United Nations Ambassador Susan Rice suggested the video, which did spark anti-American protests that day in several Muslim countries, was the primary motivation for the attack. Republicans have suggested the Obama administration downplayed the terror threat to avoid stoking public safety concerns in the weeks leading up to the 2012 presidential election. Some of the messages show that Clinton worried about how her own remarks after the attacks would later be perceived. In a September 2012 message, aide Jake Sullivan compiled all her statements and assured Clinton that she was "careful" and "never said 'spontaneous' or characterized the motives." "The way you treated the video in the Libya context was to say that some sought to (asterisk)justify(asterisk) the attack on that basis," he wrote. ___ KEEPING THE TEAM TOGETHER Clinton maintained her ties with the Democratic Party donors who supported her past campaigns and could help her in the future. In November 2012, Samuel Kaplan, a Minneapolis lawyer and major Democratic donor who was appointed U.S. ambassador to Morocco, secured a private meeting with Clinton to discuss how he and his wife "might hope to be allied" with her in the future. Former Clinton aide Lauren Jiloty wrote to Clinton in 2012 to say she had met investor Warren Buffett, who asked her whom she had previously worked for. "When I told him it was YOU, he said, 'She's my heroine!'" "We all feel that way," Jiloty wrote. The billionaire investor will join Clinton on the campaign trail later this month. She also kept up with some of the political strategists who worked in her husband's administration and would go on to run her 2016 campaign, replying to birthday wishes from her future campaign chairman, John Podesta, and requests for career advice from campaign chief financial officer Gary Gensler. ___ KISSING UP Clinton's aides and supporters showered her with gushing praise, complimenting everything from her appearance to her policies, and the volume and emotion of those messages only grew as her tenure in the Cabinet came to an end. Aides forwarded Clinton congratulatory messages even as she was still on the stand testifying before Congress in January 2013. "I'm being flooded with emails about how you rocked," deputy chief of staff Huma Abedin wrote. "And you looked fabulous." One supporter wrote a message with the subject line: "twitterverse abuzz with Hillary-kvelling," using the Yiddish word for gushing praise. Later, after several congratulatory emails were exchanged among Clinton's staff, political consultant Mark Penn sent an email to Clinton gently suggesting that perhaps it wasn't wise to lose her temper in the hearing. Penn suggested Republicans could use that moment as evidence that they had rattled her. Aide Philippe Reines leaped to Clinton's defense, writing: "Give Me A Break You did not look rattled. You looked real. There's a difference. A big one." Top aide Jake Sullivan weighed in, saying Penn delivered the same advice during her losing presidential campaign in 2008 — that it's bad for a candidate to be herself. Clinton replies, "BINGO!" ___ Follow Lisa Lerer on Twitter at: http://twitter.com/llerer ___ Associated Press writers Matthew Lee, Catherine Lucey, Jack Gillum, Ted Bridis, Deb Riechmann, Matthew Lee, Stephen Braun, Wendy Benjaminson, Tami Abdollah, Michael Biesecker, Eileen Sullivan, Eric Tucker, Jim Drinkard, Ken Dilanian, Scott Bauer, Jeff Horwitz, Matthew Daly and Alicia A. Caldwell contributed to this report.

The latest release of Hillary Clinton's emails, a substantial collection of 78,000 pages covering 2012 and 2013, offers a detailed look into her tenure as Secretary of State. These documents, meticulously reviewed by her staff, address various challenges, including rebuttals to criticism of her Benghazi testimony, the personal impact of a concussion, and even a humorous inquiry about the television channel for the show "Homeland." The full archive is accessible for those who wish to conduct their own deep dive, but key highlights are presented here for a more immediate understanding.

During the holiday season in 2012, specifically on Christmas Eve, Clinton shared a lighthearted remark about her "cracked head" in an email exchange with senior adviser Philippe Reines. This communication reveals an amusing misunderstanding: Reines had been contacting figures like NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell and former Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist not for medical advice regarding her concussion, but to enlist their assistance in countering the proliferation of unqualified "armchair head trauma experts" dominating cable news discussions.

Following her initial testimony before Congress concerning the Benghazi attack, campaign adviser Mark Penn conveyed his opinion that Clinton's emphatic statement, "What difference, at this point, does it make?" might have been strategically ill-advised. Clinton, known for her transparency, forwarded this critique to her team. Their collective response, however, swiftly dismissed Penn's feedback as misguided, asserting that her overall performance had been impeccable.

The emails also shed light on the practicalities of modern communication and entertainment. One particular exchange, involving Sidney Blumenthal, showcases Clinton's keen political analysis as she offers her perspective on the Florida GOP primary, humorously referring to the candidates as "Mittens" and "Grinch." In a separate suggestion, Reines proposed that Clinton strategically visit numerous countries during the latter part of her term, aiming to "run up the score" in terms of international engagements compared to her predecessors, a notion that apparently resonated with nations like Albania, who felt her visit held significant meaning.

Further insights emerge regarding Clinton's personal routines and reliance on staff. While she reportedly needed assistance to identify her cable provider, suggesting a potential disconnect with everyday conveniences, there's an observation that she attempted to style her hair into an elaborate bun, seemingly without consulting her dedicated hair and makeup team. Although Clinton often directs individuals to her book, "Hard Choices," for comprehensive details about her time as Secretary of State, one instance reveals her attempt to summarize her significant achievements on a single page. Regrettably, this concise overview was entirely redacted, leaving its contents a mystery.

The latest batch of emails released by the State Department offers a candid glimpse into Hillary Clinton's tenure as Secretary of State, revealing her sharp wit, political acumen, and even her everyday tech struggles. While the controversy surrounding her private email server has largely subsided, these communications continue to showcase the multifaceted nature of this seasoned political figure. The nearly 8,000 pages of correspondence, primarily from 2012 and 2013, provide insight into Clinton's thinking during her final year in office. Her inbox reveals a range of topics, from colorful nicknames for political rivals to her challenges with television channels and even lighthearted jokes about a concussion.

Here are some notable exchanges from the released emails:

*   **"Mittens and the Grinch"**: Following Newt Gingrich's victory in the South Carolina GOP primary, longtime Clinton confidant Sid Blumenthal emailed Secretary Clinton. Instead of using their given names, Clinton referred to Republican candidates Mitt Romney and Gingrich as "Mittens" and "Grinch," respectively. She expressed concern about the political implications for the Republican party, noting, "If Mittens can't beat Grinch in Florida, there will be pressure on state Republican parties to reopen or liberalize ballot access especially in the caucuses, which as we know are creatures of the parties' extremes." She also added a personal note, "Tried calling but no answer, so hope you and Jackie are somewhere fun," referring to Blumenthal's wife.

*   **Senator Graham's Support**: In a January 2011 email, State Department official Michael Posner recounted a conversation with Senator Lindsey Graham. While flying back from Kabul, Graham reportedly "expressed strong support for Secretary Clinton" despite potential political repercussions in his home state of South Carolina. Posner wrote to top Clinton aide Jake Sullivan, "He expressed strong support for Secretary Clinton and said the main reason he wants to serve on Foreign Ops is to protect our budget requests against those who will advocate for severe cuts. I thanked him for his committment and encouraged him to pursue this committee assignment. He said this won't help him politically in South Carolina but these are the issues that matter most to him." Sullivan then forwarded this message to Clinton.

*   **"I am really devastated"**: Anne-Marie Slaughter, a former top policy aide, expressed distress in an October 2012 email chain regarding Clinton's reaction to her widely read Atlantic article, "Why Women Still Can't Have It All." Clinton had reportedly conveyed disapproval, suggesting that "some women are not comfortable working at the pace and intensity you have to work at in these jobs. ... Other women don't break a sweat." Slaughter, feeling misunderstood, wrote to Cheryl Mills and Huma Abedin, "Is she really talking about me? I have been 500 percent supportive and loyal in every possible way I can be? Can I at least talk to her? I AM advocating for women!!" with the subject line, "I am really devastated." Mills and Abedin attempted to reassure her, with Abedin writing, "There is a lot going on here and [senior adviser Philippe Reines] has been pushing back hard. I know cheryl is also calling you. Happy to talk." Slaughter later expressed hope for a direct conversation with Clinton, stating, "though still very much hope[d] [the secretary] and I can actually talk some of this through at some point! Not until she leaves office, of course. I'll write her over the weekend." Abedin forwarded the exchange to Clinton, noting, "[Reines] reported you will call her. She may email you as well."

*   **"your sparkling yellow pants suit"**: In December 2012, Lanny Davis, a longtime family friend, sent Clinton an email previewing his op-ed reflecting on her time at the State Department and as First Lady. He also conveyed well wishes following her concussion. Davis wrote, "I hope you enjoy the memories - especially mom remembering your sparkling yellow pants suit! I sent you my best wishes thru cdm when I heard about your fall. Happened to me once - faint and hard knock on marble floor. Scary. But your great husband and my great wife agree we are well protected - hard-headed. Take care of yourself - and look forward to a long rest as I wrote at the end."

*   **In search of "Homeland"**: On a Sunday afternoon in October 2012, Clinton emailed Philippe Reines with a "Stupid question," asking for the channel to watch the TV show "Homeland" on Showtime. Reines provided the channel numbers for Comcast and offered to look it up if she didn't have that service. Clinton responded, "You won't be surprised to hear I'm not sure." Reines then offered further assistance and followed up to see if she had managed to watch the episode.

*   **Helping a "cracked head"**: In the days after her concussion, Clinton congratulated Senator John Kerry on his nomination to succeed her. On December 21, 2012, she asked Reines for an email she had sent out. Reines responded with a statement about Kerry and added, "Hi! Miss you!" Clinton replied, "And I miss you too! Having a cracked head is no fun at all." Reines then offered to help with her recovery, mentioning he had contacted NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell and former Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist. Clinton clarified that she was medically fine but Reines explained his outreach was to counter public doubts about her health, stating, "Sorry, didn't mean medically. Wouldn't ever do that. I meant I enlisted their help in my ongoing efforts to undermine the John Boltons and Laura Ingraham's of the world who are belittling your health. I sent Ingraham a long note. Crafting one to Bolton now. Don't worry, no profanity," adding, "Not that kind of note. Just not letting these comments stand, no matter who says them."

*   **"Publicly and directly puncture conspiracy fever"**: Following CIA Director David Petraeus' resignation and the Benghazi attacks, Sid Blumenthal offered Clinton advice regarding her own brewing controversy, along with news clippings. He commented on the Petraeus scandal, referencing Shakespearean plots and the media's reaction. Blumenthal also recounted a discussion with British ambassador Peter Westmacott about Jill Kelley's role in the Petraeus affair. His advice to Clinton was: "Publicly and directly puncture conspiracy fever on Benghazi before any closed hearing, in response to press question that you make yourself available to."

*   **Not POLITICO?**: After her Benghazi testimony, Blumenthal shared a blog post from Feministing titled "How to deal with a mansplainer starring Hillary Clinton in GIFs," which he considered the "best analysis so far of [the] hearing." Clinton enjoyed the post, replying, "Loved them!" Blumenthal also commented on his wife Jackie's glasses, finding them "very stylish, attractive."

*   **Give ... Me .... A .... Break**: Mark Penn, a former Clinton campaign adviser, sent Clinton an email that she forwarded to Reines, Sullivan, and Mills. Penn critiqued the emotional impact of her testimony, suggesting it "looks more like they rattled you on something no one outside the crazy right blamed you for anyway." Clinton acknowledged this as a "first discordant note--just to keep it real." Reines strongly disagreed with Penn's assessment, writing, "I say this as someone who has never had a problem with Mark or held him as accountable as other people do - but... Give Me A Break," emphasizing each word. He asserted, "You did not look rattled. You looked real. There's a difference. A big one."

*   **Daughters**: In an August 2012 email chain involving Clinton and her daughter, Chelsea (using the pseudonym Diane Reynolds), Reines presented an analysis suggesting Mitt Romney's candidacy was likely to fail because he had no daughters. He noted that most presidents, with the exception of Dwight D. Eisenhower, had daughters. Chelsea, under her code name "Energy," responded, "That's fascinating. What did you then conclude?!" Reines acknowledged the limited sample size but found it interesting, adding that Romney's 20-point deficit among women made sense in light of this pattern. He proposed further research into past presidential nominees who lacked daughters.

*   **Paging Atticus Finch**: Lanny Davis sent Clinton the results of a May 2012 Gallup survey showing her "near record-high favorability numbers." He offered to write about the reasons for this in his column, promising not to embarrass her. Clinton responded, "I'm afraid anything you write will be suspect in light of our long relationship!" Davis, using a frown emoticon, stated he wouldn't write it but if he could, he would highlight her qualities: "is a hard worker," "is fact-driven," and "is sensitive to the perspective of others ... as was said about Atticus Finch in 'To Kill A Mockingbird': 'she can walk in other people's shoes and see the world through their eyes.'" He concluded by calling her "a gracious person - the most important quality for a successful diplomat." Clinton then requested the email be printed.

*   **Counting trips and asterisks**: A June 2012 email from Reines to Clinton and Abedin detailed the Secretary's travel statistics. With seven months remaining in her term, Reines set a goal of visiting 110 countries and listed the 94 remaining countries, marking those previously visited with asterisks.

*   **Stargazing**: In December 2012, Clinton emailed former U.S. Ambassador

query: What was the main argument presented in the Xinhua op-ed regarding Tsai Ing-wen's suitability as Taiwan's president?

documents.text:
**Xinhua Op-Ed Links Taiwan President Tsai Ing-wen's Unmarried Status to Political Incompetence**

An op-ed published in China's official state newspaper, Xinhua, has drawn widespread criticism for suggesting that Taiwan's newly inaugurated president, Tsai Ing-wen, is unfit for her job due to her unmarried and childless status. The article, published Tuesday, linked Tsai's personal life to her political approach and policies.

According to the Xinhua piece, "As a single female politician, she has no emotional encumbrances of love, no family restraint, no children to worry about. Her political style and tactics are often emotional, personalized, and extreme." The analysis further claimed that this resulted in a short-term, detail-oriented approach to governance, with Tsai prioritizing immediate goals over long-term strategies.

The original Xinhua story was subsequently removed, as were copies posted on other Chinese news portals. However, the piece ignited a significant backlash online. Critics pointed to successful, single Asian female politicians such as South Korean President Park Geun-hye and former Chinese Vice Premier Wu Yi as counterexamples.

**Key Criticisms and Reactions:**

*   **Professor Li Yunlong** of the Central Party School of the Communist Party of China expressed outrage on Weibo, China's microblogging platform, questioning the relevance of marital status to political views and labeling the commentary as "naked discrimination against singleton." He also contrasted Tsai's situation with that of former Taiwan President Chen Shui-bian, who is married but, in Li's view, exhibited more extreme political behavior.
*   Many users accused state media of sexism and exhibiting "Straight Man Cancer," a popular Chinese term for chauvinistic and judgmental attitudes towards women.

**Broader Context of Gender Discrimination in China:**

The incident highlights a growing concern about gender discrimination in China, despite the historical narrative under Chairman Mao Zedong that women "held up half the sky." Recent reports suggest a decline in women's representation and a continued focus on traditional gender roles by state-run media.

*   In 2015, **The New York Times** reported that women held fewer than 10% of board positions at China's top 300 companies.
*   China's state-run media has been criticized for prioritizing women's appearances and marital status over equal rights. For instance, **The People's Daily** published a gallery titled "Beauty with brains" featuring female journalists, and a 2015 Lunar New Year gala included skits mocking unmarried women.
*   Feminists have actively campaigned against such portrayals, with an online petition calling for an end to the televised extravaganza.
*   Earlier in 2015, influential writer **Zhou Guoping** sparked controversy by stating that "a man can have thousands of ambitions but a woman only one" – to give birth.

**Guo Weiqing**, a professor at Sun Yat-sen University in Guangzhou, told CNN that societal expectations of traditional femininity are a source of anxiety for some men, leading to a desire for women to revert to perceived past roles. However, he noted that many Chinese internet users strongly disagree with this sentiment and view leaders like Tsai Ing-wen as empowering role models.

The Taiwanese government had not immediately responded to CNN's request for comment.

Image copyright EPA Image caption Taiwan's new leader has been outspoken in declaring that she has never been married An article on Chinese state media that said Taiwan's new leader Tsai Ing-wen has an "extremist style" because she is unmarried has prompted outrage online. The opinion piece, published by the Xinhua news agency, said Ms Tsai did not have the "emotional burden" of a family, leading to an "erratic style". Chinese social media users largely rallied in support of Ms Tsai, who has been outspoken about her single status. She was sworn in on Friday as Taiwan's first female president Tsai Ing-wen, Taiwan's shy but steely leader Taiwan: the place to be a woman in politics Her Democratic Progressive Party (DPP), which leans towards independence from China, won January's elections, ending eight years under pro-Beijing President Ma Ying-jeou. 'More emotional' Ms Tsai's singleness was a central focus of a commentary by Wang Weixing, originally published by Xinhua. Image copyright AFP Image caption Ms Tsai took the presidential oath at a ceremony in Taipei on Friday The article has since been removed from Xinhua's website, but can still be found on other news portals and micro-blogging accounts. Mr Wang, a senior military officer, pointed to an "extremist political-style development" displayed by Ms Tsai. "As a single female politician, Tsai Ing-wen does not have the emotional burden of love, of 'family' or children so her political style and strategies are displayed to be more emotional, personal and extreme," the report in Chinese read. It continued that her "erratic behaviour" influenced her political style and advised that she should take that into consideration, rather than "focus on long-term goals" for Taiwan. Alex Huang, a spokesman for Ms Tsai, told BBC News that her office had "no comments" in response to the piece. 'Typical discriminatory behaviour' But thousands of Chinese-language social media users have expressed outrage and anger towards the article, which has been reposted by several accounts on China's popular micro-blogging site Sina Weibo. Image copyright Facebook Image caption The president once said on Facebook she was "happiest" playing with her cats "This was the stupidest and most offensive thing I have read in ages," said a post from a Weibo user from the Chinese capital, Beijing. "Many women abroad admire Ms Tsai's tenacity and drive, especially the fact she is strong and independent and does not need a man to rule." "If the tables were turned and Ms Tsai was a man, she would be celebrated for being single. This is typical discriminatory behaviour but it still disgusts me," said another user on the site. Su Mei Cho, a teacher from Shanghai, left a comment in response to a post that was being circulated on the site: "If Xinhua wanted to criticise Tsai Ing-wen, do it on fair grounds and look at her ability to lead Taiwan and revitalise areas like defence and the economy. "What does her private life have to do with the way she governs Taiwan? Taiwanese celebrate her and her strong achievements and I doubt they will care as much about who she decides to date, such trivial writing and opinions expressed in this report." Over in Taiwan, netizens on the popular PTT forum were discussing the "poor taste" of opinions expressed towards their leader. "Even a woman's singlehood can make the news?" "They are probably threatened that a cat lady like Ms Tsai could beat any men she sets her mind to - they are intimidated," said another. But not everyone disagreed with the article. A user from China's Yunnan province said it was not an attack, and the article was based on facts. He questioned whether it would also be an attack for a psychologist to point out a patient's personality faults. Another user from Henan province, central China said the article was all based on public information, and said it was right to use public information to study a politician. Reporting by the BBC's Grace Tsoi, Heather Chen and Yashan Zhao.

President Tsai Ing-wen is “two-faced” and her single status means that she may be “extreme” politically, a Chinese state media column published on Tuesday. The author, Wang Weixing, is the director of the Association for Relations Across the Taiwan Straits and a scholar at the Academy of Military Science. He said that “[Tsai] is a complicated person who grew up in the twisted Taiwanese society and political environment.” “Analysing from a personality point of view, as a single female politician, she does not have the burden of feelings of love, there is no ‘family’ as a constraint, no kids as worries – her political style and executive strategy tends to be emotional and extreme,” he said. The director also said: “Ultimately, Tsai Ing-wen’s character is clearly two-faced – politically, her management style is one face, and privately she shows another face. It is very deceiving.” The article on Xinhua, originally titled “Exposing Tsai Ing-wen,” has been deleted. However, the article remains available on other Chinese media outlets that have reposted the article under the title “Xinhua: Being single may cause Tsai Ing-wen’s political style to lean towards extremism.” ‘Positive dialogue’ Tsai was elected as voters rejected closer ties with China. Beijing warned Tsai on Saturday that it would cut off critical contacts if she did not state her support for “one China,” which encompasses the idea that the mainland and Taiwan are parts of one sovereign state. In 2015, The New York Times reported that women make up fewer than one in 10 board members at the country's top 300 companies. And China's state-run media often appears more concerned with women's looks and marital status than equal rights. The People's Daily, the Communist Party's official newspaper, published an online gallery last year entitled "Beauty with brains." It featured 18 snapshots of female journalists covering the NPC in Beijing. And during the Lunar New Year holidays in 2015, a television gala watched by 690 million people included comedy skits mocking overweight and unmarried women. "They hope women will return to the way they're supposed to be," he said. But judging from many Internet users' comments, many in China would disagree with that opinion and that strong female leaders like Tsai are role models for how women really should be. A recent opinion piece published by Xinhua, the official news agency of the Chinese Communist Party, has sparked widespread criticism for its assertion that Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen is "extreme" due to her marital status. The article, appearing in Xinhua's International Herald Leader, suggested that Tsai's lack of a husband and children contributes to an "emotional, personalised" and potentially radical political style.

Tsai Ing-wen, an alumna of LSE and Cornell University, recently took office as the president of the self-ruled island. In her inaugural address, she called for an end to prejudice and a focus on the future. Meanwhile, across the Taiwan Strait, the Xinhua-affiliated publication presented a contrasting view.

The opinion piece quoted Wang Weixing, an official from a Beijing-controlled body focused on Taiwan relations, who stated, "As a single female politician, she does not have the emotional burden of love, of ‘family’ or children. So her political style and strategy tend to be emotional, personalised and extreme." Wang further advised that when dealing with Tsai, "we should bear in mind important factors such as her experience, personality and mindset."

This commentary drew immediate backlash online. Alex Huang, a spokesperson for President Tsai, declined to comment on what he termed Xinhua's "chauvinism." However, users on Weibo, China's microblogging platform, expressed outrage. One user exclaimed, "How dare you attack her personal life!" while another labeled the author a "Male chauvinist pig."

Zhang Hongzhong, a journalism professor at Beijing Normal University, criticized the article as intellectually flawed and offensive to single women. He also warned that such content from a state-run agency could damage China's international image. Following the outcry, the article was removed from the International Herald Leader's website, replaced by a "page not found" message.

The Xinhua piece appears to contradict President Xi Jinping's stated commitment to gender equality. At a United Nations summit last September, Xi emphasized the importance of empowering women in society and the economy, and supporting their leadership roles. However, reports from organizations like Amnesty International suggest that feminist activism in China has faced increased suppression since Xi assumed power in 2012. Notably, celebrations for the 20th anniversary of the 1995 UN Fourth Conference on Women in Beijing were reportedly banned last year. The underrepresentation of women in top leadership positions within China's political structure, with no women on the Communist Party's elite seven-member Politburo Standing Committee, further highlights this disparity.

The public's perception of political figures is shaped by both their professional achievements and personal presentation. While those close to a politician might see a warm individual, the wider public often struggles to look beyond their career, especially when a public figure consistently emphasizes only professional accomplishments and policy initiatives.

Consider a campaign video titled "Fighter." It might use charming archival footage to highlight past advocacy. However, when the video shifts to a contemporary interview, the setting, lighting, and even the politician's clothing are carefully chosen. This polished presentation, while projecting control and professionalism, can create distance. It makes the individual seem less like a relatable person and more like a manufactured brand. This can lead to a public perception of being a "workaholic." As theologian Martyn Lloyd-Jones noted, this state can lead to emotional self-estrangement. When individuals are consumed by work, their personal feelings may not guide their decisions, and their professional role can overshadow their personality. This diminishes the natural intimacies and emotional depth that define a person. Lloyd-Jones famously summarized such a life with the epitaph: "Born a man, died a doctor."

This exclusively professional demeanor in the public arena—marked by hard work, calculation, and goal-orientation—hinders personal connection. Observers see a role, not a fully formed person. This career-centric persona clashes with the social media age's emphasis on intimacy, personal revelation, and trust. It also contrasts with most people's lived experiences, where life outside of work often feels more vibrant. Consequently, such a presentation can foster perceptions of being Machiavellian, crafty, power-hungry, and untrustworthy.

This offers a broader lesson for those in fulfilling careers or vocations:

*   **Identity Consumption:** Even a socially beneficial profession can consume one's identity, leading to a loss of personal voice.
*   **Cultivating Sanctuaries:** It's crucial for those with deeply engaging vocations to actively cultivate and visibly demonstrate personal sanctuaries outside their professional lives.

These sanctuaries can include:

*   Play
*   Solitude
*   Family
*   Faith
*   Hobbies
*   Leisure

As Abraham Joshua Heschel described the Sabbath as "a palace in time which we build," it's more than just rest; it's an elevation enabled by work. Similarly, Josef Pieper argued that leisure is an attitude, a deliberate pause from effort to cultivate stillness for contemplation and appreciation of life. Even highly successful individuals need these personal sanctuaries to remain fully realized humans, not just productive units. Ultimately, the public may be hesitant to fully trust candidates who don't reveal these essential, non-professional aspects of their lives.

The recent buzz surrounding Kim Kardashian and Kanye West's potential romance has ignited considerable speculation. According to reports from TMZ, the two were photographed together on a Wednesday evening following a screening of "The Hunger Games," further fueling rumors of their dating status. Coincidentally, on the very same day, Kanye released a new track titled "Theraflu," which features the candid lyric, "I admit I fell in love with Kim."

While whispers of a romantic connection between Kardashian and West have circulated for years, this latest development suggests a more concrete possibility. Alternatively, it could be a masterclass in strategic marketing. Both individuals are known for their savvy understanding of public relations and may be leveraging this moment for maximum media exposure and brand enhancement. The timing of their public appearance and West's lyrical confession raises questions about the authenticity of their relationship, appearing less like a chance encounter and more like a carefully orchestrated product launch.

The lyrics from "Theraflu" further deepen this intrigue: "And I admit I fell in love with Kim / Around the same time she fell in love with him / Well that's cool baby girl, do your thing / Lucky I ain't had Jay drop him from the team." This reference, presumably to Jay-Z, a part-owner of the New Jersey Nets for which Kris Humphries played, adds another layer to the narrative.

Regardless of the current state of their romantic feelings, both Kardashian and West stand to benefit significantly from their association. Kanye gains amplified exposure and fame, while Kim potentially distances herself from the public scrutiny of her recent marriage. Their respective luxury lifestyle brands are complementary, potentially boosting sales across music, fashion, sponsorships, and reality television ventures.

Furthermore, Kanye's ambitious venture, DONDA, described by Kanye himself as an entity "comprised of over 22 divisions with a goal to make products and experiences that people want and can afford, created by architects, graphic designers, directors, musicians, producers, AnRs, writers, publicists, social media experts, app guys, managers, car designers, clothing designers, DJs, video game designers, publishers, tech guys, lawyers, bankers, nutritionists, doctors, scientist, and teachers," presents a unique platform. While the full scope of DONDA remains somewhat enigmatic, Kim Kardashian's renowned ability to connect with a broad audience could prove invaluable in promoting its diverse offerings.

The unfolding narrative of this rumored couple will undoubtedly be a focal point in media coverage. It's plausible that they have redefined the concept of "friends with benefits." However, the possibility of a more serious romantic development, perhaps even wedding bells, cannot be entirely dismissed. The media landscape will be watching closely to see how this story develops.

query: What is President Trump's main argument regarding the changes to Medicaid in the GOP healthcare bill?

documents.text: President Donald Trump took exception Wednesday with a Democratic argument regarding Medicaid funding in the new GOP healthcare bill. "Democrats purposely misstated Medicaid under new Senate bill - actually goes up," Trump tweeted with a chart. Twitter/Donald Trump One of the biggest criticisms of the Senate healthcare bill, the Better Care Reconciliation Act (BCRA), has been the projected cuts to future Medicaid spending under the legislation, which the Congressional Budget Office said would lead to 22 million fewer Americans being insured by 2026. Trump's tweet echoes an argument from Republicans that says the legislation doesn't cut Medicaid spending. But critics of the legislation note that the projected increase in funding from the federal government will be lower than the current projected rate of increase. This could have serious consequences for Medicaid recipients and state budgets. Currently, the federal government provides states with a percentage of their Medicaid funding based on a formula of how much a state actually spends. Under the BCRA, states would receive a set amount of money based on the number of people on Medicaid in that state. In other words, federal funding would grow in raw terms as the US population grows and the total number of people on Medicaid increases, but the amount per person would not be as generous as the current system. The chart below shows the difference: Andy Kiersz/Business Insider, Congressional Budget Office Also, the BCRA would end the Medicaid expansion program under the Affordable Care Act, the law better known as Obamacare, which would represent another significant decrease from the current path of funding. The Congressional Budget Office projected that federal funding for Medicaid would decrease by $772 billion over the next 10 years compared to the current system. According to analyses from The Brookings Institution, The Kaiser Family Foundation, and the CBO, the formula for the Medicaid growth rate under the BCRA would lead to increased financial stress on states and detrimental outcomes for Medicaid recipients.

Add this Tweet to your website by copying the code below. مزید جانیئے Add this video to your website by copying the code below. مزید جانیئے ہہم، سرور تک پہنچنے میں ایک مسئلہ تھا۔ دوبارہ کوشش کریں؟ بنیادی ٹویٹ شامل کریں میڈیا شامل کریں ٹوئٹر کا مواد اپنی ویب سائٹ یا ایپلی کیشن میں ایمبیڈ کر کے، آپ ٹوئٹر کے ڈیولپر اقرارنامہ اور ڈیولپر پالیسی سے اتفاق کر رہے ہیں۔ پیش منظر

## Washington's Medicaid Spending: A $71 Billion Rise, But Is It a Cut?

The core of Washington's current healthcare debate revolves around a projected $71 billion increase in federal Medicaid spending over the next decade. This substantial sum, however, is being framed as a cut by critics when contrasted with existing law and prior forecasts. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) projects federal Medicaid outlays to climb from roughly $393 billion in 2017 to $464 billion by 2026. This anticipated $71 billion, or approximately 18 percent, rise is viewed as a reduction by opponents. This perspective stems from the CBO's forecast that, without legislative changes, Medicaid spending would reach $624 billion in 2026. Therefore, the Senate's health care bill would lead to an estimated $772 billion less in total Medicaid spending over the coming decade compared to a scenario where no legislative action is taken.

This significant difference lies at the heart of the political contention. As former White House press secretary Ari Fleischer articulated, an increase in spending, even if it falls short of earlier projections, does not equate to a cut. He pointed to federal Medicaid spending of $129 billion in 2001, emphasizing that "spending only goes up" in Washington. Fleischer argued that the debate centers on the *degree* of the spending increase, not on whether it will be reduced. He also noted that the media was overlooking the $71 billion increase, drawing an analogy to a worker receiving a raise that is less than initially promised.

Critics, including Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi, highlight the $772 billion figure, representing the disparity between the bill's projected spending and current law. They also reference the CBO's projection of millions fewer individuals enrolled in Medicaid and related programs. The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) echoed these concerns, stating the bill would "enact severe cuts to Medicaid."

Despite these criticisms, Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell's office maintains that the legislation will strengthen Medicaid. They assert it will:

*   Ensure continued access for individuals with pre-existing conditions.
*   Permit young adults to remain covered under their parents' insurance plans.

Senator Pat Toomey, R-Pa., also stated that Medicaid spending will "grow every year" and "never be cut." He clarified that while spending will increase, it will do so at a "slightly slower rate."

Brooke Singman, a Politics Reporter for Fox News, contributed to this report.

Under the House health care bill, Medicaid spending "actually goes up 20 percent over the next 10 years." Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich said all the media coverage that declares the Republican health care bills would cut Medicaid is wrong, because he claimed the program actually gets a significant boost over the years. Reports about both the House and Senate bills have pointed out the measures reduce funding for Medicaid, the joint state and federal health insurance program for the very poor. Using the House bill as an example, Gingrich said it’s really the opposite. "After all the news media talking about cutting Medicaid in the House Republican bill, I did some research," Gingrich said June 27, 2017, on Fox & Friends. "It actually goes up 20 percent over the next 10 years." Gingrich said Republicans had a "communications problem" for not pointing this growth out when discussing the bill, resulting in coverage focused on deep Medicaid cuts. Those news reports mostly focus on analyses by the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office. The bill before the Senate would reduce Medicaid spending by $772 billion over 10 years by 2026, the CBO said. A similar analysis for the bill that passed the House in May would winnow Medicaid spending by $834 billion in the same time frame. So where is Gingrich getting his 20 percent increase in Medicaid spending from? We reached out to his office and didn’t hear back. But it appears he’s talking about the rate at which Medicaid will grow over the next decade — something the program’s slated to do whether Republicans pass a health care law or not. The GOP proposals, however, put major limits on future funding for that growth. Medicaid costs keep going up Medicaid originally was a program for low-income children, pregnant women, elderly and disabled individuals, and some parents, but it excluded other low-income adults. As part of its efforts to provide health coverage options for as many people as possible, the Affordable Care Act allowed states to expand Medicaid and help pay for it with more federal dollars. Thirty-one states plus Washington, D.C., currently have extended Medicaid benefits to essentially all adults making up to 138 percent of the federal poverty level. The poverty line for a family of four is $24,600 in 2017. That coverage is expensive, and it’s only going to cost more as the years go on. For calculating how the GOP bills may affect Medicaid spending, the CBO used baseline figures from March 2016 to draw estimates for how much the program would cost. "There are two reasons Medicaid costs go up: More people are being served and the cost of serving them is going up," said Joan Alker, Georgetown University public policy professor and executive director of the Center for Children and Families. Costs could rise due to increasing drug prices, inflation, an aging population with more older Americans, or any number of other considerations. "CBO's baseline projects what Medicaid will need to account for these factors," Alker said. From 2017 through 2026, the federal government would spend more than $5 trillion on Medicaid under current law, the CBO projected. Another way to measure that growth is to calculate the percent change from current spending levels to that estimated 2026 level. If the Affordable Care Act stayed in place and nothing changed, the CBO said Medicaid spending would increase from $393 billion in 2017 to $624 billion in 2026. That’s a 58.8 percent increase. Now come the semantics: The House and Senate bills both slow the rate of that increase in spending, although they do so in different ways and on different schedules. But they both assume major reductions in how high the dollar amount for funding increases. Opponents of the GOP bills call that a cut. The CBO calls that "reductions in outlays." Gingrich is saying it’s an increase. How? How the GOP bills slow Medicaid spending It looks like Gingrich focused on the projection for how the House bill would affect future funding. Starting with the $393 billion in spending in 2017, Medicaid spending would go up steadily each year to reach $474 billion in 2026, under the House bill. That’s a 20.6 percent increase. The Senate’s plan would increase funding to $466 billion in 2026, or about 18.5 percent higher than 2017. The projections assume what would happen if the bills took effect, with reductions starting in 2018. The CBO said that under the House’s bill, 14 million fewer people would be enrolled in Medicaid by 2026, relative to current law. The Senate bill would see 15 million fewer enrollees. Keep in mind that Medicaid spending goes up under any scenario. It’s just at a far lower rate under the Republican health care bills. Policy analysts told us it makes reductions to Medicaid with no allowance for how the program may need to grow in the future. "If he's claiming that there's no cut in the (House bill) because Medicaid spending in 2026 would be higher than it is now, that's largely irrelevant," Ben Sommers, a Harvard University health policy and economics professor, told PolitiFact. "Given that this dramatically reduces spending to what would occur under current law, most people would call this a ‘cut.’ " Such reductions also put new limits on how many people have access to Medicaid. The changes all but guarantee states will have to alter eligibility requirements and take away benefits — without regard to whether people actually need them. "The bills restrain the rate of increase well below Medicaid’s actual rate of increase," said Sara Rosenbaum, a George Washington University health law and policy professor. "Of course it’s a cut. If federal growth is kept artificially low, the only choice is to spend more to make up the deficit." Our ruling Gingrich said under the House health care bill, Medicaid spending "actually goes up 20 percent over the next 10 years." By any CBO projection and under any proposed legislation, Medicaid spending will increase over the next decade — because health care costs are increasing. The House health care bill limits that increase to 20 percent, while maintaing the status quo requires a 60 percent increase, according to the CBO. Both the bills in the House and the Senate limit the growth of spending. The statement is partially accurate but leaves out important details. We rate it Half True.

## Examining the Senate GOP Health Care Bill's Impact on Medicaid

The proposed Senate Republican health care bill has ignited a vigorous debate, particularly concerning its potential ramifications for the Medicaid program. While prominent figures within the administration have offered assurances, a deeper dive into the legislation's specifics, as analyzed by independent bodies, reveals a more complex picture.

### Administration Assurances on Medicaid Coverage

High-ranking officials have publicly stated that the Senate's health care proposal would not result in cuts to Medicaid or lead to recipients losing their coverage. Counselor to the President, Kellyanne Conway, asserted this week that the legislation does not include such provisions. Similarly, Health and Human Services Secretary Tom Price emphasized that the government "would not allow individuals to fall through the cracks" and "would not pull the rug out from under anybody," underscoring a commitment to maintaining existing coverage. These statements align with President Donald Trump's campaign pledge not to alter Medicaid, the federal program crucial for assisting lower-income Americans, including expectant mothers, children, seniors, and individuals with disabilities.

### A Closer Look at Projected Medicaid Spending

However, an examination of the Senate Republican health care bill, particularly through the lens of the Congressional Budget Office (CBO), presents a more nuanced perspective on Medicaid funding and coverage trajectories. While the bill does project an increase in government spending on Medicaid over time, this projected annual spending is anticipated to be lower than what the Affordable Care Act (ACA), often referred to as Obamacare, currently allocates.

According to a comprehensive review by the Congressional Budget Office:

*   **Under the Affordable Care Act (ACA):** The federal government is estimated to spend approximately $415 billion on Medicaid in the upcoming year. This figure is projected to rise to $624 billion by 2026.
*   **Under the Senate Bill (if enacted in 2018):** The estimated federal spending for Medicaid in the first fiscal year is $403 billion, with an increase to $466 billion by 2026.

The CBO further estimated that federal spending on Medicaid from the present until 2026 would be $772 billion less than what is currently projected under existing law. While the Senate bill would indeed lead to more government spending on Medicaid over this period compared to the House GOP bill, the overall federal revenue collected and the subsequent spending on the program would likely be lower than under current law. This reduction in federal investment is a key factor contributing to projections of a greater number of uninsured individuals.

### Projected Reductions in Medicaid Enrollment

The Congressional Budget Office projects that the Senate bill could result in 15 million fewer Medicaid enrollees over the next decade. This anticipated decline is expected to stem from two primary groups: individuals who are currently eligible for Medicaid and those who would become eligible in the future through the Medicaid expansion provisions of the ACA. Specifically, the CBO estimates that nearly 40 percent of adults aged 30-49, earning just above the national poverty line (approximately $24,000 for an individual), might find themselves uninsured by 2026. This scenario is largely attributed to the proposed rollback of Medicaid expansion.

### State Funding Mechanisms and Potential Consequences

States that opted into Medicaid expansion under the ACA by March 2017 would experience a reduction in federal government funding beginning in 2021. The bill proposes phasing out funding for this expansion at a lower rate by 2024. Should the bill become law, states would be presented with the option to receive federal funds through either a per capita cap, which would be determined by enrollment numbers, or a block grant. These alternative funding structures have raised concerns about the long-term stability and adequacy of support for state Medicaid programs.

### Criticisms and Political Opposition

The potential impact of the Senate bill has not gone unnoticed and has drawn significant criticism. The anticipated vote on the legislation was delayed by Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell until after the Fourth of July recess, signaling ongoing deliberations and potential challenges. Senator Shelley Moore Capito, a Republican representing West Virginia, has publicly voiced her opposition to the bill. She has argued that the legislation would negatively affect individuals who rely on Medicaid, particularly those seeking treatment for opioid addiction. Senator Capito expressed profound concern about the bill's implications for her state, which has a substantial Medicaid population and is grappling with a growing opioid crisis. She underscored the critical importance of preserving access to the preventative care and treatment services that Medicaid currently facilitates for her constituents.

Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President, stated that "there are not cuts to Medicaid" in the GOP health care proposals. This assertion has ignited a significant debate, highlighting a central point of contention in discussions about future government spending. During an interview on ABC's "This Week," Conway addressed host George Stephanopoulos's question regarding the Senate Republican health care bill's projected $800 billion in Medicaid savings. Stephanopoulos suggested these savings contradicted a presidential campaign promise to shield Medicaid from cuts. Conway clarified, "This slows the rate for the future."

This statement delves into a long-standing argument about how to define a "cut." While future savings don't always mean immediate reductions in current spending, the specific changes proposed in the GOP health care bill demand closer scrutiny.

To evaluate Conway's claim, it's vital to examine the projected financial trajectories. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) estimated that under current law, federal Medicaid spending from 2018 through 2026 would total approximately $4.62 trillion. The Senate bill, however, projects Medicaid spending to be about $770 billion less over the same period.

Looking at year-over-year spending offers further context. In 2018, the first year the bill would take effect, federal spending is projected to be around $403 billion under the Senate bill, compared to an estimated $415 billion under current law. By 2026, spending under the Senate bill is projected to reach approximately $460 billion, versus $624 billion if current law remains unchanged. Although spending increases in both scenarios, the rate of increase is notably slower under the Senate proposal. The CBO has provided visual aids illustrating these differing spending paths.

The Senate bill introduces several measures aimed at curbing Medicaid spending. A significant change concerns eligibility. Before the Affordable Care Act (ACA), Medicaid primarily covered low-income children, pregnant women, the elderly, and disabled individuals, along with some parents. However, it excluded many other low-income adults. The ACA enabled states to expand Medicaid eligibility to individuals earning up to 133% of the federal poverty level. For a family of three, this threshold was roughly $27,000 annually. The Senate bill proposes to end this expansion option starting in January 2018. Analyses suggest this would impact over 2.5 million people in states that had adopted the expansion.

Furthermore, the bill modifies federal payments to states that expanded Medicaid. Under the ACA, Washington typically covers at least 90% of the costs for individuals in the expansion group. This is a more favorable rate than the standard federal medical assistance percentage (FMAP), which ranges from 50% to about 70%. The Senate bill intends to phase out this enhanced rate for expansion states. The federal match would revert to the regular rate by 2024.

A more fundamental shift involves the program's structure. The bill proposes moving away from Medicaid's current open-ended commitment to cover a certain percentage of a state's costs. Instead, it would transition to a capped funding system. This cap could be applied on a per-person basis, or states could choose a block grant approach. While these caps would be adjusted annually, they would likely increase at a slower pace than current inflation adjustments.

The specific nature of these policy changes, rather than just the total dollar figures, is crucial. A parallel can be drawn to the debate surrounding the Affordable Care Act in 2010. At that time, Republicans, including then-House Speaker Paul Ryan, criticized the ACA for what they called cuts to Medicare, despite projections of increased overall spending. Democrats countered that spending was indeed rising. Fact-checks conducted by PolitiFact at the time generally rated these Republican criticisms as "Half True."

The current situation presents a mirror image: Democrats are now proposing reductions in payments to states, while Republicans are framing it as a slowdown in spending growth. However, a key distinction exists. While the ACA did not alter the eligible population for Medicare, the Senate Republican bill does change who is eligible for Medicaid. This reduction in the eligible population constitutes a cut in access.

Conway herself acknowledged the Republican objective of returning Medicaid to its "original moorings." She emphasized its founding purpose of assisting the poor, sick, needy, disabled children, and certain elderly and pregnant women.

In conclusion, while the Republican health care proposals would indeed slow the rate of Medicaid spending growth, leading to projected savings rather than immediate reductions, they also include significant policy changes that will reduce the number of people eligible for the program. This reduction in access is a critical factor that leads to the conclusion that these proposals represent a cut to Medicaid. Therefore, Conway's claim, while containing an element of truth regarding spending rates, omits crucial information that alters the overall impression, leading to a rating of Mostly False.

**Medicaid Under the GOP Healthcare Plan: A Deep Dive into Disputed Projections**

President Trump has publicly accused Democrats of misrepresenting the projected impact of the Republican healthcare plan on Medicaid, asserting that the program's funding would actually increase under the proposed legislation. This assertion comes amidst significant internal Republican struggles to garner sufficient votes for their Obamacare replacement plan, with even fellow party members expressing reservations about the bill. As Senate leadership grapples with finding a path forward for the controversial healthcare legislation, President Trump took to Twitter to defend the proposal, stating, "Democrats purposely misstated Medicaid under new Senate bill - actually goes up."

While it is true that the overall Medicaid budget is projected to continue rising under the GOP plan, the magnitude of this projected increase is a key point of contention. Independent analysis from the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) estimates that the Senate Republican plan would result in a reduction of $772 billion in projected spending compared to current law, spanning the years 2018 through 2026. These projections meticulously account for a range of factors, including the policy changes introduced by the bill, anticipated population growth and aging demographics, and the projected rate of increase in medical costs.

Democrats, however, argue that a more accurate comparison involves evaluating the proposed plan against existing legislation. From this perspective, they contend that the reductions in planned spending constitute significant cuts. Furthermore, the fundamental structure of Medicaid, which relies on joint funding from federal and state governments, is directly impacted. The Senate bill, as currently drafted, proposes to decrease the federal government's level of financial support for Medicaid. Healthcare experts have voiced concerns that these federal reductions may be too substantial for states to adequately compensate for, potentially leading to a diminished program.

The debate over Medicaid funding is further complicated by the potential for states to alter eligibility requirements under the Republican plan. Democrats warn that if states choose to exercise this newfound flexibility, millions of individuals could lose their Medicaid coverage. This concern is echoed by fact-checking organizations. For instance, PolitiFact previously rated claims made by Trump advisor Kellyanne Conway as "mostly false" regarding the Senate healthcare plan's impact on Medicaid. PolitiFact clarified that while Republican proposals might slow the rate of Medicaid spending increases, leading to continued, albeit slower, growth, the accompanying policy changes could indeed reduce the number of individuals eligible for the program. This reduction in eligibility, they concluded, constitutes a cut.

The American Bridge, a Democratic advocacy group, has characterized the Republican plan supported by President Trump as a move that would "structurally end the program as we know it." This strong statement underscores the deep divisions and differing interpretations surrounding the potential consequences of the proposed healthcare reforms on one of the nation's most critical social safety net programs. The projected financial implications, coupled with potential changes to eligibility and the overall federal commitment, paint a complex picture of the future of Medicaid under the Republican healthcare agenda. The CBO's detailed analysis, which projects a $772 billion reduction in spending over an eight-year period, serves as a stark indicator of the scale of the proposed changes. This figure represents a significant deviation from the trajectory of spending under the Affordable Care Act, highlighting the core of the disagreement between the two parties. The debate is not merely about whether spending increases or decreases, but about the rate of increase and the fundamental structure of federal support for a program that serves millions of vulnerable Americans.

query: What are some of the most memorable TV theme songs composed by Alan Thicke?

documents.text: 10 Best TV Theme Songs Written by Alan Thicke Alan Thicke died on Tuesday (Dec. 13) at the age of 69, reportedly from a heart attack. While Thicke was best known as a sitcom actor, he was also a successful theme song composer. And in some cases, such as the Diff'rent Strokes theme song "It Takes Diff'rent Strokes," Thicke was even a featured singer. On occasion, Thicke collaborated with his then-wife singer and Days of Our Lives star Gloria Loring -- mother of Robin Thicke -- on these projects, with her singing singing on the Diff'rent Strokes spin-off The Facts of Life theme song. As we remember Thicke's legacy, here are 10 of his most memorable compositions: Diff'rent Strokes: The Facts of Life: The Wizard of Odds: The Joker's Wild: Celebrity Sweepstakes: The Diamond Head Game: Animal Crackups: Blank Check: Whew!: Wheel of Fortune:

The actor: Alan Thicke will forever be best known as the father on the hit 1980s sitcom Growing Pains (and the father of R&B superstar Robin Thicke), but he’s had a surprisingly eclectic career. He composed the deathless theme songs to the sitcoms Diff’Rent Strokes and The Facts Of Life, wrote for the ’70s cult-hit comedies Fernwood 2 Night, America 2-Night, and The Richard Pryor Show, and presided over one of the biggest, most notorious failures in late-night television, the unfortunately titled ’80s talk show Thicke Of The Night. Thicke occasionally appears as himself on How I Met Your Mother, and as a talk-show host on The Bold And The Beautiful. He recently popped up in the overflowing supporting cast of the raunchy car comedy The Goods: Live Hard, Sell Hard, which was just released on DVD. The Goods: Live Hard, Sell Hard (2009)—“Stu Harding” Alan Thicke: My recommendation would be that people remember that I told you to watch for the Will Ferrell death scene, where it’s raining adult pleasure toys, and remember that I was the guy who told you to keep your eye out for that. And I predict that’s a classic scene in the frat-boy humor domain, to be remembered and cherished along with the memorable semen-in-the-hair scene from There’s Something About Mary, and When Harry Met Sally, the famous restaurant, the luncheon scene. I think The Goods proudly gives you one of those memorable cinematic highlights. AVC: I was watching it last night, and I went to bed right after that scene. AT: There’s nothing to do after that. That’s right. You can’t top it. It’s over. When it’s raining dildos, you’ve got nothing left to say after that. How I Met Your Mother (2008-2009)—Himself AT: The role I was born to play, that of Alan Thicke. The plotline there is that Robin Sparkles and I, Alan Thicke, worked together on a Canadian variety show some years ago, and we’ve kept in touch since. And that’s how I get my annual recurring appearance on that show and keep my SAG insurance alive. AVC: In the show, you mention playing the “I’m Alan Thicke” card. Do you find yourself playing the “I’m Alan Thicke” card a lot in real life? AT: I do, you know. I have few other characters to relate to other than myself. I have enough of a body of work now that the paternal side of Alan Thicke gets a lot of play. I do get a lot of calls to play dads. In The Goods I’m Ed Helms’ dad, and I was known all those years as Kirk Cameron’s father, and now I’m known as Robin Thicke’s father, so I find myself playing myself a lot, and frankly living up to expectations of what the public’s image of me is. They see me as a family guy, so in some cases I’m able to play the family guy gone bad. The Bold And The Beautiful (2006-2009)—“Rich Ginger” AT: I enjoyed that. That’s another one of those annual recurring things I get to do. The character comes on every six months or so just to move the plotline along. I’m used. I’m a utility guy. I play a local talk-show host. So when they want to get 11 plot points mentioned in five minutes, it’s an easy way to do it when you have one of the characters come on the talk show, and then Rich Ginger interviews them, asks them a bunch of questions, and that covers about three months’ worth of plotline on The Bold And The Beautiful. And then suddenly everybody’s caught up, and they can move forward with their story. So that’s kind of fun for me to do, because I’ve lived a bit of that life. One of my wives was a soap star, and used to get up and disappear at 5:30 in the morning every day, and I had no idea where in the hell she was going. She kept saying she was an actress on a soap. I had no reason not to believe her. And sure enough, when I got invited into that world to make some cameo appearances, I was curious to see how they worked, and it’s quite an art form. They do 400 pages of dialogue in a day. They have to be in character all the time and work ridiculous hours, and mostly get underpaid for it, which I recall her lawyer saying to me as part of our dissolution.
