It’s an interesting area of theater. AVC: So your character was an exposition machine? AT: Yeah, exactly. I’m used. I bend over, and they get their exposition. AVC: Is it exhausting working through so much dialogue in one day? AT: Well, I think that it is for the people who have to do it every day. To pop in for a couple of days is just an interesting challenge. But to have to do it every day, to be in character and do copious amounts of scenes and dialogue, that’s a lot. I mean it’s certainly not the way film people are accustomed to working, where you do a couple of pages a day. These people are literally doing, I don’t know, 20 or 30 pages a day, so that’s… it’s a volume business. Fernwood 2 Night (1977)—Writer AVC: Speaking of grueling, you were a writer for Fernwood 2 Night. That was on every night, was it not? AT: It was. That was a grueling schedule. When Norman Lear first came to me to write and produce that series, his concept was totally improvisational, that we would give a couple of guys an idea, an outline as you would an improv troupe, and they would go and improvise comedy in these characters as faux host and co-host of a nightly local talk show. And that made me nervous, the fact that we had to produce 24 minutes of material every day, and we were going to rely on improv to do that. I’ve always felt that improv looks and feels more clever when you’re there to experience it live than when you have the degree of separation that television creates. Television raises expectations. I’ve never been a big fan of improvisation, because, as I said, I think you appreciate the process and the cleverness of it more than the actual comedy in most cases. And I remembered having a conversation with no less an authority than Carl Reiner around the time we were getting ready to shoot the first episodes. I said, “Carl, Norman wants to do this improvisational show.” Carl said, “Listen. You know that I love Norman, but Mel Brooks and I are the funniest guys in the history of the English language at improvisation, the funniest team, and we could take one of your premises and give you 45 minutes of genius on any given day. And on every other day, we could give you two hours of deathly silence and come up with absolutely nothing. So you tell Norman that his friend Carl says this show needed a little writing.” So I went ahead and with my couple of writers, completely scripted verbatim the entire first week’s worth of shows. Five episodes. Took them to Norman, and he fired me. And he said, “This is not what I have in mind. I want to do an improv show. This is all crap. Get these first shows taped and you’re out of here.” So I, with my tail between my legs, went and taped the first episodes, the audience went crazy, the show was hilarious, everybody involved loved it, and, to Norman’s great credit, he handed me a note afterwards. He said “I don’t get it, I don’t understand it. I don’t know how this is happening, but it’s obviously working, so I’m going to Tahiti, have a nice time.” So he left us alone after that to do it the way that I envisioned doing it, which was with a verbatim script, but always letting Martin Mull and Fred Willard improvise over and above the script. I always made sure we taped an extra five or ten minutes a day, because invariably they came up with something wonderful. But at the same time, we were always covered in case they had brain damage on any given day. I remembered a lot of that experience when I was doing The Goods; these guys would film a couple of takes and then throw the script out and say “Play with it.” I think my experience writing and producing that stuff years ago helped me understand how these guys work in this wonderful frat-boy type of comedy movie that they do so successfully time after time, and I understood the process. AVC: Neal Brennan, who’s best known for Chappelle’s Show, directed The Goods, so he certainly has experience with improvisation. AT: Part of the mystique of shows like Curb Your Enthusiasm is the idea that they begin with a couple of plotlines and then a bunch of geniuses improvise dialogue. It’s not quite that unstructured and loose. It makes for a good urban myth, but everything’s a little more tightly scripted and programmed than that. But if a director can find the exact combination between the written word as a guideline and improvisational input from his actors, I think that’s where you’ll find the most successful work being done. AVC: You need a solid foundation to deviate from. AT: Exactly. You know what the chords are, you just don’t know what the notes are. AVC: You were also lucky to have as gifted improvisers like Mull and Willard. AT: Yeah, brilliant, brilliant. And like I said, that’s what I was experiencing in that ensemble cast on The Goods. You have to have a wonderful confluence of good ideas that came from the table, and great execution, and the ability to go deeper from your actors. America 2-Night (1978)—Writer AVC: In the second season, Fernwood 2 Night changed into America 2-Night. How did the show’s shift from being a faux-local show affect you as a writer? AT: Well, in a way, it became easier. The reason we shifted from Fernwood 2 Night to America 2-Night was the simple matter of ratings. It was around then that the networks were all feeling that celebrity big-name bookings were important in drawing a crowd and getting people into the tent. So we needed to find an excuse to have celebrities coming on the show, which was not the case at all in Fernwood, and, sure enough, the notion that we’d be moving from Ohio to Hollywood, or specifically Alta Coma, California, allowed us to be geographically within range to have celebrities on the show, so we were able to have bookings that were important at the time. Big names like Carol Burnett and Charlton Heston. And what we did was have them on the show and kind of poke fun of them, or more specifically, kind of laugh with them. These were big stars that were able to come on our show and poke fun at themselves. Stick some pins in their own balloons. And that was the very early days of that style. Nowadays, of course, it’s stock in trade if you watch programs like Head Case, or the Ricky Gervais shows, including the one he did called Extras a couple of years ago. People love to come on shows and lampoon themselves now. I did that on Jay Leno a couple of weeks ago, playing myself. I guess to some degree, I did that on How I Met Your Mother recently. But back then, it was the early days of giving stars the opportunity to poke a little fun at themselves. That was part of—if not the reason for the move to California, it was at least a product of it. AVC: Wasn’t Tom Waits on Fernwood 2 Night? AT: Tom Waits was, yes, yes, where he did the famous line “I’d rather have a bottle in front of me than a frontal lobotomy.” AVC: How was that? Creating the rationale for Tom Waits to be in Fernwood? AT: We got pretty good at creating that kind of rationale. We could occasionally have somebody pop through Fernwood. One of our famous pop-through-Fernwood episodes was one that we did called “Talk To A Jew,” the idea being that they’d never seen a person of the Jewish faith in Fernwood, and a traveling salesman was caught going 10 miles over the speed limit, and the local sheriff decided that instead of a fine, it would be a public service if he could come on television and local residents could see that Jewish people were just like anybody else, and this would be an enlightening appearance. So he came on the show, and of course it became—the political incorrectness was hilarious on that episode. In real life, there was an outcry. We got a lot of letters, a lot of response—the show was fairly controversial anyway—but a lot of response from self-righteous, mostly right-wingers saying “How could you possibly make sport of our Jewish friends in such a way?” Because we had phone-in callers saying “What is that beanie you guys wear on the top of your head?” and “What’s your favorite Barbra Streisand movie?” The interesting thing was that we also at the same time got a commendation from B’nai B’rith that Norman Lear ended up being very proud of, commending us, giving us their award of the year for media enlightenment for showing the silliness and putting a funny face on prejudice and racial stereotypes, etc. So we were getting commended by the Jewish press at the same time that some people in Alabama were wondering why we were making fun of them. It was that kind of a show. If you got the satire, it was brilliant, and if you didn’t, it was just offensive. The Richard Pryor Special? and The Richard Pryor Show (1977)—Writer AVC: Speaking of satire and controversy, in 1977 you were a writer for both The Richard Pryor Special? and The Richard Pryor Show. That must have been interesting. AT: It was fascinating, and another great experience or milestone in my improvisational life. I was a head writer for Richard’s show, and my skill there involved being able to push his button, and then push the button on my cassette recorder, and then transcribe the gems that fell out of his mouth. I quickly learned that the best way to work with Richard was to find a premise that he responded to, that made his eyes light up. After his horrible self-immolation incident, I used to tease him that I knew how to make his eyes light up, and he knew how to get the rest of his body to follow suit. The point being that if you came up with a premise, like a one-liner, as you’re sitting around at a meeting, and you say “Richard, how about if you were this old blues guy, and it was your very last appearance after 50 years with this partner of yours, and you had a few things to get off your chest?” If he was responding to that and he liked it, you could see the wheels starting to turn, and his eyes would kind of light up, and he would improvise a couple of lines in that character. Then if he really liked it—you knew it was time to turn on the tape recorder if he actually got up on his feet, got off the sofa, and started walking around performing this character, Mudbone or whoever it would be. That’s when you really knew you had something. So you turned on the tape recorder, and he’d start winging it, and you just made sure you had batteries in the machine, and you’d end up with a script. AVC: That was also a fairly controversial show, was it not? AT: Oh, it was, sure. He couldn’t do it any other way. Richard was not about to capitulate to the norms and standards of network television at the time. He was one of the guys that always railed against it, and here, all these years later, we remember that he took a stand, whether it was on racial issues, political issues, censorship issues… He tackled them all in ways that would be considered modest and mild by today’s standards, but then were considered revolutionary. People along the way in every decade have to push the envelope a little bit, and he was one of the guys that did that. Diff’rent Strokes (1978) and The Facts Of Life (1979)—Theme Songwriter AVC: In the late ’70s, you wrote two theme songs that will ricochet through pop-culture forever. How does it work when you’re writing a theme song? Do you get the script and write it from there? Do you see a pilot? What’s the process like? AT: It’s all of that. The challenge was, you have 24 seconds to do something catchy and memorable and sum up the entire premise of the show in case somebody had never seen it before. You had to do it with an internal rhyme scheme and a perky little ditty. So it was an interesting challenge that way. It varied. I did over 40 themes altogether. Those happen to be two of the most memorable ones, and long-lasting. It would vary. Sometimes it would be brought in at the very last minute, when a show was already completely shot and the pilot had been edited, and they’d say “Here, add your 24 seconds.” There were other instances, and Diff’rent Strokes was one of them, where you got the information, you were included, from day one and page one. From the notion “Well, we’re developing this idea, and we kind of have an idea that it’s a couple of young black guys with an older white guy.” And then you would get a copy of the script a month later and the revision a month after that, and then you’re invited to the taping, and then you do your own editing right through their editing, so that hopefully you all come together at the end when it’s time to deliver. I’ve experienced it both ways. Nowadays, I get paid by technology that didn’t even exist then. I get ringtone royalties now. Apparently what happens is, you get college kids after a couple of rounds of Beer Pong, and they start to bet each other who can remember the most lyrics to an iconic sitcom, and they order it up on ringtones, and I get 11 cents. It doesn’t keep up with the current state of the global economy, but it’s always a pleasant little surprise in the mail. AVC: When you were writing those songs, did you get the sense that you’d nailed it, that the themes just worked? AT: Back then, theme songs were more important. They were a part of every show. Nowadays, they don’t aspire to have memorable themes for every show, because the networks are so competitive that as soon as you hit 8 o’clock, or 8:30, or 9 o’clock, that show needs to be in progress already. They don’t want to waste the time with 25 or 30 seconds of a ditty. They get right to the action, and that’s the case in both comedy and drama now. And certainly theme songs with a lyric, they have no time for that. So I think that’s almost a lost art. Back then, we took it a little more seriously, and you knew that your song would be heard every episode. So yeah, you spent some time at it, and took a little pride in it, and worked hard. Like, the Facts Of Life internal rhyme scheme was intricate and one that I remember finishing and saying “Yeah, that’s pretty good. That all rhymes. I got a lot of rhyming words in 24 seconds.” Thicke Of The Night (1983-1984)—Host AT: I remember that when I saw the first episode on the air, I literally fainted. I was in a writers’ meeting at my house preparing the next day’s material, and I got lightheaded and walked up to my kitchen and fell. The writers heard the clinking and clamoring, the disturbance, and came and rescued me, but that’s how disturbed I was at myself. The show was lousy, and I was super-lousy in it. To this day, I can’t even really watch it. It was a dark period in my life. I’m happy to say that it did, nevertheless, lead me to my opportunity on Growing Pains. But Thicke Of The Night was… I made the big mistake of listening to, paying attention to, some of my press clippings and thinking I was all that, and I really wasn’t. And the irony is that in today’s marketplace, those ratings would be considered a huge hit. Back then, it was just me and Johnny Carson, and I was getting clobbered, but I was getting clobbered and putting up numbers that today would be competitive in late-night with anybody, including Dave [Letterman] and Conan [O’Brien]. Not that we deserved it. It’s not false modesty when I say I was pretty bad at it. I was quite terrific as an afternoon host, as a schmoozer kind of guy, more in the Mike Douglas, Merv Griffin mode, back when those shows were popular. I was good at that. I had a very successful show in Canada doing that. But in late-night, you can’t take yourself too seriously. You can’t think you’re all that or too sexy. It’s an environment in which you have to go for the jugular. You have to be prepared to be mean and nasty and get your laughs. And I say that with great admiration for the guys who do it. It really takes a stand-up mentality, where you can go for the killer joke at anybody’s expense at any time, in your monologue and beyond. That just wasn’t my background. AVC: You lacked the killer instinct? AT: I did. I don’t know if it’s just an instinct performance-wise, or even as a writer. Although as a writer for guys like Pryor, and other people I really loved comedically, like Sam Kinison, my feeling always was “I can write naughty things for other people to say.” I never had the balls to say them myself. AVC: Johnny Carson was such an icon at the time that people were almost offended that anyone would dare compete with him. AT: Oh, absolutely. And we made the terrible mistake—and I was compliant and just went along with it—but we made the terrible mistake of having an entire promotional campaign that said, “We’re going to take on Johnny and beat him, and nobody’s ever been able to do it.” That was an outrageous thing for an unknown, semi-talented, no-name Canadian, a terrible position for him to take against an American broadcasting icon. That wasn’t my idea, but I let it go, I let them do it, because they said “This is how we’re going to get attention. This is how we’re going to get eyeballs on the show.” They were half-right, but the show didn’t deserve to stick anyway. It didn’t probably deserve to die the horrible death and be the scourge of the land that it became. But we certainly invited that kind of reaction by this horrible promotional campaign. AVC: The stakes were very, very high. AT: Yeah, and I became the go-to guy as the guru of late-night failure after that. Literally, a lot of guys who followed would call me and take me to dinner to find out how I blew it so badly. Pat Sajak, Dennis Miller, Rick Dees, Arsenio Hall, they all called and asked, “Alan how did you fuck this up so bad? Tell us what mistakes not to make.” I did have a couple of important tips for the guys who followed, but it wasn’t until Chevy Chase and Magic Johnson came along that the stench of Thicke Of The Night began to dissipate. AVC: They usurped you as the people behind the ultimate failed talk show? AT: I had to be preempted by guys who raised the bar or lowered it, I guess. Growing Pains (1985-1992)—“Dr. Jason Seaver” AT: Loved it. Proud of it. Proud of what it stood for. I share the corny family values espoused on that show. Happy for the role, both, as I said, what it stood for and what it did for me and my life and my family and my career. So if that’s what goes on my tombstone, I’m perfectly comfortable with it. It was a great opportunity that made my life good and something that I can show to my 12-year-old now in reruns. Corny and dated as it is, it’s still relatable, understandable, and he can look at it and say “Yeah, I get it. Now I see what you did before I was born.”

Two days before her husband Alan Thicke passed away at the age of 69, Tanya Thicke shared a sweet photo of the couple on Instagram. “I’ve got the cutest designated driver EVER!!!” Tanya captioned a photo of herself looking adoringly at her husband while sitting in a car. “JP & @eloisedejoria sure know how to throw a party! Thx you for having us. #friendsforever #patron #family,” Tanya continued. Days earlier, Tanya. who married Alan on May 7, 2005 in Cabo San Lucas, shared a throwback photo of herself with her husband and stepson Carter Thicke. “When you’re just trying to get that perfect Xmas card pic! #2010 #tbt,” she captioned the Christmas-themed photo from 2010. The couple wed just one month after they announced their engagement in April 2005. “I’ll be on my third honeymoon, so I’m more of an authority than I care to be,” Thicke quipped in a statement about his engagement to his longtime model girlfriend. The Growing Pains actor gushed about his wife in an April 2014 interview with PEOPLE: “My wife who is a wonderful yin and yang personality – hot and cold – I call her the sexy, loveable tornado is completely fascination and out of control and very sexy.” On Tuesday, Dec. 13, Alan passed away at the age of 69, a publicist for his son Robin Thicke confirmed to PEOPLE. The veteran actor is survived by his wife, Tanya, and his three sons: Robin, Carter and Brennan.

No one misses Alan Thicke more than his family. Everyone’s favorite TV dad passed away on Tuesday after having a heart attack while playing hockey with his son Carter Thicke. Thicke is survived by his three sons, Robin, Brennan and Carter and his wife, Tanya. Fans, friends and colleagues of Thicke poured their hearts out about the late actor on social media after hearing the news and his son, Carter did the same. “Today I lost my best friend and my idol, and the world lost one of it’s finest. You are a legend and I love you Pops. Until next time,” the 19-year-old tweeted on Tuesday. Today I lost my best friend and my idol, and the world lost one of it's finest. You are a legend and I love you Pops. Until next time. pic.twitter.com/ZQd2NZMTx4 — Carter Thicke (@CarterThicke) December 14, 2016 Singer Robin Thicke also remembered his father in an interview with the L.A. Times. as “”the greatest man I ever met” and said of his passing, “the good thing was that he was beloved and he had closure.” RELATED: After the news of Alan Thicke’s untimely passing, friends and fellow celebrities were heartbroken A year and a half before his death, the hockey-loving actor shared a photo of himself with his son Carter on the rink.

The sudden passing of beloved actor Alan Thicke on December 13th sent ripples of grief through Hollywood and beyond. For his son, Carter Thicke, the loss was particularly profound, as he described his father as his "best friend and idol." The tragic event unfolded on Tuesday evening while Alan, a celebrated figure known for his role in "Growing Pains," was engaged in a beloved pastime – playing ice hockey with Carter. The 69-year-old actor suffered a heart attack, which tragically led to his death shortly thereafter, as reported by the Los Angeles Times.

The news of the Golden Globe-nominated actor's death broke swiftly, and within hours, Carter, then 19 years old, took to social media to share his heartfelt tribute. His poignant tweet, accompanied by a touching photograph of him and his father, read: "Today I lost my best friend and my idol, and the world lost one of it’s finest. You are a legend and I love you Pops. Until next time." This raw expression of love and admiration captured the deep bond between father and son.

Alan Thicke, born in Canada, shared a deep passion for ice hockey, the nation's most prevalent winter sport, with his son Carter. This shared love for the game was evident in a sweet photograph Alan posted in July 2015, showcasing the father-son duo on the ice rink. Alan captioned the image, featuring both of them in hockey uniforms, with the affectionate words: "Like Father, like son. The Thicke apple doesn’t fall far from the tree. #CanadianAndProud #Hockey #TBT." This glimpse into their shared activities highlighted the strong connection they enjoyed.

Following his father's untimely death, Robin Thicke, Alan's 39-year-old son, also spoke to the Los Angeles Times, sharing his deep respect and love for his father. Robin described Alan as "the greatest man I ever met" and found solace in the fact that his father "was beloved and he had closure." Despite the unexpected nature of his passing, Robin revealed that he had recently spent quality time with his father, during which he openly expressed his profound love and admiration. "I saw him a few days ago and told him how much I loved and respected him," Robin shared, underscoring the importance of those final moments.

Alan Thicke's legacy extends beyond his acting career. He was a devoted family man, survived by his wife, Tanya, and his three sons: Robin, Carter, and Brennan. His contributions to television and his warm, engaging personality left an indelible mark on many. The outpouring of tributes from friends, colleagues, and fans alike is a testament to the positive impact he had on the lives of those around him. The world remembers Alan Thicke not just as a talented actor, but as a loving father, a devoted husband, and a true gentleman. His presence will be deeply missed.

After the show was canceled, Loring and Thicke divorced after 14 years and he took custody of their sons Brennan and Robin, who were 11 and 9 at the time. In 1985 he took on the sitcom role that would be his most successful in the U.S. and one that most suited him — an affable and thoughtful dad on "Growing Pains." Portraying the work-from-home psychiatrist Jason Seaver, who was raising a family with his wife (portrayed by Joanna Kerns), Thicke was tailor-made for the part, having studied psychology.

The entertainment world is in mourning following the unexpected death of Alan Thicke, the beloved actor best known for his iconic role as Jason Seaver on the 1980s sitcom "Growing Pains." Thicke, who was 69 years old, passed away on Tuesday night after suffering a heart attack while playing hockey with his 19-year-old son, Carter. His sudden departure has sent ripples of grief through Hollywood, with numerous friends and fellow celebrities sharing their heartfelt tributes.

The outpouring of sadness underscores Thicke's significant impact on the industry and the personal connections he forged. Celebrities like Ellen DeGeneres expressed profound sorrow, tweeting, "America loved Alan Thicke. I'm so sad he's gone. Sending so much love to his family." Larry King echoed these sentiments, remembering Thicke as "A wonderful talent, a wonderful man." Dave Coulier shared a personal anecdote, posting, "RIP Alan Thicke. We skated side by side for years. Say hello to Gordie and The Rocket for me."

Further tributes highlighted Thicke's warmth and kindness. Bob Saget lamented, "So sad is the passing of Alan Thicke. Such a good husband, father, brother, and friend. He will be deeply missed." Candace Cameron Bure, who recently worked with Thicke on "Fuller House," shared, "I'm sad beyond words that Alan Thicke has passed away. I've known you since I was 8 years old…" Ryan Seacrest noted Thicke was "a great friend over the years," while Gilbert Gottfried fondly recalled him as a "Wife Swap partner." Russell Simmons declared, "The world has lost an icon." Alyssa Milano described Thicke as "always the nicest and happiest person in the room," and Olivia Munn remembered him as "Such a nice guy." Demi Lovato recalled meeting Thicke and his wife Tanya, sending prayers to his family. Montel Williams questioned the year's toll, stating, "Now Alan Thicke… Can we get a do-over on 2016? Too many good people lost." John Legend, who knew Thicke through his son Robin, remembered him as "always so kind to me." Thicke's recent work included a role in the 2016 sequel "Fuller House," where he reunited with former co-stars Candace Cameron Bure, Bob Saget, and Dave Coulier.

query: What changes were made to the Mega Millions game structure on October 28, 2017?

documents.text: MEGA MILLIONS FAQs Can I Buy Mega Millions Tickets in Illinois? Yes! Mega Millions is available for purchase at all Illinois Lottery retailers, online and through the mobile app. Why is Mega Millions changing? Mega Millions is changing to have to faster growing jackpots. When did Mega Millions change? The changes to Mega Millions occured on Saturday, October 28, 2017. The first drawing impacted will be the Tuesday, October 31, 2017 drawing. The last drawing under the current game structure will be Friday, October 27, 2017. How much does it cost to play Mega Millions? The price to play Mega Millions increased on 10/28/2017 to $2 (from $1). The Megaplier is an additional $1 to the cost of your ticket. What exactly has changed with Mega Millions? Beginning October 28, 2017, the starting jackpot increased to $40 million (previously $15 million). Players will now select 5 numbers from 1 to 70 (previously 75) and choose 1 Mega Ball number from 1-25 (previously 15). The smallest prize is now $2 (previously $1). The new prize structure should produce faster growing jackpots, and will have better odds of winning $1 million at 2nd prize level! How did the October 28, 2017 Mega Millions changes impact multi-draw? Beginning August 4th, 2017 the amount of advanced draws available for purchase began to decrease, preventing players from purchasing draws beyond October 27th, 2017. Multi-draw and advance play options will resume to 25 draws on October 28th, 2017. How did the October 28, 2017 Mega Millions changes impact subscriptions? Beginning August 4th, 2017 the length of subscriptions available for purchase began to decrease, preventing players from purchasing subscriptions with draws beyond October 27th, 2017. If you held a Mega Millions subscription, a new subscription will need to be purchased at illinoislottery.com on or after October 28th, 2017. Where can I find the winning numbers? Winning numbers can be found on the Illinois Lottery website: www.illinoislottery.com or the Illinois Lottery Mobile App. Where can I find information on how to play and win Mega Millions, as well as how to play my numbers in multiple drawings? You can find information on how to play Mega Millions at retail or online, how to win, and information on our popular Multidraw option all on the Mega Millions page of the Illinois Lottery website. Simply select the appropriate tab: “How to Play at Retail,” “How to Play Online,” or “How to Win.” Where and when do the drawings take place? Mega Millions drawings are conducted Tuesday and Friday evenings at 11:00 p.m. EST by the Georgia Lottery. Where can I find Mega Millions prize information? The prize payout information for Mega Millions jackpots will be updated on www.illinoislottery.com and the Illinois Lottery Mobile App by Wednesday and Saturday, following scheduled drawings. The payout information must be verified by all participating states before being released. Note: Illinois Lottery only reports the payout information for Illinois winners. For payout information of all Mega Millions participating states please visit: www.megamillions.com Can I Purchase Mega Millions On The Internet? Yes, you can purchase Mega Millions online. Just, visit illinoislottery.com. Can I purchase Megaplier on the internet? Yes, Megaplier is available for purchase on the internet at the time you make a Mega Millions purchase, for an additional cost. Is Mega Millions available for purchase on the Illinois Lottery Mobile App? Yes, you can purchase Mega Millions on the Illinois Lottery mobile app, as well as check winning numbers and find Lottery retailers. If you have an Apple iPhone, the app can be downloaded from the iTunes store. If you have an Android device, visit www.illinoislottery.com from your mobile phone to download the app. Did I win? To see if you’ve won, enter your numbers into the Winning Number History Search or scan the barcode on your ticket at your favorite Illinois Lottery retailer. You can cash your prize up to $600 at most lottery retailers. Find a prize claim center near you, to cash prizes over $600. Mega Millions Subscriptions FAQs What length of subscriptions are available for Mega Millions? You can purchase customized subscriptions for 1 to 25 consecutive drawings or purchase one of the preset subscription lengths of up to 26 weeks. How Can I Choose The Number Of Drawings I’d Like To Purchase For Mega Millions Online? Purchasing Mega Millions is easy. Start by visiting illinoislottery.com and selecting the "Buy Now" button below the Mega Millions jackpot. Then, select the number of tickets you want to purchase, pick your numbers, and select the number of drawings you would like to purchase from the drop down. After you've made all of your desired selections, select "Add to Cart." Do I Have The Same Options For My Online Mega Millions Purchases That I Have In The Retail Stores? Yes. You have the same options online that you have in retail stores. Can I extend my play and subscribe on the Mobile App? Yes, you can extend your play and subscribe to Mega Millions via the Illinois Lottery Mobile App or website. Learn more about the Illinois Lottery Mobile App. How do I buy more than one set of numbers? After you have created your ticket and added one set of numbers to your shopping cart, an "Add Another Game" section will appear under your ticket details. Just select the "Buy Now" button under the Mega Millions logo to add more tickets. Learn more about the online shopping cart and other online play features. I purchased a Mega Millions subscription. When does my subscription start? Your subscription will start with the next available drawing. What happens when my subscription ends? When the end of your subscription is approaching, you will receive a notification email of the approaching end date. After your subscription ends, you can log in to your online account and repurchase your subscription. My subscription is over and I want to replay my numbers. How can I do that? If your subscription has already ended, you can easily replay your original numbers with the same options by copying your subscription. Just log in to your Illinois Lottery online account and go to My Account>>My Subscriptions and identify the subscription you wish to replay under the "Expired Subscriptions" tab. Click the "Copy" (stacked squares) icon and the options for that subscription will be visible. You can then add those numbers to your cart as is, or make edits to your subscription details. Your new subscription will start with the next available draw as long as you have completed your purchase prior to 9:15 p.m. on Monday, Thursday or Saturday evening. On-Line Game Rules

**Illinois Lottery Pauses Powerball and Mega Millions Sales Due to Budget Stalemate**

Illinois residents eager to play Powerball and Mega Millions will have to wait, as the Illinois Lottery has suspended ticket sales for both popular games. This decision, announced Wednesday, is a direct result of the state's General Assembly failing to pass a budget that includes essential funding for the lottery for the upcoming 2018 fiscal year.

Acting Illinois Lottery director Greg Smith emphasized the importance of player confidence, stating, "Players should be confident knowing the Illinois Lottery has the money to pay these winning claims. That means the General Assembly needs to approve a truly balanced budget that includes lottery funding in order to ensure all prize payments will occur."

This unprecedented suspension could cause significant delays for winners of prizes over $25,000 if a budget agreement is not reached by July 1. This situation is not without precedent; a similar budget deadlock in 2015 led to delayed payouts for nearly 3,900 lottery winners, totaling over $112 million. During that period, the lottery estimated a revenue loss of up to $71.5 million due to these payment interruptions.

Lottery officials had previously warned of this potential suspension, citing the state's ongoing three-year budget impasse and its effect on agreements with the Multi-State Lottery Association.

The sales suspension details are as follows:
*   **Powerball:** Sales will cease at 9 p.m. CT on June 28.
*   **Mega Millions:** Sales will follow on Friday, June 30, at 9:45 p.m. CT.

It is important to note that all Powerball and Mega Millions tickets purchased before these suspension times will remain valid.

The ongoing legislative session, costing taxpayers tens of thousands of dollars daily without tangible progress, highlights a stark contrast between public pronouncements of compromise and actual efforts to achieve it. Republican Governor Bruce Rauner has actively promoted a proposal dubbed the "Capitol Compromise" across his social media, a plan developed by GOP lawmakers without Democratic input. Conversely, Democratic House Speaker Michael Madigan orchestrated votes on several of Rauner's key priorities, framing them as a "substantial compromise." Republicans, however, dismissed these actions as disingenuous. This divergence underscores how "compromise" has become a rhetorical weapon, employed by each party to assign blame for the looming budget deadline.

Despite the significant consequences of failing to reach a budget agreement, a resolution appears unlikely by the Friday deadline. Governor Rauner issued a stern warning: "If lawmakers fail to send a balanced budget package to my desk by Friday, we will have no choice but to keep them in session until they get the job done." The core of the two-year impasse remains: both parties acknowledge the necessity of a tax increase, but Rauner insists on accompanying regulatory reforms to stimulate economic growth. Speaker Madigan opposes these reforms, arguing they would disproportionately benefit the wealthy at the expense of middle-class workers.

Key points of contention and recent developments include:

*   **Property Tax Freeze Debate:** Democrats proposed a property tax freeze, which Governor Rauner has long advocated. However, Republicans criticized the Democratic plan for including too many exemptions, arguing it would not provide meaningful relief to taxpayers. The measure ultimately failed, but Democrats who voted in favor may gain political cover against potential election challenges.
*   **Governor Rauner's Demands:** Rauner is pushing for a four-year statewide property tax freeze in exchange for a four-year income tax increase.
*   **Democratic Counter-Proposal:** The Democratic plan would freeze real estate levies for four years, but with significant exemptions for Chicago, Chicago Public Schools, and financially distressed school districts. It would also exclude property taxes used for debt or pension payments, a point of contention for Republicans.
*   **Medicaid Payment Issues:** A federal judge, Joan Lefkow, indicated a willingness to order the state to increase payments for Medicaid bills. Comptroller Susana Mendoza has warned that continued delays could impact other state expenditures. Judge Lefkow agreed with Medicaid patient advocates that the state may be violating federal court orders by failing to provide adequate access to care for low-income individuals. Lawyers representing Medicaid patients are expected to present evidence of the damage caused by the unpaid bills.

Behind the scenes, legislative leaders, including House Minority Leader Jim Durkin, have engaged in closed-door meetings and negotiations. Governor Rauner is reportedly working through Durkin in these discussions. Publicly, however, each side continues to position the other as the obstacle to a budget deal.

On Wednesday, Madigan's office held votes on bills addressing Rauner's priorities in areas such as workers' compensation, property tax relief, government consolidation, and employee pensions. Durkin immediately labeled this "political theater" designed for "gotcha votes," arguing that ongoing conversations made such votes premature. Republicans contended that Democrats were voting on incomplete measures to create political advantages.

The strategy of calling votes on contentious issues allows Madigan to:

*   **Provide Political Cover:** Offer Democratic lawmakers, particularly those facing re-election challenges, a chance to vote on popular measures.
*   **Attack Opponents:** Use Republican votes against these measures as fodder for political attacks.
*   **Pressure GOP Members:** Exert pressure on Downstate Republican lawmakers whose districts are impacted by the budget impasse, potentially encouraging them to break ranks with Governor Rauner.

A notable instance of this occurred when Representative David Harris (R-Arlington Heights) was the sole Republican to support the Democratic property tax freeze, stating, "If this is what it takes to help get us to a budget, then I think we really seriously need to be looking to vote 'yes' on the bill."

The consequences of failing to enact a budget by month's end are severe. Illinois faces a potential downgrade of its credit rating below investment grade, exclusion from multi-state lottery games like Powerball and Mega Millions, and potential loss of accreditation for its universities.

## The Looming Shadow of Default: What Happens When Illinois Can't Pay Its Debts?

Illinois's prolonged state budget negotiations, now entering their third year, have conspicuously sidestepped a critical question: what are the consequences if the state is unable or unwilling to meet its financial obligations? This looming possibility is a significant concern for investors holding Illinois debt, a sentiment clearly reflected in the declining prices of the state's bonds and recent downgrades by rating agencies. These actions have pushed Illinois's debt to just one notch above "junk" status, a stark warning sign for the state's fiscal health.

### The Puerto Rican Precedent: A Stark Warning for Bondholders

While market reactions directly stem from Illinois's protracted budget impasse, a deeper investor unease is fueled by developments in Puerto Rico. The U.S. territory, grappling with its own severe debt crisis, successfully secured federal assistance. However, the recent budget adopted by Puerto Rico's government, with the endorsement of a congressionally appointed Fiscal Oversight Board, reveals a stark strategy: to prioritize other expenditures and pensioners while effectively "stiffing" its bondholders. Pensioners are largely protected, and other government expenses are slated for increases, a move that has sent ripples of concern through the financial markets.

This Puerto Rican precedent carries significant implications for Illinois. Bondholders, understandably, fear a similar outcome should Illinois face a fiscal collapse. While pensioners in Illinois might initially welcome such a prospect, the more profound lesson from Puerto Rico is the potential erosion of constitutional protections when the federal government intervenes.

### Constitutional Promises Under Pressure

**Puerto Rico's Constitutional Mandate:** Puerto Rico's constitution explicitly mandates that general-obligation bondholders be paid before all other obligations. This promise was a cornerstone of its ability to borrow at favorable rates for an extended period, despite its deteriorating fiscal health.

**Erosion of Promises:** The current plan involves foregoing more than $2 billion in annual interest payments—an amount representing 80 percent of what was due in 2017—for at least the next five years, effectively disregarding the constitutional mandate. This was overseen by the congressionally appointed Fiscal Oversight Board, a body established by the Puerto Rico Oversight, Management, and Economic Stability Act (PROMESA).

Illinois's constitution, while not containing such an explicit promise to bondholders, does contain provisions that could be vulnerable in a federal intervention scenario. Crucially, it expressly prohibits the state from impairing promised benefits to current or future pensioners. If the federal government were to step in to resolve an Illinois budget crisis, it is highly probable that bondholders would bear a significant burden. Furthermore, the state's rigid pension protections and its prohibition on progressive taxation, both enshrined in the constitution, would likely be placed on the negotiating table. This scenario, while perhaps not immediate, could be closer than many anticipate, according to experts like Ike Brannon, a visiting fellow at the Cato Institute and president of Capital Policy Analytics.

### The Inevitable Downward Spiral

The next economic downturn, coupled with a stock market correction, is poised to cause a precipitous decline in Illinois's tax revenues. Simultaneously, the value of its pension funds would likely plummet. This decline could reach a point where no achievable rate of return on the remaining pension assets could ensure solvency. Such a development could render the state unable to borrow money at any rate, inevitably forcing federal intervention.

### Shared Burdens in a Federal Bailout

Should this federal intervention occur, it is a safe assumption that the burden of adjustment will be shared broadly. Taxpayers, bondholders, and public pensioners alike would likely face difficult compromises. In the most optimistic scenario for current and former state workers, the outcome might resemble the pension reform bill that was invalidated by the Illinois State Supreme Court in 2015. That bill, proposed by Governor Bruce Rauner's administration, had sought to end automatic cost-of-living increases, raise the retirement age, and alter the calculation of a worker's final salary for pension benefit determination.

However, it is entirely plausible that pension adjustments could extend much further. This could involve ex post facto reductions in benefits for individuals who artificially inflated their final salaries to boost their pensions, or a diminution of benefits for those receiving multiple state pensions. Moreover, once federal authorities permit the abandonment of the prohibition on progressive tax rates, Illinois could well find itself resembling New Jersey, with top marginal income tax rates approaching 10 percent, as observed in analyses by organizations like the Tax Foundation.

Investors are justified in their wariness of holding Illinois bonds. However, the most critical lesson to be drawn from Puerto Rico's fiscal crisis, as highlighted by financial analysts and economists such as those at the Brookings Institution and the American Enterprise Institute, is that constitutional promises can become remarkably fragile once the federal government becomes involved. Consequently, the state's pensioners and taxpayers have as much reason to be concerned as its bondholders.

Illinois stands on the brink of a historic credit downgrade, a situation that could see it become the first state since 1970 to lose its investment-grade status. This critical juncture is a direct consequence of a prolonged budget impasse, with state leaders poised to miss a crucial deadline, pushing Illinois into its third consecutive year without a comprehensive spending plan. The Illinois House's failure to vote on a budget by Wednesday means the state government, already mired in gridlock, will not pass a budget by midnight. This delay significantly complicates an already arduous task, as approving a budget—a typically routine process that has eluded Illinois for 700 days—will now necessitate a supermajority of three-fifths.

The core of this fiscal crisis lies in the persistent disagreement between Democratic lawmakers, who control both legislative chambers, and Republican Governor Bruce Rauner. Their repeated inability to reconcile their approaches to addressing chronic budget deficits, a problem exacerbated by the expiration of tax hikes in January 2015, has led to this precarious state. John Humphrey, head of credit research for Gurtin Municipal Bond Management, which manages approximately $10.1 billion in state and local debt and has deliberately avoided Illinois bonds, expressed grave concern. "We are probably approaching that point of impaired ability to function at basic level," Humphrey stated, adding, "We’ve already probably passed that point. We haven’t seen this in a modern state before." This self-inflicted fiscal crisis has left Illinois, the fifth most populous state, with a staggering $14.5 billion in unpaid bills. Essential entities such as universities and social service providers, heavily reliant on state funding, have been severely impacted. Furthermore, Illinois's standing in the bond market has been significantly undermined. If no last-minute deal emerges before midnight, it is increasingly probable that Illinois will commence its third fiscal year on July 1 without a budget.

### Legislative Stalemate and Governor's Demands

The legislative stalemate is a primary driver of the crisis. Democratic House Speaker Michael Madigan has accused Governor Rauner of "holding the budget hostage" to his agenda. Madigan announced in an emailed statement on Wednesday that Democrats would continue working on a budget and planned to hold public hearings, beginning in Chicago on June 8. Governor Rauner, in turn, criticized the Democrats, labeling the failure a "complete dereliction of duty by the majority in the General Assembly." He insists that any tax increases must be contingent upon a property tax freeze and local control over those levies. Governor Rauner, the first Republican to lead Illinois since 2003, is adamant that any spending plan must align with his core priorities, including a property tax freeze. While the Senate approved measures on Tuesday to lock in most local real estate levies for two years, Republicans argued these provisions did not go far enough.

### Bond Market Repercussions and Credit Warnings

Bond-rating agencies have issued stark warnings of further downgrades, underscoring the potential for Illinois to lose its investment-grade status. Investors have long penalized the state for its financial instability, and this penalty has intensified amid the ongoing stalemate. Illinois's 10-year bonds currently yield 4.4 percent, a premium of 2.5 percentage points over top-rated debt. This yield gap, a key indicator of perceived risk, is the widest it has been since at least January 2013 and surpasses that of any other of the 19 states tracked by Bloomberg. Moody's Investors Service reported in a March report that the current-year operating deficit stands at approximately $6 billion. Both Moody's and S&P Global Ratings have cautioned that Illinois could face further downgrades if a budget is not enacted. The agencies currently rate the state at Baa2 and BBB, respectively, which are two notches above junk status.

### Mounting Unpaid Bills and Interest Costs

The mounting backlog of unpaid bills further exacerbates the fiscal strain. The state comptroller estimates that Illinois will incur at least $800 million in interest and fees on overdue bills by the end of June. Ted Hampton, Moody's lead analyst on Illinois, described this backlog as "headed in the direction of being a factor that just by itself really threatens the sort of financial foundations of the state," citing ongoing litigation from creditors. He added, "There is kind of an uncertain but very real legal and political limit to the state’s ability to keep deferring payments."

### Impact on Education and Social Services

A delegation of school superintendents from across the state held a press conference at the Illinois State Capitol on Wednesday, advocating for a full-year budget rather than a temporary stopgap measure and calling for reforms to the school funding formula. Illinois currently owes school districts $1.1 billion, a figure described as "unconscionable" by Tony Sanders, CEO of the Elgin-based school system, the state's second-largest. The ongoing impasse is also severely impacting social service agencies that support vulnerable populations, including children, the elderly, and individuals with mental illness. Laurence Msall, president of the Civic Federation, a group that monitors the state's finances, stated at a press conference last week, "Forty-nine other states would never try this experiment." He continued, "Only Illinois, which has seen its credit rating crumble, which has seen its social service infrastructure weaken, which has seen its higher education punched into the stomach in terms of what its future looks like, would try this experiment. It’s not working. We have to have a state budget to move forward."

### Debt Obligations and Retirement System Strain

Despite the legislative gridlock, Illinois has not defaulted on any bond payments, and state law mandates continued monthly deposits into its debt-service funds. However, the protracted conflict has hindered any progress in addressing the state's retirement system, which carries over $129 billion in unfunded liabilities—a significant stressor that has contributed to its declining credit rating.

### Previous Stopgap and Current Proposals

State leaders have until the end of June to reach an agreement before the start of fiscal year 2018 on July 1. Last year, lawmakers passed a six-month stopgap budget to ensure schools remained open and state operations continued. However, achieving consensus has proven elusive. On May 23, the Illinois Senate, with only Democratic votes, approved a spending plan that included income tax increases and an expanded sales tax. As of Tuesday afternoon, this plan still required House approval. Governor Rauner has maintained his demand for a property tax freeze before considering any income tax hikes, and he rejected the two-year property tax freeze passed by the Senate, with spokeswoman Eleni Demertzis calling it "a phony two-year freeze riddled with holes" due to its exemption of Chicago and Rauner's preference for a longer duration.

As Illinois lawmakers grapple with an impending budget deadline, Governor Bruce Rauner and House Speaker Michael Madigan are pursuing divergent paths, highlighting the deep divisions in Springfield. With the fiscal year ending Friday, the state faces significant repercussions, including potential shutdowns of transportation projects and a downgrade of its bond status to "junk" if a balanced budget isn't enacted.

Governor Rauner has declared his intention to keep legislators in session until a budget is passed, stating, "If the legislature fails to send a balanced budget package to my desk by Friday, we will have no choice but to keep them in session until they get the job done." This ultimatum comes as legislators have been in a special session since June 21, initially called by Rauner for ten days to foster agreement.

Despite the ongoing negotiations, disagreements persist regarding several of Governor Rauner's key reform proposals, which he deems essential for his signature on a budget. Nevertheless, House Democrats have advanced four reform measures, with only a four-year property tax freeze failing to pass. This particular measure, which exempted home rule municipalities and was set to take immediate effect, required 71 votes for approval.

The other reform initiatives, which garnered passage with a lower threshold of 60 votes, included:

*   Workers' compensation reform
*   Pension reform
*   Local government consolidation

The passage of some of these reforms allows Speaker Madigan to assert that he has addressed Governor Rauner's budgetary demands as part of the spending plan. Democrats urged Republicans to support these measures, even if not fully aligned with their positions. State Representative Will Guzzardi (D-Chicago) implored, "Please, take a win. We are going to put bills on the board that are not 100 percent of what you want here but they’re going to be awfully, darn close." He added, "At some point in the next 72 hours, vote on bills that you can go home and say we got reform. Take a win, please. The state needs you guys to rise to the occasion right now."

Republicans, however, rejected this sentiment, arguing that negotiations are still active and that voting on these reforms prematurely disrupts the process. They contend that these reforms are not merely the governor's preferences but are crucial for restoring the state's financial health. State Representative Steve Andersson (R-Geneva) stated, "We can’t do this. We can’t vote in good conscience on this. This isn’t about not taking the win. It’s about finishing the work and then taking the win together."

Concerns were raised by Senate Democrats regarding the four-year property tax freeze, particularly its potential impact on struggling school districts. The House Democratic proposal specifically excluded Chicago Public Schools and 18 other financially challenged districts, while also omitting pension debt. House Majority Leader Barbara Flynn-Currie (D-Chicago) highlighted the extent of their concessions, noting that the property tax bill demonstrated "how far out of the way we have gone." She explained, "This is our effort to say to the governor, although we don’t think we need non-budgetary items to govern budgetary decisions, we understand that it is very important to the person who currently occupies the second floor. So we are prepared to go a good, long way toward what it is that Governor Rauner has requested in order to see to it that we can have a budget."

While the workers' compensation bill did pass, the Illinois Manufacturers’ Association expressed opposition, citing that despite some cost-cutting changes, it increased employer costs and failed to adequately address high medical treatment expenses by not altering the medical fee schedule. Legislative leaders were scheduled to reconvene on Thursday morning. Madigan's spokesman, Steve Brown, characterized the extended special session as unsurprising, asserting, "All he [Rauner] needs to do is understand that the Legislature has gone way past middle ground to compromise. We’ve been working on his reforms."

Illinois stands on the precipice of a historic financial designation, potentially becoming the first U.S. state to receive a "junk" credit rating. This ignominious distinction, as warned by the prominent rating agency S&P Global Ratings, looms if state lawmakers fail to forge a budget agreement before the commencement of the fiscal year on July 1. This marks the third consecutive year Illinois faces the prospect of operating without an approved budget.

The state is already grappling with a profound fiscal crisis, evidenced by:

*   **A staggering $15 billion in unpaid bills.** This figure represents approximately 40% of the state's operating budget, a substantial increase from previous years.
*   **A colossal $251 billion in unfunded pension liabilities**, according to estimates from Moody's. This debt obligation pertains to retired teachers and other state workers.
*   **Critically low cash reserves**, so depleted that the state may soon be unable to fulfill obligations to lottery winners.

A downgrade to "junk" status would amplify these existing financial woes. Such a rating signals heightened risk of default, a classification that has historically preceded bankruptcy for municipalities like Stockton, California, and Detroit, as well as for Puerto Rico. While Moody's notes that only a "very small" percentage of the 8,500 local governments in the U.S. currently hold below investment-grade ratings, the implications for a state are far more significant.

S&P Global Ratings has explicitly cautioned that "Illinois is now at risk of entering a negative credit spiral." This warning, issued on June 1, followed the legislature's adjournment without a budget deal, prompting both S&P and Moody's to downgrade Illinois's credit rating to just one notch above junk.

Governor Bruce Rauner has voiced his urgency, stating on Twitter, "Time is running out." He implored lawmakers to enact a compromise plan aimed at "bring[ing] real changes to our broken system." Rauner's office has not yet responded to requests for further comment.

The political deadlock has not only created the backlog of unpaid bills but also jeopardizes essential state functions. Powerball and Mega Millions ticket sales, for instance, could be suspended within Illinois if a budget resolution isn't reached by Wednesday. The overarching concern, as articulated by S&P, is that "The unrelenting political brinksmanship now poses a threat to the timely payment of the state's core priority payments." This continued fiscal instability could further erode investor confidence and increase the cost of borrowing for the state.

Illinois is on the precipice of a significant financial milestone, poised to become the first U.S. state to have its credit rating downgraded to "junk" status. This impending downgrade, warned by S&P Global Ratings, threatens to exacerbate the state's already substantial multibillion-dollar financial deficit and will likely result in increased borrowing costs for taxpayers for years to come.

The warning from S&P comes with a stark condition: the agency will likely lower Illinois' creditworthiness below investment grade if feuding lawmakers fail to agree on a state budget for a third consecutive year. This failure to reach a budget agreement directly impacts the state's ability to borrow money for essential infrastructure projects, such as building roads, and for refinancing existing debt. As the July 1 start of the new fiscal year loomed, lawmakers meeting in Springfield for a special legislative session remained deadlocked, casting a grim outlook on the possibility of a timely resolution.

Brian Battle, director at Performance Trust Capital Partners, a Chicago-based investment firm, emphasized the widespread implications of this fiscal impasse. He stated, "It isn't a political show. Everyone in Illinois has a stake in what's happening here. One day everybody will wake up and say 'What happened? Why are my taxes going up so much?'"

### Understanding the Current Situation and Potential Consequences

**Why is this happening now?**
Ratings agencies have been progressively downgrading Illinois' credit rating for several years. However, the pace of these downgrades has accelerated due to the prolonged stalemate between Republican Governor Bruce Rauner and the Democratic-controlled General Assembly. Key concerns fueling these downgrades include:

*   **Massive Pension Debt:** The state's unfunded pension obligations represent a significant financial burden.
*   **Unpaid Bills Backlog:** A substantial $15 billion backlog of unpaid bills highlights immediate fiscal pressures.
*   **Revenue Decline:** The expiration of a temporary income tax increase in 2015 led to a notable drop in state revenue.

S&P explicitly stated its concern when it dropped Illinois' rating to one level above junk status, noting, "In our view, the unrelenting political brinkmanship now poses a threat to the timely payment of the state's core priority payments." Moody's echoed this sentiment, observing, "As the regular legislative session elapsed, political barriers to progress appeared to harden, indicating both the severity of the state's challenges and the political difficulty of advocating their solutions."

**What does a 'junk' rating signify?**
A "junk" rating is analogous to a credit score, but applied to a governmental entity rather than an individual. When a state, city, or county needs to borrow money, it issues bonds. Investors assess the risk associated with purchasing these bonds based on ratings provided by agencies like Moody's and S&P. A junk rating indicates a heightened risk that the state may default on its debt obligations. Consequently, many mutual funds and individual investors, who collectively represent over half of the bond market, will refrain from purchasing these bonds. Investors willing to assume the higher risk, such as distressed debt investors, will only do so if compensated with a significantly higher interest rate. While no other U.S. state has reached this status, municipalities like Chicago, Atlantic City, and Detroit have experienced junk ratings. Notably, Detroit saw its rating restored to investment grade in 2015 after emerging from bankruptcy, a recourse unavailable to states by law.

**What are the projected costs?**
Battle anticipates that the increased interest payments Illinois will incur the next time it needs to sell bonds could amount to "tens of millions" of dollars annually, or even more. This elevated cost of borrowing directly reduces the funds available for essential public services, including education, state parks, social services, and infrastructure maintenance. "For the taxpayer, it will cost more to get a lower level of service," Battle explained. Comptroller Susana Mendoza, responsible for managing the state's finances, concurred, stating, "It's going to cost people more every day. Our reputation really can't get much worse, but our state finances can."

**Are there other impacts?**
The state's deteriorating creditworthiness is already affecting other entities that have historically relied on state funding, such as local governments and universities. S&P has already downgraded the bonds held by:

*   The Metropolitan Pier & Exposition Authority
*   The Illinois Sports Facilities Authority

These entities are responsible for managing key Chicago landmarks and venues, including Navy Pier, McCormick Place, and Guaranteed Rate Field. Furthermore, five Illinois universities have also received a junk rating:

*   Eastern Illinois University
*   Governors State University
*   Northeastern Illinois University
*   Northern Illinois University
*   Southern Illinois University

This story has been corrected to reflect that the venue where the White Sox play is now called Guaranteed Rate Field, not U.S. Cellular Field.

query: What was the decision made by the Orlando Sentinel's editors regarding the front page after the nightclub shooting?

documents.text: Click to email this to a friend (Opens in new window) Click to share on Google+ (Opens in new window) Click to share on LinkedIn (Opens in new window) Click to share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Click to share on Twitter (Opens in new window) Editors at the Orlando Sentinel had a couple of options for Monday's front page: Offer a traditional treatment with news of the death of 50 in a nightclub shooting early Sunday morning or go with full-page editorial? When Todd Stewart, senior editor for multimedia and visuals, left a late afternoon meeting, he thought the answer was a compromise that included the news and some of the editorial. But, he thought, the compromise felt a little soft. The front page some 30 hours after the nation's worst mass shooting should make a statement. "I actually never worked up the compromise." All day, people in Orlando and around the world saw images of the crime scene. "It's time for the newspaper to put a voice to what we're all feeling," said Stewart, who started working early this morning before coming into the newsroom at 7:30 a.m. For the first 10 hours of his day, no one talked about the print newspaper, he said. "That's a change, even in the last couple of years," he said. "I'm proud of that." Unlike the profusion of cable news pundits and politicians who used the shooting as a jumping-off point to make a political point or further their own agendas, the Sentinel opted for a message of unity in its editorial: Beyond offering our abundant prayers and sympathy, we must ensure that those who survive — who will forever carry the scars from the trauma — know that they are not alone today, tomorrow or in the months and years to come. Let our community define itself by our unequivocal response: United. Included in the Sentinel's ongoing work on Sunday — 30 videos and 40 stories. Avido Khahaifa, the Sentinel's editor and publisher, isn't sure there's a right or wrong decision for Monday's front page. He understands the impulse to take a traditional approach. "I just think this called for something different from us," he said. "What we're doing kind of captures what the day has felt like."

CLOSE Scores of people were killed and many more were wounded in the shooting at a gay nightclub in Orlando, Florida. Orlando police officers seen outside of Pulse nightclub after a fatal shooting and hostage situation on June 12, 2016 in Orlando, Florida. The suspect was shot and killed by police after 20 people died and 42 were injured. (Photo by Gerardo Mora/Getty Images) (Photo: Gerardo Mora, Getty Images) A night of partying and dancing at Pulse, a gay nightclub in Orlando, turned into a killing ground and three hours of terror for early-morning revelers when a gunman opened fire around 2 a.m. ET inside the club, killing 50 people and wounding at least 53 in what is being called the worst mass shooting in U.S. history. Here’a a rough timeline of the three hours from the time the gunman gained entrance to the club, Pulse Orlando, around 2 a.m. ET to the time authorities killed the assailant around 5 a.m. ET. (All times are Eastern standard time). SIEGE BEGINS: 2:02 a.m. Shots ring out as a police officer working as a security guard engages in gun battle with assailant, Omar Mateen, 29, of Port St. Lucie, Fla., outside the Pulse Orlando nightclub around closing time, according to Orlando police chief John Mina. Mateen was armed with an AR-15-style assault rifle, a handgun and an unidentified device. The gunman makes way into club, where more than 300 people were enjoying Latino night, and opens fire, causing chaos as patrons realize what is unfolding. Mateen fired an "unknown number of rounds," the police chief said. "There were numerous shots." Eyewitnesses said people on the dance floor and around the bar area dived to the floor, with some lucky enough to flee. Jackie Smith, who was inside the club and says two friends were shot, told the Associated Press after release from Orlando Regional Medical Center: "Some guy walked in and started shooting everybody. He had an automatic rifle, so nobody stood a chance. I just tried to get out of there." 2:06 a.m. Mina Justice was sound asleep when, she told the Associated Press, she got a text from her son, Eddie, from inside the club. “Mommy I love you,” the message said. “In club they shooting.” CLOSE Mina Justice's 30-year-old son Eddie texted her from the Pulse nightclub when the shooting occurred. He said he ran into a bathroom with other patrons to hide and told her to call police. He then texted her: "He's coming, I'm gonna die." AP 2:09 a.m. About seven minutes after the first shot, the Pulse Orlando nightclub's Facebook page zapped out a chilling message: "Everyone get out of pulse and keep running." 2:17 a.m. @bjoewolf (Brandon Wolf), tweets: “Omg. Shooting at pulse. We hid in the bathroom. And we can’t find our friends." Club goer Christopher Hansen who escaped the club, later told CNN, “It’s just shocking, I just saw bodies going down.” Hansen estimates that 20 or 30 or even more shots were fired. Speaking to reporters after the assault, Florida Rep. Alan Grayson, a Democrat, said: "All the killing that was done was with an assault rifle, a single weapon...it was done very quickly also." Investigators from the office of the medical examiner investigate on the west side of Pulse nightclub. (Photo: Doug Clifford, AP) MULTI-HOUR HOSTAGE SITUATION ENSUES: 2 a.m. ET to 5 a.m. ET. Law enforcement and the shooter engage in three-hour standoff, with the assailant holding hostages. Updates and pleas for help from frightened patrons, many hiding in a restroom, trapped in the club were zapped out to police, friends and the world via text messages and social media, such as Twitter. The gunman, according to the FBI, made a call to 911 during the attack and pledged his support to the Islamic State, or ISIS. Multiple emergency vehicles and law enforcement teams respond to the scene of the shooting. Bomb units were sent to the scene, as were hazardous material teams. Around 3 a.m. Orlando Fire Department calls in the bomb squad. SWAT teams were also called in. 3:58 a.m. Orlando police, via Twitter, warn public to stay away from the nightclub area. Shooting at Pulse Nightclub on S Orange. Multiple injuries. Stay away from area. pic.twitter.com/5Di2mc6XUY — Orlando Police (@OrlandoPolice) June 12, 2016 Law enforcement officials say there was "some communication" with assailant, but would not provide details. "Once the initial shots stopped we were dealing with a hostage situation," Mina said in a press conference. Orlando Mayor Buddy Dyer says authorities learned through text messages from hostages in the club that between five and eight hostages were with the shooter in a room, while 15 to 25 others were in another room elsewhere in the nightclub. SIEGE ENDS AS POLICE STORM CLUB, KILL ASSAILANT 5 a.m. About three hours after the standoff began, Orlando police chief John Mina says the decision was made to storm the club and "rescue patrons (hiding) in a bathroom" and others still trapped inside the club in an effort to minimize loss of life. The police, Mina said, used a BearCat armored rescue vehicle to "get through a club wall." Cops also set off a "controlled detonation" around this time to distract the assailant. After the BearCat rammed the club, the shooter opened the club's door, according to Mayor Dyer, and that's when cops took aim at the shooter. Gunfire was "exchanged between the SWAT team and the suspect at 0500 hours," or 5 a.m., officials said later during a press conference. Police said the gunman was killed in the firefight. “We exchanged gunfire with the suspect, and he was dead at the scene," Mina said, adding that the SWAT team's decision to go in saved the lives of "at least 30 possible victims." 5:53 a.m. About an hour later the Orlando police department confirm via Twitter that the assailant, Mateen, had been killed: “Pulse Shooting: The shooter inside the club is dead.” Pulse Shooting: The shooter inside the club is dead. — Orlando Police (@OrlandoPolice) June 12, 2016 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB Melbourne vigil for Orlando victims | 1:43 Several hundred gather in Melbourne, Florida to honor the victims of the terror attack in Orlando. Glitz Ultra Lounge in Orlando, Florida, after the dust cleared from the shooting. All photos by Sofia Valiente VICE is tracking mass shootings in America in 2016, and comparing the numbers with their European counterparts. Read our rationale for the project and the metrics we're using here. As part of this project, every so often VICE will take a deeper look at one event that, while representative of a common type of mass shooting, didn't receive widespread coverage. This month, we look back on a February 2016 mass shooting at a nightclub in Orlando, Florida, in which two people were killed and nine more wounded. The first shots barely registered with Brandon*. From where he stood by the sinks in the men's room at the Glitz Ultra Lounge in Orlando, the noise was just a low pop-pop-pop swallowed by the din of around 300 people partying to blaring reggaeton. Outside the bathroom door, witnesses heard screams and saw people rushing from deeper within the crowded dance floor to the exits. But beyond that fulcrum of action, the sounds were so soft and brief that many in the club initially brushed them off. "The DJ said, 'Oh, it's just fireworks,'" Brandon recalls. "'Keep partying!'" Situated on the corner of Universal and Carrier, near the heart of Orlando's tourist district—a mile south of Universal Studios and down the block from the kiddy-theatrical Pirate's Dinner Adventure restaurant—Glitz seemed like it was in a safe part of town. But just before 1 AM on February 7, a mass shooting touched off in the outwardly family-friendly area. After those first few shots, a second round of gunfire unleashed by at least one additional shooter slammed into the wall separating the club from the bathroom with a loud bam-bam-bam. One bullet pierced the wall, striking a young man in the lower back as Brandon instinctively dove to the ground. Outside, the bullets ripped into the throng clustered at the edge of the floor by the men's room. Maria*, a young mother with another child on the way who was waiting for her fiancé to finish up in the bathroom, suddenly felt a bullet rip through her wrist. Then she saw a man with a gun rush past. Maria was one of at least three people shot by the bathroom door in that second burst of gunfire. By this point, the crowd near the bathrooms had devolved into a scrum rushing for the exits. But it took at least a couple more minutes before the terror penetrated the rest of the venue, still dominated as it was by throbbing neon and bass. A cellphone video shot in the bathroom shows the man who was struck in the back laying on the ground, at the end of a trickle trail of blood. Someone in the background yells, "He's shot, bro!" and a security guard pushing his way in screams, "Where?" over and over, while music continues to blast away. At 12:59 AM, a security guard referred to in a police account only as "Lehman" ran to Jerome Kenon, Landon Thomas, and Manuel Genao—all off-duty-but-uniformed Orlando Police Department officers hired to sit outside and project a sense of security. Within a minute of that trio's call to police dispatch, victims started streaming out in droves. Another video from inside the club around the same time shows the lights cut and the last attendees filing out, more confused than hectic, as security personnel scream, "Everybody out!" and a man in a long coat appears to perform CPR on a body splayed near the exit by the men's room. The rear entrance to the venue As they waited for on-duty cops to arrive, Officers Genao and Thomas followed Lehman around the exterior of the club to the exit by the bathroom. Witnesses there told them that a man with a gun had just run out of the building through that door and turned the corner toward the club's rear exit. When they went around back, the group found 23-year-old Jose Jaime Brull-Lopez holding a Glock with an extended magazine, his shirt covered in blood. He stood over the body of 22-year-old Joseph Lugo Villalobos. Once he spotted the off-duty officers and Lehman, Brull-Lopez tossed the gun onto the roof of a storage shed attached to the rear of the club. The officers then ordered him to the ground at gunpoint. Soon after, on-duty Orlando cops arrived and found another loaded magazine in the suspect's pocket. They eventually booked Brull-Lopez into custody on charges of carrying a concealed firearm and tampering with physical evidence, but initially declined to charge him for the shooting itself. Brull-Lopez, for his part, at first tried to deny that he'd been inside the club or had a gun. But he eventually admitted to tossing the Glock in a police statement. He maintained he'd never fired the gun, which he said he brought into the club for protection and pulled near the men's room because he was aware of a "beef" between people inside. He went on to claim Villalobos was his friend (although he could not think of his surname without police prompting), that the wounded man had run to him for help, and that his shirt had been bloodied while helping Villalobos to the ground. Brull-Lopez threw his Glock away, he said, because he'd acquired it illegally. On February 18, he was arrested a second time—after bailing out on his initial charges—on charges of shooting at, within, or into a building and using a firearm or weapon in the commitment of a felony, suggesting that the police now believe he was one of the shooters. In the end, the tragedy left nine people with bullet wounds and Villalobos, along with 33-year-old Jonathan Avila Rojas, dead. Local police were unsure how many shooters took part in the incident, but as of the following Monday, they suspected as many as three gunmen were involved. Stacked chairs and costume party detritus left at Glitz Four months later, the Orlando Police investigation into the Glitz shooting is still open. As such, cops have been reluctant to share names of victims and have declined to comment on the case beyond a few scant official statements. Some survivors are choosing to remain anonymous for reasons of privacy, others for fear of the shooters, one or two of whom may remain at large. Hopes that security or management from Glitz might be able to clear up what happened have also been dashed, as the club was shuttered immediately after the incident and management and official employees have been unreachable ever since. "If I asked you to search me, you probably wouldn't reach around my junk."—Robert C. Smith, nightclub security expert Despite those roadblocks, conversations with victims, witnesses, people who worked in the club, and security experts offer insight into how a massive shooting like this could happen at a venue with an off-duty police detail and a significant number of guards. Which in turn sheds light on the shocking commonality of mass shootings in clubs and other nightspots across America.
