Within minutes of the off-duty cops' call to dispatch, a number of police arrived and rapidly secured the chaotic scene. By 1:25 AM, they started releasing vehicles, and less than 20 minutes later, Universal Boulevard had been reopened to traffic. By about 2 AM, most of the wounded had either been found by first-responders or made their own way to local hospitals. A grand total of 113 police units stayed on site or in the hospital with victims, trying to figure out what had happened, on and off throughout the following day. Within 24 hours, a preliminary investigation suggested to police that the violence had been the result of a conflict between two groups of Hispanic men. By Friday, February 12, Orlando cops were saying Rojas and Villalobos had been members of the two conflicting sides, along with three of the wounded. Social media reactions on the night of the shooting and interviews with employees at businesses near the club suggest the incident may have been part of a drug-trafficking conflict between Puerto Rican gangs. Local police have not endorsed that theory, and only obliquely reference gang reactions to the attack in Brull-Lopez's arrest report. But there's a fair amount of evidence supporting the gang hypothesis. For starters, Rojas and Villalobos, as well as Brull Lopez and 21-year-old Luis Gadiel Cruz-Nazario—who was also arrested in connection with the shooting on February 18—were originally from Puerto Rico, and although they hadn't been arrested for serious crimes in the United States, Rojas and Villalobos were under investigation by Puerto Rican authorities for drug trafficking. Rojas, also known as "Pescue" and described by some in Orlando as a veritable kingpin, had also been pegged by Puerto Rican cops as a person of interest in at least four murders, and he was suspected of violating local gun laws on the island. Reports in Puerto Rican news outlets also linked him to the island's Alkaedas drug ring. For his part, Brull-Lopez doesn't appear to have clear gang affiliations, but his statement to the police on the night of the shooting does suggest he was aware of the "beef" between the alleged gang elements in the club. These details might shed a bit of light on the nature of the conflict inside Glitz that night, but they don't explain how the suspects were able to get the guns they used inside the club. According to a police spokeswoman addressing the press on the day of the shooting, the club wasn't just in an apparently safe touristy part of town—it had an "extensive security system." Indeed, people who worked in the venue say at least a dozen guards were on duty any given night, a couple of them tasked with thoroughly patting down everyone coming in for weapons. The police spokeswoman insisted at the time that "the club really did everything that it's supposed to do," and cops later stressed to local reporters that this was an isolated event. The parking lot at the Glitz Ultra Lounge Some experts I spoke to think the local police simply overstated the quality of Glitz's security protocols, either accidentally, or in an effort to assuage fears that might harm Orlando's vital tourism sector. "Small mistakes do happen," offers Robert C. Smith, an ex-cop and head of San Diego's Nightclub Security Consultants, the only American firm exclusively focused on bouncer training at the national level. Smith was chairperson of the committee that wrote California's relevant security curriculum; he also flew out to Florida in February to touch base with local clients and contacts, and boned up on the Glitz shooting before leaving town. "Allowing an underage person to slip by you happens," he tells me. "Serving someone excessively in a crowd of thousands of people every night—that happens. But allowing not [a] gun, but guns to come in, allowing people to die, that's not just a fluke of nature." When I visit in late March, it's easy to suspect Glitz was never up to snuff. A squat white building with blacked-out windows and a tattered red carpet under its black awning, perched on the edge of a strip-mall parking lot, the place doesn't exude a feeling of safety. It looks like what it is: a failed eatery—the club used to be the Volcano Bar & Grill Mexican Restaurant—sitting on a busy intersection forgotten by through traffic, reborn with an extra dose of neon. Employees in the decrepit strip mall regarded Glitz, which used to open long after they closed for the day, with mild suspicion. The receptionist at the Palm Construction School, the closest business to the club, says she assumed it was abandoned until the morning she found police cars outside. Three employees of the nearby Pirate's Dinner Adventure, along with a barber from the strip mall, cite rumors of crime and intrigue, but admit they never visited the club themselves. Rather than attracting locals, sources in the local club world say Glitz drew different crowds almost every night from all over greater Orlando—people who followed DJs or performers, rather than venue loyalists. Lawyers hired by Maria and two others wounded in the attacks—they're filing two separate civil lawsuits against the club for negligence—suspect its operators were irresponsible and failed to institute or enforce security measures proportionate to the threat level. Opened in 2013 by Setay McKnight of Apopka, a suburb north of Orlando, the club was run by DiMaggio's Ultra Lounge, an LLC registered to McKnight's home address in 2011. Whereas many of the businesses in the expensive tourist district were owned by hard-hitters in the Orlando real estate world, McKnight doesn't appear to own any other business ventures. Judging by a résumé posted to the job site Indeed, almost from the start, he left the club under the management of Arvind Kalipersaud—a 26-year-old man who'd previously worked retail and appears, according to voter registration records, to have lived at McKnight's home. There is no record of code violations or serious complaints for Glitz. But Thomas "T. C." Roberts, one of the lawyers representing Maria, says he found an eviction notice on the venue's door soon after the shooting indicating the owners owed $82,663 in unpaid rent. John Phillips, Maria's other lawyer, suspects the club was operating without insurance, citing the disappearance of McKnight and co. The attorney hopes to flush them out as the case grinds on, but based on past experience, Smith imagines some sort of settlement could be reached even if the operators don't make it to court in the flesh. In the year prior to the attack, police responded to 54 calls at Glitz for everything from fights to car burglaries. Two of these calls were for shootings in the club's parking lot: In June 2015 , a shooting injured one there; that October , another shooting injured two more. According to Smith, who cites a photo one of his trainees showed him, at least one bouncer took to wearing a Kevlar vest at the venue. And a person who worked in the club during events on and off for over two years tells me that for a while, at least, Glitz was full of people sporting gang colors and flashing signs. The club apparently cracked down on gang symbols after complaints and upped the number of security guards and cameras in the space following a large scuffle on the floor. But the event worker says many of the same gang-affiliated patrons kept coming back. Other regulars allege that, even if the venue had a large number of bouncers, their screenings were inconsistent at best. Maria says she and her fiancé were both patted down on the night of the shooting, but adds that it was a loose and quick affair that would have been easy enough to sneak something through. One witness told local reporters that while Glitz security was performing pat-downs, they never checked anyone's ankles. Another patron told Phillips that not everyone underwent even such a mediocre search. And Brull-Lopez said in his statement to police that he was able to get his handgun and spare clip into the club because he was neither patted down nor checked with a metal detector wand, as other patrons were. Brandon, who recalls that a few weeks before the shooting he saw someone packing a gun during a fight at Glitz, thinks the bouncers were allowing guns to make it in—which Smith says isn't entirely uncommon for nightlife security in general thanks to the natural temptations of bribery and friendship. Despite Brandon's suspicions, corruption isn't the only way to explain guns making it inside—the possibility of poor training and execution looms. No one involved with the club could connect me to a bouncer there, and none of the lawyers pursuing suits against Glitz are clear on whether the club employed a security firm or retained its own security in-house. But it's legal in Florida to just post on Facebook looking for people to post up at a venue door for, say, ten bucks an hour, $15 if they have a security certificate. And even those trained by dedicated firms might miss things from time to time. "If I asked you to search me, you probably wouldn't reach around my junk," Smith says. He's done this exercise with his students before—and managed to sneak cutlery past them because they don't want to get up in his crotch with their hands. (This is where Brull-Lopez told police he hid the extended clip later found in his pocket.) Check out our documentary about gun culture in the Sunshine State. &amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;lt;span id="selection-marker-1" class="redactor-selection-marker"&amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;gt;&amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;lt;/span&amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;gt; There's no real metric for measuring violence in American nightclubs, but the Glitz attack was just one of three mass shootings at US night spots that week alone. That doesn't mean club mass shootings are about to become an everyday occurrence. Nightclub violence writ large is still relatively uncommon, and when it does occur, it's usually an overzealous bouncer roughing someone up, a shooting in the parking lot beyond security's reach, or a fight without lethal weapons. And these tragedies tend to cluster on weekends. But the fact that America can witness three such incidents within a week is alarming enough in its own right. Smith doesn't buy into the fatalistic notion that this type of violence is inevitable, though. He believes we can reverse this trend—without undertaking Sisyphean gun control efforts. Citing the example of a club in a rough part of Washington, DC, that operated for eight years with nary a stabbing or shooting inside, he claims that with the right level of professionalization, training, and oversight, security guards can keep every weapon out of a dedicated venue. He notes that three (soon-to-be-four) states now require that bouncers be licensed, some with club-context-specific training—a regimen that owners and insurers are starting to favor out of concern for the effect of violence on their bottom lines. If enough states force clubs to hire sufficiently professionalized guards—people whose jobs depend on their diligence—then venues with metal detectors and comprehensive pat-downs (the protocols Orlando Police say they would have recommended if Glitz reopened) might be able to stop everyone from random hotheads to organized gangs from wreaking havoc. Of course, such measures could incur expenses that force bars operating on narrow margins to close up shop. They might also simply serve to push violence between gangs to other venues. But given the sheer number of mass shootings American clubs suffer each year, and the role lax security plays in at least some of these attacks, a new wave of regulation is long overdue. "If you know that's your crowd, I don't give a fuck if it's Crips and Bloods, Hell's Angels, or Mongols, doesn't matter to me," Smith says. "If you handle your door, no guns are coming in." * Citing safety concerns or a desire for privacy, several individuals in this story have been granted pseudonyms. Follow Mark Hay on Twitter.

On Saturday night, Brandon Wolf found himself at the Pulse Orlando nightclub, a decision he initially hesitated about but was persuaded to make by a friend. The club, buzzing with energy on Latin Night, was filled with people dancing and socializing. As closing time approached, Wolf and a friend were in the bathroom when the first gunshots rang out – perhaps 20 in total. The sounds grew closer, forcing them to flee.

At 2:02 a.m., an off-duty Orlando police officer working security at the club heard the initial gunshots. Omar Mateen, armed with an AR-15, a handgun, and an explosive device, was outside. The officer engaged Mateen, and two more officers soon joined the confrontation, opening fire. Mateen retaliated, then retreated into the Pulse nightclub, trapping dozens inside.

By 2:09 a.m., a message appeared on Pulse Orlando's Facebook page: "Everyone get out of pulse and keep running." Inside, Wolf and his friend remained hidden in the bathroom, the relentless gunfire a terrifying soundtrack. "All I heard was gunfire after gunfire," Wolf recounted. "Eventually, I thought you were supposed to run out of ammunition. But it just kept going and going." Ivory Mcneal, in the club's patio area, described the scene of panic: "People were running everywhere. Gunshots were going off. People were ducking." For the next three hours, over 100 law enforcement officers converged on the scene, assessing the unfolding tragedy while people inside made desperate 911 calls from hiding.

At 5 a.m., police initiated their entry strategy. An armored vehicle breached a wall, creating an opening. Two Distraction Devices were deployed, their loud bangs and bright lights intended to disorient. Mateen fired at the approaching officers; one bullet struck an officer's Kevlar helmet, a detail that likely saved his life. The officers returned fire, and Mateen was killed. Thirty individuals were rescued alive from the club. Tragically, 39 other people were found dead inside, with an additional two fatalities occurring outside the venue.

Shortly after 5 a.m., Brandon Wolf found himself in a 7-11 parking lot, having escaped with the friend he was with in the bathroom. A third friend was seen being taken away on a gurney, injured. However, their fourth companion, Drew Leinonen, remained missing. Wolf contacted Drew's mother, Christine Leinonen, who immediately drove from Lakeland. Upon arriving at the parking lot, she and Wolf shared a tearful embrace.

By 7 a.m., at a press conference, Orlando Police Chief John Mina confirmed that at least 20 people had been killed and 42 hospitalized. Florida Department of Law Enforcement Special Agent in Charge Danny Banks characterized the shooting as an act of terrorism, suggesting potential motivation from Islamic extremism. Orlando Mayor Buddy Dyer described the event as a "horrific crime" that would leave a lasting impact on the community.

By 10:30 a.m., officials confirmed the grim toll: 50 people dead and 53 injured. Mayor Dyer stated the immediate focus was on victim identification and family notification. Imam Tariq Rasheed of the Islamic Center of Orlando condemned the attack, emphasizing that no religious tradition could justify such violence.

At 1:45 p.m., President Barack Obama addressed the nation, labeling the attack as an act of terror and hate, and acknowledging the particular heartbreak for the LGBTQ+ community targeted at a place of gathering. U.S. Senator Bill Nelson later reported that the Islamic State had claimed responsibility.

Throughout the day, the City of Orlando began releasing the names of the victims. By 3:39 p.m., the first four were identified: Edward Sotomayor Jr., 34; Stanley Almodovar III, 23; Luis Omar Ocasio-Capo, 20; and Juan Ramon Guerrero, 22. By 5:20 p.m., two more were identified: Eric Ivan Ortiz-Rivera, 36, and Peter O. Gonzalez-Cruz, 22. The following day, by 6:20 p.m., Luis S. Vielma, 22, was identified as the seventh victim, and vigils began across Central Florida. Kimberly Morris, 37, was identified as the eighth victim by 9:36 p.m.

In the early hours of Monday morning, two more victims were identified: Eddie Jamoldroy Justice, 30, and Darryl Roman Burt II, 29. By 7:30 a.m., nine additional victims were identified: Amanda Alvear, 25; Martin Benitez Torres, 33; Luis Daniel Wilson-Leon, 37; Mercedez Marisol Flores, 26; Xavier Emmanuel Serrano Rosado, 35; Gilberto Ramon Silva Menendez, 25; Simon Adrian Carrillo Fernandez, 31; Oscar A Aracena-Montero, 26; and Enrique L. Rios, Jr., 25.

During a press conference at 7:30 a.m. on Monday, officials clarified that all victims had been removed from the scene by approximately 11 p.m. the previous night, with the extensive crime scene posing challenges. They confirmed a total of 49 victims, plus the shooter, resulting in 50 fatalities. By 8 a.m., Javier Jorge-Reyes, 40, and Miguel Angel Honorato, 30, were identified as two more victims.

The Orlando Sentinel's front page for Monday's edition was carefully designed to reflect the widespread feelings shared on Sunday after the tragic shooting at the Pulse nightclub. A deep sadness was evident in conversations, recognizing Orlando's sad place among devastating mass shootings in the United States. Yet, a different message grew stronger as the day went on: a firm belief that this terrible event would not shape the community's lasting memory.

This feeling was strongly captured in the front page message: "Let our community define itself by our unequivocal response: United." This statement, along with a touching picture of two people holding each other sadly, led eight more pages of detailed reporting in the newspaper. This thorough coverage included information about the victims whose stories were known before the print deadline.

Changing the usual front page style during a tragedy is not new, and sadly, the Orlando Sentinel expects it might not be the last newspaper to do this. The goal is that this editorial choice clearly shows the deep feelings and shared spirit felt by the community and the newspaper's staff during this hard time. John Cutter, the managing editor of the Orlando Sentinel, guided this powerful presentation.

The Pulse Orlando nightclub shooting, a devastating act of violence that occurred on June 12, 2016, remains the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history. This tragedy unfolded at Pulse, a cherished sanctuary for the LGBTQ+ community, where 49 lives were tragically lost and many more were critically injured. The perpetrator, Omar Mateen, a 29-year-old security guard, carried out this horrific assault.

Mateen, an American citizen residing in Fort Pierce, Florida, and employed by G4S, reportedly made a 911 call during the attack, pledging allegiance to the Islamic State. While the extremist group's radio outlet, Al-Bayan Radio, broadcasted a statement attributing the assault to "soldiers of the caliphate in America," the Islamic State did not officially claim responsibility. It is believed this broadcast was an opportunistic statement rather than a pre-planned endorsement.

The attack began in the early hours of a Sunday morning, shattering an evening of dancing and revelry at Pulse, a venue known for its welcoming and inclusive atmosphere. Armed with an AR-15 rifle, Mateen unleashed a torrent of gunfire. A subsequent gunfight with SWAT officers ultimately resulted in Mateen's death, bringing the total fatalities, including the attacker, to 50.

In the immediate aftermath, a somber procession commenced as medical personnel, including those from the Orlando Medical Examiner's Office, worked diligently to identify the victims. By late Sunday, all 49 victims had been identified. Orlando Mayor Buddy Dyer confirmed that 39 individuals died at the club, while 11 succumbed to their injuries at local hospitals. By Monday morning, families of 24 victims had been notified.

Witness accounts paint a harrowing picture of the terror experienced that night. Jon Alamo, a Pulse patron, described the gunman's actions as akin to a Marine firing indiscriminately, expressing his profound fear of dying. Eddie Justice, another patron, sent a series of heartbreaking text messages to his mother, Mina, including: "Mommy I love you. In club they shooting," followed by, "He's coming. I'm gonna die."

The investigation into Mateen's background uncovered several concerning details. He had been interviewed twice by the FBI in 2013 due to inflammatory comments made to co-workers, though these interviews were deemed inconclusive. In 2014, officials noted minimal contact with an American suicide bomber, which at the time did not constitute a threat. FBI agent Ronald Hopper indicated "suggestions that individual has leanings towards" radical Islamic terrorism. Trevor Velinor of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms confirmed that Mateen legally purchased at least two firearms in the week preceding the attack.

Mateen's father, Mir Seddique, speaking to NBC News, recalled his son becoming angry after witnessing two men kissing in Miami, suggesting this might have been a contributing factor to the assault. He expressed shock and offered apologies for the incident.

The incident prompted a strong response from President Barack Obama, who characterized the shooting as an "act of terror" and an "act of hate" targeting a place of "solidarity and empowerment." He urged Americans to reflect on the kind of country they wished to be.

In a separate, concerning development, an Indiana man was arrested in Southern California on the same Sunday, armed with assault rifles and bomb-making chemicals, reportedly en route to a West Hollywood gay pride parade.

The investigation, led by entities including the Florida Department of Law Enforcement, with agent Danny Banks involved, aimed to determine if Mateen acted alone. Orange County Sheriff Jerry Demings classified the event as a "domestic terror incident."

Pulse nightclub itself posted updates on its Facebook page during the ordeal, urging patrons to evacuate and later requesting prayers and support. The club's final update on Monday morning expressed gratitude for the outpouring of thoughts and love as they navigated the tragic event.

The previous deadliest mass shooting in the U.S. occurred in 2007 at Virginia Tech, where a student killed 32 people before taking his own life. Mateen's family originated from Afghanistan, and he was born in New York before the family relocated to Florida.

Associated Press writers Eric Tucker, Terrance Harris, Mike Schneider, and Tamara Lush, along with photographer Alan Diaz, contributed to this report.

Orlando Police Chief John Mina revealed new details on Monday regarding the gunman's demeanor during a critical phone call with negotiators. The individual responsible for the tragic attack at Orlando's Pulse nightclub, which resulted in the deaths of fifty people, including himself, and left over fifty others wounded, reportedly remained "cool and calm" throughout the exchange. This revelation comes as authorities continue to investigate potential motives behind the devastating incident.

During an early-morning press conference, Chief Mina elaborated on the events leading up to the SWAT team's intervention. He explained that Omar Mateen, the gunman, had barricaded himself in a bathroom with hostages following an initial exchange of gunfire with responding officers. Mina described Mateen's communication with negotiators as surprisingly composed, stating, "He really wasn't asking for a whole lot. We were doing most of the asking." This account, shared by the Orlando Police Department, offers a glimpse into the psychological state of the perpetrator during the tense standoff.

Facebook's Safety Check feature was activated following the tragic events at an Orlando nightclub early Sunday morning. This marked the inaugural deployment of the service within the United States. The devastating incident involved a gunman who opened fire at the nightclub, resulting in the deaths of at least 50 individuals. President Barack Obama characterized the event as an "act of terrorism" and confirmed it was under investigation as such.

Launched in 2014, Facebook's Safety Check tool is designed to assist users situated near a disaster. It facilitates the notification of family and friends regarding their well-being. The system prompts users identified as being within the affected zone to confirm their safety. Subsequently, with a simple click, they can disseminate this safety status to their network of friends.

Facebook has recently undertaken enhancements to streamline and expedite the activation process for this crucial tool. Concurrently, the company has been exploring methods to empower users to identify and highlight emergent local crises. For instance, the deployment of Safety Check has been automated. This ensures that trained teams, operating across different time zones, can initiate the tool's activation at any moment, eliminating the dependency on a single engineer.

A spokesperson for the social networking giant stated on Sunday, "Following the community-generated Safety Check activation this morning in Orlando, we have now activated Facebook-initiated Safety Check for a mass shooting at a nightclub there. We hope the people in the area find the tool a helpful way to let their friends and family know they are okay."

During the period spanning January to May of the current year, Facebook activated Safety Check on 17 separate occasions. This contrasts with the 11 instances recorded across the preceding two years. Recent disasters that prompted the tool's activation encompass significant events such as earthquakes in Ecuador, a destructive wildfire in Alberta, Canada, a powerful cyclone in Bangladesh, and severe flooding and landslides in Sri Lanka.

Facebook has expanded the scope of disasters for which Safety Check can be activated. This broadened list now explicitly includes terror attacks. The initial inclusion of terror attacks occurred following the events in Paris in November of the previous year. The company's decision to utilize the tool in response to a terror attack was influenced by observations that "Facebook became a place where people were sharing information and looking to understand the condition of their loved ones," as detailed in a company post at that time. Prior to this significant shift, the Safety Check tool was exclusively activated for natural disasters.

query: What has contributed to the shift in attitudes towards pit bulls and breed-specific legislation over the past few decades?

documents.text: KANSAS CITY, Mo. (AP) — For much of the past three decades, pit bulls have been widely regarded as America's most dangerous dog — the favorite breed of thugs, drug dealers and dog-fighting rings, with a fearsome reputation for unprovoked, sometimes deadly attacks. Hostility toward "pits" grew so intense that some cities began treating them as the canine equivalent of assault rifles and prohibited residents from owning them. But attitudes have softened considerably since then as animal activists and even television shows cast the dogs in a more positive light. The image makeover has prompted many states to pass new laws that forbid communities from banning specific breeds. And it illustrates the power and persistence of dog-advocacy groups that have worked to fend off pit bull restrictions with much the same zeal as gun-rights groups have defeated gun-control measures. "Lawmakers are realizing that targeting dogs based on their breed or what they look like is not a solution to dealing with dangerous dogs," said Lisa Peterson, a spokeswoman for the American Kennel Club. Seventeen states now have laws that prohibit communities from adopting breed-specific bans. Lawmakers in six more states are considering similar measures, and some cities are reviewing local policies that classify pit bulls as dangerous animals. Pit bull advocates hail the changes as recognition that breed-specific laws discriminate against dogs that are not inherently aggressive or dangerous unless they are made to be that way by irresponsible owners. The dogs' foes complain that their message is being drowned out by a well-funded, well-organized lobbying effort in state capitols. The debate puts millions of pit bull owners up against a relatively small number of people who have been victimized by the dogs. Ron Hicks, who sponsored a bill in the Missouri House to forbid breed-specific legislation, said he was surprised when nobody spoke against his proposal last month at a committee hearing. "I figured a few parents would be there who would bring tears to my eyes," the Republican said. "Would it have changed my opinion or what I believe in? No." A version of Hicks' legislation was endorsed by a House committee last month and needs to clear another committee before a full House vote. The state Senate is considering a comparable bill, as are lawmakers in Utah, South Dakota, Washington, Vermont and Maryland. In Kansas, the communities of Bonner Springs and Garden City repealed their pit-bull bans earlier this year. Summer Freeman did not know there was a ban when she moved to Bonner Springs last year after a divorce. She panicked when an animal-control officer discovered her pet and told her she had 15 days to get rid of the dog named Titan or move out of town. "I think of him like my son," she said. "He's my dog-son, I guess you could say. He's at my hip all the time. He's just a big baby that wouldn't hurt a fly." Freeman was forced to leave Titan at a shelter in Lawrence for nine months until she successfully fought to overturn the law in January. For dog owners and pit bull opponents alike, the battle is as deeply personal as any gun-control or religious issue. Each side accuses the other of lying, exploiting emotions and using bullying tactics. Pit bull owners insist their dogs are harmless, loving family members that shouldn't be blamed for something they didn't do. To opponents, they are a volatile breed whose genetics drive them to kill more than two dozen people in the U.S. each year, many of them young children. Popular television shows such as "Pit Boss" and "Pit Bulls and Parolees" on Animal Planet glorify the animals and minimize the tragedies that occur when pit bulls turn on humans, pit bull opponents say. "Everything is telling us these animals are safe if you raise them right," said Jeff Borchardt, an East Troy, Wis., man whose 14-month-old son was mauled to death a year ago by two pit bulls that tore the child from the arms of their owner, who was baby-sitting. "My son's dead because of a lie, because of a myth. My life will never be the same." The two dogs that killed Borchardt's son had lived with their owner since soon after they were born, were well-cared for and had no history of aggressive behavior, he said. Both had been spayed or neutered. That contradicts the contention that only mistreated, neglected or abused pit bulls attack people. Colleen Lynn, founder of DogsBite.org, pointed to a friend-of-the-court brief her organization submitted in a 2012 case in which the Maryland Court of Appeals declared pit bulls "inherently dangerous." "Appellate courts agree with us. Doctors and surgeons agree with us. That is credibility right there," Lynn said. "We also have the support of three divisions of the U.S. military, huge, massive bodies in the U.S. government." The Marines, Army and Air Force all have banned dangerous dogs — including pit bulls and rottweilers — from their bases because of the "unreasonable risk" they pose to safety, Lynn said. On the other side stand the American Bar Association and National Animal Control Association, which oppose breed-specific laws because they are discriminatory against a type of dog that isn't really a single breed. Three main breeds — Staffordshire bull terrier, American pit bull terrier and American Staffordshire terrier — along with mixes of those breeds are generally considered pit bulls. But many muscular, square-jawed, boxer-type dogs often are misidentified as pit bulls, making breed-specific bans hard to enforce. And because fatal pit bull attacks are a rarity compared with other causes of death such as auto accidents, dog advocates argue that breed-specific bans amount to legislative overkill. "All communities deserve comprehensive dog laws that demand responsible dog ownership and that hold reckless owners accountable when their poor decisions wind up getting other dogs or other people hurt," said Ledy Vankavage, a top lobbyist for the Best Friends Animal Society. Don Bauermeister, assistant city attorney for Council Bluffs, Iowa, led the effort to pass a local pit bull ban that took effect in 2005. He recalled first reading about the issue in the July 27, 1987, issue of Sports Illustrated, which carried a full-cover shot of an angry pit bull baring its teeth. Across the top, it said "BEWARE OF THIS DOG." After the Council Bluffs ban went into place, the number of pit bull attacks that resulted in hospitalization plummeted from 29 in 2004 to zero the past few years — proof, Bauermeister said, that breed-specific bans work. The opposition to pit bull bans, he added, is a sign that many American pet owners have lost touch with reality. "Fifty years ago, you could take a sick animal behind a barn and put it out of its misery," he said. "That's just the way it was done. Now they would investigate you for doing that. The emotional irrationality of Americans and their dogs has never been worse than it is today."

Breed-specific legislation (BSL), a policy targeting certain dog breeds for ownership restrictions or bans, remains a contentious issue in public safety and animal welfare. These laws, which can include outright bans, mandatory spay-neuter requirements, specialized licensing, increased insurance costs, or specific confinement and leash rules, often lead to the euthanasia of many pets.

Leading animal welfare and veterinary organizations, such as the American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA), the ASPCA, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and the Humane Society of the United States, strongly oppose BSL. This position is widely supported by numerous state and local veterinary and humane groups. Scientific evidence does not substantiate the claim that specific breeds are inherently more dangerous. Extensive studies on dog bites, including a comprehensive AVMA survey, have failed to identify any breed as being disproportionately prone to biting humans. Research into fatal dog bites has consistently pointed to multiple contributing factors, none of which are exclusively breed-specific.

Moreover, BSL has not demonstrated effectiveness in reducing dog bites. Analyses indicate that even if high-risk breeds could be accurately identified, the sheer number of dogs that would need to be removed to prevent a single serious injury would be prohibitively large. Real-world data corroborates this. For instance, Denver, Colorado, has reported an increase in dog bite hospitalizations following the implementation of a breed ban. Similarly, the Netherlands repealed its breed ban after a study concluded it was ineffective, and Ontario, Canada's breed ban failed to significantly decrease dog bites. Experts emphasize that the most effective approach to reducing dog bites involves promoting responsible pet ownership and implementing breed-neutral safety measures. These include comprehensive education on dog behavior, consistent training and socialization, and strict enforcement of leash laws, as advocated by organizations like the American Kennel Club (AKC).

**Why Breed-Specific Legislation Fails to Prevent Dog Bites: A Focus on Responsible Ownership and True Risk Factors**

Breed-specific legislation (BSL), which targets dog ownership based solely on breed, is an ineffective and misguided approach to public safety, according to leading animal welfare organizations. The Humane Society of the United States (HSUS) and the American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA) both oppose BSL, asserting that it does not enhance safety, reduce dog bite incidents, or protect communities. Instead, these laws are costly to enforce, harm families and their canine companions, and distract from more effective strategies for humane dog management. Research from organizations like the National Canine Research Council (NCRC) further supports the ineffectiveness of BSL, highlighting that no scientific data supports the claim that any particular breed is inherently more prone to biting.

The true risk factors for dog bites are complex and multifactorial, extending far beyond breed. These include hormonal influences, with unneutered dogs statistically more likely to exhibit aggressive behaviors. A dog's quality of life, including isolation or confinement, can lead to frustration or fear-based aggression, as noted by the ASPCA. Furthermore, a dog's upbringing, socialization from a young age, and consistent training are crucial. Individual personality plays a significant role, as each dog possesses unique temperaments. Crucially, human recognition of warning signs, such as subtle body language or overt growls, is vital in preventing bites. When these warnings are ignored, a dog may feel compelled to bite.

Effective dog bite prevention strategies should focus on understanding and minimizing these real risk factors, rather than on breed bans. Approximately 80 million dogs reside in American households, and proactive solutions are imperative. These strategies, supported by stakeholders and lawmakers, should include ensuring all dog owners have access to essential resources such as affordable veterinary care, spaying and neutering services, professional training, and comprehensive information on socialization and health. The AVMA emphasizes that focusing on responsible ownership and addressing individual dog behavior, rather than breed, is the most effective path to reducing bite incidents and promoting public safety.

The White House officially opposes Breed-Specific Legislation (BSL) at the federal level in the United States, recognizing it as an ineffective and resource-draining policy. This stance aligns with extensive research, including a comprehensive review by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

In 2000, the CDC analyzed two decades of data on dog bites and human fatalities across the U.S. Their findings indicated that:

*   Fatal attacks constitute a minuscule fraction of overall dog bite incidents.
*   Accurately calculating bite rates for specific breeds is practically impossible.
*   Individuals intent on exploiting dogs are not deterred by breed regulations; they simply shift to unregulated breeds when bans are enacted.
*   Any dog, regardless of breed, can exhibit dangerous behavior if intentionally or unintentionally trained for aggression.

Based on this evidence, the CDC officially recommends against breed-specific legislation, deeming it an "inappropriate" approach. Further details can be found in their published reports.

Instead of breed-specific policies, the CDC advocates for a community-centered strategy to prevent dog bites. This approach is considered a more effective pathway to fostering safer environments for both pets and their owners. The White House agrees that such community-based initiatives offer a more promising future for building stronger relationships between pets and the people who care for them. We encourage your continued feedback on this response and the We the People platform.

Janelle Holland rescued her pit bull Triumph (she calls him "T-Diddy") nine years ago. He was found at age 2 and had been used as a fighting dog before Janelle got him. Janelle says the dog is great with people and very friendly. (Photo: Steve Hebert for USA TODAY) Three decades after officials in more than 700 cities throughout the country began passing bans and other restrictions to keep pit bulls out of their communities, state and local governments are increasingly reconsidering their approach to what not so long ago was America's most vilified pet. Since June, at least nine communities in the Midwest have overturned pit bull bans that were on the books. Last week, Hallsville, Mo., became the latest to lift its ban after a family successfully appealed to the City Council for a change in law when it learned the family dog was a pit bull mix. Over the past two years, more than 100 municipalities across the USA have overturned bans and other restrictions that target dogs in the pit bull family, the generic term commonly used to describe the American pit bull terrier, Staffordshire bull terrier and many mixed-breed dogs with square-shaped heads and bulky builds. More communities could soon follow suit. The unified government of Wyandotte County and Kansas City, Kan., is considering lifting a pit bull ban that has been on the books for nearly a quarter-century, as part of a comprehensive overhaul of its animal control policies. The push in Kansas City (pop. 148,000) comes as Roeland Park, Kan. (pop. 6,800) recently began reviewing its ban on pit bulls. The nearby community of Bonner Springs announced this year that it was lifting its ban. Advocates argue the bans have been ineffective in reducing dog bites and led to millions of dogs being euthanized. They say too often animal control officials, law enforcement and the media misidentify offending dogs as pit bulls. "The only ones that are being affected by these bans are responsible dog owners," said Janelle Holland, a pit bull owner who was forced to leave Roeland Park more than a decade ago after learning she was violating the ban. There's been action on the statewide level as well. This year, South Dakota and Utah joined 17 other states in passing laws to prevent local governments passing "breed-specific legislation," or BSL, making it illegal for cities to pass bans targeting pit bulls or any other breed. (The South Dakota law went into effect in July, and Utah's prohibition on pit bull bans will be law on New Year's Day.) Breed-specific legislation began spreading in communities throughout the country in the mid-1980s after a surge in fatal dog bitings, including a disproportionate number of incidents initially attributed to pit bull-type dogs. The pit bull was popular in illegal dogfighting rings, and the breed developed a reputation as a favorite accessory of drug dealers and gangsters. This month, residents in the Denver suburb of Aurora, Colo., voted by a 2-to-1 ratio in a referendum to keep their pit bull ban on the books. The Aurora vote follows a vote in 2012 in Miami-Dade County, where voters opted to keep the ban by a similarly wide ratio. Fourteen-month-old Daxton Borchardt was fatally mauled last year by two pit bulls. (Photo: Jeff Borchardt) Jeff Borchardt, an East Troy, Wis.-man whose 14-month-old son, Daxton, was fatally mauled last year by two pit bulls while being cared for by a babysitter, says government leaders should look to the Aurora and Miami examples before overturning bans. "There's this pro-pit-bull movement that tries to paint these dogs as nanny dogs and sweet, lovely and kind," Borchardt said. "It's disgusting, it's dangerous, and it's irresponsible." Some groups, including the American Veterinary Medical Association, the Humane Society and the American Bar Association, have suggested governments would be better off focusing attention on problem animals in a community rather than banning any particular breed of dog. The push to end pit bull bans got a boost last year, when the Obama administration — in response to opponents of such laws petitioning the White House — said it was opposed to breed-specific legislation. Stakeholders on opposite sides of the issue cast aspersions about the evidence the others use to back their arguments. A lack of recent government or third-party data on pit bull bites further muddies the national conversation. The National Canine Research Council, which opposes breed-specific legislation, points to a 2013 study it partly funded that suggests a dog's environment has more to do than its breed with the likelihood of a dog making a deadly attack. The study, published in the Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association, of 256 dog bite-related fatalities from 2000-2009 found co-occurring factors in more than 80% of the deadly incidents, such as the absence of an able-bodied person to stop the attack, a history of abuse or neglect of the dog and the failure by owners to neuter the dogs. "It's becoming more and more obvious that breed-specific legislation doesn't improve public safety," says Janis Bradley, director of communication for the NCRC. "Its purpose is to reduce injuries from dog bites, but there is no municipality or state where it's enacted where they've been able to show that it's accomplished this." The Center for Disease Control, which opposes BSL, notes that fatal attacks represent a tiny fraction of about 4.7 million dog bites Americans suffer annually and that it's difficult to accurately calculate bite rates for specific breeds. DogsBite.org, a group that advocates in favor of BSL, points to its own research, culled from news reports of dog-bite-related fatalities, that shows 74% of incidents from 2005 to 2013 involved a pit bull or Rottweiler. Colleen Lynn, founder of DogsBite.org, dismisses the suggestion from the CDC and others that BSL doesn't work. "It's not designed to reduce all dog bites," said Lynn, who said pit bulls are an inherently aggressive animal. "It's breed-specific and meant to reduce pit bull maulings and fatalities." Even as dozens of American communities abandon BSL, some ponder its merits. This year, Riverside, Ala., a community about 40 miles outside of Birmingham, weighed enacting a pit bull ban after a 5-year-old boy was fatally mauled by neighbor's pit bull. City officials opted against it. Mayor Rusty Jessup said he would prefer not to have any pit bulls in his community of 3,000. Jessup said he didn't think his community could enforce such a ban or even positively determine the breeds of dogs. "We were just afraid that we were going to get situations where we're trying to enforce this and people are saying, 'That dog's not a pit bull, it's a boxer,'" Jessup said. " And doggone it, who are we going to have to make that determination?" Read or Share this story: http://usat.ly/1uHILDO

## Aurora Voters Uphold Pit Bull Ban, Rejecting Repeal Efforts

Aurora voters have decisively reaffirmed the city's long-standing ban on pit bulls, rejecting efforts to repeal the breed-specific legislation. Ballot question 2D, with over 70,000 votes counted across Adams and Arapahoe counties, signals a clear public mandate to maintain existing restrictions on these breeds. This decision upholds a policy that has fueled a deeply divisive debate for years, with a significant majority of residents expressing their desire to keep pit bulls out of the city.

### The Vote: A Clear Mandate

The vote revealed a stark division in public opinion. In Arapahoe County, 68 percent of voters opposed lifting the restrictions, while in Adams County, 63 percent held the same view. This ballot question, initiated by the city council, amplified a contentious issue that has drawn national attention from both proponents and opponents of breed-specific legislation.

### Advocacy and Public Sentiment

Colleen Lynn, founder of dogsbite.org and a prominent advocate for breed bans, expressed satisfaction with the outcome. She noted that the margin of victory for maintaining the ban exceeded her expectations. Lynn suggested that while pit bull supporters are often highly visible, a "silent majority" harbors reservations about sharing their communities with these dogs but chooses not to voice these concerns publicly. "We believe that the majority of Americans do not want to live next door to a pit bull period. And that is usually a very silent majority," she explained, viewing ballot measures like Aurora's as crucial avenues for this segment of the population to privately express their concerns.

### Evolution of Aurora's Ordinance

Aurora's current ordinance, originally enacted in 2005 to prohibit 10 breeds commonly identified as pit bulls, was revised by the city council in 2011. The updated ban narrowed its focus to three specific breeds: American pit bull terriers, American Staffordshire terriers, and Staffordshire bull terriers. This revision also introduced a provision allowing existing pit bulls within city limits to remain if owners paid a registration fee and adhered to specific regulations.

Furthermore, a DNA testing option was made available to owners to definitively prove whether their dogs fell under the banned categories. This measure has been supported by organizations like the American Kennel Club (AKC) in their broader advocacy against breed-specific legislation. City data indicates that approximately 90 registered pit bulls currently reside in Aurora under these provisions. This represents a significant decrease from the roughly 500 registered at the ban's initial implementation.

### The Human and Animal Cost

Tragically, since the ban's inception, the city has euthanized over 1,100 restricted-breed dogs. This statistic has been highlighted by animal welfare groups such as the ASPCA in their discussions on the impact of such policies.

### Divergent Perspectives on Public Safety

The debate consistently pits opposing viewpoints against each other, with significant input from various organizations. Opponents of breed-specific legislation, including the Animal Legal Defense Fund, argue that the focus should be on the behavior of individual dog owners rather than penalizing entire breeds. They emphasize responsible ownership and training as key factors in preventing dog-related incidents.

Conversely, supporters of the ban, including Aurora's own Animal Care Division and organizations like the National Canine Research Council, maintain that pit bulls possess inherently more dangerous characteristics compared to other breeds, thus warranting their exclusion from Aurora. Concerns have also been raised that Aurora could become a de facto "dumping ground" for pit bulls if neighboring cities maintain similar bans. This scenario has been discussed by the Humane Society of the United States in their broader analysis of breed-specific legislation's impact on animal populations across metropolitan areas.

The continued enforcement of Aurora's ban, affirmed by the recent vote, underscores the city's commitment to its established public safety policies concerning these breeds.

Breed-specific legislation (BSL) targets certain dog breeds, often "pit bull types," based on the mistaken idea that they are inherently more dangerous. These laws frequently lead to stricter rules or outright bans on owning these dogs. While communities in the U.S. and Canada have enacted BSL to reduce dog bites, it's widely seen as an ineffective and unreliable way to improve public safety. The American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA) officially opposes breed-specific laws.

Dog bites are a serious public health concern. The U.S. Centers for Disease Control (CDC) reports that over 4.5 million people are bitten by dogs annually. More than 800,000 of these bites need medical care, and about half involve children. However, a dog bite's likelihood and severity depend on many factors, not just breed. These include:

*   **Individual Dog Behavior:** A dog's past experiences, training, and unique temperament are key.
*   **Owner Responsibility:** Supervision, socialization, and proper containment play a big role.
*   **Encounter Circumstances:** The context of the interaction, including other animals or a person's actions, matters.
*   **Dog's Physical Traits:** While not a sole indicator, a dog's size and strength can affect bite severity.

BSL offers a simple, broad solution to a complex issue. This approach can divert important resources and attention from more effective, behavior-focused methods.

### Why Breed-Specific Laws Fail and Cause Harm

The AVMA's policy on dangerous animal laws strongly supports measures focusing on an animal's behavior and overall public safety. Importantly, the AVMA states that such laws "should not refer to specific breeds or classes of animals." Breed bans and restrictions are problematic for several key reasons:

*   **Difficulty in Accurate Identification:**
    *   Pinpointing a dog's exact breed, especially in mixed breeds, is very hard.
    *   BSL often relies on subjective looks instead of genetics or documented breed, leading to frequent misidentification.
    *   Studies, like those from Maddie's Fund, show that even skilled animal professionals struggle to identify breeds accurately. Many dogs are wrongly labeled as "pit bull types." This confusion makes enforcement difficult.

*   **Unfair Discrimination and Consequences:**
    *   These laws unfairly punish responsible owners and their well-behaved dogs.
    *   Dogs can face euthanasia or forced moves simply based on their perceived breed, regardless of their individual temperament or bite history.
    *   Many dogs of breeds targeted by BSL serve vital roles as service animals, in law enforcement, or in military work, yet they still face these unfair stereotypes.

*   **Failure to Address Root Causes of Aggression:**
    *   BSL doesn't tackle the underlying reasons for dog aggression, such as irresponsible ownership like poor supervision, not spaying/neutering, and lack of socialization.
    *   Implementing breed bans can create a false sense of security, potentially causing owners of other breeds to neglect essential training and socialization for their pets.

*   **Inefficient Use of Community Resources:**
    *   Communities may spend a lot of money enforcing ineffective breed bans.
    *   These funds could be better used for proven public safety strategies, like strong licensing programs, strict leash laws, and proactive interventions for dogs showing problem behaviors.

### The Challenge of Measuring Breed-Specific Risk

Accurately calculating breed-specific bite rates is nearly impossible due to unreliable data. Several factors make this difficult:

*   **Inaccurate or Unknown Breed Data:** Bite reports often lack precise breed information or rely on owner-reported breeds, which can be wrong.
*   **Underreporting of Minor Bites:** Many less severe bites go unreported, skewing statistics.
*   **Incomplete Licensing Records:** Complete and standardized dog licensing data is often unavailable.
*   **Failure to Track Repeat Offenders:** Incidents involving the same animal over time are not always recorded.
*   **Shifting Breed Popularity:** Changes in popular breeds over time can distort historical data.

Research attempting to link specific breeds to bite risk generally finds a weak or no connection. Conversely, factors linked to responsible ownership, like good socialization, neutering, and secure containment, are consistently identified as significant risk factors for preventing dog bites, according to experts like Dr. Sophia Yin.

### A Better Approach to Dog Bite Prevention

Effective dog bite prevention strategies should focus on responsible pet ownership and identifying individual dogs that pose a real risk, rather than targeting entire breeds. The AVMA supports a comprehensive approach that includes:

*   **Enforcement of General Dangerous Dog Laws:** These laws should apply to any dog showing dangerous behavior, focusing on holding chronically irresponsible owners accountable.
*   **Strict Enforcement of Animal Control Ordinances:** This includes mandatory leash laws and requirements for proper containment, managed by well-trained animal care and control officers.
*   **Prohibition and Prosecution of Dog Fighting:** This illegal activity greatly contributes to animal cruelty and the development of aggressive behaviors.
*   **Promotion of Neutering/Spaying:** This practice can help reduce behavioral issues in dogs not intended for breeding, as supported by research from the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (ASPCA).
*   **Comprehensive Public Education Programs:** These programs should teach the public about responsible pet selection, proper care, owner accountability, and effective bite prevention techniques for both children and adults.

The AVMA's detailed report, "A Community Approach to Dog Bite Prevention," offers practical advice for veterinarians, animal control professionals, and community leaders aiming to implement evidence-based prevention programs.

query: How are the daily lives of South Koreans affected by the increasing tensions between the U.S. and North Korea?

documents.text: SEOUL—The possibility of a military confrontation between the U.S. and North Korea is dominating headlines across South Korea, but in the streets of Seoul, concerns about a North Korean missile or nuclear test—or even a pre-emptive U.S. military strike—are drowned out by the rhythms of daily life. “North Korea makes threats, but they will not do it. No way,” said Yu Jeom-soon, a 59-year-old shop worker in downtown Seoul, who sat on a...

PYONGYANG/SEOUL (Reuters) - North Korea displayed what appeared to be new long-range and submarine-based missiles on the 105th birth anniversary of its founding father, Kim Il Sung, on Saturday, as a nuclear-powered U.S. aircraft carrier group steamed towards the region. Missiles appeared to be the main theme of a giant military parade, with Kim’s grandson, leader Kim Jong Un, taking time to greet the commander of the Strategic Forces, the branch that oversees the missile arsenal. A U.S. Navy attack on a Syrian airfield this month with Tomahawk missiles raised questions about U.S. President Donald Trump’s plans for reclusive North Korea, which has conducted several missile and nuclear tests in defiance of U.N. sanctions, regularly threatening to destroy the United States. Kim Jong Un, looking relaxed in a dark suit and laughing with aides, oversaw the festivities on the “Day of the Sun” at Pyongyang’s main Kim Il Sung Square. Goose-stepping soldiers and marching bands filled the square, next to the Taedonggang River that flows through Pyongyang, in the hazy spring sunshine, followed by tanks, multiple-launch rocket systems and other weapons. Single-engine propeller-powered planes flew in a 105 formation overhead. Unlike at some previous parades attended by Kim, there did not appear to be a senior Chinese official in attendance. China is North Korea’s lone major ally but has spoken out against its missile and nuclear tests and has supported U.N. sanctions. China on Friday again called for talks to defuse the crisis. Weapons analysts said they believed some of the missiles on display were new types of intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBM). The North has said it has developed and would launch a missile that can strike the mainland United States but officials and experts believe it is some time away from mastering all the necessary technology. Related Coverage A picture and its story: North Korea on parade “EARLY DAYS” North Korea showed two new kinds of ICBM enclosed in canister launchers mounted on the back of trucks, suggesting Pyongyang was working towards a “new concept” of ICBM, said Melissa Hanham, a senior research associate at the U.S.-based Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey, California. “However, North Korea has a habit of showing off new concepts in parades before they ever test or launch them,” Hanham said. “It is still early days for these missile designs.” The Pukkuksong submarine-launched ballistic missiles (SLBM) were also on parade. It was the first time North Korea had shown the missiles, which have a range of more than 1,000 km (600 miles), at a military parade. Displaying more than one of the missiles indicates North Korea is progressing with its plan to base a missile on a submarine, which are hard to detect, said Joshua Pollack, editor of the Washington-based Nonproliferation Review. “It suggests a commitment to this program,” said Pollack. “Multiple SLBMs seems like a declaration of intent to advance the program.” North Korea, still technically at war with the South after their 1950-53 conflict ended in a truce but not a treaty, has on occasion conducted missile or nuclear tests to coincide with big political events and often threatens the United States, South Korea and Japan. Choe Ryong Hae, a close aide to Kim Jong Un, addressed the packed square with a characteristically bellicose warning to the United States. Military vehicles carry missiles with characters reading 'Pukkuksong' during a military parade marking the 105th birth anniversary of country's founding father, Kim Il Sung in Pyongyang, April 15, 2017. REUTERS/Damir Sagolj “If the United States wages reckless provocation against us, our revolutionary power will instantly counter with annihilating strike, and we will respond to full-out war with full-out war and to nuclear war with our style of nuclear strike warfare,” he said. PENCE TO VISIT SOUTH State news agency KCNA said the Trump administration’s “serious military hysteria” had reached a “dangerous phase which can no longer be overlooked”. The United States has warned that a policy of “strategic patience” with North Korea is over. U.S. Vice President Mike Pence travels to South Korea on Sunday on a long-planned 10-day trip to Asia. China has also stepped up economic pressure on North Korea. It banned all imports of North Korean coal on Feb. 26 under U.N. sanctions, cutting off the North’s most important export product. China’s national airline, Air China, weeks ago canceled some flights to Pyongyang due to poor demand but it has not suspended all flights there, it said on Friday, denying a report by Chinese state broadcaster CCTV that all flights run by the airline between the two cities were to be suspended. China’s Global Times newspaper, which is published by the People’s Daily, the Communist Party’s official paper, said North Korea must have felt the shockwave from the 11-ton “mother of all bombs” dropped by U.S. forces on Islamic State-linked fighters in Afghanistan on Thursday. “It would be nice if the bomb could frighten Pyongyang, but its actual impact may just be the opposite,” it said in an editorial. North Korea on Friday denounced the United States for bringing “huge nuclear strategic assets” to the region as the USS Carl Vinson strike group with a flag-ship nuclear-powered aircraft carrier steamed closer. In Dandong, China’s main border post with North Korea, hundreds of North Koreans gathered at a cultural center carrying floral displays. Slideshow (24 Images) With the men wearing pins adorned with photos of Kim Il Sung, and the women in brightly colored traditional dress, crowds lined up to bow to portraits of their state founder before touring an exhibition of photos and North Korean paintings. KCNA was gushing in its praise of Kim Il Sung, recalling the time he met former U.S. president Jimmy Carter in 1994. “Former U.S. President Jimmy Carter was so fascinated by his personality as to say that Kim Il Sung is greater than that George Washington, Thomas Jefferson and Abraham Lincoln put together, eulogizing him as the great sun god of human destiny.” it said.

The arrival of the U.S. Air Force's WC-135 Constant Phoenix, nicknamed the "nuke-sniffer," at Kadena Air Base in Okinawa on April 7, 2017, underscores heightened U.S. readiness amid escalating tensions on the Korean peninsula. This specialized aircraft is designed to detect and collect radioactive debris from nuclear detonations, making its deployment a significant indicator of U.S. monitoring capabilities.

Here's a breakdown of key details surrounding this event:

*   **Confirmation of Arrival:** Satoru Kuba, an Okinawan who tracks military aircraft at Kadena, confirmed the WC-135's presence. Photographic evidence shared by *Stars and Stripes* further validated the report.
*   **Official Verification:** While the U.S. Air Force's 18th Wing at Kadena initially offered no comment, and Japan's Ministry of Defense withheld immediate verification, a senior official within Japan's Self-Defense Forces later confirmed the aircraft's presence, as reported by Japan's *Nikkei* newspaper.

The strategic positioning of the WC-135 Constant Phoenix in the Asia-Pacific is a recurring measure. The Pentagon has historically deployed one of its two WC-135 aircraft to the region, particularly following North Korea's 2006 underground nuclear test. The aircraft also made a notable appearance in Japan in 2011 after the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear power plant disaster, as documented by *The Washington Post*. This modified C-135B variant is equipped with advanced external flow-through devices for collecting air samples and particulate matter. These samples are then sent to specialized labs for analysis, providing critical intelligence on potential nuclear activities.

The Constant Phoenix's arrival in Okinawa coincides with a period of significant geopolitical instability. North Korea has recently conducted two nuclear tests and approximately 30 missile tests since the previous year. Acting South Korean President Hwang Kyo-ahn has warned of potential further "provocations" by Pyongyang, particularly around key dates:

*   The 105th birthday of Kim Il Sung, North Korea's founder, on Saturday.
*   The 85th anniversary of the Korean People's Army on April 25th.
*   The upcoming presidential elections in South Korea in May.

In response to U.S. missile strikes in Syria, North Korea has issued a warning of "catastrophic consequences" if the U.S. pursues a pre-emptive strike. This volatile environment has led U.S. Pacific Command to reroute the USS Carl Vinson strike group toward the Korean peninsula, a move prioritizing regional security. The Trump administration has signaled a firm stance against Pyongyang's nuclear program and is seeking increased cooperation from China. Chinese President Xi Jinping has expressed a willingness to work with the U.S. on denuclearizing the peninsula.

The specific WC-135 Constant Phoenix that landed in Okinawa had previously been stationed at RAF Mildenhall in England in March, according to photographic evidence of its tail numbers. At that time, Air Force officials told *Stars and Stripes* that the aircraft was on a routine mission, refuting speculation about its involvement in investigating a Russian nuclear test.

Prior to its arrival in Okinawa, the Constant Phoenix experienced an unscheduled diversion. While en route to Kadena, the aircraft encountered engine trouble, forcing an emergency landing in Banda Aceh, Indonesia. A spokesperson for the Indonesian air force confirmed to *The Associated Press* that the plane requested permission to land due to a single engine failure. The aircraft was carrying 20 American military personnel who were traveling from Diego Garcia. The cause of the engine failure is under investigation, and no injuries were reported.

## Navigating the North Korean Conundrum: A Strategic Approach

The Trump administration faces a familiar challenge: how to manage North Korea's escalating provocations, including the potential for a sixth nuclear test, and how to leverage China's influence to curb Pyongyang's nuclear ambitions. In a clear signal of intent, the Pentagon deployed a strike group to the western Pacific, a move National Security Advisor HR McMaster deemed "prudent" given the proximity to North Korea. This deployment was met with a defiant response from North Korea's state media, which threatened a "pre-emptive strike" against the US.

President Trump's decision to launch an airstrike in Syria during his summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping may have been a calculated message, suggesting a willingness to act unilaterally against North Korea, mirroring his actions in Syria. A subsequent tweet from Trump underscored this stance: "if China decides to help, that would be great. If not, we will solve the problem without them!"

### The Intricacies of the North Korean Challenge

Addressing the North Korean issue is as complex as brokering an Israeli-Palestinian peace deal. It involves not only mitigating regional fears of a North Korean attack but also understanding and potentially alleviating Pyongyang's deep-seated security concerns. Current US policy reportedly emphasizes increased pressure on Beijing to rein in North Korea, alongside further sanctions.

Two crucial factors warrant consideration: the strategic acumen of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, and the pivotal role of South Korea. While a prevalent view in the US attributes North Korea's intransigence to Kim's alleged irrationality – a sentiment echoed by figures like Senator John McCain, who referred to him as "this crazy fat kid" – a more compelling explanation lies in the strategic and economic logic underpinning Pyongyang's actions.

### Deterrence and Domestic Stability: Kim Jong-un's Motivations

Kim Jong-un's pursuit of nuclear deterrence, aimed at preventing a fate similar to that of Saddam Hussein or Muammar Gaddafi, provides a clear rationale for North Korea's weapons program. As one participant in Track II dialogues with North Korea recounted, North Koreans themselves posed the question: "Would the Americans have gone in and done what they did to Gaddafi, and to Syria, if they had what we have?"

Beyond deterrence, Kim's actions likely serve to project an image of strength domestically, crucial for maintaining his grip on North Korea's kleptocratic system. The more dangerous North Korea appears, the greater its leverage to extract concessions from countries like China and, particularly, South Korea.

### South Korea's Stakes and Past Precedents

China, fearing a North Korean collapse and the ensuing refugee crisis, has a vested interest in regional stability. South Korea, however, faces a more immediate and existential threat. For decades, Pyongyang has menaced Seoul with the promise of turning the South Korean capital into a "sea of fire," a threat made credible by the proximity of its weaponry to the border.

Kim Jong-un may anticipate further financial gains from Seoul, especially with the likelihood of a new South Korean president favoring engagement with Pyongyang. The 2015 memoir of former South Korean President Lee Myung-bak offers a glimpse into past negotiations. In 2009, during discussions about a potential summit, North Korea reportedly demanded substantial aid, including 100,000 tons of corn, 400,000 tons of rice, 300,000 tons of chemical fertilizers, and $100 million for road construction. Additionally, they sought $10 billion, ostensibly for an economic development bank, but likely intended to solidify Kim's domestic power base.

When Lee rejected these demands, Pyongyang shifted tactics. In March 2010, the sinking of the South Korean naval vessel Cheonan, which resulted in the deaths of 46 sailors, was attributed to North Korea.

South Korea also channeled funds to North Korea through the Kaesong Industrial Zone, a joint venture near the border. Following Seoul's closure of the zone in February 2016, South Korea's Unification Ministry stated that approximately 70% of intended wages and fees had been diverted to Pyongyang's weapons programs and luxury goods for Kim. Since its inception in the early 2000s, Seoul and South Korean companies reportedly invested over $820 million in Kaesong.

While the extent of Chinese aid to North Korea remains undisclosed, China is undeniably Pyongyang's most significant trading partner. Since 1995, the US provided over $1.2 billion in foreign assistance, a program largely halted by the Obama administration in 2009.

### The Financial Leverage and Diplomatic Imperative

China offers North Korea crucial market access and integration into the global financial system, while South Korea provides direct financial support. If Seoul were to again provide hundreds of millions of dollars to the North Korean elite, it would significantly undermine the impact of international sanctions.

South Korea stands to gain the most from eventual unification but also faces the greatest risk of a devastating conflict. Therefore, it is imperative that President Trump and Secretary of State Rex Tillerson maintain close communication and coordination with South Korea, recognizing that $450 million in cash can have a profound impact, particularly within the North Korean economy.

(Adds U.S. official plays down report in 11th paragraph, no comment from Pentagon) * South Korea expects to be told of any pre-emptive U.S. strike * China urges North Korea to halt nuclear programme * Tension rises as U.S. carrier group steams towards Korean peninsula * Trump strategy focusing on economic sanctions - U.S. officials By Michael Martina and Sue-Lin Wong BEIJING/PYONGYANG, April 13 (Reuters) - Military force cannot resolve tension over North Korea, China said on Thursday, while an influential Chinese newspaper urged the North to halt its nuclear programme in exchange for Chinese protection. Concerns have been growing that North Korea could soon conduct a sixth nuclear test or more missile launches in defiance of U.N. sanctions and stark warnings from the United States that a policy of patience was over. With a U.S. aircraft carrier group steaming to the area in a show of force and tensions rising, fears of a confrontation have been rising. China, North Korea’s sole major ally and neighbour, which nevertheless opposes its weapons programme, has called for talks leading to a peaceful resolution and the denuclearisation of the peninsula. “Military force cannot resolve the issue,” Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi told reporters in Beijing. “Amid tensions we will also find a kind of opportunity to return to talks.” While U.S. President Donald Trump has put North Korea on notice that he would not tolerate any provocation, U.S. officials have said his administration was focusing its strategy on tougher economic sanctions. Trump said on Thursday Pyongyang was a problem that “will be taken care of” and that he believed Chinese President Xi Jinping would “work very hard” to help resolve the challenge. Trump has also said the United States is prepared to tackle the crisis without China, if necessary. Trump diverted the USS Carl Vinson aircraft carrier group towards the Korean peninsula last weekend in a show of force to try to deter North Korea from conducting another nuclear test or launching more missiles to coincide with important events and anniversaries. But a senior Trump administration official described as “flat wrong” an NBC News report citing senior U.S. intelligence officials as saying the United States is prepared to launch a pre-emptive conventional weapons strike should officials be convinced North Korea was about to follow through with a nuclear weapons test. Another U.S. official also dismissed the report, calling it “speculative at best.” The Pentagon declined comment, saying, as a policy, it does not discuss future operations “nor publicly speculate on possible scenarios.” Scores of foreign journalists are gathered in Pyongyang for North Korea’s biggest national day, the “Day of the Sun”, marking he 105th anniversary of the birth of state founder Kim Il Sung on Saturday. They were taken to what officials billed as a “big and important event” early on Thursday which turned out to be the opening of a new street in the centre of the capital, attended by current leader Kim Jong Un. In 2012, two days before the centenary of Kim Il Sung’s death, it tried but failed to launch a long-range rocket carrying a satellite. It tested a newly developed intermediate-range missile on the anniversary last year, a launch that also failed. A Washington-based think tank that monitors North Korea, 38 North, said satellite images on Wednesday showed activity around the North’s Punggye-ri nuclear test site on the east coast that indicated it was ready for a new test. South Korean and U.S. officials and the think tank have been saying for weeks that North Korea could test a sixth bomb at any time. U.S. HOPES FOR CHINA INFLUENCE CIA director Mike Pompeo said North Korea was closer now than it had ever been to being able to threaten the United States with a nuclear-tipped intercontinental missile and increased its technical know-how with each new test. This in turn reduced U.S. options and “makes it more likely that you get a bad decision, a tough day for the leader of North Korea,” he told Washington’s Center for Strategic and International studies. Asked if there was hope that China would do more to slow or suspend its nuclear programme, he replied: “I’m counting on it.” Speculation about U.S. military action grew after the U.S. Navy fired 59 Tomahawk missiles at a Syrian airfield last week in response to a deadly gas attack. Washington said North Korea should see the strikes as a sign of U.S. resolve, but U.S. officials have played down the prospect of any military strike against North Korea, which would likely provoke massive North Korean retaliation and huge casualties in Japan and South Korea and among U.S. forces in both countries. The United States has remained technically at war with North Korea since the 1950-53 Korean conflict ended in an armistice rather than a peace treaty and the past six decades have been punctuated by periodic rises in tension and rhetoric that has always stopped short of a resumption of active hostilities. Analysts have interpreted China’s warnings as an effort to persuade North Korea to shelve plans for more tests. Wang warned that history would hold any instigator to account. “Whoever provokes the situation, whoever continues to make trouble in this place, they will have to assume historical responsibility,” Wang said. South Korean Foreign Minister Yun Byung-se told parliament in Seoul he believed Washington would consult Seoul if it was considering a pre-emptive strike. An influential state-backed Chinese newspaper, the Global Times, said North Korea’s best option was to give up its nuclear programme, and added that China would protect it if it did. “As soon as North Korea complies with China’s declared advice and suspends nuclear activities ... China will actively work to protect the security of a denuclearised North Korean nation and regime,” it said in an editorial. Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe underscored fears about North Korea, saying it could have the capacity to deliver missiles equipped with sarin nerve gas. But a senior Japanese diplomat said Japan also did not see a high risk of military action. On Tuesday, North Korea warned of a nuclear attack on the United States at any sign of American aggression. The White House dismissed the threat, saying there was no evidence North Korea could carry it out. North Korea regularly threatens to destroy the United States and South Korea. U.S. officials said Trump was considering sanctions that could include an oil embargo, banning North Korea’s airline, intercepting cargo ships, and punishing Chinese banks doing business with it. Customs data in Beijing on Thursday showed China’s coal imports from North Korea had plunged 51.6 percent in the first three months in 2017 from a year earlier.
