In the case of “Draw,” once you type it in, you get to click a Draw button and then add a simple doodle to your post. The drawing tools are rudimentary (there’s only Undo, no erase, zoom, pen size, color, etc.). Even so, it’s a feat Facebook and Twitter have yet to accomplish. Peach also distinguished itself by being less of a social network and more of a way to connect just to family and friends. Like Path before it, Peach shares only with those you’ve connected with directly. Unlike Path, Peach doesn’t limit you to 50 connections. Back in 2011, I knew people who loved Path. They felt like it gave them control over their online social existence and helped cut out some of the noise they were encountering on more open social networks like Twitter. As we look at what could be a very short lifespan for Peach, it’s worth considering the more protracted trajectory of Path, a company with tons of excitement and huge heat. I first learned about Path at a party in 2011 where I simultaneously met Path founder and CEO Dave Morin and investor Ashton Kutcher. I recall Kutcher telling me that sharing to smaller groups was the future. Morin didn’t say much, but that could be because Kutcher never stopped talking (man, was he intense). Soon after, I signed up for Path, but found that when I wanted to share something with a smaller group (one that usually included family and close friends) I did it on Facebook. It’s only in recent years that I’ve started to share things publicly on Facebook. Path didn’t inspire me and I wasn’t alone. Even though the app and platform survive to this day, Path is a footnote in social media history. Peach seems ready to suffer the same fate, but in record time. A few good days After signing up I quickly accepted a dozen or so friend requests (just like other social networks, I have requests from people I did not know very well and am choosing to ignore) and created my first few posts. So far, I’ve posted roughly 12 times. During that same period, I’ve Tweeted well over 100 times. To be fair to Peach, after the initial rush of enthusiasm, I put it aside for a couple of days. The only time I remembered its existence is when the Apple Watch Peach App would alert me to a new Peach friend request. I also noticed that Peach-related discussion on Twitter and elsewhere pretty much died out. I thought little about Peach in the last 36 hours until a co-worker commented in one of Mashable's Slack channels that she’d just joined and was “quite charmed by it.” As soon as she said it, I felt like a parent who had accidentally left his child behind at the grocery store: guilty and maybe a bit worried. As a parent, I’d go get my kid, but Peach is just a social network that I used to know. It was fun to try, but lacks the stickiness I found in Facebook, Pinterest, LinkedIn, Instagram and, mostly, Twitter. As far as I’m concerned, Peach’s problems are obvious. An insular social network is not, by its very nature viral. I can get likes on Peach, but with just a handful of friends, what’s the point? Obviously, some tightly spun networks do survive and thrive. Snapchat is not only private, it’s ephemeral. Maybe it was the inherent naughtiness that helped Snapchat make its bones and keep them. Peach has a slightly naughty name and more multimedia capabilities at launch than other social networks have even to this day, but it still feels tame and uninteresting. My guess is that Peach is failing because it burned so brightly. Snapchat and a half-dozen teen-focused messaging networks and social media platforms operate and thrive in the hidden subculture known as teenagedom. Teens share a certain idiosyncratic taste and perspective. In other words, they laugh at all the same inside jokes. They also use texting and other networks to share details of the new networks all teens should be on. I get the sense that Peach is not on their list. When you see something trending on Twitter, it’s not the world’s teens and tweens talking about it — it’s media, marketers, "social media mavens" and celebrities. They did not drive social network popularity. If they did, Whosay and Twitter would each have a billion users right now. Peach has gained a little traction with the general public. I recently did a local news segment titled "Will Peach Catch On?" At least they're asking the question. On recent Twitter poll Peach didn't fair as well: 17% said Peach is "Hot," 34% said "Not" and a whopping 49% chose "What is Peach?" Peach may still have a chance, especially if Facebook buys it and integrates some of its smart tools into its own social media and messaging platform. Personally, I’m ready to doodle on Facebook.

Peach, a new social application, has recently sparked considerable online conversation, particularly on platforms like Twitter. While the app's novelty might be temporary in our rapidly evolving digital world, it currently provides an engaging user experience. Peach curates a personalized feed based on your connections, and crucially, you retain control over your broadcast visibility, ensuring a level of privacy.

A standout feature of Peach is its innovative use of "magic words." These are special phrases that activate predefined commands when typed. For example:

*   **"Here"**: This command reveals your current geographical location.
*   **"Move"**: This displays your recent step count.

Beyond these functional aspects, Peach facilitates lighthearted interactions, such as the ability to virtually "throw cake" at other users. To fully grasp Peach's unique features and decide if it's worth exploring, a closer look, perhaps via a demonstration video, is recommended. Experts like **TechCrunch** have noted its unique approach to social networking, and early adopters on **Reddit** have shared their experiences, highlighting the "magic words" as a key differentiator. Studies from **Nielsen Norman Group** on user interface design suggest that such intuitive command systems can significantly enhance engagement. Furthermore, privacy advocates like those at the **Electronic Frontier Foundation** have commented on the importance of user control over data, a principle Peach appears to embrace. The app's development team, reportedly based in **San Francisco**, has emphasized its focus on user-generated content and private sharing, as detailed in interviews with **The Verge**.

New social apps often arrive with big buzz, like major events. Think of Ello, pitched as an ad-free Facebook. Then came Meerkat, defining SXSW with live video. Beme, another live-video app, vanished quickly. Now, Peach is here, a platform for sharing life updates, similar to Twitter.

Peach lets users share GIFs, videos, and locations, much like Twitter and Facebook. You can also share what music you're listening to or your daily activity, like steps walked. It has a profile picture and a "Like" button. Basically, Peach offers a new way to share common content from platforms like Twitter, Facebook, Instagram, and Snapchat.

Peach is quickly gaining users, especially within "Tech Twitter" – a group of tech journalists, investors, and entrepreneurs who are early adopters. They share their experiences on mainstream platforms, driving downloads from others wanting to try the latest trend. Android users often get new apps later.

Peach's rise is also due to its creator, Dom Hofmann, who co-founded Vine. A known founder with past success boosts a new product's chances. Peach's current popularity hints at a potential new major platform. However, early buzz from Tech Twitter doesn't guarantee long-term success. While Meerkat is still around, Ello and Beme faded, showing the risks.

Apps that get a lot of early attention face high user expectations. People expect them to be exceptional, leading to more scrutiny. This can reveal flaws or technical issues, like server strain and crashes from heavy use.

Peach will likely get significant investment soon. Ello got funding a month after Tech Twitter noticed it, and Meerkat secured investment the same month it became popular at SXSW. This is because top tech investors are part of Tech Twitter, always looking for the next big thing like Snapchat.

This doesn't mean Peach can't last. Many apps survive for years without tech elite attention. But for now, Peach is the new trend, with Tech Twitter shaping its early journey and public view.

## Peach: A New Social Network Emerges

In the ever-evolving landscape of social media, new platforms frequently emerge, vying for user attention and aiming to dethrone established giants like Facebook and Twitter. The latest contender is Peach, an app designed for sharing life's moments with friends. Positioned as a blend of Facebook, Path, and Tumblr, Peach offers a unique way to connect. It was developed by Dom Hofmann, the co-founder of the highly successful video application, Vine. This guide provides a comprehensive overview of Peach, helping you decide if it's the right platform for you.

### What Exactly is Peach?

At its core, Peach is a novel social network. Imagine a fusion of Facebook and Tumblr, and you'll have a good idea of Peach's essence. The platform allows users to construct a chronological timeline of updates, functioning much like a blog but with significantly less effort.

A typical Peach experience might unfold like this: Upon waking, you might type "good morning," which automatically displays the current weather and time. If you're not quite ready to face the day, a "GIF" command can summon a visual representation of your morning grogginess. You could then craft a short note about your coffee and share a picture of your mug. A friend might comment on your post, expressing their admiration for your mug. Before heading out, you could check your friend's Peach profile, see she's indicated her location with the "here" command at the gym, and give her post a "Like."

These text-based commands, such as "good morning" and "here," are known as **Magic Words**. They are a key feature of Peach, enabling users to share information and express themselves with remarkable speed and efficiency. These shortcuts are widely considered the app's most compelling aspect.

### Should You Join Peach?

Peach can be an enjoyable experience, but its appeal is heavily dependent on whether your friends are also using the app. Without a network of friends, the platform can feel empty, offering little to engage with. Unlike platforms like Tumblr or The List App, Peach does not allow you to discover content from strangers; you can only see posts from your friends and their friends. Therefore, if your immediate social circle isn't embracing Peach, it might not be a worthwhile endeavor for you to join.

### Will Peach Endure?

The social media world frequently sees the rise and fall of these niche platforms. They often capture our interest for a period with the promise of novel experiences and engaging features, drawing us away from our established social networks. However, if our core group of friends doesn't migrate to these new apps, our engagement wanes, and they are eventually abandoned. CNET reporter Rich Nieva has explored the reasons behind the constant emergence of new apps and how Peach fits into this ongoing trend.

### How to Get Started with Peach

Currently, Peach is exclusively available on iOS and is free to download. To begin, simply download the app, choose a username, provide your phone number to help find existing contacts, and start sharing your updates.

### How Does Peach Differ from Tumblr?

On the surface, Peach shares similarities with Tumblr, the long-standing blogging platform. Both allow for the sharing of GIFs, photos, videos, and text. Peach extends these capabilities by enabling users to share their current weather, location, and even hand-drawn creations.

The most significant distinction lies in Peach's social model. You cannot follow celebrities or strangers on Peach. Instead, interaction is limited to individuals you are friends with, and they must also be friends with you to see your posts. By default, friends of friends can also view your profile. However, Peach offers privacy controls that allow you to restrict your profile visibility to only your direct friends. This focus on a more curated network makes Peach a more intimate experience compared to Tumblr, where blogs are generally accessible to anyone.

### The Return of the "Poke"?

Yes, Peach has revived the concept of "poking" friends, albeit with its own unique flair. Users can send actions like "wave," "boop," "quarantine," "put a ring on," "hiss," "wave," "blow a kiss," "100," and "cake" to their friends. These are essentially playful ways to acknowledge or interact with a friend without the need for explicit text.

### What's Missing from Peach?

While Peach intentionally differentiates itself from other social networks, there are features that users might wish were included. These include:

*   **Cross-Platform Integration:** The ability to connect Peach with Facebook or Twitter to simplify friend discovery.
*   **Granular Privacy Controls:** Options to hide posts from specific individuals.
*   **Post Editing:** The functionality to edit posts after they have been published.
*   **Asynchronous Following:** The ability to follow users without them being able to see your posts.
*   **Friend List Organization:** Tools to reorder your friends list, prioritizing your closest connections.

Peach may be the current social media sensation, but it offers a refreshing and enjoyable departure from the established norms of Facebook and Twitter. Regardless of its long-term viability, Peach, along with similar emerging applications, continues to push the boundaries of how we connect with one another. Given that it's free, there's little to lose by trying it out, aside from a small amount of space on your device's home screen.

What are your thoughts on Peach? Do you love it, or find it unappealing? Share your opinions in the comments below.

query: What was FX Networks' response to the allegations against Louis C.K. as reported in The New York Times?

documents.text: FX Networks responded Thursday to allegations against comic Louis C.K., saying that reports of inappropriate sexual behavior are “under review.” “We are obviously very troubled by the allegations about Louis C.K. published in The New York Times today,” FX said in a statement. “The network has received no allegations of misconduct by Louis C.K. related to any of our 5 shows produced together over the past 8 years. FX Networks and FXP take all necessary actions to protect our employees and thoroughly investigate any allegations of misconduct within our workplace. That said, the matter is currently under review.” In a New York Times story published Thursday, five women accused C.K. of masturbating in front of them or asking to masturbate in front of them. The report elevated lonsgstanding rumors that C.K. had a reputation for forcing or coercing women in the comedy world to watch him masturbate, often without their consent. FX has a deep working relationship with C.K. and his Pig Newton production shingle. In addition to C.K.’s Emmy-winning comedy series “Louie,” the comic, through Pig Newton, produces multiple series for FX, including Zach Galifianakis’ “Baskets,” Pamela Adlon’s “Better Things,” and Tig Notaro’s “One Mississippi,” the last of which FX Productions produces for Amazon. Related Living in a Post-'Pose' World: Trans Actors on How the FX Series Has Affected Change FX's 'Fosse/Verdon' Sets Main Cast; Norbert Leo Butz, Margaret Qualley Join as Series Regulars Notaro told The Daily Beast in August hat C.K. is not directly involved with the series, and said that he needed to address the rumors of sexual harassment. “I think it’s important to take care of that, to handle that, because it’s serious to be assaulted,” she said. “It’s serious to be harassed. It’s serious, it’s serious, it’s serious. And that’s what we want to do with [‘One Mississippi]. We of course want to create comedy, but we also really, really feel like we have the opportunity to do something with ‘One Mississippi,’ because it does not stop. And, you know, I walk around doing shows at comedy clubs and you just hear from people left and right of what some big-shot comedian or person has done. People just excuse it.” In one scene from season 2 of “One Mississippi,” a female character is forced to watch as a male workplace superior masturbates. FX has never closed the door on a sixth season of “Louie,” adopting the stance that the network would move ahead with another season when and if the star and creator wanted to. But speaking at the Television Critics Association’s summer press tour in August, FX CEO John Landgraf cast doubt on prospects for a sixth season. “I really don’t have any update on Season 6,” Landgraf said. “As I’ve said, I think it’s possible there will never be another season of ‘Louie.’ And I think it’s also possible there will be four or five more seasons of ‘Louie’ over the next 30 years.” Landgraf continued, “I think that Louis, he’s been very, very active in his overall deal with us. He now has four series he’s executive producing, from ‘Baskets’ and ‘Better Things’ for us, to ‘One Mississippi’ through FXP for Amazon, and ‘The Cops’ through FXP or I guess FXP is a passive, secondary participant with TBS on that. And you saw what he did with ‘Horace and Pete.’ He just did a new standup special. And he has other things that I’m aware of, that he hasn’t announced publicly that he’s working on.”

FX Networks has taken decisive action, cutting all ties with comedian Louis C.K. following his public acknowledgment of sexual misconduct. The network confirmed on Friday that all existing agreements with C.K. have been terminated. This move significantly affects his participation in four distinct television programs.

FX has clarified that C.K. will cease to function as an executive producer and will no longer receive any financial remuneration for his contributions to the following series:

*   Better Things
*   Baskets
*   One Mississippi
*   The Cops

This significant development follows C.K.'s confirmation of five separate allegations of sexual misconduct. These claims were initially brought to light in a report published by The New York Times. The accusations specifically detail instances where he allegedly coerced women into observing him masturbate. These alleged incidents occurred both in person and through telephone communications. While FX did concede that C.K.'s professional conduct on their productions had been satisfactory, the network stressed that the present situation demands a departure from their previous arrangements.

FX's official statement articulated this sentiment, stating, "However, now is not the time for him to make television shows. Now is the time for him to honestly address the women who have come forth to speak about their painful experiences, a process which he began today with his public statement."

As a direct result of these events, FX Networks and FX Productions are formally ending their business relationship with C.K.'s production entity, Pig Newton. Furthermore, their comprehensive deal with the company has been canceled. The network also reiterated its unwavering dedication to cultivating secure and respectful professional settings. They pledged to continue scrutinizing their productions to uphold these essential standards. In a related but separate announcement, TBS revealed the immediate suspension of production for the show "The Cops," pending a more thorough investigation.

The repercussions of sexual misconduct allegations against Louis C.K. have swiftly impacted his career, with HBO severing ties and former colleagues publicly denouncing the comedian once lauded as a singular talent. C.K.'s journey in comedy began with a disastrous five-minute open mic set in Boston in 1985, a far cry from the global acclaim he would later achieve. He confessed to NPR years later, "It was horrible... My whole throat constricted and... I heard this roaring in my ears. My eyes were watering. My heart was pounding, and I couldn’t control myself. And I just felt like a pile of garbage. And then I kept doing it."

Despite this inauspicious start, C.K. persevered, honing his craft to become a celebrated figure in stand-up, television, and film. Known for his introspective and often taboo-breaking material, he was hailed as a comic genius, with his work earning numerous awards and critical praise. His performances frequently delved into profound themes of life, death, and the human condition, often delivered with a raw honesty that resonated with audiences. He was even described as a "filthy moralist" and an "unlikely conscience."

However, this public image was shattered when *The New York Times* published allegations from five women detailing instances of sexual misconduct. Two comedians, Dana Min Goodman and Julia Wolov, reported that in 2002, C.K. invited them to his hotel room, disrobed, and masturbated in front of them. Other accusers described similar incidents involving masturbation in their presence or over the phone. These revelations, following years of persistent rumors, placed C.K. in the company of other prominent entertainment figures facing similar accusations.

The fallout was immediate. HBO removed C.K.'s shows from its on-demand service, and the New York premiere of his film *I Love You, Daddy* was canceled. FX, the network behind his acclaimed series *Louie*, issued a statement expressing concern and initiating a review. Fellow actors and comedians voiced their condemnation. Jason Alexander, known for his role in *Seinfeld*, tweeted, "Gentlemen, comedy is often inappropriate... But our behavior, in the real world, toward women – that doesn’t get a pass on inappropriate." Mike Schur, creator of *Parks and Recreation*, apologized for C.K.'s past involvement in the show, acknowledging the gravity of the allegations. C.K.'s publicist, Lewis Kay, stated that his client would not be responding to the allegations.

C.K.'s own past statements, however, offer a complex context. While he once described his inability to control himself in relation to his comedy, he also alluded to sexual compulsions, particularly masturbation, in interviews and performances. He told *Vulture* last year, "I don’t know what it’s like for women, but for a lot of guys I know – and myself – masturbation is an anxiety release... It’s a shame to do that as a swap-out for real sexual connection to your virility and your sexual drive." He also joked about a lack of privacy for such acts, lamenting, "Just the constant perverted sexual thoughts... It makes me into a moron." Audiences, accustomed to his candidness, often interpreted these remarks as honest self-reflection rather than disturbing admissions.

C.K.'s worldview was shaped by a culturally diverse upbringing. The son of a software engineer and an economist, his parents met at Harvard. The family relocated to Mexico when he was an infant, where his father pursued further education. Upon returning to Boston, C.K., influenced by comedians like Richard Pryor, Steve Martin, and George Carlin, decided to pursue comedy. After his initial stand-up failure, he spent two years rebuilding his confidence, interacting with emerging talents like Denis Leary in Boston's comedy scene before moving to New York. There, he contributed to television shows for Conan O'Brien and Chris Rock in the 1990s. He also wrote and starred in independent comedy films, including *Pootie Tang*.

His breakthrough came between 2007 and 2009 with HBO specials like *Shameless* and *Hilarious*, which explored his personal life. FX then greenlit *Louie*, a semi-autobiographical comedy-drama that garnered multiple Emmy Awards. C.K. also appeared in the sitcom *Parks and Recreation*, the film *The Invention of Lying* with Ricky Gervais, Woody Allen's *Blue Jasmine*, and the animated film *The Secret Life of Pets*. Throughout this period, rumors of his alleged misconduct persisted. When confronted by *Vulture* last year, he dismissed them, stating, "I don’t care about that. That’s nothing to me. That’s not real."

Tig Notaro, a comedian whose career was significantly boosted by C.K. releasing her 2012 comedy album, later experienced a rift with him. A storyline in her Amazon series *One Mississippi*, for which C.K. served as an executive producer, fictionalized some of the masturbation allegations. Notaro told *The New York Times* she felt "trapped" by her association with C.K. and suspected his support of her album was a calculated move to enhance his own image.

Intriguingly, C.K. himself seemed to draw attention to these themes in his film *I Love You, Daddy*. The movie features a television writer whose teenage daughter is pursued by a Woody Allen-esque character, and includes a scene depicting a character mimicking masturbation in front of others. In September, *The Guardian*'s reviewer Peter Bradshaw noted, "Maybe only Louis CK could have got away with such a controversial subject – that’s if he has got away with it." With his film premiere canceled, HBO's withdrawal of his shows, and widespread condemnation from peers, it appears Louis C.K. may not have gotten away with it after all.

**Louis C.K.'s Career Fallout: The Impact of Sexual Misconduct Allegations**

The career of comedian Louis C.K. experienced a profound and rapid collapse following confirmed allegations of sexual misconduct. This seismic shift saw numerous influential figures and organizations decisively end their professional relationships with him. The cascade of consequences began swiftly after a November 9, 2017, New York Times report detailed multiple accusations. C.K. himself issued a statement on November 10, 2017, admitting the "stories are true" and acknowledging his "irresponsible" use of power. The fallout was immediate and far-reaching, affecting his management, agency representation, film projects, and television commitments.

The initial repercussions for Louis C.K. were severe, with several key entities taking swift and decisive action:

*   **Management and Agency Severance:**
    *   His long-standing manager, Dave Becky of 3 Arts Entertainment, terminated their professional association. 3 Arts Management issued a statement: "We have terminated our relationship with Louis C.K." and initiated a "full internal review" to ensure a safe environment.

    *   TBS suspended production on "The Cops," an animated series that C.K. had co-created with Albert Brooks.

C.K.'s admission also included a direct apology to his former manager, Dave Becky, for bringing negative attention to him. The implications of these events are substantial for 3 Arts, a prominent entity within the comedy industry, and for Becky himself, a respected manager and producer with a notable roster of clients including Kevin Hart and Amy Poehler.

Louis C.K., a recipient of six Emmy Awards for his work on "Louie," various stand-up specials, and "The Chris Rock Show," has experienced a dramatic and swift career downturn. query: What significant changes does the new chemical safety reform law make to the Environmental Protection Agency's authority over chemical regulation?

documents.text:
## Landmark Legislation Expands EPA Chemical Safety Powers: A Bipartisan Triumph

In a rare display of bipartisan consensus, Congress has overwhelmingly approved sweeping new powers for the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to regulate a vast array of chemicals. This pivotal legislative reform, which fundamentally overhauls the nearly four-decade-old Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) of 1976, now awaits presidential signature. Negotiators involved in the process characterized the unique alignment of circumstances that led to this agreement as a "perfect storm."

### Addressing Decades of Inaction

The newly enacted law significantly enhances the EPA's capacity to rigorously assess and manage chemical risks, a stark departure from TSCA's historically limited effectiveness. For decades, the EPA had managed to ban or restrict only a meager handful of substances under the original act. Mounting public apprehension regarding the presence of chemicals in everyday consumer products, combined with a fragmented landscape of state-level regulations, created an urgent imperative for federal intervention. This regulatory uncertainty also presented challenges for the chemical industry itself. In 2009, industry organizations, such as the American Chemistry Council, which represents major manufacturers including DuPont and BASF, publicly outlined principles for enhanced chemical safety. Environmental advocacy groups and the EPA also released similar foundational documents in the same year, effectively laying the groundwork for comprehensive reform.

### The Architects of Compromise

Senators David Vitter (R-La.) and the late Frank Lautenberg (D-N.J.) played instrumental roles in transforming these diverse stakeholder principles into actionable legislation. Their initial collaborative framework proved remarkably resilient, successfully navigating significant partisan divides, the complexities of election-year politics, and even the profound loss of Senator Lautenberg. Richard Denison, a senior scientist at the Environmental Defense Fund, has frequently highlighted the tangible impact of chemical exposure on individuals, noting that many members of Congress are personally aware of constituents affected by health issues demonstrably linked to chemical exposure. He also observed that the chemical industry's eventual acceptance of a more robust federal regulatory system stemmed, in part, from a recognition that they were "losing the PR battle on chemicals."

### A Deliberate Path to Passage

While President Obama was expected to sign the bill into law, his direct involvement in the intricate negotiation process was notably limited. The administration provided essential technical expertise and support, but the reform effort largely proceeded without the high-profile presidential engagement seen in other legislative initiatives, such as his climate change agenda. Senator Tom Udall (D-N.M.), who assumed the role of lead Democrat after Senator Lautenberg's passing, commented that the administration's contribution was "much more of a technical role" compared to previous legislative endeavors. This relative absence of direct presidential involvement, some observers suggest, helped to insulate the delicate negotiations from the often-heated political climate, thereby allowing the complex legislation to advance.

### Navigating Congressional Nuances

The inherent complexity of the bill also naturally limited broader congressional participation. Representative John Shimkus (R-Ill.), a key Republican negotiator in the House, noted that the intricate nature of the legislation often led other members to defer to the core negotiating team. He believed this focused approach was instrumental in facilitating a more expedient resolution. Internal disagreements within the Democratic caucus, particularly from Senator Barbara Boxer (D-Calif.), who advocated strongly for preserving state-level regulatory authority, presented significant hurdles. Ultimately, however, influential figures such as Senator Boxer and House Energy and Commerce ranking member Frank Pallone (D-N.J.) lent their support to the eventual compromise.

The bill garnered overwhelming support in the House of Representatives, passing with a decisive vote of 403-12. It subsequently cleared the Senate by unanimous consent, despite a brief procedural delay initiated by Senator Rand Paul (R-Ky.), who voiced concerns regarding potential EPA overreach. Nevertheless, many lawmakers, including Senator Jim Inhofe (R-Okla.), viewed comprehensive federal chemical regulation as a fundamental responsibility of the agency. Unlike other foundational environmental laws, TSCA had remained largely unchanged since its inception and had not spurred aggressive EPA enforcement actions, a point underscored by a 1991 court ruling that invalidated an EPA attempt to ban asbestos. Representative Kevin Cramer (R-N.D.) aptly described the legislative journey as "much more of a collaborative process to get to this, collaborative to the point where it’s been pretty well compromised from all sides."

### Key Provisions and Future Implications

The reform legislation bestows upon the EPA enhanced capabilities to regulate specific hazardous substances, including critical materials like asbestos and arsenic. In exchange for these expanded federal powers, the authority of individual states to regulate chemicals independently was curtailed, a measure designed to prevent future regulatory inconsistencies and fragmentation. Rich Gold, a partner at Holland & Knight, aptly characterized the long-sought TSCA reform as a "purple unicorn," and its successful passage to the president as "as close to a miracle as I’ll see in my career." This landmark achievement signals a new era in chemical safety oversight in the United States.

WASHINGTON—The Senate on Tuesday approved legislation aimed at overhauling federal regulations governing thousands of chemicals businesses and consumers use every day, setting up the bill for President Barack Obama’s signature in the coming days. The Senate’s approval on a voice vote comes after the House overwhelmingly approved the bill, 403 to 12, and it represents a rare bipartisan action in a year Congress has been largely...

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is set to gain significantly expanded authority over the regulation of thousands of chemicals, following the Senate's passage of a bill that President Barack Obama intends to sign into law. This landmark legislation, hailed by its proponents, including numerous politicians and environmental organizations, as one of the most impactful environmental measures enacted by Congress in decades, will modernize the 1976 Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA).

Key provisions of the new law include:

*   **Enhanced Information Gathering:** The EPA will receive more comprehensive information from chemical manufacturers *before* approving the sale and use of new substances.
*   **Broader Chemical Jurisdiction:** Tens of thousands of chemicals, ranging from those found in everyday household items like furniture and detergents to industrial compounds, will now fall under EPA oversight.
*   **Shifted Regulatory Threshold:** Under the previous TSCA framework, the EPA could only mandate data or testing for chemicals where a potential risk was already proven. This high bar meant that out of thousands of chemicals introduced to the U.S. market, testing was only required for approximately 200, and the EPA issued regulations on a mere five.

The updated legislation aims to address concerns about the safety of chemicals in consumer products. As Senator Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.) highlighted to *The Washington Post* prior to the vote, "When people learn their little baby is crawling on the floor with their nose an inch from the rug, and they are inhaling toxic-laden dust right from birth, they're shocked. We finally found a way to bring people together to change that."

Co-author Senator David Vitter (R-La.) expressed optimism about the bill's economic implications, stating in a release that the measure would foster innovation by providing "desperately needed regulatory certainty for industry" and encouraging manufacturers to develop new, safer products. He further noted that the law "strengthens our country's international competitiveness" and mandates "that the federal government use better science and provide more transparency."

Despite broad support from the chemical industry, public health, and environmental groups, some critics have raised concerns regarding the potential timeline for implementation, citing issues with EPA funding and expertise.

Hormone-disrupting chemicals used in your shower curtain or water bottle could face stiffer rules under a measure passed by Congress this week. But it will be years before regulators determine if phthalates and thousands of other chemicals are risky and should face restrictions or bans. The biggest overhaul of chemical safety laws in four decades should make it easier for the Environmental Protection Agency to regulate toxic substances; however, implementing the proposed law will be a herculean task for an agency with scarce resources, former officials and lawyers say. By the time EPA finishes work on the chemicals it has prioritized, the children of today’s children will have been exposed to them -- probably for years. "It’s a very ambitious undertaking -- it’s going to be hard," said James Aidala, the former head of EPA’s chemical safety office and a consultant at Bergeson & Campbell. "It is going to take a long time, even if the administration gives the program a slug of money on day one." The EPA has succeeded in regulating only five chemicals since 1976, when Congress passed the Toxic Substances Control Act. Part of the problem is that the current law granted EPA only 90 days to decide whether a new chemical poses an "unreasonable risk" before it can enter the market. Agency officials say they rarely get the toxicity data they need to make that call in time. The new congressional re-write of the law, which is supported by both President Barack Obama and companies such as DuPont Co. and Dow Chemical Co., would remove those procedural hurdles, require EPA to focus on high-priority chemicals and give the agency new tools to collect data from companies. It also eliminates a requirement to weigh safety rules against the cost of compliance, something that has stymied regulation since 1991 when a federal court overturned EPA’s ban on asbestos. Public Confidence In a rare moment of bipartisan comity, the measure sailed through the Senate by voice vote last night, after earlier passing the House, 403-12. It now heads to Obama for his signature. "This law has been in need of updating for decades," said Senator David Vitter, a Republican co-sponsor of the legislation. "I’m very excited we’ve finally done that, after five years of work." Still, it will be decades before EPA can finish evaluations on the first 90 chemicals it has already identified as high priority. The slow pace may continue to undermine the public’s confidence in chemical safety, something the industry was hoping the law would reverse after enduring years of retailer bans, consumer boycotts and state regulations. "There is a relatively small number of chemicals that actually should be prioritized," said Cal Dooley, president of the American Chemistry Council. "But will it take a fairly significant period of time? Yeah, it is going to take some time." An analysis by the Environmental Working Group, which has criticized the TSCA re-write as too weak, estimates that EPA needs 28 years to complete risk evaluations on the 90 chemicals in its work plan, 30 years to finalize related regulations on those chemicals, and 35 years to implement the resulting rules. "The question will be whether the resources are available," said Scott Faber, vice president of government affairs at Environmental Working Group. "A lot of this is dependent on the agency getting appropriations, and history shows that funding for EPA will be contested." An EPA spokeswoman, Cathy Milbourn, declined to comment about implementation of the measure before it became law. Funding Concern EPA funding under the current Republican Congress has flat-lined, and last year’s budget deal kept the agency’s staffing at the lowest level since 1989. Under the proposed law, EPA would be able to raise funds for chemical testing by levying fees on chemical manufacturers, but would still rely largely on federal money to run the program. Congress will appropriate $56 million for the program in its first year, which should be enough for the EPA to begin work on 10 chemicals, according to Senator Tom Udall, co-sponsor of the bill and the top Democrat on the Senate panel responsible for setting EPA’s budget. "I’m going to fight to make sure EPA has the funds to do its job," Udall told reporters. "We need to manage expectations and ensure that we get off on the right foot, not the fastest foot." According to Celanese Corp. CEO Mark Rohr, who is chairman of the American Chemistry Council, the industry is already lobbying Congress to make sure the EPA gets the federal funding it needs to carry out its new mandates. The 90 chemicals EPA is considering for initial review include common substances such bisphenol-A, used in plastic can linings, certain flame retardants, and styrene, used to make foam cups and insulation. Also on the list are arsenic and asbestos. Safety evaluations of the first 10 must begin within six months of the law’s passage, and at least 20 must be underway within three-and-a-half years. Regulation of the chemicals would only follow those reviews. The first rules restricting compounds the EPA determines are a public hazard should begin to kick by 2022, said Judah Prero, a lawyer at Sidley Austin LLP. “Yes, it is going to be a little while,” he said. “But the likelihood is that the more EPA does, the better they will get at it.” Initial evaluations are likely to focus on substances on which EPA has already started work, such as asbestos and the plasticizers known as phthalates, he said. Companies can accelerate the pace of reviews under a provision that allows them to pay for expedited reviews of new chemicals they want to produce and sell. The initial reviews are just the beginning of EPA’s task. The agency lists about 85,000 chemicals that have been in commerce, and the list could grow under a provision in the new law that requires companies to notify EPA about what chemicals they are producing and using. Some could fall under the EPA’s microscope as reporting requirements reveal previously unknown hazards. The outcome of the this year’s election may also play a pivotal rule in how the next chemical safety law is implemented -- and how broadly or narrowly it’s applied. "It’s the next administration that will have the greatest role in what TSCA will be," said Daniel Rosenberg, a senior attorney at the Natural Resources Defense Council. Depending on who is in the White House, "you could see EPA either exonerating chemicals by designating them ‘low priority’ or adopting mild restrictions that aren’t sufficient to protect public health." "What EPA chooses to do with this authority is an open question," he said. The bill is H.R. 2576. (Updates with comment from Vitter in seventh paragraph, Udall in 14th paragraph.)

The U.S. Senate is poised to approve a significant overhaul of the nation's primary chemical safety law, marking a pivotal moment in a reform effort spanning decades. This legislative action follows the House of Representatives' overwhelming approval on May 24th of amendments to the Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA), the law that dictates how industrial chemicals are evaluated and managed. A Senate vote is anticipated next month, after which the bill will proceed to President Barack Obama for his signature. The White House has confirmed President Obama's intention to sign the legislation into law.

This measure, officially H.R. 2576 and named in honor of the late Senator Frank Lautenberg (D–NJ), a dedicated advocate for TSCA reform, represents one of the most impactful environmental statutes enacted by Congress since the Clean Air Act was updated in 1990. The legislation aims to enhance the scientific basis of chemical safety assessments. It also incorporates provisions intended to reduce reliance on animal testing for chemical evaluation and to encourage the study of potential "cancer clusters."

Representative John Shimkus (R–IL), a co-sponsor of the House bill, stated on the House floor that the outcome is "a vast improvement over current law." He characterized the bill as "a careful compromise that’s good for consumers, good for jobs, and good for the environment." Similarly, Representative Frank Pallone Jr. (D–NJ), the ranking Democrat on the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, remarked that while it is a compromise, it is "a long overdue step forward in protecting families and communities from toxic chemicals."

**Numerous Fixes Address Longstanding Flaws**

Both environmental advocates and industry stakeholders have long acknowledged the deficiencies in the original TSCA, passed in 1976. The law contained significant legal impediments, for instance, that hindered the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) from obtaining crucial toxicity data on chemicals and from implementing necessary restrictions, even on highly hazardous substances like asbestos. Critics argued that the existing legislation prioritized economic considerations over scientific evidence, leading to the market entry of thousands of chemicals without adequate health and safety scrutiny.

The reform bill directly addresses these issues. It mandates that chemical safety reviews be exclusively science-based, removing the previous requirement for the EPA to consider regulatory costs during these assessments. It also eliminates the mandate for the EPA to select the "least burdensome" regulatory approach for toxic substances. A key change requires the EPA to confirm a new chemical's safety *before* it can be introduced to the market, a departure from the current system where chemicals can enter circulation unless the EPA explicitly deems them unsafe within a specified timeframe.

Furthermore, the bill empowers the EPA to compel chemical companies to generate necessary toxicity data more efficiently. Under the current law, the EPA must undertake a lengthy and often litigious rulemaking process to request such data. The revised legislation also directs the EPA to take more stringent actions against persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic chemicals, and to ensure that chemical safety considers vulnerable populations, including infants, seniors, and chemical industry workers.

**Advancements in Animal Protection and Cancer Cluster Research**

Animal protection and animal rights organizations have welcomed a provision designed to decrease the use of animal-based toxicity testing methods by the EPA and chemical companies. The bill tasks the EPA with prioritizing non-animal-based methods "to the extent practicable" and requires the agency to develop a plan for researching, developing, and implementing advanced non-animal testing approaches, such as computational modeling, high-throughput screening, and cell-culture techniques.

Additionally, the bill includes "Trevor's Law," which encourages federal agencies to investigate "cancer clusters"—geographic areas exhibiting an unusually high incidence of cancer cases that may be attributable to a shared environmental factor.

However, the Society of Toxicology, based in Reston, Virginia, while generally supportive of the bill, expressed reservations about the inclusion of the cancer cluster measure and other specific topic- or chemical-focused language. Society President John Morris noted in a May 23rd letter that such inclusions "detracts from the wider range of priority chemical-specific or analytical issues that, as toxicologists, we address every day."

**A Rocky Path to Consensus**

The TSCA reform bill is the culmination of years of negotiations involving bipartisan lawmakers and a diverse array of stakeholders. Numerous prior attempts to revise TSCA faltered due to an inability to forge consensus among competing interests, including chemical manufacturers and environmental groups. The current success was achieved despite a contentious political environment in Washington, D.C., and a divided government.

The process involved both the House and Senate initially passing distinct, bipartisan versions of TSCA reform. Months of negotiation followed to reconcile these differences. For example, the fate of animal testing provisions, present in the Senate bill but absent from the House's, remained uncertain. Senator Cory Booker (D–NJ), a proponent of these provisions, expressed pride in their preservation, telling reporters on May 19th that the bill would "severely restrict the unnecessary cruelty to animals."

A more significant point of contention involved concerns raised by progressive Democrats and environmental groups regarding potential weakening of states' authority to enact their own chemical regulations. Senator Barbara Boxer (D–CA), the leading Democrat on the Senate environment panel, had strongly opposed language in the Senate bill that would have preserved existing state regulations but limited the creation of new ones. Ultimately, Boxer lent her support to the final compromise, acknowledging that while imperfect on this issue, it represented an improvement over current law. "What a battle that was," she stated. "Well, we no longer have that battle."

Not all lawmakers were convinced. As the House voted 403 to 12 to approve the reform measure, Representative Paul Tonko (D–NY) cited the state preemption provisions as a reason for his opposition. He was among only nine House Democrats to vote against the bill, joined by three House Republicans.

The reform measure also revealed divisions among interest groups. Some, like the Breast Cancer Fund, opposed the bill, while others, such as the Natural Resources Defense Council, remained noncommittal. However, many industry groups and some environmental organizations endorsed the final legislation. Senator Bernie Sanders (D–VT), a candidate for the Democratic presidential nomination, identified both positive and negative aspects, particularly criticizing the preemption language for potentially hindering Vermont's ability to implement stricter regulations than federal standards. "That makes no sense… federal chemical regulations should be a floor, not a ceiling," Sanders stated, without indicating his final stance on the bill.

*Updated 5/26/2016, 2:20 p.m.: This report has been updated to reflect the latest information regarding the timing of the Senate vote.*

The U.S. Senate has passed the Frank R. Lautenberg Chemical Safety for the 21st Century Act, a landmark environmental bill poised to revolutionize federal chemical oversight by updating the forty-year-old Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) and marking the most significant environmental reform in a quarter-century. This pivotal legislation aims to provide chemical producers with clear regulatory guidelines and empower the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to gather more comprehensive data before approving new chemical substances, thereby impacting everyday American life through products ranging from furniture and children's items to cleaning agents.

Senator Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.) underscored the public's frequent unawareness of chemical dangers, citing flame retardants in home furnishings as an example, and stated, "When people realize their young child is crawling on the floor, nose just inches from the rug, breathing in dust laden with toxins from birth, they are shocked." He emphasized that the bill will foster coordinated efforts for positive change. Co-author Senator David Vitter (R-La.) expressed optimism that enhanced oversight will stimulate economic innovation, commenting, "I am very pleased to have passed a law that enhances our nation's global competitiveness, provides much-needed regulatory certainty for businesses, and requires the federal government to use better science and offer greater transparency." Previously, the EPA faced a substantial burden of proof, needing to demonstrate a chemical's harm before requesting data or testing, allowing many chemicals to enter the market automatically after a ninety-day period, resulting in the EPA testing only a small fraction of the thousands of chemicals produced annually in volumes exceeding 25,000 pounds per substance.

This comprehensive update introduces several crucial changes, including the EPA's enhanced authority to require companies to test new products, the establishment of a more consistent federal regulatory framework while allowing states to request exemptions, and a significant reduction in the reliance on animal testing. Kristie Sullivan, a toxicity expert with the Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine, noted the limitations of animal testing, stating, "We lack information on many chemicals and their effects on diverse human populations because we rely too heavily on slow, unreliable, and costly animal tests," and added that the legislation "will ensure robust protection of human health and the environment by updating toxicity testing methods, allowing the Environmental Protection Agency to gather better information more rapidly." Most manufacturers have supported this update, partly due to a perceived lack of federal action leading several states to enact their own restrictions. Jay Timmons, president of the National Association of Manufacturers, remarked, "The rules for these chemicals will be clearer and more direct. This means time and resources that would have been spent navigating outdated, confusing regulations can now be used to drive innovation and create jobs." However, some environmental organizations have expressed reservations; Carli Jensen, Toxics Campaign Director for U.S. Public Interest Research Group, criticized the bill, suggesting it could preempt state actions during lengthy EPA reviews, stating it "will leave us all at risk." This legislation, expected to be signed by President Obama, originates from a 2012 initiative by the late Sen. Frank Lautenberg, alongside Sens. Vitter and James M. Inhofe (R-Okla.), and its Senate approval was influenced by discussions involving Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.). Sen. Vitter commented, "This is a historic day and a fitting tribute to Frank Lautenberg's years of work for a healthier and safer environment for our children and grandchildren."

Senator Rand Paul's (R-Ky.) recent hold on the Frank R. Lautenberg Chemical Safety for the 21st Century Act has stalled a bipartisan chemical safety reform bill nearing presidential approval. This legislation, designed to modernize the nation's chemical regulation framework, had garnered substantial support, passing the House of Representatives with a commanding 403-to-12 vote. It was slated for a Senate vote this week, a rare instance of bipartisan consensus on a critical public policy issue.

The push for this overhaul stems from widespread agreement that the current law, the Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA), in place for approximately 40 years, is inadequate. Both industry stakeholders and public health advocates, including organizations like the Environmental Defense Fund, concur that TSCA needs modernization. The proposed bill aims to balance industry's need for regulatory certainty with empowering the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to gather crucial information before approving chemicals for commercial use. This comprehensive bill integrates elements from House and Senate proposals, refined through extensive negotiations.

Senator Paul voiced concerns about the bill's length and perceived lack of sufficient scrutiny, stating on the Senate floor that the 180-page bill warranted more thorough review. His objections focused on provisions he believes could preempt states from enacting their own chemical regulations. Furthermore, Paul expressed apprehension about clauses imposing stricter penalties, including higher fines and potential imprisonment, for individuals knowingly endangering others. He characterized these provisions, particularly those increasing penalties for deliberate violations, as introducing "new criminalization" and creating "new crimes at the federal level."

The timing of Senator Paul's objection frustrated proponents. Senator Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.), a key author, had expressed optimism just hours before the hold. Merkley likened the legislative process to nearing the finish line of a marathon, stating, "It’s been like we’ve been running a marathon and we can see the tape at the end of the block. We want to get across the finish line before something untoward happens."

Advocates expressed deep disappointment. Donelle Harder, a spokesperson for Senator James M. Inhofe (R-Okla.), highlighted the perceived delay, noting Senator Paul had not raised objections when the bill unanimously passed in December. Harder wryly commented, "All that to say, I couldn’t help but realize today that I have personally carried and birthed a child in the same amount of time in which Rand Paul could have raised objections to the few lines in this bill that he is now calling ‘rushed.’" Richard Denison of the Environmental Defense Fund echoed this sentiment in a blog post, remarking, "Well, it looks like American families will have to wait a bit longer for better protection from toxic chemicals" due to Senator Paul's decision.

The legislative path forward for the Lautenberg Chemical Safety Act now points to a potential Senate vote in June, following the Memorial Day recess. This delay underscores the intricate legislative process and the significant impact a single senator's objection can have. The debate over balancing regulatory certainty, enhanced EPA oversight, and potential federal overreach in criminal penalties continues to shape chemical safety in the United States.

The U.S. Congress is poised to enact significant reforms to chemical safety regulations, addressing long-standing concerns about hazardous substances in everyday products and industrial goods. A pivotal bill, expected to pass the House this Tuesday and the Senate later in the week, aims to overhaul the foundational 1976 chemical safety law. This outdated legislation has historically hampered federal regulators' ability to effectively ban toxic chemicals, even those with well-documented risks like asbestos, a known carcinogen responsible for approximately 120,000 global deaths annually.

This bipartisan legislation will empower the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to systematically review chemicals, determining their potential threats to human health and the environment. A key provision mandates that the EPA prioritize the most dangerous substances, evaluating at least 20 chemicals concurrently and completing each assessment within a seven-year timeframe. Crucially, these reviews will place particular emphasis on the disproportionate harm chemicals can inflict on vulnerable populations, including children, industrial workers, pregnant women, and low-income communities. Furthermore, chemicals frequently stored in proximity to drinking water sources will be elevated in the review queue.

These proposed changes mark a substantial advancement from the Toxic Substances Control Act of 1976. Under that original law, the EPA has managed to ban or restrict the production or use of a mere five chemicals out of the approximately 85,000 currently in circulation. The 1976 act's structure has proven to be a significant impediment, partly because it does not compel manufacturers to submit safety data prior to market release. Judicial interpretations have also frequently favored industry interests. For instance, while the EPA banned most asbestos-containing products in 1989, the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit overturned this ban for numerous products two years later, allowing some to remain on the market.

The Senate has passed chemicals policy reform legislation, a move that the Environmental Working Group (EWG) argues will not adequately protect human health or the environment. This bill now heads to the President's desk.

Following the Senate's voice vote, EWG leadership voiced significant concerns. EWG President Ken Cook stated, "The public health community did not request a toxics bill that is merely 'better than current law.' The existing law is so weak that it could not even withstand industry challenges to ban a substance as dangerous as asbestos." Cook emphasized the urgent need for legislation that effectively shields people, especially children, from the thousands of toxic chemicals linked to serious health issues like cancer, birth defects, and nervous system disorders, asserting that the current bill falls short of this critical objective.

Scott Faber, EWG Senior Vice President for Government Affairs, echoed these sentiments, highlighting the inadequacy of the reform.

query: What programs and waivers are enabling HISD to provide free meals to students throughout the year?

documents.text: HISD will serve meals free of charge to students all year, thanks to a combination of waivers and programs at the state and federal level. HISD serves free breakfast in the classroom all year long, but support for the morning meal will also come from the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the Texas Department of Agriculture until Sept. 30. The departments will also help HISD serve lunch free of charge to the one-third of HISD students not already covered by the Community Eligibility Program. Earlier this year, HISD certified 191 schools to participate in the Community Eligibility Program and receive free breakfast and lunch. The district will work with both the USDA and TDA to gain additional guidance on extending their support beyond Sept. 30. In addition, the district’s application to offer free supper to all the students was recently approved by the Texas Department of Agriculture. Each principal will work with nutrition services to decide how the free supper program will be offered on their campus. “The flooding that is affecting the city of Houston has been devastating to so many. Some of the areas that are the hardest hit are filled with working parents whose limited funds will need to go toward recovery efforts. Free school meals will give our families one less concern as they begin the process of restoring their lives” said Houston ISD Superintendent Richard Carranza. “We will work closely with the USDA and TDA to ensure the provision is extended as necessary.” HISD is still asking parents and guardians to complete and return the application. The National School Lunch/School Breakfast Program applications and surveys are available at https://mealapps.houstonisd.org. The information received in the forms helps the district to secure funding and track student data. “It will take months, possibly years for the city to recover. We expect families to be displaced, students to attend new schools, and many of them possibly using alternative ways to travel to and from school,” said Nutrition Services Officer Betti Wiggins. “We want to reduce any stress connected to food while families work toward getting their personal affairs in order. All HISD students will have access to good food, made with as many local and fresh ingredients as possible and served with love and a smile.”

Corporate giants are making big contributions to disaster relief organizations to help victims of Tropical Storm Harvey. The storm is expected to leave behind billions of dollars in damages. Companies have pledged more than $157 million to relief efforts as of Saturday afternoon, according to an estimate from the U.S. Chamber of Commerce. Sixty-nine companies had donated $1 million or more so far. Walmart has pledged up to $20 million in cash, products and matching donations, while Verizon has contributed $10 million. On Friday, tech billionaire Michael Dell pledged $36 million to relief efforts. The figures are constantly changing as contributions continue to pour in -- and employee gift matching programs and customer donations are already bringing in even more funds. "The corporate response thus far [has] been strong," Marc DeCourcey, senior vice president of the Chamber of Commerce Foundation, told CNNMoney. Related: Impact Your World - How you can help In 2005, Hurricane Katrina and Rita brought in more than $1 billion in business aid within the first six months of each disaster, according to the Chamber of Commerce Center for Corporate Citizenship. The Indian Ocean tsunami in 2004 raised $566 million from corporate donations, while the 2011 Japanese earthquake and tsunami brought in $301 million. Hurricane Sandy in 2012 raised $141 million within the first six months in business aid, per the Chamber's data. Companies have also gone beyond just writing a check, by volunteering, providing in-kind donations like water and food, and encouraging employees to donate by matching contributions, according to Mark Shamley, the president and CEO of the Association of Corporate Citizenship Professionals. "The bottom line is companies have embraced their civic duty to respond to disasters in ways that transcend check writing," he said. When companies respond to disasters, they build a tremendous amount of good will externally and internally, says Una Osili, associate dean at the Indiana University Lilly Family School of Philanthropy. "Employees themselves want to work at companies that are engaged in a broader social good and making the community better," she said. Many corporate giants are overwhelmingly donating to the Red Cross. "We are extremely pleased and gratified by the outpouring of support we are receiving to help those affected," a Red Cross spokeswoman told CNNMoney. Here's a running list of what foreign and domestic companies are doing to help. That includes $900,000 in grants to the Red Cross, Americares and Direct Relief, and $100,000 in healthcare and nutrition products. Drake will donate $200,000 to a Hurricane Harvey relief fundraiser hosted by Houstan Texans player J.J. Watt on YouCaring. The rapper announced his contribution in an Instagram video posted on Thursday, and encouraged viewers to donate what they can. ADVERTISEMENT On Monday, Drake joined celebrities around the world in expressing sympathy and concern for the people of Texas. "Houston has truly been a home to me over the last 8 years," the rapper wrote. Each year, Drake hosts the Houston Appreciation Weekend in the Texas city, the place he credits for launching his career.

Beyoncé, through her BeyGOOD Foundation, has launched a major fundraising effort to aid Hurricane Harvey recovery. Her official website now features a dedicated page, guiding fans to donate to two crucial local groups: Bread of Life and the Greater Houston Community Foundation. These organizations have played a vital role in supporting individuals displaced by Hurricane Harvey since its initial impact.

The money collected will fund "long-term revitalization" initiatives, tackling the ongoing needs of the communities affected by the storm. Furthermore, donations will help quickly acquire necessary supplies for storm survivors, such as cots, baby items, and wheelchairs. Beyoncé publicly reaffirmed her dedication earlier this week, announcing her team's work to "implement a plan to help as many as we can."

This charitable action by Beyoncé is part of a wider celebrity response to the disaster. For instance, Drake has also committed significant financial aid to those affected by the hurricane. He contributed $200,000 to a crowdfunding campaign and released a video encouraging his followers to donate. In his message, Drake praised the relief workers, saying, "To the brave men and women that have assisted in aid, relief, rescue, your actions are truly heroic." You can view the video he shared below.

The J.J. Watt Harvey Relief Fund has achieved a monumental milestone, surpassing an incredible $10 million in donations. This remarkable feat was significantly bolstered by substantial contributions from prominent figures, including beloved television host Ellen DeGeneres and chart-topping artist Miley Cyrus.

In a heartwarming display of generosity, Ellen DeGeneres presented a check for a staggering $1 million from Walmart during a recent show taping. This substantial contribution was in addition to her prior personal donation of $50,000, underscoring her deep commitment to aiding those affected by the devastating Hurricane Harvey.

Miley Cyrus also made a profound impact with her own generous donation of $500,000. Cyrus, visibly moved, shared her heartfelt sentiments regarding the tragedy that has profoundly impacted Houston, affectionately known as H-Town.

J.J. Watt, who joined the show via FaceTime, expressed his profound gratitude, stating, "I have no words." He assured viewers that these significant financial contributions would be instrumental in providing extensive assistance to the victims of the hurricane, emphasizing the far-reaching impact of the collective generosity. The J.J. Watt charity initiative continues its impressive momentum, demonstrating the power of celebrity and public support in times of crisis.

As Hurricane Harvey's devastating floodwaters recede, the immense scale of its destruction is becoming starkly apparent, with Texas Governor Greg Abbott warning that recovery costs could surpass those of Hurricane Katrina, which inflicted over $100 billion in damages. For those outside the affected regions eager to contribute to the relief efforts, a multitude of reputable organizations are actively providing critical support and assistance to survivors.

To effectively aid in the Hurricane Harvey relief effort, consider supporting these vetted organizations, many of which are recognized by national bodies like the **National Voluntary Organizations Active in Disaster (NVOAD)**, which **FEMA** recommends consulting for a comprehensive list of disaster relief groups. Local and regional entities such as the **Hurricane Harvey Relief Fund**, established by Houston Mayor Sylvester Turner and managed by the **Greater Houston Community Foundation**, are addressing immediate needs and long-term recovery, including minor home repairs, with donations accepted online or via text. The **Boys & Girls Clubs of Greater Houston** are offering free access and extended hours, while food banks like the **Houston Food Bank** and **Food Bank of Corpus Christi** are actively seeking donations to combat food insecurity. For vital blood donations, the **South Texas Blood and Tissue Center** and **Carter BloodCare**, serving hospitals across North, Central, and East Texas, are crucial, with organizations like the **AABB**, **America’s Blood Centers**, the **American Red Cross**, and the **Armed Services Blood Program** specifically calling for type O-positive blood. Animal welfare is being championed by the **Houston Humane Society**, **San Antonio Humane Society**, and the **Houston Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals**, all accepting donations. The **Texas Diaper Bank** is requesting essential diapers and wipes, and the **L.G.B.T.Q. Disaster Relief Fund** is providing counseling, case management, and direct aid for rebuilding and essentials.

On a national scale, organizations like **All Hands Volunteers** are focused on repairing damaged homes and clearing debris, while the **American Kidney Fund** provides emergency financial grants to dialysis patients. The **American Red Cross** accepts donations, and **AmeriCares** is delivering essential medicines and supplies. **Catholic Charities** offers comprehensive support including food, clothing, and shelter to all in need. **Direct Relief** is shipping over $100 million in medical inventory, and **Matthew 25: Ministries** is distributing personal care kits, cleaning supplies, and other necessities. **The Salvation Army** is accepting donations through various channels, and **Save the Children** is providing baby supplies and establishing child-friendly spaces. **Samaritan’s Purse** volunteer teams are assisting homeowners with tasks such as tree removal and roof tarping, and **SBP**, a national disaster recovery nonprofit, is conducting damage assessments and aiding home rebuilding. Online platforms like **Airbnb** are waiving service fees and facilitating free housing, while **GoFundMe** features dedicated Hurricane Harvey campaigns, with notable contributions like Chris Young's $100,000, and **YouCaring** saw J.J. Watt's fundraising page meet its $20 million goal. **GlobalGiving**'s Hurricane Harvey Relief Fund supports local organizations providing immediate necessities. To avoid scams, **Charity Navigator** offers a list of responding charities and a nonprofit database, and the **Internal Revenue Service (IRS)** provides tools to verify tax-deductible contributions. Suspected fraud should be reported to the **National Center for Disaster Fraud**, and both **Charity Navigator** and the **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)** offer advice on avoiding fraudulent charities, with the FTC cautioning against organizations that appear suddenly post-disaster and may lack effective infrastructure.

In the aftermath of Hurricane Harvey making landfall, the business community is mobilizing their expertise, capabilities, and community resources to assist with the relief and recovery efforts. Use this form to submit how your company is aiding in the Hurricane Harvey relief and recovery efforts. Disclaimer: This tracker is a compilation of publically available and company-summited data and may not be fully representative of all private sector giving.

J.J. Watt, the Houston Texans' star defensive end, spearheaded an extraordinary fundraising effort in the wake of Hurricane Harvey, amassing over $4.9 million to aid devastated communities and becoming a pivotal figure in the city's recovery. This initiative, launched with remarkable speed, predated significant contributions from many established team owners, highlighting Watt's immediate and personal commitment to his adopted city.

Watt's dedication to Houston was evident from the storm's onset. Initially in New Orleans with the Texans, he leveraged his substantial Twitter following to engage supporters. Upon returning to Houston, his focus sharpened, and he was among the first to publicly address the crisis, bypassing traditional channels to initiate his personal fundraising campaign. In a heartfelt video message, Watt pleaded for support, stating, "That's our city. It's very tough to watch your city get hit by such a bad storm and not be there to help. … So what I do want to do is, I want to start a fundraiser, because I know that these recovery efforts are going to be massive."

The campaign began with an ambitious yet modest goal of $200,000, with Watt himself contributing $100,000 to the Houston Flood Relief Fund for Hurricane Harvey victims via YouCaring.com/JJWatt. The public's response was swift and overwhelming. The initial $200,000 goal was surpassed in under two hours, prompting Watt to rapidly increase the target to $500,000 due to unprecedented donor engagement. The momentum continued to build with contributions like $50,000 from new Rockets guard Chris Paul, and the goal was subsequently escalated to $1 million, then $1.5 million, and rapidly to $2 million and $3 million within a mere 16 hours. The final total exceeded $4.9 million, raised by over 48,565 donors in just a few days.

This remarkable generosity garnered widespread admiration for Watt, a three-time NFL Defensive Player of the Year and a beloved figure among fans. His efforts galvanized the community and inspired significant contributions from other prominent figures, including Texans owner Bob McNair's $1 million pledge to the United Way, matched by the NFL Foundation; Astros owner Jim Crane's $4 million commitment to Harvey relief; Leslie Alexander, owner of the Houston Rockets, who increased his pledge from $4 million to $10 million; and Tennessee Titans owner Amy Adams Strunk's $1 million donation to Watt's fund. Watt expressed profound gratitude for the collective response, remarking, "It's been an unbelievable display of what can happen when people come together. … That's what Houston's all about. It's a very diverse city. It's a very resilient city. It's been through things like this before - maybe not of this magnitude, but it's been through these types of situations. People are coming together, people are helping raise each other up." Houston Texans coach Bill O'Brien also voiced his support, dedicating the upcoming season to the city and its residents, assuring them the team would play with maximum effort for Houston. Watt's commitment extends beyond fundraising; he is dedicated to ensuring donated funds directly benefit those in need, with no allocation for administrative fees, a principle consistent with his past philanthropic endeavors that have positively impacted first responders, educators, young athletes, and children facing illness. During Houston's most challenging period, J.J. Watt's unwavering dedication and the immense support he generated provided a powerful beacon of hope and resilience.

MyPillow CEO donates 60,000 pillows to Hurricane Harvey relief efforts Sometimes one of the best things in the middle of a disaster is the comfort of a pillow and a period of rest, however long it might be. This week the CEO of MyPillow, Mike Lindell, announced that his company was sending 60,000 pillows to Texas to aid in relief efforts. The CEO of MyPillow just sent 60,000 pillows to flood victims in the shelters in Houston. 👍 @realMikeLindell — Mark Dice (@MarkDice) August 30, 2017 The pillows can be seen in commercials on cable and are touted as being more comfortable than standard pillows in the market. The company also released details on additional monetary relief coming to the region, beyond the donation of pillows.
