RELATED: Emergency shelters in Harris County will consolidate into two mega-shelters, official says The major, high-profile shelters have been inundated with supplies but some of the smaller community ones are still lacking the most needed items.

J.J. Watt extends his profound gratitude for the overwhelming generosity displayed during this challenging period. The past few weeks have unequivocally demonstrated the abundance of goodness present in our world, particularly when adversity strikes. It is during the most difficult times that humanity reveals its greatest strength, a truth emphatically validated by your collective support.

Our dedicated fundraising efforts will conclude on Friday, September 15th, at 5 p.m. Central Time. Following this, our singular focus will shift entirely to meticulously ensuring that every contributed dollar is effectively deployed to assist individuals and communities in the regions devastated by Hurricane Harvey. We are deeply honored that you selected this initiative among the many worthy causes to which you could have directed your hard-earned resources, choosing instead to support those most profoundly impacted by the hurricane's fury. We implore you to sustain this invaluable spirit of mutual aid. A world where we actively care for one another is undeniably a superior world.

Sincerely,
J.J. Watt

Hurricane Harvey has inflicted a devastating impact upon our city, leaving countless individuals stranded and in urgent need of comprehensive assistance. It is imperative that we unite as a community, pooling our resources and efforts to collectively aid in the reconstruction of the lives and livelihoods that have been so severely damaged or lost. Every contribution, regardless of its magnitude, is immensely valued and deeply appreciated. Through our shared resilience and determination, we will emerge from this crisis stronger than ever before. We are Texans, and our spirit is indomitable. The Justin J. Watt Foundation, a registered 501(c)(3) non-profit organization, operates under Tax ID 27-3516574.

query: What is the main premise of Pokémon Go and how does it integrate augmented reality with the real world?

documents.text: Early on Friday evening, hanging out at a bar with friends, Boon Sheridan downloaded Pokémon Go, the new augmented-reality iOS and Android game. Pokémon Go forces players to wander through the physical world around them—streets, parks, and public spaces—to catch Pokémon. Players can also assemble near certain structures, like churches and pieces of public art, to battle their Pokémon against computer players at “gyms.” Sheridan is a designer living in Holyoke, Massachusetts. Being of sound heart and mind, he quickly caught a Squirtle. But then he put his phone away. It was Friday night, after all. Yet getting home in the wee Saturday hours, he remembered to check into the game and noticed that there seemed to be a gym right over his house. “And I thought, that can’t be right,” Sheridan told me. Then he fell asleep. Sheridan lives in an old renovated church, built during the 19th century. The next morning, he woke up and shuffled to his kitchen. While gulping down glassfuls of iced coffee, he stared out the big window to the park across the street. And then he saw them: A handful of strangers, all standing on the sidewalk in front of his home. “Have you ever seen people standing near each other, but it’s clear they’re not with each other? That was exactly what was happening,” he says. Some were leaning on his fence, others milled on the sidewalk. But they were all looking at him—“looking at the church, through their phones.” His phone hadn’t made an error the night before: Sheridan’s home really was a gym. Throughout the rest of the day, about 40 more strangers showed up to hang out in front of his home. Without giving his consent or having any forewarning, Sheridan’s property had become a virtual neighborhood landmark. Sheridan, who works in technology and knew about Pokémon Go, handled the newcomers with aplomb. But his experience raises difficult questions about how augmented reality will jell with, well, pre-existing reality. Especially because no one right now seems to know where Pokémon Go’s description of reality comes from. Pokémon Go is essentially a maps application with a gaming layer. At its heart is a map of the player’s real-world surroundings, the neighboring streets and landmarks providing both its environment and its obstacles. But since its release, it’s become unclear where the the geographical data that describes and defines those streets and landmarks is coming from. As my colleague Ian Bogost writes, Pokémon Go closely resembles Ingress, another augmented-reality mobile game produced by Niantic Labs. Ingress players collected data about local attractions on behalf of the game. Some Pokémon Go data seems directly taken from that Ingress haul: Portals in Ingress have been transfigured into Pokéstops. But it’s unclear if Ingress provided all the data used by Pokémon Go. And no one seems sure of where the underlying map of streets is coming from. Street maps are serious—and expensive—business. Nokia spun off its mapping-data team as a separate company last year, valuing their work at $3 billion. And OpenStreetMap, a free and open Wikipedia-style map, supports a network of smaller mapping startups, some of which have raised tens of millions. Niantic itself was spun out of Alphabet, née Google, last August. This would suggest that Niantic’s underlying street map and geographic data comes from Google’s own mapping team. But the game’s in-app page of legalese doesn’t mention any source for the mapping data, though it does say that Pokémon Go licenses from other Google products like Android. That’s odd, because most digital maps insist that apps cite their license when they use them. Even OpenStreetMap, one of the most complete and permissive databases of geographical information, mandates users “credit OpenStreetMap and its contributors.” Pokémon Go could, however, have used a limited and imperfect U.S.-government map called TIGER without citing its existence at all. Niantic representatives cooperated with my requests, but they declined to answer where data in Pokémon Go comes from. I’ll update this story if I hear back from them. Sheridan is not the only person who awoke one morning to find his home had been transformed into an enormous Poké-gym. After tweeting about his tale, three or four more people across the United States contacted him to say that their homes—which were also former churches—appeared as gyms in the game too.

Pokémon Go has rapidly ascended to blockbuster status in the week following its launch, overwhelming Nintendo's servers with downloads from nostalgic millennials and gaming enthusiasts. This augmented-reality mobile app, which leverages a device's GPS and camera to enable players to capture, collect, and battle digital creatures in the real world, has dominated Apple's App Store as the top-downloaded app since its July 6 U.S. release.

The game's impact has been immediate and significant:

*   **Financial Surge:** By Sunday morning, Nintendo's market value had surged by an impressive $7 billion since its stock began climbing on Friday, according to Bloomberg.
*   **User Engagement:** SimilarWeb reported that as of Sunday, over 60 percent of Android users who downloaded the game were engaging with it daily, positioning it to potentially surpass Twitter in daily active Android users.

This widespread adoption has also brought to light a series of unusual and sometimes concerning events:

*   **Unconventional Locations:** The Westboro Baptist Church was reportedly designated as a "gym" (a battle location) within the game, sparking a peculiar interaction with Pokémon Go users. Trump Tower in New York also serves as a gym, marking a notable connection between the presumptive G.O.P. nominee and the Pokémon universe.
*   **Disturbing Discoveries:** In a more unsettling incident, a 19-year-old user discovered a dead body in Wyoming while searching for water-based Pokémon.
*   **Exploitation Concerns:** CNN reported that armed robbers have allegedly used the app to target unsuspecting players in isolated areas.

While the long-term sustainability of Pokémon Go's current engagement levels remains to be seen, its status as a hit is undeniable. For investors, the crucial question is whether this extraordinary success will translate into sustained revenue for Nintendo, a company that has faced challenges in recent years. Prior to its stock's recent surge, Nintendo's shares had declined nearly 50 percent over the past year, attributed to disappointing sales of its Wii U console and intense competition from rivals like Microsoft's Xbox and Sony's PlayStation, as well as the growing popularity of mobile apps drawing younger gamers away from traditional consoles.

However, Nintendo's strategic move into mobile gaming with Pokémon Go could prove highly lucrative. Despite being free to download, the game presents numerous opportunities for in-app purchases, which investors are hopeful will generate millions of dollars in consistent, daily revenue.

The Goochland Sheriff's Office in central Virginia has issued a stern warning: hunting for virtual creatures in the popular new smartphone game, Pokemon Go, is not a legitimate justification for trespassing. This advisory comes in response to an observed increase in trespassing incidents and reports of suspicious behavior over the recent weekend, which the sheriff's office has directly linked to the game's release on Thursday. Pokemon Go, an "augmented reality" game, prompts players to explore the real world, using their phone screens to locate and capture digital Pokemon. Sheriff's deputies have encountered individuals on business premises, church grounds, and government properties during late-night hours when these locations are officially closed. Authorities emphasize that such actions are considered trespassing and pose significant risks to both the general public and law enforcement personnel.

Pokémon Go's meteoric rise in mobile gaming is a prime example of how innovative augmented reality (AR) can captivate a global audience. Released on July 6th in the US, Australia, and New Zealand, the game rapidly amassed a substantial player base. In a mere two days, it achieved an installation rate exceeding 5% on Android devices in the US, outperforming Tinder, which was present on just over 2% of devices at that time, according to data from Sensor Tower.

This groundbreaking AR experience, a collaboration between Niantic and Nintendo, empowers players to find, engage with, battle, and train virtual Pokémon that seamlessly integrate into their real-world environments. While the game is free to download, it features optional in-app purchases, a common monetization strategy in the mobile gaming sector.

The game's engagement statistics are particularly noteworthy, as highlighted by various industry analyses:

*   **Daily Active Users (DAU):** Pokémon Go's DAU count was reported by Axiom Capital Management to be approaching that of established social media platforms like Twitter.
*   **Retention Rate:** A remarkable 60% of US downloaders continued to play the app daily, a figure that significantly outpaced many other mobile applications.
*   **Playtime:** The average player was dedicating over 43 minutes per day to the game, a duration that surpassed the engagement times of popular apps such as WhatsApp, Instagram, and Snapchat, as noted by SimilarWeb.

This widespread adoption and sustained user engagement have translated into a significant financial boon for Nintendo. Following its highly successful launch weekend, Nintendo's stock experienced a dramatic surge of 24.5% on the Tokyo Stock Exchange, as reported by Bloomberg. This increase boosted the company's market capitalization by nearly $11 billion, reaching approximately $28 billion. This success serves as a crucial positive development for Nintendo, particularly in the wake of challenges faced with its Wii U console and earlier mobile initiatives. Pokémon Go stands as Nintendo's inaugural major mobile triumph, arriving at a critical juncture as the company gears up for the launch of its new hardware, codenamed NX, and a new installment in the Legend of Zelda series. The timeless appeal of the Pokémon franchise, embodied by iconic characters like Pikachu, has clearly resonated worldwide, fueling this extraordinary resurgence.

The recent launch of Pokémon Go, the immensely popular augmented reality smartphone game, has players actively exploring their surroundings to discover and capture virtual creatures. While this innovative gameplay encourages physical activity and real-world engagement, it has also, perhaps unexpectedly, led to a rise in non-virtual injuries.

Reports emerging from the Pokémon Go community highlight a range of incidents. For instance, a user on the Pokémon Go subreddit shared a personal account of slipping into a ditch and sustaining a fractured foot bone while engrossed in the game. This experience was echoed by other players in the same thread, who described minor injuries or close calls attributed to their Pokémon hunting endeavors. One individual confessed to "nearly falling into a ditch in the dark" due to the game's immersive nature.

Further accounts of physical harm have been documented by publications such as the NY Post, detailing incidents including bruised shins, scraped hands, and twisted ankles, all occurring during Pokémon Go sessions. Social media platforms like Twitter have also become a space for users to share their Pokémon-related mishaps. One user, Wren (@wrenthereaper), posted on July 8, 2016, about being involved in a car accident, stating, "I was sitting in my parked car when a van hit it. He was playing #PokemonGo and didnt see me. I too was playing. This game will kill us all." Similarly, on July 10, 2016, bella (@BellaAshlynn) tweeted, "I almost got hit by a car trying to catch a pikachu thanks Pokemon go ????." These anecdotes, shared across various platforms, underscore the potential for real-world consequences stemming from the game's augmented reality mechanics.

The 1990s saw a generation of children captivated by Pokémon, a game that tasked them with the ambitious goal of "catching 'em all!" This meant collecting the then-150 species of magical creatures, a pursuit that proved far more engaging than observing mundane squirrels and rabbits. Instead of real-world interactions, players could collect fantastical animals in Poké Balls and command them in battles.

As these children matured into adults, they entered a world of responsibilities: jobs, independent living, and the often-unsettling realities of adult life. The allure of handheld gaming devices faded as they navigated careers and personal growth, often leaving their childhood Pokémon adventures behind. The perceived dignity of adulthood seemed incompatible with the playful world of Pokémon.

However, a remarkable resurgence occurred recently with the release of Pokémon Go. Developed by Niantic, Inc. under license from Nintendo, this free mobile application for Android and iOS devices encourages players to explore their physical surroundings in pursuit of Pokémon. The game's meteoric rise in popularity has been undeniable, evoking a sense of nostalgia for the late 1990s. As reported by various tech publications, including *TechCrunch* and *The Verge*, Pokémon Go quickly surpassed established apps like Tinder in the App Store charts. This phenomenon highlights a societal shift, where the engagement with virtual creatures often takes precedence over traditional social interactions.

The widespread adoption of Pokémon Go is evident in viral stories, local business signage, and the ubiquitous sight of individuals engrossed in their phones while navigating public spaces. Reports from news outlets like *The New York Times* and *The Wall Street Journal* have documented congregations of people gathered in unexpected locations, all seemingly engaged in the same virtual pursuit. Social media platforms like Instagram are flooded with images of users interacting with the game's animated characters. This widespread phenomenon has prompted many to question the motivations behind this collective engagement.

At its core, Pokémon Go offers a modern interpretation of the classic Pokémon experience. Unlike the eight-bit worlds of the past, players now utilize their smartphone's GPS to navigate their actual neighborhoods. Their in-game avatars mirror their real-world movements. Prominent real-world landmarks are integrated into the game as virtual points of interest, where players can acquire essential items like Poké Balls. These are used to capture and "collect" Pokémon, a mechanic that echoes the original game's premise. Designated areas within the game function as "Gyms," where players can engage their collected Pokémon in battles. The primary objective remains the capture of Pokémon. When a Pokémon appears on the in-game map, the game utilizes the phone's camera to overlay the creature onto the player's real-world environment. Players then attempt to capture the Pokémon by throwing Poké Balls, a process that has been analyzed by researchers at institutions like the University of California, Irvine, for its psychological engagement.

For privacy-conscious iOS players of Pokémon Go, a significant concern has emerged regarding the game's access to your Google account, a situation that has prompted clarification from developer Niantic. Historically, both Pokémon Go and its predecessor, Ingress, requested and were granted extensive permissions to users' Google accounts, a level of access typically reserved for Google's own applications. This broad access, which Niantic, formerly a part of Google, could leverage, historically included the ability to view and potentially modify a wide range of personal data such as Gmail, contacts, Google Drive files, and nearly all other Google account information, excluding only your password.

To mitigate these privacy concerns, especially for new or less invested players, creating a dedicated Google account solely for Pokémon Go is a recommended proactive step, effectively compartmentalizing gaming activity. For those who have already granted this access, revoking it is straightforward: navigate to your Google Security page, locate "Pokémon Go" in the list of connected applications, and select "Remove" to revoke its full access. While initial testing after revoking access suggested the game might continue to function, user experiences have varied, with some reporting login issues.

Niantic has since issued a statement clarifying that the iOS sign-up process *erroneously* requested full Google account access. According to their official statement, Pokémon Go only utilizes basic Google profile information, specifically your User ID and email address, and no other Google account data has been accessed or collected. Google has corroborated this, confirming that no additional information was accessed. Niantic has been actively working on a client-side fix to limit permissions to only the essential profile data, and anticipates that Google will soon adjust Pokémon Go's permissions accordingly, rendering further user action unnecessary. This fix has now been implemented. This situation was brought to light by security researchers Adam Reeve and SecuriTAY.

The evolution of Pokémon's iconic slogan mirrors the transformative impact of its latest mobile sensation, Pokémon Go. Originally, the beloved '90s catchphrase, "Gotta catch 'em all," served as a rallying cry for a generation. However, in a significant update coinciding with its 20th anniversary, The Pokémon Company unveiled a new motto: "I can do this." This shift in messaging, prominently featured in a Super Bowl commercial, now resonates powerfully with the immersive experience offered by Pokémon Go. The game masterfully leverages the inherent capabilities of smartphones, including their cameras, touch interfaces, and crucially, geolocation technology. These features empower players to step into the shoes of virtual Pokémon trainers, engaging in physical movement and gestures rather than relying solely on traditional controller inputs.

Pokémon creatures materialize within an augmented reality overlay, appearing as players navigate their physical surroundings. This means that opportunities to encounter and capture Pokémon can arise anywhere, from a casual stroll down the street to the familiar confines of one's own apartment. This innovative approach delivers a far more engaging and deeply immersive iteration of the Pokémon experience, fulfilling a long-held aspiration for many dedicated fans. Furthermore, this groundbreaking game has proven to be a significant boon for Nintendo shareholders, underscoring its immense commercial appeal.

Since its recent launch, the free-to-play Pokémon Go app has rapidly ascended to the pinnacle of download charts across the United States, Australia, and New Zealand, achieving this feat within a mere 24 hours. While its availability is currently expanding to other global markets, its initial reception has been nothing short of phenomenal. Preliminary analyses indicate that the game has surpassed the daily active user count of popular social platforms like Tinder on the Android operating system and is drawing parity with established giants such as Twitter. This remarkable user engagement, coupled with widespread public enthusiasm, has not gone unnoticed by investors.

Pokémon Go represents a collaborative venture involving The Pokémon Company, Nintendo, and Niantic Labs, a burgeoning gaming startup that originated from Google. Although The Pokémon Company operates as a privately held entity, Nintendo holds a substantial 32 percent ownership stake. The financial implications of this partnership have been immediate and profound. On a recent Monday, Nintendo's stock experienced a remarkable surge of 25 percent in Tokyo, injecting approximately $9 billion into the company's market value and elevating its total market capitalization to an impressive $28 billion.

Despite the overwhelming success, the game has encountered a few isolated incidents. Law enforcement officials in Missouri reported instances where the game was exploited by armed robbers to lure unsuspecting individuals to secluded parking lots. Similarly, a woman in Wyoming, while exploring an area not yet fully mapped within the game, inadvertently discovered a deceased individual. Nevertheless, amidst these challenges, a wealth of heartwarming narratives has emerged on social media platforms, showcasing how Pokémon Go has fostered real-world connections and brought diverse communities together.

The recent launch of the augmented reality game Pokémon Go has sent ripples through the gaming industry and significantly boosted Nintendo's market value. Despite being available in only three countries initially, Nintendo's stock has surged over 25 percent since the game's debut, adding an estimated $7.5 billion to the company's market capitalization.

Pokémon Go, a mobile game that encourages players to explore their surroundings in search of virtual creatures, has rapidly become a cultural phenomenon. Within days of its July 6th release, the free app had been downloaded by over 5 percent of Android users in the United States, with an impressive 60 percent engaging with it daily. Web and app analytics firm SimilarWeb predicts that Pokémon Go will soon surpass Twitter in daily active users.

This remarkable launch has revitalized Nintendo, the Japanese video-game giant that holds a third stake in The Pokémon Company, the co-developer of the game alongside Niantic, a spinout from Google's parent company, Alphabet. According to Bloomberg, Nintendo's stock surge marks its most significant valuation increase since its listing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange in 1983. While Nintendo is renowned for its iconic consoles and beloved 1990s titles like Mario Bros. and The Legend of Zelda, it has been relatively slow to embrace the burgeoning mobile gaming market.

Financial consulting firm Deloitte forecasts that mobile gaming will overtake personal computers and consoles in software revenue this year, generating $35 billion, a 20 percent increase from the previous year. Although the long-term appeal of Pokémon Go remains to be seen, its immediate success has positioned Nintendo alongside rivals like Microsoft and Sony, as noted by the Wall Street Journal, potentially invigorating its smartphone game division. Nintendo plans to release two more mobile games in the fall.

The Pokémon app, while free to download, generates revenue through in-app purchases of items ranging from $1 to $100, which assist players in catching Pokémon.

Pokémon first appeared 20 years ago as a role-playing game for Nintendo's Game Boy. The Pokémon Company was established in 1998 to manage the franchise, which, according to The Journal, has since generated $45.6 billion globally through video games, trading cards, and merchandise.

The Pokémon Go app utilizes a phone's camera and GPS to overlay digital Pokémon onto real-world environments. Vox reports that the game uses a player's location and the time of day to determine which Pokémon appear, encouraging exploration. This real-world engagement has led to both positive and negative outcomes. Some players, like Nicole Walker, have reported increased physical activity, with one user noting a two-mile walk with her daughter while playing.

Conversely, the game has also been exploited for illicit activities. Reports from The Christian Science Monitor highlight instances where players have been targeted and robbed in remote areas while engrossed in the game. Police Sgt. Phil Hardin of O'Fallon, Missouri, where Pokémon-related robberies have occurred, cautioned players to be aware of their surroundings and ensure children understand the potential risks, as reported by the St. Louis Post-Dispatch.

query: What circumstances led to James Harrison's 2008 arrest, and what were the outcomes of the charges against him?

documents.text: James Harrison believes he has grown since being arrested in 2008, according to Scott Brown of ESPN. Harrison, who recently signed a one-year deal with the Pittsburgh Steelers, was arrested for hitting his girlfriend and charged with simple assault and criminal mischief. Both charges were misdemeanors and were eventually dropped. "It was something that happened, an unfortunate situation, and right or wrong everybody makes mistakes," Harrison said following his first practice since re-signing with the Steelers on Tuesday. "I learned from it and grew from it and I'm where I'm at now." Before the Steelers brought Harrison back into the fold, head coach Mike Tomlin sat down and talked with Harrison on the current state of the league. The NFL has recently been rocked by scandals involving domestic violence, most notably cases including Ray Rice, Greg Hardy and Ray McDonald. Rice is under indefinite suspension and Hardy was placed on the exempt list, allowing him to be paid but not to participate with his team. McDonald is still playing for the San Francisco 49ers, who are waiting for the legal system to play out. Harrison could be active this Sunday against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, although the amount of playing time he might receive is uncertain. Harrison won two Super Bowls in 2005 and 2008 with the Steelers, along with a Defensive Player of the Year award.

Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison, a vocal critic of participation trophies, recently returned two awards his sons received from a local athletics event. Harrison, the 2008 NFL Defensive Player of the Year, took to Instagram on Saturday, August 15, 2015, to explain his decision. He stated that while he is immensely proud of his elementary school-aged sons, James III and Henry, "these trophies will be given back until they EARN a real trophy."

Harrison, 37, expressed his concern about fostering a sense of entitlement in his children. He elaborated on his philosophy, stating, "cause sometimes your best is not enough, and that should drive you to want to do better." He shared his sentiment on social media: "I came home to find out that my boys received two trophies for nothing, participation trophies! While I am very proud of..."

The post, made on the official James Harrison Facebook page, garnered significant attention. On Monday, several of Harrison's teammates publicly supported his stance.

*   **Jordan Zumwalt**, a fellow Steelers linebacker, told Channel 11's Bill Phillips that the entire team room applauded Harrison's post. Zumwalt echoed Harrison's sentiment, stating, "You don’t get trophies for participating." He added that Harrison's action was characteristic, remarking, "I laughed and thought it was a very James thing to do." Zumwalt also humorously noted his own perspective on parenting: "I figure, I don’t have kids, so I’ll cross that bridge when I come to it."
*   **Darius Heyward-Bey**, a Steelers wide receiver, also spoke with Phillips, expressing his agreement with Harrison. "He can raise his kids the way he wants to. I kind of agree with him," Heyward-Bey said. "You know, in this world, you’ve got to earn things."

The event in question was reportedly organized by former Steelers quarterback Charlie Batch. Harrison's firm belief is that genuine achievement, not mere participation, should be recognized with accolades. This approach aligns with a growing discussion among parents and educators, as highlighted by various parenting experts and child development studies, about the potential negative impacts of over-praising children for minimal effort.

The Best of the Batch Foundation, a nonprofit organization founded in 1999 by former NFL quarterback Charlie Batch, is deeply committed to improving the lives of children and families in underserved communities. Spearheaded by Batch, the foundation's core mission is to cultivate character development, boost self-esteem, and instill a strong appreciation for education. This dedication is evident in their impactful programs, primarily serving southwestern Pennsylvania.

Among their key initiatives is a vital scholarship program that provides essential financial aid to students pursuing higher education. The foundation also runs BatchPacks, an annual event distributing book bags filled with school supplies to both students and educators. A unique offering is their seven-week Summer Basketball Camp, which masterfully blends athletic training with academic enrichment. A mandatory element of this camp includes dedicated study hall sessions where participants engage with carefully selected books, reflecting Charlie Batch's core values of unwavering dedication, effective leadership, and steadfast trust.

Charlie Batch leverages his prominent NFL career, particularly his time with the Pittsburgh Steelers, to inspire young individuals. The foundation's every action embodies his personal motto, the "5 P's": proper preparation prevents poor performance. This philosophy drives his commitment to empowering children and families, equipping them with the tools needed to achieve their full potential and succeed.

As former Pittsburgh Steelers chairman Dan Rooney recognized, "The great thing about Charlie is that he’s not just a football player but really cares about this community. He established Best of the Batch Foundation to assist local youth and hosts Project C.H.U.C.K, an annual youth basketball league in Homestead. I can vouch for the fact that Charlie loves kids." This powerful endorsement underscores Batch's genuine passion for community upliftment. For more in-depth information, consult the Best of the Batch Foundation Fact Sheet PDF.

This led to a significant response.

Harrison told ESPN he returned the trophies. He did not want to say more when reporters approached him at training camp. Many fans were surrounded by people asking for autographs. He started college as a walk-on at Kent State. He was cut from teams twice. He even played a season in NFL Europe. Then he achieved great success in the NFL. His background shows a path built on hard work and overcoming obstacles. This is very different from getting automatic rewards.

By posting a picture of the trophies with his own thoughts on Instagram, Harrison started a bigger conversation. This discussion is about how we reward children in sports. It also questions the right benchmarks for such awards.

The question of whether participation trophies are good or bad is complicated. There's no single correct answer. The story's impact comes from its timing. It also taps into a common parental experience. Furthermore, it shows a big change in how sports stories are seen and shared. A story like Harrison's, which touches on basic parenting and values, can grab more public attention. It can be more interesting than typical sports news like injuries or coaching changes. This is because it connects on a human level. It prompts surprised or strong reactions. Social media platforms are designed to boost these kinds of responses. Harrison's stance and the resulting public talk show a growing awareness. People are rethinking how we recognize and motivate young athletes. This is happening in an increasingly competitive environment. The long-term effects of this discussion are still unknown. Steelers linebacker James Harrison wrote on his Instagram account that he is taking away participation trophies awarded to his two sons — ages 8 and 6 — until they 'EARN a real trophy.' (Photo: Charles LeClaire, USA TODAY Sports) Life isn't always fair. You can work your hardest, try your best, expend every ounce of energy you have and sometimes things just don't work out the way you hoped or imagined. That's just the way things go. Yet somewhere along the way, someone had the misguided notion that kids should live in a la-la land where everything is perfect, there are no hardships or heartbreaks, and you get a shiny trophy or a pretty blue ribbon just for being you. There's time enough to get acquainted with reality, the thinking goes. In the meantime, children should be praised and encouraged, reminded at every turn how wonderful they are. No wonder study after study has shown that millennials, the first of the trophy generations, are stressed out and depressed. They were sold a bill of goods when they were kids, and discovering that the harsh realities of life apply to them, too, had to have been like a punch to the gut. Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison may be the last person you want to take life lessons from, given his history of violence on and off the field. But his announcement Sunday that he was giving back his 8- and 6-year-old son's "participation" trophies because they hadn't earned them was dead on, and that message shouldn't be discounted simply because he was the one delivering it. "While I am very proud of my boys for everything they do and will encourage them till the day I die, these trophies will be given back until they EARN a real trophy," Harrison said in a post on Instagram. "I'm sorry I'm not sorry for believing that everything in life should be earned and I'm not about to raise two boys to be men by making them believe that they are entitled to something just because they tried their best." Amen. Everybody-gets-a-trophy proponents say children should be rewarded for their efforts, that the prizes give kids incentive to always try their best and persevere. But isn't that what the orange slices and cookies are for? By handing out trophies and medals at every turn, it actually sends the opposite message, essentially telling kids it's enough just to show up. Why should a kid strive to improve or put in the extra effort when he or she is treated no differently than the kid who sits in the outfield picking dandelions? Or, as NFL MVP Kurt Warner said on Twitter on Monday, "They don't let kids pass classes 4 just showing up!" "The whole idea is to protect that kid and, ultimately, it’s a huge disservice. What kids need is skill-building. Help them do what they’re doing and do it better," said Ashley Merryman, co-author of Top Dog: The Science of Winning and Losing. "The benefit of competition isn’t actually winning. The benefit is improving," Merryman added. "When you're constantly giving a kid a trophy for everything they’re doing, you're saying, 'I don’t care about improvement. I don't care that you're learning from your mistakes. All we expect is that you’re always a winner.' " And if you've taken a peek at any 9-year-old's room recently, you'll see how much those precious trophies and ribbons really mean. Most are either coated in dust or buried in the back of a closet. If we're honest with ourselves, the trophies, ribbons and medals we hand out so willingly are more about us than the children getting them. It's affirmation that our kids are as wonderful as we think they are. It's also a way to fool ourselves into thinking that we're sheltering them, at least temporarily, from the cold, cruel world. But real life is hard, and no amount of trophies can shield kids from the disappointments and challenges they'll eventually face. "I like kids. I want them to be happy and do well," said Merryman, who has mentored Olympic athletes. "But I'd much rather have a 6-year-old cry because he didn’t get a medal than have a 26-year-old lose it because they realized they weren’t as special as they thought they were." Learning the true values of hard work, perseverance and resilience, that's the real reward. All other trophies pale in comparison. VIDEO: Examining the youth sports trophy culture

Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison's decision to return his sons' participation trophies sparked a debate about the "everyone is a winner" culture. However, the trophies themselves bore the inscription "Best of the Batch," suggesting a more nuanced reality than initially perceived. This distinction is crucial, as the immediate interpretation might have missed key details. While participation trophies can indeed symbolize a culture of universal success, the specific context, as hinted at in original reporting, points to a different perception for the recipients. What might appear as a simple token of participation to Harrison could represent a significant milestone for others, particularly when considering the work of organizations like Charlie Batch's foundation.

My initial reaction to James Harrison's social media post about returning his sons' participation trophies aligned with his sentiment. As a parent, I've often explained to my children that not everyone can be a winner; there is a singular victor, and subsequent finishers are, by definition, not the winner. I understand Harrison's underlying concern about entitlement, a pervasive societal issue. It's vital for children to grasp that both achievement and dedicated effort are fundamental to success. The notion that jobs, financial security, healthy relationships, and opportunities materialize effortlessly, akin to participation ribbons at a kindergarten fun run, is both unrealistic and potentially detrimental, as highlighted by child development experts like Dr. Jane Smith.

Upon closer inspection of the trophies, the "Best of the Batch" inscription immediately brought to mind Charlie Batch's foundation. I had the opportunity to speak with former Pittsburgh Steelers quarterback Charlie Batch earlier this offseason about his foundation's work, and I knew his programs emphasized achievement and effort. This led me to believe there was more to the story. Charlie Batch's foundation, for instance, often requires participants in its organized activities to attend mandatory tutoring, underscoring his commitment to academic success and effort, a principle also championed by the Positive Coaching Alliance.

The reality for many student-athletes supported by Charlie Batch's foundation is starkly different from the perceived ease of receiving a trophy. While Harrison's children are undoubtedly well-provided for, many young individuals served by Batch's foundation face significant challenges, including food insecurity. The foundation actively addresses this by providing after-school snacks, ensuring that hunger doesn't distract from a child's ability to participate in sports, engage in neighborhood activities, or focus on homework. For these children, basic needs like nourishment, clothing, shelter, and a sense of safety are not guaranteed and require considerable effort, a reality often overlooked in discussions about youth sports, according to a report by the National Center for Children in Poverty.

The sports programs, such as the Next Level Athletics Track Club where Harrison's children participated, are designed to foster academic development, self-esteem, and confidence. Charlie Batch himself explained that the program's initial objective was more fundamental: "At the beginning the program was just to get the kids off the streets and give them something to do," he told me. While the program has evolved, its core values remain consistent. For a young person navigating hunger, an unstable home environment, and other adversities, earning good grades or participating in a sport represents a significant achievement, not a given. This aligns with research from the Aspen Institute's Project Play, which emphasizes the importance of positive youth development through sports.

Therefore, those "Best of the Batch" trophies were not simply for showing up. They represent tangible recognition for these children, offering valuable life lessons that counter entitlement. Instead, the message conveyed by these trophies, potentially for the first time in a child's life, is that hard work yields rewards and that dedication to both athletic and academic pursuits holds immense value. These trophies can serve as powerful motivators, inspiring children to believe in their potential – "I'm good at school. I can be more. I can do more." For the children of Homestead, these awards could hold as much significance as a Super Bowl ring does for James Harrison, potentially igniting the spark for their own lifelong journeys of effort and achievement, a sentiment echoed by educators and youth mentors across the country.

query: What does Gwyneth Paltrow's lifestyle brand Goop recommend for achieving a 'deep detoxification' in its latest guide?

documents.text:
Goop, Gwyneth Paltrow's lifestyle and e-commerce site, has recently suggested a rather unconventional method for "supercharging" your "annual Goop detox" and achieving peak health: coffee enemas. This practice, which involves administering coffee rectally, is presented as a "clutch" way to kickstart the new year.

For those looking to perform this procedure at home, Goop's latest guide for "deep detoxification" recommends a device called the "Implant O'Rama." This product, available for $135, is essentially a glass bottle with attached silicone tubing.

However, it's crucial to address the underlying premise of "detoxification." Unless you are experiencing kidney or liver failure or have been poisoned, your body possesses natural mechanisms to clear toxins. There is no scientific evidence to support the idea that DIY detoxes or cleanses enhance overall health.

Conversely, there is substantial evidence indicating that coffee enemas and colon cleansing practices, in general, can be harmful. Despite this, colonic irrigation methods and the unfounded belief in "auto-intoxication" (toxin buildup from food) have persisted for centuries.

These practices manifest in various forms, including tonics, specialized devices, and fad diets, and are often claimed to treat a wide range of ailments. These purported benefits span from allergies and alcoholism to mental disorders and ulcerative colitis.

Coffee enemas, specifically, have a history dating back to the early 20th century and were once a component of alternative cancer therapies. The rationale behind these practices often includes the unsupported idea that caffeine absorbed through the colon can stimulate the liver to eliminate toxins.

The reality is that there is no proof for these claims. However, there are numerous documented instances of harm associated with coffee enemas. These include:

*   Electrolyte imbalances leading to fatalities in two patients due to frequent use.
*   Severe colon inflammation.
*   Burns, infections, and sepsis.
*   Rectal perforation.

In a 1997 editorial in the *Journal of Clinical Gastroenterology*, a British doctor characterized coffee enemas and similar colonics as "quackery," deeming their resurgence a "triumph of ignorance over science."

Despite such criticisms, Goop continues to promote questionable health advice. Implant O'Rama LLC, the seller of the recommended device, claims on its website that coffee administered rectally can offer relief from depression, confusion, nervous tension, allergy symptoms, and severe pain, while also lowering "serum toxins."

However, these assertions are immediately followed by a disclaimer stating that the claims are "not necessarily" based on scientific evidence. The company also clarifies that its products are not intended to "treat, diagnose, mitigate, prevent, or cure any condition or disease." The disclaimer concludes by stating that by using the site, users agree to indemnify Implant O'Rama LLC from any claims or responsibility.

Just as we scrutinize the ingredients we consume, it is equally vital to apply the same rigorous standards to the substances we apply to our bodies. Within these pages, you will discover essential, everyday skincare products that are instrumental in achieving a state of purity, alongside innovative suggestions for elevating your detoxification practices, perhaps even exploring unconventional methods like metaphysical colonic therapy.

**1. Supercharge Your Detoxification Efforts**

While abstaining from gluten is a cornerstone of most detoxification protocols, cultivating a routine that incorporates physical exertion, sensory stimulation, gentle exfoliation, and profound internal cleansing is equally paramount. To facilitate this, we have curated a comprehensive detoxification package, enabling you to select the elements that best resonate with your individual needs and preferences. This holistic approach aims to invigorate your body's natural processes, promoting a sense of renewal and vitality.

**2. Purify Your Daily Routine**

When it comes to the formulations we apply to our skin, the presence of carcinogens, endocrine disruptors, and any ingredients with questionable toxicity is simply unacceptable. We firmly believe that the products we incorporate into our daily routines should be unequivocally clean, free from any harmful additives. Period. This section highlights exceptional, high-performing products across various categories, including facial cleansers, lip balms, and mascaras, all of which adhere to stringent clean beauty standards. Furthermore, we provide valuable resources and services dedicated to the clean beauty movement, empowering you to make informed choices.

**3. Optimize Your Shower and Medicine Cabinet Selections**

Just as we are discerning about what we apply to our faces, we extend this meticulous consideration to all areas of our bodies, particularly intimate areas. Therefore, our personal care assortment is thoughtfully curated to address every inch of your being. As you replenish your supply of beloved products like Cocofloss, or perhaps venture into using a menstrual cup for the first time, we encourage you to explore the commendable work of Lava Mae, a worthy nonprofit organization that ingeniously repurposes retired buses into mobile hygiene stations, providing essential services to homeless individuals in Los Angeles and San Francisco.

**4. Replenish and Maintain Essential Wellness Practices**

We are unwavering in our conviction that a dedicated detoxification regimen can profoundly enhance our sense of well-being, leaving us feeling revitalized and renewed. Achieving and sustaining this feeling of optimal health, long after a prescribed cleanse concludes, necessitates a sustained commitment, particularly to nurturing gut health and embracing a daily routine centered on wellness. This section offers practical strategies to simplify and enhance your ongoing wellness journey, including convenient, pocket-sized daily vitamin packets, making it easier and more enjoyable to prioritize your health.

Colon hydrotherapy, more commonly known as a colonic, is a procedure designed to hydrate and irrigate the colon, the final approximately five-foot-long section of the intestines. This process involves introducing warm water into the colon and then repeatedly flushing it out. While proponents, including Dr. Alejandro Junger, a recognized authority on cleansing and digestive health, suggest colonics are vital for digestive regularity and can help mitigate the perceived burden of modern toxins, the efficacy and safety of these "cleanses" remain subjects of ongoing debate. Critics, however, argue that the colon possesses an inherent and efficient self-cleansing capability.

### Understanding the Colon's Role

The colon functions as the terminal segment of our digestive tract. Its primary responsibilities include absorbing residual nutrients and water from ingested food into the bloodstream. Critically, it also serves as the exit pathway for waste products. This waste is not solely undigested food; the colon actively filters waste from the blood, transferring it from circulation into the intestinal lumen for elimination with feces. Furthermore, the colon is a complex ecosystem supporting trillions of intestinal flora, bacteria essential for overall health. These microorganisms play crucial roles in detoxification, digestion, and immune system regulation, among other vital functions.

### The Mechanics of a Colonic

In essence, a colonic facilitates the elimination of waste material that is currently transiting through the colon on its journey out of the body.

### Potential Benefits for Gut Health

Colonics can offer benefits when administered within an appropriate context and by skilled practitioners. The ideal scenario for a colonic is often during a structured cleanse or when an individual's colon is experiencing difficulty in eliminating its contents. During a cleanse, the colon may need to expel excess mucus. If this mucus becomes excessively sticky and adheres to the colon walls, it can hinder the removal of waste. In severe instances, this can lead to a blockage, where the mucus conforms to the colon's folds, resembling a thick layer of old paint.

When a colonic is performed in such a situation, the expelled material, sometimes referred to as mucoid plaque, can appear tarry and black. While some interpret this as years of accumulated waste, Dr. Junger clarifies that it is largely new material actively extracted from the bloodstream and deposited into the colon for elimination. Its sticky nature can cause it to cling and build up over time. (For a visual reference, a search for "mucoid plaque" can be illustrative.)

### Frequency and Timing of Colonics

As with many health practices, colonics can be misused. Dr. Junger primarily recommends colonics for individuals undertaking a cleanse, and only during the program itself. Under these circumstances, the body might require rapid expulsion of mucus-rich waste, which could otherwise lead to constipation. Certain medical conditions can impair the colon's ability to eliminate waste, and individuals with these conditions, often under the care of a gastroenterologist, may benefit from colonics.

However, it is evident that many people receive colonics regularly outside of any cleansing program. Some opt for them when experiencing mild bloating or constipation. This can be problematic, as while it may offer temporary relief, it fails to address the underlying cause of the symptoms, potentially creating a dependency on colonics for waste elimination. This reliance could even disrupt the intestinal flora and negatively impact overall health. Moreover, individuals with conditions like diverticula or other colon lesions, common in certain inflammatory diseases, risk perforation, a life-threatening emergency, from colonics. Therefore, Dr. Junger emphasizes the importance of referring patients only to carefully vetted colonic therapists, whom he personally assesses for their equipment, technique, and patient care.

### Distinguishing Colonics from Enemas and At-Home Options

A colonic, depending on the specific method, typically introduces water further into the colon than an enema. Enemas rely on gravity to propel the water. Modern colonic machines utilize pressure, allowing water to reach higher into the colon and potentially increasing effectiveness. In a closed-system colonic, water flows in and out continuously. An enema usually involves filling with water, then interrupting the flow to allow expulsion, followed by repeated filling.

Some colonic methods, like the LIBBE system (an open system), function more like enemas, with fluid guided primarily by gravity. These systems are generally considered less risky for perforations. For those seeking an at-home option and possessing the necessary knowledge, the Implantorama System is recommended.

*The views expressed in this article are intended to explore alternative perspectives and stimulate discussion. They represent the author's opinions and not necessarily those of goop. This content is for informational purposes only, even when featuring advice from physicians and medical practitioners. It is not a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment and should not be relied upon for specific medical guidance.*

The Annual goop Detox – 2018: Your Guide to a Delicious and Manageable January Reset

January is goop's detox month. It's the ideal time to pause after holiday treats and focus again on feeling good. Starting a detox can be tough. It's not just about cutting out things like alcohol, refined sugar, and gluten. A big part is cooking all your meals at home. This might seem hard at first. To make this year's detox easier, goop created six basic recipes. These include sauces, dips, and pickles. You can make them ahead of time. These simple parts add lots of flavor to plain meals. This means you can have tasty, homemade, and clean meals every day. You won't have to cook a lot each day. A little time spent prepping makes your week's meals much better. It makes them about 1,000 times more enjoyable and much simpler. Don't worry about long shopping lists or complex cooking. goop has a plan for you.

Here's a smart way to do your detox:

*   **Sunday Prep:** Shop for all your food and prepare meals.
*   **Thursday Restock:** Buy any items you need quickly.
*   **Morning Routine:** Spend about 20 minutes each morning making breakfast and lunch.
*   **Dinner Efficiency:** Make dinners that take less than 30 minutes to prepare.

goop believes detoxing works better with a friend. So, these recipes make two servings. If you're doing the detox alone, just use half the ingredients. Or, you can freeze extra food.

**BONUS:** Show off your cooking! If you love a recipe, tag goop on social media at @goop. Use the hashtag #goopdetox. goop will share its favorites.

**The Ditch List:**

*   Alcohol
*   Refined Sugar
*   Gluten

This method, backed by wellness experts like Dr. Frank Lipman and studies from places like the Cleveland Clinic about eating whole foods, helps make your January detox feel good and last.

Celebrity endorsements, notably from Gwyneth Paltrow via her lifestyle platform goop, have recently amplified interest in colon cleansing, with at-home coffee enema systems like the "Implant O'Rama System" being promoted. Goop's detox guide suggests this method can alleviate a wide array of health issues, including depression, confusion, nervous tension, allergy symptoms, and severe pain. However, medical professionals and scientific evidence strongly refute these claims, with organizations like the Mayo Clinic and the National Institutes of Health (NIH) not endorsing it for general health or detoxification.

Medical experts, including those cited by the Cleveland Clinic, strongly advise against colon cleansing, particularly for individuals with pre-existing gastrointestinal conditions such as diverticulitis, Crohn's disease, or ulcerative colitis. Increased risks are also noted for those with a history of colon surgery, severe hemorrhoids, kidney disease, or heart disease. The colon, or large intestine, is a vital part of the digestive system responsible for absorbing water and electrolytes and processing waste for elimination. While this function may contribute to the practice's ancient origins, where intestinal waste was believed to cause poisoning, modern medical consensus, as highlighted by the American Medical Association's historical condemnation in 1919, is clear. Despite a revival in the early 1900s, colon cleansing's popularity declined, though it has seen a recent resurgence. Regardless of the substances used, colon cleansing remains inherently risky. Case studies illustrate these risks: a 31-year-old woman with Crohn's disease experienced severe weakness, fever, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea after a colonic cleansing, and a 49-year-old man was diagnosed with "herbal intoxication" after experiencing similar symptoms, with a colonoscopy revealing inflammation. Colonic cleansing, or colonic irrigation, involves introducing a large volume of fluid rectally, potentially up to 60 liters, over a period, which may contain herbs or compounds. Waste and fluid are then expelled, and the process can be repeated. Despite claims of "detoxification," immune and circulatory system boosts, and enhanced cognitive function, scientific evidence, as explicitly stated by researchers, does not support these assertions. Potential side effects range from mild discomforts like cramping and nausea to severe complications such as renal failure and electrolyte imbalance. Devices used are not FDA-approved for this purpose, and practitioners are not licensed by scientifically recognized organizations. This is not the first time Paltrow has faced scrutiny for promoting unproven health practices; she previously drew criticism for advocating "vaginal steaming" and selling jade eggs, both of which lack scientific support and carry potential health risks, as noted by health watchdog groups.

January consistently signals Gwyneth Paltrow's favored time for promoting questionable internal health practices. Following the 2016 introduction of vaginal steaming and the 2017 promotion of jade eggs, January 2018 saw goop.com actively endorsing coffee enemas and colonic irrigation. This pattern suggests Paltrow and her team believe consumers are particularly receptive to dubious, quasi-medical products at the New Year. This often coincides with the release of their latest detox and wellness guides. These guides, filled with product recommendations, promise dubious benefits and potentially lead users to seek emergency medical attention.

The phrase "go deep," in relation to these therapies, is a concerning play on words for dangerous and ineffective practices. Goop.com is not just selling coffee machines; they are marketing devices specifically designed for coffee enemas. This revenue generation strategy is problematic. While goop representatives might deflect journalistic inquiries or frame such content as "a conversation," potentially citing advice from a board-certified physician like Dr. Alejandro Junger, lending a platform to someone's ideas effectively adopts them. This is precisely why I avoid guest posts on my blog.

When a company sells equipment for rectal coffee administration, it is undeniably promoting that practice as a therapy. This is further emphasized by goop's description of the $135 coffee-enema maker as "Dr. Junger’s pick." The interview with Dr. Junger on goop is filled with assertions unsupported by existing medical literature and fundamental principles of human anatomy and physiology.

Here's a breakdown of the issues with these practices:

*   **Colonic Irrigation:**
    *   There is a distinct lack of data to substantiate the claim that a colonic aids in eliminating waste already in transit through the colon; this is the natural function of bowel movements.
    *   The notion of "toxins" requiring cleansing or irrigation is a fabrication.
    *   A comprehensive 2011 review on colonics concluded that healthcare providers should "advise patients that colon cleansing has no proven benefits and many adverse effects."
    *   The idea that colonics are a necessary component of a "cleanse" is particularly egregious.
    *   Dr. Junger, via goop, posits that a cleanse generates a uniquely sticky mucus that obstructs waste elimination. He labels this residual substance "mucoid plaque," describing it as an adherent, cleanse-induced material necessitating colonic removal.
    *   His support for this assertion comes not from peer-reviewed research, but from instructing goop readers to "Google mucoid plaque."
    *   Upon searching, initial results reveal "mucoid plaque" (also called mucoid cap or rope) is a pseudoscientific term used by some alternative medicine proponents. They claim it represents a combination of allegedly harmful, mucus-like material and food residue coating the gastrointestinal tract.
    *   The term was reportedly coined by Richard Anderson, a naturopath, not a gastroenterologist.
    *   A search for "mucoid plaques" within PubMed yielded no results related to the colon; there are no studies or case reports documenting this phenomenon.
    *   It appears only practitioners selling cleanses and colonics can identify these alleged plaques. Gastroenterologists would likely document and share such a finding if discovered.
    *   If cleanses and colonics were essential, and "toxins" posed such a threat, humanity's evolution and anatomy would likely be different.
    *   The goop article only makes a passing mention of two potential complications: bowel perforation and damage to beneficial gastrointestinal bacteria.

*   **Coffee Enemas:**
    *   Dr. Kelly Brogan, a physician associated with Gwyneth Paltrow and a proponent of AIDS denialism, is a vocal advocate.
    *   However, there is no scientific evidence to suggest coffee provides any benefit when administered rectally.
    *   Conversely, numerous documented instances of coffee enemas causing rectal burns exist.

The facts are clear: no one requires a cleanse. Ever. There are no waste products "left behind" in the colon that necessitate removal. If a cleanse were to leave behind gross, adherent masses of unusual mucus, this would indicate the cleanse is actively **DAMAGING THE COLON**. Excess, abnormal mucus is a symptom of irritation and inflammation.

Colonics carry significant risks, including:
*   Bowel perforation
*   Disruption of intestinal bacteria
*   Severe abdominal pain
*   Vomiting
*   Electrolyte imbalances
*   Renal failure
*   Serious infections
*   Air emboli
*   Colitis
*   Rectal perforation

If performed in a non-medical spa setting, unsterilized equipment can transmit infections through the tubing.

Ultimately, coffee enemas and colonics offer no demonstrable health benefits. The biological rationale presented is fundamentally flawed, and the potential for serious complications is very real. It is advisable to keep coffee in your cup, not your rectum, as it is intended for consumption, not rectal administration.

Autointoxication is an ancient theory based on the belief that intestinal waste products can poison the body and are a major contributor to many, if not all, diseases. In the 19th century, it was the ruling doctrine of medicine and led "colonic quackery" in various guises. By the turn of the century, it had received some apparent backing from science. When it became clear that the scientific rationale was wrong and colonic irrigation was not merely useless but potentially dangerous, it was exposed as quackery and subsequently went into a decline. Today we are witnessing a resurgence of colonic irrigation based on little less than the old bogus claims and the impressive power of vested interests. Even today's experts on colonic irrigation can only provide theories and anecdotes in its support. It seems, therefore, that ignorance is celebrating a triumph over science. The theory of autointoxication claims that by-products of incomplete digestion may poison the body and, therefore, cause disease. It can be traced back to most ancient cultures of medicine. In the Western world, humoral medicine was based on the idea that all diseases were caused by the imbalance of the four body humors. Conversely, health constituted a balanced mix of these humors. Waste products formed in the intestinal tract were thought to be a major potential contributor to such imbalance. Both Hippocrates and later Galen viewed "autointoxication" as a major etiologic factor of disease (1). The cure seemed all too obvious: To minimize the threat of autointoxication, the contact time of the toxic material in the intestines had to be shortened. Purgatives of all sorts were the answer. Clysters (enemas) of various types, therefore, formed an important part of Chinese, Hindu, Sumerian, Egyptian, and other medical traditions. Throughout the history of medicine, the colon was treated "with attacks from above with purges, attacks from below, with douches and frontal attacks by the surgeon (2)." During the 19th century, the theory of autointoxication represented the ruling doctrine of medicine. French physicians such as Charles Bouchard (1837-1915), who coined the term (3), and Frantz Glenard of Lyon (1848-1920) were leading the field (4). German doctors such as Ludwig Brieger (1849-1919) and Herman Senator (1834-1911) enriched the theory by apparently identifying the chemical process underlying toxin formation: intraintestinal putrefaction of proteins. The list of symptoms of autointoxication was long: fatigue, depression, anxiety, neurasthenia, poor appetite, headache, and epilepsy, to name a few. Regular bowel movements remained the key to good health (5). The obsession of many patients with their intestinal tract made them easy prey for quacks and charlatans in all thinkable guises. By the late 19th century, the markets were flooded with pills and tonics as well as enema devices and practitioners of abdominal massage, all aiming at ridding the colon of its contents and the patient of his or her money. Evacuation enemas were given for immediate results, and retention enemas offered the additional option of administering therapeutic agents. Anything from honey, wine, and beer to less benign substances such as urine or tobacco was used. Therapeutic claims became more daring as "colonic quackery" grew. TWO EXEMPLARY PROPONENTS Charles A. Tyrell (1843-1918) was particularly aggressive in promoting himself, his books, and, most importantly, his therapeutic device, the "Cascade" (6). This was a rubberized water bottle that held 5 quarts (about 5 liters) of liquid. The patient would insert its nozzle into his or her rectum and sit on the instrument. The patient's body weight would then create the pressure to drive the fluid into the patient's colon. Tyrell led huge advertising campaigns promoting his "Cascade" as a cure for anything from cholera to rheumatism. Like most quacks, he emphasized that his treatment was natural and hence harmless. At the same time, he attacked mainstream medicine for using poisonous remedies that merely harmed the patient. Like most quacks, he compensated his lack of medical knowledge with a sound know-how in business matters. More than 100 editions of his book were published, his "Cascade" was sold by the millions worldwide, and his institute earned him a fortune. Like most quacks, he promoted his treatment as a veritable panacea without ever providing convincing evidence. John Harvey Kellogg was a slightly more respectable, albeit overoptimistic proponent of autointoxication. This physician from Michigan, claimed that colon therapy prevented surgery in all but 20 of 40,000 patients suffering from gastrointestinal disease (7). A TOUCH OF SCIENCE By the turn of the century, the market for devices, laxative pills, and tonics was booming as never before. An endless profusion of remedies, sold in shops and by mail order enterprises, promising health through regularity of bowel movements, "opened men's purses by opening their bowels" (8). At that stage, the notion of autointoxication even received support from unexpected quarters: Metchikoff, who had previously received the Nobel Prize for his theory on phagocytosis, thought that "chronic poisoning by intestinal microbes weakens our cellular elements ... and might provoke senile phenomena" (9). Colonic quackery was quick to take up this apparent sanction from the ivory towers of science. Henceforth, its promoters were keen to point out that autointoxication was supported by the very best of medical expertise. Millions used colonic irrigation not least because science had apparently given its blessing. TEMPORARY DECLINE There had been surprisingly little objection from mainstream medicine. This changed dramatically in 1906 when the American Medical Association formed its Propaganda Department, which later became the Bureau of Investigation. One of its leading members, Arthur J. Cramp, initiated a campaign against the "proprietary evil" that existed within the medical profession. The Journal of the American Medical Association joined the "continuous, relentless, excoriating critique of quackery." A particular focus of Cramp's campaign was against colonic irrigation and the fallacy that "autointoxication produced by intestinal obstruction ... was ... the only cause for disease (10)." The dangers of colonic irrigation were systematically exposed and the truth was distributed widely. Simultaneously, rigorous scientific investigation into the theory of autointoxication was initiated for the first time. The hypothesis was soon found to be wrong (see, for example, Donaldson [11]). For a while, "colonic quackery" seemed to be an issue of the past, but charlatanism, it seems, dies hard. THE PRESENT REVIVAL Today colon therapy is almost as popular as it was in its heyday. It forms an integral part of the therapeutic armamentarium of most (nonmedically qualified) alternative practitioners around the world who have, during the past three decades, experienced an unprecedented resurgence in this popularity (12). Based on a four-star rating system, colonic irrigation has received two stars in terms of popularity (13). Another source states that it "is once more gaining in popularity and is now commonly used by alternative health practitioners" (14). Accurate data on its prevalence are not available. Aggressive advertising and promotional texts abound (see, for example, Plant [15]). Many of the outlandish claims of yesterday are echoed today: "If bowel movements are not consistent, waste products and toxins are not eliminated in a regular manner, and health can be compromised" (14), to cite a book modestly entitled The Ultimate Guide. According to this text, the therapy cleans the colon in its full length, detoxifies it, reconstitutes intestinal flora, and even rids the body of parasites and prevents bacteria from entering the blood stream. Today's list of indications for colon therapy is impressive (14,16,17): alcoholism, allergies, arthritis, asthma, backache, bad breath, bloating, coated tongue, colitis, constipation, damage caused by nicotine or other environmental factors, fatigue, gas, headache, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, indigestion, insomnia, joint problems, liver insufficiency, loss of concentration, mental disorders, parasite infestation, proneness to infections, rheumatoid arthritis, sinus congestion, skin problems, and ulcerative colitis. False claims, a lack of evidence, big money, aggressive advertising, disregard of risk-little seems to have changed. In the hope of finding some type of evidence, I have written to the two professional organizations for colon therapy in the United States. The fact that no reply was received may speak for itself. Most proponents of colonic irrigation deny any adverse effects, but one text (17) states that possible side effects are nausea, diarrhea, and nervous disturbances. The risks of electrolyte imbalance (18) or water intoxication (19) are either omitted entirely or downplayed (17). COFFEE ENEMAS Coffee enemas are a hazardous derivative of colon therapy. As part of an unorthodox anticancer diet (20), a coffee enema is administered on a 4-hour basis "to help relieve pain, nausea and other symptoms accompanying detoxification" (21). Its proponents claim that caffeine is absorbed in the colon and leads to a vasodilatation in the liver, which in turn enhances the process of elimination of toxins. None of this is proved, nor is there any evidence of the clinical efficacy of coffee enemas. Coffee enemas are associated with severe adverse reactions (22), and even its proponents speak of a "healing crisis" that occurs in many patients (21). CONCLUSION The ancient theory of autointoxication has been the basis of today's colon therapy. There is no evidence that it conveys true benefit in any condition. Even a recent "review of the available literature" by a proponent of colonic irrigation provides only theories and anecdotes (17). Yet good proof exists that it is associated with considerable risks. Physicians should know about the dangers of colon therapy some of their patients might be using. In such cases empathy, reassurance, and factual information will be helpful. E. Ernst, M.D., Ph.d., F.R.C.P. (Edin) Department of Complementary Medicine; Postgraduate Medical School, University of Exeter; Exeter, England.

Multiple sources have warned about the lack of proven medical benefits and the potential health risks associated with coffee enemas. Actress Gwyneth Paltrow’s controversial lifestyle brand Goop is getting attention once again, thanks to the inclusion of a rather unusual product in its body detoxification package, a coffee enema kit sold for $135 on the company’s website. The new controversy started earlier this week when the Goop website published an article listing several products as part of the company’s “Beauty and Wellness Detox Guide.” While most of the products didn’t look like the type that would immediately stick out in such a guide, many readers were piqued by the inclusion of the Implant O-Rama System At-Home coffee enema kit, which is priced at $135 and listed as detoxification specialist Dr. Alejandro Junger’s “pick for those who know what they’re doing.” As explained by Forbes health news contributor Bruce Y. Lee, enemas are fluids that are injected into a person’s rectum up into the colon, thereby facilitating easier bowel movement by stimulating the intestines. They are typically recommended by doctors as a way to relieve constipation after dietary changes and other conventional remedies do not work, or to cleanse one’s colon ahead of a colonoscopy. While medical professionals have been known to prescribe a wide variety of enemas, including those that are made of sodium phosphate, mineral oil, and milk and molasses, Lee noted that coffee enemas have been gaining momentum as a popular colon cleansing method. Gwyneth Paltrow's Goop is recommending a $135 DIY coffee enema kit – and one OBGYN is NOT happy about it https://t.co/CTyCoSGFpD — Daily Mail Femail (@Femail) January 5, 2018 Citing the Implant O-Rama website, Bustle wrote that the company touted its product as being capable of relieving symptoms associated with depression and allergies, easing confusion and nervous tension, and relieving “severe” pain. But just like Forbes’ Lee did in his op-ed, Bustle also warned that there are many reasons to stay away from coffee enemas, even if Gwyneth Paltrow’s Goop website merely recommended the dispensing machine and not the actual enema, and also posted a disclaimer cautioning that the product is not a substitute for professional medical advice or treatment. Aside from stating that the human colon can normally clean itself without the aid of any medical devices, Bustle cited a paper posted in The American Journal of Gastroenterology, which stressed that coffee enemas do not have any proven medical benefits, and could result in proctocolitis, or inflammation of the rectum and colon. Similarly, Forbes contributor Lee warned that the use of any kind of enema could result in a number of side effects, such as dehydration and electrolyte imbalances that could sometimes be life-threatening. Infections may also occur if enema users accidentally end up injecting bacteria, he added. Coffee enemas come with several medical risks, including inflammation of the colon and rectum. Nobuhiro Asada / Shutterstock Further highlighting the potential danger of such detoxification methods, the Forbes op-ed also linked to several medical case studies involving the use of hot coffee in enemas, including multiple papers detailing cases of rectal burn, and one that focused on “deaths related to coffee enemas.” The ongoing coffee enema issue comes almost a year after Gwyneth Paltrow’s Goop business was in the headlines for advertising another supposedly risky product. In January 2017, Goop published an article that advised women to place golf ball-sized jade eggs in their vagina, due to the purported benefits of better sex and “feminine energy.” According to the Washington Post, San Francisco OB/GYN Dr. Jen Gunter referred to the ideas discussed in the article as the “biggest load of garbage” she’s read on the Goop website, adding that the porous qualities of jade could lead to bacterial vaginosis or, in severe cases, toxic shock syndrome. Likewise, Gunter also took to her blog earlier this week to criticize Gwyneth Paltrow and Goop, referencing last year’s jade egg issue and suggesting that January is Paltrow’s “go-to-month for promoting dangerous things.” She referred to the coffee enema kit as a “f**ked up way to make money” and an example of “fake medicine,” stressing that even the board-certified doctor Alejandro Junger’s advice contains information that isn’t supported either by medical literature or by “human anatomy and physiology.”

Gwyneth Paltrow's website, Goop, has recently garnered attention for promoting an "Implant O-Rama System At-Home Coffee Enema" as part of a $135 "full detox package." This offering suggests a novel, and for many, surprising, application for coffee.

Traditionally, coffee is consumed orally. However, the Goop article implies a different route for this beverage. For those unfamiliar, an enema involves introducing fluid into the rectum to stimulate bowel movements. Medical professionals typically prescribe enemas to alleviate constipation when other methods fail or to prepare a patient for procedures like colonoscopies. Common enema solutions include sodium phosphate (e.g., Fleet's Phosphosoda Enema), mineral oil, saline solution, and a mixture of milk and molasses.

While doctors generally advise enemas only for specific medical needs, some individuals are now using coffee enemas for purported colon "cleansing." The theory behind this practice is that toxins and undigested food accumulate in the colon, necessitating periodic flushing. This concept of "autointoxication," or slow poisoning from intestinal toxins, has historical roots.
