MUST WATCH Fox Business GOP debate in 90 seconds 01:30 "Back in September, my friend Donald said he had his lawyers look at this in every which way," Cruz said. "There was nothing to this birther issue." He added: "Since September, the Constitution hasn't changed. But the poll numbers have." While there has been plenty of animosity between Trump and most of his rivals, the billionaire businessman and Cruz have been on largely friendly territory for much of the campaign season. That's changed, however, as the polls in Iowa tighten ahead of the February 1 caucuses. Trump, hoping to eat into the senator's support in Iowa, has repeatedly questioned whether Cruz, whose mother was a U.S. citizen, is a natural born citizen. But in his attack against Trump on Thursday, Cruz noted that some of the more extreme theories on the topic would conclude that someone can only become president if both parents were born in the United States. Under that standard, Cruz noted, Trump himself would be ineligible for the presidency because his mother was born in Scotland. "On the issue of citizenship, Donald, I'm not going to use your mother's birth against you," Cruz said. Trump shot back: "Because it wouldn't work." It wasn't just Trump who put Cruz on the defensive. In the final moments of the debate, Rubio slammed his fellow senator for hiding behind the pretense of conservative values. JUST WATCHED Rubio to Cruz: We all saw you flip your vote Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Rubio to Cruz: We all saw you flip your vote 02:00 In one of his strongest moments of the evening, Rubio listed a string of issues on which he said Cruz had flip-flopped, including immigration and ethanol subsidies. "That is not consistent conservatism," he says. "That is political calculation." When Cruz shot back, "I appreciate you dumping your oppo research file on the debate stage," Rubio was ready with a sharp retort: "It's your record." 'New York values' Trump was more dominant Thursday than in previous debates. In one of the most memorable exchanges of the night, Trump responded to Cruz' recent accusation that the businessman "embodies New York values." JUST WATCHED Cruz, Trump debate 'New York values' Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Cruz, Trump debate 'New York values' 01:37 Asked to explain what that meant, Cruz said New Yorkers tend to hold "socially liberal" views, and its residents tend to revolve around "money and media." "Not a lot of conservatives come out of Manhattan," he said. Trump was ready with an emotional response. He recalled seeing the World Trade Center towers collapse on September 11, 2001. The resilience of New Yorkers in the aftermath, Trump said, revealed the true spirit of New York. "I saw something that no place on Earth could have handled more beautifully, more humanely than New York," Trump said. "The people in New York fought and fought and fought. We saw more death and even the smell of death and it was with us for months." Having clearly won the moment, Trump added: "We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made." Cruz also had sharp words for The New York Times for publishing a story this week that said Cruz failed to properly disclose large loans he received during his Senate campaign. JUST WATCHED Ted Cruz deflects NYT 'hit piece' Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Ted Cruz deflects NYT 'hit piece' 01:20 Asked about the controversy, Cruz quickly retorted: "Thank you for passing on that hit piece on the front page of The New York Times." "The entire New York Times attack is that I disclosed that loan on one filing with the United States Senate ... but it was not in the second filing with the FEC," Cruz said. "Yes, I made paperwork errors ... but if that's the best hit the New York Times has got, they better go back to the well." Alternatives to Trump and Cruz Christie and Rubio also tussled on stage, as the two men touted their conservative bona fides. Rubio accused Christie of being on the same page as President Barack Obama on issues such as gun control, Common Core and support for Planned Parenthood. JUST WATCHED Sen. Rubio and Gov. Christie's heated exchange Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Sen. Rubio and Gov. Christie's heated exchange 01:17 "Unfortunately, Gov. Christie has endorsed many of the ideas that Barack Obama supports," the Florida senator said. Christie deftly responded, pointing to an infamous moment in a past debate when Rubio accused Bush of attacking him only because someone had instructed the former Florida governor to do so. It appears that "same someone has been whispering in old Marco's ear, too," Christie said. He pushed back on Rubio's accusations as he touted his executive experience in the New Jersey governor's mansion. "This is the difference between being a governor and being a senator ... when you're a senator, what you get to do is just talk and talk and talk," Christie said. "Two years ago, he called me a conservative reformer that New Jersey needed. That was before he was running against me." Muslim ban, gun control The candidates also sparred over Trump's controversial proposal to temporarily ban Muslims from entering the U.S. The idea triggered waves of criticism but many of Trump's supporters say it holds merit in light of the growing threat of ISIS. "We have to stop with political correctness," Trump said, insisting he would not change his mind on the issue. Bush repeatedly called on Trump to "reconsider," warning that the idea makes it "impossible" to build a coalition with other Arab countries necessary to defeat ISIS. JUST WATCHED Bush: Trump's ban makes it impossible to take out ISIS Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Bush: Trump's ban makes it impossible to take out ISIS 01:23 "All Muslims? Seriously? What kind of signal does that send to the rest of the world?" Bush said. Others, including Kasich and Christie, said they were on board with at least temporarily not allowing Syrian refugees into the country. "I've said from the beginning, we should take no Syrian refugees of any kind," Christie said. And coming on the heels of Obama's new executive actions on gun control, several candidates offered impassioned defenses of Second Amendment rights. "I am convinced that if this president could confiscate every gun in America, he would," Rubio said. Christie, meanwhile, blasted the actions as inconsistent with democracy. "That's not a democracy, that's a dictatorship," he said. Undercard debate Earlier Thursday, Carly Fiorina dominated a smaller, less-glamorous debate stage when she joined two other low-polling candidates to discuss everything from national security to technology and Hillary Clinton. Fiorina's candidacy has largely been defined by memorable debate performances. And even though she was dropped from the prime-time stage at the debate, she still delivered. Right out of the gate, she dealt a sharp personal attack on Clinton, the Democratic front-runner. "I'm not a political insider. I haven't spent my lifetime running for office," the former Hewlett-Packard CEO said. "And unlike another woman in this race, I actually love spending time with my husband." Minutes later, Fiorina turned to Trump. "Despite Donald Trump's bromance with Vladimir Putin ... Russia is our adversary," she said.

Senator Ted Cruz's recent campaign rhetoric in Iowa, particularly his repeated invocation of "New York values," warrants a closer examination. As his campaign faces challenges in the state, Cruz appears to be leveraging this phrase to appeal to the religious right, suggesting that Iowans inherently understand its negative implications. However, given Cruz's history of employing vague and often unsubstantiated phrases, such as the "Washington cartel," he owes his audience a more direct explanation of what these "New York values" truly represent.

What specific characteristics does Cruz believe define these "New York values" that he so readily contrasts with those of Iowa? The term could encompass several interpretations. For instance, does it refer to an elite educational background, a trait Cruz himself possesses with degrees from Princeton and Harvard? Alternatively, could "New York values" signify an embrace of diversity and tolerance, qualities that have historically positioned New York as a welcoming hub for immigrants? This interpretation stands in stark contrast to Donald Trump's evolving stance on immigration, a shift from previously praising legal immigration to now opposing it, even for individuals with skills crucial to the economy, as noted by various immigration policy analysts.

Another possibility is that Cruz is alluding to crass materialism. However, this accusation seems inconsistent with Cruz's private interactions, where he has reportedly courted New York donors with a different tone, particularly on the sensitive issue of abortion, compared to his public pronouncements in Iowa. If Cruz intends to critique Trump's personal life, such as his multiple marriages, he should be explicit. While divorce is less stigmatized today, Cruz needs to clarify if he is implying something more serious regarding Trump's relationships with women, a point that has been debated by social commentators.

Cruz's strategy appears to be one of indirect criticism, employing vague accusations against Trump without offering specific evidence or engaging in direct confrontation. This "ring and run" tactic may not be perceived by many voters as a sign of strength.

Beyond these specific insinuations, Cruz's rhetoric aligns with a broader pattern within right-wing Republican politics: the tendency to label major urban centers and entire states as outside the realm of "real America." His assertion that New York values differ from Iowa values fits this mold. This perspective is problematic, revealing an inability among some politicians to engage with demographics beyond their established conservative base. As noted by political strategists, Cruz himself will likely need to campaign for votes in large cities like Cleveland and Philadelphia. Dismissing these urban centers as foreign or lacking in values alienates a significant portion of the electorate, both in primaries and general elections. Such rhetoric reinforces negative stereotypes of the GOP as provincial, bigoted, and exclusionary. For Cruz, it represents a lazy argument from someone struggling to connect authentically with a broader electorate. His difficulty in forging political alliances, partly due to a tendency to self-promote by disparaging others and questioning their motives, mirrors the condescending and divisive tone of the current presidency, a characteristic the nation does not require in another leader.

New York Gov. Andrew Cuomo (D) went on a media tour Friday morning to attack Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) for knocking his state the day before. Cruz, a 2016 presidential candidate, suggested during a debate that voters should think about the "values" of Republican front-runner Donald Trump's hometown. The Texas senator praised the "many wonderful" people of New York state, but he added that "everyone understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal, are pro-abortion, are pro-gay marriage, focused around money and the media." In at least four separate Friday-morning interviews, Cuomo went on the attack. "Not only is what he said anti-New York. It's anti-American," Cuomo said on CNN's "New Day." Cuomo continued: "I think it's sad. I think it's disturbing for all Americans, not just for New York. He is practicing the politics of division. He's trying to divide people. He's trying to divide this country. It doesn't work, and it's antithetical to who we are." Cruz raised the specter of Trump's "New York values" after the real-estate mogul brought up Cruz's birth in Canada almost daily for two weeks. Trump has suggested Cruz might not be eligible to become president, even though Cruz's mother was an American when he was born. Trump campaign rallies have even reportedly starting playing "Born in the U.S.A." The Queens-born Trump countered at the debate by saying "New York values" could be seen in how his city responded to the terrorist attacks on September 11, 2001. Cuomo backed Trump on that claim during another interview, on MSNBC's "Morning Joe." "It's a very good point," Cuomo told host Joe Scarborough. "And I think Trump raising it was politically expedient possibly, but also resonated with people. As Cruz is saying anti-New York [things] and trying to bring up a negative stereotype that is offensive ... And by the way, Joe, 9/11 is the symbol of this country's better angels." Cuomo also pointed out that Cruz raised campaign funds in Manhattan. "So it's very disturbing on many levels and hypocritical," he said on MSNBC. "Criticizing New York money — I'm sure now he won't take a donation from anyone in New York obviously. " In a third interview, on NY1, Cuomo further said that Cruz "offended gays." "He was offensive to women," Cuomo continued. "He offended 18 million New Yorkers. He offended one of the largest congressional delegations, by the way, in the country, and you can't govern that way. (Cuomo attacked Cruz during a radio interview as well.) Other New York entities also went after Cruz for the attack line. Rep. Pete King (R-New York), a frequent Cruz critic, told the senator to "go back under a rock." Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams, a Democrat, bashed Cruz on Twitter. And the front cover of Friday's New York Daily News depicted the Statue of Liberty giving the middle finger to Cruz.

The Empire State Building in the skyline midtown Manhattan, September 19, 2015. (Photo by Melina Mara/The Washington Post) I am a New Yorker in both of the senses of the descriptor. I was born in Rochester, on Lake Ontario in Western New York -- a Snow Belt city second only to Buffalo. (When I lived in California, I used to actually miss the stuff.) I now live in New York City as I have for nearly a decade -- the New York of New York, the town so nice they named it twice, all that. I've seen more minor league baseball games in cities like Utica, Auburn, and Watertown than I have Mets or Yankees games, but now I take the subway to Citi Field. I've lived or worked in Chinatown, Little Italy, the Garment District, the Upper East Side, Midtown, and the Upper West Side. Ted Cruz is not a New Yorker. He was born in Canada, as we have been reminded regularly for the past week or so. He lived there while a young child, moving to Texas before attending college at Princeton and Harvard. Then, he worked in Washington for a few years before moving back to Texas and beginning his political career. The closest Cruz got to living in New York was probably when he was attending Princeton, which is in New Jersey. Maybe he traveled to New York on occasion from Cambridge when he was at Harvard, but he never lived here. The difference between living in New York state or New York City and simply visiting the city is vast -- a chasm. Unless you are entertaining guests or running a particularly painful errand, no one that lives here spends much time in those places that are the only ones most tourists see. It's like that classic Onion headline: "Woman Who 'Loves Brazil' Has Only Seen Four Square Miles Of It." People who have detailed thoughts about life in New York have not seen New York. It's like writing a movie review off the trailer. Then Michael Bloomberg made everything worse. The former mayor, who's tied with Donald Trump for the number of political parties in which he's found a home over the years, pushed for a series of policies that tightly linked "New York City" with liberal nanny-statism. When I was in New Hampshire last weekend, I told someone I lived in Manhattan, and he made a joke about getting a big soda. People. That law didn't even pass. But now New York has joined Berkeley in the public imagination, despite being about as much like Berkeley as Houston is like Calgary. So Cruz, questioned by a radio host on Tuesday, dismissed Trump's recent affection for playing "Born in the USA" at his rallies -- a dig at Cruz -- by disparaging Trump's roots. "I think he may shift in his new rallies to play ‘New York, New York,’" Cruz said, "because Donald comes from New York and he embodies New York values." Later that evening, Fox News followed up. What does this mean? This is from Cruz's interview with Fox tonight. What values are different? pic.twitter.com/qZLsdoB0Dt — Sopan Deb (@SopanDeb) January 13, 2016 "They're not Iowa values," says Cruz, "and they're not New Hampshire values." First of all, there's an awful lot of New York state that is nearly identical to New Hampshire in topography, culture and values. If I drop you outside of Glens Falls or I drop you outside of Concord, about the only differentiator you'd see is Yankee hats versus Red Sox ones. Head north of New York City, and you'll find a lot of conservative Republicans mad at Gov. Andrew Cuomo's (D) restrictions on gun ownership, heading to church regularly, and apathetic about gay marriage. But Cruz isn't referring to New York state. He's referring to the New York City he saw on "Law and Order" or witnessed from the window of the cab he took down 42nd Street because the train was too complicated to figure out. He's referring to Democratic politics and political correctness and limits on sodas and Mayor Bill de Blasio (D) and all the things that the New York Post gets mad about all the time. He's referring to the cultural values that are mostly only dominant in the wealthier parts of Manhattan -- the parts where he goes to fundraise. That's not really Donald Trump's New York, either. Yeah, the guy used to be a Democrat and, yeah, the guy is rich and lives in a skyscraper (that happens to have his name on it), but Trump is a product of the outer boroughs made good. Trump grew up in Queens, for God's sake -- a wealthy part of Queens, but still. Queens. Trump was handed a business and a big bank account by his father, but the attitude that defines Trump is of the less-wealthy outsider forcing his way into the game. It's the guy who wanted to play across the East River against his father's wishes. The glitz and glam with which he surrounds himself is the sort of thing that old money Upper East Siders find gauche, which is part of the reason he loves it. Let's sweep all of that to the side, though. Let's talk about New Yorkers. Like most Iowans and most New Hampshirites, I assume, most New Yorkers -- as in people from the city -- don't care all that much about politics. Heck, they probably care less than people from Iowa or New Hampshire at this point. The basic mode of operation in the city is to mind your own business unless everyone is having fun talking about something crazy, in which case you join right in. A lot of people thought Bloomberg's sugary beverage limit was stupid, too -- and Bloomberg, who is very much a New Yorker, didn't care. Bloomberg didn't care about term limits. Did. Not. Care. In a way that was partly about being super-rich and partly -- maybe more -- about being a New Yorker. New Yorkers can't be the tough, gruff people that are rude to tourists and bad drivers and namby-pamby bleeding hearts who want to impose their values on the rest of the country. You can't have it both ways. I extend a sincere offer. Mr. Cruz, I invite you to come to New York and live in a small apartment in an unfancy neighborhood for a month. I invite you to come and figure out your favorite bodega and which train car to get in to optimize how quickly you can get back out of the station and to wait in line for a doctor at one of the city's many weird health-care facilities. I invite you to experience New York City and learn about New Yorkers beyond the people who pay $2,700 to shake your hand or who offer you water in green rooms. If, after that, you think that our values are abnormally leftie compared to those of Iowa City (which backed President Obama by 35 points in 2012), we'll accept your critique demurely. Ha ha, no. We won't care. Update: Pro-Cruz SuperPAC @KeepPromise1 says NY values = emphasis on $ over morality, self-aggrandizement over humility. pic.twitter.com/cNUJIoUjWM — Dan Gallo (@dangallo) January 13, 2016 Back when Abercrombie actually had big lines, the people waiting to get in were what we New Yorkers like to call "tourists." This "dis" is a bit like going to Times Square on New Year's Eve and saying, "And yet no one is volunteering at a homeless shelter!"

New York City is taking no guff from Sen. Ted Cruz. At least that is the case for the New York Daily News, which used the front page of its Friday issue to slam the conservative Texas senator, who in turn blasted wealthy businessman and Republican front-runner Donald Trump over his so-called New York values. The paper’s cover delivers a graphic of the Statue of Liberty making an obscene gesture with one finger and this headline: “Drop Dead Ted.” The cover, a riff on the paper’s famous “Ford to City: Drop Dead” headline from 1975, came after Republican contenders locked horns in a debate in South Carolina Thursday night—likely the last debate before the Iowa caucuses kick off the nominating process on Feb. 1. Epic @NYDailyNews cover "drop dead Ted. go back to Canada" after Cruz questions "NYC values" pic.twitter.com/JRY2Ojksor — Sara Eisen (@SaraEisen) January 15, 2016 During one heated clash, Cruz tried to raise doubts about Trump’s legitimacy as a Republican candidate. “I think most people know exactly what New York values are,” Cruz said. “Everyone understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal.” In response, Daily News columnist Mike Lupica pointed to a man who gave the shirt off his back to another man in need on a Manhattan subway. Here’s Lupica’s summation of his views: The best of this city is the best of this country, and always has been. When it was hit in a way that no American city had ever been hit, it came together and rose up together in a moment as thrilling as any America had ever seen. Trump offered his own sharp rebuttal during the debate: “We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made.” To be sure, the Daily News is no Trump booster. In a recent cover, the paper took Trump to task for suggesting a ban of all Muslims attempting to enter the U.S. According to the results from a new NBC News/Wall Street Journal poll, Trump continues to lead among Republican candidates with the real-estate mogul appearing as the first choice for 33% of would-be Republican primary voters compared with 20% for Cruz.

During an appearance on the Howie Carr Show, Senator Ted Cruz offered his perspective on Donald Trump's ongoing criticisms of his presidential campaign. Cruz suggested that Trump's persistent attacks are indicative of his concern over Cruz's increasing standing in the race. He even speculated that Trump, whom Cruz described as embodying "New York values," might begin playing Frank Sinatra's iconic song "New York, New York" at future rallies, a symbolic gesture to his New York roots.

Cruz characterized Trump as appearing "rattled" by Cruz's growing prominence. He pointed to Trump's "direct attacks" as evidence, noting that these criticisms were present at every campaign stop. While Cruz initially stated he would not engage in reciprocal attacks, he later subtly challenged Trump on two separate occasions.

On Tuesday, Cruz informed reporters that he observed supporters of Democratic candidate Hillary Clinton appearing "eager to support Donald Trump and the attacks that are being tossed my direction." He echoed this sentiment during the radio interview, proposing a potential coordination between Clinton's supporters and Trump's criticisms.

Cruz also addressed Clinton's assertion that Trump had previously been a Democrat. He remarked, "Hillary would know well how to identify Democrats. She has been a partisan Democrat herself obviously and, uh, she and Donald know each other well." Cruz elaborated on his belief that Clinton's key supporters were actively amplifying Trump's attacks against him. He theorized that these Democratic efforts might stem from polling data indicating Trump's significant losses to Clinton, while Cruz himself consistently defeats her. "I beat Hillary and I think that’s got the Hillary folks a little bit concerned and so they’re doing everything they can to amplify Donald’s attacks," Cruz explained.

Regarding Trump's comments about evangelicals and Cuba, Cruz extended an invitation to Trump to join him at church. Cruz stated, "Any time someone is attacking your faith that starts to suggest they’re getting really nervous about what’s happening in the race." He added, "I would be happy to invite Donald to come with me to church any time he would like. He would certainly be welcome there. And, uh, you know, at the end of the day, my focus is not to engage in the mudslinging."

query: What prompted the backlash against Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the owners of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre?

documents.text:
## Vegan Restaurateurs Face Backlash Over Farm-to-Table Meat Consumption

The founders of the popular, celebrity-endorsed vegan restaurant chain Cafe Gratitude and its sister establishment, Gracias Madre, are facing intense criticism and death threats after it was revealed they are raising, slaughtering, and consuming animals on their farm north of San Francisco. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, who launched the first Cafe Gratitude in 2004 and have attracted a following including Gwyneth Paltrow and Beyoncé, have defended their practices, citing "regenerative agriculture" methods employed at their Be Love Farm in Vacaville.

### A Shift in Philosophy

The controversy erupted when animal rights activists circulated blog entries and photos from the farm's website dating back to spring 2015. These materials depicted a freezer stocked with pastured beef, jars of gravy, and Matthew Engelhart enjoying a hamburger, alongside posts detailing their "transition" into consuming meat products after nearly four decades of vegetarianism.

"We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals," Matthew Engelhart explained to The Hollywood Reporter. He characterized the public reaction as a "mob mentality," noting protests outside their restaurants, a Facebook boycott page, and a surge of negative Yelp reviews. He further stated, "Cows make an extreme sacrifice for humanity but that is their position in God’s plan as food for the predators. We can be part of that sacrament."

### Purist Vegan Outrage

The Engelharts' pivot has deeply alienated some of their most dedicated patrons. Purist vegans, who frequented the southern California branches of Cafe Gratitude, known for its uniquely named "affirmation" dishes like the "accepting" sushi bowl and "grateful" kale salad, have expressed strong disapproval. Online comments range from accusations of hypocrisy to fervent wishes for retribution. Critics argue that the Engelharts are "duping vegans to support their animal killings."

### Beyond the Restaurants: A Broader Empire

The Engelhart empire extends beyond their restaurants. They have authored several books, including "Sacred Commerce: Business as a Path of Awakening," and their personal website, Eternal Presence, references a board game they created called "The Abounding River Board Game." This game, reportedly featured on every table at their San Francisco flagship, was designed to encourage players to embrace an "unfamiliar view of Being Abundant" and cultivate a "spiritual foundation" for financial perspectives.

A February blog post from Be Love Farm highlighted their transition into meat products as a "big week." The post described the transition as "happening deep within our beings, we know it is a necessary and important part of our growth as well as the sustainability of our farm... as we open our hearts further to the presence of love..." This announcement was accompanied by images of their new beef products alongside pastoral scenes of their cows.

### A History of Controversy

This is not the first time the Engelharts have faced public scrutiny. In 2011, they cited "aggressive lawsuits" from disgruntled employees as the reason for closing or selling eight Cafe Gratitude locations in the Bay Area. These lawsuits pertained to tip-pooling practices and allegations of unpaid overtime. At the time, the Engelharts stated on Facebook that defending themselves in court would be too costly. Another lawsuit involved an employee who claimed he was fired for refusing to attend the controversial Landmark Forum, a self-improvement course linked to Werner Erhard's self-realization programs. The Engelharts eventually settled these disputes, with some restaurants being sold or transferred to family members.

The couple reportedly met at a Landmark Forum seminar. Terces Engelhart, whose name is "secret" spelled backward, had previously left a conventional life with her husband and children in Santa Cruz, selling their possessions and embarking on a road trip. Her daughter, Mollie, now co-owns a vegan beer garden and a vegan ice cream parlor in Los Angeles with actor Woody Harrelson.

### Influences and Future Uncertainty

Los Angeles-based activist Mary Cummins, founder of Animal Advocates, suggests that the Engelharts may have been influenced by the controversial views of Zimbabwean ecologist Allan Savory. Savory's work, particularly his book "Holistic Management," advocates for the use of livestock on grasslands as a means to reverse climate change and prevent land degradation. While Savory's ideas have both proponents, such as Hunter Lovins, and detractors, like George Monbiot, it remains to be seen how this controversy will impact the Engelhart empire, which serves approximately 28,000 meals daily and offers various events and packages at Be Love Farm. The question lingers whether patrons will continue to engage with the farm, particularly after learning about the Engelharts' personal meat consumption, even after interacting with their farm animals like cows Carmella and Cinnamon.

What a wonderful week it was on the farm. Our great friends who own Ca Momi in Napa gifted us with some of their truly delicious pizzas and profiteroles to make my recovery from a surgical procedure easier on me. I was so grateful for the added rest and kindness. I then got inspired to make my own profiteroles since we have this amazing farm cream and voila, see them in the picture above. Here I am piping them onto the parchment paper getting ready to bake them. Matthew continued his pruning of all the trees while the boys, Jaxon and Jack put mulch around the base of the trees. You can see that the almonds are in full bloom and the nectarine blossoms are coming on in their beautiful pinks! It was a big week as we begin to make the transition into our own meat products after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism. While I would clearly say we are in transition and that transition is happening deep within our beings, we know it is a necessary and important part of our own growth as well as the sustainability of our farm. Certainly a part of us wants to either deny the inevitability of death or simply not let ourselves get present to the reality of it, for our animals as well as ourselves. But we know that while we die a little bit each day as we open our hearts further to the presence of love, and as we are the caretakers of our farm animals the responsibility for their health and well being lies with us. This is clearly a longer discussion and we invite you to one of our upcoming farm dinners over the next 9 months, where we share more about this process. Here Matthew is straining off beef broth and our freezer full of pastured beef, our pastured beef. Here are the girls full of Spring clover sitting down chewing their cud in the warm Spring breeze of the midday. and introducing Jack (blonde) and Jaxon, our apprentices on the farm and a welcome youthful pair of energetic young men. With no rain in sight we have started to irrigate already, which brings me to my question for the week. How do you nourish your soul? Think about it. Have a great week, Love, Terces

The owners of a popular California vegan restaurant chain, Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre, are facing intense backlash from animal rights activists. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, a husband-and-wife team, have reportedly been receiving death threats after it was revealed they have returned to eating meat and are raising and slaughtering animals on their Be Love Farm in northern California.

This controversy erupted when blog posts from the couple's farm surfaced, detailing their decision to end 40 years of vegetarianism. According to reports from The Hollywood Reporter, these posts included images of cattle being raised on their farm, freezers stocked with beef, and Matthew Engelhart enjoying a hamburger.

"They are duping vegans to support their animal killings," stated activist Chase Avior, who has been organizing protests at the restaurants, to The Hollywood Reporter. Avior described the couple's new approach as "regenerative bulls--t!" on a Facebook page dedicated to boycotting the establishments.

The Engelharts, who launched the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004 and expanded to Southern California around 2011, capitalized on the growing demand for healthy, sustainable food. Their restaurants have become renowned celebrity hotspots, attracting patrons like Emma Stone, Andrew Garfield, Kerry Washington, Jay Z, and Beyonce.

Matthew Engelhart explained their dietary shift to The Hollywood Reporter, stating, "We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals." This explanation, however, has not appeased outraged activists.

The backlash has manifested in various forms, including one-star Yelp reviews and protests outside the restaurants. Demonstrators have held signs labeling the Engelharts "hypocrites" and proclaiming "no animal is grateful to die," a pointed reference to the restaurant's name. "People have taken up the mob mentality," Matthew Engelhart commented on the situation.

## Vegan Restaurant Owners Face Backlash Over Farm Practices

Hollywood's vegan community is in an uproar following revelations that the owners of the prominent vegan restaurant chain Cafe Gratitude have been raising and slaughtering animals at their Northern California farm. This has led to a wave of anger, online criticism, and even death threats directed at proprietors Matthew and Terces Engelhart.

### The Be Love Farm Revelation

The controversy ignited when animal rights activists uncovered a 14-month-old blog post on the Engelharts' Be Love Farm website. The post detailed their transition back to a meat-inclusive diet after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism, accompanied by images of beef broth and a freezer stocked with beef from their own dairy cows. Matthew Engelhart confirmed to The Hollywood Reporter that they have raised chickens for eggs and cows for milk, cheese, and butter for sale for several years. He stated that they have "harvested," or slaughtered, a few cows, with the meat being shared among friends, neighbors, and their community, rather than sold.

### Betrayal and Boycotts

This news has deeply unsettled many vegans who have patronized Cafe Gratitude, believing the brand was built on a commitment to veganism and animal rights. Carrie Christianson, who initiated a Facebook boycott group with over 570 members, expressed the sentiment of betrayal: "You are patronizing a restaurant that you think has that philosophy, and it turns out it doesn't." Plans for a protest at the Larchmont Village location have also been announced.

Matthew Engelhart described the situation as "mob mentality" and expressed his sadness and discouragement that personal choices made in private have led to such extreme reactions. "People have taken up the mob mentality," he stated. "It saddens me that the choices we made in the privacy of our home would lead people to feel so betrayed that it's elevated to threats on our lives. I'm very discouraged."

### Cafe Gratitude's Rise and Brand Identity

The Engelharts launched the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004, expanding to Southern California in 2011 with locations in Larchmont Village, Venice, and the Arts District in Los Angeles, as well as in San Diego and Newport Beach. The chain, which also includes the sister concept Gracias Madre, is known for its "I Am" named dishes, such as "I Am Whole" and "I Am Humble." The restaurants have become popular with the entertainment industry, attracting celebrities like Beyonce Knowles and Ariana Grande.

### The Engelharts' Perspective: Regenerative Farming

The Engelharts frame their return to consuming animal products as a natural evolution stemming from their work at Be Love Farm. Matthew Engelhart explained their reasoning: "We started to observe nature, and what we saw is that nature doesn't exist without animals. Neither does natural farming. You know, you can't buy organic vegetables that aren't fertilized with animal residue. So that was our discovery. We aren't on a soapbox." They view their practices as part of a "sustainable, regenerative" approach to food production.

### Critics' Disagreement and Calls for Transparency

However, critics argue that the Engelharts' personal choices are inseparable from their public vegan brand. Chase Avior, a screenwriter and director organizing the protest, stated, "This is a vegan restaurant company that's been a leader in the vegan community. If they're going to go around and say it's not about animal rights, it's not about speaking about the animals horrifically abused, it's just about better nurturing a plant-based lifestyle, that's something different. A lot of vegans have a right to feel betrayed by this."

Musician Moby, who opened his own vegan restaurant, Little Pine, expressed hope that the Engelharts would discontinue their animal farming practices. Cooking Channel personality Jason Wrobel penned an open letter on his website, echoing sentiments of feeling misled and that their financial support was used for unethical purposes. He emphasized the need for greater transparency, stating, "I feel that my hard-earned money has been used for purposes that are unethical, cruel and out-of-alignment with my values. And I strongly feel that a more public announcement from the company should have been made about the practices at the farm, not tucked away in some obscure blog post."

### Past Controversies and "Sacred Commerce"

The Engelharts have faced scrutiny in the past regarding their business practices, including lawsuits related to tip-pooling and a mandate for employees to participate in the Landmark Forum, a personal development organization. The couple, who met at Landmark Forum, believe they are being held to an unfair standard of purity. "Somehow we got made mom and dad of some vegan movement," Matthew Engelhart remarked. "We never signed up for that. It's crazy. Do [the protestors] check in on the diets of every other vegan restaurant owner? Do they check in on the diets of the owners of Whole Foods? We are baffled." He maintained that their restaurants remain committed to a plant-based diet and that he is entitled to change his personal dietary choices.

Chandra Gilbert EXECUTIVE CHEF With the arrival of Gracias Madre in Los Angeles, Chef Chandra Gilbert finds themself once again featuring bold flavors and seasonally-conscious ingredients. The veteran chef, who has 33 years of experience working in the restaurant industry; including stints at Alice Waters' Café Fanny in Berkeley, Cowgirl Creamery and Greens Restaurant in San Francisco; has been with the Café Gratitude family since 2004 when they began working as Director of Operations for the esteemed family of vegan restaurants in the Bay Area. It was during that time that the idea for Gracias Madre began entering into the conversation with Café Gratitude Founders Matthew and Terces Engelhart. Together, they co-designed the restaurant's aesthetic and co-created the menu featuring produce from the Engelhart's Be Love Farm in Vacaville, bringing Gilbert's dream to fruition with Gracias Madre's San Francisco opening in 2009. The restaurant, located in San Francisco's Mission District, quickly garnered critical acclaim for its vibrant interpretation of Mexican cuisine. With the opening of Gracias Madre in West Hollywood in January 2014, Gilbert brings that passion to Los Angeles with a menu that blends produce from Be Love Farm with ingredients sourced from local farmers markets.

Café Gratitude: A Culinary Journey Rooted in Sacred Commerce and Plant-Based Excellence

Café Gratitude stands as a distinguished collection of plant-based restaurants, widely recognized for its exquisite gourmet cuisine and unwavering dedication to health and environmental sustainability. At the core of its operations lies a unique philosophy termed "Sacred Commerce." This guiding principle is built upon pillars of inspired service, fostering transparent communication, and cultivating a profound sense of gratitude. The overarching aim of this approach is to cultivate a world brimming with abundance, where the act of sharing food and connecting with community becomes a vibrant and life-affirming celebration.

**The Culinary Visionary: Executive Chef Seizan Dreux Ellis**

Spearheading Café Gratitude's innovative culinary direction is Executive Chef Seizan Dreux Ellis. Chef Ellis, who became an integral part of the team in Venice, California, in April 2012, plays a pivotal role in the development and execution of the meticulously crafted organic, plant-based menus that grace all Southern California locations. His culinary philosophy is deeply rooted in creating health-conscious yet intensely flavor-driven dishes, making sophisticated plant-based dining accessible to a broad spectrum of patrons. Chef Ellis currently oversees the culinary strategy for the following esteemed establishments:

*   **Arts District, Los Angeles:** Located at 300 S Santa Fe Ave., Los Angeles, CA 90013, this vibrant location operates daily from 8 AM to 10 PM.
*   **Larchmont Village, Los Angeles:** Situated at 639 N Larchmont Blvd., Los Angeles, CA 90004, this popular spot is open daily from 8 AM to 10 PM.
*   **Venice, California:** Found at 512 Rose Ave., Venice, CA 90291, this location offers dining from 8 AM to 10 PM daily, with 90-minute parking available in the downstairs garage with validation.
*   **San Diego, California:** At 1980 Kettner Blvd., San Diego, CA 92101, guests can enjoy meals from 8 AM to 10 PM daily. Valet parking is available Thursday through Saturday from 5:30 PM to 11:30 PM.
*   **Gratitude, Newport Beach:** This upscale iteration, located at 1617 Westcliff Drive, Newport Beach, CA 92660, serves guests from 8 AM to 10 PM on weekdays and weekends. The to-go and cafe hours extend from 7 AM to 10 PM daily.
*   **Gratitude, Beverly Hills:** The sophisticated Beverly Hills outpost, at 419 N Canon Drive, Beverly Hills, CA 90210, is open daily from 8 AM to 10 PM.

**A Commitment to Organic and Sustainable Sourcing**

Café Gratitude places paramount importance on the use of the finest organic ingredients. This commitment is a testament to their deep respect for both the earth and the individuals who partake in their food, recognizing the intrinsic interconnectedness of all. This dedication extends beyond the kitchen, actively supporting local farmers, championing sustainable agricultural practices, and prioritizing environmentally friendly products. Their alignment with initiatives such as "Kiss the Ground," an organization dedicated to promoting regenerative agriculture, further underscores their commitment to a healthier planet. Each dish is prepared with an evident dedication to care and a palpable sense of love.

**Embracing a Mindset of Gratitude and Abundance**

Beyond the exceptional cuisine, Café Gratitude invites its patrons to embrace a transformative mindset. The restaurant encourages guests to actively choose:

*   **Loving their life:** Cultivating a deep appreciation for their own existence.
*   **Adoring themselves:** Fostering self-compassion and self-worth.
*   **Accepting the world:** Embracing the present reality with an open heart.
*   **Being generous and grateful daily:** Integrating acts of kindness and thankfulness into everyday life.
*   **Experiencing abundance:** Recognizing and appreciating the plentiful aspects of life.

This holistic philosophy aims to enrich the dining experience, encouraging guests not only to savor nourishing food but also to cultivate a positive and abundant outlook.

**Further Exploration and Community Engagement**

For those interested in learning more about the vital principles of regenerative agriculture, the "Kiss the Ground" organization offers extensive resources. Individuals seeking to join the vibrant Café Gratitude community or explore potential employment opportunities are encouraged to visit their dedicated employment section. For any inquiries or media-related requests, direct contact with the restaurant is welcomed.

This week proved to be filled with excitement, expansion and LOVE. It started off with a trip to Los Angeles for the training of 112 new employees for the upcoming opening of the new Cafe Gratitude in the Arts District of downtown LA. Whenever we think, “really more employees, more food, more….. we stop and put our attention on the employees of the past who have become such amazing, inspiring, leaders in the world, and that always has us sign up for the next Cafe. Thank you to all those who keep our attention on what matters most, the opening of hearts, the expansion of love, the right livelihood of our work place… you make all the difference. On our lunch break at the new employee training, look what showed up to entertain us all! Fortunately my trips to LA include a snuggle with my two newest grandsons, Valentine (6 months) and Rio (2 months). pretty precious. Being a grandmother is the BEST EVER! This week on the farm we harvested some GIANT lettuce… see the first picture of my sweet husband holding one of the giant heads! We also cooked our first hamburgers, see Matthew eating his first in over 40 years! I promise this transition has been challenging and insightful and we are ever so thankful for the sacrifice that our cows made. Here is Matthew’s response to someone’s questioning our adding meat to our diet, a great question. Perhaps I’ve e failed to explain adequately why I would pursue the path of eating flesh after 40 years of vegetarianism. The answer is non-violence but non-violence to the whole system, all species. Agriculture is most violent destructive force on the planet. It has caused the extinction of more species then any other activity. Earth balance margarine is made from Canadian canola. Organic or not this product required a plow to destroy Canadian prairie an act of violence against burrowing owls, ferrets, prairie dogs, dozens of insect and bird species. If that prairie had been maintained in grassland with well managed cows or buffalo those species would not be killed or displaced. 30% of the world’s surface is grasslands, Grasslands need herds of ruminants to thrive, when they are removed, the grasslands degenerate into desert. Desertification is violence against all species including humans. The conflict in Syria and Iraq can be easily tied to desertification, mismanaged soil. Herding ruminants are our best tool to restore fertility to the earth, keep the earth covered, and reverse desertification and climate change. If I knew of a better or even another solution, I would choose it. We need cows to keep the earth alive, cows make an extreme sacrifice for humanity but that is their position in God’s plan as food for the predators. Cows maintain the grass, predators maintain the herd by culling the weak and sick. We can be part of that sacrament. Sacrifice is part of life. As in the passion of Christ, we all have to spill our blood for humanity to know the Father. The cow’s sacrifice was been ordained, ours we must choose. Wendell Berry quote: “To live we must daily break the body and shed the blood of Creation. When we do it knowingly, lovingly, skillfully, reverently, it is a sacrament. When we do it ignorantly, greedily, clumsily, destructively it is a desecration.” We attended a Allan Savory workshop on a neighboring farm this week and I had the privileged of sitting next to him at dinner. Legend has it that Biltong is the food that gets credit for the strength of the men in South Africa, it is a peppery salted cured meat, made from the muscle cuts. Again I remind you all that we feel like vegan ism is a great response the the abuse of animals in the industrial food system, AND that animals raised in a healthy and humane way are a great source of nutrition for humans. It is so important that we bridge the gap of “knowing our food” and don’t sell out for distancing ourselves from the full cycle of life and death. This week I am reminded of the power of God’s grace and the importance of letting it in. Breathe. Love. Terces P.S. Flashback… look at these photos of cold brewed coffee and almond milk, now sold in grocery stores… 11 years ago this month when we opened our first Cafe Gratitude in the Mission District of San Francisco, people would ask us daily, “How do you get milk out of an almond?” and “What is cold brewed coffee?” We’ve come a long way baby!

documents.text: Mr. Perez’s wife and father-in-law had urged him not to go to work that day, Chief Acevedo said. “His response was, ‘I’ve got work to do.’” Mr. Perez, who worked in the department’s division of traffic enforcement, was a “sweet, gentle public servant,” the chief added. Outside Houston’s main shelter, lines formed. As rain fell in the early afternoon, scores of people waited outside the George R. Brown Convention Center, where more than 9,000 people had already taken shelter. A pile of wet American Red Cross blankets sat near the end of one line. On Sunday and Monday, evacuees were able to enter the sprawling complex without delay. Buy by Tuesday, while the convention center appeared to be organized, there were some signs of strain. Some people set up bedding in the main corridor, an area that had been mostly empty a day earlier, to get away from the increasingly crowded main dormitory. Nathan Malbrue, who was sitting on the edge of an inflatable mattress, said he was not bothered by the growing crowd. He said he was in the hallway, near a medical station, because of a heart condition. “Just bring everybody in,” he said. “This is a big building.” But Cora Watson, 58, feared that the convention center would be overwhelmed. “Move them to hotels or something,” she said, her voice barely audible. Reservoirs are above capacity in Houston. Water began flowing over an emergency spillway at a major Houston flood-control reservoir on Tuesday, the Army Corps of Engineers said, adding to flooding in the area about 15 miles west of downtown. So far the overflow at the Addicks reservoir dam is minor, about 100 cubic feet per second, Edmond Russo Jr., deputy district engineer for the Army Corps’ Galveston district, said at a news conference. But it should rise to about 4,500 cubic feet per second — about 120 million gallons per hour — by Thursday, he said, as the reservoir levels peak. The rate should decline to current levels two days after that, he said. But the large flow could bring up to 2 feet of water to the streets east of the reservoir. The water will eventually flow into Buffalo Bayou, which passes through downtown. Dr. Russo said that dam safety operators were monitoring the structure and that it was in no danger. Levels at a second reservoir nearby, Barker, are increasing as well and its two spillways are expected to overflow starting Saturday, he said. Water in the two reservoirs, which are normally dry, has risen quickly since Harvey first began inundating the area. Efforts to reduce the levels by controlled releases through gates in both dams have not been sufficient. In addition to the flooding over the spillway, both reservoirs have been spreading into nearby subdivisions as the water has risen. A spokesman for the Harris County Flood Control District said that about 2,500 homes near Addicks reservoir, and 670 near Barker, have flooded already. The city also has to worry about having enough potable water. Houston’s Northeast Water Purification Plant, one of three plants that supply water to the city, is flooded. While the system is still working, even with much of its equipment underwater, city officials are worried about their ability to keep it running. A levee breach threatened a village near Houston. A levee designed to protect the community of Columbia Lakes, 40 miles southwest of Houston, from the Brazos River was breached, Brazoria County officials said. Columbia Lakes is a small resort village with a country club and golf course, and is surrounded by levees. Residents were ordered to “GET OUT NOW!!” according to a Twitter message, although many had already left after a mandatory evacuation order was issued Sunday.

**Houston's Dam Crisis: Unprecedented Flooding and Overspills from Tropical Storm Harvey**

Houston is currently confronting a catastrophic flood emergency, a direct consequence of Tropical Storm Harvey's relentless assault. Two vital flood control structures, the Addicks and Barker dams, have been pushed beyond their operational capacity, leading to unprecedented overspills. This dire situation, amplified by record-breaking rainfall, significantly escalates the threat of severe inundation for communities situated downstream. Jeff Lindner, a senior flood control engineer with the Harris County Flood Control District, has characterized the event as "something we've never faced before," a stark acknowledgment of the unpredictable and overwhelming nature of the water's impact. The persistent deluge, a direct result of Harvey's slow and potent trajectory, has already inflicted widespread devastation across Texas and is now extending its reach into Louisiana, as reported by the National Weather Service.

### Dam Operations and Engineering Challenges

Engineers are engaged in a critical, ongoing effort to release water from the Addicks and Barker dams. This controlled release is a necessary measure to alleviate the immense pressure on these vital flood control structures. These dams play a crucial role in managing water levels within Buffalo Bayou, Houston's primary river system. However, the Addicks reservoir, located to the west of the city, witnessed its water level surpass an alarming 108 feet (32.9 meters) on Tuesday. This critical threshold resulted in water spilling directly over the dam's crest. Compounding the immense challenge, flood gauges at the Barker dam are currently non-operational. This failure is attributed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to the overwhelming floodwaters that have rendered the monitoring equipment useless. The anticipated overspill from these dams is projected to cause further inundation along Buffalo Bayou, though the exact magnitude and extent of this impact remain uncertain. Further south, in Brazoria County, officials confirmed a significant levee breach at Columbia Lakes. This breach necessitated an immediate and urgent "Get out now!" evacuation order for residents in the affected areas, as declared by county authorities.

### Harvey's Impact: A Storm of Historic Proportions

Tropical Storm Harvey made landfall near Corpus Christi on Friday, marking its arrival as the most powerful hurricane to strike Texas in over five decades, according to data from the National Hurricane Center. The storm's immense rainfall totals have necessitated the evacuation of thousands of Houston-area residents. Rescue operations are ongoing for countless individuals still stranded by the rising waters. Tragically, at least nine fatalities have been confirmed in the wake of the storm. Among the confirmed victims is a family of six whose van was swept away by floodwaters, and an elderly man who is believed to have drowned. In response to the widespread devastation, President Donald Trump and First Lady Melania Trump have visited Texas to personally assess the extensive damage and offer support.

### Record-Breaking Rainfall and Future Projections

**Record Rainfall Totals:** A neighborhood in south Houston has recorded the highest rainfall total from a tropical storm in U.S. history since meteorological records began in the 1950s. The site at Mary's Creek at Winding Road has received an astonishing 49.2 inches of rain. This figure surpasses the previous record of 48 inches, as meticulously documented by the National Weather Service. Forecasts issued by the National Weather Service indicated that some areas in and around Houston could experience an additional 12 inches (30 cm) of rain on Tuesday alone, further exacerbating the already dire flood conditions.

**Future Landfall and Preparedness Efforts:** As of Tuesday, Harvey was expected to make landfall again on Wednesday morning, with its trajectory pointing towards southwestern Louisiana. Residents of New Orleans, who were somberly marking the 12th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, were actively preparing for heavy rain and flash floods over the subsequent two days. This preparedness was guided by advisories issued by the city's emergency management agency, highlighting the ongoing threat posed by the storm's remnants.

The BBC is actively collecting personal accounts from individuals and communities affected by the devastating impacts of Tropical Storm Harvey. If you wish to share your experiences, please email haveyoursay@bbc.co.uk. Kindly include a contact number if you are willing to speak with a BBC journalist about your situation.

Hurricane Harvey's catastrophic rainfall has pushed Houston's Addicks Reservoir beyond its limits, leading to an unprecedented overtopping of its spillway. This critical event has initiated an "uncontrolled release" of floodwaters, posing a significant and prolonged inundation threat to surrounding neighborhoods. Compounding the crisis, a separate levee failure south of the city has necessitated an urgent evacuation order, highlighting the widespread impact of the storm.

### Addicks Reservoir Breach: A Historic First

The Addicks Reservoir, strategically located west of downtown Houston, has experienced its spillway breach for the first time in recorded history. This development presents an immediate and severe danger to the numerous subdivisions situated in its vicinity. Meteorologist Jeff Lindner, affiliated with the Harris County Flood Control District, characterized the situation as entirely unprecedented. He elaborated that the water levels would continue their ascent, thereby intensifying the volume of water flowing into residential areas.

### Columbia Lakes Levee Failure and Evacuation

In the wake of the Addicks Reservoir's overflow, officials in Brazoria County issued a critical warning regarding a levee breach at Columbia Lakes. Residents in the affected area were strongly urged to evacuate immediately, with the stark and urgent message disseminated: "GET OUT NOW!!" This directive underscores the immediate peril faced by those in the path of the rising floodwaters.

### Record-Breaking Rainfall and Infrastructure Strain

These critical infrastructure challenges are occurring concurrently with the establishment of a new record for tropical system rainfall in Texas. A specific location southeast of Houston has recorded an astonishing 49.32 inches of rain, a testament to the storm's intensity. Lindner further observed that bridges, roadways, and other vital structures in various parts of Houston were beginning to succumb to the immense hydrostatic pressure exerted by the floodwaters.

### Malfunctioning Flood Gauges and Structural Integrity

The extreme water levels observed in both the Addicks and the neighboring Barker reservoirs have led to the malfunction of their respective flood gauges. The Flood Control District is actively engaged in efforts to repair these instruments, with law enforcement personnel providing crucial manual readings in the interim. Edmond Russo, representing the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, confirmed that the spillways on both reservoirs are engineered and meticulously maintained to accommodate such overflow events and are anticipated to maintain their structural integrity. The Barker Reservoir spillway is projected to begin overflowing by Friday.

### Extended Flooding Projections and Water Flow Dynamics

Residents residing in subdivisions surrounding the Addicks and Barker reservoirs must prepare for flooding that is expected to persist for an extended duration. Lindner projected that this inundation could last "four to five weeks," potentially extending "until October." He emphasized that the primary challenge lies in accurately predicting how the released water will interact with the existing drainage systems and its ultimate flow path. Current assessments indicate that the excess water is likely to move towards the Sam Houston Tollway, subsequently heading south toward Interstate 10, and ultimately discharging into Buffalo Bayou, which leads directly to downtown Houston.

### Proactive Evacuation Advisories and Uncharted Territory

County officials are maintaining close surveillance over six neighborhoods situated near the reservoirs and are strongly advising residents to undertake proactive evacuations. Lindner cautioned, "Once the water comes into the street you aren't going to be able to leave." He had previously informed Fox News that the spillover would result in "serious flooding in immediate areas," commencing as a gradual trickle and escalating into an uncontrolled release. The risk extends even to second-story homes. While downtown Houston may be spared direct inundation, officials acknowledge the inherent uncertainty given the unprecedented nature of this situation, which Lindner described to Fox News as "uncharted territory" for the city.

### Controlled Releases and Their Implications

The Army Corps of Engineers commenced releasing water from the Addicks and Barker reservoirs on Monday in response to water levels rising at a rate exceeding 6 inches per hour. However, Lindner indicated that these controlled releases were insufficient to keep pace with the relentless inflow of water. This measure, implemented with the objective of safeguarding the business district, simultaneously carried the risk of flooding thousands of additional homes in nearby subdivisions. The two reservoirs, originally constructed following severe flood events in 1929 and 1935, were designed with the capacity for controlled downstream water release.

### Brazos River Threat and Storm's Devastating Toll

In Houston's southwestern suburbs, Fort Bend County officials issued a warning on Tuesday, projecting that the Brazos River was anticipated to crest at an alarming 59 feet. The Fort Bend County Office of Emergency Management disseminated an advisory urging residents in affected subdivisions to prepare for significant inundation. With an additional two feet of rain forecast on top of the more than 30 inches already recorded in some areas, authorities expressed profound concern that the most devastating impacts of Hurricane Harvey might still be unfolding. As of Tuesday, at least 14 fatalities had been directly attributed to the storm, including a family of six who perished while attempting to escape the rising waters. Houston Police Chief Art Acevedo conveyed his deep apprehension to the Associated Press, stating, "I'm really worried about how many bodies we're going to find."

### Statistical Rarity of Rainfall

The rainfall amounts being produced by the storm are statistically expected to occur only once in over 1,000 years, according to Edmond Russo, a deputy district engineer for the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. This extraordinary precipitation underscores the unparalleled nature of Hurricane Harvey's impact.

The situation in Houston remains dire, as dams begin overflowing with rains that have already wrought catastrophic damage and swept away thousands of homes, leaving the city’s residents trapped with limited recourse for shelter and no hope for evacuation. Fortunately, a brave journalist like ABC World News Tonight anchor Tom Llamas is entrenched right there on the scene, bearing witness to the many desperate people who are reduced to stealing food from supermarkets, and ensuring their stories get told directly to the cops. Llamas, who has previously won awards for his coverage of Hurricanes Sandy and Irene, is clearly no stranger to the devastation they can cause or the frenzy they can set off in those who are just struggling to survive within them. But then, he’s also not about to let some people just make off with bread or whatever before it can become waterlogged garbage—not without making a purchase. So when Llamas tweeted he was “witnessing looting right now at a large supermarket in the NE part of Houston,” adding that police were on the scene after having “just discovered a body nearby,” he did his civic duty and immediately informed the police of another dying thing that needed their attention: the public’s respect for corporate property. “We informed police of the looting and Coast Guard is flying overhead,” Llamas brayed proudly. “Multiple officers now on the scene.” Advertisement Still, not everyone appreciated Llamas moving so swiftly to rescue a corporation from not having an accurate inventory for future insurance claims. In fact, some people have reminded Llamas that the Society of Professional Journalists calls for reporters to “show compassion for those who may be affected adversely by news coverage”—or just regular human compassion. Others have pointed out that Llamas was openly admitting to diverting first responder resources, which might otherwise be used for rescuing people stranded in their homes, to making sure no one takes soon-to-be-rotting food. Some have suggested that, in the wake of local authorities declaring that anyone caught looting will face mandatory jail time, disaster victims who are forced to undertake extreme measures to secure survival supplies were essentially being sentenced thanks to nothing more than his own moral grandstanding. Advertisement After the backlash, Llamas attempted to “clear this up”—deleting his original tweet, and clarifying that some of the people who had entered the supermarket to steal food amid the worst natural disaster to ever hit Texas had their “faces covered,” presumably to avoid identification by the media and police. In light of Llamas’ clarification, some have also recommended Tom Llamas wade right into that supermarket, find the largest extant pineapple, and go fuck himself with it—but only after paying for it. Advertisement Anyway, be sure to follow Tom Llamas for more hard-hitting investigative stories about people fighting for their lives and where the police can arrest them.

Following Hurricane Harvey, Texas residents have reported hundreds of instances of price gouging on essential items, particularly bottled water. Attorney General Ken Paxton's office has received at least 600 complaints related to the storm, encompassing scams, fraud, price gouging, and issues with charities.

The majority of these complaints specifically involve inflated prices for:

*   Bottled water
*   Fuel
*   Groceries
*   Shelter

According to spokeswoman Kayleigh Lovvorn, these reports include allegations of gas stations charging exorbitant prices, such as $3.50 per gallon of fuel, and in one extreme instance, $20 for a single gallon. Paxton's office is actively investigating these claims by issuing Civil Investigative Demands (CIDs) to the businesses in question. Lovvorn noted that a CID was prepared the same evening a particularly egregious case was reported.

Another reported incident involved a business allegedly selling cases of bottled water for $99. A widely shared photograph from a Houston resident depicted cases of bottled water being sold for $43 at a local Best Buy.

A Best Buy spokesman addressed the pricing issue, attributing it to a "big mistake" made by a few employees at a single store. The company stated its focus is on assisting those affected by the hurricane, not exploiting them, and apologized for the incident, assuring it would not recur. The spokesman explained that the error stemmed from employees pricing a case of water based on the individual bottle price for each bottle within the case, as they do not typically sell cases of water.

Attorney General Paxton emphasized the severe penalties for price gouging, particularly when targeting individuals over 65, stating that the cost can reach up to $250,000. He assured that his office is actively pursuing those engaging in such practices.

Texas residents are encouraged to report any instances of price gouging or other scams by emailing consumeremergency@oag.texas.gov or by calling (800) 621-0508.

Turner later announced via Twitter that he was pushing back the start time to midnight, "... to allow volunteers and others to do their great work." Officials said people should ask for credentials and badges if approached by anyone who claims they're from the organization. In addition to the city's relief efforts, officials added in their statement that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement is "not conducting immigration enforcement operations in the affected area." The giant shelter set up at the George R. Brown Convention Center in downtown Houston was packed with more than 9,000 evacuees — nearly double its planned capacity. Surrounded by people taking a smoking break outside the convention center, Danielle Brown hugged her boyfriend, Lorenzo Harps. They arrived Sunday night after being rescued by boat from a ground-level apartment. “Everything was already flooded,” said Brown, huddled beneath a relatively dry overhang as rain poured down. “We had to move to the top floor, to a neighbor.” Before moving up, they tried putting electronics, clothes and keepsakes on counters and shelves. The water climbed to 3 feet within hours before businesslike rescuers evacuated them by boat the next day. The wrath of Harvey was Brown’s first brush with a tropical storm or hurricane. Her message to the untold numbers of residents experiencing a similar crisis: “Be prepared. Be cautious,” she said. “Grab only what you need. Grab your life.” Turner, at a news conference, said two or three more city shelters would open soon. And he again addressed criticism leveled at city officials for declining to call for an evacuation in advance of the storm. “You cannot evacuate 6.5 million people within two days,” he said. “You cannot. That would be chaotic. ... You would be putting people more in harm’s way.” Brett Schere, 34, of Drippings Springs, Texas, traveled to Houston with a convoy of 12 other people and seven boats to help stranded residents. They’ve pulled out more than 120 trapped flood victims. Two of his buddies got trapped by the floodwaters in Spring and Cypress, Texas. Acevedo called Sgt. Steve Perez, a 34-year police veteran, a "sweet, gentle public servant." Authorities said it could be days before the full extent of the storm is known. “We know in these kind of events that, sadly, the death toll goes up,” Acevedo told the Associated Press earlier Tuesday. “I’m really worried about how many bodies we’re going to find" when the water recedes. Acevedo also said police had arrested a group of four armed "hijackers" and three looters. He issued a warning to would-be offenders. "Word to the wise — don't come to Houston because you are going to be caught," he said.
