Shkreli's other business accomplishments are a mixed bag, but all in all pretty impressive for a guy who started off by losing all of his investors' money at least twice. He has a real talent for short selling : "One ranking, by short selling data firm Activist Shorts, puts the performance of his published bearish calls above those of Citron Research, Kase Capital Management’s Whitney Tilson, Greenlight Capital and Jim Chanos’s Kynikos Associates LP," Bloomberg reported yesterday. Turing Pharmaceuticals, his private drug company that has been in the news so much recently, at least ... seems to throw expensive parties? His more recent investment, KaloBios, isn't going that great: It closed yesterday well above where it was when Shkreli announced his position, but was halted today and is looking perilous. Still, I can't shake my sense of amazement that Shkreli seems to have gambled on redemption and won. If you believe the allegations in today's indictment, he lost (or stole) all of his investors' money, then lied to them to string them along, Ponzied it up by raising new money to keep them happy, and then finally found an investment that allowed him to pay off his earlier investors, with profits all around. He wasn't caught because his scheme, like most such schemes, eventually crashed under its own weight. His scheme worked! He was caught, as far as I can tell, for being so abrasive about everything. How could shareholders not complain; how could prosecutors not go after him? Still, it is quite an accomplishment. If he is a securities fraudster, he should be an inspiration to securities fraudsters everywhere. An Appendix About That Wu-Tang Album Martin Shkreli owns the only copy of the Wu-Tang Clan's latest album, "Once Upon a Time in Shaolin." He apparently paid $2 million for it. He has not listened to it. His plan for it is unspeakable, or rather it is speakable -- he has spoken it -- but it is not quotable here. Shkreli and the Wu-Tang Clan "spent months finalizing contracts and devising new legal protections for a distinctive work whose value depends on its singularity." One provision that was not included in those contracts, but that came into existence spontaneously through its own perfection, like God in the ontological proof, was this clause: The buying party also agrees that, at any time during the stipulated 88 year period, the seller may legally plan and attempt to execute one (1) heist or caper to steal back Once Upon A Time In Shaolin, which, if successful, would return all ownership rights to the seller. Said heist or caper can only be undertaken by currently active members of the Wu-Tang Clan and/or actor Bill Murray, with no legal repercussions. Again, this is fake, but so utterly lovely that it became real in the hearts and minds of people around the world, including both RZA and Shkreli himself. And now Shkreli has been indicted by the intriguingly named (and only recently confirmed) U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of New York, Robert L. Capers. Do you see where I am going with this? Assuming that the whole indictment is not part of a Wes Anderson movie from a screenplay by Pynchon, what will happen to the album? The indictment seeks criminal forfeiture "of any property, real or personal, which constitutes or is derived from proceeds traceable to any such offenses," so perhaps it will end up forfeited to the government. (Don't count on it, say experts, but we have sailed way past the outer limits of expertise here.) If it does ... then what? The government regularly auctions seized property to raise cash, so perhaps some other rich private buyer will end up owning the album. Perhaps it is cursed and brings an indictment for securities fraud to whoever possesses it. But nothing requires the government to auction it; the Justice Department could release the album for free so that we can all enjoy this part of our cultural patrimony. Or for all I know it could just hand it over to Joe Biden. Also someone has FOIAed the album, which won't work -- it hasn't been forfeited yet, for one thing -- but is still pretty funny. This column does not necessarily reflect the opinion of the editorial board or Bloomberg LP and its owners. To contact the author of this story: Matt Levine at mlevine51@bloomberg.net To contact the editor responsible for this story: Zara Kessler at zkessler@bloomberg.net

Turing Pharmaceutical CEO and Most Hated Man on the Internet Martin Shkreli was arrested for securities fraud Thursday morning and Twitter couldn't be happier: On the fourth day of Christmas my true love gave to me a warrant for Martin Shkreli. Responding to the rumor that the contract for the album includes that allows the Wu-Tang Clan to steal the album back, but only if they do so heist-movie style, and with Bill Murray, some implored the Clan to jump into action:

Martin Shkreli, a figure widely recognized for his controversial pharmaceutical price increases, was arrested on Thursday in connection with securities fraud charges. While this development brought satisfaction to his numerous detractors, the indictment itself was not related to his widely criticized business strategy of acquiring essential medications and then drastically inflating their prices. Instead, federal prosecutors contend that Shkreli orchestrated a Ponzi scheme during his earlier involvement with hedge funds and the pharmaceutical sector.

The allegations, as detailed by the U.S. Department of Justice, depict a trajectory where an early reputation as a "boy genius" was overshadowed by a pattern of alleged deception and mismanagement. Despite becoming one of the most publicly reviled individuals online in 2015, Shkreli's alleged criminal activities reportedly began on a more modest scale, by Wall Street's standards.

**Early Career and Initial Setbacks**

Shkreli's professional journey commenced on Wall Street as a teenage intern at Jim Cramer’s hedge fund, Cramer, Berkowitz & Co. He later established his own firm, Elea Capital Management, in 2006. However, this venture quickly encountered significant challenges. In 2007, Lehman Brothers initiated legal action against Shkreli and his fund, alleging failure to cover a substantial losing stock market bet, which resulted in a $2.3 million judgment against him.

**Deception in Hedge Fund Operations**

The federal indictment primarily focuses on Shkreli's alleged actions beginning in 2009, when he founded MSMB Capital Management. According to the Justice Department, Shkreli is accused of misleading potential investors to secure crucial funding. Key allegations include:

*   **False Claims of Liquidity:** He allegedly misrepresented the fund's possession of sufficient liquid assets, implying easy withdrawal capabilities for investors.
*   **Misrepresentation of Oversight:** Shkreli is accused of falsely stating that an independent auditor was overseeing the fund's operations, which was not the case.
*   **Concealment of Past Failures:** He allegedly omitted the fact that he had lost all the capital from his prior fund, Elea Capital.

In a specific instance cited by the Justice Department, Shkreli reportedly informed an investor that MSMB had $35 million under management, when in reality, the fund held only $700. Despite having already lost most of the initial $700,000 raised, he allegedly persuaded the investor to transfer over $1 million, followed by an additional $250,000. In total, approximately eight investors are said to have contributed $3 million to Shkreli's fund.

By February 2011, MSMB Capital was reportedly in severe financial distress. A failed short-selling strategy allegedly left Shkreli indebted to Merrill Lynch for $7 million. Coupled with other trading losses, the fund's assets reportedly dwindled from $1.12 million to just $58,000. The Justice Department asserts that MSMB Capital ceased trading that month, effectively becoming defunct. Shkreli is accused of continuing to provide investors with fabricated performance reports, thereby concealing the fund's collapse and his own unauthorized withdrawal of $200,000.

**The Emergence of MSMB Healthcare and Retrophin**

To allegedly sustain his operations, Shkreli is said to have employed a "fake it till you make it" strategy. In February 2011, the same month MSMB Capital reportedly imploded, he launched a second hedge fund, MSMB Healthcare. He is accused of again misrepresenting his fund's assets and concealing his poor track record to attract investors, ultimately raising $5 million from 13 individuals.

Shkreli then allegedly channeled some of these funds into Retrophin, a company that would later become infamous for its role in his price-gouging practices, notably with the kidney medication Thiola. Retrophin also became a central element in Shkreli's alleged Ponzi scheme.

**Unraveling the Alleged Scheme**

In 2012, Merrill Lynch pursued arbitration regarding the $7 million debt and secured a $1.3 million settlement. With MSMB Capital possessing no assets, Shkreli allegedly used funds invested in Retrophin, supported by fabricated and backdated paperwork, to satisfy the debt owed to Merrill Lynch.

Around the same period, Shkreli reportedly informed the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), which was investigating his activities, that MSMB Capital was being liquidated with $2.6 million in assets, despite it having no actual assets. To conceal this, he and his lawyer, Evan Greebel (who was also indicted), allegedly created documentation to show a stake in Retrophin that the fund had never truly held.

Furthermore, Shkreli's original hedge fund investors, who had allegedly been misled for years, began seeking the return of their investments. Initially, Shkreli is said to have attempted to settle these claims through Retrophin. However, after Retrophin became a public company, its external auditor raised questions about these settlements.

On the advice of Greebel, Shkreli allegedly shifted to paying former investors through sham "consulting" contracts totaling over $7 million. Eventually, Retrophin's board uncovered these transactions, leading to Shkreli's dismissal as CEO and a subsequent $65 million lawsuit against him. Shkreli publicly dismissed these accusations as "preposterous," but the Department of Justice evidently held a different view.

Andrew Wiseman's bold Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request targeted the highly exclusive Wu-Tang Clan album, "The Wu – Once Upon A Time In Shaolin," held by controversial pharmaceutical executive Martin Shkreli. This one-of-a-kind double album, a product of years of secret creation, had recently been purchased by Shkreli before his arrest. Wiseman's initiative, humorously dubbed the "Best FOIA ever," ignited public interest in making this rare musical artifact available online, potentially even for download.

Despite the public's hope for immediate access, the FBI's New York field office clarified via Twitter that no seizure warrant had been issued for the Wu-Tang Clan album in relation to Shkreli's arrest. This critical detail confirmed that the coveted LP remained in Shkreli's possession and was not confiscated by law enforcement.

Wiseman himself announced his intentions on Twitter, tweeting, "Just FOIA'd the FBI to try to get the Wu-Tang Clan album from Martin #Shkreli - Let's party!" He outlined his vision for a successful request: a celebratory party at his home where the album would be released freely. Instead of payment, Wiseman suggested attendees donate to charities, specifically mentioning @TheMissingMaps. This endeavor showcased Wiseman's innovative approach to information access and his philanthropic aims, seeking to use a unique situation for a positive outcome. The entire episode highlighted the intriguing intersection of music, law, and public interest, all stemming from a memorable FOIA request.

A well-pedigreed corporate lawyer with extensive experience in high-stakes mergers and acquisitions played a critical role in helping pharmaceutical executive Martin Shkreli orchestrate a series of allegedly fraudulent transactions, according to a federal indictment unsealed on Thursday. Evan L. Greebel, most recently a New York partner at the white-shoe law firm Kaye Scholer LLP, was charged with one count of wire fraud, a crime that carries a maximum 20-year prison sentence. Mr. Shkreli has been arrested on securities and...

That way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved.Then the Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.The goal is to fix all broken links on the web . NEW YORK (AP) — A boyish-looking entrepreneur who became the new face of corporate greed when he jacked up the price of a lifesaving drug fiftyfold was led away in handcuffs by the FBI on unrelated fraud charges Thursday in a scene that left more than a few Americans positively gleeful. Martin Shkreli, the former hedge fund manager under fire for buying a pharmaceutical company and ratcheting up the price of a life-saving drug, sits in an awaiting car after being taken into custody following... Shkreli, the former hedge fund manager vilified in nearly every... (Associated Press) FILE - In this Oct. 1, 2015 file photo, AIDS activists pour cat litter on an image of Turing Pharmaceuticals CEO Martin Shkreli in a makeshift cat litter pan during a protest highlighting pharmaceutical drug... (Associated Press) Martin Shkreli leaves the courthouse after his arraignment in New York, Thursday, Dec. 17, 2015. Shkreli, the former hedge fund manager vilified in nearly every corner of America for buying a pharmaceutical... (Associated Press) Martin Shkreli, the former hedge fund manager under fire for buying a pharmaceutical company and ratcheting up the price of a life-saving drug, is belted into an awaiting car after being taken into custody... (Associated Press) Martin Shkreli, center, leaves the courthouse after his arraignment in New York, Thursday, Dec. 17, 2015. Shkreli, the former hedge fund manager vilified in nearly every corner of America for buying a... (Associated Press) Martin Shkreli, the former hedge fund manager under fire for buying a pharmaceutical company and ratcheting up the price of a life-saving drug, is escorted by law enforcement agents in New York Thursday,... (Associated Press) In this courtroom sketch, entrepreneur and former hedge fund manager Martin Shkreli, second from left, appears in court for his arraignment on fraud charges, Thursday, Dec. 17, 2015, in New York. Prosecutors... (Associated Press) U.S. Attorney Robert Capers walks past a poster detailing Martin Shkreli's scheme before speaking to reporters during a news conference in New York, Thursday, Dec. 17, 2015. Shkreli, the former hedge... (Associated Press) Martin Shkreli, a 32-year-old former hedge fund manager and relentless self-promoter who has called himself "the world's most eligible bachelor" on Twitter, was arrested in a gray hoodie and taken into federal court in Brooklyn, where he pleaded not guilty. He was released on $5 million bail. If convicted, he could get up to 20 years in prison. He left court without speaking to reporters. His attorneys had no immediate comment. Hours later, Shkreli tweeted: "Glad to be home. Thanks for the support." Online, many people took delight in his arrest, calling him a greedy, arrogant "punk" who gave capitalism a bad name and got what was coming to him. Some cracked jokes about lawyers jacking up their hourly fees 5,000 percent to defend him in his hour of need. Prosecutors said that between 2009 and 2014, Shkreli lost some of his hedge fund investors' money through bad trades, then looted Retrophin, a pharmaceutical company where he was CEO, for $11 million to pay back his disgruntled clients. Shkreli "engaged in multiple schemes to ensnare investors through a web of lies and deceit," U.S. Attorney Robert Capers said in a statement. Shkreli was charged with securities fraud and conspiracy. A second defendant, lawyer Evan Greebel, of Scarsdale, New York, was charged with conspiracy and also pleaded not guilty. A spokesman for Shkreli released a statement saying he denies the charges and "expects to be fully vindicated." "It is no coincidence that these charges, the result of investigations which have been languishing for considerable time, have been filed at the same time of Shkreli's high-profile, controversial and yet unrelated activities," said spokesman Craig Stevens. In September, Shkreli was widely vilified after a drug company he founded, Turing Pharmaceuticals, spent $55 million for the U.S. rights to sell a medicine called Daraprim and promptly raised the price from $13.50 to $750 per pill. The 62-year-old drug is the only approved treatment for toxoplasmosis, a rare parasitic disease that mainly strikes pregnant women, cancer patients and AIDS patients. The move sparked outrage on the presidential campaign trail and helped prompt a Capitol Hill hearing on drug prices. Headlines called the Brooklyn-born Shkreli such thing as "America's most hated man," the "drug industry's villain" and "biotech's bad boy" — and those were just some of the more printable names. Hillary Clinton called it price-gouging and said the company's behavior was "outrageous." Donald Trump called Shkreli "a spoiled brat." Bernie Sanders returned a donation from Shkreli. Prosecutors said the investigation that led to Shkreli's arrest dated back to last year, before the furor over the drug-price increase. Shkreli defended the increase by saying that insurance and other programs would enable patients to get the drug and that the profits would help fund research into new treatments. But he also made an unapologetic business-is-business argument for the price jump. In fact, he recently said he probably should have raised it more. "No one wants to say it, no one's proud of it, but this is a capitalist society, a capitalist system and capitalist rules," he said in an interview at the Forbes Healthcare Summit this month. "And my investors expect me to maximize profits, not to minimize them or go half or go 70 percent but to go to 100 percent of the profit curve." Amid the uproar, Shkreli said Turing would cut the price of Daraprim. Last month, however, Turing reneged. Instead, the company is reducing what it charges hospitals for Daraprim by as much as 50 percent. While most patients' copayments will be $10 or less a month, insurance companies will be stuck with the bulk of the tab, potentially driving up future treatment and insurance costs. On Thursday, Robert Weissman, president of the watchdog group Public Citizen, said Shkreli got "a deserved comeuppance." "Al Capone was brought down for tax evasion, but he committed many worse crimes," Weissman said. "So if Shkreli's arrested for securities violations, it's a comparable justice." Shkreli is known as a prolific user of Twitter and often livestreams his work day over the Internet, inviting people to chat with him at his desk. He refers to those who follow him online as his "fans." Recently it emerged that he bought the only copy of a Wu-Tang Clan album titled "Once Upon a Time in Shaolin," which the hip-hop group sold on the condition that it not be released publicly. He said he paid $2 million. The FBI's New York office said on Twitter that agents did not seize Shkreli's album. Said Capers, the chief federal prosecutor: "We're not aware of how he raised the funds to buy the Wu-Tang album." Last month, Shkreli was named chairman and CEO of KaloBios Pharmaceuticals after buying a majority stake in the struggling cancer drug developer. After his arrest, its stock fell by more than half Thursday before trading in the company was suspended. ___ Associated Press writers Larry Neumeister and Jennifer Peltz in New York and Linda A. Johnson in Trenton, New Jersey, contributed to this report.

query: How did Stephen Miller's perceived lack of policy experience influence the New York Times' portrayal of him as a senior adviser in the Trump administration?

documents.text: The New York Times is trying to slap senior White House adviser Stephen Miller as a “far-right gadfly with little policy experience” who supposedly got promoted beyond his capabilities into President Donald Trump’s administration. Miller is a tough and determined populist, but he is portrayed merely as a bomb-throwing email spammer in the article by Times reporters Glenn Thrush and Jennifer Staunheir: advertisement As a top aide to Mr. Sessions, the conservative Alabama senator, Mr. Miller dispatched dozens and dozens of bombastic emails to congressional staff members and reporters in early 2013 when the Senate was considering a big bipartisan immigration overhaul. Mr. Miller slammed the evils of “foreign labor” and pushed around nasty news articles on proponents of compromise, like Senator Marco Rubio of Florida… The ascent of Mr. Miller from far-right gadfly with little policy experience to the president’s senior policy adviser came as a shock to many of the staff members who knew him from his seven years in the Senate. A man whose emails were, until recently, considered spam by many of his Republican peers is now shaping the Trump administration’s core domestic policies with his economic nationalism and hard-line positions on immigration. It’s curious the Times would claim Miller had “little policy experience,” given how many prominent conservative writers credit his back-room efforts with Alabama Republican Sen. Jeff Sessions for derailing wage-slashing globalist trade deals and immigration-expanding legislation. At age 22, Miller was one of youngest (if not the youngest) press secretaries on the Hill for then-Minnesota Republican Rep. Michele Bachmann. At age 24, Miller became the press secretary for the Senate Judiciary Committee, where he first worked with Sessions. As CNN reports, “it was with Sessions where Miller came to be known as a skilled operator and true believer in conservative immigration policy. Miller helped his boss become the most outspoken critic of the 2013 bipartisan Gang of Eight immigration reform bill, penning a handbook filled with talking points on the issue and helping him lead the charge behind the scene to kill the measure.” Sessions credited Miller, then his communications director, for quickly and thoroughly compiling critical facts about the Gang of Eight bill that stopped it from passing through the House and landing on President Barack Obama’s desk. “We had been working on the ideas in it for months, and Stephen put it in the handbook in a very quick time in a very cogent fashion. It was very timely and it impacted the outcome of the vote,” Sessions told Politico in June. Miller “captured Sessions’ voice,” as another Sessions staffer said, “was able to anticipate what he needed for an interview or a speech.” Miller’s reach extended far. “A combination wonk and flack who not only formulates policy but also writes speeches, press releases, and op-eds and assists reporters with scoops and story pegs and telling details, Miller is the populist counterpart to liberal wunderkinds Ezra Klein and Ben Rhodes,” wrote Washington Free Beacon editor-in-chief Matthew Continetti in December. “He’s one of the most effective aides in Washington—despite having lived here for less than a decade.” National Review editor Rich Lowry attributed the Gang of Eight bill’s failure to Sessions and Miller. “He did more than anyone perhaps except Jeff Sessions himself to bring down the Gang of Eight bill,” Lowry wrote on Feb. 2. “It’s easy to see how he climbed so high in the Trump world, and in the area of immigration policy, few are as committed or as fluent on the details.” Radio host Laura Ingraham also credited Miller for his role in halting the Gang of Eight bill. “Stephen was very important from an inside policy angle in the effort to stop and expose the Gang of Eight bill and the Trans-Pacific Partnership,” Ingraham said. “He knew their vulnerabilities, and understood the substantive argument that led to their defeat.” “The ‘gadflies’ are those who spouted mindless generalities to support the doomed initiatives,” she added. Miller’s arrival in the White House thrilled conservatives who recognize his tireless work on behalf of a nationalist, populist agenda on immigration and trade. “I’ve known Stephen Miller for many years, and he is a principled conservative, who played a pivotal role for the president during the campaign as a key speechwriter and advisor,” Fox News host Sean Hannity told Breitbart News. “I also know he is exactly the type of person any administration needs. He is smart, he works long hours, rolls up his sleeves, and serves the president and the country, and never looks for credit.” “What the Alt-Left Radical media is doing is systematically trying to vilify and demonize any and all people who support and serve the president,” Hannity added. “The media, generally speaking, has lost any credibility they may have had left after the Wikileaks revelations. As I have said many times, ‘Journalism is dead,’ and they have devolved into the propaganda arm for the Democratic Party.” Best-selling conservative author Ann Coulter called the Times article “shockingly honest.” “I THOUGHT IT WAS A FANTASTIC ARTICLE,” Coulter told Breitbart News. “I never knew Miller used to be a chain-smoker, which makes me love him even more! (No wonder he’s so smart.) ‘Gadfly’ is just one word in an otherwise SHOCKINGLY honest NYT article.” “It was more like the old NYT, with reporters who may have been lefties, but were at least serious reporters,” she continued. “Most of the drivel from the NYT these days reads like something from Salon. This article got the facts right and threw in this one snippy word—a word that was belied by the rest of the article. Good reporting. Good article.” Coulter has frequently heaped praise on Miller and on Trump for hiring him: I'M IN HEAVEN! Trump hires Sen. Sessions' brain trust, Stephen Miller – https://t.co/yUNCGAGQLE He's not backing down on immigration. — Ann Coulter (@AnnCoulter) January 25, 2016 Fantastic hit piece in Politico on the magnificent Stephen Miller, Trump aide and my favorite human being – https://t.co/nRdqOyNJM8 — Ann Coulter (@AnnCoulter) June 27, 2016 Trump immigration advisor Stephen Miller on CNN. You know he's killing it when the other panelists gasp & guffaw – https://t.co/BzKgQfWB8y — Ann Coulter (@AnnCoulter) March 27, 2016 Politico tries to destroy Stephen Miller with relentless, crazily negative bias, but screws up by quoting him. https://t.co/nRdqOyNJM8 — Ann Coulter (@AnnCoulter) June 27, 2016 Miller himself said he is carrying out the same agenda that propelled Trump to the White House. “My driving passion throughout my professional career has been to fight for social and economic justice for working families who are not paid enough, respected enough, or protected enough because of a system that tilts unfairly towards the largest and powerful special interests,” Miller told Breitbart News. “I am now privileged beyond words to work for a president who is a tireless voice and unyielding champion for every working person in this country who deserves a brighter future.”

On February 12, 2017, Senior Policy Advisor Stephen Miller, speaking on the CBS News program "Face the Nation," declared that President Trump had accomplished more in his initial weeks than many presidents achieve in an entire term, a statement made during a period marked by the challenging implementation of an executive order on immigration. "Face the Nation," a cornerstone of Sunday morning public affairs programming, has been a fixture on CBS since its debut on November 7, 1954, establishing itself as one of the longest-running news broadcasts in television history.

The esteemed program, hosted by John Dickerson, who also serves as Political Director for CBS News and contributes to Slate magazine and its podcast "The Political Gabfest," consistently features a distinguished array of guests, including high-ranking government officials, influential politicians, and prominent international figures. Viewers interested in engaging further with the content and reporting from "Face the Nation" and CBS News can explore various platforms, such as subscribing to the "Face the Nation" channel, watching full episodes, and following the program on social media channels including Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, and Google+. Additionally, CBS News offers subscriptions to newsletters for the latest news and original reporting, mobile apps for on-the-go access, and CBS All Access for new episodes, live local news, and full seasons of popular shows.

Stephen Miller, a senior policy adviser, has been a constant presence by President Trump's side for over a year. His journey from a young conservative in liberal Santa Monica, California, to a key architect of the Trump administration's "America First" agenda reveals a consistent dedication to challenging liberal orthodoxy, particularly on issues of race and identity.

During his time at Santa Monica High School, Miller frequently clashed with administrators, voicing his objections to multiculturalism, Spanish-language morning announcements, and the lack of mandatory Pledge of Allegiance recitation. These sentiments were further articulated in a Duke University sophomore column titled "Santa Monica High’s Multicultural Fistfights," where he labeled his alma mater a "center for political indoctrination." Miller wrote in the 2005 article for FrontPage magazine, "The social experiment that Santa Monica High School has become is yet one more example of the dismal failure of leftism and the delusions and paranoia of its architects."

Before becoming a top adviser to President Trump and a prominent advocate for the executive order temporarily halting entry from seven Muslim-majority countries, Miller honed his skills as a culture warrior. Like Trump, Miller developed his conservative identity within liberal environments, which lent him credibility among fellow conservatives for his willingness to engage in battles on opposing turf.

His early career included frequent calls to the nationally syndicated "Larry Elder Show," where he gained recognition in conservative media circles for his controversial stances and his role as a champion for those who felt marginalized by political correctness. Miller produced a series of sharp columns on race, gender, and other contentious topics. At Duke, he became known to Fox News viewers as a staunch defender of the white lacrosse players who were wrongfully accused of rape. By his late twenties, Miller was a key aide to then-Senator Jeff Sessions, playing a role in thwarting efforts by immigrant advocacy groups to create a path to citizenship for millions of undocumented Hispanic immigrants.

Now 31, Miller, alongside former Breitbart News chief Stephen K. Bannon, is considered a principal architect of Trump's populist "America First" agenda. This agenda has significantly impacted the national debate on immigration and trade, and has raised concerns among traditional U.S. allies. Miller, officially the senior adviser to the president for policy, joined the Trump campaign in January 2016, when Sessions was one of the few Republican officials to endorse Trump's candidacy. While Trump's campaign leadership saw changes, with Bannon joining later, Miller remained a consistent figure whose influence and profile grew. He contributed to some of Trump's most forceful speeches, including the Republican National Convention acceptance address where Trump declared, "nobody knows the system better than me, which is why I alone can fix it." Miller also frequently served as a warm-up act for Trump at rallies. In one notable instance in Wisconsin, he criticized Senator Ted Cruz for supporting increased legal immigration, arguing it would lead to more Muslims entering the country without regard for national security.

Following reports of Miller's significant role in crafting the executive order that imposed a 90-day ban on citizens from Iraq, Syria, Iran, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, and Yemen, he has faced increased scrutiny. MSNBC's Joe Scarborough, host of "Morning Joe," criticized Miller, stating, "you can just write executive orders and tell all of your Cabinet agencies to go to hell."

For Miller, his position in the Trump White House represents a natural progression, allowing him to work for a president who appears to share his drive to challenge political opponents. Larry Elder, the Los Angeles-based conservative talk-show host whom Miller considers a mentor, noted, "The way that people on the left abuse and slam people on the right — that’s probably the thing that’s most concerned Stephen." Elder elaborated on Miller's concerns regarding "the lack of fairness. The left wing dominance in academia. The left wing dominance in the media. The left wing dominance in Hollywood."

Miller's ideological development began in a left-leaning high school, where he perceived a segregation in social and academic life, coupled with an administrative focus on multiculturalism. In an interview with The Washington Post, Miller stated, "My best judgment at the time was that the educational answer that had been provided, which was to reject the melting-pot formula in favor of an educational formula that focused on all the things that made us different, was not working."

Miller rejects the label of "provocateur," asserting that his aim is not to seek attention but to "battle against slim odds, a stacked deck and powerful entrenched forces, in pursuit of justice." He explained that he moved away from his parents' more liberal politics in Santa Monica after subscribing to Guns & Ammo magazine and engaging with the writings of Charlton Heston, former president of the National Rifle Association.

Miller began appearing on Elder's show, which aired in 300 markets, after the 9/11 attacks, feeling a lack of patriotism in his hometown. Elder recalled Miller's first call to the show was to protest his school's failure to recite the Pledge of Allegiance daily, a requirement by state law. In his writings, Miller detailed his efforts to reinstate the pledge, citing a recalcitrant administration that resisted even after he pointed out the legal violation. He wrote in a letter to the editor at the time, "Osama Bin Laden would feel very welcome at Santa Monica High School."

Miller described the campus instructional environment as "breathtakingly PC." Mark Kelly, the principal at the time, stated he did not recall the episode as a major conflict. Kelly confirmed that upon Miller flagging the issue, he researched the law and realized the school needed to change its policy. Miller was subsequently invited to lead the pledge after its reinstatement. "Stephen was right," Kelly recalled. This experience validated for Miller the importance of confronting powerful opposition.

Miller also advocated for an on-campus speech by David Horowitz, a controversial conservative figure who became an early mentor and later introduced Miller to Jeff Sessions. Horowitz recalled being impressed by Miller's inherent combativeness, stating, "One of the things that struck me when I became a conservative was that conservatives don’t have any fight. They don’t have any stomach for it. . . . Stephen Miller had that from the get-go."

Cultural identity issues particularly animated Miller. In a high school newspaper column titled "A Time to Kill," he advocated for a violent response to radical Islamists. "We have all heard about how peaceful and benign the Islamic religion is, but no matter how many times you say that, it cannot change the fact that millions of radical Muslims would celebrate your death for the simple reason that you are Christian, Jewish or American," Miller wrote.

Ari Rosmarin, a civil rights lawyer who edited the student newspaper, recalled Miller's particular criticism of a Mexican American student group. "I think he’s got a very sharp understanding of what words and issues will poke and provoke progressives, because he came up around it and really cut his teeth picking these fights that had low stakes but high offense," Rosmarin commented. This skill contributed to Miller's status as a minor celebrity in conservative intellectual circles, noted for his passion, age, and background. According to Elder, Miller appeared on his show 70 times before graduating high school. Rosmarin observed, "He found a really unique role to play that was deeply attractive to national conservatives. He was like a lonely warrior behind enemy lines."

Within Santa Monica High School, however, where ethnic diversity and liberal values were celebrated, Miller became somewhat of a pariah. This environment reportedly made him more assertive. A former teacher, speaking anonymously due to fear of professional repercussions, stated, "He had to come on a little strong as a defense mechanism — just to survive. He came under a lot of fire, even from teachers."

At Duke, Miller's biweekly column for the student newspaper frequently drew criticism from classmates. In one piece, he argued that laws mandating equal pay for men and women would harm businesses, attributing pay disparities largely to women taking time off for childbirth, being less inclined to seek raises, and avoiding hazardous work. He wrote, "The point is that the pay gap has virtually nothing to do with gender discrimination. Sorry, feminists. Hate to break this good news to you." In another column, "The Case for Christmas," Miller, who is Jewish, argued for the holiday's broader recognition as a "crucial American holiday," stating, "Christianity is embedded in the very soul of our nation."

Miller gained national attention following the false rape accusations against three white Duke lacrosse players, a case marked by racial tension. The players were later exonerated, and the local district attorney was disbarred for misconduct. Miller wrote extensively on the case and appeared on national television to discuss it. In November 2006, he wrote, "This travesty has been allowed to continue because we live in a nation paralyzed by racial paranoia," arguing that professors and others feared defending the students due to the district attorney's framing of the case as a racial crusade, which would incur political costs with the black community. Reflecting on his role, Miller told The Post, "The one takeaway I have from it is that in a difficult moment, I took a stand on principle — and I was correct." He later stated that while his college writings reflected his views at the time, he hoped his thoughts had matured on various issues, though he declined to specify areas of change.

Miller's outspokenness in the lacrosse case brought him to the attention of Richard Spencer, a white nationalist and Duke graduate student at the time. Spencer claimed to have become friendly with Miller through the Duke Conservative Union in the fall of 2006. "He was very out in front, very bold and strong," Spencer said. Spencer's later assertion to the Daily Beast that he was a "mentor" to Miller was angrily denied by Miller, who stated, "I condemn

At a campaign rally in Las Vegas on Saturday evening, President Trump actively endorsed Senator Dean Heller, a Republican from Nevada. He urged attendees to reject Heller's opponent, Representative Jacky Rosen, a Democrat from Nevada. Trump characterized Rosen as "Wacky Jacky." He asserted that a vote for her would lead to increased taxes. He also claimed it would result in weak borders. Ultimately, he suggested, this would mean more crime.

The President also discussed key initiatives of his administration. On international trade, Trump expressed optimism. He stated that relations would "work out." He also said that current discussions were "just starting, but it's going to happen." He elaborated on this point. He remarked, "we're the piggy bank that everybody likes to rob from." Regarding immigration, Trump emphasized the necessity for the U.S. to "be very strong." He affirmed that his administration was performing "a very good job." Some immigration policy experts echoed this sentiment.

In foreign policy, President Trump commented on his relationship with North Korean leader Kim Jong Un. He described it as having "good chemistry." He also said that they "get along great." He characterized Kim Jong Un as "a smart, tough guy." He also called him "a great negotiator." This view contrasts with assessments from some national security analysts.

President Trump also used the occasion to criticize Senator John McCain of Arizona. He did not name him directly. He specifically referenced the Senator's crucial "no" vote on the GOP health care bill in the previous year. The strained relationship between Trump and McCain is well-documented. Numerous political commentators have noted this. Trump has previously disparaged McCain's service as a prisoner of war. He stated a preference for "people who weren't captured." Senator McCain is battling an aggressive brain cancer. He has publicly stated that Trump lacks "principles and beliefs." Many in the medical community and political observers share this sentiment.

White House senior policy adviser Stephen Miller. (Nicholas Kamm/AFP via Getty Images) White House senior policy adviser Stephen Miller appeared on ABC’s “The Week” on Sunday, spouting a bunch of false talking points on alleged voter fraud. (He also repeated similar claims on other Sunday talk shows.) To his credit, host George Stephanopoulus repeatedly challenged Miller, noting that he had provided no evidence to support his claims. But Miller charged ahead, using the word “fact” three times in a vain effort to bolster his position. Here’s a guide through the back and forth. STEPHANOPOULOS: Let me move on, though, to the question of voter fraud as well. President Trump again this week suggested in a meeting with senators that thousands of illegal voters were bused from Massachusetts to New Hampshire and that’s what caused his defeat in the state of New Hampshire, also the defeat of Senator Kelly Ayotte. That has provoked a response from a member of the Federal Election Commission, Ellen Weintraub, who says, “I call upon the president to immediately share New Hampshire voter fraud evidence so that his allegations may be investigated promptly.” Do you have that evidence? Stephanopoulus is referring to a Feb. 10 Politico report of a closed-door meeting Trump held with senators to discuss the nomination of Judge Neil Gorsuch to the Supreme Court: “The president claimed that he and Ayotte both would have been victorious in the Granite State if not for the ‘thousands’ of people who were ‘brought in on buses’ from neighboring Massachusetts to ‘illegally’ vote in New Hampshire. According to one participant who described the meeting, ‘an uncomfortable silence’ momentarily overtook the room.” Ayotte lost her Senate race by about 1,000 votes but did not challenge the results; Hillary Clinton defeated Trump in New Hampshire by nearly 3,000 votes. MILLER: I have actually, having worked before on a campaign in New Hampshire, I can tell you that this issue of busing voters into New Hampshire is widely known by anyone who’s worked in New Hampshire politics. It’s very real. It’s very serious. This morning, on this show, is not the venue for me to lay out all the evidence. This is false. PolitiFact New Hampshire in November gave the state’s governor, Chris Sununu, a “Pants on Fire” for claiming that voters were bused in — and Sununu quickly retreated from his comment. New Hampshire Secretary of State Bill Gardner said voter fraud was not widespread problem, largely because the law requires voters to show a valid identification at the polls. If an ID is lacking, the voter’s photo is taken, they have to sign an affidavit affirming their identify and then state officials follow up. Sununu later said he did not mean to imply that “I see buses coming over,” saying it was more of a figure of speech. “Sununu said he was referring to an incident over Portsmouth state Sen. Martha Fuller Clark allowing Democratic staffers to live at her house in the 2008 and 2012 elections,” PolitiFact reported. “Those staffers voted in New Hampshire elections using Fuller Clark’s address, which is not illegal, as they were living in the state at least 3 months before the election, the Attorney General later ruled.” Tom Rath, a former New Hampshire attorney general and prominent Republican in the state, tweeted this after Miller’s comments: Let me as be unequivocal as possible-allegations of voter fraud in NH are baseless,without any merit-it's shameful to spread these fantasies — Tom Rath (@polguru) February 12, 2017 We sent the White House the PolitiFact article and asked the White House for additional evidence. We will update if we receive a response. MILLER: But I can tell you this, voter fraud is a serious problem in this country. You have millions of people who are registered in two states or who are dead who are registered to vote. And you have 14 percent of noncitizens, according to academic research, at a minimum, are registered to vote, which is an astonishing statistic. Ugh. Miller has again resorted to bogus claims that we have repeatedly debunked. To repeat: A 2012 Pew Center on the States study found problems with inaccurate voter registrations, people who registered in more than one state (which could happen if the voter moves and registers in the new state without telling the former state) and deceased voters whose information was still on the voter rolls. But the primary author of the Pew report tweeted in response to Trump’s staff’s claim that he “can confirm that report made no findings re: voter fraud.” We found millions of out of date registration records due to people moving or dying, but found no evidence that voter fraud resulted. — David Becker (@beckerdavidj) November 28, 2016 As to the 14 percent figure — stemming from research by Old Dominion University professors, using data from 2008 and 2010 — that also has been misrepresented by Trump and his staff. They have ignored updates and challenges to the research. The researchers have also warned that “it is impossible to tell for certain whether the noncitizens who responded to the survey were representative of the broader population of noncitizens.” One of the researchers, Jesse Richman, wrote about the Trump staff’s use of his research. The results “suggest that almost all elections in the US are not determined by noncitizen participation, with occasional and very rare potential exceptions,” he said, noting that “there has been a tendency to misread our results as proof of massive voter fraud, which we don’t think they are.” In other words, the researcher whom Miller is citing says his research does not show what Miller claims. STEPHANOPOULOS: You can’t make a — hold on a second. You just claimed again that there was illegal voting in New Hampshire, people bused in from the state of Massachusetts. Do you have any evidence to back that up? MILLER: I’m saying anybody — George, go to New Hampshire. Talk to anybody who has worked in politics there for a long time. Everybody is aware of the problem in New Hampshire with respect to — STEPHANOPOULOS: I’m asking you as the White House senior — hold on a second. I’m asking you as the White House senior policy adviser. The president made a statement, saying he was the victim of voter fraud, people are being bused from — MILLER: And the president — the president — the president was. STEPHANOPOULOS: Do you have any evidence? MILLER: If this is an issue that interests you, then we can talk about it more in the future. And we now have — our governance is beginning to get stood up. But we have a Department of Justice and we have more officials. An issue of voter fraud is something we’re going to be looking at very seriously and very hard. But the reality is, is that we know for a fact, you have massive numbers of noncitizens registered to vote in this country. Nobody disputes that. False. As shown above, this is disputed even by the researcher whose work is being cited by Miller: “There has been a tendency to misread our results as proof of massive voter fraud, which we don’t think they are.” MILLER: And many, many highly qualified people, like Kris Kobach, the Kansas secretary of state, have looked deeply into this issue and have confirmed it to be true and have put together evidence. And I suggest you invite Kris Kobach onto your show and he can walk you through some of the evidence of voter fraud in greater detail. Miller mentioned Kobach, but the latter’s efforts at proving voter fraud have been mocked in Kansas. In a scathing editorial titled “Kris Kobach is a big fraud on Kansas voter fraud,” the Kansas City Star accused the “publicity-seeking” Kansas secretary of state of throwing out “wild claims” and wasting taxpayer funds as part of “loathsome attacks on U.S. immigration policy.” State Rep. John Carmichael, a Democrat, has introduced a bill to strip Kobach of his prosecutorial power because he has “dramatically overstated the frequency of voter fraud during his tenure as Kansas’ secretary of state,” the Wichita Eagle reported in January. “Carmichael noted that Kobach has not brought a single case against a noncitizen for voting illegally. All of the cases he has brought concern U.S. citizens accused of voting in more than one state.” STEPHANOPOULOS: Just for the record, you have provided absolutely no evidence. The president’s made a statement. MILLER: The White House has provided enormous evidence with respect to voter fraud, with respect to people being registered in more than one state, dead people voting, noncitizens being registered to vote. George, it is a fact and you will not deny it, that there are massive numbers of noncitizens in this country who are registered to vote. That is a scandal. As noted, the “enormous evidence” has been repeatedly debunked. MILLER: We should stop the presses. And, as a country, we should be aghast about the fact that you have people who have no right to vote in this country registered to vote, canceling out the franchise of lawful citizens of this country. That’s the story we should be talking about. And I’m prepared to go on any show, anywhere, anytime, and repeat it and say the president of the United States is correct 100 percent. STEPHANOPOULOS: Well, you just repeated, though, you just made those declarations. But, for the record, you have provided zero evidence that the president was the victim of massive voter fraud in New Hampshire. You provided zero evidence that the president’s claim that he would have won the general — the popular vote — if 3 million to 5 million illegal immigrants hadn’t voted, zero evidence for either one of those claims. The Pinocchio Test Stephanopoulos is right. The White House continues to provide zero evidence to back up its claims of voter fraud. Officials instead retreat to the same bogus talking points that have been repeatedly shown to be false. It’s pretty ridiculous to cite research in a way that even the researcher says is inappropriate, and yet Miller keeps saying 14 percent of noncitizens are registered to vote. The Republican governor of New Hampshire has admitted that he was wrong to say buses of illegal voters voted in the election, and yet Miller shamelessly suggests that is the case. Miller cites a supposed expert on voter fraud, Kobach, who has been mocked for failing to prove his own claims of voter fraud. Miller also repeats a claim about people being registered to vote in two states, even though that is not an example of voter fraud. Miller earns Four Pinocchios — over and over again. Four Pinocchios (About our rating scale) Send us facts to check by filling out this form Keep tabs on Trump’s promises with our Trump Promise Tracker Sign up for The Fact Checker weekly newsletter Share the Facts Washington Post 4 5 Washington Post Rating: Washington Post Rating: "You have 14 percent of non-citizens, according to academic research, at a minimum, are registered to vote, which is an astonishing statistic." Stephen Miller White House senior policy adviser in an interview on ABC News’s "This Week" Sunday, February 12, 2017 02/12/2017 Read More info

MSNBC's "Morning Joe" hosts, Mika Brzezinski and Joe Scarborough, delivered a scathing critique of Stephen Miller, a senior advisor to President Donald Trump, after his recent appearances on Sunday talk shows. Miller, who advises the president, faced sharp criticism from the "Morning Joe" hosts for his combative stance against the judiciary. This occurred despite earning praise from President Trump himself.

Brzezinski voiced her strong disapproval, stating, "It’s so much worse than I thought." She actively promoted the hashtag #MillerTime2Go on her Twitter account. Joe Scarborough, who had previously criticized Miller's role in immigration executive orders, continued his pointed remarks.

Scarborough emphasized core principles of American governance. He pointed out that presidential decisions are subject to judicial review. This fundamental concept was articulated by Alexander Hamilton and James Madison in the Federalist Papers. It is a cornerstone of Madison's Constitution. He described Miller's performance as "horrendous" and "an embarrassment."

On Sunday, Miller defended the president's use of executive orders. He directly challenged judges who opposed them. Speaking on Fox News Sunday, Miller stated, "We do not have judicial supremacy in this country. We have three co-equal branches of government."

Brzezinski also commented on the administration's apparent support for Miller. She remarked, "They love him. They love this guy." Scarborough issued a warning about the potential consequences if Miller's assertions were put into practice. He predicted, "We would have impeachment proceedings within the next six months. Donald Trump would be impeached." He concluded his remarks by observing, "There are some anti-Constitutional forces in that White House."

Stephen Miller's profound impact on Donald Trump's political messaging, particularly as a principal speechwriter, is undeniable. Miller possessed a remarkable ability to embody and amplify Trump's sentiments, crafting words that often felt indistinguishable from the former president's own. This deep resonance frequently allowed Miller to set the tone at Trump's campaign rallies, articulating messages that became defining elements of the Trump platform. As Miller himself articulated, "We’re going to build that wall, and we’re going to build it out of love."

Jason Miller, who collaborated with Stephen Miller during the Trump campaign, highlighted his colleague's unwavering conviction. He characterized Stephen as "a true believer in every sense of the word, not just in this message of economic populism but in President Trump as a leader." Jason Miller further observed, "Steve’s fiercely loyal and has a better understanding of the president’s vision than almost anyone." The stylistic congruence between Trump and Miller was so pronounced that distinguishing their voices was often a challenge. Stephen Miller adeptly modulated his naturally more formal speaking style to emulate Trump's characteristic visceral and spontaneous delivery.

This close working dynamic was frequently initiated by Trump himself. During the campaign's final months, Miller was part of an exclusive cadre of staff who traveled extensively with Trump. He was often summoned for speechwriting duties with a direct directive from Trump: "Ready!" Accounts from campaign insiders suggest the former president insisted on meticulous control over nearly every word, reportedly criticizing staff who altered his phrasing or introduced overly intricate policy details.

Miller's political philosophy has demonstrated remarkable consistency and inflexibility, even as his speechwriting evolved. This steadfastness was evident from his early years. At Santa Monica High School, a predominantly liberal environment, Miller frequently found himself at odds with peers and administrators, notably challenging events such as Mexican heritage celebrations and protests against the Iraq War. His political inclinations were further underscored by a 2003 essay, "How I Changed My Left Wing High School," which chronicled a high school tenure marked by significant political engagement.

During his time at Duke University, Miller, who is Jewish, continued his confrontational approach. He briefly associated with Richard Spencer, who later gained notoriety as a prominent white supremacist. Both were active within the university's Young Conservatives chapter, a period that appeared to foreshadow Miller's enduring ideological commitments.

query: What qualifications and experiences does Merrick Garland possess that make him a suitable nominee for the Supreme Court?

documents.text:
## Merrick Garland: President Obama's Supreme Court Nominee

Following the unexpected death of Justice Antonin Scalia, President Barack Obama faced a critical decision regarding his Supreme Court nominee. While some, like Senator Orrin Hatch (R-UT), a prominent member of the Senate Judiciary Committee, suggested President Obama might avoid a contentious nomination battle by not appointing a liberal justice, reports indicated a different path. Multiple sources revealed President Obama intended to nominate Merrick Garland, the chief judge of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit, to fill the vacancy.

Garland's qualifications for the Supreme Court are substantial, marked by a distinguished career in law. Key aspects of his background include:

*   **Extensive Judicial Experience:** Nineteen years of service on the DC Circuit, a court often considered the nation's second most influential.
*   **Academic Excellence:** Graduated with high honors from Harvard Law School.
*   **Clerkship:** Served as a clerk for Justice William Brennan.
*   **Private Practice:** A tenure as a partner at the multinational law firm Arnold and Porter.
*   **Government Service:** Significant roles within the Department of Justice, including leadership positions in the criminal division and as Principal Associate Deputy Attorney General.

At 63, Garland is the oldest nominee to the Supreme Court since President Nixon appointed Justice Lewis Powell in 1971. This means that, if confirmed, Garland is unlikely to serve as long as Justice Scalia, who had nearly 30 years on the bench. Garland's age may have contributed to Senator Hatch's earlier positive remarks about him as a potential nominee.

Furthermore, Garland is known for his reputation for moderation. An example of this can be seen in a 2003 opinion he joined, which held that the federal judiciary could not assert habeas corpus jurisdiction for aliens held at a U.S. military base leased from another nation. This decision effectively barred Guantanamo Bay detainees from seeking relief in civilian courts. The Supreme Court later reversed this decision in *Rasul v. Bush*. However, legal experts remain divided on whether Garland's initial stance was dictated by existing legal precedents.

Garland also possesses a relatively conservative record on criminal justice matters. A 2010 analysis by Tom Goldstein of SCOTUSBlog found that Judge Garland "rarely votes in favor of criminal defendants’ appeals of their convictions." Goldstein identified only eight such published rulings during Garland's thirteen years on the DC Circuit, alongside seven instances where he voted to alter a defendant's sentence or allow for further sentencing arguments.

It is important to note that Garland's record does not suggest he would align with the Court's conservative wing if confirmed. He would likely vote with the liberal justices more often than not, though he might occasionally cast a dissenting vote. As Goldstein observed in 2010, when Garland was considered for the seat vacated by Justice John Paul Stevens, "to the extent that the President's goal is to select a nominee who will articulate a broad progressive vision for the law, Judge Garland would be a very unlikely candidate to take up that role."

Ultimately, the nomination of Merrick Garland appears to be a strategic move by President Obama to challenge Senate Republicans who have vowed to block any nominee from his administration. However, the effectiveness of this strategy is uncertain, especially given Senator Hatch's initial praise and subsequent refusal to meet with any of President Obama's potential nominees for Scalia's seat, a stance echoed by many within the GOP caucus.

When considering potential nominees for the Supreme Court, media reports have identified a shortlist of five individuals: Ketanji Brown Jackson, Merrick Garland, Jane Kelly, Sri Srinivasan, and Paul Watford. This analysis proceeds with the assumption that this list accurately reflects the President's considerations. The ultimate decision may hinge on the President's assessment of Judge Ketanji Brown Jackson's qualifications, specifically whether her experience on a district court and the U.S. Sentencing Commission is deemed sufficient for the Supreme Court; if so, she emerges as the likely choice. Alternatively, Judge Sri Srinivasan presents a strong contender. In a scenario where Judge Brown Jackson is not selected for the Supreme Court, the administration might simultaneously nominate her to fill Judge Srinivasan's current seat on the D.C. Circuit, strategically positioning her for a future Supreme Court appointment.

Four primary criteria are expected to guide this significant decision, ranked in order of importance: **Objective Qualifications**, encompassing the nominee's current position, tenure, prior roles, and educational background; **Legacy**, considering the nominee's potential lasting impact on the Court's jurisprudence, the significance of being a "first," and the likely duration of their service; **Politics**, involving the electoral benefits of the nomination for the Democratic candidate, the ability to frame Republican opposition, and the potential to drive voter turnout; and **Confirmability**, a factor that, while typically crucial, holds less weight in this specific context due to the current Senate composition. The fact that confirmability is the least important factor in this unique circumstance—given Republican control of the Senate and their stated intention to block any nominee—significantly influences this analysis, placing the nomination at the center of the presidential race. While the Senate majority possesses the power to reject any nominee, the public's potential to hold them accountable for such actions provides a crucial check, making the selection of a nominee with electoral considerations in mind as legitimate as the Republicans' decision to withhold consideration.

### Ranking the Nominees by Key Factors:

**Objective Qualifications (Ranked):** Merrick Garland, as a long-serving appellate judge, chief judge of the D.C. Circuit, and former senior DOJ official, stands out distinctly with unparalleled qualifications. Sri Srinivasan, an appellate judge and former DOJ official, follows, with Paul Watford and Jane Kelly, both appellate judges, ranking next. Ketanji Brown Jackson, a district court judge and former vice chairman of the U.S. Sentencing Commission, rounds out this category.

**Legacy (Ranked):** Ketanji Brown Jackson holds the potential to be the first African-American woman and likely the most liberal justice, positioning her at the forefront of legacy considerations. Sri Srinivasan could be the first Asian American justice, followed by Jane Kelly, who would be the Court's fourth female member. Paul Watford would be the third African-American Justice and an ideological counterpoint to Justice Thomas, while Merrick Garland, as a white man and the oldest candidate, ranks last in this category.

**Politics (Ranked):** Ketanji Brown Jackson, an African-American woman with two unanimous confirmations, offers significant political advantages, particularly for Hillary Clinton. Jane Kelly, a woman with a unanimous confirmation, and Sri Srinivasan, an Asian American with a unanimous confirmation, follow. Paul Watford, an African American, and Merrick Garland, a white man, rank lower in this political calculus.

**Confirmability (Ranked):** Merrick Garland is widely seen as a universally acceptable candidate, making him the most confirmable. Sri Srinivasan, regarded as a moderate Democrat, is next, followed by Jane Kelly, who had strong support from the Republican chairman of the Judiciary Committee in the past. Ketanji Brown Jackson is considered difficult to oppose given her race and gender, while Paul Watford is ranked lower due to his less prominent public profile in this context.

**Advocacy Among Washington Elites (Ranked):** Sri Srinivasan is highly regarded in Democratic legal circles as the best candidate, superior to Garland due to age. Merrick Garland is deeply respected, though not among the most liberal potential justices. Ketanji Brown Jackson and Paul Watford are known to D.C. lawyers, with Jane Kelly best known for support from Senator Grassley, which is less relevant now.

**Support Among Advocacy Groups (Ranked):** Ketanji Brown Jackson is the choice of most civil rights groups, followed by Jane Kelly, who would have strong support from women's groups. Sri Srinivasan is advocated by Asian-American organizations, and Paul Watford would be supported by civil rights groups, with Merrick Garland ranking lower in this specific context.

While it is not a simple mathematical equation and the President's weighting of these factors may differ, Brown Jackson and Srinivasan emerge as distinct from the other three candidates. Merrick Garland would statistically be the third most likely nominee, but his age may prove decisive. Ultimately, if the President does not view Judge Brown Jackson's lack of appellate court experience as a disqualifier, she is the most probable choice, given her slight edge in this analysis. It is also worth noting the legacy of Sri Srinivasan's D.C. Circuit nomination, which faced objections from core Democratic constituencies, particularly labor groups, due to his past work at a D.C. corporate law firm; while these concerns were unfounded, they were never fully resolved. If, as reliably reported by individuals involved in prior nominations, the President will not appoint a district judge, Srinivasan becomes the likely nominee. In such a scenario, nominating Judge Brown Jackson to the D.C. Circuit simultaneously could offer some of the benefits of her appointment and position her for a future Supreme Court bid.

President Obama has put forth a significant nomination to fill the 113th Supreme Court vacancy: Merrick B. Garland. This choice carries the profound potential to shape the Court's trajectory for years to come, a decision that has already sparked considerable debate. Judge Garland, a respected 63-year-old jurist, has cultivated a strong reputation within legal circles, earning commendation from a broad spectrum of political viewpoints, a testament to his widely acknowledged legal acumen. His selection is specifically intended to occupy the esteemed seat left vacant by the passing of Justice Antonin Scalia, a pivotal moment for the nation's highest court.

The nomination of Merrick B. Garland is poised to ignite a contentious confirmation process, as his perceived centrist judicial philosophy directly challenges the stated intentions of Republican senators who had previously vowed to obstruct any nominee presented before the upcoming election. This strategic move by President Obama places these senators in a precarious position, potentially compelling them to re-evaluate their earlier commitment to blocking a confirmation. The anticipated confrontation is expected to be a protracted affair, potentially spanning several months, with an uncertain resolution that will undoubtedly influence the Court's ideological balance. In anticipation of this pivotal struggle, numerous well-funded organizations, including the American Bar Association and the Judicial Crisis Network, have already commenced their strategic activities, signaling the high stakes involved.

The core details of this consequential nomination are as follows: The nominee is Merrick B. Garland, a centrist appellate judge with a distinguished career. The seat in question is the 113th position on the Supreme Court, a vacancy created by the untimely death of Justice Antonin Scalia. Judge Garland's background highlights his extensive experience and broad respect, having received praise from members of both major political parties, a rare feat in today's polarized climate.

Senate Democrats are employing a new strategy to link the Supreme Court vacancy to the ongoing debate over abortion. Led by prominent female senators, including Washington's Patty Murray, the party aims to frame the Republican refusal to consider a nominee as part of a broader "war on women." This tactic is designed to energize women voters and highlight what Democrats perceive as a Republican agenda to restrict reproductive healthcare.

The core of the Democratic message, articulated by Senator Murray and others, is that while Senate Republicans are delaying their constitutional duty to fill the Supreme Court vacancy, they are simultaneously prioritizing hearings on sensitive issues like late-term abortions. Senator Murray plans to state:

*   "While they say they won’t even hold a hearing on a Supreme Court nominee to fulfill their constitutional responsibilities — they were eager to hold a hearing to attack women’s constitutional rights."
*   "They love to talk about the Constitution — unless we’re talking about a woman’s constitutional right to make decisions about her own body, or the part that lays out the Senate’s responsibilities when it comes to filling Supreme Court vacancies."

This approach seeks to draw a stark contrast between the Senate's inaction on a Supreme Court nominee and its swift action on a hearing concerning late-term abortions.

The Republican response, however, sought to counter this narrative. A spokesperson for Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell pointed to recent bipartisan legislative achievements, such as the confirmation of a new education secretary and the passage of education legislation, to argue that the Senate is indeed fulfilling its duties. This suggests that the Republican leadership views the Democrats' strategy as a politically motivated attempt to shift focus.

The Judiciary Committee hearing, titled "Late-Term Abortion: Protecting Babies Born Alive and Capable of Feeling Pain," is central to this debate. This hearing is linked to legislation, championed by Senator Lindsey Graham, that proposes a ban on abortions after 20 weeks of pregnancy. This bill previously faced a Democratic filibuster.

Organizations like Planned Parenthood have criticized the Republican leadership, accusing them of prioritizing "abortion politics" over their constitutional obligations. They argue that the focus on this legislation distracts from the urgent need to fill the Supreme Court vacancy. Conversely, anti-abortion groups, such as SBA List, have defended the hearings, suggesting that highlighting support for late-term abortions could be politically disadvantageous for Democrats. They also emphasize the importance of the Supreme Court's role in abortion-related cases, especially with the vacancy and the potential for a "pro-abortion" justice to be appointed.

President Barack Obama has nominated Merrick Garland to the Supreme Court, a move that directly addresses the nation's concerns about the court's ideological balance, particularly following the passing of Justice Antonin Scalia. Garland, widely regarded as a judicial moderate, currently serves as the chief judge of the influential D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals. His nomination is set to become a focal point of the intense partisan debate surrounding the future composition of the highest court in the land.

Garland's journey to this pivotal nomination includes several key stages:

*   **Early Life and Education:** Born and raised in the Chicago suburbs, Garland pursued his undergraduate and law degrees at Harvard College, graduating in 1977.
*   **Judicial Apprenticeship:** His legal foundation was significantly shaped by prestigious clerkships with Judge Henry Friendly of the Second Circuit Court of Appeals and Supreme Court Justice William Brennan, both appointed by President Dwight D. Eisenhower.
*   **Public Service Career:** The majority of Garland's professional life has been dedicated to public service. Before his judicial appointments, he was a partner at the law firm Arnold & Porter. His government roles included:
    *   Special Assistant to the U.S. Attorney General
    *   Assistant U.S. Attorney for D.C.
    *   Deputy Assistant Attorney General
*   **Key Prosecutorial Roles:** As Principal Associate Deputy Attorney General, Garland played a crucial role in overseeing the prosecution of major cases, including the Oklahoma City bombing and the investigation into Theodore Kaczynski, the "Unabomber."
*   **D.C. Circuit Tenure:** President Bill Clinton nominated Garland to the D.C. Circuit in 1997. He has served on this court for nearly two decades, rising to the position of chief judge in 2013.

The path to confirmation for Garland faces considerable political hurdles. Senate Republican leaders have declared their intention to block any hearings for Obama's nominee, arguing the decision should be deferred to the next president. However, Garland's established reputation as a fair-minded and moderate jurist may temper opposition from Republican senators. His extensive background in law enforcement, including his involvement in prosecuting high-profile capital punishment cases, could also serve as a shield against partisan attacks. Conversely, his Supreme Court clerkship under the liberal Justice Brennan and his D.C. Circuit appointment by President Clinton could be leveraged by Senate Republicans.

At 63, Garland would be among the older associate justices appointed to the Supreme Court. This age factor might be seen as somewhat unexpected, as presidents often prefer younger nominees for longevity. Reports indicate that Garland was previously considered for Supreme Court vacancies that were ultimately filled by Justices Sonia Sotomayor and Elena Kagan.

Garland and his wife, Lynn, married in 1987 and have two daughters. According to The New York Times, his personal interests include tennis and skiing.

President Obama faces a significant decision in nominating Merrick Garland for a Supreme Court vacancy, a choice that involves a complex interplay of qualifications, political realities, and symbolic representation. This document explores why Garland is a prominent contender, examining his extensive legal background, judicial philosophy, and the potential obstacles he might encounter during a contentious confirmation process.

### A Deeply Qualified Judicial Candidate

Merrick Garland's professional history presents a compelling case for his judicial suitability. A **magna cum laude** graduate of Harvard Law School, his early career included a distinguished clerkship with Justice William J. Brennan Jr., a jurist renowned for his contributions to liberal jurisprudence on the Supreme Court. Following his clerkship, Garland refined his legal acumen as a partner at a prominent Washington law firm before embarking on a notable career within the Justice Department. There, he played a crucial role in overseeing two of the most significant domestic terrorism cases in U.S. history, underscoring his ability to manage intricate and high-stakes investigations. Currently serving as the chief judge of the D.C. federal appeals court, Garland is widely considered a leading candidate for President Obama's Supreme Court nomination, according to individuals familiar with the selection process. One source close to the deliberations described Garland as being "in his own league" and "pretty unassailable from a reputational standpoint."

### Navigating Political Hurdles

However, the path to Supreme Court confirmation is seldom smooth, and Garland's candidacy is subject to several political considerations that could impede his progress. Legal experts and those who have spoken with administration officials suggest that Garland's **two-decade track record as a judge** might become a point of contention. Should Republicans scrutinize his judicial decisions, they may uncover material that could be leveraged against him in a fierce confirmation battle. This contrasts with many other potential nominees, who are younger and have shorter judicial histories, potentially offering fewer avenues for opposition.

### Demographic Considerations and Symbolic Weight

Furthermore, Garland's demographic profile—a white male—comes into play during a period when Democrats may seek a nominee who can galvanize minority support within the party's base, particularly in a presidential election year. Civil rights organizations, for instance, are actively advocating for President Obama to nominate the **first black woman** to the Supreme Court. Barbara R. Arnwine, president of the Transformative Justice Coalition, while acknowledging Garland's "incredible qualifications and an impressive background and résumé," believes that several equally qualified female black judges exist. She posits that nominating Garland would "send a message that the status quo of American privilege is still in place." Given the Senate Republicans' stated intention to block any nominee, the president's choice could carry significant political symbolism. One legal expert, who closely follows Supreme Court matters and spoke anonymously out of respect for Garland, suggested that with confirmation appearing unlikely, the president might opt for a nominee who can energize key constituencies.

### A History of Consideration

Merrick Garland has been a finalist for Supreme Court vacancies twice before, during the selection processes that ultimately led to the appointments of Justices Sonia Sotomayor and Elena Kagan. Despite not being chosen previously, those who know him indicate he would be honored by the consideration. Jamie Gorelick, a former deputy attorney general who worked with Garland at the Justice Department, described him as "supremely qualified" for the high court and noted that an appointment "requires lightning to strike." Gorelick specifically lauded Garland's leadership in supervising the investigations into the Oklahoma City bombing and the Unabomber cases, highlighting his ability to lead complex, cross-departmental initiatives. She characterized Garland as a "brilliant lawyer and judge" known for his collegiality, even with those holding differing ideological views.

### Past Confirmation and Bipartisan Support

Merrick Garland was nominated to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit by President Bill Clinton in **1997** and was confirmed by a **76-to-32 vote**, a tally that included support from **32 Republicans**. In **2010**, when Garland was considered for the vacancy that went to Justice Sotomayor, Senator Orrin G. Hatch (R-Utah), a significant figure in judicial selection, stated that he had known Garland for years and believed him to be a "consensus nominee," asserting there was "no question" he could be confirmed.

### A Pool of Potential Nominees
