President Obama had previously asserted that Mitt Romney was experiencing "Romnesia," portraying him as an inconsistent candidate unable to recall his past conservative policy stances.

Stephanie Cutter defended the Obama campaign's references to "binders of women" and Big Bird, emphasizing that these were phrases Romney himself had used. "We are not the ones that brought up Big Bird," Cutter explained. "Big Bird is important because that’s the only thing Mitt Romney could point to as to how he’s going to reduce the deficit." She highlighted that deficit reduction is a critical issue in the campaign, and President Obama has presented a comprehensive plan to address it. Cutter described "Romnesia" as a lighthearted term to describe Romney's shifting campaign strategy. "He’s run as the ideal tea party candidate, severely conservative…and in the last two weeks of the campaign he’s running to the middle," she said.

Madden countered by accusing the Obama campaign of pettiness. "I don't think the message to voters right now ought to be playing Scrabble with your opponent's name when you have 23 million people struggling to find work," he remarked.

Further insights into this exchange can be found in discussions on 'Face the Nation,' featuring Stephanie Cutter and Kevin Madden, and analyses of the campaign's use of figures like Big Bird.

Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich on Sunday had some choice words for President Barack Obama, who said in a debate last week that he finds it "offensive" that Republicans are questioning his and his administration's veracity on Libya. The Georgia Republican, who ran unsuccessfully for president, referred to the Iranian hostage crisis, which lasted 444 days when Ronald Reagan was seeking to unseat President Jimmy Carter in 1980. “Should Ronald Reagan not have talked about it for 444 days?" Gingrich said on CNN's "State of the Union." "The fact is, we're in the middle of a mess in the Middle East, the mess keeps evolving, there continue to be incidents." He noted that the Obama administration failed to adequately protect U.S. Ambassador Chris Stevens and three others who were killed in Benghazi, despite requests for more security at the U.S. Consulate there. He said the Obama administration must answer for its failings. "If that offends the president, then that’s his problem and he ought to get over it," Gingrich said. Read more about: Barack Obama, Newt Gingrich, Libya

Gallup Editor in Chief Frank Newport vigorously defended the polling company's methodology on Sunday, emphasizing its "extremely solid" foundation in the face of criticism from the Obama campaign. This defense emerged as Gallup's latest seven-day tracking poll revealed Mitt Romney leading Barack Obama by a significant margin of 51 percent to 45 percent among likely voters. This particular finding stood in contrast to numerous other polls that depicted a much closer, dead-even race between the two candidates.

Appearing on "Fox News Sunday," Newport directly addressed host Chris Wallace's inquiry about the Obama campaign's claim that Gallup's polling was "way out of line." Newport explained that such strong reactions are a recurring phenomenon, a pattern he traced back to the company's founder, Dr. George Gallup. He shared his personal experience, noting that across his six election cycles, beginning in 1992, he has consistently observed "heated commentary from either side on polls." Newport further elaborated on this observation, stating, "People come at you from either side if they don't like the results."

Newport reaffirmed his unwavering confidence in Gallup's established approach to polling. He asserted, "Our methodology is extremely solid." He also highlighted the company's commitment to transparency, saying, "We’re very open about how we do it." Newport concluded his remarks on this point by stating, "We’re doing great but it’s not unusual for people to bite back at you who don’t your findings."

In addition to these points, Newport provided a crucial clarification regarding Gallup's polling practices concerning party affiliation. He unequivocally stated that Gallup has never applied weighting to its results based on party affiliation. "We do not weight by party at all. We never have and we don’t now," he affirmed with certainty. Newport articulated his belief that party identification is an attitudinal variable, meaning it is susceptible to shifts and changes, much like an individual's voting intentions. This perspective helps explain why Gallup does not rely on fixed party weighting in its analysis.

Sen. Dick Durbin (D.-Ill) on Sunday joined the chorus of Democrats criticizing House Oversight Committee Chairman Darrell Issa (R-Calif.) for releasing documents related to the attacks in Libya on the committee’s website. Appearing on "Fox News Sunday," the Senate majority whip said the administration was continuing to “engage in a comprehensive investigation of what actually occurred” on Sept. 11 attack on the U.S. Consulate in Benghazi that killed Ambassador Chris Stevens and Republicans are rushing to politicize the event. On Friday, Issa posted 166 pages of State Department communications related to the attack on his website, without redacted the names of several Libyans who are working with the U.S. “Lets gather the evidence, let’s make sure we understand exactly what did occur. But jumping to conclusions, I mean Darrell Issa does a document dump on his website with sensitive information about those in Libya who are helping keep America safe, I mean it shows the lengths many will go to politicize this tragic situation," Durbin said. Read more about: Dick Durbin, Darrell Issa, Libya

David Axelrod, a key strategist for President Obama's re-election efforts, voiced significant doubts about recent polling figures, specifically a recent NBC/WSJ survey that showed a national deadlock between President Obama and Mitt Romney, with both candidates at 47%. Axelrod expressed his dissatisfaction with the pollsters, noting what he perceived as contradictions within their recent data collection.

He emphasized that these same pollsters had previously reported substantial leads for the Obama campaign in critical swing states. For instance, they had indicated an 8-point lead in Iowa, along with advantages in Ohio and Florida. Axelrod questioned the validity of these conflicting outcomes, stating during an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press," "I don’t know how to square all the polling that NBC is releasing."

Notwithstanding these reservations about particular surveys, Axelrod reaffirmed the campaign's ongoing narrative that the election would be intensely contested. He conveyed assurance in the campaign's present position, declaring, "We feel good about where we are. We feel we’re even or ahead in these battleground states."

Axelrod proposed that early voting statistics from these pivotal states offered a more dependable measure of voter sentiment. He characterized the early voting participation as "very robust, and it's very favorable to us," contending that it provided a better assessment of the race's momentum than public polls, which he described as "all over the map."

The NBC/WSJ poll that drew Axelrod's attention, released on a Sunday, was the first instance this year where Romney had achieved parity with President Obama among likely voters across the country. This contrasted with earlier polls from the same period, which had shown Obama holding a 3-point lead in late September and a 5-point lead earlier in that month.

query: What is the primary goal of the Internet Archive's 'No More 404' initiative as discussed in the article?

documents.text: These crawls are part of an effort to archive pages as they are created and archive the pages that they refer to. That way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved.Then the Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.The goal is to fix all broken links on the web . Crawls of supported "No More 404" sites.

Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT), a company dedicated to realizing Elon Musk's vision of a 700 mph maglev train, has recently shared exciting new information about its proposed levitation technology. HTT revealed on Monday that it plans to implement a passive magnetic levitation system. This innovative technology was initially developed by scientists at Lawrence Livermore National Labs in the United States.

This Los Angeles-based company intends to build a groundbreaking system. It will propel passenger-filled pods through a low-pressure tube at speeds that could reach up to 760 mph. Elon Musk, the original inventor, has famously likened the Hyperloop to a combination of the Concorde supersonic jet, a rail gun, and an air hockey table. His ambition is to create a future where travel between Los Angeles and San Francisco takes less than 30 minutes.

The proposed method for levitation and propulsion involves several distinct steps. First, as a pod enters the tunnel, magnetic fields within the tunnel structure will interact with the pod. This interaction will lift the pod off the track, achieving levitation. Following levitation, a specific "thrust force" will be applied. This force will push the pod forward, accelerating it rapidly. The goal is to reach speeds close to the speed of sound, which is approximately 767 mph. To slow the pod down, the same thrust force will be applied in reverse. During this deceleration process, the pod's onboard batteries will be recharged. Hyperloop refers to this energy recovery method as regenerative braking.

Bibop Gresta, the Chief Operating Officer of Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, spoke about the benefits of this approach. He stated that using a passive levitation system means no power stations are needed along the Hyperloop track. This, he explained, makes the system highly suitable for its intended purpose and helps keep construction expenses down. Gresta also pointed out significant safety advantages. He noted that levitation happens solely due to the pod's movement. Therefore, even if there is a power failure, the Hyperloop pods will continue to levitate. They would only touch the ground after reaching very low speeds.

Despite these developments, the Hyperloop project still faces skepticism. Critics have voiced doubts about its practicality. They often cite potential major challenges concerning both the project's cost and its overall safety.

Elon Musk's Hyperloop Dream Has Its 1st Public Demo Enlarge this image toggle caption Hyperloop One Hyperloop One Updated at 3 p.m. ET. Out in the Nevada desert today, the world got a good look at the first public test of the Hyperloop — a concept that could someday become a new mode of transportation. Don't call it a Wright Brothers' "Kitty Hawk" moment just yet, though. The demo focused on only one piece of a very complicated system. The Hyperloop, envisioned by Tesla and SpaceX founder Elon Musk, would move passenger-filled pods through special tubes at incredibly high speeds — as in possibly crossing the 400 miles between San Francisco and Los Angeles in a mere 30 minutes. Musk offered up his ideas in 2013, and others got to work trying to build it. One of the startups that jumped at the challenge is Hyperloop One, formerly Hyperloop Technologies, tested its propulsion mechanism today. Take a look, via CNBC: The Wall Street Journal wrote earlier in the day: "The focus of the test will be more on the propulsion technology — whether it can actually move the sled — than the speed. The track is shorter and there will be air resistance, two slowing factors which will be eliminated in future tests." On Tuesday, Hyperloop One announced new funding and new partners in transportation and engineering. One of those partners is Danish architect Bjarke Ingels. Gizmodo says that Ingels, known for big public infrastructure projects, "will give some much-needed design direction for how Hyperloop One's projects might start to integrate with the cities they're meant to serve." Gizmodo also has this statement from the architect: "With hyperloop we are not only designing a futuristic station or a very fast train, we are dealing with an entirely novel technology with the potential to completely transform how our existing cities will grow and evolve, and how new cities will be conceived and constructed." Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd said in a statement that the company would work with these new partners "to redefine the future of transportation, providing a more immediate, safe, efficient and sustainable high-speed backbone for the movement of people and things." toggle caption Joshua Caldwell of Cryptic Butterfly Photography/Hyperloop One So will any of this actually work? NPR's Elise Hu asked the question when Musk announced his idea almost three years ago. Electrical engineer Marc Thompson told her it seemed that it would. But that's not the end of the story, said the Worcester Polytechnic professor: "The devil's in the details in terms of testing, safety, passenger safety, egress, vibration, all that engineering stuff." Former U.S. Department of Transportation Assistant Secretary Emil Frankel also had reservations, which he shared with Elise at the time. "To talk about these kinds of leapfrogging technology in a context when we can't really adequately maintain our existing infrastructure is really not terribly realistic. At least not in terms of the public policy debate," Frankel said. Hyperloop One and competitor Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, or HTT, don't seem deterred. HTT is making progress, too, Wired notes: "We think we will be able to demonstrate full Kitty Hawk capabilities by the end of this year."

The future of transportation is on the cusp of a seismic shift, driven by an intense race to perfect the Hyperloop system. This revolutionary concept, first publicly detailed by Elon Musk's SpaceX, promises to redefine travel by propelling passengers and cargo at unprecedented speeds, potentially exceeding 750 miles per hour, within sealed, low-pressure tubes.

### Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT) Achieves Key Milestone

A significant development has been announced by Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT), a consortium comprised of seasoned professionals hailing from prestigious organizations like NASA and Boeing. HTT has successfully licensed passive magnetic levitation technology, a critical component for their prototype. This technological leap is fundamental to enabling a system that could drastically reduce travel times. Consider the possibility of traversing the distance between Los Angeles and San Francisco, a journey that currently demands six hours by car or an entire day by train, in a mere 30 minutes. HTT is set to unveil its functional prototype in the Nevada desert this week, marking a tangible step towards realizing this ambitious vision.

### The Competitive Landscape: HTI and skyTran

This pivotal announcement from HTT comes just days before a prominent competitor, Hyperloop Technologies Inc. (HTI), is scheduled to present its own technological advancements to investors and the media. HTI will be showcasing its progress at the same desert location. Both HTI and HTT, headquartered in Los Angeles, are actively pushing forward with their distinct approaches to Hyperloop design, underscoring the competitive fervor in this emerging sector.

Adding another dimension to this rapidly evolving field is skyTran, an innovative company based at NASA Research Park. skyTran has already demonstrated its unique technology, which involves compact, two- and four-person vehicles. The company has ambitious plans to construct a 30-mile track in Lagos, Nigeria, and has secured substantial funding totaling $30 million, while maintaining a close collaborative relationship with NASA.

### The Promise and Perils of Hyperloop

The primary appeal of Hyperloop technology lies in its potential to offer ultra-fast, environmentally sustainable travel, entirely independent of fossil fuels. However, significant hurdles remain. Ensuring the system's unwavering reliability and meticulously addressing the inherent safety concerns associated with near-supersonic speeds are paramount. While foundational principles such as magnetic levitation (maglev) are already integrated into existing high-speed rail networks, the Hyperloop faces considerable challenges in terms of extensive infrastructure development, navigating complex regulatory approvals, and securing massive financial investment. For instance, a proposed Hyperloop connection between Los Angeles and San Francisco has been estimated to incur costs exceeding $6 billion, highlighting the scale of the financial undertaking.

### Key Players and Their Distinct Approaches

A closer examination reveals the unique strategies and contributions of the leading entities in this groundbreaking field:

*   **Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT):** This company is leveraging passive magnetic levitation, a concept with roots in the pioneering work of physicist Richard Post. Their system is engineered to optimize cost-effectiveness by integrating magnets directly onto the pods. These magnets interact with coils embedded within the guideway tubes, generating the necessary lift and propulsion. HTT benefits from financial backing and the profound expertise of its team, which includes veterans from the space technology sector. Previously, HTT announced plans to integrate its technology into a solar-powered city in Quay Valley, California, and has engaged in discussions with the Slovakian government regarding the potential development of a Hyperloop route.

*   **Hyperloop Technologies Inc. (HTI):** Co-founded by Shervin Pishevar, an early investor in Uber, and Brogan BamBrogan, a former SpaceX engineer, HTI has successfully raised $37 million in funding. The company is diligently advancing its Hyperloop technology and is on the verge of presenting its latest breakthroughs to the public and potential investors.

*   **skyTran:** Operating in close partnership with NASA, skyTran is developing personal rapid transit pods. They have an active project underway in Lagos, Nigeria, with the goal of implementing their innovative transportation system.

*   **SpaceX:** Elon Musk's pioneering company, which brought the Hyperloop concept into mainstream public discourse, has expressed its intention to potentially construct a test track, likely situated in Texas. Notably, a team from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) emerged as the victor in an inaugural SpaceX-sponsored Hyperloop competition, presenting a design that mirrored HTT's approach by utilizing passive magnets on the pods.

The ongoing development of Hyperloop technology represents a multifaceted and complex endeavor. It necessitates a delicate equilibrium between fostering pioneering innovation and confronting the practical realities of ensuring safety, achieving cost-effectiveness, and establishing the robust infrastructure required for widespread adoption.

Hyperloop One's recent propulsion system test in the Nevada desert marks a significant, albeit early, step towards revolutionizing future transportation. This landmark event, held at their 39.9-acre facility, saw the Los Angeles-based company successfully demonstrate its linear induction propulsion technology. A select group of journalists and supporters witnessed a sled, powered by electromagnetic stators, accelerate to approximately 115 mph. While the initial acceleration reached 2.4 Gs, the test concluded with the sled engaging a sandpit for deceleration.

This initial demonstration, though not reaching the ultimate speeds envisioned for the Hyperloop system, represents a crucial milestone. Unlike the future iterations that will utilize permanent magnets for levitation, this test sled made contact with the metal rails, a factor that likely influenced its speed. Hyperloop One co-founder and CTO Brogan BamBrogan emphasized the significance of this achievement, stating, "This is the first step in a long journey."

The electromagnetic propulsion system is a foundational element of the comprehensive transportation network that Hyperloop One, formerly known as Hyperloop Technologies, aims to deploy. The concept of Hyperloop itself originated with entrepreneur Elon Musk, who envisioned sealed pods traveling at near-supersonic speeds within low-pressure tubes, elevated on pylons. Musk's 2014 white paper and subsequent open-sourcing of the concept ignited a competitive race among various entities, including rival Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT).

Hyperloop's potential extends to creating a low-energy, solar-powered, and low-emission mass transit solution. Experts suggest it could alleviate congestion on major travel corridors, such as the routes between New York and Washington D.C., and Los Angeles and San Francisco, potentially reducing travel times to as little as 35 minutes for the latter. Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd expressed optimism, projecting cargo transport by 2019 and passenger services by 2021, contingent on continued governmental cooperation, similar to the support received in Nevada. BamBrogan confirmed that further testing is the immediate priority for Hyperloop One.

The primary obstacle to realizing Elon Musk's Hyperloop vision, beyond the substantial cost of constructing the "tube" itself, may lie not in engineering, but in human physiology. While Hyperloop Transportation Technologies, a key player in this ambitious endeavor, has highlighted the tube as the major expense, a functional, passenger-carrying system faces a more fundamental challenge: passenger comfort. The current pitch for Hyperloop, a 750 mph pneumatic tube promising a 35-minute commute from San Francisco to Los Angeles for Tesla owners, largely overlooks the impact of such extreme speeds on the human body.

Commuters prioritize comfort alongside speed. The allure of a 750 mph journey, as envisioned by Musk, fails to account for the inevitable accelerations and decelerations that will occur. Unlike a constant-speed journey in a vacuum, a real-world Hyperloop will involve frequent stops, especially if it's intended to replace existing rail infrastructure. Furthermore, the varied terrain of the United States, with its inclines and declines, will necessitate significant changes in speed and direction. High-speed trains in countries like China and Japan, typically topping out around 200 mph, already experience passenger discomfort during acceleration, deceleration, and turns.

The human body can tolerate approximately 0.2 Gs of acceleration vertically or laterally. Hyperloop's projected speeds could generate forces up to seven times greater than those experienced on Japan's Shinkansen bullet train, according to some analyses. These G-forces, acting in multiple directions as the pod navigates turns and terrain, can cause significant disorientation and nausea. The gastrointestinal tract's response to these forces can lead to motion sickness, a problem that would be exacerbated by frequent stops and turns.

While the Hyperloop Pod Competition, sponsored by SpaceX, mandates prototypes capable of 240 mph, and teams like MIT are developing even faster designs, the fundamental issue of human tolerance remains. If speeds prove too uncomfortable, the system will inevitably have to slow down. It's crucial to remember that human physiology is relative; what one person finds tolerable, another may not. However, the inherent nature of Hyperloop's design, with its potential for sharp turns and rapid speed changes, poses a universal challenge to passenger comfort. As this intriguing technology progresses, a healthy dose of skepticism is warranted until actual human passenger reactions to speeds approaching nine-tenths the speed of sound are thoroughly understood and addressed.

Browser game tests you to survive Elon Musk’s Hyperloop Excited by the prospect of high-speed travel by riding in a pod through airless tubes in Elon Musk’s concept of the Hyperloop? Want to give it a try now? Well, there’s a 8-bit styled web browser game that has you navigating a pod with the hope of keeping it one piece. Called Break-a-Pod, it’s ridiculously hard in a similar way as Flappy Bird was, but it was actually created by a team that’s building a pod prototype with the hope of bringing it to SpaceX, the company behind Hyperloop. The game has you controlling a pod’s speed and stability, with a goal of “going as fast as you can without crashing or running out of power.” It’s simple, but it’s also much harder than it looks. You only need to use the four arrow keys, with up to accelerate, down to brake, and right/left to keep it stable. Hitting the sides of the tubes too many times or losing control of the pitch sees you instantly explode. The team behind the game, which calls themselves rLoop, after forming on Reddit, explains that they “hope that the game will act as a vehicle to make the Hyperloop concept fun and accessible to a wider audience, and inspire further research and engagement.” Break-a-Pod was also released as a way to bring attention to rLoop’s IndieGoGo campaign, which has a goal of raising $66,000 to finish building their prototype pod. Should they receive enough funds, the game might also see a mobile release on iOS and Android, giving them another source of raising money for the Hyperloop project. SpaceX has already held an open contest for pod designs. Out of hundreds of entrants, the rLoop team was selected as one of 18 finalists, and has been invited to the next stage of the competition. Scheduled to be held in California later this year, the event is supposed to see the prototypes demonstrated, but SpaceX hasn’t yet finished building a section of test track. VIA The Verge

The allure of the Hyperloop, a concept promising 600 mph travel between major cities, has captivated imaginations since Elon Musk's 2013 announcement. While the technical feasibility of this futuristic transportation system has been a frequent topic of discussion, a critical, often overlooked challenge looms large: the immense bureaucratic and land acquisition hurdles inherent in any large-scale infrastructure project. This is a reality that even the most ambitious technological advancements must confront.

In 2015, Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT) took a significant step by filing a permit to construct a 5-mile prototype in Quay Valley, a planned eco-friendly community in Central California. Dirk Ahlborn, CEO of HTT, stated, "After over two and a half years of research and development our team has reached another important milestone. This will be the world’s first passenger-ready Hyperloop system." He further noted, "Everyone traveling on California’s I-5 in 2016 will be able to see our activities from the freeway." This initiative, while a promising development for a test track, does not diminish the fundamental obstacles facing the broader Hyperloop vision.

The excitement surrounding Hyperloop often centers on its innovative technology, with the press dissecting the engineering intricacies. However, the practicalities of connecting cities like San Francisco and Los Angeles are far more complex than the propulsion systems or vacuum tubes. The fundamental issue is securing the rights to the vast swaths of land required for such a route. This involves navigating a labyrinth of legal processes, including land acquisition, potential eminent domain proceedings, and securing easements. Beyond land rights, the project faces a cascade of other bureaucratic requirements. Environmental impact reports, a mandatory component for any significant development, can be lengthy and complex, often leading to delays and increased costs. Furthermore, liability considerations, especially for a novel high-speed transportation system, present another significant hurdle that requires careful legal and regulatory attention.

As Josh Stephens aptly points out, enthusiasm and technological prowess alone cannot overcome these terrestrial challenges. "This combination of enthusiasm and magnetism doesn’t buy farmland. It doesn’t ease eminent domain takings. It doesn’t blast through bedrock or relocate utilities. It doesn’t design station area plans. It doesn’t write [Environmental Impact Reports] or dismiss [California Environmental Quality Act] suits," he argues. Trains, regardless of their propulsion method, are fundamentally bound to the earth. The very same geographical features and existing infrastructure that complicate projects like California's High-Speed Rail—mountains, urban areas, agricultural land, and natural habitats—will equally complicate the Hyperloop.

The tendency for Hyperloop proponents to hint at rapid deployment and seamless travel without adequately addressing these monumental public policy challenges, the necessity of governmental cooperation, and the sheer financial investment required, risks transforming the concept from a practical solution for mass transit into a speculative endeavor. The more these fundamental issues are sidestepped, the less the Hyperloop will resemble a practical innovation and the more it may be perceived as an unrealistic, perhaps even misguided, techno-utopian dream, akin to the ambitious, yet potentially unachievable, vision of Quay Valley itself.

This skepticism is not new. Even before the official announcement in August 2013, concerns were raised about the feasibility of building a Hyperloop between San Francisco and Los Angeles without resorting to extensive underground or underwater construction. Even with such extreme measures, the fundamental need to secure land rights and engage extensively with governmental bodies would remain.

While we await with earnest optimism the progress of Hyperloop Transportation Technologies in building their working prototype in Quay Valley, a community envisioned on undeveloped land, the broader implementation of the Hyperloop remains a significant question mark. Until a Hyperloop company demonstrates a legal and lobbying infrastructure as robust as its technological development team, the prospect of widespread Hyperloop adoption within our lifetime remains a subject of considerable doubt. The initial permit for the Quay Valley prototype was filed in 2015, with the community itself planned for Central California. The initial announcement of the Hyperloop concept by Elon Musk was made in the summer of 2013. The proposed speed for the Hyperloop is 600 miles per hour. Hyperloop Transportation Technologies is distinct from a competing company, also named Hyperloop Technologies. The original publication date of this analysis was January 20, 2016.

query: What were President Trump's thoughts on the intelligence community leaks regarding his campaign's alleged contacts with Russia during the press conference?

documents.text: Minutes after President Donald Trump’s press conference concluded on Thursday afternoon, CNN’s Jake Tapper called it “unhinged�? and “wild.�? Here are nine quotes from the presser that back up that assessment. 1) At the beginning of the press conference, Trump blasted intelligence community leaks about his campaign’s alleged contact with Russia as “fake news.�? A reporter asked Trump to clarify whether he meant the leaks were fake. Here’s the exchange: REPORTER: I just want to get you to clarify this very important point. Can you say definitively that nobody on your campaign had any contacts with the Russians during the campaign? And on the leaks, is it fake news or are these real leaks? TRUMP: Well, the leaks are real. You’re the one that wrote about them and reported them; I mean, the leaks are real. You know what they said, you saw it, and the leaks are absolutely real. The news is fake because so much of the news is fake. So one thing that I felt it was very important to do — and I hope we can correct it. Because there’s nobody I have more respect for — well, maybe a little bit, but the reporters, good reporters. 2) After reporter April Ryan asked Trump about the Congressional Black Caucus, the president asked if she could set up a meeting with them. Ryan, who is black, is the White House correspondent and Washington bureau chief for American Urban Radio Networks in Baltimore. RYAN: When you say the inner cities, are you going — are you going to include the CBC, Mr. President? In your conversations with your — your urban agenda, your inner city agenda, as well as — TRUMP: Am I going to include who? RYAN: Are you going to include the Congressional Black Caucus and the Congressional — TRUMP: Well, I would. I tell you what, do you want to set up the meeting? Do you want to set up the meeting? RYAN: No, no, no. I’m not — TRUMP: Are they friends of yours? RYAN: I'm just a reporter. TRUMP: Well, then set up the meeting. RYAN: I know some of them, but I'm sure they're watching right now. TRUMP: Let's go set up a meeting. I would love to meet with the Black Caucus. I think it's great, the Congressional Black Caucus. I think it's great. 3) Trump also used the question about fake news as an opportunity to criticize the ratings of CNN, saying he no longer watches the network because of its “anger and hatred.�? At one point, CNN’s Jim Acosta began trying to ask a question before Trump told his employer to be “straight.�? TRUMP: I mean, I watch CNN, it’s so much anger and hatred, and just the hatred. I don’t watch it anymore because it’s not very good. ... I think it should be straight. I think it should be — I think it would be frankly more interesting. I know how good everybody’s ratings are right now, but I think that actually — I think that’d actually be better. I don’t watch it any more because it’s very good — he’s saying no. It’s OK, Jim. It’s OK, Jim, you’ll have your chance. But I watch others too. You’re not the only one so don’t feel badly. But I think it should be straight. I think it should be — I think it would be frankly more interesting 4) At another point in the press conference, Trump explained why he wouldn’t explain whether he will retaliate against reports of Russian provocation. I'm not going to tell you anything about what I’m going to do. I'm not going to talk about military stuff. I will not say, “We are going to attack Mosul in four months. We are going to attack in one month. Next week, we are going to attack Mosul.�? In the meantime, Mosul is very, very difficult — you know why? I don't talk about military and certain other things. You were going to be surprised to hear that, by the way, my whole campaign I said that. I don't have to tell you. 5) Trump was also asked whether by attacking “fake news,�? he’d be undermining the press. It’s hard to summarize his answer, which touched on the negativity of the press, the importance of the institution, and whether he’d be a good reporter. REPORTER: When you call it "fake news," you're undermining confidence in our news media. ... TRUMP: No, no. I do that. Here's the thing. Okay. I understand what you're — and you're right about that, except this. See, I know when I should get good and when I should get bad. And sometimes I'll say, "Wow, that's going to be a great story." And I'll get killed ... But I know what's good. I know what's bad. And when they change it and make it really bad, something that should be positive — sometimes something that should be very positive, they'll make okay. They'll even make it negative. So I understand it. So, because I'm there. I know what was said. I know who's saying it. I'm there. So it's very important to me. Look, I want to see an honest press. When I started off today by saying that it's so important to the public to get an honest press. The press — the public doesn't believe you people anymore. Now, maybe I had something to do with that. I don't know. But they don't believe you. If you were straight and really told it like it is, as Howard Cosell used to say, right? 6) At another point, Trump talked about how the press would misrepresent the press conference he was giving. He said that he was not “ranting and raving,�? but that the media coverage would say he had been. (The precise wording of the question in the exchange below is unclear.) I won with news conferences and probably speeches. I certainly didn't win by people listening to you people. That's for sure. But I'm having a good time. Tomorrow, they will say, "Donald Trump rants and raves at the press." I'm not ranting and raving. I'm just telling you. You know, you're dishonest people. But -- but I'm not ranting and raving. I love this. I'm having a good time doing it. But tomorrow, the headlines are going to be, "Donald Trump rants and raves." I'm not ranting and raving. 7) Trump was also asked if “there was anything�? he could reveal to convince the media and public that the leaks over Russia and Flynn amounted to “fake news.�? He began by citing a Rasmussen poll showing his approval rating north of 50 percent, before talking about the dangers of uranium and how Hillary Clinton had a “stupid plastic button�? as part of her Russian reset. By the way, it would be great if we could get along with Russia. Just so you understand that. Tomorrow, you will say, "Donald Trump wants to get along with Russia; this is terrible." It is not terrible. It is good. We had Hillary Clinton try to do a reset. We had Hillary Clinton give Russia 20 percent of the uranium in our country. You know what uranium is, right? It’s this thing called nuclear weapons. And other things. Like lots of things are done with uranium. Including some bad things. But nobody talks about that. I did not do anything for Russia. I've done nothing for Russia. Hillary Clinton gave them 20 percent of our uranium. Hillary Clinton did a reset, remember, with the stupid plastic button that made us all look like a bunch of jerks? 8) Later, Trump was asked if Vladimir Putin was “testing�? him with military provocations. Trump’s response included a warning about how nuclear war “would be like no other�?: If Russia and the United States actually got together and got along — and don't forget, we're a very powerful nuclear country, and so are they. There's no upside. We're a very powerful nuclear country, and so are they. I have been briefed. And I can tell you one thing about a briefing that we're allowed to say because anybody that ever read the most basic book can say it: Nuclear holocaust would be like no other. They're a very powerful nuclear country, and so are we. If we have a good relationship with Russia, believe me, that's a good thing, not a bad thing. 9) In one of his most relaxed moments, Trump ruminated on a career he decided not to pursue. “I'd be a pretty good reporter,�? he said. “Not as good as you.�?

President Donald Trump seemed at varying points exasperated, energized, amicable and aggressive as he sparred with reporters Thursday. | Getty All the things Trump covered at his epic news conference The president went over a lot of ground during his 80-minute impromptu performance. President Donald Trump held court with reporters for 80 minutes Thursday, producing no shortage of news in his first solo press conference as commander in chief. Facing a host of issues and controversies that have swirled around his administration in the four weeks since he inherited the White House, Trump confronted them, seeming at varying points exasperated, energized, amicable and aggressive as he sparred with reporters. Story Continued Below Here are the key moments from the president’s exchanges on a range of topics: OBAMA “To be honest, I inherited a mess,” Trump said, blaming former President Barack Obama for handing off what Trump claimed was “a mess.” “We’ll take care of it, folks,” he added. “We’re going to take care of it all. I just want to let you know, I inherited a mess. Beginning on Day One, our administration went to work to tackle these challenges.” OBAMACARE Trump said Republicans are in the final stage of repealing and replacing Obamacare, setting mid- to early March as a timeline to submit a plan. “We should be submitting the initial plan in March — early March — I would say,” Trump said. “And we have to, as you know, statutorily and for reasons of budget, we have to go first. It’s not like — quite frankly, the tax would be easier, in my opinion. But for statutory reasons and for budgetary reasons we have to submit the health care sooner. So we’ll be submitting health care sometime in early March, mid-March.” RUSSIA Trump said he did not direct former national security adviser Michael Flynn to discuss sanctions with the Russian ambassador. “I fired him because of what he said to Mike Pence. Very simple,” he told reporters. “Mike [Flynn] was doing his job. He was calling countries and his counterparts. So it certainly would have been OK with me if he did it. I would have directed him to do it if I thought he wasn’t doing it. I didn't direct him, but I would have directed him because that’s his job.” There’s no comparison between WikiLeaks and U.S. government leaks, Trump said. “When WikiLeaks, which I had nothing to do with, comes out and happens to give, they’re not giving classified information,” he said. “They’re giving stuff — what was said in an office about Hillary cheating on the debates, which, by the way, nobody mentions.” One case involves “highly classified information,” Trump said, while the other is “John Podesta saying bad things about his boss.” Hillary Clinton’s “stupid plastic button” made Americans look like “a bunch of jerks.” Clinton in 2009 gave the Russian foreign minister a red button as a gift to represent the reset between both nations. The button, however, misspelled the Russian word for “reset” on it, instead having the word for “overcharged.” “Hillary Clinton did a reset — remember — with the stupid plastic button that made us all look like a bunch of jerks. ‘Here, take a look.’ He looked at her like, ‘What the hell is she doing with that cheap plastic button?’” Trump said. “The greatest thing I could do is shoot that ship that’s 30 miles offshore right out of the water,” Trump said, referring to a Russian spy ship spotted off the coast of Connecticut. “Everyone in this country’s gonna say, ‘Oh, it’s so great.’ That’s not great. That’s not great. I would love to be able to get along with Russia.” Not good, not good, not good, the president said as a reporter mentioned the Russian spy ship found on the East Coast of the U.S., North Korea’s recent test-launch of a ballistic missile and a Russian plane that buzzed a U.S. Navy ship before delivering a bizarre response. “When did it happen? It happened when — if you were Putin right now, you would say: ‘Hey, we’re back to the old games with the United States. There’s no way Trump can ever do a deal with us because’ — you have to understand, if I was just brutal on Russia right now — just brutal — you would say, ‘Oh, isn’t that wonderful.’ But I know you well enough. Then you would say: ‘Aww, he was too tough. He shouldn’t have done that.’ “Nuclear holocaust would be like no other,” Trump said. He stressed that it’s OK if the U.S. got along with Russia and said he wants to do the right thing for the American people and, secondarily, the world. “Don’t forget: We’re a very powerful nuclear country and so are they,” he added. “There’s no upside. We’re a very powerful nuclear country and so are they. I’ve been briefed, and I can tell you one thing about a briefing that we’re allowed to say because anybody that ever read the most basic book can say it: Nuclear holocaust would be like no other.” Does anyone believe Clinton would be tougher on Russia, Trump asked incredulously. “Does anybody in this room really believe that?” Trump said, noting Clinton’s attempted reset with Russia as secretary of state. “They say I’m close to Russia. Hillary Clinton gave away 20 percent of the uranium in the United States,” Trump said. “She’s close to Russia. You know what I gave to Russia? You know what I gave? Nothing.” Trump won’t telegraph his moves. “I’m not gonna tell you anything about what response I do. I don’t talk about military response,” Trump said, refusing to publicly state a potential response to the Russian spy ship spotted off the coast of Connecticut or North Korea’s test-launch of a ballistic missile over the weekend. He went on to mock past administrations for announcing their every move. “I don’t want to be one of these guys that say, ‘Yes, here’s what we’re going to do,” Trump said with glee. “I don’t have to do that. I don't have to tell you what I’m gonna do in North Korea. And I don’t have to tell you what I’m gonna do with Iran. You know why? Because they shouldn’t know, and eventually you guys are gonna get tired of asking that.” “Russia was a ruse,” the president charged. Aside from speaking to Russian President Vladimir Putin twice since winning the election, Trump maintained that he has nothing to do with the Kremlin and hasn’t even made a call there in years. “We had a very good talk, especially the second one — lasted for a pretty long period of time,” Trump said of his calls with Putin. “I’m sure you probably get it because it was classified so I’m sure everybody in this room perhaps has it, but we had a very, very good talk. I have nothing to do with Russia. To the best of my knowledge, no person that I deal with does.” DISCRIMINATION No one is less racist or anti-Semitic than Trump, according to the president. “No. 1, I am the least anti-Semitic person that you’ve ever seen in your life,” he told a reporter. Trump, however, drew widespread condemnation last month for omitting the slaughtering of 6 million Jews in his Holocaust Remembrance statement, although Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on Wednesday declared at a joint news conference that “there is no greater supporter of the Jewish people and the Jewish state than President Donald Trump.” “No. 2, racism,” Trump continued. “The least racist person … I hate the charge. I find it repulsive. I hate even the question.” IMMIGRATION Blame Homeland Security Secretary James Kelly for the travel ban executive order rollout, which the president insisted was “very smooth” and “perfect,” actually, despite it causing chaos at airports and sparking massive protests. Trump said a “bad court” made a “bad decision” when it blocked his executive action from being enforced nationwide and added that he would introduce a new order next week. Trump said he wanted to give a month’s heads-up. “But General Kelly, now Secretary Kelly, said if you do that all these people will come in the month — the bad ones. You do agree there are bad people out there right, that not everybody [is] like you?” he asked. “You have some bad people out there, so Kelly said you can’t do that, and he was right. … Now if I would have done it a month, everything would have been perfect. The problem is we would have wasted a lot of time and maybe a lot of lives because a lot of bad people would have come into the country.” “We are gonna deal with DACA with heart,” Trump pledged, softening his position on undocumented immigrants who came to the U.S. as children. Casting what to do with the program as a very difficult decision for a father and grandfather who loves children, Trump conceded that it’s tough for him to follow the law on that issue but argued that while some of the children who come in join gangs and deal drugs, many are “absolutely incredible.” “I have to deal with a lot of politicians — don’t forget — and I have to convince them that what I’m saying is right,” he added. MEDIA The president blasted media from all over the country, accusing outlets from Washington, New York and Los Angeles, specifically, of speaking “not for the people but for the special interests and for those profiting off a very, very obviously broken system.” He maintained that the press is dishonesty, which he added was “out of control.” Trump credited his administration for its “incredible progress” in just one month. “I don't think there's ever been a president elected who in this short period of time has done what we’ve done,” he said. “I turn on the TV, open the newspapers and I see stories of chaos. Chaos. Yet it is the exact opposite. This administration is running like a fine-tuned machine.” The media with hatred, Trump charged. The public, Trump argued, may not be able to discern what’s true when they read reports, but he knows what’s real and what’s fake. “I know when you’re telling the truth or when you’re not,” the president said. “I just see many, many untruthful things. And I’ll tell you what else I see: I see tone. You know the word: tone. The tone is such hatred.” Trump revised his characterization of CNN, which he often derides as fake news. “I’m changing it from fake news, though,” he told CNN’s Jim Acosta, pausing for emphasis. “Very fake news. … And you’re not related to our new — you know, I looked at that name,” Trump said, referencing his newly named labor secretary nominee, Alexander Acosta. “I said: ‘Wait a minute. Is there any relation there, Alex?’ No, I checked it. They said, ‘No, sir.’ I said, ‘Do me a favor and go back and check the family tree.’” “I’d be a pretty good reporter,” the president mused at one point. Trump contended that he knows when he should get a good or bad story but accused the media of often turning “positive” stories into “negative” or “OK” articles. “I understand it. Because I’m there,” Trump said. “I know what was said, who’s saying it. I’m there. So it’s very important to me. Look, I want to see an honest press. … It’s so important to the public to get an honest press. The public doesn’t believe you people anymore. Now maybe I had something to do with that. I don’t know. But they don’t believe you.” The president disputed media reports of a White House in chaos, insisting there’s “zero chaos” in his “fine-tuned machine” of an administration and that “half” of chief of staff Reince Priebus’ job consists of “putting out lies by the press.” “He’s working so hard just putting out fires that are fake fires. I mean, they’re fake,” Trump said. “They’re not true. And isn’t that a shame? Because he’d rather be working on health care. He’d rather be working on tax reform.” Trump called out for a “friendly reporter” after tense exchanges with reporters. “I want to find a friendly reporter. Are you a friendly reporter?” Trump asked. “Watch how friendly he is.” Trump wasn’t a fan of the unfinished question, which delved into an uptick of anti-Semitic threats and how the administration planned to address them. “He said he was going to ask a very simple, easy question, and it’s not,” Trump said. “It’s not. It’s not a simple question, not a fair question. OK, sit down. I understand the rest of your question.” “Now that’s what I call a nice question,” Trump said moments later, praising a UNF News reporter who inquired about first Lady Melania Trump’s decision to open the White House visitor’s office. “Thank you very much.” “Do you want to set up the meeting?” Trump asked April Ryan, a black reporter who covers the White House for American Urban Radio Networks. Ryan asked the president if he would speak to congressional black and Hispanic caucuses about his agenda for urban areas and inner cities. “No, no, no, I’m just a reporter,” she said to Trump, who in turn asked if the lawmakers were friends of hers. “Set up the meeting. Let’s go,” he told Ryan. “Set up a meeting. I would love to meet with the black caucus.” CABINET Trump opened his news conference by announcing his new nominee to head the Labor Department: Alexander Acosta. “He’s a member and has been a member of the National Labor Relations Board and has been through Senate confirmations three times — confirmed, did very, very well,” Trump said. “So Alex, I wished him the best. We just spoke and he’s going to be — I think he’ll be a tremendous secretary of labor.” Trump’s former nominee, Andrew Puzder, withdrew his nomination Wednesday after Senate Republicans informed the White House that Puzder lacked the votes to be confirmed. ISLAMIC STATE Trump has ordered the Pentagon to submit a plan to defeat the Islamic State, he said, noting that Defense Secretary James Mattis is “over there now working very hard.” Trump described the Islamic State as a “small group” that has burgeoned and “celebrates the murder and torture of innocent people in large sections of the world.” “They’ve spread like cancer,” in the president’s words. “ISIS has spread like cancer. Another mess I inherited.” ELECTORAL COLLEGE Trump wasn’t supposed to win, he reminded reporters. “I wasn’t supposed to get 222 [Electoral College votes]. They said, ‘There’s no way to get 222 — 230 is impossible. 270, which you need — that was laughable,” Trump said. “We got 306. Because people came out and voted like they’ve never seen before. So that’s the way it goes. I guess it was the biggest Electoral College win since Ronald Reagan.” Some of Trump’s claims, however, are false. Obama won 332 electoral votes in 2012, and Trump only received 304 votes in the Electoral College due to a couple of faithless electors who voted against him. DRUGS “Drugs are becoming cheaper than candy bars,” Trump said. He announced that he has instructed the homeland security and justice departments “to coordinate on a plan to destroy criminal cartels coming into the United States with drugs.” The U.S. is “becoming a drug-infested nation,” he said, promising to curb the flow of drugs.

Jeffrey Lord, a CNN contributor and vocal supporter of President Donald Trump, offered a contrasting perspective on Trump's Thursday press conference during an appearance on CNN with hosts Jake Tapper and Wolf Blitzer. While many observers characterized the 77-minute event as a "meltdown" and a display of "weapons-grade crazy," Lord presented a significantly different interpretation.

Lord stated, "I think we saw two different press conferences." He elaborated on his viewpoint, explaining, "From my perspective, I thought he was relaxed, he was funny, he was on point. He took the whole issue of the media, and he had a very candid conversation." He further emphasized the president's demeanor, describing him as "candid, very dedicated to the job, very in command." Lord also drew a parallel to past interactions, noting, "This is the kind of conversation that I personally had with him a couple years ago in which he said some version of this same thing that he said today, except he was more specific."

The discussion touched upon President Trump's remarks regarding former national security advisor Gen. Michael Flynn and his repeated assertion that "Russia is fake news," while simultaneously acknowledging, "the leaks are absolutely real, the news is fake."

Wolf Blitzer, referencing Lord's experience in the Reagan White House, questioned the presidential nature of Trump's confrontational style with reporters, asking, "You used to work in the Reagan White House — is that really presidential four weeks into a new term?" Lord responded by suggesting this approach might define Trump's leadership style, stating, "I think this is going to be his presidential style."

Jake Tapper brought up a specific exchange where an NBC reporter challenged President Trump's credibility in labeling the media "fake news" while allegedly making untrue statements. The reporter's question was framed as, "How do you have credibility to call us fake news?" Lord dismissed this as a "simple mistake" and characterized the reporter's inquiry as "nitpicky."

Following this, Lord launched into a critique of CNN's coverage. He recounted an anecdote from the campaign trail: "I had somebody here locally say to me during the campaign, you know, they recognized me, and they came over and they said — and it was a comment about CNN, which I’ll be happy to make on air here, just so you know." He then shared the sentiment expressed to him: "They said if Donald Trump sneezed, CNN would spend four days talking about his damage to the environment." Lord concluded by asserting that this perception, not only of CNN but of the media at large, is precisely what President Trump is addressing.

The full video clip of this segment is available for viewing.

Fox News anchor Shepard Smith directly addressed President Donald Trump's criticisms of the press on Thursday, urging the President to answer legitimate questions from news organizations, particularly concerning inquiries about Russia. During a lengthy press conference, Smith highlighted the President's repeated dismissal of these crucial questions, stating, "Your opposition was hacked and the Russians were responsible for it, and your people were on the phone with Russia on the same day it was happening. Are we fools for asking the questions?" Smith emphasized the paramount importance of these inquiries, asserting, "We are not fools for asking this question, and we demand to know the answer. You owe this to the American people."

Reporters from prominent publications, including **The New York Times** and **The Washington Post**, persistently asked Trump about reported conversations between his campaign aides and Russian officials. President Trump, however, consistently characterized these reports as "fake news," stating, "Russia is fake news. Russia — this is fake news put out by the media." He maintained that he was unaware of any improper communications between his campaign aides and Russian officials. Smith countered this narrative, arguing that journalists are not fools for seeking answers and that "The people deserve an answer to this question at very least." He further elaborated, "Your supporters will support you either way. If your people were on the phone, what were they saying? We have a right to know. That you call us fake news and put us down like children for asking these questions on behalf of the American people is inconsequential."

Smith also vocally defended CNN reporter Jim Acosta, who had pressed Trump for answers on multiple occasions. Acosta and Trump have a documented history of contentious exchanges at press conferences, as noted by **CNN** itself. During Thursday's event, Trump escalated his criticism of CNN, labeling it "very fake news." Smith, in his defense of Acosta, described him as "an accomplished reporter, a guy I've never met, but a good reporter." Jim Acosta later expressed his gratitude for Smith's support via a tweet, as reported by **Variety**. Earlier in the press conference, Acosta had prefaced a question by stating, "Just for the record, we don't hate you. I don't hate you." President Trump responded by suggesting Acosta inquire about CNN President Jeff Zucker's hiring and commented on CNN's current ratings, a point also highlighted by **The Hollywood Reporter**.

The president's pronouncements on the economy often presented a stark contrast. On one hand, he declared, "Jobs have already started to surge." Yet, on the other, he lamented, "Jobs are pouring out of the country." This economic doomsaying directly contradicted a triumphant tweet he posted that morning, where he boasted, "Stock market hits new high with longest winning streak in decades. Great level of confidence and optimism - even before tax plan rollout!"

Amidst this, a cottage industry has emerged, dedicated to diagnosing the president's mental state from afar. While such speculation is inappropriate even for licensed professionals, it's difficult to ignore the impression that President Trump might not be enjoying his current role. He reportedly eyed the presidency for decades, and now that he occupies the White House, he appears deeply unhappy. One might even understand why. His administration has been beleaguered by leaks, stemming from internal rivalries within the West Wing to intelligence officials providing information for news stories that have damaged his administration and led to the resignation of National Security Adviser Michael Flynn. This significant event, though it feels like a distant memory, occurred just this past week.

Furthermore, the president's signature immigration executive order has been blocked by federal courts. The much-touted border wall remains unbuilt, and Mexico has shown no inclination to fund it. Several key Cabinet positions are still vacant. Progress on repealing and replacing the Affordable Care Act has been minimal. He is beginning to grasp the slow pace of political action. Concurrently, congressional Republicans have started to initiate investigations into various questionable actions taken by the Trump administration.

Despite these challenges, President Trump attempted to project an image of stability. "I turn on the TV, open the newspapers and I see stories of chaos. Chaos," he stated. "Yet it is the exact opposite. This administration is running like a fine-tuned machine, despite the fact that I can’t get my cabinet approved." He asserted that, given the circumstances, he had already achieved a great deal. "In each of these actions, I’m keeping my promises to the American people. These are campaign promises," he explained. "Some people are so surprised that we’re having strong borders."

However, his demeanor and words often suggested a different reality. "I’m not ranting and raving," he declared, while seemingly doing just that. Other indicators of frustration are apparent. Instead of spending weekends at the White House, he has frequently traveled to Mar-a-Lago, his Florida resort, a location where he appears to feel more at ease. This Saturday, he is scheduled to hold an event described by his aides as a campaign rally, effectively launching his 2020 reelection bid. These excursions serve as opportunities to depart Washington, but they also highlight a president who seems to miss the excitement of the presidential campaign. During that period, he was the underdog who consistently defied expectations, and he had not yet faced the demanding work of governing. This sense of nostalgia was evident in his reflections on the election, recalling how few people believed he could win.

President Donald Trump's February 16, 2017, press conference, a 77-minute affair, echoed the unvarnished style of his 2016 Republican primary campaign. During this event, he highlighted his poll numbers and directly confronted what he termed the "dishonest media." The newly inaugurated president adopted the role of a media critic, expressing a clear desire for retribution.

Key moments and criticisms from the press conference included:

*   **Criticism of News Outlets:** Trump specifically targeted CNN, remarking on its "poor ratings," and referred to the BBC as "that's another beauty."
*   **Response to Anti-Semitism Question:** When questioned about anti-Semitism, Trump's response to the questioner was, "OK, sit down, I understand the rest of your question."
*   **Assertion of Media Distrust:** He declared to the assembled press, "the public doesn’t believe you anymore," signaling a departure from traditional presidential decorum.

This confrontation with the White House press corps followed a week marked by significant negative news. These developments included:

*   **Mike Flynn's Resignation:** National Security Adviser Mike Flynn resigned after the *Washington Post* reported that a Justice Department official had informed the White House that Flynn was vulnerable to blackmail due to misleading senior administration officials about his conversations with the Russian ambassador during the transition period.
*   **Allegations of Russian Contact:** The *New York Times* published a report citing unnamed U.S. officials who believed some Trump campaign associates had been in contact with Russian intelligence officials during the 2016 election.
*   **Intelligence Information Withholding:** A story in the *Wall Street Journal* suggested that intelligence officials were withholding information from President Trump.

Tensions escalated when Trump declined to answer questions from TV news reporters about Flynn and Russia during a joint press conference with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. CNN's Jim Acosta interpreted Trump's decision to call on only conservative and Christian media outlets as a sign that "The fix is in."

At a press conference ostensibly called to announce the nomination of law school dean Alexander Acosta as Labor Secretary, Trump humorously remarked to the CNN reporter that his staff had "check[ed] the family tree" to confirm his nominee was not related to him.

Alice Stewart, a former reporter, current CNN contributor, GOP strategist, and Trump supporter, commented on the event. She stated, "You can’t say he’s not responsive to the press. He answered all the questions from all over the press corps and put to rest the long-standing dialogue that he shuts down certain news outlets."

A backlog of questions was addressed during the briefing:

*   **Flynn's Dismissal:** Trump confirmed Flynn's departure, clarifying that it was not for his conversation with the Russian ambassador but for withholding information from Vice President Mike Pence.
*   **Russia Allegations:** Trump dismissed the *New York Times* story regarding alleged campaign contact with Russian officials as "a joke" and "fake news," suggesting it was fabricated to explain Hillary Clinton's election loss. He attributed leaks to "probably from the Obama administration."
*   **Wikileaks Comparison:** When questioned about his past praise for WikiLeaks releasing emails from Clinton campaign operatives, Trump differentiated by stating that the current stories involved "highly-classified information," unlike "John Podesta saying bad things about the boss."
*   **Impact on Russia Dealings:** Trump suggested that "false, horrible, fake reporting makes it much harder to make a deal with Russia."

The president also made factual inaccuracies, stating he won 306 electoral college votes when the actual number was 304. He also wrongly claimed his win was the "biggest electoral college win since Ronald Reagan," overlooking better performances by George H.W. Bush, Bill Clinton, and Barack Obama.

Former CNN correspondent Frank Sesno, author of "Ask More: The Power of Questions to Open Doors, Uncover Solutions, and Spark Change," described the situation as "just crazy." He opined, "I know that this may scratch an itch that he has about the media and he feels this a really unfair situation and it may resonate with his base. This does not play well over time."

Stewart, however, expressed a different perspective: "From a communications standpoint, I think attacking the media is not a smart move, but it’s worked for him. His goal was to change the narrative and all you can say now is, ‘Mission accomplished.’"

In support of his performance, Trump cited the Rasmussen poll, indicating 55 percent of Americans approved of his job performance. Sesno, however, felt the optics were negative, with Trump appearing "overly obsessed with the media," and concluded, "He’s certainly not winning over the skeptics and the fence sitters."

Trump, conversely, believed the negative optics were on the side of the media. He stated during the press conference, "And I’ll tell you what else I see. I see tone. You know the word ‘tone.’ The tone is such hatred. I’m really not a bad person, by the way. No, but the tone is such — I do get good ratings, you have to admit that — the tone is such hatred."

Despite the media narrative, Trump asserted that his administration was functioning effectively, stating, "I turn on the TV, open the newspapers, and I see stories of chaos. Chaos. Yet, it is the exact opposite. This administration is running like a fine-tuned machine."

President Donald Trump's declaration of inheriting a "mess" from his predecessor, Barack Obama, has drawn considerable attention. While these claims suggest significant inherited challenges, a closer look at economic data and the territorial status of the Islamic State group reveals a more complex picture. This analysis examines some of Trump's key statements from a February 16, 2017, news conference, contrasting them with factual information.

During the conference, President Trump stated, "To be honest I inherited a mess. It's a mess. At home and abroad, a mess." However, the economic conditions Obama faced upon entering office in 2009 were notably more severe than those Trump inherited. Obama took the helm during the most significant economic downturn since the Great Depression, marked by:

*   Unemployment rates that climbed to a peak of 10 percent.
*   A sharp decline in the stock market.
*   A struggling automotive sector.
*   Widespread risk of home foreclosures affecting millions of Americans.

In contrast, Trump began his presidency with unemployment at a considerably lower 4.8 percent. The Dow Jones Industrial Average, which had fallen dramatically in Obama's early years, saw a substantial recovery of around 200 percent by the end of Obama's term, a trend that has continued. Furthermore, when Trump assumed the presidency, a larger percentage of the population had health insurance, incomes were rising, and job growth was strong. While the Trump administration has pointed to a decrease in the labor force participation rate, this trend had already begun to reverse near the end of Obama's tenure. It is recognized that factory and coal mine jobs have been declining for over three decades, and many individuals with only a high school education have experienced a reduction in real income. The homeownership rate has also decreased despite overall economic improvement, leaving some communities feeling left out of a recovery that officially began years earlier.

Regarding the Islamic State, Trump asserted, "ISIS has spread like cancer, another mess I inherited." This statement is also contradicted by available facts. The Islamic State group began losing territory before Trump's inauguration, not only in Iraq and Syria but also in Libya. Sustained military efforts during Obama's final two years had significantly weakened IS, pushing them toward collapse in their primary Iraqi stronghold of Mosul. While the group remains a dangerous threat capable of inspiring terrorist acts beyond its diminished territory, its territorial control had already started to recede. The "cancer" analogy itself was previously used by Obama's former Secretary of Defense, Ash Carter, to describe the campaign against IS, aiming to limit its "metastasis" beyond its core in Iraq and Syria.

President Trump also described his administration as "running like a fine-tuned machine, despite the fact that I can't get my Cabinet approved." This claim stands in stark contrast to the reality of his first month in office, which was characterized by numerous missteps and controversies. Compared to Obama's initial month, Trump's administration enacted far less significant legislation. Republican-led congressional committees launched investigations into the Trump team's pre-inauguration contacts with Russians and the persistent leaks that led to the rapid resignation of his national security adviser. His nominee for Secretary of Labor withdrew due to insufficient Republican support. By many measures, the administration appeared to be in a state of near paralysis during its initial days, causing unease among allies and concern among many members of Congress, who described the situation as "constant disruption." For many Republicans, and certainly for Democrats, the idea of a "fine-tuned machine" seemed questionable. In contrast, Obama's first month saw the signing of a $787 billion stimulus package, a law expanding health care for children, and the Lilly Ledbetter bill addressing equal pay for women. Trump's actions have primarily involved executive orders, which bypass congressional approval and typically have limited scope. His controversial executive order temporarily banning entry for refugees and visitors from seven Muslim-majority countries was subsequently blocked by the courts. Major policy initiatives, such as tax cuts and a replacement for the Affordable Care Act, have yet to materialize. On the day of the news conference, Trump was signing into law a regulation rollback concerning mining near streams. Congress had sent him little other significant legislation.

Trump also highlighted his Electoral College victory, stating, "We got 306 because people came out and voted like they've never seen before, so that's the way it goes. I guess it was the biggest Electoral College win since Ronald Reagan." This claim is inaccurate. In the seven preceding presidential elections, five winners achieved a larger Electoral College majority than Trump. These included George H.W. Bush in 1988, Bill Clinton in 1992 and 1996, and Barack Obama in 2008 and 2012. When a reporter pointed out this overstatement, the president attributed the information to what he was "given" and reiterated that it was "a very substantial victory." It is worth noting that Trump ultimately received 304 electoral votes due to two electors abstaining in December, though he had secured enough states in November to reach 306.

Finally, Trump asserted that the appeals court blocking his travel ban "has been overturned at a record number." This statement is also factually incorrect. Other federal appeals courts have experienced a higher rate of their decisions being overturned by the Supreme Court than the San Francisco-based 9th Circuit, which initially froze his immigration action. In the most recent full term, the Supreme Court reversed 8 out of 11 cases originating from the 9th Circuit. However, the Atlanta-based 11th Circuit had a perfect record of reversal, with the Supreme Court overturning all three cases it heard from that circuit. Over the past five years, five federal appeals courts have had their decisions overturned at a greater rate than the 9th Circuit. While the 9th Circuit is the largest of the 13 federal courts of appeals, and thus handles a greater volume of cases, its reversal rate as a percentage of cases heard by the Supreme Court is comparatively lower, according to statistical analyses. The Supreme Court agrees to hear only a small fraction of appealed cases, and the very act of agreeing to hear a case often indicates a likelihood of reversal, as the court overturns approximately two-thirds of the cases it reviews.

## President Trump's First Major Press Conference: Addressing "Fake News" and Russian Allegations

President Donald Trump held his first comprehensive press conference since his inauguration, a wide-ranging event that lasted over an hour and was marked by significant tension. The primary focus of the conference was to vehemently refute what the President termed "fake news," particularly concerning alleged ties between his associates and Russia. This address was a direct effort to counter a challenging week of headlines and to assert the stability and progress of his administration.

### Seizing the Narrative and Asserting Control

The press conference, announced with minimal advance notice, served as a strategic platform for President Trump to regain control of the public narrative. As NPR national political correspondent Mara Liasson, who was present at the event, observed, the President aimed to highlight his administration's achievements and address pressing concerns head-on. Liasson noted that Trump engaged directly with difficult questions posed by various reporters, employing his characteristic commentary to shape public perception.

"Story after story after story is bad. I won. I won. And the other thing - chaos. There's zero chaos. We are running - this is a fine-tuned machine," President Trump declared during the conference. This statement was a clear attempt to project an image of an organized and effective administration, directly contrasting with media portrayals.

### Confronting Russia Allegations and Personnel Changes

A central theme of the press conference was the persistent news cycle surrounding Russia. Regarding the resignation of National Security Adviser Michael Flynn, President Trump attributed the decision to Flynn's failure to fully inform Vice President Mike Pence. When questioned about the nearly two-week delay in informing the Vice President, Trump suggested he did not view Flynn's actions as a significant issue.

Addressing allegations of contacts between his campaign and Russian intelligence officials, the President stated unequivocally, "The leaks are real, but the news is fake. I have nothing to do with Russia." When pressed further on campaign contacts, his response was, "Not that I know of." These statements aimed to distance his campaign and administration from any improper dealings with Russia.

### Beyond Russia: Policy Announcements and Challenges

Beyond the Russia-related matters, President Trump used the press conference to announce upcoming policy initiatives. He stated that a replacement for the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare) would be presented by mid-March. Additionally, he indicated that a revised executive order on the travel ban would be issued the following week, designed to address objections raised by appellate courts.

Mara Liasson highlighted instances where President Trump was challenged on the accuracy of his statements, particularly concerning his Electoral College victory numbers. When questioned about public trust in light of providing what was described as inaccurate information, Trump attributed it to the data he had been provided. This demonstrated a presidential tactic of retracting or reframing statements under direct questioning.

The President was also asked twice about anti-Semitic incidents, such as swastikas appearing at Jewish community centers. He attributed these acts to his political opponents, stating, "They'll do signs, and they'll do drawings that are inappropriate. It won't be my people. It will be the people on the other side to anger people like you." Liasson observed that the President accepted no personal responsibility for these incidents.

### An Extraordinary Performance and its Aftermath

Reflecting on the atmosphere of the press conference, Liasson described it as an "extraordinary and unprecedented performance." She suggested that President Trump, whether intentionally or not, offered a deep dive into his own psyche. She anticipated that the event would resonate positively with his core supporters and potentially reassure nervous Republicans on Capitol Hill regarding the White House's operational efficiency, thereby countering perceptions of dysfunction.

However, Liasson concluded that it remained to be seen whether the President had truly calmed the waters after a tumultuous first month in office. The press conference, while a significant event, did not definitively resolve the underlying tensions and questions that had emerged in the early days of his presidency. The President's characteristic combative yet engaging persona, reminiscent of his campaign style, was on full display, as noted by Liasson, who described him as defensive, frustrated, aggrieved, and combative, yet also possessing a certain charm. Reports from within the White House indicated that the President was pleased with his performance and in high spirits following the lengthy address.

President Donald Trump points to a member of the media as he takes questions during a news conference in the East Room of the White House in Washington, Thursday, Feb. 16, 2017. (AP Photo/Pablo Martinez...
