Yet plenty of professional investors and longtime students of the market argue that dating the bull market to the initial snap-back rally from a 12-year low overlooks some of the setbacks since then and perhaps overstates the bull's age. Here are some alternative start dates commonly cited by those who think the bull market truly got rolling well after the tumultuous swings around the financial-crisis climax: Source: CNBC September 2011: In a sort of financial-crisis aftershock, stress in European debt markets, surging oil prices and a U.S. debt-ceiling standoff struck a still-fragile economic recovery and dragged the S&P 500 down 19.4 percent from May to September that year. The decline exceeded 20 percent on an intraday basis and most global indexes suffered declines of at least that much. The record keepers file this under the heading of "severe market correction," but to many it was close enough to consider the recovery from the September 2011 akin to a new, or renewed, bull market. March 2013: In some respects, this equity advance didn't begin to behave as a real bull market until late 2012. Treasury yields had bottomed in mid-2012, and riskier assets benefited from the unclenching of tensions that this represented. Risk spreads on speculative high-yield debt began collapsing, and for the first time in this economic cycle, the price-earnings multiple on stocks began expanding. By early 2013, with Washington gridlocked, the Federal Reserve looking to end quantitative easing and equities appearing cheap relative to bonds, the S&P gained upward momentum. By late March 2013, the index finally eclipsed its former all-time high from October 2007 of 1565. Some strategists say this was the end of the "secular bear market" that had begun in the year 2000 and the start of a new "secular bull market." The term "secular" refers to a long-term structural move in asset prices. An earlier secular bear lasted from 1966 to 1982, when the Dow Jones Industrial Average was contained below the 1000 market, followed by the stupendous secular bull of 1982 to 2000. Obviously within these periods are significant cyclical swings, often of more than 20 percent against the long-term trend. There's no real science to defining or playing such periods. And, frankly, there have been so few that it's hard to generalize about their behavior with too much confidence. But the idea that a push to a new all-time high touches off a decade-plus of further upward movement has some evidence behind it, and the likes of Bank of America Merrill Lynch have been using this framework since the 2013 breakout. February 2016: The S&P 500 fell "only" about 15 percent from the summer of 2015 to the early-2016 low as oil prices collapsed and global recession and deflation fears flared. Yet that understates the carnage beneath the surface. The small-cap Russell 2000 sank 26 percent over eight months. And Strategas Research technical strategist Chris Verrone notes that 63 percent of the stocks in the S&P 500 index fell at least 20 percent from their 52-week high. So, bear market or not, it was a pretty good washout - one that many now argue cleansed the market of excesses, rebuilt value and reinvigorated the bull. In some ways this is akin to the Asian financial crisis of 1998, when the S&P 500 never fell by more than 20 percent but 70 percent of all stocks did. Once it bottomed, that bull market put in a ferociously lucrative final push over the next 18 months. So how much does this matter for an investor trying to set expectations for future equity performance? Not terribly much. Even if we say the bull is eight years old, and therefore a "mature" one, bull markets don't die of old age; they are put down by recession, financial panic or the Fed. On the other hand, even if we decree that the bull market truly started five-and-a-half years, or four years ago, or 13 months ago, it doesn't change the fact that stock valuations are close to the top of their historical range and the eight-year returns delivered by the market are among the best ever. Ryan Detrick of LPL Financial looked back at all eight-year periods since 1936. The past eight years' gain was only exceeded 35 times, placing the current run in the top four percent of all past periods. Most prior instances occurred in clusters in the mid-1950s, and then the late-'90s and early 2000. The one-, five- and ten-year average and median returns following the other such periods aren't great – small losses one year out and subpar appreciation for longer periods. Granted, those averages are heavily undercut by the Tech Bubble readings. But taken together, they suggest that no matter when you say the bull market began, it's certainly not over but it's probably not early.

**Navigating Wall Street's Enduring Bull Market: Longevity, Outlook, and the Specter of Correction**

Wall Street's remarkable bull market, now in its eighth year, continues to defy expectations of a premature end. Market strategists, including those at prominent institutions like UBS Securities and Kingsview Asset Management, largely agree that the sustained period of gains is poised to persist. This optimism is fueled by a confluence of factors, including robust corporate earnings, the anticipated impact of tax reductions, and a generally business-friendly administration, as reported by Reuters. However, these seasoned observers also sound a note of caution, highlighting that the market's elevated valuations could indeed pave the way for a correction, potentially as significant as 10 percent.

### The Case for Continued Growth: Beyond the Calendar

The fundamental argument underpinning the continued ascent of equities, as articulated by numerous analysts and strategists, centers on the low probability of a near-term recession. Paul Nolte, a portfolio manager at Kingsview Asset Management in Chicago, eloquently captures this sentiment: "Just because the (bull) market has hit a birthday, or is among the longest ever, doesn’t mean that it will ‘die’ of old age. Markets don’t really pay attention to the calendar or the candles on the cake." This perspective is echoed by Ryan Detrick, senior market strategist at LPL Financial in Charlotte, North Carolina. Detrick believes that with earnings growth projected to continue, the bull market could potentially extend into its ninth year, even if the journey ahead is characterized by a "rocky" path.

### A Statistical Snapshot of Resilience

Key statistics vividly underscore the market's impressive performance. Since its low point on March 9, 2009, the S&P 500 index has experienced a staggering surge of 250 percent. This makes the current bull market the second longest on record, notably uninterrupted by a 20 percent decline – a common precursor to bear markets. The rally has also demonstrated remarkable resilience, maintaining its upward trajectory even through a year of declining corporate earnings in 2016. This sustained performance was partly supported by historically low interest rates, which rendered equities a more attractive investment compared to U.S. Treasuries. As of Wednesday, the index had already risen nearly 6 percent in 2017, closing at 2,362.98. A December Reuters poll had projected the S&P 500 to finish 2017 at 2,350, a forecast bolstered by expectations of President Donald Trump's economic stimulus initiatives, including proposed tax cuts and deregulation efforts.

### The Inevitability of Corrections and Valuation Concerns

Even with the potential for a market correction looming, strategists generally concur that the bull market can persist. Julian Emanuel, executive director of U.S. equity and derivatives strategy at UBS Securities, anticipates a healthy 5 to 10 percent pullback. He views such a correction not as a harbinger of doom, but as a necessary mechanism to "refresh" the market and create attractive buying opportunities. However, Emanuel also points to a critical concern: "valuations have outrun earnings potential and the state of fundamentals generally." The S&P 500, at the time of this analysis, was trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of approximately 18, a figure that stands above its historical average of 15. For these valuations to normalize, a sustained and robust improvement in corporate profits is deemed crucial.

### Earnings Growth and Economic Indicators: Pillars of Support

Thomson Reuters I/B/E/S data projects earnings to grow by a healthy 10.7 percent this year, a figure that, while representing a slight downward revision from earlier estimates, still signifies substantial growth. Furthermore, positive U.S. economic data, including a strong February jobs report, suggests an economy that is far from a downturn. This economic resilience provides additional foundational support for stock prices. Kingsview's Nolte, who foresees a potential pullback of up to 5 percent, reiterates this optimistic outlook, stating, "We do not yet see any of the warning signs that point to a larger decline. A recession does not seem to be in the cards just yet." This sentiment is further reinforced by analyses from firms like Goldman Sachs, which have highlighted the ongoing strength in consumer spending and business investment as key drivers of economic stability. The Federal Reserve's cautious approach to interest rate hikes, as detailed in their recent policy statements, also contributes to a supportive environment for equities.

8 Things to Know about the Bull Market on Its 8th Birthday The stock market has accomplished something that only one other bull market in history has achieved — it survived to see its eighth birthday. It was eight years ago today that the Dow Jones industrial average sank to its recent nadir of 6,547, as hundreds of thousands of workers were losing jobs and the global financial system teetered on the edge of collapse. Since then, though, the Dow has skyrocketed more than 14,000 points to 20,855. The broader S&P 500 index of U.S. stocks has come roaring back too, returning an annual 20% to investors who had the nerve to stay in the scary market. But this was by no means a straight shot back up. In fact, the second-longest bull market in the post-World War II era has been unpredictable, puzzling, and rather peculiar. Here's how: 1. This Bull Has Been Unloved "The current bull market has been the least loved, the most questioned and the most doubted bull that we certainly can recall in near 34 years of experience in the markets," noted Oppenheimer chief investment strategist John Stoltzfus in a recent report. That's an understatement. Even as this bull market more than tripled the value of stocks over the past eight years, the percent of households that actually own equities has sunk from 62% in 2008 to 52% last year, according to a survey by Gallup . That's the lowest level of stock ownership in the 19 years that Gallup has been tracking this data. 2. This Bull Has Been Persistent World events since 2009 have been anything but supportive for a major stock market rally. Major industries such as banks and automakers neared collapse. The Federal government shutdown for 16 days. And political posturing in Congress in the Obama years brought the nation close to defaulting on the national debt. Overseas, major European nations were crippled by a debt crisis, terrorists attacks spread around the globe, and China's economy endured a dramatic slump. Yet stocks kept chugging along. 3. This Bull Was Nearly Gored Last Year Since President Trump's election in November, the stock market has been on a a roll, as most major economic metrics have been pointing higher. So it's easy to forget that as recently as a year ago, there was real doubt that equities would make it to this anniversary. One year ago stocks were battered by a series of bad headlines, such as low growth in China and weak global demand for oil. A few months later U.K. voters decided to leave the European Union, and eventually Donald Trump was elected president — a plot twist that some analysts predicted would cause stocks to crash. And yet stocks rebounded from their disastrous 2016 winter to deliver 22% over the past 12 months, according to CFRA, the third biggest year-over-year gain since March 2009. 4. This Bull Has Been Surprisingly Calm Despite all of last year's uncertainty, stocks have been remarkably subdued. In fact, compared to the bull market of October 1990 to March 2000, investors could afford to take a long, deep breath for the past 12 months. "During year eight, the S&P 500 recorded 85 [days of more than 1% gains or losses] in 1998, but only 23 in the year just past," according to CFRA's Sam Stovall. 5. This Bull Is Getting Expensive " Most importantly for investors, both stocks and bonds are now significantly more expensive than eight years ago," says David Kelly, chief global strategist for J.P. Morgan Funds. The current price/earnings ratio for the S&P 500 now rests at 18 based on projected profits over the coming 12 months. That's about 12% above the market's 25-year average, per Kelly. That could be a result of investors hoping that a GOP-dominated federal government will unleash billions in tax cuts and spending to jumpstart persistently lackluster post-recession economic growth. By another P/E calculation, the market is even frothier. Based on 10 years of averaged corporate profits, the stock market's P/E now stands at 29.8. The only other times the market has been this expensive by this measure was during the dot.com bubble and the Great Depression. 6. This Bull Is Egalitarian Different sectors of the market have experienced their own highs and lows. Financial stocks, for instance, gained 149% from March 2009 - 2010, according to CFRA, but fell 9.5% two years later, before leading the pack last year. Two years ago sleepy, defensive-minded utility stocks earned investors the best return (14.8%), while energy companies lost big league (-16.2%). These rough edges, though, have smoothed out over the long haul. From 2009 to 2017, there's only a 1 percentage point difference in annual returns between the top and second best sectors — consumer discretionary 25.7% vs. real estate's 24.6%. Five sectors returned between 22% to 26% per year, while the weakest performer (energy) returned a respectable 9.4%. 7. This Bull Has Gotten a Big Assist from the Fed Normally, there's a pattern to bull markets. First, stocks rise, foreshadowing a rebound in economic growth. Then once the economy actually gets into gear, the Federal Reserve starts to tap on the brakes by raising interest rates to make sure the economy — and inflation — don't overheat. The cumulative effect of Fed rate hikes are often what helps bring about the end to bull markets. This time around, though, the Fed has kept interest rates at historically low levels throughout the eight years of this bull. The Fed has lifted rates only two times throughout this entire rally — each time by a mere quarter of a percentage point. But that may change as the Fed is expected to lift rates next week. 8. This Bull Will Turn 9 This bull market has dovetailed with one of the longest business cycles in American history. But all things end, which have some worried that a recession, and stock market declines, could eventually reappear. But old age would be the wrong cause of death, should it occur. "Bull markets don’t die of old age, they die of fright," says Stovall. Which means there has to be something to cause investors to sell en masse and move their money into safer assets. And usually that involves concerns over negative economic growth, i.e. recessions. So will the economy contract in 2017? It doesn't appear likely. Demand for automobiles and homes remain strong, and the Fed has signaled multiple times that it believes the economy is strong enough to endure higher interest rates. But that doesn't mean you should expect prices to rise forever, or at least as much as they have in the recent past. "With earnings growing more slowly, dividend yields and bond coupons low, and the potential for P/E ratios and real yields to mean revert, a traditional 60/40 U.S. stock/bond portfolio could well generate returns over the next five years that are less than half the roughly 8% annualized gain that such a portfolio provided over the past 25 years," says Kelly.

## The Enduring Strength of the Dow Jones Industrial Average: A Post-Crisis Performance Analysis

Eight years ago, the financial landscape was dramatically reshaped by a significant market downturn. For investors who possessed the foresight to invest in the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) during that challenging period, the subsequent years have likely yielded substantial portfolio growth. The DJIA, a distinguished index comprising 30 of the most prominent U.S. corporations, serves as a crucial barometer of the broader market's health and is globally recognized as a leading proxy for the U.S. stock market's performance.

### A Remarkable Recovery and Growth Trajectory

Following the turbulent economic climate of March 2009, when the Dow Jones Industrial Average found itself hovering around the 6,500-point mark, the index has since embarked on an impressive ascent, surpassing an astonishing 200% increase. This remarkable surge can be largely attributed to the accommodative monetary policies implemented by the Federal Reserve and other international central banks. These measures were strategically enacted in response to the severe 2008-2009 financial crisis, aiming to stabilize and stimulate economic activity. In contrast, during this same period, alternative investments such as gold experienced a more modest increase of 30.8%, and crude oil saw a gain of 17.5%, highlighting the distinct outperformance of equities.

### Global Comparisons: The Dow's Leading Edge

When juxtaposed with the performance of international equity markets, the Dow's trajectory stands out as particularly exceptional. For instance, the Stoxx Europe 600 index advanced by 138%, the United Kingdom's FTSE 100 rose by 108%, China's Shanghai Composite added 50%, and Japan's Nikkei saw a gain of 175%. While these international indices demonstrated positive growth, the DJIA's robust expansion underscored the resilience and recovery of the U.S. corporate sector.

### Standout Performers Within the Dow

Among the Dow components that have maintained their presence in the index since 2009, Home Depot Inc. has emerged as a truly standout performer. Its stock price has appreciated by an astounding 608%, showcasing exceptional business growth and investor confidence. American Express Co. and Walt Disney Co. have also delivered robust returns, with both companies' stock prices surging by over 500%, demonstrating strong operational performance and market leadership.

### New Entrants and Their Impact

Several companies have been added to the Dow's esteemed roster since the commencement of this significant bull market, and their inclusion has contributed to the index's overall upward trajectory. Notably, UnitedHealth Group Inc. has experienced a remarkable 752% increase in its stock price since the bull market began. Visa Inc. has also seen its stock price skyrocket by an impressive 524%. Apple Inc., a more recent addition to this blue-chip index, has surged nearly 1,000% since 2009. However, it is important to note that its performance since becoming a Dow component two years ago has been a more modest 9.3%, reflecting the dynamic nature of stock market performance over different time horizons.

### Performance of Recent Dow Additions

To provide a clearer picture of how newer components have fared, here's a look at the performance of some recent Dow additions:

| Company                 | Returns since March 2009 | Date Added to Dow | Returns since Inclusion |
| :---------------------- | :----------------------- | :---------------- | :---------------------- |
| Cisco Systems Inc.      | 136%                     | June 8, 2009      | 72%                     |
| Travelers Cos.          | 245%                     | June 8, 2009      | 179%                    |
| United Health Group Inc.| 752%                     | Sept. 24, 2012    | 201%                    |
| Goldman Sachs Group Inc.| 176%                     | Sept. 23, 2013    | 53%                     |
| Nike Inc.               | 457%                     | Sept. 23, 2013    | 65%                     |

*Source: FactSet*

### Varied Performance Among Dow Constituents

However, it is crucial to acknowledge that not all Dow stocks have achieved such exceptional growth. Major oil company Exxon Mobil Corp. has seen a relatively modest increase of 23% since March 2009. Its competitor, Chevron Corp., has performed better with an 87% gain during the same period. Other established companies, including International Business Machines Corp., Procter & Gamble Co., Verizon Communications Inc., and Wal-Mart Stores Inc. (now Walmart Inc.), have also posted comparatively subdued returns, illustrating the diverse performance within even the most prominent index.

### Departures from the Dow and Their Performance

Since 2009, a limited number of companies have been removed from the Dow Jones Industrial Average. These include AT&T Inc., Bank of America Corp., Alcoa Corp., Citigroup Inc., General Motors Co., Kraft Heinz Co., and HP Inc. Interestingly, among these former components, Bank of America and Citigroup have actually outperformed some current Dow constituents like Goldman Sachs and J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. in terms of percentage gains since March 2009. While AT&T's 77% gain fell short of Verizon Communications Inc.'s 87% increase, Alcoa Corp., which was replaced by Nike Inc. in September 2013, has seen a 77% rise. Data for General Motors, Kraft Heinz, and HP were unavailable for comprehensive comparison due to factors such as mergers, spin-offs, and, in the case of General Motors, a significant government bailout following the 2008 financial crisis, which impacted their historical performance metrics.

## Navigating Market Peaks: Opportunities and Caution Amidst Bullish Sentiment

Eight years ago, on March 9, 2009, the stock market hit its lowest point of the last bear market, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average closing at 6,547.05. Since that trough, the Dow has more than tripled. An initial investment of twenty thousand dollars in the Dow index at that time would now be valued at approximately sixty-four thousand dollars.

While the majority of these market gains occurred during Barack Obama's presidency, the Dow has still climbed over twenty-five hundred points, or about twelve percent, since Donald Trump's election. Last week, the Dow surpassed twenty-one thousand for the first time. This period also saw the initial public offering (IPO) of Snap, the social media company behind Snapchat. Despite consistently operating at a loss, Snap was valued at thirty-four billion dollars by the market at the close of its first trading day.

President Trump highlighted these positive trends on Twitter, stating, "Since November 8th, Election Day, the Stock Market has posted $3.2 trillion in GAINS and consumer confidence is at a 15 year high. Jobs!"

### Investor Influx and Underlying Skepticism

Observing such figures, many individuals are motivated to invest. Approximately seventy-five billion dollars in new capital has entered U.S. equity and exchange-traded funds since October, according to the Investment Company Institute. This suggests investors anticipate the continuation of the bull market under Trump's administration. While this may prove true, skepticism remains.

This skepticism is not rooted in political opposition or concerns about the inherent economic and financial risks associated with a leader like Trump in the White House. Instead, it stems from nonpolitical factors:

#### Key Concerns for Investors

*   **Valuation:** The current market valuations are a significant concern.
*   **Monetary Policy:** The Federal Reserve's actions regarding interest rates are a key consideration.
*   **Herding Behavior:** The tendency for investors to follow the crowd can distort market dynamics.

While not arguing for a full-blown speculative bubble akin to the dot-com era or the early 2000s housing market, the stratospheric valuation of Snap serves as a warning sign. Although Snap's stock has since declined, it doesn't compare to the speculative IPOs of 1999 or the prevalence of risky mortgage loans between 2004 and 2007.

However, the price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 index, a widely watched valuation metric, currently exceeds twenty-six. This is not as high as during the late 1990s, but it surpasses levels seen in any other bull market since World War II. Other valuation indicators, such as Robert Shiller's cyclically adjusted P/E ratio and James Tobin's "Q ratio," also signal potential risks. Investment analyst Andrew Smithers notes that while current stock market overvaluation is not at the extreme peaks of 1929 and 1999, it is nearing other historical highs seen in 1905, 1936, and 1968.

### Counterarguments from Market Bulls

Market bulls often present two counterarguments:

1.  **Interest Rates:** With interest rates at historically low levels, stocks offer better value compared to bonds and other fixed-income investments.
2.  **Market Momentum:** Even overvalued markets can continue to rise significantly before a correction.

#### The Federal Reserve's Role and Future Outlook

Regarding the first point, the Federal Reserve's commitment to low borrowing costs has undeniably fueled the bull market. However, the Fed is now raising interest rates, and the pace of monetary tightening may accelerate. The Federal Open Market Committee is widely expected to increase the federal funds rate by a quarter point at its upcoming meeting, with projections indicating at least two more hikes by year-end. While rising interest rates don't always lead to stock market declines, historical data shows a correlation in periods such as the mid-2000s, late 1990s, late 1970s, and late 1960s. The early 1990s were an exception, attributed to a surge in productivity and GDP growth that boosted corporate profits.

While President Trump's promises of economic growth could potentially lead to a similar outcome, the current low unemployment rate (4.8%) and weak productivity figures make sustained growth unlikely in the view of many economists.

#### The "Trump Factor" and Investor Psychology

The "Trump factor" undoubtedly contributes to the market's upward trajectory. Many prominent hedge fund managers have welcomed the President's proposals for tax cuts and deregulation. David Tepper, head of Appaloosa Management, remarked that such policies could "release animal spirits." He further suggested that in the current environment of decent growth, low inflation, and pro-business policies, investors "can't be short."

This sentiment, the fear of missing out, appears to be a primary driver of the market's buoyancy. Professional investors are buying stocks because others are, and they risk being penalized if they deviate from this trend. George Soros, for instance, reportedly lost a billion dollars last year after shorting the market. John Maynard Keynes cautioned that herding behavior can lead markets far from rational valuations, and such mispricing can persist. He is famously quoted as saying, "The market can stay irrational for longer than you can stay solvent."

Therefore, predicting short-term market movements is inherently difficult. With many investors remaining optimistic, stock prices could continue to climb for a period. However, overvalued markets eventually correct and revert to levels supported by economic fundamentals, and these corrections are rarely gentle. There is little reason to believe this cycle will be different.

Wall Street's current bull market, characterized by a sustained rise in stock prices, is nearing a significant historical marker. As it approaches its eighth anniversary on March 9th, its remarkable performance has begun to quiet those who doubted its endurance. This extended period of growth is not only proving resilient but is also statistically noteworthy, securing its place in market annals.

In terms of performance, this bull market ranks as the fourth-longest on record, underscoring its substantial gains. While the epic bull run of the 1990s, which saw a 417% surge, remains the top performer, the current market exhibits considerable staying power. Research from organizations like the **National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER)**, which officially dates U.S. business cycles, would likely place this bull market among the longest in modern history. This sustained growth has fueled significant wealth creation, with the stock market's value increasing by over $21 trillion, according to data from **S&P Dow Jones Indices**.

Several key factors have underpinned this market's success, as highlighted by analyses from financial institutions such as **Goldman Sachs** and **J.P. Morgan**:

*   **Top Performing Stock:** Incyte (INCY), a biotechnology company, has been a standout performer. Its stock has experienced substantial appreciation, largely propelled by the success of its bone marrow treatment, Jakafi, and a robust pipeline of potential future drug therapies, as noted in reports by **FierceBiotech**.
*   **Biggest Underperformer:** Southwestern Energy (SWN) has been the bull market's most significant laggard, heavily affected by a sharp downturn in oil prices, a trend extensively covered by **Bloomberg** and **Reuters**.
*   **Leading Sector:** The consumer discretionary sector has led the market over the past eight years. This outperformance is largely attributed to the resilience of U.S. consumers, a phenomenon frequently discussed by economists at institutions like the **Federal Reserve**. Companies within this sector, including Home Depot, Starbucks, and Nike, have benefited from an improving economic climate and consumers' willingness to spend on non-essential items, a trend detailed in market research from **Nielsen** and **Morning Consult**.
*   **Lagging Sector:** Conversely, the energy sector has lagged considerably. This underperformance is understandable given the severe earnings contractions experienced by companies in this industry, a situation exacerbated by declining energy prices, as documented by the **U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA)**.

## Navigating the Unprecedented Calm: Is This Bull Market a Sign of Strength or Complacency?

The current bull market, now in its eighth year, continues its remarkable ascent from the Great Recession's depths without faltering. This rally, the second longest on record, has surprisingly avoided the typical existential crises that often plague extended market periods. While valuations appear stretched and the market's longevity places it in rare territory, volatility, a key indicator of investor anxiety, remains notably absent. This unusual market tranquility has ignited a significant debate: is this calm a sign of a healthy market, or are investors simply ignoring potential dangers in the current political climate? Regardless of the underlying reasons, the outcome has been overwhelmingly positive for those invested. Since November, equity values have surged by an impressive $3 trillion. Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist at CFRA, advises investors to be wary of a "fear-of-missing-out mindset." He cautions that this could signal overconfidence and mark the market's final lap. Stovall further notes that "volatility will remain a potential challenge to the intestinal fortitude of many investors and cause their emotions to become their portfolio’s worst enemy."

### Historical Context and Uncharacteristic Stability

Historical data compiled by CFRA, dating back to World War II, reveals that the S&P 500 Index's 250 percent advance since 2009 surpasses any bull market at the eight-year mark, and indeed, all those that concluded earlier. Typically, stocks tend to become more volatile as rallies mature, but this trend is conspicuously absent today. Over the past twelve months, the S&P 500 has experienced only 23 days with 1 percent gains or losses. This stands in stark contrast to the 85 such days observed during the eighth year of the 1990-2000 bull run. Fueled by accommodative monetary policy from central banks and a doubling of corporate profits, this enduring bull market shows few signs of waning as it enters its ninth year. Following President Trump's election in November, the S&P 500 has climbed 11 percent. Notably, it has gone 55 days without an intraday swing of 1 percent, the longest such period recorded since at least 1982.

### The Complacency Conundrum: Metrics and Concerns

The question of whether surging markets without volatility are inherently good or bad is a frequent topic of discussion on trading desks across Wall Street. Recently, charts emerged suggesting that "complacency" has reached record highs. This is calculated by dividing the S&P 500's price-earnings ratio of approximately 22 by the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), which is hovering near 11. This quotient has now surpassed levels seen during the dot-com bubble. Even the Federal Reserve has acknowledged the unusual lack of market turbulence. In the minutes from the central bank's most recent meeting, some officials "expressed concern that the low level of implied volatility in equity markets appeared inconsistent with the considerable uncertainty attending the outlook" for President Trump's pro-growth agenda. However, Michael Shaoul, chief executive officer of Marketfield Asset Management, argues that neither the P/E ratio nor the VIX, individually or in combination, are reliable indicators of a market top. He stated in an interview, "This idea can be summed up by the old term ‘two wrongs don’t make a right.’ We can complain that valuations are no longer appealing and that the steady staircase-like move is unnerving, but we cannot say that these factors in themselves mean the end is nigh."

### Investor Behavior and Shifting Dynamics

The consistent gains have successfully lured investors back to equities. Data compiled by the Investment Company Institute and Bloomberg reveals that American equity ETFs and mutual funds have seen inflows of $60 billion over the last four months. This amount has only been surpassed twice in similar periods during this bull market. In contrast, from 2009 to 2016, withdrawals exceeded $200 billion. Investors' past reluctance to embrace equities may be understandable. While market disruptions following Britain's vote to leave the European Union and President Trump's election proved temporary, they represented two of only five instances in just two years of what Deutsche Bank AG categorizes as an extreme shift in equity volatility. Prior to this period, it took two decades to record five such events. The current market tranquility indeed appears somewhat out of sync with historical patterns. In an average bull market, the number of days with a 1 percent move typically more than doubles to 65 by year seven, after declining in the initial three years. During the Internet boom, the only post-war bull market that outlasted the current one, year nine saw such days surge to 95.

### Potential Catalysts for Future Volatility

John Stoltzfus, chief market strategist at Oppenheimer & Co., suggests that while President Trump's proposed tax cuts and increased public spending could accelerate corporate profit growth, anticipating the continuation of this peaceful advance might be a miscalculation. He outlined numerous potential catalysts for volatility outbreaks in a March 6 note, including Federal Reserve tightening, disappointment with Trump's policies, and risks associated with the French presidential election and China's currency policy. Stoltzfus commented, "The ever so unloved and disdained bull market might have more upside than even its earliest adapters and supporters project." However, he added, "With the transition taking place in Washington, changes in monetary policy stateside as well as upcoming elections abroad with issues around populism and nationalism, we expect the market will not show much tolerance should it detect investor complacency."

query: What were the key targets of the ISIS attacks in Iran, and what was the impact on casualties?

documents.text: Gunmen and bombers stunned Iran on Wednesday when they stormed parliament and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, leaving 12 people dead in what appear to be the first ISIS attacks in the country. After a string of ISIS-claimed attacks targeting Western countries, many are left wondering — why would the group suddenly target Iran? The World reached out to William McCants, author of "The ISIS Apocalypse" and director of the Project on US Relations with the Islamic World at the Brookings Institution, to get some insight. How do ISIS and its adherents view the Islamic Republic of Iran? In the first instance, they see it as a religious competitor. Iran is dominated by [Shiite Muslims], one of the two major sects of Islam, and it's a sect that ISIS views as a rival. There is also a strategic competition, of course, with Iran for control in Iraq. ISIS has been fighting Iranian forces off and on since 2006, when it was founded. And it has long wanted to carry out an attack inside of Iran, but could not do so because al-Qaeda told it "no" in the early years. And once ISIS split from al-Qaeda, it may not have had the assets to do so. Talk a little more about the religious rivalry. Isn't it true that in the mindset of ISIS, the Shiite [also called Shia] Muslims are not just rivals, but also apostates? That's right. Sunni jihadists in general have a dim view of the Shia. They believe Shia leaders are apostates, there's a debate among jihadists as to whether the hoi polloi are apostates. For ISIS, they are certainly apostates — all of them. Ignorance of religion is no excuse, they would argue. Shia Muslims are all beyond the pale, and, in ISIS's view, worse than infidels like Jews and Christians, because these Shia, they argue, have made a choice to place themselves outside of true Islam, as they would see it. ISIS is losing territory in Iraq and Syria, and even in its longtime stronghold of the Syrian city Raqqa. So, why would ISIS want to attack the regional power of Iran now? On the face of it, it doesn't seem to make much sense. But there are a couple of reasons why. One is to, of course, punish Iran for its meddling in Iraq. Two, to force Iran to go all-in in Iraq, to commit even more forces and turn this into a major sectarian war. The thinking here being that, if this war is clearly divided along sectarian lines, ISIS would gain more support from the Sunni locals, who fear Iran. Another reason to carry out an attack like this would be to push the Iranian government into cracking down domestically on the Sunnis [inside Iran]. ISIS would hope in that instance that the crackdown would be wide-ranging and would create a lot of alienation among the Sunnis in Iran, and that they would then support ISIS's cause. Another way to think about this is through the lens of recruitment and competition with al-Qaeda. ISIS used to be a part of al-Qaeda. They split in recent years. And they have been competing with one another for market share among global Sunni jihadists. ISIS has had trouble attracting recruits over the last year or so, because security has tightened and it's losing a lot of territory. So, it wants to retain momentum to show that it's still in the fight, and what better way to do that in the jihadist world than to carry out a strike in the heart of Iran, at two of its major symbols of power? In the same vein, this is part of a global series of attacks that have happened over the past few days, either inspired or directed by ISIS, that are also meant to show that the organization is still in the fight, can still recruit. It's the Muslim month of fasting, Ramadan. And during the same month last year, ISIS also carried out a high-profile series of attacks, probably toward the same end — to attract more recruits. So, while on the face of it, it doesn't look like a good idea to poke a bear like Iran, there can be other reasons to do it that are smart, at least from ISIS's perspective. Is it your sense that ISIS had been trying to carry out an attack like this in Iran for some time? We know from a series of internal ISIS and al-Qaeda documents that ISIS has wanted to carry out an attack in Iran for a long time. But its parent organization al-Qaeda discouraged it. That's because al-Qaeda has a logistics hub inside of Iran, and also has a number of people being held by the Iranian government. And al-Qaeda didn't want to lose either the hub or its people, so it said, "no." Once the two organizations split, then ISIS could of course pursue its designs to carry out an attack in Iran, but then it would have to build up its capability. ISIS has had a number of people — Sunnis from Iran — go fight for its organization in Iraq. And perhaps it's one of their members who carried out today's attack. We'll see about that. So, after 2014, when ISIS split with al-Qaeda, it was probably a matter of lacking capability rather than any other reason why we haven't seen this kind of attack before. What's significant about the two sites of today's attack in Tehran — at Iran's national parliament building, and the mausoleum of the Islamic Republic’s founder, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini? Well, the parliament is the heart, of course, of the Iranian government — its lawmaking body. And the shrine is the spiritual heart of Iran's revolutionary government. Khomeini was the founder of the republic, and some whispered that he was something even more, approaching messianic status. So this is an attack that went against both the heart of Iran's premier deliberative body, but also the spiritual heart of the revolution. Last question, Will. What comes to mind in the aftermath of this attack when you think about what's at stake for the United States? It's about demonstrating solidarity with any country that has been through an attack on innocent civilians. The US terrorist list is dominated by Sunni groups, but not exclusively. In the past, the US has designated groups [as terrorist organizations] that threatened Iran domestically that did not pose a threat to the US. I think it's important to uphold that principle. This interview was condensed and edited for clarity.

Iran was shaken on Wednesday by coordinated assaults on its Parliament building and the shrine of its revolutionary founder. These attacks, carried out by armed assailants, some of whom were disguised as women, represent the most severe terrorist incident to strike the Islamic Republic in years.

The near-simultaneous attacks unfolded over several hours, catching Iran's security forces off guard and disrupting the nation's carefully cultivated image of stability in a volatile region.

Key details from the events include:

*   **Casualties:** At least 12 people were killed and 46 were injured.
*   **Attackers:** All six known assailants were killed during the incidents.
*   **Detainees:** Five suspects were reportedly taken into custody, though their identities remain undisclosed.

Brig. Gen. Hossein Salami, deputy commander of the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps, vowed retribution, stating, "We will avenge the blood of those martyred in today’s terrorism attacks."

Recent assaults on potent symbols of Iranian ideology, claimed by ISIS, are poised to intensify tensions with Saudi Arabia, Gulf states, and the Trump administration, with global repercussions. The terrorists targeted Iran's parliament and the tomb of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the founder of the Islamic Republic, striking at the very heart of the 1979 revolution. These attacks, deeply shocking to ordinary Tehranis who have largely been spared such violence, represent a significant escalation.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has accused Saudi Arabia of orchestrating these coordinated assaults. Regardless of the accusation's accuracy, the attacks are likely to have substantial political fallout beyond Iran, exacerbating friction with the United States, Saudi Arabia, and the Sunni Muslim monarchies of the Gulf. ISIS's swift claim of responsibility, if true, signals the group's success in bringing its divisive brand of terror to the doorstep of its principal Shia adversary.

Key targets of the attacks included:

*   **The Iranian Parliament (Majlis):** A heavily fortified government building in the city center.
*   **The Tomb of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini:** An immense complex south of Tehran, treated as a shrine and visited by tens of thousands annually. Security at the shrine is notably lighter than at the parliament, a situation expected to change. For Iranians, an attack on Khomeini's tomb is comparable to an assault on the Lincoln Memorial in Washington D.C.

Should ISIS's claim be substantiated, this marks their first successful strike within Iran, despite previous reported attempts. The group is engaged in an ideological and military conflict with Iranian forces and their allies in Syria and Iraq. ISIS has previously vowed to "conquer Iran and restore it to the Sunni Muslim nation as it was before," asserting that Iran's clerical leaders and their Persian predecessors have persecuted Sunnis for centuries. ISIS finds allies among militant groups in Iran's Sistan-Baluchistan province, which has a significant Sunni population, recalling past attacks by Sunni extremists like the Jundallah network.

These atrocities occur as ISIS's self-proclaimed caliphate faces imminent collapse, with Iranian-backed forces and U.S.-backed militias closing in on its strongholds. In response, ISIS has urged followers to attack enemies wherever they reside, a pattern potentially reflected in recent attacks in Manchester, Kabul, Baghdad, Marawi, and London.

Conservative Iranian leaders are expected to echo the IRGC's stance, blaming Saudi Arabia and, by extension, the Trump administration. Iran remembers past U.S. efforts to destabilize the country and its unofficial support for groups like the Mujahedin e-Khalq. The critical question is whether Trump's overt hostility is encouraging a resurgence of these destabilization tactics. Trump's recent condemnation of Iran, his call for its international isolation, and his strong support for Saudi Arabia, including a significant arms deal and backing for its ostracism of Qatar, have heightened regional tensions and warnings of impending conflict.

Iran's leaders reject Trump's accusations, contending that Riyadh is ISIS's primary supporter and highlighting Iran's alignment with the U.S. in combating jihadis in Iraq. They argue that Iran's quasi-democratic political system, with an elected parliament and president, challenges the "sponsor of terrorism" narrative, a reality that appears to provoke Trump, the Saudis, and ISIS alike.

The video shows a bloody body on the floor next to a desk. An Iranian member of Parliament said the images in the video were from inside his office, and he said the victims were his “colleagues.” A voice on the video is heard in Arabic saying: “Do you think we will leave? We will remain, God willing.”

TEHRAN (Reuters) - Iran faced an unprecedented assault on Wednesday as suicide bombers and gunmen targeted the nation's parliament and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini in Tehran, resulting in at least 13 fatalities. The Islamic Revolutionary Guards immediately pointed fingers at regional rival Saudi Arabia, while the Islamic State group claimed responsibility and issued further threats against Iran's Shi'ite majority, whom the hardline Sunni militants consider heretics.

Iranian Foreign Minister Javad Zarif, via Twitter, stated, "Terror-sponsoring despots threaten to bring the fight to our homeland. Proxies attack what their masters despise most: the seat of democracy." While not explicitly naming a country, his remarks appeared to allude to comments made by Saudi Arabia's deputy crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman al-Saud, in May. At that time, Prince Mohammed suggested Riyadh would bring the regional influence struggle to Iran.

Saudi Arabia, a Sunni Muslim nation, denied any involvement in the Tehran attacks. However, the assault intensified existing tensions between Riyadh and Tehran, as both nations compete for dominance in the Persian Gulf and influence across the wider Islamic world. This incident occurred just days after Riyadh and other Sunni powers severed ties with Qatar, accusing it of supporting Tehran and militant organizations. These attacks marked the first time Islamic State claimed responsibility for assaults within Iran, a Shi'ite Muslim country that is a key player in the fight against IS forces in neighboring Iraq and Syria.

Reza Seifollhai, deputy head of Iran’s National Security Council, informed state television that the attackers were Iranian nationals who had joined Islamic State. Iranian police reported arresting five suspects. Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei asserted, "These fireworks have no effect on Iran. They will soon be eliminated." He further stated, according to state TV, "They are too small to affect the will of the Iranian nation and its officials." The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a powerful entity, accused Riyadh of orchestrating the attacks and vowed retribution. A statement from the Guards declared, "This terrorist attack happened only a week after the meeting between the U.S. president (Donald Trump) and the (Saudi) backward leaders who support terrorists. The fact that Islamic State has claimed responsibility proves that they were involved in the brutal attack."

U.S. President Donald Trump expressed his condolences to the victims, adding, "states that sponsor terrorism risk falling victim to the evil they promote." The U.S. State Department and U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres both condemned the violence. Saudi Foreign Minister Adel Al-Jubeir stated he was unaware of who was responsible and that there was no evidence of Saudi extremist involvement.

**Dressed as Women**

According to Mohammad Hossein Zolfaghari, deputy interior minister, attackers disguised as women breached the main entrance of the parliament. He confirmed that one of the assailants detonated a suicide vest. A video released by the IS news agency Amaq featured a man purportedly inside the parliament saying, "Oh God, thank you. [Gunshots]. Do you think we will leave? No! We will remain, God willing." Police helicopters circled the parliament building, with snipers positioned on its roof. Iranian media reported that the incident concluded within five hours, with four attackers killed. A journalist present at the scene described the chaos: "I was inside the parliament when shooting happened. Everyone was shocked and scared. I saw two men shooting randomly."

Shortly after the parliament attack commenced, a bomber detonated a suicide vest near the shrine of Ayatollah Khomeini, the revered founder of the Islamic Republic, located a few kilometers south of the city, Zolfaghari stated. A second attacker was killed by security forces. The shrine is a significant site for tourists and religious pilgrims. Mohammadali Ansari, the shrine's overseer, recounted, "The terrorists had explosives strapped to them and suddenly started to shoot around." By late evening, Deputy Interior Minister Zolfaghari confirmed a death toll of 13, with 43 individuals injured.

The Intelligence Ministry announced that security forces had apprehended another "terrorist team" planning a third attack. Reza Seifollhai of the National Security Council indicated that Iran had previously thwarted 58 similar attacks, without specifying a timeframe.

**Regional Animosity**

These attacks followed weeks of escalating rhetorical hostility between Riyadh and Tehran. In notably direct remarks on May 2, Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who also serves as Saudi defense minister and is a son of King Salman, declared his intention to protect his country from what he termed Iranian efforts to dominate the Muslim world. He suggested that any competition for influence between the Sunni kingdom and the revolutionary Shi'ite theocracy should occur "inside Iran, not in Saudi Arabia." The following day, Iran accused Saudi Arabia of fomenting regional tension, characterizing the prince's comments as "destructive" and proof of Riyadh's support for terrorism.

The attacks could also heighten internal divisions in Iran between the newly re-elected President Hassan Rouhani, who presents himself as a reformer, and his hardline clerical and Revolutionary Guard rivals. However, President Rouhani pledged that Iran would emerge more united and resolute in its fight against regional terrorism and violence. "We will prove once again that we will crush the enemies’ plots with more unity and more strength," he stated. In an appeal for solidarity, Rouhani's chief of staff, Hamid Aboutalebi, tweeted praise for the security services: "Applause to the power and firmness of our revolutionary guards, Basij (volunteer militia), police and security forces."

Nevertheless, two senior government officials, who requested anonymity, suggested the attacks might trigger a blame game. One official commented, "They (hardliners) are very angry and will use every opportunity to grow in strength to isolate Rouhani." The other official predicted the attacks would push Iran toward "a harsher regional policy."

Militant attacks are uncommon in Tehran and other major cities. However, two Sunni militant groups, Jaish al-Adl and Jundallah, have been engaged in a protracted and deadly insurgency, primarily in remote areas, for nearly a decade. Iran's southeastern Sistan and Baluchestan province, bordering Pakistan and Afghanistan, is home to the Balouch minority and has long been a focal point for Sunni insurgents opposing the Shi'ite-led republic. Last year, Iranian authorities reported foiling a plot by Sunni militants to bomb targets in Tehran and other cities during the Muslim holy month of Ramadan.

Saudi Arabia's Foreign Minister, Adel Al-Jubeir, has issued a strong condemnation of Iran's regional activities, advocating for punitive actions against Tehran. He specifically called for measures to address Iran's alleged interference and its support for terrorist organizations. Speaking from Paris, as reported by Al Arabiya News Channel on Wednesday, June 7, 2017, Al-Jubeir asserted that Iran provides refuge to leaders from groups such as Al-Qaeda and other terrorist entities. He characterized Iran as the world's leading state sponsor of terrorism. Al-Jubeir emphasized that for Iran to be recognized as a "normal state," it must commit to upholding international law and abandon its current "political ideas."

The Foreign Minister detailed a consistent pattern of aggression exhibited by the Iranian regime. He pointed to a history of attacks on more than a dozen embassies over the past 37 years as evidence of this behavior.

These pointed remarks were made in the broader context of Saudi Arabia's recent decision to sever diplomatic ties with Qatar. This significant move was undertaken alongside seven other nations, including Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, Yemen, Maldives, Mauritius, and Mauritania. According to Al-Jubeir, this action was a direct consequence of Qatari policies that have had a detrimental impact on the region, particularly affecting the Gulf states. He maintained that Qatar had failed to honor its anti-terrorism agreements. Instead, he alleged, Qatar continued to offer support to terrorist groups.

The Saudi Foreign Minister outlined specific demands for Qatar to address these concerns. He urged Qatar to:

*   **Immediately cease all support for extremist groups.**
*   **Discontinue any backing for organizations like Hamas and the Muslim Brotherhood.**
*   **Undertake a fundamental "change their policies" to actively foster regional peace and stability.**

Al-Jubeir further clarified the intent behind these actions. He stated that the objective was not to inflict harm upon Qatar. Rather, it was a necessary decision made to convey a clear message. "We decided to make it clear that enough is enough," he declared. He expressed a genuine desire for Qatar to eventually become a constructive ally in the collective pursuit of regional peace and stability.

These significant statements from the Saudi Foreign Minister occurred concurrently with Kuwait's ongoing diplomatic efforts. Kuwait has been actively mediating to de-escalate the rising tensions between Qatar and its neighboring Gulf states.

On June 7, 2017, Tehran experienced a rare and devastating series of coordinated assaults. Armed militants, some disguised as women and equipped with suicide vests, launched a violent attack on the Iranian Parliament building. This initial strike was swiftly followed by another group of attackers who stormed the mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini, the late Iranian revolutionary founder.

The assailants engaged in a deadly rampage, firing on civilians, taking hostages, and detonating explosives. These twin terror attacks, highly unusual in Iran's tightly controlled security environment, resulted in significant casualties:

*   **12 people were killed.**
*   **More than 40 individuals were wounded.**

Iran's intelligence ministry announced the arrest of an additional "team" believed to be plotting a third attack, as reported by Reuters.

ISIS quickly claimed responsibility for the violence, releasing a graphic video purportedly filmed by one of the attackers. If confirmed, this would mark the jihadist organization's first incursion into Iran, according to The Guardian. ISIS, which adheres to a distorted interpretation of Sunni Islam, considers Shiite Muslims, the majority in Iran, to be apostates.

However, Iran's Revolutionary Guard pointed fingers elsewhere, accusing Saudi Arabia of orchestrating the attacks. A statement from the Revolutionary Guard linked the assault to a recent meeting between the U.S. president and "backward leaders who support terrorists," implicitly referencing President Trump and Saudi Arabia. The statement further suggested that ISIS's claim of responsibility was evidence of Saudi involvement with the Sunni extremist group.

This accusation comes at a particularly volatile moment in the region. Saudi Arabia and other Sunni-majority nations had recently severed diplomatic ties with Qatar, citing its alleged support for radical groups like the Muslim Brotherhood and ISIS. Qatar's engagement with Iran was also seen as a factor in this decision. President Trump, during his first foreign trip to Saudi Arabia, had emphasized a shared goal with Sunni allies to isolate Iran.

While the Saudi foreign minister denied any involvement of Saudi extremists, Wednesday's attacks, amplified by President Trump's strong anti-Iran rhetoric, intensified the existing regional rivalry. As Reuters noted, the events could also exacerbate internal tensions within Iran between moderate factions, who supported President Hassan Rouhani's recent reelection, and conservative hard-liners.

In the wake of the terrorist attacks in Iran, U.S. President Donald Trump issued a statement on Wednesday that drew a direct connection between the nation's actions and the violence it experienced. The attacks, which targeted the Iranian parliament and the Mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini in Tehran, resulted in the deaths of at least 12 people. While Iran's Revolutionary Guards initially blamed regional rival Saudi Arabia, the Islamic State of Iraq and Syria (ISIS) claimed responsibility, releasing a video that purportedly showed militants inside the parliament.

President Trump expressed condolences for the innocent victims and the Iranian people, stating, "We grieve and pray for the innocent victims of the terrorist attacks in Iran, and for the Iranian people, who are going through such challenging times." He then issued a pointed warning: "We underscore that states that sponsor terrorism risk falling victim to the evil they promote." This statement comes as the U.S. has designated Iran as a state sponsor of terrorism.

The context of these attacks is significant, occurring shortly after Saudi Arabia and other Sunni powers severed ties with Qatar, accusing it of supporting Tehran and militant groups. ISIS has a history of threatening Iran, a key player in the fight against the militants in Iraq and Syria.

Key points regarding Iran's role and the broader regional dynamics include:

*   **State Sponsorship of Terrorism:** The U.S. designation of Iran as a state sponsor of terrorism is a significant factor in the geopolitical landscape.
*   **Support for Militant Groups:** Iran is known to provide arms and funding to various terrorist organizations, notably the Shia group Hezbollah.
*   **Regional Influence:** Iran has been actively expanding its influence across the Middle East, including its alignment with Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, who faces accusations of war crimes.
*   **Nuclear Deal Opposition:** President Trump has consistently criticized the nuclear deal negotiated by the Obama administration with Iran.

The twin assaults in Iran highlight the complex and volatile nature of the region, with ISIS exploiting existing tensions and rivalries. As reported by Reuters, the events underscore the interconnectedness of state actions and the persistent threat posed by extremist groups.

Wednesday's coordinated attacks on Iran's parliament and the Imam Khomeini Mausoleum, which claimed 12 lives, underscore a long-standing threat. For years, Iran has been a prominent target on the agenda of the so-called Islamic State (IS). Despite its geographical proximity to territory controlled by the jihadist group, making it seemingly more vulnerable than Western nations, Iran had, until this recent incident, successfully thwarted attacks within its borders.

This resilience was attributed to a robust counter-terrorism program. As announced by Iranian authorities in May 2016, a significant operation dismantled 20 terrorist cells and foiled plots targeting 50 locations nationwide. This proactive stance was complemented by an anti-IS messaging campaign, efforts to contain the conflict within Syria and Iraq, engagement with diverse political and religious factions, and extensive intelligence operations. Iran's security apparatus, known for its effectiveness, played a crucial role in these preventative measures, aiming to undermine IS ideology and prevent attacks on Iranian soil. However, given Iran's status as a major target, complete immunity was always unlikely.

The current landscape sees IS militants facing setbacks in Iraq and retreating in Syria. This has led to an increased focus on external attacks. In recent months, the group has intensified its anti-Iran propaganda and sought to recruit from the country's Sunni minority. Wednesday's attack, therefore, represents a significant morale boost for IS, particularly as it marks their first successful operation within Iran, a major Shia nation and a relatively stable one in the region.

The attacks are likely to fuel calls for retribution, especially among hardliners, potentially complicating President Hassan Rouhani's administration's efforts to scale back Iran's involvement in Iraq and Syria. Public support for the Revolutionary Guards, perceived as national protectors, and for Iran's anti-IS initiatives is also expected to surge, mirroring the reaction seen in 2014 when IS seized large swathes of Iraq.

However, Iran's existing extensive commitments in Iraq and Syria strain its resources. Furthermore, its military engagement in Syria is not universally popular, often viewed as a costly endeavor with limited benefits and a high toll of Iranian lives. As noted by Dina Esfandiary, a Fellow at the Centre for Science and Security Studies (CSSS) at King's College London and an Adjunct Fellow at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS), the complex geopolitical landscape and Iran's resource limitations present ongoing challenges.

The Steubenville rape trial verdict ignited a firestorm of online criticism, not only for the case itself but also for CNN's handling of the coverage. During live reporting from the courtroom, anchor Candy Crowley and correspondent Poppy Harlow faced backlash for what many perceived as misplaced sympathy towards the convicted Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond. Harlow's statement, "Incredibly difficult, even for an outsider like me, to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believed their lives fell apart," was particularly scrutinized. Legal expert Paul Callan also commented on the profound lifelong consequences of becoming registered sex offenders.

While these observations were factually accurate, the overall tone of the CNN segment was widely interpreted as insensitive. The report, in its effort to provide context for the legal proceedings, inadvertently appeared to foster sympathy for the perpetrators. This perception was starkly contrasted with the overtly offensive online comments from genuine Steubenville rape sympathizers. For instance, a sarcastic tweet read, "The Steubenville story is all too familiar. Be responsible for your actions ladies before your drunken decisions ruin innocent lives." Another, more disturbingly sincere, declared, "So you got drunk at a party and two people take advantage of you, that's not rape you're just a loose drunk slut."

The Steubenville rape trial, a case that proved deeply polarizing, highlighted distorted perceptions of justice. The blogosphere quickly mobilized to critique CNN's reporting. Gawker's Mallory Ortberg, for example, took issue with the emphasis on the convicted individuals' "promising futures." Ortberg argued, "Their dreams and hopes were not crushed by an impersonal, inexorable legal system; Mays and Richardson raped a girl and have been sentenced accordingly." She further contended that such reporting often frames the narrative with anonymous female victims and "dynamic, sympathetic, complicated male figures," a point particularly salient given the victim's testimony was not heard until late in the trial.

The media's focus on the perceived tragedy of the convicted individuals' ruined lives was evident even before the verdict. For instance, just prior to the trial, "Good Morning America" published an extensive preview titled, "The Steubenville Rape Case: What You Haven't Heard." This piece recounted the case's events, dedicating significant attention to Mays, an "honors student," and Richmond, described as coming from the "wrong side of the tracks." The report concluded with a sympathetic tone, lamenting that the two teenagers "face incarceration in a detention center until their 21st birthdays and the almost-certain demise of their dreams of playing football." While expressing empathy for the accused might have been more palatable before their conviction, the reporting still fell short of objective journalism.

For those disturbed by this coverage, it's important to note that the Steubenville case is far from concluded. The state of Ohio is set to convene a grand jury to investigate potential further charges, signaling an impending encore for the media circus. Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine, in a statement following the verdict, emphasized, "As I said already, any rape is a tragedy. But, it is even more of a tragedy when that victim is continually re-victimized in the social media." The hope is for a more balanced and sensitive approach to future reporting, avoiding the harmful patterns seen in this case.

An update on Monday revealed that CNN was not alone in its missteps. As questions surrounding the grand jury hearing emerged, it became clear that MSNBC and Fox also allowed the victim's name to be broadcast. This highlights a broader issue within media coverage of sensitive cases. The initial reporting on the Steubenville case, particularly the focus on the perpetrators' futures and the subtle undertones of sympathy, underscores a critical need for greater sensitivity and ethical consideration when reporting on sexual assault.

Key statistics underscore the vulnerability of victims and the systemic challenges they face:

*   According to RAINN (Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network), **1 in 6 American women has been the victim of an attempted or completed rape in her lifetime.**
*   **90% of victims of adult sexual assault are female.**
*   Furthermore, **only 36% of rapes are reported to the police.**
*   Of those reported, **only 3% of perpetrators are convicted of rape.**

These figures make media portrayals that inadvertently re-victimize survivors all the more damaging. The Steubenville case serves as a potent reminder of the responsibility media outlets bear in ensuring their reporting upholds justice and respects the dignity of survivors.

CNN's extensive coverage of the Steubenville rape case verdict, which centered on the convictions of high school football stars Trent Mays and Ma’lik Richmond for the rape of a 16-year-old girl, garnered significant public attention, amplified by social media and mainstream news outlets. During its 11 a.m. broadcast, CNN featured live reports from reporter Poppy Harlow, who was on the scene in Ohio, and anchor Candy Crowley, broadcasting from Washington, who introduced the developing story, noting the national attention the case had received and the recent ruling by Judge Thomas Lipps of Hamilton County Family Court. Judge Lipps committed both defendants to the Department of Youth Services for a minimum of one year and a maximum period until they turned 21, explaining that the case was heard in juvenile court, meaning a judge, not a jury, determined the verdict and sentence.

Poppy Harlow described the courtroom atmosphere as "incredibly emotional" and "incredibly difficult" to witness, particularly for the two young men with promising futures, recounting that Ma’lik Richmond collapsed upon hearing the sentence, expressing despair, "My life is over. No one is going to want me now." Harlow emphasized the seriousness of the crime, which involved the rape of a 16-year-old girl during alcohol-fueled parties in August. CNN legal contributor Paul Callan joined the discussion, acknowledging the "courtroom drenched in tears and tragedy" and noting that such scenes of destroyed lives are common, elaborating on the long-term consequences for the convicted individuals, specifically mentioning the mandatory registration as sex offenders required under Ohio law and similar statutes in most other states, a designation he stressed would significantly impact their future employment and community integration, potentially having a more profound effect than their time in juvenile detention. Crowley brought Harlow back to discuss the victim's experience, with Harlow confirming that the victim's life would "never be the same again" and that testifying was a difficult decision for her. Harlow shared a statement from the victim's mother, who expressed pity for the two young men, stating that human compassion is a "God-given gift" that the defendants displayed a lack of, pointing to their social media posts as self-incriminating evidence. The coverage, as detailed by CNN correspondents like Poppy Harlow and legal analysts such as Paul Callan, underscored the profound and lasting impact of the crime and the legal repercussions on all parties involved, particularly the young defendants and the victim, with additional context provided by organizations like the Associated Press, whose photographer Keith Srakocic captured images of the defense attorneys with their clients, offering a visual layer to the reporting.

The media's handling of the Steubenville, Ohio, rape case, particularly following the convictions of two teenage boys, ignited significant criticism from journalists, media watchdogs, and advocacy groups. Concerns were raised about a perceived pattern of leniency and victim-blaming in reporting by major outlets, including CNN, Fox News, and MSNBC.

### Framing the Narrative: Focus on the Victim vs. the Accused

Critics, such as Lauren Wolfe, director of the Women's Media Center's Women Under Siege project, and Xeni Jardin, highlighted instances where reporting seemed to unduly focus on the victim's actions, such as describing her as "allegedly drunk," rather than centering the assault itself. This approach, they argued, risked perpetuating rape culture, a phenomenon Wolfe described as being reflected by the mainstream media. She questioned whether the public should expect more from journalistic institutions, particularly in their tendency to prioritize the defense strategies of the accused over the victim's experience.

The controversy was further fueled by the decision of Fox News, CNN, and MSNBC to reveal the woman's name, a move that Think Progress decried as "journalistic negligence." This act contrasted sharply with the ethical considerations often applied in such sensitive cases.

### Questioning Media Priorities and Framing

Wolfe specifically criticized ABC's *Nightline* for framing the case as a "cautionary tale for teenagers living in today’s digital world." She contended that the core issue was the rape itself, not the broader implications of online activities. This framing, she argued, diverted attention from the fundamental crime.

The media's emphasis on the convicted boys' futures was also a point of contention. CNN's Poppy Harlow's comment that the convicted boys "literally watched as their life fell apart" drew criticism for its apparent lack of attention to the victim's trauma. This echoed problematic framing seen in *The New York Times'* 2011 reporting on a gang rape in Cleveland, Texas, where similar insensitivities were noted.

### The Role of Defendants' Identities and Athleticism

Initial media coverage often provided minimal details about the young woman, instead focusing heavily on the male defendants. For instance, interviews with the attorney and former guardians of one convicted teen, such as those conducted by Matt Lauer, presented the young man in a favorable light, raising concerns about the insufficient focus on the victim's well-being and the impact of the crime.

A significant portion of the media narrative also revolved around the boys' identities as football players. As BuzzFeed's Katie Heaney noted, an accuser's athletic achievements could be framed as an "alibi," subtly shifting blame or minimizing the severity of the offense. Salon's Irin Carmon critiqued CNN's coverage, observing that while footage of the victim might have been scarce, the narration by anchors like Harlow was not. Carmon argued that the gravity of the crimes was downplayed and replaced by a "sideshow," suggesting that such leniency was seldom extended to young offenders from less privileged backgrounds.

### Commendable Reporting and a Call for Context

Amidst this widespread criticism, both Wolfe and Carmon commended Yahoo Sports' Dan Wetzel for his reporting. Wolfe praised his column for "smashing victim-blaming" and for exploring the town's "culture of extreme arrogance." Wetzel suggested that the local football team might have fostered an environment where impunity was common, citing evidence from social media and text messages that depicted a team that was "spiraling out of control."

Wetzel also offered crucial context on rape as a crime rooted in power and control, citing expert opinions and emphasizing the pervasive arrogance in Steubenville. While acknowledging the inherent difficulties in covering rape cases, Wetzel's approach highlighted the media's responsibility to provide comprehensive, context-rich reporting that moves beyond mere sensationalism and avoids perpetuating harmful narratives. His work served as a counterpoint to the more problematic coverage, demonstrating a more responsible and insightful way to address such sensitive issues.
